Administrative Provisions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
a
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of
the Administrative Provisions Bill I would
like to say in dealing with this measure,
which is of
highly technical character,
that I am sure the House will agree with
me that if ever there was a case where the
attachment of a memorandum to the print
of a measure was justified it is the case of
the present Bill.
As the memorandum says, it deals solely
with points of technical difficulty which
have shown themselves in the course of the
administration of various departments. May
I give an instance; Clause 1 seeks to remove
difficulties which have arisen owing to a
certain conflict between Orders of the Joint
Exchequer Board and the provisions of the
Ministries of Northern Ireland Act of 1921.
They are merely technical difficulties, such
as the vesting of property under a Joint
Exchequer Board Order in a Minister instead
of a Ministry. But though they are purely
technical, it is necessary to remove them by
legislation as the powers of the Joint Exchequer Board passed with the issue of its
Order in each particular case.
It will be noted that provision has been
made in sub-section 5 of the Clause to give
retrospective effect to this Clause of the Bill
in order to remove any doubts as to the
legality of administrative action already
taken which either transgressed
Joint
Exchequer Board Order in carrying out the
provisions of the Ministries of Northern
Ireland Act, or vice versa.
a
The Bill further makes for the unification
of administration of the legislation dealing
with the Irish Sailors and Soldiers Land
Trust, all the powers now being conferred
upon the Ministry of Finance instead of
being divided between that Ministry and the
Ministry of Home Affairs. Unnecessary
duplication is hereby avoided.
The same Clause, Clause 2, adjusts minor
difficulties in regard to the issue of birth and
death certificates under the Unemployment
Insurance Acts, assigning the duty of issue
to the Registrar-General, an officer of the
Ministry of Finance, who already exercises
similar duties in regard to health insurance,
etc., instead of to the Ministry of Home
Affairs.
95 Administrative Provisions Bill THE SENATE. County Officers and Courts Bill 96
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
This same Clause further removes any doubt
as to the proper department for the administration of harbour matters by specifically
assigning them to the Ministry of Commerce.
The third Clause legalises a simplified form
of mortgage deed, found in the schedule, for
the convenience of local authorities borrowing
on the security of the rates.
Clause 4 is very technical, and is, I think,
sufficiently explained in the memorandum.
Clause 5 is useful as legalising shortened
forms for the designations of the various
Ministries which have already passed into
current use. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill, although very
technical, as the noble Viscount has told us,
is undoubtedly very necessary, and there is
nothing in it, I think, that can invite anything in the nature of criticism. I should
like to observe that I presume that Subclause 2 of Clause 1, dealing with the assets
of the Petty Sessions Clerks Fund and the
Fund under the Dogs Regulation Act, is
designedly left in the way it comes before
us, because on comparing that Sub-Clause
with others we are told nothing as to the
purpose to which the Minister of Home
Affairs is to apply those funds. The funds
in question have always been used, as the
House knows, for some purpose more or less
ancillary to the Summary Jurisdiction
Courts. I imagine that is the intention and
that it has been deliberately decided that it
would be better not to say anything more
about it.
The other little point to which I wish to
refer arises out of the very interesting
Clause 5. A most valuable Clause, it will
shorten very much the language of our Acts
of Parliament. It is really a step towards
that private Interpretation Act which we are
all awaiting. I think one of the first Bills
introduced in this Senate applied the principles of the Imperial Interpretation Act to
our Statutes, but we are beginning to feel
the need of a separate Interpretation Act for
what I might describe
pressions, and "Ministry" is one of those
expressions. Another is "Resident Magistrates," which does not come within the scope
of this Bill. But there remains the question
of the "Gazette," and I would venture to
suggest that the Government should consider
whether it is worth while to insert here that
the expression "Gazette" means the "Belfast
The expression can only apply
Gazette."
to
as our own exa comparatively few Acts because we
generally mean the "Belfast Gazette," but
I think it is perhaps worth consideration
whether a few words should be added there.
As the Bill stands, as a whole, I need scarcely
say that I support it.
As
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator who
has just spoken with regard to Sub-section 2
of Clause 1, I will inquire into the question
he has raised. I think that what he suggests
is probably the intention of the Bill.
regards the question of whether we should
embody the expression "Gazette" in Clause
5 so that the word "Gazette" should mean
the "Belfast Gazette," I am not entirely in
accord with the hon. Member. I think it
is a small matter and not of very great
importance, but I will inquire into it and
give the hon. Member an answer at a later
date.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
a
Bill committed to
whole House for Tuesday.
Committee of the
County Officers and Courts Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of
the County Officers and Courts Bill, I do not
think that the House will require any lengthy
statement from me in explanation of the
Measure, because the printed memorandum
attached to the Bill supplies a concise and,
I think, a very clear description of its provision and purpose. The Bill is based upon
the findings of the Viceregal Committee of Inquiry of 1919, over which Judge Wakely
presided. I may remark that there was
simultaneously sitting in Great Britain a committee of the same nature as that under Judge
Wakely upon whose findings were based those
reforms which were embodied in the County
Courts Act passed by the Imperial Parliament
very recently. The recommendations of both
commissions to which I have referred were
very similar, and the Bill before the House
to-day is, consequently, along the same lines
as the County Courts Act of the Imperial
Parliament to which I have already referred.
Without going into great detail, I may state
quite shortly the principle behind this Bill,
which is to give to officers who are performing work of a public character the status
and conditions of service of Civil Servants,
and by reorganisation, or rather the constitution of a new general County Courts Service
in Northern Ireland, to provide the encouragement and due reward for efficiency by
giving opportunities of promotion and the
ultimate prospect of superannuation at the
end of service.
Under the present system, or rather lack of
system, the clerical employees of the Clerks
of the Crown and Peace have an insecurity
97 County Officers and Courts Bill-

7 MAY 1925
Second Reading


of tenure, which is quite incompatible with
the public character of the work which they
perform. They are the employees, not of the
Crown but of the Clerk of the Crown and
Peace in his personal and individual capacity.
They have the very limited prospects of promotion which an isolated and self-contained
office of a Clerk of the Crown and Peace
affords. They have no prospects of superannuation, and yet their work is not of a private
character, nor their service of a personal sort.
This Bill, as I have already said, will remove
the present anomalous conditions attaching
to their office, and give them their proper
status as established Civil Servants.
I should draw the attention of the House
to the fact that the Bill does not touch existing Clerks of the Crown and Peace. In the
first place, they are sufficiently protected by
the Government of Ireland Act, and, in the
second place, they are servants of the
Imperial Government as Local Registrars of
Title, and as Registration Officers for the revision of voters' lists.
Existing officers are specifically dealt with in
Part II of the Bill. It is provided that any
person over 17 years of age with an aggregate
of seven years of service in the office of any
Clerk of the Crown and Peace may, upon a
certificate of fitness issued by the Lord Chief
Justice with the concurrence of the Ministry
of Finance, elect to join the new County Court
service within twelve months of the passing
of the Act. It will be noted, I am sure, with
pleasure by the House that special provision
has been made for the full recognition of war
service in calculating this period of seven
years.
Apart from these employees of the Clerks of
the Crown and Peace, the Registrar of Belfast
is specifically given the option of inclusion in
the scheme by Clause 7, Sub-section 19, as he
is the only Registrar of a Civil Bill Court who
is a whole-time officer.
I think that at the present stage the House
will not expect me to go into any further
detail on this Bill, but I shall be happy to
give any information in my power, if any
question should be raised later in the debate.
I beg to move.
Mr. GREER: I have read this Bill over
very carefully, and I think it is a good Bill.
It raises the clerks who are at present employed in the offices of the Clerks of the
Crown and Peace to the position of Civil
Servants, and as they are doing public work,
I think that is the position they should occupy,
and be subject to the Civil Service rules.
The Act provides that the whole service of
the Clerk of the Crown and Peace at his office
is to be general service, not confined to each
County, and provision is made for the interchange of Clerks of the Crown and Peace or
the clerks in their offices from one County to
another. I think that is a very valuable provision, and, on the whole, I think it is a good
Bill. I must say it is rather better now than
it was when it was introduced in another
place, because some Amendments have been
inserted during its progress through the other
House, that I intended to have moved to
have introduced here.
There are one or two points I would like to
refer to in the Bill. I think a clerk, with less
than seven years' service, ought to have the
right to decide whether to come in under
Clause 7 of the Bill. I think that if he had
four years' service he ought to be in a position
to say whether it will be better for him to be
a Civil Servant or to earn his livelihood in
some other way. He might have to satisfy
the Civil Service examiners or some other
Court of that kind, if he had only four years'
service, that he was competent to discharge
the duties of the office.
In regard to superannuation, it is rather a
difficult question, but I think it is not fair
to the clerks who have had long service in
these offices, and who are good clerks and
specially qualified, to make them decide
whether they will come into the Civil Service or not, and to say that if they do come
into the Civil Service they cannot have more
than two years superannuation. I know two
men who have over thirty years' experience
in the work of the office, and they would only
get two years' superannuation. I think that
is not quite proper. I was going to move an
amendment on that point, but I am sorry to
say that I was told it would be out of order,
because it would be putting an additional
charge on the Government.
There is an amendment I do intend to move
to Clause 11, about the times for holding the
Courts. It is very necessary that this time
should be flexible, because sometimes there is
a good deal of business coming on in the
Court, and sometimes there is very little,
and there may be a new body that will re.
quire the use of the Courthouse. An order
was made in the Act of Parliament passed in
1851, stating that the Judge of the Court
should in the Michaelmas term fix his four
Courts for each term in the subsequent year.
In that case his practice was to confer with
the Solicitors practising in the Courts, and
with his own Clerk of the Peace and see what
were suitable dates. Every year he fixed the
dates for those Courts.
Then, under the County Courts Act of
1877, power was given to the Lord Lieutenant
in Council to arrange within what dates the
Courts should be fixed by the Judge of the
D
99 County Officers and Courts Bill-
Second Reading 100
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Greer]
Court.
That power has ceased, and it is
desirable to have a new Section providing for
the fixing of the dates of the Courts. I am
afraid this Section is not very happily
worded, because I think it will be held that
it gives the Governor of Northern Ireland
the power each year to fix the precise dates
of the Court, and I would propose to insert
some words providing that he should merely
make a Privy Council Order, similar to the
one the Lord Lieutenant had the power to
make under the Act of 1877, providing for a
period of time within which the Courts
should be held, and then let the Judge, as in
the past, continue to fix the definite dates.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I welcome this
Bill,
having regard to the improved
status which it gives to the Clerks
in the Crown and Peace Offices. I have
come in contact with them on many
occasions and I have been struck with the
efficiency with which they have discharged
their very important duties. They are
rather more than clerks or employees, and
they often perform duties that might be discharged by the Clerk of the Crown if he
were there, and more particularly in regard
to the Registrations of Titles, of which I
think they are masters. Anything that will
enhance their position and improve their
status is distinctly desirable from every
point of view.
I do not propose to follow Senator Greer in
the details he has gone into because I have not
been able to give the Bill that close examination that the hon. Member has, nor perhaps am I as capable of doing so, but I just
wish to say a few words in support of the
Bill so far as it improves the position of the
clerks.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I rise to support
Senator Greer in his plea on behalf of the
old clerks now in the offices of Clerks of the
Crown and Peace. I myself know of the
case of a clerk who has spent his entire life
in the office of a Clerk of the Crown and
Peace and who has done very good public
work, and I hope the appeal Senator Greer
has made on behalf of such persons will be
sympathetically considered by the Government.
With regard to the fixing of the dates for
holding the Courts, I rather regret that I
have not had the Bill in my hands for a
longer time because I should have liked to
have submitted it to a friend of mine who
takes a special interest in this matter. He
takes such an interest in it that a short
time ago he drafted a complete scheme covering a whole year's work of the Courts, and
he showed how a considerable amount of
public time could be saved and the general
convenience of the public could be considered. He gave me the scheme he had
drawn up and I sent it, at his request, to
the Ministry of Home Affairs. I do not
know what became of it but I regret that I
have not had the Bill a little longer in my
hands because I would have submitted it to
him and he might have given his views upon
it, as he is an authority on the subject. My
real reason for rising is to support the appeal
of Senator Greer on behalf of those who have
spent a long time in the service.
Mr. LESLIE: I have only two very minor
observations to offer on the Bill. The first
relates to the provision which, as the noble
Viscount suggested, we should all welcome,
for making allowance for war service in
calculating the period of employment. The
provision is in Sub-clause 2 of Clause 7, and
it limits the duration of any period of employment which may be counted to an
aggregate of four years of service in His
Majesty's forces. At first sight I cannot see
the reason for that limitation. Why should
he not be credited with the full amount of
his war service? The House knows only too
well what we generally mean by the late war.
It began on 4th August, 1914, and when the
silence fell in the Autumn of 1918, more than
four years had elapsed, and even then the
war was not over. As a matter of fact the
war did not end officially until the dates prescribed by different Acts of Parliament
having relation to different belligerents.
As far as I remember Turkey was the last,
but it was four years from August, 1914,
before the late war terminated, and I suggest that if possible a man who may have
served during any of that time should be
allowed to count his full service.
As regards the expression in the paragraph, "the late war," the occasion will
probably arise in the future when we shall
have to define that more precisely. In an
Empire the size of the British Empire we are
always exposed to some kind of war with
some dusky braves in Africa or elsewhere,
and we cannot always go on referring to the
last war as the late war. The point will
arise in the future.
The other small matter I desire to refer to
is in Clause 13, dealing with Regulations. I
should like to suggest that the Regulations
contemplated are essential Megulations
which we ought definitely to decree shall be
printed and shall be put on sale.
I think
also that we might not unreasonably require
that they shall be laid on the Table of the
House. After all, these Regulations will
deal with conditions of promotion and conditions of appointment, and they will be of
some interest to the general public. I think
they should be laid on the Table of the
House, and that we should provide that they
shall be printed. Taking the Bill as
whole I am distinctly and definitely in favour
of it.
a'
Civil Pensions Bill
Adjournment
7 MAY 1925
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I think I may say that on the whole
31 the House has given this Bill a warm
reception. The points raised by most of the
hon. Members who have spoken are chiefly
Committee points. I imagine that this is a
Bill which we cannot amend to any radical
extent because it would be
breach of
privilege, and therefore I am afraid that we
may not be able to do what the hon. Member, Senator Greer, has asked us to do.

a
As regards the point raised by Senator
Leslie with reference to the four years' service in the late war, I should imagine,
although I cannot speak with authority, that
there would be very few who would come
under this Sub-section who would have
served over four years in the late war. Such
cases would be extremely rare, I should
think. As regards the hon. Member's
suggestion that we should substitute some
other phraseology for the expression "late
war" because of the possibility that we may
have another war in some part of these
dominions, I should like to express the hope
that we shall never be engaged in another
war, and that this will always be the late
war.
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill be
now read a Second time," agreed to.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move that this House do now adjourn. It is proposed that the House should
adjourn until Tuesday next, and for the information of the House I think it would be
as well if I said that we shall probably have
to sit on three days next week-Tuesday,
Wednesday, and Thursday. On Tuesday it
will be necessary to appoint a Committee on
two local Bills, and to take the Committee
Stage of the Bills we have read a Second
time this afternoon. I do not think there is
any more information as regards the sittings
of the House that I can give hon. Members
at the present moment.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes before Four o'clock
until Tuesday next.

periods" to the first "of" in line 37 (not

105 Co. Officers and Courts Bill--

12 MAY 1925
Committee.
E

a
da
This Amendment deals with the matter that
I mentioned on the Second Reading of the
Bill. While quite agreeing with the Noble
Viscount that my proposed Amendment is not
likely to be of service to any large number of
persons, I still venture to think that the
Amendment is desirable, because it is quite
possible for a man to have accumulated more
than four years of service during the late war,
and in the case of any person who did do that
that person would be of the class which we
most desire to have. In any case he cannot
get the advantage of the Section unless he
has a certificate from the Lord Chief Justice
and the Ministry of Finance that he is a fit
and proper person to be employed in that
service, and I do not think the extra concession would do any harm to anybody else, and
so far as I know it would not be against any
established precedent. On the contrary, I am
inclined to think that if we removed the
restriction altogether as regards the aggregate
of four years' service, and withdrew that
restriction it would make our tribute rather
more gracious. Accordingly, I beg to move.
Mr. GREER: I do not
a
see why there
should be any restriction at all. Why should
there even be
seven years' restriction?
There is a provision in the Section that a man
must have attained
certain amount of
cleverness and ability before he is taken into
the Civil Service, and, therefore, I think the
seven years' restriction should be withdrawn
a
20 from the Section of the Bill so that if a
clerk has been four years in an office of the
Clerk of the Crown and Peace he should be
entitled to elect whether to continue in the

d
Mr. GLENDINNING: I suggest that this

(2) Every person employed at the date of

1921, and bound while SO employed to

The Sub-section in the Bill rules out all

and Peace under an agreement that they
should retain their position so long as they

they should be entitled to promotion, if earned
☑ and if possible. It is contrary to public
policy to
The CHAIRMAN: I am sorry to interrupt
the hon. Member, but if he is moving an
Amendment he must move it after the
Amendment we are dealing with has been disposed of. Is the hon. Member actually
moving an Amendment?
Mr. GLENDINNING: If I am in order.
The CHAIRMAN: You are not in order
at the moment. Your Amendment can be
moved after Senator Leslie's Amendment has
been dealt with.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I was following on
the same lines as the hon. Member, Senator

The CHAIRMAN: The hon. Member is
entitled to make a Speech, as Senator Greer
did, but not to move an Amendment at this
moment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I quite appreciate Senator Leslie's
reasons for desiring that this Amendment
should be inserted in the Bill, but I can assure
the hon. Member and the House that his
Amendment is
The actual
unnecessary.
proviso as it stands in the Bill was inserted
in deference to representations in another
place, and since its insertion the war service
of the officers concerned has been investigated. This investigation shows that the
further amendment proposed by the hon.
Member would not have the effect of granting
additional rights to one single officer, and
therefore I think the hon. Member will
realise that the Government see no reason
for accepting his amendment.
In reply to Senator Greer, I understand
that he was referring to sub-Section 2 of this
Clause, and that he wishes to delete the
words referring to the period of seven years,
and put in some other words. This question
has been very seriously considered by the
Government, and I have to tell the hon. Member that they are not prepared to make any
alteration in the number of years. I would
also like to point out to the Committee that
there is no question of examination, and the
Government are not prepared, so far as my
information goes, to make any alteration.
a
-
Mr. LESLIE: I thank the noble Viscount
for having pointed out to us that the Bill as
originally drafted has been, shall I say, enriched by the insertion of
provision
which ceased to be operative from the time
of its insertion. That being the case there
is no object in asking the Committee to insert an unnecessary benefaction, and I ask
leave to withdraw the amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The CHAIRMAN: Now, if the hon. Member, Senator Glendinning, wishes to move an

107 Co. Officers and Courts Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I did not intend to
go so far as to move an amendment. I was
merely asking the Government to alter a
clause in the way I suggest.
The CHAIRMAN: In that case that is
moving an amendment.
Mr. GLENDINNING: Well, then, i am
quite prepared to move an amendment.
The CHAIRMAN: Will the hon. Member
write out his amendment and hand it in?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
Perhaps I might make a suggestion to the
hon. Member, and that is that he should put
down his amendment for the Report stage.
The CHAIRMAN: Will the hon. Member
agree to that course?
Mr. GLENDINNING: I am quite agreeable to that.
Mr. GREER: There is another matter
arising out of this clause that I desire to
refer to. I do not think it would be in order
to move an amendment, but I wish to ask
the Government to consider the case of three
Chief Clerks in the Clerk of the Crown and
Peace Offices with a view to seeing if they
will bring in an amendment to this clause.
There are three Chief Clerks in the Crown
and Peace Offices in the Six Counties who
have had over thirty years' service. One has
had thirty-eight years' service. The services
of these three clerks have been continuous and
always devoted to the business of the Clerk
of the Crown and Peace Office. They are
very intelligent men who have had very
responsible work to perform, and they have
discharged with great ability duties that
have extended over a variety of subjects,
because the Clerk of the Crown and Peace
Office is the most important legal office in
the city or town and these men have been
practically carrying on the business of the
office for over thirty years.
According to my reading of the Bill, if they
decide to become Civil servants they will
have to retire at sixty years of age, and
then they will only get a compassionate
allowance of two years of whatever salary
they were in receipt of for the last year of
their service. I think that is really very
hard on these three men. It would not cost
very much and I think these men should
get at least ten years superannuation allowance, and I do not think it should be left to
the Ministry of Finance to say what allowance they should get. If possible it should
be fixed in the Bill, so that these men will
know where they stand in arranging their
affairs and how much they will be entitled
to receive, instead of having to wait until
they retire. I would ask the Government to
consider whether it would not be possible to
look into their case and to give them an
allowance of at least ten years.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: What Senator Greer wants the
Government to do is to increase the compassionate allowance to certain clerks who have
done very valuable service for something like
thirty years. I might remind the hon. Member that if an Amendment was moved to do
what he suggests it would be ruled out of
order. It would not be competent for this
House to insert such an Amendment in the
Bill, and if it did so the other House would
be perfectly justified in refusing to agree to
it. The Government have considered this
question very fully. The Ministry of Finance,
as the hon. Member knows, has a. certain
discretion in cases of this kind. I cannot
hold out any hope that the Government will
do what the hon. Member suggests, but I will
put the matter before the proper authorities,
and see what can be done.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Decision of Ministry of Finance)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Payment of expenses of clerical

a

and office requisites of
Clerk of the

Mr. GLENDINNING: I presume the
effect of this Clause is that in future all the
stationery, printing, office requisities, and
allowances will be issued through His
Majesty's Stationery Office in Belfast. At
present this stationery, printing, etc., is included in the contracts given by the County
Councils to firms in the Six Counties. Under
this Clause that will all be taken from them
and centralised in His Majesty's Stationery
Officein Belfast, with the result that the
trades concerned will suffer to that extent in
their business. It is quite possible that some
little economy may be effected by the change,
but I question the prudence of removing
everything from the counties to Belfast. As
regards one of the trades affected, the
printing trade, I know that throughout the
Six Counties it is in a condition of distress at
the present time. The printing trade in the
counties suffers greater disabilities than the
trade in Belfast inasmuch as the wages are
very high, and it is the object of the employers to maintain their staffs and keep them
in employment. Therefore, I think it is more
or less a mistake on the part of the Government, for the sake of a very little economy,
to remove all these benefits from the counties

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
This Clause is also a privileged Clause with
which this House is not competent to deal by
way of Amendment. I would venture to
suggest to the hon. Member that the question
he raises is really a matter for the Government. Under this Bill the County Court
officers become Civil Servants, and are looked
upon as Government officials, and, therefore,
all the arrangements which it is necessary to
make should be made as provided for in the
Bill. When the proper time comes the hon.
Member might make his representations in
the proper quarter, and perhaps his views
might be followed, but even if the Government
were prepared to accept his suggestion it is
not competent for us to put an Amendment of
that description in the Bill which is now before the House.

Bill Court in and for the county borough
of Belfast or the county borough of Londonderry.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to insert the

"(3) Any regulations made under this
Section shall be laid before Parliament as
soon as may be after they are made, and
shall be published in the Belfast Gazette."
When I put down on the Paper the Amendment which stands in my name I had not
had an opportunity of consulting the various
precedents; I mean the precedents laid down
by our own Parliament dealing with matters
111 Co. Officers and Courts Bill.
Late Rt. Hon. W. F. Massey. 112
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
of that kind, and I am rather inclined to
think that if I had had that opportunity I
the Amendment
2might have worded
somemay
what differently. However, I think I
submit it to the Committee as it stands.

and
The point I desire to make is, first of all,
that such regulations as are suggested in
this Section are of a class which ought
to be laid before Parliament,
essentially of a class of which notification should be given in the Belfast
Gazette." I rather gather from our recent
discussion on the Rules Regulation Bill that
unless Parliament does intervene now and
say definitely that such and such a rule shall
be published in the "Belfast Gazette" it
might happen that it might not be so published. These regulations will deal with the
duties and hours of attendance of persons
and so on, and I think they should be both
laid and published.
As regards the precedents to which I was
referring, I may say that I cannot find any
precedent set by our own Parliament for
requiring any regulation made by His Grace
the Governor to be laid before Parliament,
and I must admit that it might very well
be thought that a more proper channel for
conveying such regulations to Parliament
would be by the process of a Command paper.
Nor can I give any precedent for laying
before Parliament rules and regulations made
with the concurrence of the Lord Chief
Justice, but I do find a precedent as in our
recent Bill dealing with the simplification
of appointment to the Commission of the
Peace I do definitely find there, that
regulations made by His Grace shall be
published in the "Belfast Gazette."
The English Superannuation Act of 1859
also seems to point to matters of this kind
being laid before Parliament and therefore,
I feel that I was justified in placing this
amendment before the Committee in the form
in which I had originally put it on the
Paper. I have taken the form itself from
what I might call one of the minor administrative sections of the Education Act which
refers to laying before Parliament, instead of
from another Section of the same Act which
provides that any regulations laid before
Parliament should be subject to annulment
if an Address was moved in the ordinary
way. I thought it was sufficient in this case
to move the amendment as it appears on the
Paper.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am obliged to the hon. Senator
for his amendment. He is right in saying
that these regulations are of some importance, and the Government are prepared to
accept the amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.

MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
I should like to state for the knowledge and
convenience of Members that owing to the
state of business in another place it will not
be necessary for this House to meet tomorrow. We propose that the House should
sit on Thursday, when the business will be
the consideration of the further stages
of the Bills which have been under consideration this afternoon, the proposed new Standing Orders relative to Divorce, and any other
Bills that may be received from another
place. That will be the business for Thursday.
I am afraid I am not in a position to say anything with regard to the business for next
week.
In moving the adjournment I think I shall
be voicing the wishes of hon. Members if I
place on record the sorrow which we feel as a
legislative assembly at the passing of a great
New Zealand statesman. I refer to the Right
Hon. W. F. Massey. (Hon. Members: Hear,

THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 14th May, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Local Bills.
AMENDMENT TO STANDING ORDERS.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move that
the Standing Orders of this House be
amended by adding at end the following
Orders:-
PART XIII.
DIVORCE BILLS.
a
174. A Bill for the Dissolution of a
marriage between persons domiciled in
Northern Ireland shall not be introduced
into Parliament except upon
Petition
to bring in such Bill. Such Petition shall
be signed by the party to the said marriage
who desires the dissolution thereof (hereafter in these Orders called "the
Petitioner"); and there shall be annexed
to such Petition a printed copy of the proposed Bill, which shall be deemed to form
part thereof.
175. Every such Petition shall be lodged in
the office of the Clerk of the Parliaments, together with the following documents:-
(a) an official copy of the proceedings
taken or had in the Court having jurisdiction over matrimonial causes, at the
place of his or her domicile or residence,
or in some other Court having jurisdiction in that behalf, at the suit of the
Petitioner;
(b) in any case in which any trial at
nisi prius has been had, or any writ of
within the United
inquiry executed
Kingdom, wherein the Petitioner has
been party, a report of the proceedings
upon such trial or writ of inquiry.
The accuracy of the said reports shall
be verified by the Affidavit of the
Petitioner.
176. A copy of such Petition and Bill shall
be delivered to the other party to the said
marriage (hereinafter in these Orders called
"the Respondent") within seven days of the
lodgment of such petition, in the office of the
Clerk of the Parliaments; and in case of
infancy, such copy shall be delivered to the
guardian or next relation of full age, not
concerned in the consequences of the Bill.
177. Every such Petition and Bill shall
thereupon be referred to the Examiner of
Petitions for Local Bills, who shall certify
to both Houses whether or not the Standing
Orders have been complied with in respect
of them.
If the Examiner is satisfied that it is
impossible for the Petitioner to deliver a
copy of the Petition and Bill to the
Respondent, he may accept proof of
substituted service.
178. If the Examiner shall certify that
Standing Orders have been complied with,
the certificate shall be laid upon the Table
of the House of Commons, whereupon the
Bill shall be deemed to have been read a
First Time, and shall be recorded in the
Votes as having been so read.
179. The Respondent, if desirous of being
heard upon the merits against any such Bill
shall enter an appearance in the office of
the Clerk of the Parliaments, on or before
the seventh day after the day on which the
Bill has been read a First Time in the House
of Commons.
180. No such Bill shall be read a Second
Time in the House of Commons earlier than
the third, or later than the fifth day on
which the House shall sit after the day upon
which such Bill has been read a First Time.
181. Every such Bill, after having been
read a Second Time in the House of Commons,
shall be sent to the Senate, and shall be laid
upon the Table of that House, and when so
laid shall be deemed to have been read a
First Time, and shall be recorded in the
Minutes as having been so read.
182. No such Bill shall be read a Second
Time in the Senate earlier than the third, or
later than the fifth, day on which the House
shall sit after the First Reading thereof.
183. Every such Bill, after having been
read a Second Time in the Senate shall
(whether the appearance mentioned in
Section 179 of these Orders has or has not
been entered) be referred to a Joint Standing
Committee of both Houses, to be called the
"Joint Committee on Divorce Bills."
184. The Joint Committee on Divorce Bills
shall be appointed at the commencement of
each Session, and shall consist of the Speaker
of the Senate, four Members of the House
of Commons appointed by that House, and
three other Members of the Senate appointed
by the Senate. The Speaker of the Senate
Standing Orders
Amendment.
14 MAY 1925

shall be the Chairman of such Committee,
and in his absence the chair shall be taken
by such Member thereof as the Joint Committee may choose. Three Members (of whom
the Chairman shall be one) shall form the
quorum.
185. The said Joint Committee shall inquire upon lawful evidence as to whether the
averments in the Preamble are provided to
the satisfaction of the Committee or not. The
Petitioner shall attend the Committee, in
order to his being examined, if the Committee
think fit, whether there has or has not been
any collusion directly or indirectly on his
part, relative to any act of adultery that may
have been committed by his wife, or whether
there be any collusion directly or indirectly
between the petitioner and the respondent,
or any other person or persons touching such
Divorce Bill, or touching any proceedings or
sentence of divorce had in any Court for
matrimonial causes, or touching any action
at law which may have been brought by the
Petitioner against any person for criminal
conversation with the Petitioner's wife.
186. The Joint Committee shall report to
both Houses their findings upon the several
matters mentioned in Section 185 of these
Orders, and shall state whether in the judgment of the Committee the Bill should pass
or not, and, if so, whether with any and what
amendments.
187. The report upon every such Bill shall
lie upon the Table of both Houses, and every
such Bill, if amended in Committee, shall be
ordered to lie upon the Table of the House
of Commons; but if not amended in Committee, it shall be ordered to be read the
Third Time in that House.
188. In the case of Divorce Bills ordered
to lie upon the Table of the House of
Commons which have been amended by the
Joint Committee three clear days shall
intervene between the Report and the consideration of the Bill.
189. Every such Bill, when it has been read
a Third Time in the House of Commons, shall
be sent to the Senate, and shall, if amended
by the Joint Committee or by the House of
Commons, be laid upon the Table of the
Senate, but if not so amended may be put
down for Third Reading in the Senate.
190. In the case of Divorce Bills ordered
to lie upon the Table of the Senate, which
have been amended by the Joint Committee
or by the House of Commons, three clear
days shall intervene between the arrival of
the Bill in the Senate Land the Third
Reading.

191. The following Standing Orders
lative to Local Bills shall apply to Divorce
Bills, with the modifications and adaptations
herein stated:-
(a) No. 79 (as to admission by Examiner
of Affidavits);
(b) No. 80 (as to special report by
Examiner);
(c) Nos. 83 and 84.
(d) Nos. 103 to 109 inclusive.
(e) No. 111.
192. The Table of the Fees to be charged
at the Houses of Parliament, being Appendix
B to the standing Orders relative to Local
Bills shall apply to Divorce Bills, unless
otherwise directly stated in the said
Appendix, as if in such Table the words
"the Petitioner" were substituted for the

Respondent" for the words "the

Senate.
£200
In rising to move the Amendment of the
Standing Orders of the Senate and the
House of Commons relative to Local Bills,
I think it will be expected of me to explain
to this House how it is that so large an
addition to our Standing Orders for Local
Bills has become necessary.
Hon. Members are no doubt aware that
by the Matrimonial Causes and Marriage
Law (Ireland) Amendment Acts of 1870 and
1871, the former matrimonial jurisdiction
of ecclesiastical courts was transferred to
the Crown, the jurisdiction being to be
exercised by a Judge of the Court of Probate sitting as the Judge of a newly-established Court of Matrimonial Causes and
Matters.
So far as Divorce was concerned, the
jurisdiction of that Court was limited to
the grant of divorce a mensa et thoro,
that is, a judicial separation which did not
break the matrimonial link and, therefore,
did not allow of the re-marriage of either
of the parties. Divorce a vinculo, the complete rupture of the marriage tie which
carried with it the right of re-marriage,
was only obtainable, as heretofore, by the
process of Private Bill in the Parliament
of Westminster.
Standing Orders—
Amendment.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
It is not for me here and now to pass
judgment upon a system which kept the
method of dealing with matrimonial causes
in Ireland in a fashion so antiquated and
comparatively so costly, a fashion which
had long been abandoned elsewhere
throughout the British Empire. That is
not a subject for argument to-day. The
facts are simply as I state them and it is
with them that we are called upon to deal.
The task before us at present is to provide
for the immediate continuity of the rights
of the subject, whatever changes we may
exercise at some future date.
When the Government of Ireland Act of
1920 established the Parliament and
Government of Northern Ireland, a twofold problem arose in relation to Divorce
Bills from persons domiciled in Northern
Ireland. In the first place, it had to be
decided whether the Imperial Parliament
was the proper Parliament to accept Bills
of this nature, or whether a Petition for a
Divorce Bill should now be lodged with
the Clerk of the Parliaments of Northern
Ireland. That question has now been
decided, the Clerk of the Parliaments at
Westminster having notified the Clerk of
the Parliaments of Northern Ireland that
the House of Lords, with whom all
Divorce Bills used to originate, have
decided that they can no longer accept
Petitions for Divorce Bills from Northern
Ireland.
That being so, a second and more difficult
problem has now arisen, namely, the assimi
lation of the machinery of our Parliaments,
so far as possible, to the custom and practice
which has held for centuries in these matters
at Westminster. Our difficulties will be recognised when it is remembered that at Westminster the decision as to these Bills was
taken on the Second Reading in the House
of Lords when Counsel argued the case at
the Bar of the House before the Law Lords,
that is to say, before those Members of the
Upper Chamber who sat there in virtue of
high judicial office. Other proceedings were
of a more or less formal character.
We have here in our Senate of Northern
Ireland no element comparable with the Law
Lords at Westminster, but that difficulty is
not perhaps quite so serious as it might at
first sight appear, because at Westminster no
Petition for a Divorce was ever granted
unless proceedings for a judicial separation,
a Divorce a mensa et thoro, had first been
undertaken and obtained from the Court of
Matrimonial Causes or other court having
jurisdiction in such matters. For that I will
refer to the suggested Order No. 175 on the
Paper, which is adapted from the Standing
Orders at Westminster dealing with these
Bills. It will, therefore, appear that matters
of law will already have been threshed out

between the parties and the decision for or
against the Bill will probably rest upon the
proof of facts alone.
I do not propose, and I know the House
will not expect me, to take these Orders
seriatim, but the system we are asking the
House to adopt is, shortly, as follows:-
A Divorce Bill will commence in the
House of Commons, if the Examiner has
certified that Standing Orders have been
complied with. The laying of a certificate
to that effect on the Table of the House
of Commons constitutes a First Reading.
Between the third and sixth sitting day
after the First Reading the Bill is read a
Second time in the House of Commons and
carried up to the Senate, its being laid on
the Table of that House constituting a
First Reading there.
Between the third and sixth sitting day
the Bill will be read a Second time in the
Senate, whereafter it will be referred to
a Joint Standing Committee of both Houses.
called the Joint Committee on Divorce
Bills. Hon. Members will find the constitution of that Committee laid down in
Standing Order 184. The proceedings
before that Committee will be found in
Standing Order 185.
The Joint Committee will then report
to both Houses their decision whether the
Bill should pass or be rejected, or be passed
with amendments, and, if so, with what
amendments.
If there are no amendments, the Bill goes
to the House of Commons for a Third
Reading, and then, if passed without
amendments, to the Senate for a Third
Reading.
If there are any amendments in Committee the Bill must lie on the Table of
the House of Commons for three days
before coming for a Third Reading; and.
if amended in Committee or in the House
of Commons, when it comes to the Senate
for a Third Reading, a similar period of
three clear days must elapse before this
stage is taken.
I hope I have made myself clear upon a
somewhat technical subject. I can assure
the House that the greatest care has been
taken with the drafting of these Orders, and
that, so far as possible, they are an adaptation of the Standing Orders on Bills of this
nature at Westminster. Orders which, as I
have already said, have proved satisfactory
over a very considerable period. I beg to
move.
Mr. SPEAKER: I understand that the
hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) wishes to speak on
the Orders as a whole. I think I ought to
mention that I propose to take each Order
separately as I do the Clauses of a Bill.
Standing Orders-
14 MAY 1925
Mr. LESLIE: I only intend to offer a few
general observations on the subject of these
Standing Orders. In the first place one can
only say that the Senate, not for the first
time, is deeply indebted to the noble
Marquess for the concise and yet very clear
manner in which he has expounded to us
a procedure which from our point of view
is somewhat novel. I think the point in the
speech of the noble Marquess that will
appeal most to all of us is the suggestion
that this proposal is really intended, or expected to be, of a temporary character,
because I am sure that we should all prefer
that these unfortunate matters should be
dealt with by our Courts of Justice.
There is this advantage, amongst others,
in that course. It is, after all, the unalienable right of every subject in the United
Kingdom who has the misfortune to be involved in legal proceedings to have the option
of appeal, if occasion arises, all the way to
the House of Lords. In accepting these
Orders, as we shall of course accept them,
there will be a final stage which can be subject to no appeal because the proceedings of
the Joint Standing Committee of Parliament
will not be subject to revision by any court
of any character.
I do not imagine that any serious difficulty
will arise, because although there might be
little points here and there throughout the
Orders it is not necessary to go into them,
for, as has been pointed out by the Noble
Marquess, these Orders are based on the
practice of the House of Lords and the usage
of the House of Lords would be sufficient to
settle any question that might arise in
practice. The Joint Standing Committee will
also have the assistance of the Examiner or
Standing Counsel.
I do not think there will be any difficulty
in the matter, but I do hope that at as
early a date as possible the necessary arrangements will be made between the two
parties because it will require legislation by
the Parliament at Westminster as well as
by our own Parliament, and also, I suppose,
the consent of the present judges in Northern Ireland-to enable a change to be made,
and the change I would like to see made is
that our own Courts of Law here should be
able to deal with petitions for divorce just
exactly as they do in England. To the proposed Standing Orders themselves I have no
objection.
Mr. GREER: I want to make
Mr. SPEAKER: If the hon. Member would
stand straight upright when he wishes to
speak it would be easier to catch my eye.
Mr. GREER: I am sorry, but I thought
another hon. Member wished to speak before
me. Later on I hope to speak to a particular
move.
LILLIX2
Amendment. S171
bservations/
Order on which I have an amendment to
I quite agree with the observations
of Senator Leslie that it would be very desirable to conform as far as possible with
the English practice in regard to this
matter. I was glad to hear from the Leader
of the House that these provisions are likely
to be only of a temporary character, and I
do hope that when the Government comes to
consider the question fully they will bring
forward a Bill to confer on His Majesty's
judges in Northern Ireland the right to
grant a divorce in the same way as the judges
do in England.
There is no reason why parties should have
to come here with a Bill to get a complete
divorce, and I do not think a Committee of
the kind suggested is a very good body for
considering the matter. No doubt the parties
can only come here after a decision had
been given by the Court, after legal
evidence, for a separation, but the Committee
that is to be set up by these Standing
Orders will have to go into the question
of whether the evidence as brought before
them is lawful evidence.
I am referring to Standing Order 185,
on which very difficult questions of law
will arise, and I do not think that a Joint
Committee of both Houses is the proper
tribunal to deal with that question. While
I approve of something being done now,
I am glad to understand that these proceedings will be only temporary and that
ultimately there may be a Bill brought in
to make the law in Northern Ireland on
this subject the same as the law in
England.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I agree with what
Senator Greer and Senator Leslie have
said with regard to these new Standing
Orders, although I think this is a somewhat cumbrous way of dealing with the
matter. When Ireland was undivided the
divorce laws in England did not apply
here, but the ease with which the business
is done in England is an indication that it
might be done here very much better than
under the method proposed. Of course, in
this country, for reasons that we cannot
explain, we have not as much business of
this kind as they have in England and
therefore the natter is not of such great
importance.
The two hon. Members who preceded me
suggested that this is a temporary expedient, but I did not gather from the
speech of the noble Marquess that that
was so. He gave no indication that there
would be any change. Of course, a Joint
Committee is not a tribunal that can deal
with evidence as satisfactorily as a Judicial
tribunal, and I do not think it will be
able properly to consider evidence of
a
Standing Orders--

THE SENATI

[Mr. Armstrong]
complicated legal character without legal
assistance. However, at the moment I feel
that these additional Standing Orders are
a necessity, and I have no desire to oppose
them.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member who has just spoken
said I held out no hope that this was only
a temporary arrangement. I was able to
say in my opening remarks that the task
we are engaged in at the moment is to
provide for the immediate continuity of the
rights of the Subject, whatever changes we
may contemplate for the method of their
exercise at some future date. Beyond that
I do not feel I am at liberty to go, but I
think that answers the hon. Member's remarks.
STANDING ORDERS 174, 175, and 176
agreed to.
STANDING ORDER 177.
Every such Petition and Bill shall
thereupon be referred to the Examiner of
Petitions for Local Bills, who shall
certify to both Houses whether or not
the Standing Orders have been complied
with in respect of them.
If the Examiner is satisfied that it is
impossible for the petitioner to deliver
a copy of the Petition and Bill to the
Respondent, he may accept proof of a
substituted service.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Standing
Order 177 to leave out from the word
"Respondent" (Bill to the Respondent) to
the end of the Order, and to insert instead thereof the words:-
"within the prescribed time, he may
direct the delivery thereof to be substituted in such manner as he may deem
right, and may extend the prescribed
time for such delivery."
a
We are now preparing the way for
very important class of legislation involving
very serious results. I think it is important we should see that if possible
both parties are represented, and I do not
think this Order is satisfactory from that
point of view. In my opinion we must
frame some Standing Order that will give
the Respondent in these proceedings
reasonable notice that a Bill for a divorce
is being brought forward in this Parliament. It is quite possible that the Respondent may be living in India, or Africa,
or Canada, or outside the British Empire,
and I think we must frame a Standing
Order that will entitle him to receive due
notice of these proceedings. The way I propose to do it is by inserting this Amendment in Standing Order 177.
It is obvious that if the Respondent
happens to be living outside jurisdiction
it will be impossible to serve him within
the prescribed period of seven days. That
point has occurred to the framer of these
Standing Orders, because he says that if
the Examiner is satisfied that it is impossible for the Petitioner to deliver a copy
of the Petition to the Respondent he may
accept proof of substitute service. That
throws the onus on the person bringing
forward the petition to decide what will
be a proper kind of substitute service to
effect. The Respondent might not have anyone acting for him in this country and the
person bringing the Petition might decide
to serve the agent acting for the Respondent
abroad, and send a copy of the Petition to
him. On the other hand, the Examiner
might think some other method of service
would be better, and in any case all that
could not be done in seven days.
The Examiner would then have to report
that the Standing Orders were not complied
with, and the matter would have to come
Defore the Standing Committee for consideration and decision. They could not do otherwise than report that the Standing Orders were
not complied with, and then they would have
to decide whether they should go on, although
the Standing Orders had not been complied
with, or whether the Bill should be thrown
out. I think it would be very improper
that the Standing Committee should be able
to decide to go on with a Bill in the absence
of the Respondent, and with the knowledge
that he had not received notice. Therefore
I beg to move the Amendment which stands
in my name.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDEKRY:
The words which the hon. Member moves to
add to Standing Order 177 appear to me to
strengthen the Standing Order and to
carry out the meaning
that Order.
Therefore I am willing to accept his
Amendment.
of
Mr. GREER: I am much obliged to the
Noble Marquess.
to.
Amendment agreed to.
Standing Order 177, as amended, agreed

Questions to Minister.
TEACHERS' TRAINING COLLEGES.
The following Questions stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Joseph Cunningham:-
To ask the Leader of the House if he can
state how much it will cost per student to
have Roman Catholic teacher students trained
at the Hammersmith College, and what proportion of the cost will be paid by the Government of Northern Ireland and by the individual student; and if he will give the corresponding figures for students trained in the
training college of the Northern Government
in Belfast.
To ask the Leader of the House whether
it is intended to make any grant for the
structural alterations of Hammersmith College in order to fit it for the reception of
students from Northern Ireland and, if so,
whether he will state the amount of the grant,
and whether it is to be made from the
Northern Ireland Exchequer or by deduction
pi from the Imperial contribution.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I should
be very pleased if the Leader of the House
✓ would oblige me with the information asked
for in the two questions down on the Order
Paper in my name. If we take the two
questions together it will probably be more
convenient for the Noble Marquess to answer
them.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY.
I think it will be simpler if I reply to the
hon. Senator's questions point by point as
set out on the Paper and more convenient
than if I take them together.
The fees at St. Mary's Training College,
Hammersmith, are £100 18s 4d per student,
inclusive of charges of £3 for books and
£1 5s Od for a highly efficient organization of
games. Of this amount the Ministry of
Education will pay £76 13s 4d and the
student the balance of £24 5s.
Teachers' Training Colleges. 126
At Stranmillis Training College the fees
amount to £97 105 0d, exclusive of charges for
books, of which the Ministry of Education
pays £75 and the student the balance of
£22 10s Od.
I would remind the hon. Gentleman that
over and above this charge of £75 per student
which falls upon the Government in respect
of each student at Stranmillis there fall
charges for capital expenditure which must
be taken into account if a just comparison is
to be made between the cost per student to
the Government at Stranmillis and at Hammersmith.
At Stranmillis, in addition to
these charges which fall upon the Government, there are charges for books which the
student has to meet, while the charges for
games which the Government pays are by no
means comparable with those which the
authorities of the St. Mary's Training College at Hammersmith, assisted by the fee
charged to each pupil for this purpose, have
to meet.
No other expenditure-and here I am replying to the hon. Gentleman's second question
on the Paper-no other expenditure beyond
that for students' fees is payable by the
Ministry through the English Board of Education to the College at Hammersmith, and,
therefore, no grant whatsoever is to be made
by the Ministry, either through the Board
of Education or directly, for any structural
alterations rendered necessary by the reception of students from Northern Ireland at
that Training College. I think that answers
the questions put to me.
Mr. JOSEPH
you, my Lord.
CUNNINGHAM: Thank
Mr. LONG: Arising out of that answer,
might I ask if the teachers trained at the
Hammersmith College will be accepted by the
Roman Catholic Church in Northern Ireland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
This arrangement was come to after negotiation direct with the Roman Catholic Church,
and I am glad to be able to say that we have
effected this compromise, and those teachers
will be accepted by the Roman Catholic
Church in Northern Ireland.

County Officers and Courts Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments
read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this Report be
now received.
The following amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Glendinning:-
Page 6, line 20, leave out from "been" to
"and" in line 24, and insert "Is employed
since the 22nd November, 1921."
Mr. GLENDINNING: I do not propose to
move the amendment standing in my name
Mr. LONG: With regard to Clause 5, 1
should like an explanation as to why the
pension of a clerk of the Crown and Peace
is fixed at one forty-fifth of his salary, multiplied by the number of years service, instead of one sixtieth? My experience of officers of local authorities is that in all cases
their pension is based on one-sixtieth of their
salary, multiplied by the number of years
service, and I would like some explanation
as to why that is departed from in this case.
Personally I should have thought that such
a departure would have been more desirable
in the case of persons with smaller salaries.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I thank Senator Glendinning for not
moving his amendment. With regard to the
point raised by Senator Long, the reason the
pension is based on one forty-fifth of the
salary is that those who under this Bill are
appointed Clerks of the Crown and Peace
must be solicitors of good standing in their
profession. Such a solicitor would necessarily be of an age much in advance of the
age at which a person ordinarily enters the
Civil Service, and it was therefore considered
that unless the pension was based on a higher
scale than that usually adopted we might not
get the type of Civil Servant we desire. For
that reason the pension scale was increased
in this case.
Mr. LONG: I beg to thank the Noble
Viscount for his explanation, the reasonableness of which I must admit.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Amendments were accordingly Reported and agreed to by the House.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time on Wednesday next.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
It is proposed that we should adjourn until
Wednesday next, when the House will be
engaged on purely formal business.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Four
o'clock until Wednesday next.

ELECTION OF SENATORS.
Mr. SPEAKER informed the House that he
had received Certificates from the Clerk of
the Crown, that the Viscount Charlemont, of
Drumcairn, Stewartstown, County Tyrone,
and Sir George Smith Clark, of Dunlambert,
Fortwilliam Park, Belfast, were, on the 14th
day of May, 1925, duly elected Senators to
serve in the Parliament of Northern Ireland,
in the room respectively of the Viscount
Pirrie and Mr. Hugh de Fellenberg
Montgomery, deceased.
NEW MEMBER SWORN.
James Edward Caulfield, Viscount Charlemont, Sworn, and signed the Roll.

Affairs Provisional Order Confirmation
(Londonderry County Borough) Bill a Second
time;
They have passed the Pharmacy and
Poisons Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of the Senate.

- MINISTRY OF HOME AFFAIRS PROVI-
SIONAL ORDER CONFIRMATION

BILL.

a
Moved: That this Bill be now read
