Housing Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The general object of this Bill is to develop
and accelerate housing operations in Northern
Ireland; in the first place by extending
generally to rural districts the benefits of
the Act of 1923, under which the Ministry of
Home Affairs is empowered to make grants
to private persons building small houses in
approved industrial centres in rural areas,
and, secondly, by increasing the rate of
Government grant from £60 to £80 in cases
in which the houses are completed within six
months from the date of the commencement
of building.
Although the Housing Act of 1923 has been
in operation for little over a year, very considerable progress has been effected as a
result not only of the activities of private
builders but also of local authorities. The
progress of housing operations in Northern
Ireland during the last two years has been
very fully explained from time to time in
Parliament, and in particular reference
might be made to the very full statement
which was made by my right hon. Friend
the Minister of Finance, in his Budget statement, and to the further statement made by
the Minister of Home Affairs during the
Second Reading Debate on the Rent Restriction Bill, in which it was pointed out that
in the case of the operations of local authori
ties 685 houses have been approved by the
Ministry of Home Affairs, of which upwards
of 100 have been completed. Further schemes
on the part of local authorities now under
consideration comprise an additional 1.156
houses.
Turning to private builders, certificates
have been issued by the Ministry in respect
of 2,106 houses, of which 890 have been
actually completed, and the information
before the Ministry of Home Affairs goes to
show that the rate of building is, if anything,
on the increase. However, while the results
up to date give no grounds for actual dissatisfaction, and while a considerable step
has been taken towards the alleviation of
the shortage in housing, it is the opinion of
the Government that it is essential to take
every possible step within the limits of its
financial resources not only to alleviate
but actually to meet the housing difficulty
in Northern Ireland. With this object in
view, as has already been pointed out, it is
proposed to increase from £60 to £80 the
Government subsidy in the cases of all houses
commenced after the passing of this Bill,
and completed within six months after the
date of commencement.
A very careful examination has been made
of the duration of building operations in
Northern Ireland during the last year, and
it has been found that out of a total of 300
odd houses 19 per cent. were built in four
months or under, 40 per cent. were built in
five months or under, and 60 per cent. were
built in six months or under, whilst 75 per
cent. were built in seven months and under.
Up to the present there has been no incentive to the private builder to accelerate
his operations, as he realised that if he completed his house before 1st April, 1926, he
would be eligible for the Government subsidy. The fact that so many as even 60 per
cent. of the houses were built within six
months appears to be an indication that if
an incentive in the form of bonus such as is
indicated in the Bill were afforded for acclerated building, it is not unreasonable to
assume that instead of 60 per cent. being
built in six months we should find 80 per cent.
or 90 per cent. built in that same period.
Second Reading.
Housing Bill-

3 JUNE


TE
ba
Bi
A
Bal
to
ゴ

It may be argued that by putting a premium
on accelerated building there would be a
tendency towards hurried and inefficient
work, but it should be pointed out that
before a grant is paid on any house that
house is carefully inspected by the Ministry's
Engineering Inspector, and no grant is paid
unless he is fully satisfied that it has been
completed in a proper manner as prescribed
by the Act of 1923, and that it otherwise
fully complies with the
Ministry's
regulations.
The reason for the extension of the benefits
of the Act of 1923 to rural districts is not
far to seek. Under the Labourers' Act very
generous financial provision is made in the
case of Rural District Councils erecting
labourers' cottages, and considerable
advantage is being taken at the moment of
these terms. Prior to the Government of
Ireland Act, 1920, a total of over 6,000 houses
was provided by Rural District Councils for
labourers in Northern Ireland, and since
that time upwards of 1,400 cottages have
either been built, or are being built at the
present moment, or it is proposed to build
them.
The Housing Act of 1923 provided a further
inducement to employers of labour or other
persons to build houses for artisans and
others in industrial centres in rural
districts. Since the passing of that Act representations have been made to the Government that there is an urgent demand for
houses in the smaller villages which would
not ordinarily come within the scope of the
activities of Rural District Councils, and
that also if similar terms to those in
operation in urban districts were applied to
rural districts, many farmers would take
advantage of them to build cottages for
their own labourers.
Accordingly,
case
as
a
will be observed, the
present Bill applies the Government housing
subsidy of £60 unreservedly to the
of houses erected
by private
builders in rural districts, and it will be
further noticed that having regard to the
fact that the average floor space of
labourer's cottage is 500 square feet, special
provision is being made in the Bill empowering the Ministry of Home Affairs to apply
the subsidy in special cases to houses built
in rural districts of 500 square feet and upwards. It should also be added that the £20
bonus for accelerating building applies to
houses built in the rural districts.
The third feature of the Bill, a new one in
the history of Local Government administration in Northern Ireland, deals with the
powers vested in the local rating authorities
to exempt from rates for a period not exceeding ten years all houses which come
within the scope of this Bill. The powers to
be given to the local authorities are optional
and additional to and not derogatory of any
of their existing powers, either to make
lump sum grants or to issue loans free of
interest.
or
These new powers are designed primarily
to give relief to the occupiers of the new
houses, whether they be purchasers
whether they be tenants. It has been urged
that the Government subsidy and the existing grants paid by local authorities tend
in the main to benefit the builder only.
It has been the considered view of the Government that while to a certain extent this may
perhaps be true, yet in the long run the
question of continued building operations
becomes an economic problem, and the
builders who are anxious to dispose of the'r
houses quickly find it to their advantage to
make due allowances to purchasers in respect
of the subsidies they have received from the
Government and the local authorities. However, in order to meet any possible grounds
of objection on the score of the policy embodied in the principal Act, it is the opinion
of the Ministry that power should be given
to the local authorities to conter this benefit
of the remission of rates on the ocupier.
The Bill, it will be observed, is a short one,
and comprises only six clauses. I beg to
move.
Mr. LONG: With some of the provisions
of this Bill I am not very much enamoured.
However, if the end justifies the means we
should be satisfied, I suppose. As the end we
all have in view is the provision of more
houses, I think the Government are certainly
justified in the steps they have suggested in
this Bill. With the hon. Senator from
Armagh (Mr. Armstrong), who spoke on the
Rent Restrictions Bill, I am sorry I cannot
agree that there is not a shortage of houses
in rural districts.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: The shortage is not so
acute.
Mr. LONG: I quite agree it is not so acute.
However, in the County of Londonderry I can
vouch for the fact that there is a very considerable shortage of houses, and in my view
the more effective way of providing houses
for agricultural labourers would be by increasing the grant that rural district
councils at present receive. I may be
wrong, and I trust I am, but in my view the
hope of the Noble Leader of the House that
the farmers will provide houses for their
labourers under this Bill is not likely to
materialise. It has been found in the past
that building houses for agricultural
labourers is not a financial success, and the
prospect is not a very pleasant one for a man
with capital. After the land has been pro-
Housing Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
vided, and the cost of the house is taken into
account, the very small rent that the labourers can pay is not sufficient to recoup the
person who has speculated.
The rural district councils are in a difficult
position. They are keenly anxious to provide
more houses, but they feel that the ratepayers
are not able to bear any further burden, and
they find from experience that they are not
able to build houses at a price which will
enable them to be let at an economic rent.
A burden is therefore thrown on the ratepayers of the county, which we fee they are
not able to bear. At first sight, as a raember
of a local rating authority, I viewed with
great disfavour this section regarding the
exemption of houses from rates. On closer
examination, however, and on reflection, I
have no objection whatever to offer to the proposal. I take the long view of the matter,
and in my view we will have very much more
property to levy rates on at the end of ten
years than if this Bill had not been projected.
For these reasons, therefore, I welcome on
behalf of the local authorities this Measure.
I very seriously suggest to the Government,
however, that if they really want houses for
the agricultural labourers, the real solution
of the matter is an increase in the grants to
local authorities. I beg to support the Second
Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: This Bill is certainly one
which should commend itself to us all,
because its object is one in which we take a
very great interest, as we have shown, I
think, on more than one occasion. It also
marks a continuous step in the policy which
we have all been supporting, that is to say.
the policy of increasing the housing accommodation for the working classes. We have
made tw oprevious attempts, as the Noble
Marquess has just reminded us, to deal with
this matter. We passed Bills both in 1923
and 1924, and now, if I understand the present
Bill correctly, we are taking another and a
further step which, by increasing the grants
to £80, and by removing the last distinction
between urban and rural areas, is likely to
give great satisfaction. There has been a
good deal of comment in the country districts
directed towards the apparent lack of favour
to the rural districts. That distinction, such
as it was, is now being removed.
own
I hope, I think I may say I expect, that the
farmers to whom the Noble Marquess referred, will take advantage to some extent
of this Bill, and will build houses for their
labourers, because after all I am
sure my hon. Friend, the Chairman of the
Londonderry County Council (Mr. Long) will
bear me out in saying that there has always
been great difficulty in bringing the
Labourers Acts into operation. I know that
very often the Site Committees were not as
reasonable as they might have been. They
sometimes wanted particular corners that the
farmer did not want them to take, and the
farmer had a grievance inasmuch as the land
was taken from him, whether he liked it or
not for a house, and even then not necessarily for his own men. Now he will have
the distinct opportunity that he has not had
before to build his own houses, and ultimately, if he does that, he will add to the
capital value of his farm, because every farm
with a proper proportion of working-class
houses upon it will be much more valuable
than a farm without them. Any development in the erection of houses in the rural
areas as opposed to the continued extension of
the urban areas is very much to be wished for,
and is likely, I think to benefit the whole
Province.
I have heard a few remarks passed as to
the somewhat sudden introduction of this
particular Bill. It has been observed that
nothing was said in the Gracious Speech
about a further Housing Bill this year. 1
should have thought myself that a visitor of
this nature would have been none the less
welcome because its arrival was somewhat
unexpected. I take it that the reason is the
Government have only just been able to make
sure from further examination of the second
Colwyn Report that the money is available.
I imagine that that is the reason why this
Bill has been brought in, not certainly without such notice as was possible, but perhaps
somewhat suddenly.
The Noble Marquess has referred, as did
my hon. Friend, to Clause 3. The principle
it embodies, as has been said, is quite new
to this Senate, but it is not quite new to
this Parliament, for a Clause of very much
the same nature was inserted in the Bill of
1923, when it made its first appearance in
another place. The proposal then made was
a limit of five years, which is, of course, the
same thing. Sir Crawford McCullagh, who
is a great authority on the housing question
in Belfast, another hon. Member who then
represented, and, I am glad to say, still represents, both the City and County of Londonderry, criticised the proposal, and I think
it did not find favour with another hon.
Member who sits for one of the divisions
of the City of Belfast. Certainly I remember
that I myself, when speaking in this House
on the Bill of 1923, as it came to us, said
I did not regret the disappearance of the
proposal from the Bill.
I am afraid I have to confess that I am
not at all sure that, when I expressed that
opinion on the proposal which is now before
the House, I fully appreciated the possible
effect of a paragraph in a section of the
English Housing Act of 1923. I see from my
note on the older Bill that I had referred to a
Clause in the older Housing of the Working
Classes Act of 1890, which gave powers to
Second Reading.

Housing Bill-
3 JUNE 1925

re
eve
the


F
gas and water companies to supply gas and
water without charge during a period as
having, perhaps, some bearing on the subject.
I do not feel really that a proposal of that
sort to supply gas and water, any more than
a proposal to give a free ticket along
the railway, is strictly relevant to this
matter.
We have, I think justification of a rather
better kind, and that is that this proposal
will have the effect of accelerating the building of houses. After all, that is what we
want. I am quite sure that the proposal will
find more support now than it would have
received two years ago. I have tried during
the time at my disposal to ascertain views
upon this point, and I have certainly observed that there is very little said against
the proposal now. I am not at all certain
that five years will not be sufficient period
instead of ten, and I propose to suggest
that to to the Government for consideration later on. I think we ought to consider
that question when we are taking the Bill in
Committee. There are two other matters I
wish to mention, both curiously enough
connected with the same Clause.
One of
them is whether we ought not to provide a
limitation of time for exemption in relation
to the commencement of the building,
whether we ought not to say definitely it is
our intention that exemption shall not be
given after the houses are built. I think that,
perhaps, is also a matter which we ought to
consider. I think, too, the Government
should be so good as to consider whether
the intention is to exempt from what for
this purpose I will call the Consolidated
Rate. As we know, in the rural districts the
Poor Rate provides not only for the relief
of the poor, but for other matters, especially
charges for sewerage and water schemes in
particular districts, and for lighting and for
compensation for criminal injuries.
a
I think it is very much a matter for consideration whether exemption from rates
should cover separate charges and particularly
possible charge for malicious
injuries. But these matters are really of
detail, and I should not have mentioned
them to-day had it not been for the fact that
I understand the Committee stage is to he
taken to-morrow, and therefore the time
available is not very long. Taking the Bill
as a whole, I certainly desire to thank the
Government in general and the Minister of
Home Affairs in particular for having
brought the matter before us again. I am
quite certain that the Bill will assist very
materially in the object we have in view.
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I inquire if this
Bill and the Bill related thereto contemplate
the building of steel houses or affording
facilities for any class of houses of that
character? Three ol four types of steel
houses have been approved by the Ministry
on the other side, and I did not gather
whether it is in contemplation to permit
that class of building here. This may not
be either the time or the place to go into
the question whether houses of that sort are
quite suitable for this distinctly humid
climate in which we have to live.
I just
wondered whether
the facilities here
mentioned would apply to that type of
houses if it was decided that was a type to
encourage on account of rapidity of
construction.
a
Mr. GREER: I hope and believe that this
Bill will encourage building and stimulate
private enterprise, notwithstanding what
my hon. Friend on the left (Mr. Long) has
said of farmers, because they will not have
to pay for the ground. They can easily give
a site of their own ground, and they will in
that way have
residence for their
labourers at hand, near the place where they
would have to work. I think they would be
very glad to avail themselves of this site. I
also am very anxious to have a reply to the
question which has just been asked by the
hon. Senator (Colonel Sinclair), because 1
believe that if these houses can be constructed of steel or some other material
which would be much cheaper than brick or
stone, there would be a great many of these
houses built because they would really not
cost very much.
There is just one point I want to refer to
in Section 2, where the period within which
houses must be completed is extended to six
months. I think that some provision should
be inserted that if a strike should occur
among the workers the period of the strike
should not be included, because after all six
months is a short time.
Then I see no difficulty in regard to Clause
3, which enables local authorities to exempt
houses from the payment of rates, and I
think my hon. Friend has taken the correct
view of the situation that the local authorities will be willing to do so, because they will
take the long view of the matter, and find
that they will ultimately benefit by bringing a number of people into the neighbourhood and increasing the rate capacity.

have very much pleasure in supporting the
Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Senator Leslie I think must have
omitted to notice that in Clause 3 the
exemption from rates may be up to ten years.
It is permissible but not obligatory in any
Under these circumstances, I do not
know whether he will desire to alter the
sense.
Housing Bill
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
limit which it is possible to prescribe under
the Bill.
no
With reference to steel houses, such houses
should come within the scope of the Bill, but
I understand that up to now there has been
application for steel houses. From
another point of view they are hardly to be
recommended in Northern Ireland as compared with houses of other materials, I
believe, owing to the climatic conditions.
But they are not excluded from the Bill, and
any person proposing to put up houses of
this character would come within the scope
of the Measure.
The hon. Member (Mr. Greer) referred to
the six months which may be interfered with
by a strike. That is a point on which I will
make inquiry. It is a matter which has not
been brought before me as yet. It is
certainly one for consideration, although I
hope that like myself he does not anticipate
that in this great effort by all sections of the
community our work will be interfered with
and hampered in this way. I hope the hon.
Member like myself will take an optimistic
view of the future, and feel that by the
extra inducement building operations throughout Northern Ireland will be accelerated in a
manner which will enable us, as I said, not
only to alleviate the difficulties surrounding
the shortage of houses at the present moment,
but meet the whole question and cope with
these difficulties.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.-
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at five
minutes before Five of the
clock.
177 Finance (Estate Duty) Bill-

4 JUNE 1925

THE SEΝΑΤΕ.

Thursday, 4th June, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
MINISTRY OF HOME AFFAIRS
PROVISIONAL ORDER CONFIRMATION
(LONDONDERRY COUNTY BOROUGH)
BILL.
The following report was made from the
Joint Committee on the said Bill: That they
had considered the said Bill and examined
the allegations thereof which were found to
be true, and the Committee had gone through
the Bill, and directed the same to be reported
without Amendment to the House.
Finance (Estate Duty) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move the
Second Reading of this Bill. Hon. Members
will have noticed from the speech of the
Imperial Chancellor of the Exchequer, in
introducing the Budget, that the increases in
the rates of Estate Duty were made in order
to balance the loss of revenue which would
result from the concessions which were made
to the super-tax payers. Under the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, super-tax is a
Reserved Service, while Death Duties have
been transferred to the Government of
Northern Ireland. It, therefore, follows that
any legislation of the Imperial Government
in regard to super-tax will extend to
Northern Ireland, and on this account it is
expected that the reserved revenue will suffer
in a full year to the extent of approximately
£85,000 owing to the reductions in the
super-tax.
The House will, therefore, doubtless appreciate the necessity of following Great Britain
in increasing the rates of Estate Duty in
order to balance the loss which we will
sustain by the remission which has been given
in connection with super-tax. The Bill now
before the House is for the purpose of bringing the new Estate Duty rates into force on
the day on which they come into force in
Great Britain. I may, perhaps, anticipate
inquiries on this particular point if I mention
to the House that the relationship between
our own Estate Duty Office and Somerset
House is close, and it greatly facilitates
administration if any legislation passed by
this Parliament affecting Estate Duty to the
extent as legislation passed by the
Imperial Parliament affects Estate Duty in
Great Britain comes into force on the
same day as the British Act.
same
We cannot exactly foresee on what day
the British Finance Act will have the force
of law, and so it is proposed that this Bill
should be made operative by Order in
Council. I hardly think there is any necessity for me to-day to elaborate more the
points in this Bill, so I beg to move that the
Bill be now read a Second time.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to say that on
this occasion the speech of the Noble
Marquess has left me entirely unconvinced.
In fact, it seems to me that the necessity is
imposed upon me of moving the two formal
Amendments which are necessary in order
to transmute the proposal of the Noble
Marquess into a resolution that the Bill
be read a Second time this day six months.
It may, perhaps, at first sight appear somewhat paradoxical, but I submit that the effect
of the Amendments which I propose to move
will not necessarily involve the final rejection
of the Bill, because our Constitution provides,
as will be remembered, not only that a Bill
of this class, a Bill imposing taxation, can
neither be introduced nor amended in this
Chamber, but also that if such a Bill is rejected
here it is open to the promoters of the Bill,
with the acquiescence, of course, of His Grace
the Governor, to bring that Bill during
the current Session before a Joint Sitting of
both Houses of Parliament, sitting and
deliberating as one body.
a
The fact that we have this power of forcing a Joint Sitting before the enactment or
the rejection of
Finance Measure is
finally decided upon is one of the points in
which we differ from the House of Lords in
Great Britain. The Second Chamber in
Great Britain is at present, so far as financial
matters are concerned, and has perhaps
been left far too long, in a position of
absolute impotence. We are not in the same
category at all. Our powers in these
matters are the same, and I think they
were conferred upon us in exactly the same
words as the powers recently granted to the
Union of South Africa. If we have powers
in this matter we have also responsibilities,
and if we have the responsibility of limited
veto, that veto, being, in the exact words
which Lecky used, “Limited and defined by
law," it follows that we should exercise that
power fearlessly, and if we do so we may
expect that our decision will be favourably
received in general.
We have not in this House any organised
or regular Opposition, and I think that perhaps we have done very well in the past without that Opposition, but the fact that there
is no regular Opposition to the proposals of
the Government, while it gives the private
Member greater freedom, seems to me to
impose on the Private Member who has
generally supported the Government greater
responsibility. If we have the responsibility
it follows that we must endeavour to dis-
179 Finance (Estate Duty) Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Leslie]
charge that responsibility both faithfully and
honestly, and I submit that we cannot do
that if we allow important Bills to pass
through this Chamber without very full discussion. So far as the Bill now before us is
concerned, if I may venture to form an
opinion on the published reports of the proceedings in another place, I am bound to say
that the Bill does not appear to have
received any discusion or consideration
whatever. The only speech made on the Bill
was that of the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Finance, in introducing it, and
I think it is for us to fill the void.
The noble Marquess has already reminded
us that this question of Estate Duty is one
solely for this Parliament, and he has told
us that the genesis of the proposal is to be
found in what I am afraid I must describethough the noble Marquess did not do so-as
a feat of financial juggling essayed by the
Chancellor of the Imperial Exchequer in an
attempt to balance an anticipated loss from
a reduction in super-tax against an anticipated increase to be gained by imposing an
additional duty on estates which pass at
death, and estates, I need scarcely remind
the House, for this purpose do not
necessarily refer solely to landed property.
'They need not include any landed property
at all. An estate for death duty purposes is
the aggregation of all the assets of the
deceased, whether those assets are real or
personal.
The introduction of this Bill raises the
whole question of Estate Duties, and as to
those there are of course two parties. There
is the party that is in favour of Estate Duty
as one of the means of obtaining revenue
for the country. That party refers to
Estate Duties as deferred Income Tax, and
it speaks of ransom and the duty of making
posterity pay part of the cost of the war.
'The other party, which is not so much in
favour of Estate Duty, speaks of its effect on
unemployment and the discouragement of
thrift it is likely to bring in its train, and
of the inducement it holds out to those
fairly wealthy to distribute their property
in their lifetime or sink their capital in the
purchase of an annuity. That may very well
be the best thing a man can do for himself.
but it is not popular with his natural heirs
or with the Minister of Finance or the
Chancellor of the Exchequer.
I do not want to go too far into the whole
question of Estate Duties this afternoon. J
should prefer to confine myself to the
severities and inequities which seem
accepted as inseparable from their operation, and as to these I must invite attention.
not so much to the Bill before us, because
that only gives the new rates it is proposed
to impose, and it does not tell the House
what the old rates were. There is nothing
a
in the Bill which indicates the amount of
the increases. Those who desire to find out
how the present Bill affects the existing law
as regards Estate Duties must turn to a
White Paper, a Financial Statement for
1925-26, which was presented to Parliament
on behalf of the Minister of Finance not
very long ago. Turning to page 14 of this
White Paper, we see that the proposed increase begins at £12,500. An aggregation of
assets which amounts in value to £12,500 is
to have an additional duty of 1 per cent.
imposed upon it. An estate of a capital
value of £1,000,000 is to have an additional
duty of 1 per cent. imposed upon it. The
Bill only applies to estates of
capital value of between £12,500 and
£1,000,000, but the great severities
are to be found in the intermediate estates.
I will take the first point, where the amount
is £12,500. At the present moment an estate
of the capital value of £12,500 is liable to
an estate duty of five per cent., representing
£625. When an estate has been broken up,
before the legatees or friends get it they
have, of course, to pay legacy duty in
addition, the legacy duty varying in accordance with the degree of relationship. I am
disregarding the legacy duties entirely,
because they are not touched by the Bill,
but at present this estate of a capital value
of £12,500 carries an estate duty of £625.
The additional impost imposed upon that
estate will be £125, making the duty on the
small property £750 in all. Now this estate
of £12,500, if I may assume the rate of
interest to be four per cent. for the whole
thing, that is, neglecting household furniture,
and so on, which does not produce actual
income, produces for its owner an income of
£500 a year. An income of £500 per annum,
as I need scarcely remind this House, does
not pay super-tax.
Let me go quickly up the scale to an estate
of £40,000 capital value and treat it in the
same manner. At present the estate duty
on an estate of £40,000 appears to be ten per
cent. £4,000 in all. It is proposed to add
to that an additional three per cent. But
again an estate of £40,000 at four per cent.
only produces £1,600 per annum for its
owner, and an income of £1,600 does not pay
super-tax. The proposals in this Bill are to
go downwards from £50,000 capital value
where the super-tax begins to £12,500. This
extra death duty is being imposed-indeed
the Chancellor of the Imperial Exchequer
has said so in order that the super-tax
payers may benefit. It is plain, I think, from
an examination of these figures that if we may
use a phrase which has become traditional,
the hen roosts which it is desired to raid
are not the property of the people who are
going to get relief. Or to change the
metaphor and put it another way, the
Chancellor of the Exchequer is desirous of
181 Finance (Estate Duty) Bill-
Second Reading.
4 JUNE 1925

-

el


a
emptying
coffer which he has done
nothing to fill, and that coffer is the
children's money box, because it is not
the dead man who pays the estate
duty; it is his heirs. For myself I can only
describe such a proposal as extremely unfair, and if I had at my command any
reliable information as to the number of
estates between £12,500 and £50,000 as compared with estates of between £50,000 and
£1,000,000, I feel pretty confident I should
qualify my adjective with a vigorous adverb.
a
I do not think it is necessary to take
up time by comparing the comparative
benefits of the relief in super-tax with
the increment of death duty, but I
think if you remember that super-tax
is rarely paid by people in their
first youth at all events, it will
be seen that the benefit-the gift, if
there is a gift-made to the super-tax
payers, is of
very Grecian character
indeed. I know that super-tax is, as the
noble Marquess has told us, a reserved service, but if I understand the matter
correctly, the reduction in super-tax, over
which we have no control, is automatically
reflected in the calculation of our Imperial
contribution. I think that is so indubitably.
So far as the effect of Estate Duty goes,
although it may very well be, and in most
cases undoubtedly is, that no landed property
is comprised in the estate, still if there is
landed property, passing on death, the increased estate duty falls with special severity
on such property. I know it is true that
there are not now in Northern Ireland many
landed properties of any great extent, and
in the future there will probably be none at
all, because the last Land Purchase Bill has
become an Act, and no land for practical
purposes will remain for renting purposes,
but there are still a small number of landed
gentry resident in Northern Ireland who
are occupying houses of some size, and
which perhaps are surrounded by a certain
number of acres, demesne and home farm.
I have not myself observed since I came to
reside permanently in Northern Ireland
twenty years ago, that the people in the
localities where these houses are desire that
the families occupying them should be
driven out or compelled to go elsewhere by
the pressure of taxation. I should have said
that the general feeling in Northern Ireland
is a desire that all such people should remain, and I must confess that it is with surprise I see in this Bill an indication that our
own Government takes a different view. Had
this proposal come before us from
Socialistic Government we might have understood it, but it is certainly not the Bill
which we should have expected from the
Government we have to-day.
a
The maintenance of such places as I have
mentioned has for some years been costly and
unprofitable. The man with a £50,000 property at the present bearing 11 per cent., will
have to pay a lump payment of £2,000 in
addition to the 11 per cent. which the estate
already bears. A very large percentage of the
particular class of persons of whom I have
been talking will find it impossible to carry
on if that additional tax is laid upon them.
Passing on now to one of the phases of the
inequitable operation of a Measure of this
character, I desire to point out that the
rates of Estate Duty fall with equal severity
on estates whatever the amount. Whether
an estate passes on to one person or whether
it is distributed amongst, let us say, a man's
widow and half a dozen children, the effect
is the same. The capital value of that
estate is depreciated by the same amount no
matter how many claimants there may be
upon it. In any case it is it must be the
widow or orphans who suffer. The man is
dead and is not affected.
There is also the disastrous unsettlement
that will arise by the perpetual re-opening of
this thing. A man must be left alone to make
his financial arrangements. He really cannot
be expected to be perpetually revising his
schemes and
plans just because the
Chancellor of the Exchequer thinks a profit
may be gained.
The question of insurance has always been
mentioned in this connection. It was said,
if I remember rightly, in 1894, when Sir
William Vernon Harcourt introduced the
duty, that a man of 35 years could insure
his life for X sum and for Y premium. It was
said, and the argument was never answered,
that there were some people whose age exceeded 35. In any case a man of 35 years of
age in 1894 is considerably more than that
now. Those who have experienced the taxation and the prices of the last few
years are not, speaking generally, in
a position to afford any additional premium. There is no doubt, I think, speaking
from the financial standpoint, that the
middle classes will feel these duties most.
There is not only the small country gentleman of whom I have spoken, the man with
a £1,500 to £3,000 income, but there is your
professional man, your small trader, who is
meeting with some success, and even your
farmer-any one of those may be expected
to leave £12,000 or £13,000 behind him.
If he does he is to be additionally penalised.
All these classes belong to the residential
class, whose continued presence in Northern
Ireland I should have expected the Government to show a desire to encourage.
Now, what is it we expect to get by introducing this further duty. We have to turn
again to the financial statement, and we see
that the increased revenue in Northern
Ireland is in a full year the sum of £55,000.
This year we will only get £25,000, but the
183 Finance (Estate Duty) Bill-
Second Reading. 184
THE SENATE

[Mr. Leslie]
ultimate additional increase to be be expected
is £55,000. I do not know what degree of reliability the Minister of Finance attaches to that
estimate. I think, if I may say so, it cannot
be very high. The Minister, with his accustomed frankness-he is always perfectly frank
-said that any calculation of the product of
the estate duties must necessarily be entirely
spéculative. It is obvious that must be so,
because it depends not only on the mortality
but on the special rate of mortality amongst
the class that pay estate duty.
a
We have not got, of course, the whole
Budget of the right hon. Gentleman before
us, and we are not concerned, I think, for
the moment with the effect on the general
financial proposals of reduction in revenue,
or rather in the anticipated revenue which
will result if my proposal is carried. We
are primarily concerned this afternoon about
matters which I must ask, and which I must
press for an answer to, and that is what
justification there is for imposing this
additional tax in Northern Ireland in
time of peace especially. We must remember
that the death duties in the standard year
-and the standard year for the purposes of
the Colwyn Committee is 1923-24-are calculated at £434,000. We find from the financial
statement, to which I have SO often
alluded, that the amount put down
for the financial year 1925-26 is estimated at
£495,000. Now that £495,000 includes, of
course, £25,000 additional which is expected
to result from the increased duty. Taking
that away, we have £470,000, which exceeds by
£36,000 the amount set down for death duties
in the standard year. As I say, I think we
are entitled to ask very definitely what is
the pressing necessity for this extra tax. I
cannot find that any satisfactory explanation
has yet been given. I have here the report of
the statement made by the Minister of
Finance in another place when he spoke as
follows in relation to these death duties.
He said-I am quoting from the official
"Hansard" of 7th May last :-
As the House knows, the control of Death
Duties is reserved to our own Parliament,
but in this case I propose to follow the
British precedent for this very important
reason, that if we decided to strike out on
our own line, and thereby reduce our
revenue by £25,000, such reduction would
automatically be added to our Imperial contribution, and therefore become a charge on
the resources of Northern Ireland.
I do desire to ask what is the statutory
or other authority relied upon to support that
statement, if indeed it does correctly represent what the right hon. Gentleman said and
what he meant. The statement is that "such
reduction would automatically be added to
our Imperial contribution and therefore become a charge on the resources of Northern
Ireland." I confess that I should have
thought that the fact would have been the
exact contrary. We are aware that owing to
unexpected windfalls in the matter of death
duties there was an unexpectedly large return of something like £140,000 from death
duties which went towards a sinking fund,
but I cannot translate that, nor can I find
anything in the Colwyn Report which
amounts to any admission that services reserved to ourselves transferred services-I
cannot find that there is any liability on us
so to deal with that transferred item of
revenue as to bring it into conformity with
the practice in Great Britain. And in fact
if I had been able to find anything of that
sort I do not think I should be inclined to
bow down to it, because it seems to me to be
an abnegation of the rights and privileges of
this Parliament, and it seems to me to be our
duty to protect our own taxpayers.
As regards the general question of conformity with Great Britain, I cannot, I am
afraid, admit the argument of clerical convenience as taking us very far. We are
aware that we have departed from bringing
ourselves into correspondence with Great
Britain in various lines of legislation. We
have examples in the recent Criminal Law
Amendment Act in which we have not followed Great Britain. We have examples in
Local Government matters, such as, for
instance, the small matter of giving control
of war memorials, and the two Bills which
are about to engage our attention this afternoon-the Housing and Rent Restriction
Bills are not exactly the same, and we have
one instance as regards taxation. I do not
desire to cite this case of where we differ
from Great Britain in the matter of taxation
in any spirit of hostility, because the reason
for it has been more than once explained,
and I think we are all of us here fully in
accord with the Minister of Finance. But it
is a fact that a reduction of the Entertainment Tax was given in Great Britain which
for reasons perfectly sound was not given in
Northern Ireland. The point remains that
we have in fact taxation which differs from
Great Britain.
I hope it will not be thought that I have
acted improperly in initiating a debate which
perhaps would have been carried on more
appropriately elsewhere. But I may point
out that those who sat elsewhere and had
an opportunity of debating this matter did
not do so. I hope I shall be acquitted of
anything in the nature of presumption for
that reason. But I hope I shall be given an
answer to the specific question as to the
authority behind the Minister of Finance's
statement as regards automatic increase and
as to what justification there is for imposing
a tax on a particular class of individuals. 1

repeat, I do not seek to fasten on our own
Government any responsibility whatever for
the inequity of the proposals as between
super-tax payer and the State. On the contrary, I am sure they are just as much cognisant of that as I am myself. I do
think that the Government in this matter
has been led into temptation, and I do hope
121 that this House is about to deliver them from
evil. I beg to move that the proposal of the
Noble Marquess be amended by the omission
of the word
and by the addition at
the end of the words "this day six months."

now
دو
The MARQUESS OF LONDON DERRY :
We have had a most interesting speech from
the hon. Senator who has just sat down, and
he has made a most drastic proposal in suggesting that this Bill which I have had the
honour of introducing shall be read this
day six months, which amounts to
rejection of the Bill.
a
I am not sure
that he really intends this to take place,
but his intention as I understand it is
to ventilate a subject on which there is
bound to be a great diversity of opinion, and
to give this House an opportunity of considering the case on its merits. I find myself
in a certain amount of difficulty in replying
to him. I have the natural antipathy of the
taxpayer which is shared by all of us who sit
around here and which is only tempered by
the feeling that we are all bound to contribute to the necessities of the State, and in
that we are bound to feel a certain amount
of sympathy with the Chancellor of the Exchequer who is called upon to impose these
onerous burdens upon us for the purpose of
the financial obligations of the country. I
always think the Chancellor of the Exchequer
is in a very difficult position. He is pressed
to be economical and to reduce taxation, and
yet he is pressed again on the other side by
hon. Members who are responsible to their
constituents to engage in extravagances of all
descriptions for the purpose of satisfying
those who clamour for relief or otherwise in
these matters. And consequently he has to
follow a very difficult course, and has to
✔steer a line which is bound to be a very unpopular line. The hon. Member who has just
sat down began by giving us a historical discussion concerning the powers of this
Chamber, and I am sure that everyone in
this House is just as jealous as he is
of our powers which are superior, I think,
to those of the Second Chamber on the other
side of the water. But in the specific matter which he has brought before this House
to-day I have as I have said a certain
amount of difficulty in giving him
answer. The Chancellor of the Excheqüer in
introducing his Budget said that the increase
in the rates of estate duty were made in
order to balance the loss of revenue resulting from concessions in super-tax. This particular statement may satisfy some of those
an
is
parties in the discussion who will come within
the ambit of the concessions, but there are
others who are being called upon or whose
successors will be called upon to pay extra
duties and who in their lifetime will not have
an opportunity of obtaining relief in supertax. That certainly appears to be a hardship.
But if the Chancellor had left out that paragraph in his speech my hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie) would have had no cause of complaint.
The Chancellor of the Exchequer might
have said:
"It
my duty to
raise
money from whatever sources
I think best, and I believe that
these owners of property up to a certain
figure are the best source, and therefore I
will impose additional death duties on that
particular section of the community." And
in another part of his speech he might have
said: "I was anxious to give relief to a
certain number of super-tax payers," but he
went further for some reason, and alluded
to the balance, as the hon. Member is quite
right in pointing out. But it does not differ
so much from that point which I am now
pressing upon the hon. Member.
I go on now to the theory, which I think
is a sound one, that we should do all in our
power to remain on parallel lines and carry
out the same point of view as on the other.
side of the water. The hop. Member has.
quite rightly pointed out exceptions, but
after all we hope they are only exceptions,
and that in future we shall become more
closely allied and be able to carry out the
same principles in taxation and legislation
as are enacted over the water. There is one.
thing that in a certain degree we suffer at
the present moment. My hon. Friend
opposite will naturally see that I make that
point, that as regards old-age pensions we
are not on the same lines. But I can assure
the House that the whole intention of the
Government, which I believe is merely an
expression of the opinions of the whole Province, is that we ought to do everything in
our power to remain on exactly the same
footing-in fact on parallel lines-as across
the water. In my judgment, and I hope to
carry the House with me when I say this,
in matters of taxation it is vitally important
we should remain as close as we can to the
other side. It is not only the clerical part
of this matter, to which the hon. Member
has alluded: it is that the whole finances of
these two countries are so closely interlocked that any different system of taxation
would only create anomalies and difficulties
which would be very hard to overcome if
they once were brought into force That is
the reason why, while sympathising, as I do
deeply, with the hon. Member when he
speaks of a section of the community who are
indeed hard hit by the death duties-all
sections are hard hit-I would ask him to
realise the exigencies and needs of the
187 Finance (Estate Duty) Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
present moment in the direction of finance,
and to feel as far as the Chancellor of the
Exchequer is concerned-I
am holding
personally no brief for the Chancellor of
the Exchequer, but speaking merely as a
supporter of the Government-it is with no
fractious desire that he has brought this
legislation into operation, but merely because he has the burden on his shoulders of
finding the necessary sum to cover those
expenses with which the country is faced.
The hon. Member has spoken of the
statement made by the right hon.
Gentleman, the Minister of Finance,
which I have not before me at the
present moment, and I have no doubt
that he is fully aware of the intricacies-I
can use no other word of the system of
finance that operates between the two
countries, and which might have been made
a little plainer since the report of the
Colwyn Committee. I have not looked up
that point, so that I am sorry I am not
able at the moment to give the hon. Member
(Mr. Leslie) that full answer to his question
which he requires. He is aware of the
formula, or of the order, which laid down
the amount of contribution which is paid, and
he is also aware that the whole of the taxation
is collected by Great Britain and handed
back to us. Taxation in this country is
collected by Great Britain, and is handed
back to us for the purpose of defraying the
charges on those various services which come
under our direct control. With this is
interlocked and bound up the whole question
of the contribution of this country to Great
Britain, with the result that there are many
benefits, which may seem to accrue to us
by various branches of taxation, which go
to increase the contribution in certain
circumstances
However, if the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie)
will allow me, I shall be glad to give him
all the details of the point which the
Minister of Finance made in his speech. I
was not aware that he was going to allude to
that particular point or I should have taken
particular steps to have made myself fully
acquainted with the intricacies of the financial relations between Great Britain and
Northern Ireland. I cannot claim to be
anything more than ordinary-perhaps less
than ordinary and I have found that the
details of these financial relations would require more than the capacity of my brain
at the present moment without due assistance from documents and notes in front of
me to give the hon. Member the answer he
requires.
I regret that the hon. Member should have
adopted the drastic step of proposing that
this Bill should be read a Second time this
day six months. I sincerely hope he will
not pursue his course to the bitter end, but
will realise that after all a Budget is
enacted once every year, and if certain
taxation is found to be onerous we have the
power to review the situation next year. I
feel sure that if we find, and he finds, that
burdens are imposed on us which we are
unable to bear no doubt the same situation
will arise on the other side of the water,
and we shall have remissions given in
another year in the taxation which is called
upon to defray the immense charges which
are placed upon the State at the present
moment. While I am not pretending that
the answer I have given to the hon. Member
(Mr. Leslie) can bring great consolation to
him or to those for whom he has so eloquently
spoken, still in the difficulties with which
we are confronted at the present moment,
and with the desire which we have, theoretically and practically, to place ourselves and
to remain on the same footing with Great
Britain, I would appeal to him, if he can
see his way, to withdraw the Amendment
which he has moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I confess that I had hoped
that other hon. Members of this House would
have evinced some interest in the matter
under discussion, and would have given us
the benefit of their views on this subject. As
regards the points made by the Noble
Marquess, I hope he will acquit me of having
urged extravagance on the part of the
Government or of the Ministries generally.
As regards what has been said about the
inter-locking of our financial relations with
Great Britain, while I entirely concur with
regard to that, I must point out that if it
were intended that we were not to deal with
transferred revenue an arrangement should
have been made in 1920. As a matter of
fact the contrary was the case. Whether I
am to interpret what the Noble Marquess
has said as holding out any hope of a reduction of the death duties in the ensuing year
not I hope I may take some scintilla of comfort from what he says. I notice
he did not commit himself, and I have no
desire to make him do so. He has said that
a Budget occurs annually. I quite agree,
but, after all, a man can only die once, and
the relief which may be given in some few
years' time may not be of any use to that
man or that man's heirs.
or
This is a matter which really ought not
to stand over. I cannot say and indeed I
know I am not expected to say that I have
been in any way comforted by what has been
said by the noble Marquess. But the question
at the moment is whether I should press my
Amendment to its logical conclusion and
put this House to the trouble of dividing, or
whether I should ask leave to withdraw. I
think I should have preferred to divide the
House on this occasion, and would have
done SO if any other hon. Member had
spoken on the matter; but, as nobody else

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of the
Appropriation Bill I do not think it is
necessary for me to say anything to the
House. It is the usual Bill that comes
before us every Session, and I do not think
it is necessary for me to add anything in
explanation of it. I beg to move.

Unemployment Insurance (Temporary Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of
the Unemployment Insurance (Temporary
Provisions) Bill I should inform the House
that this Bill makes provision whereby any
amendment of the Unemployment Insurance
Acts made by the Imperial Parliament in
its
present Session may, so far as the
Government deems advisable, be applied to
Northern Ireland by Order in Council of
the Governor. This power to apply legislation by Order in Council is limited in two
ways. In the first place, it lapses upon
further provision being made by our own
Parliament with respect to Unemployment
Insurance. In the second place, it ceases
absolutely on the last day of the present
year.
This Bill is similar in character to the
Bill which was introduced under similar
circumstances last year and accepted by our
that certain
Parliament. I am
objections were then taken to this form of
legislation, and I have no doubt that it will
aware
be held that those objections apply to the
present Bill with equal force. But I have
yet to hear of an alternative method of
avoiding the occurrence of
serious
difference between the conditions of Unemployment Insurance in Northern Ireland
and in Great Britain, which has less to
urge against it. I beg to move.
a
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: There is
not much in this Bill to be debated, but at
the same time I think there is a considerable
amount of interest being exhibited in this
question throughout our area, more so than
there was last year when a similar Bill
was put through. There is no doubt that it
is time something was done. Not only are
the workers of that opinion, but evidently
public men and employers are of a similar
opinion. I noticed during the past few days
considerable controversy going on in the
Press in regard to the remarks of one gentleman who is in a position to be able to state
opinions which have created a considerable
amount of criticism. One sure thing is that
the time has arrived for something to be
done. We have been paying and paying, and
I believe a considerable number of people
have been praying earnestly for trade to be
revived. Unfortunately, instead of any improvement in trade, I think we are now
in a worse position really than has existed
for a few years past. Therefore I say it is
necessary that something should be done.
A suggestion has been made that a conference should be called to consider whether
or not bringing public men, employers, and
workers together might obtain some solution. I would like to say here that I do
think there might be some good come out of
that. We have in this part of the country
a very reasonable class of employers, and
at the same time we may say that we have
a
very reasonable class of trade union
officials. We are not troubled to a great extent with the extreme fanatic who is known
on the other side, and I do think that if
it is possible to bring the employers and
these reasonably sensible trade union officials
together, with the assistance of the public
men of the city, it is quite possible that
there may be some good come out of it.
I know the Government have done their
very best to provide for the workers who
are unable to get work, but there is a considerable opinion outside that there are a
great number of impostors imposing on these
relief schemes and the unemployment
benefit. There are bound to be some impostors among such a large number of people
in receipt of this benefit, but I am satisfied
that the impostors are very small in number
compared with the genuine cases. If one
goes along the streets at the present time and
191 Unemployment Insurance Bill.

THE SENATE
Local Authorities Bill.
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
looks at those employed on relief work he
will find dozens and dozens of really good,
skilled tradesmen quite prepared to work
with shovel and pick rather than walk about
drawing the dole. That is one instance to
show that the workers are quite prepared to
do any class of work, if it is possible to get
it, rather than draw the dole.
I do say that the time has arrived for a
serious position to be taken up. It is quite
possible that we have some people who have
ideas that might be put into operation. At
the present time we have a considerable
number of agricultural workers flocking into
the city, and I do not see why, in a case such
as that, the Government, who are responsible
for paying the dole, should not be able to
send a number of these younger agricultural workers out to the Colonies or
Dominions. Surely it would be money well
spent. Six months' benefit in advance, I
suppose, would give them free passages, and
these men then would be employed on farm
work, for which they are best adapted and
for which they are so much needed in those
countries.
i
The most serious question I believe is
whether an industrial centre such as ours
will be able to provide for a population such
as we have in the future. If it is not the
sooner we do something to distribute the
population and get them into better positions
somewhere else the better. I believe there
is something in the statement that we have
a population for which we will not be able
to provide in the future. The shipyards are
not likely to be busy in the near future, but
even if they were there would still be a considerable number of unemployed in that class
of labour. No class is more affected than
the iron working trades. It is possible for
the woodworkers to find employment in
different ways-building is very good-but
the iron workers are in a worse position than
any other class. I want to make a suggestion, if the Government is interested in
the conference that may be held. Mr.
Brownlee, the chairman of the Engineering
Union, appealed to Mr. Baldwin, the Imperial Premier, to consider whether he could
find a means of providing employment for
the skilled workers, such as engineers,
boilermakers, and men of that class. If the
Government could find some means of employing those men, surely it would be a much
greater benefit to the State than setting them
to work with the pick and shovel. Owing
to his skill and knowledge every one of
those men must be more valuable than the
non-skilled worker. I would like to make a
suggestion, in case the projected conference
is held, that it should consider what
can be done for the skilled iron worker. I
have no objection to the Bill which is proposed for Second Reading. I am pleased
that the Minister of Labour is keeping pace
with the Imperial Government. On the
last occasion I complimented the Minister of
Labour on keeping pace with Great Britain,
because at that time there was an increase
of benefit for the workers; but there is every
possibility on this occasion that there will be
a decrease instead of an increase, and the
workers may not be so anxious to welcome
the change. I have much pleasure in supporting the Second Reading of this Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed."
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.

CLAUSE 1 (Prolongation of Duration of
the Principal Act 10 and 11 Geo. 5, с. 17).
(1) Subject to the provisions of this Act,
the Increase of Rent and Mortgage Interest
(Restrictions) Act, 1920, as applying in
Northern Ireland (in this Act referred to
as "the principal Act") shall continue in
force with respect to dwelling-houses to
which it applies, until the following respective dates, namely:-

(a) in the case of a dwelling-house of
which the rateable value exceeds fiftytwo pounds, until the first day of
November, nineteen hundred and twentysix;
(b) in the case of a dwelling-house of
which the rateable value exceeds twentysix pounds and does not exceed fifty-two
pounds, until the first day of May,
nineteen hundred and twenty-eight; and
(c) in the case of a dwelling-house of
which the rateable value does not exceed
twenty-six pounds, until the first day of
May, nineteen hundred and thirty.

Mr. GREER: The first point I want to
raise on Section 1 of this Bill is a small one.
It may not, perhaps, have been noticed by
the Government, but in the Act of Parliament of 1920, which provided for the control
of these houses, the words used were "where
the amount of standard rent or rateable
value does not exceed so much," and I think
they should be decontrolled by the same
words. I see here the amount of the
standard rent has not been included; it
merely depends upon the rateable value. I
think it would be simpler and more correct
to
decontrol the houses by the same
expressions as those by which they were
controlled. I know of one or two cases
where it would make a difference, whereas
if you use the same words you decontrol the
same class of property. I do not know what
view the Government may take, but I would
like to hear the point dealt with.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
On a point of Order. I beg to point out
that the hon. Member has not moved any
Amendment.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in page 3, line
12, before the word "rateable" ("of which
the rateable value exceeds") to insert the
words "rent or."
The CHAIRMAN: I understood that the
hon. Member had moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I would like to ask my hon.
Friend to explain a little more clearly how
it is proposed to decide what class the house
is in, because it is possible for a house to be
in one class by rent, and another class by
rateable value. That is the trouble I am in.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1
think it would be convenient if I were to
take the three Amendments of my hon.
Friend together. The effect of my hon.
Friend's Amendments would be to have a
dual instead of a single basis of determination in regard to the houses which would be
liable to decontrol under Clause 1 of this Bill.
The Bill as drafted, and as it passed through
the Lower House, provides that the rateable
valuation shall be the determining factor,
whereas if the Amendments were accepted
the issue would be confused by having an
alternative basis of determination, namely,
the rent. It is a fact that in the original
Rent Restrictions Act the rent and rateable
valuation were taken as the determining
factors, but it should be remembered that
the original Acts were intended more for
Great Britain than for Ireland, and that
in Great Britain the valuation approximates
more closely to the rent than in Ireland,
and it varies more also. In Ireland the
valuation is of a much more fixed character,
and is more easily understood. Also, it does
not vary with the rents like the valuation
across the water. Accordingly the valuation
is a much more important factor and a much
more intelligible factor in Ireland than the
rent. Speaking, as I do, for the Government,
we feel that to accept the Amendment would
tend to complicate the Bill, and would also
tend to complicate the administration of the
Act when it became law. Accordingly, I
would venture to ask the hon. Member to
consider the objections which I have put
forward on behalf of the Government, and,
if he can see his way to do so, to withdraw
the Amendment.
Mr. GREER: I feel that there is a great
deal in what the Leader of the House has
said in connection with the point I have
raised, but I thought it right to raise it, as
the words were different. I am quite satisfied
with what I have heard, and I beg leave to
withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move, in page 4,
line 5, to leave out the word "eight" ("nineteen hundred and twenty-eight") and to insert instead thereof the word "seven."
This Amendment raises a different point
altogether. It aims at the decontrol of
houses at an earlier period than is named
in the Bill. I want to bring forward a
proposal of my own for hastening the construction of these houses. I feel some
diffidence in raising this matter, but I hope
it may lead to the matter being considered
by the Joint Committee between the Belfast
Corporation and the Government and other
persons concerned. I am making the point
very much in the interests of the workers,
because I consider the present conditions are
G
Rent Restrictions Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Greer]
really scandalous. The overcrowding that is
taking place in some parts of Belfast is
beyond conception. There are six subtenants and their families living in one
house, and ten or twelve sub-tenants living
in other houses. I hope hon. Members will
bear with me just for a minute or two while
I try to develop this point. There are a
number of tracts in Belfast which are laid
out for builders. Streets are laid out, and
they are properly equipped for building, and
I look upon the present difficulty as only a
temporary one. I think that in a few years
we will have plenty of employment in Belfast,
and plenty of houses in Belfast. The present
conditions, I hope and trust, are only
temporary.
My point is this: why not build temporary
houses which will get over the present difficulty, and then when prosperity returns
replace them with proper brick and mortar
houses? I know that the Belfast Corporation
does not approve of galvanised iron houses.
There are numbers of them in Belfast, and
although they are not as good as brick and
lime houses that are properly equipped and
so on, yet I am sure the working class will
consider that they would be housed much
more comfortably in galvanised iron houses
than they are by becoming sub-tenants and
having to take one room for their families.
Then it would be an easy matter to have them
constructed in a short period of time. It
would not take nearly as long as the time
that is given for building the houses in the
Bill.
In my opinion it would be a very much
better thing for the working man if he could
be housed in this way immediately than if
he would have to wait for months, and perhaps years, before he could get a proper
house. I believe it would encourage private
enterprise more than anything else, because
a private individual could put up a house of
that kind very cheaply. I am told that
between £200 and £300 would pay for a house
of that description. The site could be obtained in any one of these streets that are
ready for building, and the private speculator could proceed in the usual way to put
up a block of half-a-dozen houses.
The CHAIRMAN: I do not see that what
the hon. Member (Mr. Greer) is saying for
the moment has very much to do with his
Amendment to Clause 1 of the Bill dealing
with houses to be built. It is not quite clear
to me that temporary houses have anything
to do with his actual Amendment.
Mr. GREER: I bow, of course, to your
ruling, but I thought that if houses could be
built in this way they could be constructed
much sooner, and, therefore, there would not
be so long a time required as is given in the
Bill. In that way I thought I was in order.
The CHAIRMAN: Perhaps the hon.
Member will continue. I see there may be
something in it.
Mr. GREER: I have very little more to say.
The CHAIRMAN: It is not easy to see it
at first glance.
Mr. GREER: My point is that in a very
few months, or perhaps in a year or two
years, all the houses that are required in
Belfast could be built if they were built in
that way, of galvanised iron or steel or by
some other method of construction. Therefor, it is not necessary, in my opinion, to give
this length of time, 1st November, 1928. The
houses could all be built in that time, and,
therefore, I move my Amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1
may not allow myself to follow the hon.
Member in his digressions which you have
ruled are not entirely in order, but perhaps
I might be allowed to suggest to him that
every one of these temporary houses will
occupy a vacant site, and that we hope under
a Bill which will pass through Committee.
a short time from now, we shall expedite
building, and that houses of a more serviceable and more lasting nature will be put up
on these sites which would be occupied by
the houses which the hon. Member is hoping
to see speedily erected. But if I may get
back to the subject of the Amendment which
the hon. Member has moved, I am glad to
say we feel great sympathy with a great deal
of what he has said. It is obvious that the
overcrowding in Belfast is something which
requires a remedy, a drastic remedy, and
one which one hopes can very speedily be
put into force. The hon. Member's Amendments are designed with the object of achieving this in the way he thinks the best
way, but I would like just to put this point
before him, that the dates which are prescribed in the Bill as it stands have been
arrived at after most careful consideration
by the Government of all the factors for and
against early decontrol, and this decision
has only been arrived at after consultation
with all the interests concerned. I can
assure the hon. Member (Mr. Greer)
that we have spent many anxious moments
over this particular point, and the Government taking all these questions into consideration with, as I say, the opinions of the
interests involved and all the reasons for
and against, have arrived at the date
which they believe to be the best.
I
would venture to ask the hon. Member to
realise that those are considerations which
actuated us in coming to the date we have
fixed.
Mr. GREER: I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment.

CLAUSE 3 (Amendment of S. 2 of the principal Act as to increase of rent in respect
of repairs).
The following Amendments stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. GREER:
Leave out Clause 3, and insert the following
New Clause :-
"3. (1) Where part of a dwelling-house
to which the principal Act applies is
lawfully sub-let, and the part so sub-let
is also a dwelling-house to which the principal Act applies, then, in addition to any
increases permitted by paragraphs (a) to
(e) of Sub-section (1) of Section two of the
principal Act, an amount not exceeding
ten per cent. of the net rent of the dwellinghouse comprised in the sub-tenancy shall
be deemed to be a permitted increase in
the case of that dwelling-house, and an
amount equivalent to five per cent. of the
net rent of the dwelling-house comprised
in the sub-tenancy shall be deemed to be
a permitted increase in the case of the
dwelling-house comprised in the tenancy.
Sub-section (2) of Section three of the principal Act shall not apply as respects any
increase permitted under this Sub-section.
(2) Where part of any such dwelling-house
is so sub-let, the tenant shall, on being so
requested in writing by the landlord,
supply him, within fourteen days thereafter, with a statement in writing of any
sub-letting, giving particulars of occupancy,
including the rent charged, and should the
tenant without reasonable excuse fail to do
so or supply a statement which is false
in any material particulars he shall be
liable on summary conviction to a fine not
exceeding two pounds.”
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Mr. DICKSON: I beg to move, in Clause 4.
page 7, line 35, at end, to add-
("(h) the tenant has not regularly
occupied, or does not at the date of the
passing of this Act personally occupy the
house, and has usually sub-let the house,
furnished or unfurnished.
The existence of alternative accommodation shall not be a condition of an
Order or Judgment on the grounds specified
in paragraph (h) of this sub-section.")
This Amendment, which stands in my
name, is for the purpose of doing away with
abuse by an individual tenant. It does
not affect ordinary seaside residences, but
simply a tenant who does not require the
house for his own use, who lives in lodgings
or elsewhere, and lets the house at a profit
rent. I do not think it is reasonable that he.
should be allowed to continue as the rackrenting landlord of a house of that description.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
However desirable and laudable the intention
and the purport of the Amendment which
has been moved by my hon. Friend (Mr.
Dickson) may be, I regret to say that it is
an Amendment which the Government feel
they cannot accept. The whole subject of
sub-letting has already been dealt with comprehensively in paragraph (i) of Clause 4, on
page 8 of the Bill.
The paragraph puts a stop to the practice
of sub-letting without the consent of the landlord. It is the opinion of the Government
that, so far as the future is concerned, it
would be neither expedient nor practicable
to go further than this.
Most lettings, as hon. Members are aware,
in seaside resorts have a clause prohibiting
sub-letting. If there has been no such clause
in the past, it is not the policy of the Government to introduce new conditions and to alter
a state of affairs which has existed for some
considerable time-certainly since before the
war. For these reasons I cannot, on behalf
of the Government, accept the Amendment
which has been moved by my hon. Friend.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg leave to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 5 (Notice of Increase of Rent)
and 6 (Limitation of arrears or overpayments) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
!
Rent Restrictions Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
CLAUSE 7 (Suspension of increase of
rent on ground of disrepair.)
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon Member (Mr. Leslie) asked questions
on the Second Reading, and perhaps I might
be allowed to give him the information which
he requires on the Question that Clause 7
stand part.
a
The word "certificate" applies to
specific document signed by an executive
officer of the sanitary authority, and indicates
in detail the actual works, if any, required
to be executed in order to put a dwellinghouse into a reasonable state of repair. The
certificate can be used as documentary evidence by the tenant in resisting a claim for
permitted increase of rent on the grounds of
repairs.
The word "report" in sub-section 2 of
Clause 7 is intended merely to cover the
statement by the sanitary authority that the
works mentioned in the certificate have been
properly carried out, and is not necessarily
required as documentary evidence in the
courts. The question of the terms to be used
in this connection is of trivial importance,
and in the opinion of the Government it
would be ludicrous to make any change in the
Bill now in this connection. I hope that
answers the questions which the hon. Member asked me on the Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite content, and I
thank the Government. I found myself that
part of the expression was borrowed from an
earlier Act. I must say in both Clauses 7
and 8 the use of the word "report" is a
little obscure, but I have no doubt but that
if having regard to the experience of usage
it is shown that no inconvenience has been
experienced we may safely leave the matter
as it stands.
*
*
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Provisions as to repairs).
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
(4) Where the landlord has, whether before
or after the passing of this Act, served a
notice to increase the rent of a dwellinghouse by the maximum amount permitted
by paragraph (d) of sub-section (1) of
section two of the principal Act or by that
paragraph as amended by this Act and the
tenant has paid the increased rent in
accordance with such notice, the landlord
shall be deemed to be responsible for
keeping the dwelling-house in a reasonable
state of repair, as if he had entered into
an express covenant in that behalf.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move in Clause 8, page 13, line 12,
after the word "shall" (the landlord shall be
deemed) to insert the words, "notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in
the tenancy agreement or implied therein
by law."
Under the Landlord and Tenant (Ireland)
Act, 1860, which is commonly known as
Deasy's Act, a tenant is responsible for repairs in the absence of any express agreement to the contrary. In the case of the
normal tenancies in Belfast this Act has
been generally ignored, and the landlords
have in the majority of cases, without any
express covenant to this effect, regarded
themselves as being responsible for the repairs, and on the strength of that assumption they demanded and received the
additional 25 per cent. allowed by the
principal Act of 1920.
This Amendment will make it clear
beyond doubt that notwithstanding anything
in the Act which I have just mentioned, or
in any covenant or agreement entered into
between the landlord and the tenant, the
landlord shall be deemed to have been
responsible in any cases where he has served
the notice of increase of rent and has received the increased rent.
Amendment agreed to
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The next Amendment in the same Clause is
in line 13, after the word "be" (to be responsible) to insert the words, "and always
to have been since immediately prior to the
service of such notice." This is consequential on the previous amendment
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended. ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Powers of Courts to determine
certain questions).
Mr. MAGEE: I beg to move after Clause
9 to insert:-
Section fifteen of the Summary Juris
diction (Ireland) Act, 1851, shall have effect
as though two pounds five shillings were
substituted for one pound in Sub-section
(1) of the Section aforesaid.
This Amendment is intended to extend the
jurisdiction of the Petty Sessions Courts. At
present that jurisdiction extends to cases of
weekly and monthly lettings where the rent
does not exceed £1 per month. The recom
mendation that this Amendment should be
carried into effect was passed by the
Committee which sat to consider the
Rent Restrictions Act, and according to the
printed Report no member of that Committee dissented from that recommendation.
It was in 1851 that the jurisdiction of the
Petty Sessions Court was fixed, and there
