The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1
desire to move the Second Reading of the
Government Loans and Exchequer Pro-
Second Reading.
Loans Bill-

THE SENATE
So,
of
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
visions Bill. This Bill which I have
the honour to recommend to the House
this afternoon marks, if
I
may say
the transition of the Government
Northern. Ireland from adolescence
to manhood. Sheltered as we have been by
the British guarantee, we have for the four
years which have elapsed since the
machinery of our self-government was set up
been under test; we have been proving at
once the stability of our institutions and our
own ability to make proper and profitable
use of them. Those in charge have been
learning the art of the conduct of a nation's
business, and I think I may claim that
this Bill is proof that that lesson has been
learnt, and that the confidence of the world
has been gained, because it shows that we
are ready now to compete with other Governments on equal terms in the money markets
of the world.
In another place yesterday, my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, in a speech
remarkable for its lucidity and its optimism
-an optimism well-founded upon a basis of
disclosed facts and figures, a reasoned and
reasonable optimism-urged the adoption of
the scheme proposed in this Bill for setting
up the machinery to enable the loyal
Province of Ulster to issue her own loan
stock, guaranteed solely by the industry and
wealth of her own people. All who have read
my right hon. Friend's statement to which
I have referred will recognize that this is no
gamble; that this step which
propose
to take is no leap in the dark.
we
My right hon. Friend pointed out that ever
since our first financial year, 1921-22, we have
been able to show an increasing surplus, the
total accumulation in the four years' being
over a quarter of a million. Furthermore,
a close examination of the figures of trade
for the last year shows that in spite of the
depression under which our great industries,
the linen industry and the shipbuilding industry, have been labouring and are still
labouring, the balance of trade is yet in our
favour. I am sure that it will strike the
House as a remarkable recommendation for
the proposed Ulster stock that the trade
balance is on the right side now when the
two great industries I have named are experiencing such difficulties. For the time
will come, and I hope that time is coming
soon, when these industries will resume all
their former prosperity and the balance of
trade in our favour be so much the higher.
Hitherto we have been compelled to use
the doubtful expedient of issuing long-term
loans to local authorities, financed from
short-term Treasury Bills and borrowings
from our own funds. That course was in its
nature only a temporary one to enable us to
acquire a sound foothold and to achieve that
necessary reputation for stability in the
world of finance, with which only the experience of some years of actual existence
could possibly endow us. The present
Measure marks the end of the old system.
We propose, therefore, to revert to the
accepted practice for financing local loans
in Great Britain and to establish by the
present Bill a special fund called the Government Loans Fund, from which, in the words
of the first Sub-section of Clause 1 of the
Bill "all Government loans shall be issued,
and not out of the Exchequer." In the same
way
as in Great Britain, Parliamentary
authority for new issues of stock from the
fund must be sought annually, and our
annual Act dealing with such loans will
correspond with the Public Works Loans Act
passed annually by the Imperial Parliament.
Parliamentary control of the operations of
the fund is thus abundantly secured and the
interests of the investor thereby well safe
guarded. I should perhaps explain to hon.
Members that the present Bill, while setting
up the machinery of the fund, also takes the
place of the annual Act for the first two
years, giving the Government power to issue
stock to an aggregate amount of two million
pounds from the passing of this Bill till
March 31st, 1927, an amount which it is
estimated will suffice to cover the repayment
of existing loans to the Exchequer from the
fund and new loan requirements during the
period.
I do not think that the House will expect
me to take the Bill clause by clause. Part 1
sets up the machinery for the establishment
of the Loans Fund, with which I have been
dealing. I ought perhaps to stress Clauses 5
and 8 of the Bill, because they provide for
the absolute security of the investor, in
that they place the whole credit of the State
behind the stock in which he is investing.
With the provisions of this Bill we are
making the Ulster loan stock a first-class
security, a trustee security, which is safer
than any other security that the wit of man
can devise.
Part II of the Bill deals with certain
Amendments of the existing law which the
process of time and experience have proved
to be necessary. The details are somewhat
technical, but I shall be glad to answer so
far as I can any questions which hon. Members may wish to raise.
I feel that I should explain to the House
that we are asking that this Bill should go
through all its stages to-day, and this
answers the question asked earlier by the
hon. Member opposite (Senator Dorman). This
is a mode of procedure which none of us
very much like. We naturally prefer that
all these Bills should go through their
necessary stages in the normal way and that
Loans Bill.
Second Reading.
15 OCTOBER 1925


t

everybody should have an opportunity of
making himself familiar with all their
provisions. But 1 would venture to point
out that it is a matter of great urgency that
this Bill should be passed at the earliest
possible moment. I might go further and
say with regard to financial Bills that come
before this House that we actually have no
control over the details of them. We are
called upon to say "aye" or "no" to the
passing of such a Bill as this, and that is some
excuse for the speed with which I am asking
the House to pass the Bill. The Measure is
essentially of that class of legislation which
is not susceptible of Amendment by this
House.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think the House
will be at all inclined to quarrel with the
Government for the suggestion that we should
dispose of this Measure to-day. It must
have been plain to all of us, either through
the advance copy of the Bill as it was introduced in another place, or from the newspaper reports this morning of the speech
delivered elsewhere by the Minister of
Finance, following on the speech he had
delivered some few days earlier when moving
the resolutions which are required under the
Standing Orders that prevail in another
place--it must have been plain to everybody
that the major portion of the Bill which
would be submitted to us would be devoted
to setting up machinery to give effect to
decisions which have more than once over a
long interval of time been taken by this
House.
We had, I think, always in mind that the
temporary financial Measures we passed
should give place as speedily as possible to
some more general system, and we must have
had that constantly in our minds when we
gave our consent to the Measures dealing
with Housing and so forth which appear in
the Schedule to this Bill.
I think we are
again indebted to the noble Marquess for
giving us a very careful precis of the provisions of the Bill which does make it very
much easier to understand.
I must admit that the very first Clause
gave me a certain amount of difficulty because
I was rather inclined to think that Appropriation Acts might be referred to instead
of the Public Works Loans Acts which have
just been mentioned and which are passed
with very great frequency by the Imperial
Parliament, dealing with the matters appertaining to the particular year. I think the
explanation as regards the Public Works
Loans Acts obviates any difficulty as regards
some of the, perhaps I may say, perplexing
details of the earlier part of the Bill.
Coming to the loan itself, which is the
principal portion of the Bill, I think it is
only necessary to wish the loan the most
complete success and to say that everybody
will look forward to the issue of the first
prospectus with more than usual interest.
I am sure the prospectus will be a full
prospectus and will contain a full account of
the security which is behind the issue. I am
sure that everything will be disclosed that
ought to be disclosed and which will tend
to invite individuals to invest. I think the
noble Marquess, the Leader of the House,
said the loan will be a trustee security by
virtue of the Government of Ireland Act and
it will apparently be issued not in Bonds
but in Registered Stock.
I come now to another point in the Bill
on which I should like a little information.
I find a reference in Clause 5, Sub-Clause 4,
which I do not at first sight appreciate.
The object of the Sub-Clause appears to me
to be to render a sum of money, a rather
hypothetical sum of money, which may never
come into existence and which if it does come
into existence is not likely to be large, to
the Minister of Finance, and it does seem
to me that the wording of the Sub-Clause
places a sum of money at the disposal of
the Minister of Finance and enables it to
escape the control of Parliament. I do not
know what precedent there is for a provision
of this nature, but I confess that I should
myself rather prefer that the concurrence of
the Comptroller and Auditor-General should
have been required.
There is always a difference, as I am sure
the House appreciates, in our financial arrangements. Owing to the economy of our
public officials, which we always try to maintain and for which we never get any credit
outside, we have not got so many sets of
officials in Northern Ireland as they have
in Great Britain. In Great Britain Local
Loans matters are attended to partly by the
National Commissioners and partly by the
Treasury. Here the Minister of Finance is
acting in the dual capacity, but the fact
scems to remain that we are proposing to
give him control of a sum of money without
coming to Parliament as regards its disposal.
If there is any precedent for that I confess
I should like to be referred to it. The point
is really a very small one and not one to
occasion any controversy, whatever the decision may be, but I think it is a matter
to which attention should be called.
The only other point to which I desire to
call attention is Clause 13, and as regards
that I want to express what I feel would be
the general opinion, and that is a feeling
of satisfaction that the Government have
been able to find a further small sum of
money for the Agricultural Science Laboratory at the University. I am sure that is
a proposal which will commend itself to all of
I have nothing further to say except
to repeat my good wishes for the success of
the loan.
us.
The Late Senator Woods. 252
Loans Bill-
THE SENATE
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to wish the Minister
of Finance success in this laudable undertaking to consolidate and unify the finances
of the Government. The question of the
finance of the State unfortunately does not
receive that amount of consideration by the
citizens as a whole that it deserves, and on
behalf of the section of the community I
represent in this Senate I rise to express my
approval of this Bill and to wish wholehearted success to the Minister of Finance,
because I believe his proposal will tend towards the exercise of economy in the finances
of Northern Ireland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
thank hon. Members for the reception they
have given to this Bill. My hon. Friend
(Senator Leslie) has asked for a precedent
for transferring the balance to the Exchequer.
Mr. LESLIE: Not transferring it.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
should say not transferring it. A precedent
will be found in the Labourers (Ireland)
Act, 1906, Section 15. It was at first intended
to leave any excess income to accumulate in
the Income Account, but I feel sure that
Senator Leslie will understand that this
would militate against clear annual accounts
and it was therefore decided not to do this
but to transfer any such excess to the Exchequer, leaving a small working balance in
the Fund. In practice the Exchequer will
not benefit from this course considering that
over a period of years the rate of interest
payable by local authorities will be adjusted
to meet charges on the Income Account.
I
think that answers the question of the hon.
Senator.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
--(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion negatived.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to thank the Noble
Marquess for what he has said. I am sure
the explanation will be quite satisfactory to
everybody concerned.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(The

Motion for Adjournment.
THE LATE SENATOR J. A. WOODS.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, That this House do no adjourn.
On that Motion I would like to say a few
words with reference to the great loss this
House has sustained since we last met by
the death of Senator Woods. I know that in
expressing my regret and sympathy with his
family I am voicing the feeling of every
Member of this House. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) We appreciated to the fullest
extent the presence of Senator Woods in this
House. I am sure that his genial personality
endeared him to everyone with whom he came
into contact as it certainly did to those of us
in this House with whom he had worked
during the past four years.
The late Senator Woods was a genuine
representative of Labour, and he brought to
bear upon our deliberations a ripe experience
of all those questions which came before this
House, which is certainly poorer by the fact
that Senator Woods is no longer with us.
He occupied the position of a Deputy Speaker
of the Senate and we naturally placed himin
that position because we knew quite well that
the honour of this House and the traditions
of Parliamentary Institutions were quite safe
in his hands and that he would have discharged those duties with honour to himself
and with credit to this House.
I do not feel that on this occasion you will
expect me to say more with reference to our
late Friend. That his death is a great loss
to the Senate is something we all recognise
and we can place on record our appreciation
of the services he rendered and our sense of
the loss we have sustained by his passing.
I beg to move that this House do now
adjourn.
It
is
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
hardly necessary for me to speak on this
Motion after the very kindly reference that
has been made to the late Senator Woods by
the noble Marquess, the Leader of the House.
I happen to have been acquainted with
Senator Woods longer than any other Member
of this House. I knew him for a considerable
number of years, when he worked, as I did,
in the shipyard, and I must say that he was
Mundoubtedly a man who always endeavoured
to do his duty to the best of his ability. On
the occasions when he spoke he always expressed himself without giving offence to
others. I always noticed that he was most
sensitive and most anxious to give no offence
and showed no ignorance when dealing with
any matter. I desire to join with the noble
Marquess in expressing sympathy with the
wife and family of the late Senator Woods in
the great loss they have sustained.

Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I would
like to say a very few words to supplement
the remarks of the previous speakers. Those
of us who are original Members of the Senate
always admired the conduct of the late
Senator Woods and his attitude towards the
business that came before the House.
was never one of those who made
necessarily long speeches, but when he did
say anything he did it so shortly and simply
and spoke so much to the point that he conveyed everything he intended to. He was the
type of man who was well worthy of any
honour that could be bestowed upon him, and
he was an able representative of his own
particular section of the community in this
House. His death is a great loss not only
to this Senate but to the party he represented, and I am sure every Member of this
Chamber sympathises with his widow and
family in their bereavement.
of
Mr. GLENDINNING: I should also like to
be associated with the tribute to the memory
of the late Senator Woods. I came here a
considerable time after the Senate was set
up and one
the first of the
original Members with whom I came
in contact was Senator Woods. Since
then I have had frequent opportunities
of discussing with him matters pertaining to
the business of this House and the Government in general, and I have invariably been
struck with the great intelligence and understanding he displayed in connection with any
legislation that came before this Parliament.
One thing that greatly struck me in regard
to him was the kindly disposition he showed
not only in the House but outside.
I endorse what Senator Joseph Cunningham has just said, that though Senator
Woods as a representative of Labour fought
the battle of those he directly represented
here he did so without saying an unkind word,
and in everything he said he displayed a
true sympathy with those for whom he was
speaking. I regarded him as a most genial
and kind-hearted colleague whose demise
came to me as a great shock. I take this
opportunity of adding my testimony to the
services he rendered as a Member of this
House, and I greatly deplore his loss.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to join wholeheartedly in the expression of condolence so
ably voiced by the Leader of the House. I
felt that I was under a deep debt of gratitude
to the late Senator Woods for the generous
treatment he accorded to me on my first
attempt to address this Senate, when the
noble Marquess informed me that what I
said was out of order. At a time when death
removes a Member of the House it is the
privilege of Members of all parties to join
in an expression of sorrow at the unexpected
death of an esteemed colleague.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I would like to associate myself with the remarks that have been
made with reference to the late Senator Woods.
I do so chiefly because I represent a distant
part of this Province, and have nothing to
say to Belfast one way or another. But
during the last four years since I have had
the honour of sitting with the late Senator
Woods in this House I always admired him
and I am able to endorse what the other
Senators here have said that he spoke in
that nice moderate way we all admire.
will not add any more to what the Senators
have said, but merely wish to say that I feel
we have lost a great friend in the late
Senator Woods.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I am authorized by

Government Loans Exchequer Provisions
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Seed Potatoes Supply Bill, to which they desire the
concurrence of the Senate.
Seed Potatoes Supply Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First

Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This Bill continues temporarily certain
enactments (whether of the Parliament of
the United Kingdom or of the Parliament of
Northern Ireland), which may be regarded
as being still experimental or liable to revision. A single date only is fixed for their
continuance, namely, the 31st December,
1926.
The Measure has been framed so as to fit
in with the corresponding provisions of the
British Bill of the present year, which
applies to Northern Ireland "in so far as it
deals with any enactment relating to a subject with respect to which the Parliament of
Northern Ireland has not power to make
laws." The present Bill supplements this provision by dealing with enactments relating to
subjects with respect to which the Northern
Ireland Parliament has legislative power.
Thus the two Measures taken together deal
with all temporary laws applicable to
Northern Ireland according as they come
within the power of one or other of the two
Parliaments.
I hardly think hon. Members will require
me to go into the details of the various
Measures continued, but I may perhaps call
their attention to one of the Measures which
has been allowed to expire this year. Doubtless hon. Members will have noticed that we
are not seeking to continue the Inspectors of
Weights and Measures Act (Northern Ireland), 1923. That Act made provision for the
appointment of Inspectors of Weights and
Measures, other than members of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, at a time when no members of that Force were qualified to fill the
office. I am happy to say that a sufficient
number of the members of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary are now qualified, and we are,
therefore, able to dispense with the powers
given us under the Act. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: The Bill before us this afternoon is a very short one. I am inclined to
think that perhaps the most interesting item
in the First Schedule is the continuance of
the Employment of Women, Young Persons
and Children Act, 1920, for another year.
That Act was passed as the House will remember, to give effect to a convention at
Genoa. This convention itself gave effect to
a
convention held the previous year at
Washington which gave the Secretary
of State power for five years to make
regulations respecting the employment of
women, young persons and children on
the application of representatives of employers and employees. I think such power
was something in the nature of an experiment and it is distinctly satisfactory to find
that it has worked well and has given cause
for its continuance. The Second Schedule
is extremely short. The third item dealing
with one of the sections of our own National
Health Insurance Act is obviously necessary.
The other two items do not call for particular observations at the moment. I think
we shall all be glad if we do not see them
any more. For the moment all that seems
necessary is to pass the Bill to carry us on
to the end of 1926.
257 Census Bill-
Second Reading.
20 OCTOBER 1925

-d
ea

the
this


:
T.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House; the House to be in Committee tomorrow.
Census Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second reading of this
Bill I do not propose to go into great detail
on the Clauses, for these Clauses really explain themselves. There are no very intricate Clauses or matters connected with this
Bill. As hon. Senators know, for many years
past a Census has been taken in the United
Kingdom at intervals of ten years. The last
was in 1921, but as hon. Senators will recollect it was not possible to take a Census
in Ireland in that year owing to the disturbed state of the country, so that there
has been no Census taken in Ireland since
the year 1911-14 years ago. The Census
figures are consequently very much out of
date, and though estimates of the total population of Northern Ireland are made from
time to time, based upon the 1911 figures
adjusted for births, deaths, migration, etc.,
they can only be considered approximately
correct. Estimates of population in towns
and other administrative areas are very unreliable, and the framing of estimates of age,
distribution, et cetera, of the population,
either as a whole or in any particular areas,
is a task of great difficulty.
Representations have been received from
time to time from public bodies regarding
the necessity for a Census, and the Cost of
Living Committee in their Report dated 20th
September, 1923, also recommended "that the
Government of Northern Ireland should, in
the near future, have a Census taken and in
that connection obtain further information
as to the housing accommodation, such as
was obtained in the 1921 Census in Great
Britain." The matter has also been raised
in Parliament from time to time.
Accurate figures of the numbers and distribution of the population, together with
particulars as to age, sex, occupation, size of
family, et cetera, would be of material
assistance for administrative purposes, and
it is very desirable that Northern Ireland
should not lag behind other progressive communities in possessing all the data necessary
for statistical research. The Government
have, therefore, decided to introduce this
Measure providing for the taking of a Census
in Northern Ireland next year. I beg to
move.
Mr. BARBOUR: I think the Government is
pushing an open door in advocating this Bill,
and we may congratulate ourselves that the
state of affairs enables us to contemplate the
taking of a census without any trouble. It is
a happy thing that we can remove this gap
that has existed in these figures and that
there will be forthcoming as a result of this
accurate figures as to our population.
There is one point on which I would like
some assurance, and that is one that is raised
in Section 3, sub-Section (2.) The suggestion
is made there that a great deal of the work
will be carried out by members of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary. It is also suggested that
additional people will be employed for the
purpose of making this census. Now there is
one thing the Government of Northern Ireland, I think, has introduced. It is an
innovation as far as I know. I think it is
a very healthy innovation, namely, that anyone whether he takes up voluntary service for
the Government or one of the local councils
or whether he takes up official service for the
Government, is obliged to take an oath of
loyalty to His Majesty the King and to the
Government. I would like to have some
assurance that these people who may be employed under this Bill will have cast upon
them a like obligation.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure there will be concurrence throughout the North of Ireland in
the taking of the census, but I am equally
sure that there will be a percentage, not, I
hope, higher than the average percentage of
recalcitrant people who will object to being
asked to fill a form of any kind, supplying
ages and so on. I think we ought to ask the
noble Viscount for the customary assurance
that the return will contain the usual provision that the details given by the citizens in
their return will be used and published in the
form of abstracts only, and that they will be
protected from curiosity and so forth. Perhaps
there will not be wanting some people who
'will think that by filling up the census form
they may be exposing themselves to prosecution in connection with insurance matters
and so on. As regards the sanctity of the
form I think Clause 9 of the Bill really goes
a very long way, and I am quite sure that
the usual paragraph on the form itself will
cover the remainder.
am
The Bill before us departs from precedent
in several directions and I am inclined to
'wonder whether part of the departure is not
due to a very laudable desire to re-establish,
to some extent, other records of which we
have unfortunately been deprived. I
afraid that all the previously existing Irish
census returns have been destroyed, and I
fear some other statistics shared the same
fate. Another way in which we have struck
out a little in a new line seems to be that we
recognise in this Bill for the first time that
the duty of filling up the form rests on the
person required to prepare the form. Hither-
T
Adjournment. 260
Census Bill.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
to the suggestion in the Statutes rather was
that the enumerator was to do the work himself. Now I am glad to say the householder
is the man who will be called upon to give
particulars.
a
I think that these departures from precedent
in some minor ways will be matters which
will raise some discussion in Committee, but
there are two matters which I propose
specially to bring before the House where
there has been
change. In former
Measures of this kind it has always been the
custom to provide that those unfortunate
people who spend the census night in jail or
in an asylum should be represented on the
form only by initials. I think a provision
of that sort should be introduced again.
Again, it was the practice expressly to provide that no questions should be put to the
people other than the questions necessary to
obtain the information required to be provided. I think we ought as a concession to
popular prejudice to put that in again.
ex-
These matters can be raised in the Committee stage and I will not dwell further
upon them this afternoon, but there is one
suggestion I would like to make. I presume
that the return forms when we see them will
have upon them the usual tables and
amples showing how the details should be
given. I have no doubt we shall have the
opportunity of renewing acquaintance with
the Morans. They have served us faithfully in
this matter for 70 years. I would suggest
that their very good service should be recognised by our recognition of the fact that they
may be entitled to more than one christian
name for I see that we are asking in this
Bill for full names. On the previous occasion in 1911 I distinctly remember I was
approached by several people in my own
district who wanted assistance as to the way
they might properly record their various
names. They pointed out that their children
had more than one name. I think we might
make a further departure from precedent in
giving more than one name in the examples.
I do not think the actual working out of
the Bill will give any serious trouble, but
there are one or two matters which will require further consideration.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think the answer to the question which was
raised by the hon. Senator Barbour who
addressed the House first on this subject is
that the Constabulary will be assisted by
Civil Servants who have already taken the
Oath of Allegiance. Consequently that meets
the point he raised. The hon. Member (Mr.
Leslie) who followed him raised some Committee points which I can assure him will be
considered when the time comes, and if he
feels very strongly about them, perhaps he
will put them down in the form of an Amendment.
Clause 9, I think, answers the question
about the privacy of the information. The
Census Bill is merely for the purpose of
gaining certain information. With reference
to the further question aboutchristian names
I am not sure what object he desires to
obtain by this-whether it is the average
christian names held by the citizens of
Northern Ireland. I am sure that would be
information of the highest interest, and if
he feels very strongly on the point he can
raise it in Committee.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: There is one
question I wanted to ask with regard to this
Bill. I think the expenses are to be borne
by Northern Ireland. It seems to me that a
question of this kind of taking the census of
a part of the United Kingdom is the service
of the United Kingdom and part of the expense should be borne by the Imperial Parliament.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
have not considered the question the hon.
Senator has asked but I will certainly do so.
I appreciate the point he raises-that this
matter is in the interest of Great Britain as
it is in that of Northern Ireland, but I ani
not sure when we take all things into consid.
eration that it does not really become a local
matter which it is not actually necessary for
Great Britain to obtain. It certainly is a
matter for consideration and I am sure if
'we can relieve the Province of expenditure
it is our duty to do so.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House; the House to be in Committee
on Thursday next.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
(The Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
Minutes before Four of the
Clock.
Seed Potatoes Bill-
Second Reading.
21 OCTOBER 1925
?

THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 21st October, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
JOINT STANDING ORDERS COMMITTEE.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that the Senators
following be named of the Joint Standing
Orders Committee on Divorce Bills :-
Mr. T. M. Greer.
Mr. J. G. Leslie.
Mr. W. J. McDowell.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Seed Potatoes Supply Bill (N.Ι.), 1925
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I am sure this Bill,
the Second Reading of which I have the
honour to move, will commend itself to the
united support of hon. Members. The object
of the present Bill, which is similar in form
to many Acts passed by the Imperial
Parliament in former days, is to regularise
and to ratify arrangements which have been
already made by my right hon. Friends, the
Minister of Finance, the Minister of Home
Affairs, and the Minister of Agriculture, for
supplying seed potatoes of a good quality
to poor farmers and occupiers in certain
parts of County Fermanagh.
The wet season of 1924 was responsible for
a general failure of the potato crop in these
districts, with disastrous results to the
poorer classes of farmers and occupiers, and
it became apparent in the early spring of
this year that special steps should be taken
in the first place to make provision for a
supply of good seed potatoes, and in the
second place to arrange terms of repayment
which would be within the means of the
persons who required them.
From reports made by the inspectors of
the Ministry of Home Affairs and the
Ministry of Agriculture, it appeared that
these conditions were generally prevalent in
the Unions of Irvinestown and Enniskillen,
and in certain areas in the Unions of Clogher
and Lisnaskea. In the latter cases the
Minister of Agriculture was enabled to make
special arrangements for meeting the deficiency, but in the cases of Enniskillen and
Irvinestown it was found necessary to invite
the co-operation of the Boards of Guardians
in regard to the distribution of the seed
and the payment therefor.
The Boards of Guardians of the two Unions
expressed their willingness to co-operate with
the Government in this respect, and finally
a system was arrived at whereby farmers
owning farms of valuation of £15 and under
and persons of similar class were allowed to
purchase seed from the Board of Guardians
up to 5 cwt. in the case of farmers, and
2 cwt. in the case of labourers occupying
land.
The seed in question was obtained by the
Ministry of Agriculture with special care and
under special supervision, and was of the
very best quality. It was sold to the applicants at the price of ordinary seed-27 a ton
or 7s a cwt.
Further, under the arrangements made, the
applicants were not reqnired to pay cash
for the seed, the price being spread over a
period of two years, and being collected from
the persons in question with the ordinary
rates. It may be added that the seed in
question originally cost the Government
about £10 a ton, although it was sold at
the normal market price for the poorer
classes of seed, namely, £7 a ton. The difference in cost was borne by the Government.
Altogether 801 tons were distributed in the
Enniskillen area, and 691 tons in the Irvinestown area. I think this is a Bill which
commends itself to hon. Members, and I will,
therefore, not trespass further on your indulgence. I beg to move.
a
Mr. LONG: I am sure the consideration
that the Minister of Agriculture has shown
for those in necessitous circumstances is
deserving of every praise, and is a matter
on which he is to be congratulated. I think
the Measure is one that will commend itself
to this House. One criticism that I would
make is that instead of applying the Bill
to this particular year, as these cases are
recurring in the poorer districts almost every
year it would have been
wise thing to
have empowered the Boards of Guardians to
do at any future time what is now contemplated in this Bill. The only other criticism
that I have to make is that I think 5 cwt.
of potatoes is insufficient to grant to each
person. Five cwt. of seed would be merely
sufficient to plant an area of slightly over
one rood, and for a farmer with a farm of
£15 valuation would not be adequate for his
wants. If the Government could see their
way to consider the two suggestions I have
made-that the Bill should empower the
Boards of Guardians to sell seed not only
this year, but at any future time, and that
the quantity should be increased-I should
be very glad.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I have only had the
Bill since I came into the House, and I would
like to know if it applies to the whole of
the Six Counties or to County Fermanagh
263 Seed Potatoes Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
a
[Mr. Hill Dickson]
alone. Another point I would like to have
cleared up is who the persons are that are
to receive the seed? Are they only people
with a valuation under £15, or does it apply
to other farmers of
higher valuation?
Even if it does apply to the Six Counties
I do not think that in our particular area
of County Down, especially in the northern
end, there would be any necessity for it.
However, I am very glad to support it for
counties like Fermanagh where there is a
necessity for it.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to approve of the
principle introduced in this Bill. I think
it has been unfortunate in the past that
Governments have not paid sufficient attention to assisting those engaged in the
cultivation of the soil in the interest of
the people as a whole. Much of the sorrow
and sadness caused by the decreasing of our
population during the last sixty or seventy
years has been due to the negligence of
Governments with regard to this particular
aspect of social legislation.
Governments have been more interested in
exacting rent from the cultivators of the
soil in the past than they have been in
assisting the cultivators of the soil to make
the best possible use of their land by directing the attention of cultivators to the
potentialities of the soil, and in directing
them to produce the greatest possible proportion from the area they control in the
interests of the people as a whole. Looking
at it from that aspect I have been greatly
struck by reading some of the provisions of
the oldest law in the world, the law of
Hamurabbi. I find in the provisions of that
law that all persons in the occupation of
land were compelled to cultivate it, and
even in the case of those who held land in
return for services granted to the State, if
they neglected to cultivate that soil continuously for three years any citizen had the
right to enter thereon and cultivate it in
the interests of the people of the State. The
only persons who would be excluded would
be those who held their land under feudal
terms and could prove that their inability
to cultivate it was due to the fact that they
were engaged on the King's business.
We are living in a country with 15,000,000
acres of arable land, and a celebrated economist of the last century, Lord Lauderdale,
distinctly declared that Ireland was capable
of supporting in health and comfort 2,000
persons for every 500 acres of arable land,
which means that if Ireland was cultivated
to-day according to the conditions suggested
by Lord Lauderdale we should be capable
of supporting in health and comfort
population of 60,000,000 people.
a
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
Under Protection.
Mr. DORMAN: The Leader of the House
suggests it would be under Protection. That
is questionable, because I have had the
privilege of seeing the early pioneers in many
countries, and I know that the pioneers in
all new countries make their first purpose
or object in the use of the soil to produce
the greatest possible proportion of use values
for themselves, and then to produce as large
a margin as they possibly can in order to
exchange it for the other necessaries and
comforts which they think essential. I just
make these remarks because this is a question I have given much attention to. I
have seen all parts of the country, and I
have seen what a revolution has been made
by the cultivation of what was regarded as
useless land. One of the most remarkable
instances of that is the Trappist Order in
the west of Waterford. They turned a barren
mountain into a veritable paradise by the
application of labour to the land. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Measure.
The MARQUESS OF LONDON DERRY:
If the hon. Member (Mr. Dickson) who has
asked me what the scope of the Bill is will
read Clause 1 he will see that the Bill is
legalising what has already been done. That
has been done in the County of Fermanagh,
so that the Bill does not apply to the whole
of the Six Counties. Senator Long, whose
knowledge on all these matters is second to
none, suggests that further provision of this
sort should be made and that a larger allotment of seed should be made to those concerned. This is one of those questions to which
there are two sides. The people themselves
are not particularly anxious to receive assisance. As a whole they desire to carry on
their own livelihood by their own efforts, and
there is a point-and I am not sure that
in some cases we had not reached it, and
gone beyond it when the State's coming in on
his behalf will tend to neutralise the power
for effort of the individual concerned. This
is a matter on which it is possible to open
wide discussion that I would not
venture to do so now.
such
a
There is also the question of economy. I
have stated that the Government are paying
the difference between the cost of the seed
and the price they receive for it, and it is
very difficult for the Government at the present time to draw a right line between the
demands that are made for ensuring that
every economy is practised and the wisdom
of expending money on certain particular
objects. I do feel, and I see that the House
agrees with me, that this is a Measure of a
very necessary character. I will repeat to
Senator Hill Dickson that it covers a period
from January to July, 1925, and is really
retrospective in its application.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.

CLAUSE 4 (Preparation and filling up of
forms and making of Returns).

(3) The returns for the purpose of the

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause 4,
page 2, line 26, at the end of paragraph (3)
to add:-
"Provided that the initial letters only of
the Christian names and surnames of persons
being patients, paupers, prisoners, or persons under temporary restraint present at
midnight on the census day on the premises
of any public or private hospital, sanatorium, convalescent or nursing home,
workhouse, poorhouse, infirmary, civil
prison, lock-up, or other place of detention
shall be stated in any such return."
This is a matter to which I referred on
the Second Reading of the Bill. The Amendment which I have put down proposes in
effect to retain for the unfortunate persons to whom it relates the protection, the
'absolute protection, which has always
hitherto been given to them. Our Bill, as
was said the other day, departs to some
extent from precedent, inasmuch as we have
to work our Census now under a control
other than that of the Lord Lieutenant.
But I want, if I may, to read to the Senate
Section from the Census (Ireland) Act,
1910, which was repeated in the Census (Ireland) Act, 1920, the Act which as you will
remember, was abortive owing to reasons
'which we dealt with the other day. The
Section in the Act of 1910 runs as follows:-
a
3. The Governor, master, or chief
resident officer of every prison, workhouse,
barrack, hospital, or lunatic asylum, and
of every public or charitable institution
which may be determined upon by the
Lord Lieutenant, shall act as and be the
enumerator of the inmates thereof, and
shall conform to such instructions as may
be sent to him by the authority of the
Lord Lieutenant for obtaining the returns
required by this Act, so far as may be
practicable, with respect to such inmates,
but the initial letters only of the
Christian names and surnames of such inmates shall be stated in any such return.
The effect of that provision, as will be
observed, was this: The actual name of the
unfortunate person, the inmate of the workhouse, lunatic asylum, or prison, was not
disclosed to anybody at all. The Governor
or Master of the institution, whose business
it was to send in the return, was, of course,
already aware of it, but neither the enumerator or any of the Clerks in the Census
Office, nobody whatever, was acquainted with
Census Bill-
Committee.

tet

that name. Consequently it was absolutely
impossible for any of the details of that form
to be disclosed by any person, or for access
to the information to be obtained by any
person. It was a case of absolute impossibility because the information cannot
leak out from the form when it is not on
the form.
I shall no doubt be reminded that in the
Bill before us we are taking exceptional
precautions to ensure secrecy, and I have not
the least doubt that the greatest precautions
will be taken and the utmost care will be
used, but at the same time there is always
the possibility of arrangements, however
perfect they may be, breaking down somewhere, and in the past the cumulative wisdom of the Census Authorities in passing
Irish Bills across the water has led them
to maintain this absolute protection. It
seems to me that this House, on behalf of
the unfortunate persons concerned should
insist that the same absolute protection
should be given, and that is the purpose of
my Amendment. I am sorry that the method
we have developed of referring to this class
of people in the Schedule rather than in the
body of the Bill imposes on me the necessity
of submitting to the House a somewhat
lengthy form of wording, but provided the
principle for which I am contending is
accepted, there would not be the least objection to reducing the words if the experts
can preserve the same idea in the body of
the Bill in some shorter form. But for the
moment I ask the Committee to accept the
principle that the absolute protection given
in the past shall not, without the direct
authority of Parliament, be now taken away.
I beg to move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I take it that the hon. Member who
has moved this Amendment feels very strongly
on the point, judging from the speech he has
delivered to the House. He thinks some sort of
injustice or injury is going to be perpetrated
on the people to whom he wishes this particular Amendment to apply. It is quite true, as
the hon. Member said, that in the Census
(Ireland) Act, 1910, the initials only are
asked for, and the Bill now before the House
is undoubtedly a departure from the Irish
Census Acts of years ago, and we have
modelled it more or less on the Acts of Great
Britain. So far as the Act in Great Britain
is concerned the full name is given. In this
Bill we have got very stringent safeguards
as regards secrecy. Hon. Members have only
got to turn to Clause 10 of the Measure to
see the penalties that are to be inflicted for
the wrong use of information given on the
Census forms, and I feel very strongly
that, having regard
these safeguards-and they are real safeguards
the Government accepted the hon.
-if
to
Senator's Amendment it might be
taken by the public that we had not confidence in these safeguards that we have put
into the Bill, and as hon. Members can see
the inevitable result would be that public
confidence would be shaken in the arrangements we have made in regard to maintaining secrecy. If the hon. Member is logical,
and if he really desires that only the initials
of these persons should be given, he might
just as well argue that only the initials of
everyone should be placed on the census
paper. On the ground that it would tend to
shake the confidence of the public in the
arrangements we have made the Government
are not prepared to accept the hon. Member's
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to hear from the
noble Viscount that the Government is obdurate, and the only thing I can do is to
reply to the points raised in the speech of the
noble Viscount. As regards the Act of 1920
applicable to the English Census, I am unable
to say exactly what happens under it. The
Act itself does not reproduce the Section I
read from the Irish Act of 1910, because the
system adopted in England under the Act of
1920 is entirely different to the system adopted
in Ireland. The arrangements in England
were to be made by Order in Council, and
what they were I do not know.
The noble Viscount referred to the terrible
penalties to be inflicted on people who disclosed information, but the person who wants
the protection I am asking for does not want
penalties. What he wants, and is entitled
to, is the same absolute protection as he has
hitherto enjoyed, and not a protection depending on words and arrangements, however
excellent they may be. He wants the absolute protection of his name not being on the
Census form.
As regards the confidence of the public
being shaken by the Government declining to
take away a privilege that has previously
existed, I am quite unable to follow the noble
Viscount. The public as a whole is not going
to read the Act, when it becomes an Act, and
I am afraid the public does not care very
much about that part of the matter. I do
think the public might very reasonably ask,
"
Why did you take away a privilege that
has always hitherto existed? What reason
can you give for taking away a protection
which is absolute and substituting something
else which depends on efficiency of arrangements?" Under the old law these details
were communicated to nobody except the
officer in charge of the institution. The
enumerator did not know and none of the
clerks knew. There is always some risk of
involuntary disclosure, and there is always
the chance of an enumerator of local know-
Census Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
ledge getting to know something which the
enumeratee would prefer he should not know.
The only safe course is to adhere to the
practice of the previous Irish Bill.
As regards the suggestion of the noble
Viscount that the initials only should be
used in all other cases, I must point out that
so far as I know the object of giving the full
Christian names is to enable the Census
officials to exercise some check on the statement as regards sex and so forth, and you
cannot do that efficiently if you have not got
the names. But if the Government decide
on initials only I am sure the public will be
glad, because it will save them trouble in
filling up the forms. I am bound to regard
myself to some extent as a trustee for the
unfortunate persons whose privilege it is
proposed to take away, and much as I regret
it, I am unable on this occasion to withdraw
the Amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
regret the hon. Member is proposing to take
the course which he has adumbrated, although he is quite entitled to do so. The
case he is making is not one of so much
substance as he suggests. It is a matter of
regret to me that Senator Leslie is really
questioning the integrity of those who are in
charge of the census, instead of letting it
go as an understood thing that in all matters
of this description those who are concerned
are naturally as high-minded as the hon.
Member is himself and would under no circumstances divulge the information which is
given.
a
sexes.
There are many matters of
private
character in a census, including some which
touch the susceptibilities of both
I have heard that the secrets of age have
been given in trepidation and absolute
secrecy to the officials marked out to do this
work, and I have never heard of any secret
of this description being given away. Speaking quite seriously, I think the hon. Member
is casting somewhat of a slur on the integrity of the officials in this country. It is
one of our proud boasts that the public
service can always be trusted. There is the
Finance Bill, for instance, and the Budget,
which for years has contained secrets of the
highest importance and yet it has always
been taken for granted, and our trust has
never been falsified, that the information
these officials could give away in many cases
to their advantage is always kept secret until
it is divulged by the Chancellor of the Exchequer at the box.
I feel that this matter of the census is in
keeping with the ideas we have always held.
It is quite true that in former Census Bills
in Ireland the methods suggested by Senator
Leslie have been adopted. These are methods
with which, speaking for myself, I have very
little sympathy. I think the substitution of
initials for names is something which the
public have not looked on with favour lately,
and I do feel that in matters of this description to follow the hon. Member's suggestion
would not be progressive. It is not consistent with the ideas of the majority of the
people in this country, and chiefly on the
ground that it is casting a slur on the manner
in which all these undertakings are carried
out by those who have the honour to be in
the Government service I would venture
to ask the hon. Member to withdraw his
Amendment. There is no reason why he
should respond to any suggestion I make but
I feel that to have a division on this matter
is casting an aspersion on public officials
which I am sure is the last thing he wants
to do.
Mr. MAGEE: While I do not for a moment
consider that there is any intention on the
part of Senator Leslie to cast an aspersion
on the officials responsible for the census I
think the Amendment is one which on first
consideration one might regard as reasonable, but when you come to think it over
you must remember that this census is not
for to-morrow only but for all time. We all
know the great advantage that was derived
from the old census in regard to Old Age
Pensions. There was a record of the exact
circumstances of the individuals concerned
and one never knows what may transpire in
the future. I think it is better to have the
full information and for that reason I cannot support the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: It is perhaps convenient to
deal first with the speech of Senator Magee.
He seems to suggest that my Amendment
ought not to be accepted in order that it
may be easier to refer to these returns for
the purposes of Old-Age Pensions. If I have
correctly understood the noble Viscount he
never said that these papers are going to be
used for Old-Age Pensions or any other purpose for which the names and actual details
will be disclosed.
Turning to the more serious point made by
the noble Marquess, the Leader of the
House, I think there is every reason why I
should respond to the suggestion made by him
because we do not much care for a division
amongst ourselves. Still there are matters
on which one is bound to assert one's
thoughts, especially on a matter which is
not vital to the Government. I should be the
last person to question the integrity of any
branch of his Majesty's Civil Service. I know
far too much about his Majesty's Civil Service to think of doing anything of that sort,
but to some extent, if the Committee will
allow me to say so, I am in a special position. I have some full knowledge of the
officials of the Civil Service. The ordinary
Census Bill-
Committee.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in the First
Schedule, first and second columns, to leave
out Paragraph 8.
A point arises on this Paragraph, and I
am sure the Committee will appreciate that
the Amendment as I have put it on the
Paper is not intended to be the final word
on the matter. I have put down the suggestion to leave out Paragraph 8 in order that
we might pursue the matter a little further
and see if anything better can be done.
The paragraph I am proposing to amend
is, if one may say so, the necessary omnibus
paragraph which a Schedule of this nature
would have to include, but I want to see
whether we can do anything better
whether the arrangements are as perfect as
they could be.
or
It will be seen if we look down the Schedule that the people employed on night
work, such as at the docks, are not caught
in the Schedule. Neither, I think, are the
people who spend the night on the roadside,
or a night watchman, and I am afraid that
| Leslie, J. G.
Massereene and Ferrard, Viscount
McDowell, William John
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
Turner, Sir William
the Constabulary who happen to be absent
on duty are not included. The getting of
any of these people included in the return
must depend on the issue to them individually of the form and on the fact that they
arrive home at their ordinary dwellinghouse before the time at which the return
is required to be filled in. I am afraid it
is not easy to ascertain from the Bill what
is the time that the form is required to be
completed, but I imagine that will be dis
closed when the actual forms are delivered.
and, I hope, in fact I believe, that the local
knowledge of the persons delivering the
forms will be of great assistance in enabling
very few people to escape filling in the form.
But I thought I ought to put something on
the Paper in order to direct the attention
of the Committee to this arrangement. For
the moment then I beg to move to leave out
Paragraph 8.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I really do not see that there is
any great substance in what the hon.
Member has said. It is perfectly true that
in all census returns there is bound to be
a certain leakage. The hon. Member mentioned several cases which he thought might
have come under the First Schedule, such
as night watchmen and the men employed
at the docks at night, as well as members
of the Constabulary who are out all night.
But paragraph 8 is really a saving paragraph
to rope in persons who are not included in
the preceding seven paragraphs. It must be
remembered that the enumerators who are
responsible for collecting these returns will
be chosen very carefully and will be men
who will know the district in which they
are working. They will know the houses
they have got to go to as well as the inhabitants, and if they go to the house where
anyone is away when the return is collected,
on the following day or it may be two
days afterwards, I do not think it
will be found that the persons who
away when the form was delivered
were
Census Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
will be omitted from the return. Of course
there will be some people who will not be
enumerated, but then this paragraph is
intended to catch those who do not come
in under the other seven paragraphs. The
matter has been considered very carefully.
It is a pretty long Schedule as it is, and the
Government do not think they can add any
other paragraphs except those which would
rope in the people who are not otherwise
enumerated.
Mr. LESLIE: If I am told that this paragraph is the best the Government can do my
object has been attained, and I beg leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

دو
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in paragraph
3, to leave out all the words after age
("Age in years and months").
"
The first point on the Second Schedule
arises in paragraph 3, where the Bill asks
for the age in years and months. The former
practice in Ireland, as the Committee will
remember, was to require the age last birthday. I think age last birthday" was the
original proposal, with the addition that in
the case of infants under one year the age
should be stated in months. It is now proposed to ask for the age in years and months,
but I think before the Committee accedes to
that they ought to be satisfied that the additional information is required for some real
purpose, and that when it is given it is likely
to be of some use. These Census forms, as
we know, will fall into the hands of people
throughout the country of various degrees of
education, and it may be that the ordinary
rural dweller will remember the year in
which he was born and his age, but it is
somewhat doubtful if he will remember the
exact date of his birth. The point seems to
be as to whether there is any real reason for
this departure from the previous Acts. If
there is, before we pass this paragraph the
real reason on which the Government rely
ought to be given to the Committee.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The answer to the hon. Senator is
that the words "in years and months" are
put in solely because we believe we will get
greater accuracy. In the last census in Great
Britain "years and months " were inserted
in the form, and it has been found that they
got far greater accuracy than they did in the
former census. We feel that as we are starting a new census for the first time in Northern Ireland it would be an advantage to put
in these words, because the people are more
likely to pay more attention to the date on
which they were born if they know it. It is
merely on the ground of greater accuracy
that the Government desire to retain the
words in the paragraph, and, therefore, cannot agree to this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount has suggested that the reason the Government inserted years and months" is that it is
possible that they will get greater accuracy.
I do not see what test is going to be applied,
or was applied in England, as to whether the
particulars given in the Census return in
question were more accurate than hitherto.
You cannot test the accuracy of the information, and if that is the only explanation
I do not think it is a very strong one. If you
determine, therefore, on carrying out this
proposal the only point for the Committee to
consider is whether the people of Northern
Ireland exist to be experimented upon or
whether we should wait until our second or
third Census of Northern Ireland, and experiment with the department responsible
for taking the Census. The people of Northern Ireland might prefer to see that department experimented upon first. However, it
is not by any means a vital question, and if
the Government prefer to put the people to
the necessity of disclosing the number of
months over a given age I have no personal
objection. I think it is a matter about which
the public can form its own opinion, and I
think it is a case in which the Committee
will give me leave to withdraw my Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Paragraph
5 to leave out all the words after "upwards"
("In respect of persons aged 16 years and
upwards") and to insert "condition as to marriage, e.g., single, married, widowed, etc.,"

This again is rather a departure from
previous practice. I ought perhaps to tell
the Committee that when I first thought of
putting an Amendment on the Paper I proposed to word it somewhat differently. I
proposed originally to suggest that the words
"respect of persons aged 16 and upwards"
should be taken out, because it seemed to
me rather curious that we should go out of
our way to shut out information as to those
cases in which girls under sixteen happened
to be married. But I am informed that the
real object in leaving the words in is to
enable one column to do double duty, which is
a mode of economy that is very admirable.
I am also informed that the number of such
marriages is only about one a year and,
therefore, that figure is negligible. There is
a possibility of young married girls coming
e! from across the water, but the number canle
not possibly be large, and therefore there
does not seem to be any objection to those
the words remaining.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in paragraph
7 to leave out from "birth" ("place of birth")
to the end of the paragraph.
The reason I put down this Amendment
to alter paragraph 7 is really to give an
21 Opportunity for discussion.
as
Hitherto,
Members of the Committee will remember,
the information asked for as to the place of
birth has been
'Place of birth; if in Ireland state town;
if elsewhere state country."
It is now proposed to ask for the place of
birth, and if born in Northern Ireland the
name of the county and the town or townland. It may be that some people, including
those I have referred to in asylums and other
institutions, will remember the name of the
county and the town they were born in.
They will remember to have heard it but
will not remember the actual fact. But
when we come to townlands we have to consider two or three things. Townland boundaries vary a little during the lives of some
of the people who will be required to fill up
these returns, people of sixty or seventy
years of age. During that time quite a number of townlands will have become merged in
new urban districts and so forth, and also,
for that matter, a townland or part of a
townland may have shifted itself from one
county to another. I do not know whether
anybody has any views as to whether we
ought to insert so much in the Bill or
whether, as occurred to me, we might have
simply stopped after "birth" and left the
form itself to be put into some shorter form
of detail. That is the only point I want
to bring before the Committee. From one
point of view there can be no object in
inserting "townland" because if you took
the census for Old-Age Pensions purposes,
as was suggested, but which you do not do,
the name of the townland would be of value
if you could be sure it was correct. But,
as you say you do not want that, the only
object is to show that the person in question
was born in a rural rather than in an urban
district, which is a fact of value to statisticians. For the moment I would suggest that
we should stop after "birth."
a
a
census we
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In this matter the Government
feel that it would be
mistake to omit
the words which the hon. Member wishes to
omit. In
want to get
as correct returns as we possibly can
and I think the hon. Member will agree with
me that there are many people in Northern
Ireland who only know the place of their
birth as the townland where they were born.
We might have put in "urban or rural district, or council area," which would convey
very little to a certain number of people
in the rural districts. The Government feel
that on the ground that they want to get
the answers as accurate as possible it would
be a mistake to leave out these words.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Paragraph
12 to leave out from "Whether" ("Whether
entitled") to "under" ("under the National
Health Insurance Acts") and to insert
"insured."
The question here is a very simple one.
If you are going to refer to the National
Health Insurance Acts I venture to suggest
that you ought to ask the people the direct
question as to whether they are insured
under the National Health Insurance Acts.
That is a very plain question to which you
ought to get a straight answer. Everybody
knows whether they are insured or not, but
quite a number of them will not know
whether they are entitled to benefits under
the Act. I am not at all sure that the
proner Amendment would not have been to
leave it out altogether, but if it is deliberately intended to insert it I do suggest that
you ought to put a straight question and
expect
I, therefore,
a straight answer.
suggest this alteration.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Senator is under the impression that we are trying to camouflage
something or other. The point is that the
Government do not wish to know the number
of persons who are insured, but what they do
want to know is the number of persons who
are entitled to benefit, which is a different
thing. You have people who have paid to the
National Health Insurance Fund only two
or three times and then dropped their payments, and they may put themselves down
as insured. We do not want to know the
number of people insured, but the number
of people who are entitled to benefit, and
therefore, to alter the paragraph as suggested would defeat the object of the
Government.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Paragraph
13 to leave out the word "crippled" ("blind,
crippled, or mentally afflicted").
"
The suggestion I make here, principally
for the sake of discussion, is to leave out
the word crippled." The whole question
of defining terms as to the various infirmities
of members of the household has always
been one of difficulty, but we have never
made inquiry as to whether an individual
was a cripple or not. When we were discussing the Education Act with regard to
mentally defective children the word
"
دو
crippled" was put in, but the objection
that is obvious to all of us to the use of
the word "crippled" is what do you mean
by a cripple? I suggest that if the word
cripple is used ordinary men, for some
reason or other, fly to the conclusion that
you are referring to someone who goes about
on crutches. Therefore, it limits cripples to
some infirmities of the lower limbs. A man
may be crippled if he has lost his arm, and
the question also arises as to the disabled
soldier. He is a man who is a disabled
cripple. Is the word "crippled" sufficiently
definite to justify its inclusion in the census
form? It is not a point of great importance
and I do not suggest that any great injury
is done to anybody. The information given
will only be the opinion of the person a
to whether the person in respect of whom
the return is sent in is a cripple or not.
"
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The objection which the hon.
Member has to the word crippled" may
to a certain extent be applied to the whole
of this paragraph. You might apply the
same objection to the words "mentally
afflicted." It must be a matter of degree,
and, though I fully appreciate what he has
said as regards the definition of a cripplethe majority of people will consider it refers
to the lower limbs-the matter has been
carefully considered, and if the hon. Member
can suggest a more suitable word perhaps
the Government will not object to its insertion. At the same time I think the word

crimpled" is the most easily understood
word, and the Government think it should
be left in.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I expect a solution of the
difficulty will be found by some note on the

who fills it up, and by the tact of the
enumerator, if he is
person of local
knowledge. As it stands we have not been

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave out
Paragraph 14.
The question here again arises as to
whether Paragraph 14 as it stands is the best
we can do. What is it to begin with that
we want to get at? We have already got, or
propose to get, under Paragraph 6 information as to orphans, but as regards Paragraph 14 we should not get information as
to orphans. It leaves out the very few
children of people whose marriages have been
dissolved, but, worse than that it leaves out
for all practical purposes nearly all the ille
gitimate children in Northern Ireland, and,
281 Census Bill—

22 OCTOBER 1925
Seed Potatoes Bill.


as the Committee will remember, the percentage of illegitimates is four per cent. It
also leaves out the children of the area whose
fathers or widowed mothers attempt to escape
the return. Therefore, it seems to me that
we cannot hope to get an absolutely inclusive
Ereturn under Paragraph 14, and the point
remains can we do any better. If it were
agreed to leave out the Paragraph now something better might be inserted at a later
stage. As the paragraph stands I understand it includes all children under 16 years
of age who are at home. The probabilities
are that owing to the census day being the
18th of April the great majority of the
children will be at home at that time, but
there may be children in the Free State and
so on. I imagine the object is to obtain
statistical information which in abstract
form may be produced for the purpose of
certain social measures which are understood to be under contemplation at the
moment. I think that is the purpose of the
Paragraph. It is not for the purpose of ascertaining the fertility of marriage by asking
the number of children born alive. For the
moment I beg to move that we leave out
this paragraph.
때

to
VISCOUNT MASSERREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Member, the
object of this paragraph is to provide information which is essential for the purpose of
calculating the cost of social insurance
schemes and also for statistical purposes.
Although it is perfectly true that a good
deal of this information can be deduced from
the answers to other questions on this census
form, it is felt that we should get the information for the purpose of ascertaining the
cost of social services more correctly under
the wording of this paragraph. The hon.
Member raised a point about illegitimate
children. I doubt whether that is a proper
question to ask and I doubt if we should
get a correct answer if we did ask it. This
again is a new paragraph which was not in
former census Bills, but it corresponds to a
question asked in Great Britain. If we find
by experience that the information obtained
under this paragraph is not worth the
trouble then we shall drop it. For the
moment we desire to retain it.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the information with
regard to legitimacy would be sufficiently
- obtained from some of the other inquiries in
the form. I have no wish to press the matter
at all, and, therefore. I ask leave to with-
- draw the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

CLAUSE 1 (Validation of Supply of Seed
Potatoes by Guardians and Repayment of
Price by Purchasers).
Mr. BARBOUR: I want to ask the Leader
of the House if he can tell us if there is no
other way that can be devised for ensuring
the repayment of any amount of relief
granted under this Bill other than by special
assessment in each individual case. The
making out of these assessment notes is a
very complicated job, and there are a good
many special assessments now. I think that
from the point of view of the people who have
to pay the rates it would be desirable if we
could in any way obviate the necessity of
adding a further special rate for them to
have to take a note of.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am to a large extent in sympathy with the
hon. Member. The question has not been
raised before, but between now and the Third
Reading I will make inquiries as to whether
there is some more convenient machinery by
which these payments can be made, and if
there is such machinery it can be provided
for on the Third Reading.
