285 Old-Age Pensions Bill.

27 OCTOBER 1925
Second Reading.
THE SEΝΑΤΕ.

Tuesday, 27th October, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Business of the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move: That Standing
Order No. XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting, in respect of the Census Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the noble
Viscount has some good reason for the suggestion he has made in moving this Motion,
but I think it is only fair to point out that
the Bill in question is one of some importance and one which breaks new ground in
several respects. If the House assents to the
Motion it means the sacrifice of the right of
re-committing the Bill, and it also rather
handicaps any hon. Senator who might wish
to speak on the Third Reading or to contribute to the debate initiated by any hon.
Member, inasmuch as the Debates of Thursday last are not in the hands of Members
of this House. If there is a real definite
necessity to put the Bill through its final
stages to-day I should be the last to object,
but I think it is asking a little too much of
the House to assent to that course without
any explanation.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I fully appreciate what the hon.
Senator has stated to the House, but the
point is that the Bill is required very
urgently in another place and we must get it
through its stages in this House as early as
possible.
On Question, Motion agreed to
Ordered: That the Standing Order be dispensed with in respect of the said Bill.
Old Age Pensions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
Leader of the House): I do not anticipate
that I shall meet with any opposition in this
House in moving the Second Reading of the
Old Age Pensions Bill. Hon. Senators will
remember the debate which took place here
on the 21st of April last, when my hon
Friend, Senator Joseph Cunningham, raised
the question of the differences between the
conditions governing the qualifications for
Old Age Pensions in Northern Ireland and
in Great Britain. He spoke very earnestly,
as he always does, on behalf of the old and
the poor, and he urged the Government,
with that moderation and sanity of outlook
'which invariably secure him an attentive
hearing in this House, that we should take
steps as early as possible to amend our Old
Age Pensions law and bring it into line with
the law in Great Britain, which had recently
been amended by the Old Age Pensions Act
of 1924.
In the course of his remarks the hon.
Member was scrupulously fair. He never
suggested that our hesitancy in following the
British law on Old Age Pensions was due to
lack of sympathy, and I am sure that was
never in his mind. I will venture to repeat
what I said in my reply to him on that
occasion. I said;
"I can assure the hon. Member that the
Government are not behind him in any
way in the desire to place this Province
on exactly the same level as in Great
Britain. However, until our financial relations with Great Britain are fully estab
lished to the complete understanding of all
parties concerned, I think the Minister of
Finance would be incurring a responsibility
he is not entitled to incur by running the
risk of imposing upon our finances a burden
which in the future we may not be able
to bear".
The hon. Member will remember my using
those words. I concluded by expressing the
hope that, when he again asked a question
on the same subject, I should be able to give
him a far more favourable answer than I
could give him then. I feel sure all these
matters are fully within the recollection of
the hon. Member.
The House will note with pleasure that
our performance has not lagged far behind
our promise, because the Bill before the
House this afternoon embodies proposals for
the removal of the so-called thrift disqualifications from our Old Age Pensions
scheme, and those who in their working days
have put by a little to eke out the assistance
offered from State resources will no longer
be discouraged by having the results of their
thrift brought into account against them in
reckoning the amount of their pension.
This Bill brings the law as to Old Age
Pensions into line with that in Great Britain.
The memorandum circulated with the Bill is,
I feel, a sufficient explanation of the effect
of the change proposed, but I should, perhaps,
inform hon. Members that the effect of the
proviso to sub-section (1) of Clause 1 will
be to prevent persons living on our borders,
but not within them, from expatriating
themselves and coming in simply and solely
for the purpose of taking advantage of our
provision for old age under the present Bill.
The Act comes into operation on the First
of April next, and hon. Members will find
287 Old-Age Pensions Bill.

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
in the memorandum that the estimated cost
of the change is approximately £143,000 per
annum, while the estimated addition to the
pensionable population is 5,500 persons. In
addition, we expect that the great number
of the 3,500 pensioners at present drawing
pension will receive an increase in their
pensions. I beg to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am very
glad that the Government has found itself
in a financial position to be able to remove
the Thrift Disqualification Clause in the
Old-Age Pensions Act. It is true that when
the matter was raised on a previous occasion
I was satisfied that it was only the financial
position of the Government that prevented
it keeping step with Great Britain in regard
to this matter. I am sure there will be a
considerable number of old people who will
be very thankful that this action has been
taken by the Northern Government, and
none will appreciate it more than the old
trade union members who have subscribed
their contributions for forty years
Superannuation benefit and who now find
that they will be able to draw full benefit as
well as a State pension.
for
It has been unfortunate in the past that
in most cases of State aid the person who
was not so thrifty as others was the one to
benefit. The thrifty person has generally
been penalised. In many cases I have noticed
that the person who has been careless as to
whether he worked or not or as to whether
he contributed anything towards his old age
or bad times was the person who benefited
to the full extent by any State aid.
very glad that this Thrift Disqualification
Clause has now been removed, and that industrious working people will receive the
full benefit of the Old Age Pensions Act.
I am
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite sure the Bill
will be received with acclamation throughout
the Province, and I think that in congratulating the Government on its introduction
we ought not to forget that our congratulations are due to the hon. Member on my
left (Senator Joseph Cunninngham) who, as
the Leader of the House has just said, first
initiated the discussion on this question. As
regards the Bill itself there is little to say.
The first point that occurs to me is, where
is the money to come from? I think I am
right in saying-although I would like
to corrected if I am wrong-that this
money, or practically the whole of it,
will be obtainable by
reduction
in the amount of our Imperial contribution, inasmuch as, if I understand
the matter correctly, we shall be allowed this
sum provided we do not exceed the same
amount per head of our population as is
a
I think
being expended in Great Britain.
that is right, but I should like to be reassured on the point.
There is another small matter I should
like to be assured upon. I want to ask the
Government whether they can do something
rather better for us than Column 1? I make
the request because this is a Bill which,
unlike many other Bills, the public is likely
to want to buy, to read, and to refer to, and
I cannot help thinking that the first thing
any member of the public who may happen
to become possessed of Column 1-"A person
entitled to an old-age pension whose residence qualification depends on paragraph (f)
in the proviso to sub-section (2) of section
three of the Old Age Pensions Act, 1911, as
adapted by Article 9 of the Irish Free State
(Consequential Adaptation of Enactments)
Order, 1923" - will do if he wants the infor
mation will be to turn up sub-section (2) of
section three of the Old Age Pensions Act,
1911, and look for paragraph 8. If he takes
that not unnatural course he will be somewhat bitterly disappointed because paragraph 8 is not there. Paragraph 8 is the
creation of the Adaptation Order, which is
referred to in the same column, and I suggest that if possible the Government should
bring up something at a later stage expressed
in language which is a little more understandable by the public. I have nothing
more to say about the Bill except to wish it
success in its operation.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I feel gratified at the manner in which hon.
Members have received the Bill, which I
have had the privilege of presenting to them
to-day. I am glad to see that the hon.
Member who has just spoken (Senator Leslie)
is fully aware of the formula which governs
the adjustment of finance between this
country and Great Britain, and is aware of
the manner in which the contribution is re
gulated as passing from this country to
Great Britain and being returned to us on
behalf of services in certain capita.
With reference to the further remarks
the hon. Senator made he has, I understand,
taken the trouble to have dived into the
Act referred to in the Schedule, and he has
discovered a discrepancy which I must admit
I have not discovered myself. Nothing will
give me greater satisfaction or pleasure than
to probe into the question as deeply, and
perhaps more deeply, than the hon. Member
has done, and on a further stage of this
Bill I shall be very happy to enlighten him.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for to-morrow.

SECOND SCHEDULE. (Particulars to be

8. Nationality.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in the Second
Schedule, paragraph 8, before the word
"Nationality" to insert the words, "If born
outside the British Islands." The first thing
I have to do is to ask the permission of the
Senate to alter a word in the Amendment
which I propose to move. I see I have inadvertently used the expression "British
Isles" instead of the proper expression,
"British Islands."
The paragraph in the
Second Schedule which I desire to amend was,
as the Senate will remember, rather sprung
upon us, if I may say so, because no notice
of it was given on the Paper, and I believe
the circumstances of its introduction were
such that no notice could have been given
on the Paper.
But the fact that notice was not given was
really unfortunate, because the House might
have considered this paragraph in conjunction with the preceding paragraph 7, which
deals with the place of birth. The points
we have to consider as regards paragraph 8
are, what the information is that we are
really desirous of obtaining, and whether we
are likely to get that information unless we
give some special instructions on the form
or otherwise. The great majority of the
people who will be required to fill up this
form will be people who were born within
the British Islands, and if so, they are of
course British subjects, and there is nothing
more to be said about it. The intention of
the inquiry, as I understand it, is to find out
what aliens there may be in Northern Ireland on the night of the Census, and perhaps
the country of their origin.
It will be remembered that the question of
being a British subject or not depends on
place of birth, and a person born within the
British Islands of alien parents is still a
British subject unless it happens to be one
of those rare cases where the parents have
taken out certificates of alienship. We do
not want to impose on our population-
1,250,000 of them, more or less-any unnecessary labour, either on the subject
making the return or on the officials
engaged in examining the returns, and if we
can relieve a great number of our people of
the necessity of specifying Nationality, I
think it would be a good thing to do.
What we really want to get at is the
number of aliens in Northern Ireland, and it
occurred to me that the words I have
suggested might possibly have that result. I
am quite prepared to admit there are other
ways of doing the same thing, but I think
the point is to limit the number of replies.
The House will remember that there are
already fifteen questions on which information
is desired, and I think that if those fifteen
questions are examined it will be found that
the number of questions really works out at
between thirty and forty, and nearer forty
than thirty. It would be desirable to
abstain from increasing that number if it
can be avoided. Accordingly I beg to move
the Amendment which stands in my name.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are quite in accord
with the object embodied in Senator Leslie's
Amendment. From his speech I gather that
his object is to prevent the necessity of those
people who were born in the British Islands
answering the question as to Nationality.
The Government have no objection to the
hon. Member's Amendment, except that
they consider the form of the Amendment
is not quite so good as the words in
the one I propose to submit to the House.
If the hon. Member is prepared to
accept the form of the Amendment I propose
to move I would ask him to withdraw his
Amendment and agree to the Amendment of
the Government. The Amendment I propose
and which I think will satisfy the hon. Senator, is in the Second Schedule, to leave out
paragraph 8 and insert instead thereof the
following new paragraph:--
8. In respect of persons born in a foreign
country, nationality, and whether resident
in Northern Ireland or a visitor.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very much obliged to
the noble Viscount. I quite agree that the
Amendment the Government proposes fully
carries out the principle which I had in
mind, and I also agree that the words are
more descriptive and better than my words.
Therefore I should like to withdraw my
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in the Second Schedule, to leave out paragraph 8, and to insert
instead thereof the following new paragraph:-
8. In respect of persons born in a foreign
country, nationality, and whether resident
in Northern Ireland or a visitor.
Amendment agreed to.

Seed Potatoes Supply Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Ahe MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving the Third Reading of the Seed
Fotatoes Supply Bill there is one question
which the hon. Member sitting opposite me
(Senator Barbour) asked me to which I
should like to give him a reply.
The scheme adopted in the Bill may, no
doubt, cloaked as it is in Parliamentary verbiage, seem to the average man in the street
to be a cumbersome and complicated mode of
repayment, but in point of fact it is the
simplest and most effective system available
to us.
Before we carried out the arrangements
for the distribution of the seed, we consulted
the Fermanagh County Council and their
officials regarding the system of repayment,
and they cordially fell in with the scheme
and assured us that it would not involve any
appreciable additional trouble to them.
Apart from that the scheme now introduced is eminently convenient from the point
of view of the poorer classes of farmers who
cannot pay cash. This principle is a principle that is frequently resorted to in local
government administration, and is not,
therefore, anything new.
Other methods have been considered and
tried, and have been proved to be unworkable.
I hope the explanation I have ventured to
give the hon. Member may satisfy his
natural desire to facilitate the course of these
operations, because I believe that in the
methods we have adopted we have found
the least difficult way of carrying out this
transaction.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to convey
my thanks to the noble Marquess for the
wery considerate way my question has been
answered. I must confess that in reading
the Bill I did not at first realise that it was
confined to the County of Fermanagh. As
the acquiesence of the people there concerned
has been gained by the Government, I have
nothing further to say except that I am obliged for the information.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate agree to
the said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move: That this House do now
adjourn. The business to-morrow will be
formal.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Four
o'clock.

The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.
CLAUSES 1 (Amendment of statutory condition as to means, 8 Edw. 7, c. 40; 9 & 10
Geo. 5, c. 102), 2 (Amendment of law as to

SCHEDULE.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the course of the debate on the
Second Reading of this Bill here yesterday
my hon. Friend, Senator Leslie, who, I regret,
is not in his place to-day, asked us whether
it would not be advisable to amend the
schedule of the Bill, in order to remove from
it a taint of obscurity, which the hon.
Gentleman finds attaching to it, owing to
the difficulty which may be experienced in
following a somewhat tortuous course of legal
reference.
I may say at once, that, after giving to
the suggestion that consideration which we
always give to anything from my hon. Friend,
we do not think it necessary to bring forward
an amendment, in view of the fact that we
propose, as soon as the Bill becomes law,
to issue leaflets explaining the effect of the
Bill to all persons now in receipt of pensions
at less than the maximum rate, who might,
therefore, by virtue of the provisions of the
Bill, be entitled to have their pensions increased. Such persons will receive with these
explanatory leaflets forms on which application for increase of pension may be made.
There will also be available at the various
Customs and Excise Offices throughout Northern Ireland a supply of similar leaflets for
issue to inquirers generally, so that any
person not now in receipt of a pension may,
by obtaining one of these leaflets, make himself fully acquainted with the provisions of
the Bill.
I think the hon. Member will agree that
these measures will be sufficient to render
any amplification of the text of the Bill
itself unnecessary, especially in view of the
fact, which I think is indisputable, that very
few of the old people over seventy are themselves ever likely to read the Statute. The
position will be made perfectly plain to them
in the explanatory leaflets, which are the
only documents in which they are at all
likely to interest themselves. I think it is
necessary to make this explanation to the
House in view of the question raised by
Senator Leslie.
Schedule agreed to.
Title and Preamble agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without
Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved: That this Report be now received.
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).

The Drainage Bill.

Col. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: In case the

Widows' Pensions Bill-

29 OCTOBER 1925

Thursday, 29th October, 1925.
THE SENATE.
.
The Scheme, therefore, is on a contributory
basis and this is its fundamental principle.
Many reasons have operated in the decision
that this principle should be adopted for
the Scheme. I have just referred to what is,
perhaps, the main one-its effect upon the
moral fibre of the people. But I may also
mention two further somewhat more material
reasons for our adoption of the principle of
self-help.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.

Special Order made by the Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ireland under the

Widows', Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Pensions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): As Leader of the
House, it is my pleasing duty to-day to introduce this important Bill in this House.
Hon. Members will, doubtless, already have
taken the opportunity of perusing the Memorandum which has been presented in explanation of its main provisions, but I feel that
some few words of further explanation will
be expected from me. Our scheme for
Widows', Orphans', and Old Age Pensions has
become so familiar to everybody from discussion in the Press and the debates which
have taken place in the course of its passage
elsewhere, that it will not be necessary for
me to give more than an outline of its provisions.
In this Bill, we are seeking to add to the
existing schemes of social insurance, to our
Health and Unemployment Insurance
schemes, a measure of help for those who are
handicapped in life through no fault of their
own-the widows and orphans of workers in
industry, and the workers themselves who
have reached such an age that they are no
longer able to maintain themselves with full
vigour. Let me say here and now that the
benefits provided are not sufficient to maintain fully the persons for whom they are
intended. We have never laid claim to that.
In our opinion it is not the business of the
State to provide such full maintenance, but
rather to lay the foundation of thrift and
to encourage building thereon by the workpeople themselves by their own efforts. A
scheme of this kind will encourage, rather
than diminish, the self-respect and selfreliance of the people. A scheme on a noncontributory basis would, in our opinion,
tend to sap those very qualities of selfreliance which have made our people a great
people.
In the first place, the system of social insurance which has grown up amongst us in
the last 15 years, embracing as it does the
large measures of Health and Unemployment
Insurance, has been designed on this basis.
It is the system which has been accepted by
this country as the one which is most suitable, supported as it is by the united efforts
of the whole nation assisting in due proportion, workers and employers by direct contributions and the general public indirectly
by means of moneys voted by Parliament.
On this ground alone the Government would
be prepared to take its stand in formulating
a contributory scheme.
But there is another reason which cannot
be lost sight of in Northern Ireland. The
relations which exist between Northern Ireland and Great Britain in matters of
finance are well understood by hon. Members,
and I need not enlarge on the necessity for
any scheme in this country being kept, so
far as the cost to the Exchequer is concerned, within the limits of the Funds which
prove to be available.
A non-contributory
scheme could not be maintained in Northern
Ireland with the resources which are at the
disposal of the Government. Hon. Members
will realise that that is a very material
reason why the Government cannot grant
pensions on a non-contributory basis.
And now, let me speak shortly on the Scheme
itself. It is designed for the assistance of
those who are already within the ambit of
the Health Insurance system, that is, roughly,
those who are in the service of an employer,
and whose remuneration
lies within
certain limits. That remuneration is up to
£250 a year, or £5 per week. In Northern
Ireland there are about 325,000 persons of
both sexes so insured. The exempt, excepted,
and voluntary classes under the Health Insurance Acts who will be drawn in under this
scheme for widows, orphans, and old age insurance, will raise this figure to about
350,000. The number of dependants of these
persons would exceed 350,000. The total
numbers who are in a position to benefit by
the scheme if necessity arises will, therefore,
be upwards of 700,000.
Pensions for the widows of insured persons dying after the 4th of January next,
the date when it is proposed the Bill should
come into operation, will be at the rate of
10s a week, with an additional allowance for
299 Widows' Pensions Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[The Marquess of Londonderry].
children up to the age of 14 or 16 if they
are still at school. The allowance is 5s a
week for the eldest or only child, and 3s for
each other child. That is to say, the allowance for the eldest insurable child, irrespective of children outside the scheme,
through age or other reason, is 5s weekly,
with 3s for each of the other insurable
children.
Where both parents are dead, children are
referred to in the Bill as orphans, and for
them pensions at the rate of 7s 6d per week
each are provided.
It will be necessary, of course, for the insured person to have fulfilled certain conditions regarding his insurance before his
widow and children will be entitled to benefits. Two years' residence in the country will
be required, and 104 contributions will be
looked for. These contributions may, however, have been contributions under the
Health Insurance Statutes, before the new
Measure becomes operative, and, in these
circumstances, the test cannot be regarded as
a very severe one.
There will be, at the commencement of the
Act, a number of widows and orphans whose
husbands or parents, as the case may be, will
not have paid any contributions towards the
scheme, but for whom it is considered
essential that some provision should be made.
It would not be possible out of the funds
which will be available to make payments to
the same extent in their case as is proposed
should be done in the case of dependants of
insured persons dying after the 4th of
January. The additional allowances and
pensions for the children and orphans will be
the same, as it is not felt that these can be
reduced, but the pension for the widow will
be granted only if there are children of the
marriage under the age of 14, and, where
payable, will be discontinued six months
after the youngest child has reached the age
of 14.
cases,
When the circumstances are considered, and
when it is remembered that no insurance
contributions will have been paid in these
in the expectation of pensions for
widows and orphans, it will be admitted that
this is a generous provision. It is estimated
that when the Scheme takes effect 5,500
widows and 16,000 dependent children will be
entitled to participate in the pensions and
allowances
In addition to the benefits for widows and
orphans, Old Age Pensions will be provided
at the age of 65 from the 2nd January, 1928,
for insured persons and their wives. The
pension will be at the rate of 10s a week,
and will be payable, without regard to the
means of the claimant or any of the other
considerations laid down in the existing
Old Age Pensions Acts. At the age of 70 an
Old Age Pension payable under this Bill will
become automatically an Old Age Pension
under the Old Age Pensions Act.
There are, at present, a number of insured
persons over the age of 70 who are not entitled to pensions under the Old-Age
Pensions Acts, or who are drawing reduced
pensions, by reason of the fact that they
have some financial resources of their own.
It is thought that such persons should not
be shut out from the privileges of the new
scheme, and, therefore, from the 2nd of July
next they will be granted pensions without
regard to their means, subject to the fulfilment of certain conditions in regard to their
insurance. The date, 2nd July next, has been
chosen for administrative
work of arranging for the payment of pensions and allowances to dependants of men
who will have died before the 4th January
next will be considerable, and a further
period will be required to enable the administering department to consider claims for
Old Age Pensions, and make arrangements
for payment.
reasons. The
The contributions required to be paid by
employers and their workpeople will be 9d
a week in the case of a man, divided equally
between the parties. In the case of a woman,
44d, 2d of which will be provided by the
worker and 2d by the employer. To the
total amount so obtained by direct contributions of workers, and employers there will
be added a State contribution of £145,000 per
annum. This is the amount which the
Government have been advised will be required to be added to the weekly contributions during the first ten years of the
scheme in order that the benefits proposed
may be paid. The State contribution will be
reconsidered at the end of ten years, when
an actuarial report on the working of the
scheme will have been obtained.
It has been urged in many quarters that
this is a very considerable burden to place
on employment at the present moment, especially in view of the depressed condition
which has prevailed in most of the main
industries of the country for a prolonged
period. This aspect of the question has received the fullest consideration by the Government, and, if it were not for the measure
of relief to be afforded under the Health
and Unemployment Insurance Scheme, there
would be much hesitation before asking the
country to accept the new proposals.
The House will have noticed that, in view
of the proposal to pay Old Age Pensions at
the age of 65, benefits under the Health and
Unemployment Insurance Schemes will be,
as from the date upon which such pensions
become payable, cut off at that age. This
will relieve the two Schemes in question of
301 Widows' Pensions Bill-

29 OCTOBER 1925
Second Reading.
:
-
{
T


ॐ
a liability for a period of life when the risk
of illness and unemployment is high. It is
possible, in the circumstances, to make reductions in the rates of weekly contributions,
and, when these reductions which are referred to in the Memorandum which is in
the hands of Members of the House, are
taken into consideration, the net increase
for insurance in industries coming under
both the Health and Unemployment Schemes
is a modest one. The net result is that we
are seeking a total increase in contributions
for all forms of social insurance of 2d each
from the employer and worker in the case
of men, and of 1d each in the case of women.
Although any imposition at the present time
will naturally be regarded with a certain
amount of suspicion, this cannot be regarded
as a very heavy imposition, and I feel sure
that, in view of the great and lasting benefits to be conferred on workers it will be
cheerfully accepted by employers of labour,
though we do not for a moment deny that we
asking them, in common with the
workers and the whole body politic, to
shoulder a new burden.
are
I think I have said all that is necessary
for me to say at this moment about the details of the provisions of this Bill. It is our
carnest hope that, in spite of its cost, it
will prove a great boon, not only to the
widows, children, and old persons, whom it
is designed directly to assist, but also to the
people of the country at large in mitigating
poverty, in removing the anxieties of the
distressed, and in alleviating some of the
root causes of the industrial unrest which
prevails at the moment. If this is accomplished, as we most sincerely hope it will be
accomplished, as the result of the provisions
of this Measure, our scheme will have earned
what it is hoped that it deserves, the favour
of the whole people of this country, whose
interests we are seeking earnestly to secure.
It is with confidence that I recommend the
Bill to the favourable consideration of this
House. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: In the first place I desire to offer my sincere thanks to the noble
Marquess, the Leader of the House, for the
very able exposition he has given of this
particular Bill from his and the Government's point of view. I desire, notwithstanding, to move the Amendment placed in my
name on the Paper :-
To leave out the words after "that"
and insert "this House, while willing to
pass a non-contributory Measure to confer
pensions on our widows, orphans and the
aged and reduce the age limit to sixtyfive years, while removing the thrift disqualification clauses of previous Acts, declines to give a Second Reading to a Bill
imposing an additional contribution on our
lowly paid wage-earners, and on our industries, which are in a depressed condition, while making no provision for many
widows, orphans, and families where the
father is incapacitated, and providing for
those who are qualified allowances which
are inadequate for their support."
I fully expect that the attitude which 1
am compelled to take up through conviction
to-day will be grievously misrepresented in
the Press as the conduct of my colleagues
in another place has been grievously misrepresented in the Press, and my conduct here
will probably be designated scandalous and
a heartless lack of consideration for the aged
and the poor. I beg to anticipate that accusation by informing hon. Members of this
House that I have taken a very deep interest
in both branches of the community who are
coming under the purview of this particular
Bill for the last forty years, that I have
not only devoted time to the study of the
question, but I have devoted hour after hour
of unremunerated labour to endeavouring to
rouse up public interest at the street corner
and at every possible opportunity that I
could concentrate the thoughts of my fellowcitizens on the question of old-age pensions
40 years ago, and on widowed mothers' pensions and orphans' pensions for the last
fifteen years.
I happened to be connected with an organisation composed of poor men in Belfast who,
during the period of the war, invited to
Belfast a man whose name will go down in
history as the father or apostle of widowed
mothers' pensions, namely, the learned judge
of the Children's Court in New York. We went
to the trouble out of our scant earnings to pay
his expenses to Belfast, and to advertise in
the Press a public meeting to be held for the
purpose of stirring up interest in this question during the period of the war. That
gentleman was driven to take a deep interest
in the question of widowed mothers' and
orphans' pensions through his experience as
judge of the Children's Court in the
State of New York. He was not satisfied
with hearing evidence which was given as to
the criminal acts of those who were brought
before him from day to day, but after
sitting in court he took the trouble to visit
the homes of these children, and he
amazed and shocked when he discovered that
a vast amount of the juvenile criminals of
that particular State were the children of
the widows of working men or their orphans.
He consequently started to rouse up public
opinion, and finally succeeded in introducing
in the State of New York a Bill, and placed
widowed mothers' and orphans' pensions
upon the Statute Book. So earnest was the
learned judge upon this particular question
that, at his own expense, he came to Europe,
and while he visited Europe he had the
privilege, I understand, of addressing Memwas
Widows' Pensions Bill---
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Mr. Dorman]
bers of the House of Lords in a committee
room of the House of Lords in Great
Britain. He also had the privilege-I am
using his own words now of addressing a
meeting of Commoners in a Committee room
of the House of Commons, and I was sufficiently interested in this particular question
from the time that he visited these two
Committee rooms to follow from day to day
very attentively the utterances of all the
leading politicians on the other side of the
water to ascertain if one of them had in
public approved of this scheme of widowed
mothers' and orphans' pensions, and I
looked in vain.
It has been my privilege to contest a seat
in Belfast for the Municipality and the
Poor-Law, to contest three elections, and on
my programme presented to the electors on
each occasion there was included my promise
to support whole-heartedly a Measure of noncontributory widowed mothers' and orphans'
pensions. I wonder how many of the representatives of another place, and how many
of the representatives in this honourable
Senate, can point back and say that their
sympathies and their feelings have been so
touched in the past with regard to this great
question that they have devoted an equal
amount of time and labour to its consideration in order to impress the public with its
importance.
Pensions are not something new in the
British Empire. The study of the pensions
of the British Empire is an intensely interesting study. Up till the period of what we
are so fond of commemorating in Belfastand I believe justified in commemorating in
one respect-up to the period of the great
revolution of 1688, the King, acting on that
old superstition of the Divine right
of kings exploded by John Locke,
disposed of pensions in his own right. They
were given in perpetuity in the form of land,
the rent of which was to constitute the
pension. Some of these pensions did not
cease with the life of the recipients. They
are carried on by the direct or collateral
line, and the community is paying them
to-day. But at the period of that great
revolution Parliament took to itself the
right to regulate State pensions, and from
then till probably the third decade of the
last century pensions were given lavishly
by the politicians across the water.
It is true that pensions had been given
before 1688 for services which I myself will
not sully the records of Hansard by mentioning, but they were given for other
services from 1688 until the third decade of
the last century. The first mention that I
have come across of the urgency and importance of old-age pensions is in the
writings of a working man who lived at
the close of the 18th century. In the eighties
of that century he suggested that when
every working man had come to the age
of fifty his physical powers of producing
the wherewithal of life for himself and
his dependants began to wane, and it was
then obligatory upon the State to step in
and provide him, at least for the fifth decade
of his life, with half his subsistence, and
when he got to the age of sixty he had
then so far declined in his vital powers that
it was only reasonable to expect that he
would be beyond labouring, and the State
should shoulder the obligation of supplying
him with full subsistence in return for the
labour he had given to the State in his
early manhood.
He suggested a Budget with a graduated
Estate duty, whereby it would be possible
to confer at that remote period a full subsistence pension on every working man at
the age of sixty. At that time there was
no such thing as pensions for the police,
pensions for the Army, and pensions for the
Navy, so far as the lower ranks of this
Service were concerned, although there were
pensions for the upper classes of both the
Army and Navy. There were no pensions
for our Civil Servants, for those engaged in
our Revenue, and in our Municipalities, and
the modern outlook was then practically
unknown.
Since then pensions have been
introduced for the Army, the Navy, police,
harbour police, dock police, civil servants,
municipal servants, and all branches of
service given for State welfare. Under
these conditions the State has shouldered its full responsibility to provide
for those who receive the pensions.
I wonder what would be thought by the subordinate officers of the Army and by our
Midshipmen on the quarter deck of our men
o' war, if they were called on from the time
they entered the service of the State to contribute from their income towards a pension
that they might never live to receive, and
that they might never qualify for if they
never got married?
I think I am justified in saying there would
probably be more noise and more disturbance
and more uproar in the State if such a suggestion went forth from the Government of
Great Britain or Northern Ireland to these
particular branches of our services. I believe
the gentlemen occupying these positions
would revolt against such a proposal, but I
beg to remind hon. Members of this House
that the Parliament of Great Britain that
is now introducing this scheme of pensions
has itself compelled proprietary industrial
insurance companies who have entered into
contracts of insurance to provide free and
paid-up policies for the premium payers,
(which conditions were not in the original
contracts), after a certain period in the event
Second Reading. *306
Widows' Pensions Bill-
29 OCTOBER 1925
.


of the premium holders being denied by unfortunate circumstances an opportunity of
continuing the original contracts into which
they had entered. Yet here this very same
Government proposes to introduce a scheme,
not of pensions, but of annuities, contributory annuities, making no provision whatever for any one of the individuals who is
compelled to pay the premium, if through
any circumstances beyond his own control
during his lifetime it is impossible for him
to qualify for a pension.
I am very pleased the noble Marquess has
recognised the inadequacy of this pension.
I took the trouble to 'phone to the Dogs'
Home to inquire what it would cost if I sent
a dog to the home while I was away on holiday, and I was informed it would cost 6s 6d
per week. I asked what it would cost me to
support a cat in the home, and I was told
3s 6d per week. I asked what it would cost
for two, three or four dogs, and I was told
I would have to pay an equal charge for
each. Yet here we are as a deliberate legislative assembly discussing the generosity of
the State in giving 50 per cent. to the
widowed mother of a working man and 50
per cent. to support her child of that which
it would cost to support a dog in the Dogs'
Home in Belfast.
I believe the Government of Great Britain
is instituting what it conceives to be one of
the best electioneering slogans it is possible
to have for the next General Election across
the water, but I am convinced that the education of the people is so progressing that
that slogan will not have the effect the
Government expect. Let me just make a
comparison with what a generous Government is doing in Great Britain and Northern
Ireland to-day with what one of our great
municipalities is suggesting should be done
with its old-age pensioners. It has been
pensioning its servants for many years in
proportion to the salary they were in receipt
of at the time they became qualified for the
pension, and these pensions averaged from
9s 6d per week to 24s per week. 24s was the
maximum pension given to the municipal
= servants of the City of Bradford, and 9s 6d
was the minimum. But the conscience and
feelings of the municipal councillors of
Bradford became, after inquiry, so touched
with the miserable condition of the pensioned
servants that they have come to the conclusion that 15s should be the minimum for
food alone, and consequently they have decided to raise the pension to a minimum of
30s a week, plus a charge for insurance
premiums, plus a charge for rent, plus a
charge for mortgages.
I would remind hon. Senators that before
the war broke out exhaustive inquiry was
made into this particular subject, and it was
discovered at that period that the minimum
we
for a male averaged 35s a week and the minimum for a female averaged 25s a week. Now
hope and expect that old worn-out
workers and widows and orphans will rest
satisfied to be levied from the first day they
go to work with a compulsory insurance
premium for a certain contingency which
they may never live to realise. Hon. Members
in the Imperial and Northern Ireland Parliaments may coat over this Contributory Pensions Bill-this pill, I would call it with a
coating of oratorical and rhetorical sugar,
but that sugar will soon be dissolved and
the bitterness of the pill will be felt by the
people who are imposed upon without their
will or consent being obtained, and who are
compelled to contribute out of what all our
standard economists recognise as a minimum
subsistence wage. I have very great pleasure
in moving this Amendment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The previous speaker seemed very anxious about what
the Press will say of him when they make
a report of his speech. After listening to
him for some time I have been wondering
what he thinks of himself, and what he
thinks the poor widows will think of him
after attempting to destroy a Bill which is
intended to benefit them to such a great
extent. I cannot understand a man who is
supposed to be so interested in the welfare
of the poor and the workers, coming along
with an Amendment, and attempting to destroy the very Bill which, as I said, is likely
to confer such benefit on these people.
think I have as good an opportunity of
knowing the feelings of the workers as any
other persons. There has certainly been
great scrambling amongst a number of these
men for credit for pressing the Government
to introduce this Measure. There is hardly
a crumb left for an individual like myself
who has worked for some 25 years continuously in the interests of the workers in
my spare time gratuitously. I do not want
any credit, but I want to thank the Government, and to say how pleased I am to be
here to-day to give them my support on the
introduction of this Measure.
I
I am perfectly satisfied that the workers
throughout the country will be very thankful.
The workingman's mind will rest to some
little extent content now. He will know
that if he is called away from this earth
there will be some little provision made for
his widow and orphans when he is gone,
and the poor wife will not be looking to the
future with such dread as she has done in the
past, for no one knows when the breadwinner
will be called away. What is to be done for
the wife and children left behind? The lot of
the widow has been very hard in this world.
It has been very difficult. The churches
have endeavoured to do what they could to
307 Widows' Pensions Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
relieve the burden, There are other organisations also giving their assistance to
offer slight relief. I have some knowledge of
some of the organisations I have referred to.
I am a member of two of the committees
that distribute voluntary subscriptions to
widows. I can assure vou that these people
were very thankful when through one organisation they only got £6 per annum to
tide them over the difficult times they were
passing through.
I remember one night an applicant having
a case before one organisation. The case
was one where the husband had been taken
away for an operation, and had died on the
operating table, and the poor woman was left
with five children, the youngest I think only
a few days old. The Board on that particular
night, I remember well, certainly granted
the maximum grant they were able to give,
but they also delved into their pockets to
give assistance to that poor widow. If I have
not been associated with the great judge
from New York, I have been trying to do
something practical to assist these poor
people that have been in such difficult
circumstances. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
I wish these people that are talking so much
about the want and misery of the poor
would endeavour to assist them, and not be
trying to fleece them as much
they
possibly can. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
It must be admitted that these people that I
say were so thankful for £6 per annum to
tide them over their difficulties when they
come to realise the benefits bestowed on them
by this Bill, will be extremely thankful.
as
The amount may not provide luxury and
allow them to be extravagant, but it will
certainly in future provide shelter and
food which
the very necessaries of
life. If the widow is an industrious woman
she can augment that small amount to an
income that would be a comfortable one for
a
are
woman and children. I think SO
far as the amount is concerned there is nothing to be complained of. Then we have to
be very careful not to be extravagant ourselves. We have to be careful not to impose too great a burden on industry and so
ruin the sources of employment, because the
workers that are living, after all, require
something to keep them alive, and they require employment to provide for their
wives and families before their death. So I
say it is necessary for us not to be extravagant in giving these pensions.
A good deal has been said about the contributory system. I do not know whether my
Friend has had any knowledge or experience
of trade union work, but that is one thing
that certainly every candidate that enters
into a trade union has impressed upon him
---that it is a contributory system that he has
entered and undertaken to be a partner in.
I think I remember part of the initatory
address to candidates who entered the A.S.E.,
because I was for a considerable number of
years either reading the address to candidates or listening to it being read. I believe
it ran:-
By small contributions regularly paid
through life the bed of sickness may be
soothed, and old-age rendered comparatively
comfortable, and by a judicious application
of the funds so contributed, the woes of all
are alleviated, and the joys increased by
thus being shared amongst the many by
means of union.
Our Friend the previous speaker asks what
is to become of the man who subscribes all
through life and never becomes entitled to
benefit. That man may consider himself
a lucky individual. He contributes a small
amount to benefit some worse off than
himself in this life.
That was impressed on all those who entered a trade
union. If a man were lucky enough to enjoy
good health, and constant employment, he
very willingly paid his contributions for the
benefit of others less fortunate than himself.
Therefore, I think this contributory system
is entirely on a level with what has been
going on for years and years in the trade
unions. I do not expect our Friend (Mr.
Dorman) to have any knowledge of trade
unions, because he was probably going down
to the sea in ships when the trade unions
were at their best. They have been undoubtedly robbed in recent years of some of
their very great benefits. As a collectivo
bargaining organisation the trade union
was
very good, but it is turning now
to something of a political organisation.
But I think it proves undoubtedly what the
noble Marquess said at the early part of his
statement that the contributory system
served the people of this country better than
any other.
Mr. DORMAN: The working class only.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It is only
the working class that are to be beneficiaries under this Measure. Certainly those
connected with trade unions have become
quite accustomed to it. No man will grumble
about a small sum being deducted from his
wages weekly when he knows that if he is
called away something will be done for his
wife and children. I am indeed very pleased
to be here to be one of those to have a little
to say in the passing of this Act. I am very
glad indeed that the Government are making
such progress. By this Measure they will be
relieving many poor creatures who have been
suffering in silence and hiding their poverty.
In future their lives will be brighter and

st

more pleasant than they have been in the
past. I will give all the support I can to
the passage of this splendid Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I hardly think that it is necessary for me at
the close of this debate to add very much to
what I have already said. I feel that the hon.
Member who has just spoken has made a very
good reply to the remarks which fell from
my hon. Friend (Mr. Dorman), who sits
opposite me. I was thinking while he was
speaking and moving the Amendment standing in his name how very surprised and
taken aback he would be if I rose and, on
behalf of the Government, accepted his
Amendment and adjourned the House. I do
not know exactly what he would say to the
people of Belfast if the Government decided on
his Amendment to proceed no further with
this Bill. I take it that he moved the
Amendment after the Parliamentary manner
of making it a peg on which to hang a speech
in order to put forward the point of view
which he has in his mind. I for one certainly do not quarrel with him. I have no
addoubt that before much time has elapsed we
shall have debates on Socialism and Individualism in this House. It will certainly
give me a certain amount of satisfaction. to
reply. But the hon. Member has made a
speech in which he has partially developed
Socialism in the sense that the State is to
maintain a certain part of the population.
That is what it comes to. He does not tell
us how the State is going to do it. Where
are the funds coming from? He is also desirous, I see, of maintaining individuals
when they arrive at 50 years of age. I am
not sure that the great majoritybutes
at
-4
コ

I
Mr. DORMAN: I did not do that.
quoted a political philosopher who suggested
the advisability of that being done.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
withdraw what I said if it offends the hon.
Member. I will say he quoted philosophy of
the early eighteenth century, that it would
be a good thing if individuals of 50 years
were maintained. If that is the substance
of the suggestion I think the hon. Member
had better consider the record of insurance
companies and he will find that individuals
of fifty, men and women, certainly do not
consider themselves in their dotage at that
age. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I am
speaking personally perhaps. (Laughter.)
They have no desire that a grateful State
should maintain them when they reach the
age of fifty. The hon. Member has opened
up a very wide field in which I do not feel I
should be entitled to follow him.
The system of insurance in this country
has, as I have said, been in existence for
A fifteen years. It is a system which I believe
the great majority of the people of this
country believe in. They like the opporeit

tunity of contributing and subscribing and
thereby encouraging qualities of thrift. The
hon. Member desires something totally
different, and that is, that there should be
no thrift, saving, or insurance contributions.
Mr. DORMAN: I do not like to interrupt,
but-
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member will
have a chance of speaking again.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member seems desirous of eliminating all the attributes of thrift and individual
ism, considering that the State should maintain the population, or a great portion of
them, instead of the population maintaining
the State.
Mr. DORMAN: What I pointed out in my
speech was this that this is a new pensions
scheme, and that hitherto the municipality
or the Government authorities gave pensions
and gave them in a non-contributory scheme.
We
a
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: If
the hon. Member will allow me, I am not
endeavouring to do him any injustice.
differ entirely on fundamentals. He is
Socialist, and I am not. When the hon.
Member refers to pensions being given by the
State in the form of superannuation I would
remind him that those pensions are really
deferred pay. He will find that a great
many servants of the State do not receive
the remuneration they would receive if they
were engaged in a private occupation, for
the simple reason that the pensions they receive from the State for services they have
directly rendered to the State come to them
in the form of deferred pay, which is called
pension. It is a name which covers a multitude of gratuities which people receive. It
is a wde subject the hon. Member entered
into, and I have no desire to follow him except to say that when he speaks of State
pensions in the way he has done he is really
referring to deferred pay, because the remuneration a civil servant would receive
from a private employer is higher than the
remuneration given by the State for similar
work.
The hon. Member went on to make an
unfair sneer about electioneering tactics, and
said this Bill was an attempt to induce
people to vote for the Unionist party at the
next election. I think I might be allowed
to say that the Socialist party has nothing
to learn from any other party in regard to
electioneering tactics. With the exception
of the right hon. Gentleman sitting opposite
me (Colonel Sharman Crawford), I think I
have engaged in as many elections as any
Member of this House, and I can speak with
authority and knowledge as to what both
parties have done in that respect. In introducing a Bill of this description I do not feel
that I need say anything further in regard

to that matter, except that I do join issue
with the hon. Member when he makes an
unfair sneer of that character.
to
With reference to the Bill itself, I feel
there are many points I shall have a better
opportunity of elaborating on the Committee
stage, but I shall be very happy to answer
any question on the Bill which any hon.
Member may be anxious to put
me. I am grateful to the hon. Member
who sits opposite me (Senator Joseph
Cunningham) for the manner in which
he has supported the Bill. He has
put forward the motive and ideas behind the
Bill in far more eloquent language than I
could hope to do. He is one of those who
speak with knowledge and experience, and
I am very grateful to him for the support
he has given to the Measure. The Bill has
been brought forward in what it is hoped
Armstrong, Н. В.
Bangor, The Viscount
Barbour, H. A. Milne
Clark, Sir George S.
is the spirit of the evolution of the times,
which is the principle of insurance, and
bringing before the people themselves the
mutual benefits of insurance to which the
three parties-the employer, the worker, and
the community-contribute.
I do feel that
we shall have made a great step forward when
we place this Bill, as I hope we shall, on
the Statute Book.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before putting the
Question, I desire to point out that in the
form I am putting it those who are in
favour of Senator Dorman's Amendment must
say "Not Content," and those who are opposed to it must say "Content."
Question put: That the words proposed
to be left out stand part of the Question.
