Mr. LESLIE: The Bill which is now before us has been for some time-as I think
the noble Marquess said when speaking on
the other motion-before the House in one
form or another, and it has been more before
the country than before this House. I am
afraid I must confess that I have not yet
been able to make myself as conversant with
all the details of the Measure as I hope to
do before we part with it. I have not seen
the memorandum to which reference was
made by the noble Marquess. I think the
difficulty which at first sight appears to exist
in the consideration of the Bill disappears
to a great extent when it is borne in mind
that the object to be achieved is to graft
on to a non-contributory measure of Old-
Age Pensions a contributory Measure bringing in an old-age pension at an earlier
age. One has to keep that fact in mind
in order to avoid being tripped up by an
apparent repetition in the Bill.
As regards the Bill itself, there are very
divided opinions. There are some who take
an optimistic view and they use the Bill
as a telescope through which they see the
promised land flowing with milk and honey
for widows and orphans, and there is something to be said on their side. On the other
band there is the pessimist, and the Bill
was opposed-although I do not say it is
Massereene and Ferrard, The Viscount
M'Dowell, W. J.
Porter Porter, J.
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
Turner, Sir William
still being opposed by the Ulster Farmers'
Union, by the Belfast Chamber of Commerce,
and apparently by those who describe themselves as Socialists. It is remarked that the
opposition to the Bill from the farmer's
point of view is due to the fact that some
slight addition is to be made to his burden,
very slight, but still an addition, and what
he wants is not a shilling addition to his
burden but sovereigns taken off.
It is also remarked that the burden is a
cumulative burden, because it will increase
not only the cost of production but also the
cost of the raw material used in the production and of everything that is required to
make use of that raw material. Then
we have the financial theorists who
point out that the financial resources of
Great Britain and Northern Ireland are not
in a position to stand an estimated addition
to the National Debt of about £800,000,000.
At the moment, as we know, there is a very
active newspaper campaign being conducted
across the water with a view to urging the
Government of Great Britain to postpone
the operation of the Act of 1925 until some
later date than that of January next, when
the Act will otherwise come into operation.
Amongst all these conflicting opinions it
is perhaps not very easy for us to see what
course we ought to steer, but, fortunately,
from that point of view, at all events, sitting
here as a Second Chamber, the Bill has
313 Widows' Pensions Bill-

29 OCTOBER 1925
Second Reading.
We
been accepted in another place where
the direct responsibility for that part of
the matter rests. I do not think, in spite
of the heavy burden, that we are much concerned. I think we are much more concerned
with the details. We ought to satisfy ourselves that the apportionment is fair.
certainly ought not to consent to the
Measure unless we are satisfied that no undue burden is being placed on the employee,
and I think Senator Joseph Cunningham
agreed that there was not, for he took the
view-if I might perhaps extend his expression a little-that it was the duty of
everybody to provide by some method or other
of insurance for the maintenance of any
widow or orphan he might leave behind. He
- seemed to accept that as a cardinal principle of morality of the working classes. It
is certainly a principle we all agree with,
and it seems to
to dispose of any
objection on the part of the employee.
me
I cannot quite pass from the consideration
of the terms which are to be given to the
contributors without comparing the generous
assistance which the Bill gives them with the
ruthless rapacity that is being shown by
the Chancellors of the Exchequer on both
sides of the water as regards confiscating
the expected fruits of long-standing insurance policies, the whole of those fruits being
swallowed up by increasing death duties.
☐ When dealing with the contributory system
the State is assisting, and I am very glad it
does assist, and that the benefit of those
provisions will remain.
The position of the employer seems to
be a little more difficult, because it is plain
that the employer will have to contribute
the extra penny or twopence. He is not
going to get any direct relief from any other
burden in compensation. Once thinks as a
= possible indirect relief of some reduction in
poor rates. Well, a reduction of this sort,
= if I remember rightly, was adumbrated in
1908, when the first Old Age Pensions Act
was brought forward in Great Britain. I am
afraid it has never materialised. Against
that it is fair to say that the great war
was a very disturbing factor, and it was
scarcely to be anticipated that ordinary
economic expectations would be realised.
- Then, again, there must be a good deal of
inefficient labour still, in the way of people
Over a certain age who are kept on really
more from compassion than because they
are able to earn their full wage. There is
a possibility of relief in that form.
On the whole there will be a distinct
addition to the burden cast upon the em-
-ployer by this Bill. That brings me to the
first real question I want to ask. It is a
- point on which I have not yet been able to
- satisfy myself, that is, the equity of the
relief it is proposed to confer in regard to
existing rates for existing insurances. As I
understand the proposal it is suggested that
the ordinary weekly contribution should be
decreased both as regards Health and (Unemployment Insurance by a small sum, and that
reduction is justified because the claims on
the Health and Unemployment Funds are
expected, and I think properly expected, to
be reduced in future. But at present it is
open, as I understand, to a Health Insurance
Society to give old-age pensions at an earlier
age than 70, as one of the additional benefits
to be secured to the contributors under the
ordinary Health Insurance.
It is very commonly said, as the House
well knows, that the main supporters
of the Health Insurance funds are the agricultural labourers and the domestic servants.
Neither of these classes are, as a rule, very
much afflicted with sickness, and both of
them are fairly long lived. It is safe to say
that they have piled up large Health Insurance funds which are put away, and if these
funds were made use of for this particular
purpose in the way of a reduction of the age
for old-age pensions and other things,
there would be no necessity to ask for so
large an increase in contribution as this Bill
asks for. I do not myself know the real
state of the Health Insurance funds and what
control the Government has over them.
They are not, of course, in the same position
as the Unemployment Fund, as to the position of which we unfortunately know too
much. But I should like information on
their state.
It
Again, I should like to ask whether the
insertion of the date 29th April is deliberate.
The 29th April is-it occurs both in
Clause 3 and Clause 7-as the House
will remember the date on which the
Imperial Bill was introduced into the House
of Commons at Westminster. One would
think at first sight that the day our Bill
was introduced in the House of Commons
here 7th October should be inserted.
occurs to me that for some reason I have not
yet grasped the date of 29th April may be
deliberate and intentional. Then I should
like to ask for rather more explanation as
to what benefit it is possible for an unmarried man of 68 to get under this Bill.
An unmarried man of 68 will in the ordinary
course of things become 70, and therefore be
entitled to a non-contributory old-age pension
before he can possibly get anything under
this Bill, because the 65 pension does not
come into operation before the appointed
day in January, 1928. I think there may be
some answer to that.
I come now to what I think is one of the
principal difficulties under this Bill, that is,
the case of illegitimate children. I approach
Unemployment Insurance Bill-316
Widows' Pensions Bill-
THB SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that question with very great diffidence,
partly because we have to keep in mind if
we should attempt to deal with any more
than the Bill itself deals with-we have to
keep in mind the necessity for not infringing the prerogatives of those sitting elsewhere. I do not think any difficulties are
likely to be suggested here. I do not think we
should be going further than what has been
done across the water, and accepted on the
ground that the amendments carried out the
wishes of the Commons or made clear this or
that the basis of which was no doubt in their
minds. There is that difficulty. There is
the further difficulty that our Bill follows
exactly in these matters the lines of the
Imperial Bill that has passed in the Imperial House. There is the final difficulty in
dealing with illegitimate children, and the
extreme difficulty of making sure that you
are dealing with orphans. You have to make
sure that both the parents are dead. I think
that difficulty was alluded to elsewhere, and
I think that the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Labour, dealt with that question
of being sure that you were dealing with
orphans.
I
am, myself, most anxious to avoid
making such a suggestion as that we should
give a pension to the widow of an unmarried
man or an allowance to an unborn child. I
think something remarkably like both of
these suggestions has already been made. I
am inclined to think at the moment that the
difficulty of ascertaining the orphanhood of
illegitimate children may perhaps be exaggerated, and it may not perhaps be impossible to make an alteration providing for
one class of child which might perhaps be
accidently excluded. If it is at all possible,
I should propose to try to do something on
the Committee Stage. There will arise at
that stage other small matters, none of them
of very great importance-but there will be
trifles that will call for a few moments consideration, and that consideration they, no
doubt, will receive. I conclude I am correct
in supposing we shall not be taking the
Committee Stage before next week, and time
will be given in which one can draw attention to these small matters I have referred
to.
Speaking again of the Bill as a whole, in
spite of the imposition on the employer and
on the employee, and in spite of the drag
upon our funds, I am convinced that the
opposition to the Bill will disappear, and
our people, as a whole, will welcome the
Measure and will endeavour to make it work.
I believe myself that this Bill will effect an
improvement in our social system, and I
have great pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
rose.
Mr. SPEAKER: The Noble Marquess cannot speak again without the concurrence of
the House.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
We have come back to the main question.
Mr. BARBOUR: I suggest that the Leader
of the House be permitted to speak with the
concurrence of the House.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I was only intending to tell the hon. Member
that it is proposed that the Committee Stage
should be taken one day next week.
The hon. Member asked about the 29th of
April. The reason that date has been put
into the Bill is in view of the reciprocal
arrangements which it is proposed to effect
with Great Britain. The other question
which he asked me was, I think, in reference
to a man of 68. He would not come under the
Bill because it does not come into force till
1928. The insurance contributions are being
reduced by one penny, in view of stopping
benefits under the Bill at 65. The approved
societies may give superannuation allowances
out of surplus funds. It is not being done
to any great extent now owing to the cost.
On Question, Motion (That this Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the whole
House, the House to be in Committee on
Tuesday.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: In
rising to move the Second Reading of the
Unemployment Insurance Bill, I would remind the House that prior to the summer
adjournment, in June last, we passed the
Unemployment Temporary Provisions Act,
which gave power to the Governor to apply
by Order in Council so much as he thought
fit of the provisions contained in the Unemployment Insurance scheme which was
ponding in the Imperial Parliament. We did
that in order to maintain the principle which
has over and over again secured the approval
of Parliament that our legislation for Unemployment Insurance should be kept on
parallel lines with that in Great Britain.
Upon that principle I do not propose to
argue further to-day. The necessity for it
has been repeatedly acknowledged on all
sides.
The Governor, by Order in Council dated
the 27th August last, in the exercise of his
317 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 29 OCTOBER 1925
Second Reading.
コ

r
S
powers under the Act to which I have referred, applied to Northern Ireland those
provisions of the Act recently passed by the
Imperial Parliament which related to the
power of the Minister of Labour to waive the
first statutory condition, to the extension of
the waiting period and to the relief of Trades
Unions. The Order in Council made these
provisions effective on and after the 1st of
October, in this year.
The Bill which is before the House to-day,
in accordance with the terms of the Unemployment Temporary Provisions Act, to
which I have been referring, embodies those
provisions of the Unemployment Insurance
Act, 1925, of the Imperial Parliament, which,
as I have said, were applied by the Order in
Council of the 27th August. That is to say,
it gives power to the Minister, first to waive
the first statutory condition up to the 30th
June, 1926. That first statutory condition
made it necessary for a claimant to prove
that not less than thirty contributions had
been paid in respect of him since the beginning of the first of the two insurance years
immediately proceeding the beginning of the
benefit year in which the application for
benefit was made. The Minister of Labour
had power to waive this condition from the
commencement of the Unemployment Insurance Act, 1924 (No. 2), until the first day of
October, 1925. We are continuing that power
How.
In the second place, the Bill provides for
the restoration of the "six days" waiting
period before benefit becomes payable.
In the third place, it provides relief to the
Trades Unions from making greater provision for unemployment out of their own
funds than would have been required under
the Unemployment Insurance Act of 1920 as
originally enacted, namely, a maximum in
the case of a man of 5s per week for 15 weeks
in any insurance year. So much for the
provisions already applied by Order in
Council.
Over and above this, the Bill embodies
those provisions of the Unemployment Insurance Act, 1925, of the Imperial Parliament which becomes effective on the 4th
January, 1926. That is to say, it provides,
under Clause 3, for the revision in the rates
of contribution in accordance with the rates
laid down in the first and second schedules.
Under the existing Acts the contributions
in respect of a man stand at 10d from the
employer, 9d from the worker, and 6åd from
the Exchequer. They were intended to continue until the debt on the Unemployment
Fund had been cleared, when they were to
be reduced to 6d from employer, worker,
and Exchequer alike. For the sake of sim-
-plicity I am taking the rates of contribution
for men.
lower.
Those for women and juveniles are
As contributions will become payable from
January, 1926, under the new Contributory
Pensions Scheme, it is proposed to reduce
both Health and Unemployment Insurance
contributions at the same time. We are taking
off 2d from the Unemployment contribution
and d fom the Health contribution as a
partial set-off against the 4d per week payable by employer and worker under the
Pensions Scheme. The result is that the net
increase for persons covered by all three
Insurance Schemes-Unemployment, Health
and Pensions-is 2d per week each for employer and worker. In order to make up for
the reduction in Unemployment Insurance
contributions from these two sources, there
will be a fixed increase in the Exchequer
contributions in respect of a man from 62d
to Sd from the 5th of April 1926, with a
further provision that, if the debt on the
Unemployment Fund in any quarter exceeds
the debt on the 31st December, 1925, the Exchequer will contribute an additional penny
per contribution during that quarter.
Further, if the debt on the Unemployment
Fund falls below the amount of the debt at
the 31st December, 1925 and appears to the
Minister of Finance and the Minister of
Labour likely to remain below that figure
the contribution from the employer will be
further reduced a penny, namely, to 7d,
which is the same rate as that payable by
the worker. I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to protest against
the two principal provisions of this Bill. In
the first place I think it is unreasonable and
unjust to extend the waiting period from
three days to six. We all recognise that
human life cannot be continued without the
necessaries of life. An unemployed man is
just as much in need of food during the six
waiting days as he is when working, and
this provision will inflict a very serious injustice upon the lowly paid workers, and not
only on the workers but on a large proportion
of those who are dependent on the custom of
the workers as distributors of the necessaries
of life from day to day.
The worker who is compelled to have three
days added on to his present waiting period
must of necessity incur a debt if he is going
to provide the necessaries of life for his
family. That again will re-act upon the
worker, inasmuch as it is a well-known commercial practice that when a distributor of
commodities has to wait for the repayment
of a debt he is compelled to put up the price
of his goods in order to cover that particular
risk. I was surprised in reading the newspaper yesterday to find that the Royal
Humane Society is conferring its medal
upon three miners who had risked their
319 Unemployment Insurance Bill. THE SENATE
Old-Age Pensions Bill. 320
[Mr. Dorman]
lives for a period of a week in swimming
through a mine to convey food to a pit pony
that was confined in the end of a cutting.
Twice or three times a day three men swam,
at the risk of their lives, to bring the
necessary food to prolong the life of the pit
pony, and here we are removing the low and
miserable means of life afforded by the Unemployment Insurance Act to perhaps
thousands of working people.
I also wish to protest against the inquisition this Bill is going to place in the hands
of the Minister of Labour. I think it is unreasonable to expect the heads of a family to
be subject to the inquisition of their own
children as to what their total earnings are
from week to week. I would not like my
boy or girl of eighteen years of age who was
seeking extended benefit under the Insurance
Act to come to me from week to week and
compel me to give him or her exact information as to what my earnings were at that
particular time. I would like to draw
attention to this aspect of that question. If
a criminal action was brought for a false
statement in that connection, whom is the
law going to hold responsible? Assuming the
parent refuses to give that necessary information to the child, and that child inadvertently gives wrong information to the Ministry, and becomes thereby liable to a criminal
action, is the father to be made responsible
for refusing to give information to his child?
I think these two principal features of the
Bill are very unjust, and will press to a
grievous extent on the unemployed in their
present precarious condition.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am sure
we all regret very much that these Unemployment Bills are continually being presented to this House. We have been hoping
for an improvement in trade and for better
times for the workers, but unfortunately
that time has not yet arrived. The Minister
of Labour is cutting down expenditure to
the lowest limit, and while there are some
cases in which such economy is justified, I
think the Ministry should, as far as it is
able, show some sympathy in those cases
where the husband has a large family.
have had several very hard cases brought
to my notice. I have heard of the soulless
Civil Service, and I do hope they will
show as much sympathy and charity as possible to those who deserve it in the administration of this Bill.
I
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
These Bills which deal with unemployment
are always Bills in which we naturally take
the deepest interest. It is very difficult for
a Minister to appear to take up any harsh
attitude when his sympathies are probably
as well developed as those of any Member of
this House. The hon. Members who have
spoken have raised important points, but I
suggest they are points which are more
appropriate to the Committee Stage of this
Bill.
As hon. Members know, we are under a
disability in this Chamber in regard to
finance, because we cannot insert any Amendment in a Bill which increases the charge to
the public. At the same time I feel that the
points that have been raised are points of
a certain amount of substance which can
best be dealt with on the Committee Stage.
The hon. Member who has just left the
House (Mr. Dorman) referred to the waiting
period, and naturally there is some substance in the arguments he put forward, but,
in reverting to the six days' waiting period,
it is believed a man is better able to stand
a gap of six days at the beginning of a period
of unemployment than he is after a prolonged period without work.
Hon. Members are aware that the gap is
a question of means. We know quite well
that in connection with the granting of unemployment insurance benefit the great mass
of the workers are doing their part honourably and honestly. We all recognise that,
but there are others, and when legislation
has to be enacted, it covers the wrong-doers
as well as the honest. But I do feel that
the question of the gap is an important one.
There is also the question of short time. I
am capable of giving instances where men
have earned a very good wage in the first
three days of the week and have received unemployment benefit for the other three days.
That is something that is not contemplated
by any of us or in any Act of Parliament,
and these things only happen in very isolated
cases. But they do happen, and I believe
it is the duty of everyone to see that anomalies of this description are rectified.
With regard to the other points raised, I
shall be very happy to discuss them on the
Committee Stage, and perhaps hon. Members
will put them down in the form of Amendments.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for Monday next.
Old Age Pensions Bill.

Ferrard.)
Mr. LESLIE: I rise only for a moment to say
I regret I was unable to be here yesterday,
and so I was unable to hear what the noble
Viscount said in response to the question 1
Old-Age Pensions Bill.

raised on the Second Reading. I understand

words in the First column of the Schedule to

ordinary person would certainly much prefer
to get the explanation in the form of a leaflet
rather than from an amendment to an Act of
Parliament.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Senator is correct. It is
proposed to issue an explanatory leaflet which
these people may read and not have to delve
into Acts of Parliament. I only regret that
when I was making this statement in the
House the hon. Senator did not find it convenient to be in the House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.--
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

And a Message was sent to the House of

agree to the said Bill without amendment.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
It is suggested, if it meets the convenience
of the House, that we should meet again next
Monday.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Could
the noble Marquess give us a little bit more
information beyond Monday, as to how this
House will be occupied next week?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: On
Monday the First Reading of the Drainage
Bill, the Committee Stage of the Widows',
and Orphans' and Old Age Contributory Bill
will be taken. Then there is the Committee
Stage of the Unemployment Insurance Bill,
and one or two other Measures which will
be set out in the Whip as soon as it can
possibly be done.
Mr. LESLIE: Must we have the Committee
Stage of the Widows' and Orphans' Bill on
Monday? It does not give us much chance
of putting amendments down.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: If
it meets with the convenience of the House
we will take it on Tuesday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes after Five of the
Clock.

Handling of Cattle in Transit.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Barbour:-
To ask the Leader of the House if he can
hold out any hope that the Government
will attempt to effect improvements in the
handling of cattle in transit from the farms
in Ireland to the ports in England.
Mr. BARBOUR: In asking the Question
that stands in my name as to whether the
Government can hold out any hope of an
improvement in the handling of cattle in
transit from Ireland to England and Scotland-I quite realise that I am calling attention to Regulations that come under the
Imperial authority, but at the same time I
do not think that entirely frees our Government from bringing its influence to bear to
try to remedy a state of affairs which a great
many people regard as detrimental to the important interest concerned. We have had
evidence of the farmers' organisations having called attention to this matter. The
Ulster Agricultural Organisation Society has
commented upon it, and the Ulster Farmers'
Union has also called attention to it. We
have had letters in the Press from market
salesmen and from individual farmers, and I
think those of us who know the individual
farmer will realise that when he takes to
voicing his grievance in the paper it is a
grievance that he really feels and he only
gives expression to his grievance in that way
after having suffered a considerable time.
I maintain that the present condition of
affairs keeps our cattle at the bottom of the
market, and any business man will realise
there is always a crowd at the bottom of the
market. I think our Government and the
Imperial Government should do everything
they possibly can to open an access to the
top of the market for those of our farmers
who export cattle to the other side. From
investigations that have been made we have
had complaints that not infrequently in-calf
heifers are shipped which should not be
shipped. I am ready to admit that there is
a good deal of difficulty in connection with
that, but it is not such as should warrant
us believing that an improvement cannot be
made.
We have had a statement from a tanner in
England who conducts a very high-class
trade, and when asked his opinion about
Irish hides he said he would not touch them
because they were so badly damaged that
they were not satisfactory raw material for
his business. There is a direct bar to hides
from Ireland getting to the top of the
market. Then we come to the cattle purchaser across the water. I have a statement, for which I can give authority, that
on one occasion in Glasgow not very long
-
!


325 Handling of Cattle in Transit. 2 NOVEMBER 1925 Handling of Cattle in Transit. 326
ago, after a calm night, a thousand cattle
were offered in the market, and this salesman gave it as his opinion that each of
those cattle had deteriorated in value to the
extent of £4 on account of the treatment it
had received in crossing the channel and
in being put on the boat.
I can only take that as a liability of £4
per head that every farmer is subject to
with regard to every beast he ships across
the water. This is our big market, and it
is deplorable that in these times of great
trade depression when there is a business
with a normal flow of trade any effort should
be spared that would tend to decrease
that liability of £4 per head of cattle
which I maintain any farmer has to
stand in the shipment of his cattle.
Remember that on this particular occasion it
was a calm night at sea. Last week there
was a sad decrease in the number of cattle
shipped across the water. We shipped some
4,000 head of cattle and depreciation at the
rate of £4 per head means a loss of £16,000.
There is no doubt that the purchaser of the
cattle in Northern Ireland very carefully
allows for that possible depreciation in the
amount he pays the farmer for the cattle.
I think there must be room for decreasing
that possibility of such a very heavy depreciation.
I remember in the old days of the Department of Agriculture with what pride the
fact was announced that owing to the activities of the Department they felt justified
in stating they had raised the average value
of cattle in Ireland about £1 per head. How
fruitless and abortive all those efforts are
when we know that every beast that is shipped has to pass through a net, and in passing
through that net there is a liability to depreciation of four times the amount which
all the labour of the Ministry of Agriculture
had been able to secure as an increase on
what they found before they commenced to
function.
I share with the English people and the
Scotch people that pride in the reputation we
enjoy, not without justification, for always
showing a full measure of humanity towards
animals which we have to handle. But I hate
living in a fool's paradise, and I think it
is a blur on our escutcheon to allow this
traffic to be conducted in this way when we
know there is cruelty, a great deal of which
could be avoided if proper study and care
and attention were given to the matter. I am
not asking this question in any vexatious
spirit. I feel too serious about it. If business men in the linen trade were to feel they
were liable to depreciation at this rate they
would be able to bring such pressure to bear
that a remedy would be quickly found, and
it would be quickly applied. I think the
farmer, who is somewhat less articulate than
the merchant, feels there is an injustice
being done because in his case remedies are
not found, whereas were the same conditions
to exist in other activities I feel sure a
remedy would be found.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Would
I be in order in making a few remarks now,
or after the Question has been answered?
Mr. SPEAKER: You had better make
them now.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I agree
with every word Senator Barbour has said.
A great many people not directly connected
with the cattle trade seem to think that the
whole of the cruelty chiefly arises through
the action of the drovers in beating the unfortunate cattle. I think the far more important thing if this question is going to be
considered is what takes place on board the
steamers that carry these cattle. I have had
experience on long voyages of cattle
coming home from foreign ports where the
cattle were housed most comfortably, and,
as far as foresight could go, saved from injury. I do not know whether any of you
going on cross-Channel boats were free from
sea sickness and able to go down to see what
the cattle were undergoing. They are
packed like herrings, and tied. If they slip
down there is no one to get them up again.
More gross cruelty takes place aboard the
steamers than takes place before they are
put on board, and this might be avoided. I
think they should see that accommodation is
provided on board the steamer because I believe that more than half the damage takes
place on the steamer.
my
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
feel that no one will quarrel with the eloquent and forcible manner in which this
question has been put forward by my two
hon. Friends, and I regret I have come down
to the House with only the answer given to
me by the Department. If I had realised
this would develop into a debate I should
have come with more material at my disposal. I know hon. Members will realise that
the Department of Agriculture is not
Department, and I am only the mouthpiece
for it in this House. Hon. Members have
raised the question on two grounds. The
right hon. Gentleman (Colonel Sharman-
Crawford) who has just spoken, based it
mainly on the ground of humanity, and my
hon. Friend (Mr. Barbour), who spoke first,
based it on two grounds, humanity and the
material value which is lost to the farmers
of this country by the handling of the cattle
shipped from this country to other parts.
We all naturally recognise, and I feel it
is something we should reiterate as often
as possible, that agriculture is the main industry of Northern Ireland and should be
Business of the House.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
considered in that way in regard to all its
bearings. The answer which I have for the
hon. Member is the same as he began his
speech with, which is that the Government
of Northern Ireland has no statutory powers
in regard to the transit of cattle, but my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Agriculture, has already explained in another place.
It was in answer to one of the hon. Members
(Mrs. Chichester) he has explained to Mrs.
Chichester that he has been in consultation
with the Imperial Authorities on this important matter, and in that way these
Authorities are devoting their persistent attention to the subject, and I am hopeful that,
given the thorough co-operation of the other
interests concerned, the necessary improvements will be achieved.
I feel sure the hon. Member will just look
at that as the stereotyped official answer
given by the Government, but I can assure
him that the right hon. Gentleman who is
the Minister of Agriculture in this country
is fully alive to this difficulty and is working with his accustomed energy. I will go
further and I will say I will take the matter
up with him myself after the speeches I
have heard from the two hon. Members. If
it is necessary I will endeavour to hasten
on the wheels of the office in the direction
in which the two hon. Members are desirous
they should move.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
desire to move that this House do now adjourn. I think perhaps hon. Members would
like to know what the programme is which
we have before us. We have now received
the Drainage Bill and this House has given
it a First Reading. It is proposed that the
Second Reading should be debated to-morrow,
Tuesday. On Tuesday also will be taken the
Third Reading of the Unemployment Insurance Bill. On Wednesday there will be the
Third Reading of the Widows, Orphans,
and Old-Age Contributory Bill, and it is
proposed we should take the Committee Stage
of the Drainage Bill on that day, but as regards that point I am willing to leave myself in the hands of the House. If we have
the Second Reading on Tuesday it may seem
a short time to have the Committee Stage
on Wednesday. It will be more convenient
for us to have the Committee Stage on
Wednesday, nevertheless I am open to representations from the hon. Members. On Thurs
day we will take the Report and Third Reading. I beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I suppose it is ignorance on my part, but what
is the meaning of "appointed" which appears on the Notice Paper? It is at the
bottom of the page below Mr. Barbour's
question.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In answer to the right hon. and
gallant Member a "Bill Appointed" means
that a Bill is put down to be discussed or
appointed for a certain day, but it does not
necessarily mean it will be taken.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Is it a
usual thing to put on the Paper?
Mr. LESLIE: In the matter of the Drainage Bill I am rather inclined to suggest that
perhaps the Government might postpone
their decision until the Second Reading has
been taken. I think the question of Amendments being put down and what these
Amendments will be is likely to depend a
good deal on the course of the debate on the
Second Reading. It is really a short time
to put down Amendments for a rather
serious Bill of that class, not so much on account of the time occupied in drafting
Amendments as in respect of the time which
is occupied in thinking out these Amendments. If we take the Second Reading of
an important Bill one day and the Committee
Stage the next I think the interval is
scarcely long enough to enable hon. Senators
to consider these points. The Drainage Bill
is not one of which I myself have any particular knowledge, but I suggest the Government might leave the other question open
until the conclusion of the Second Reading
debate, and then perhaps the Government can
tell us on what day they propose to take it.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I think my hon. Friend has made a very
reasonable suggestion. I do not know
whether he means that the length of time
between the various stages is responsible for
the number of Amendments, but I certainly
agree with him that if we find the Bill is a
more important or less important matter we
can discuss when the Committee Stage will
be taken on the adjournment.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes before
Four of the Clock.
Widows' Pensions Bill-
Committee.
3 NOVEMBER 1925


THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 3rd November, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Private Business.
WRIGHT'S DIVORCE BILL.
Amendment made by the Joint Committee
agreed to.
Widows' and Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The Marquess of DUFFERIN AND AVA
in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Contributory pensions for
widows, orphans, and persons between the
ages of 65 and 70) ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Meaning of "insured").
2.-(1) In this Act, unless the context
otherwise requires, the expression "insured" means insured under the National
Health Insurance Act, 1924 (in this Act
referred to as "the Insurance Act") or
the Acts repealed by that Act, and in
relation to any person who is deemed to
be insured for the purposes of this Act,
includes such a person; and the expression
"insurance in relation to a person so
deemed to be insured shall be construed
accordingly.
دو
(2) For the purposes of this Act, but
not for any other purpose, a person insured
under the Insurance Act shall be deemed
to continue to be so insured during any
period during which he is by virtue of
sub-section (4) of section twelve of the
Insurance Act entitled to sanatorium
benefit.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 2,
page 2, lines 39 and 40, to leave out “sub-
Section (4) of Section twelve of." This is
the first of the Amendments which I have
I
the honour of bringing before the House
this afternoon. I rather regret to find that
there are no Amendments on the Paper
except those standing in my own name.
should like to take this opportunity of
saying that, as I think hon. Senators will
observe by reading the Paper, the great
majority of these Amendments have been
put down rather with the view of calling
attention to and obtaining an explanation
of the various points of difficulty than for
any other reason. But this first Amendment
is an Amendment to which I think some
importance should be attached. It seems to
me that this Bill is likely to be read by
a good many people-people particularly who
give the Departments concerned their assistance by serving on Insurance Committees
and the like, and I think it is our duty
to make the wording of the Bill as simple as
may be possible. I think that all we are called
upon to do in this case is to put any
genuine seeker after information on the
broad highway of the Insurance Act, and
we are not called upon to take him up the
somewhat muddy lanes of sub-section (4) of
Section 12, which will only bring him up
against a dead wall.
The history of this particular matter is
that sub-section (4) of Section 12 deals primarily with medical benefit. Medical benefit
under the Insurance Act does not apply to
Northern Ireland. There is a special set of
sections in the Insurance Act which do apply
to Northern Ireland. When you get to
Section 120 you find yourself referred to the
Sixth Schedule and when you get to the
Sixth Schedule of the Insurance Act you find
that sub-Section (4) of Section 12 has to be
read as though the words "sanatorium benefit" were inserted there. This special Clause
deals with sanatorium benefit. If a person
wants information about sanatorium benefit
he may not unreasonably be expected to consult the index of the Statute Book. If you
turn up sanatorium benefit in the index you
will not find yourself referred to sub-section
(4) of Section 120. I, therefore, suggest that in
the interests of clearness we should leave
out these words, for I maintain we want our
Acts of Parliament to be as plain as they
can possibly be made. It is not easy to get
an Act of Parliament expressed in proper
terms understood by anybody. I do not for
a
moment suggest that the reference is
wrong, but I do suggest that it is not clear.
I think we want an Act of Parliament as
plain as it can be.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Senator has looked far but I am
not sure that he has looked quite far enough.
We think and maintain that these words are
necessary, having regard to the interpretation Clause, page 45, lines 23 to 28, Section
120, sub-Section 6 of the Insurance Act,
Widows' Pensions Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
which deals with sanatorium benefit, which
says sanatorium benefit may be extended to
dependents of insured persons. It is not desired to regard such persons as insured persons for the purposes of this Bill. I think
if the hon. Member will take these matters
into consideration he will see that the words
are necessary for the construction of the
Bill, having relation to other Clauses, which
come later on. I regret we cannot accept
the hon. Member's Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: That being so there is nothing for me but to ask the House for permission to withdraw it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Widows' pensions).
3.-(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, the widow of a man who dies after
the commencement of this Act and who is
insured at the date of his death but has
not attained the age of seventy before the
commencement of this Act shall, if the
statutory conditions hereinafter mentioned
are complied with, be entitled to a widow's
pension:
Provided that the widow of a man who had
attained the age of sixty at the date of the
marriage shall not, if the marriage takes
place after the twenty-ninth day of April,
nineteen hundred and twenty-five, be entitled to a widow's pension, unless either-
(a) there are or have been one or more
children of the marriage; or
(b) at the date of the death of her husband three years or more have elapsed
since the date of the marriage; or
(c) she was immediately before the marriage in receipt of a widow's pension.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 3, lines 15 and 16 to leave out "twentyninth day of April" and to insert "seventh
day of October." This is the question of the
29th day of April, which the Committee will
remember I raised on the Second Reading,
and if I understand him correctly the noble
Marquess told us it would be necessary to
maintain the date 29th April in the Bill for
the sake of reciprocal arrangements made
with Great Britain. The 29th April was the
day on which the Imperial Bill was introduced. The 7th October was the day on
which our Bill was introduced in the Northern Parliament. There is some little point
in taking our own date if we can, because
it is just possible there may be some case of
hardship by going back to the 29th April,
which was before any announcement was
publicly made in Northern Ireland that it
was intended to apply the provisions of the
Imperial Act to Northern Ireland. If on
further consideration the Government still
think it is practically convenient to maintain April I imagine the House would not be
disposed to disagree. Cases of hardship
would obviously be very few, and if this
Amendment should be accepted and the date
altered it would be necessary on Report to
make Amendments in some other Clauses.
I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
think the hon. Member himself has replied to
his own Amendment. The date in the British
Act, which is in Clause 3, is 28th April,
and I feel justified in saying that in view of
the repeated statements which I myself have
invariably made, and my colleagues also, that
in matters of social legislation Northern Ireland should proceed on parallel lines with
Great Britain it might fairly be assumed
that when this Act was introduced in the
British House of Commons it would necessarily follow as a corollary that the same
legislation would be introduced in Northern
Ireland. Therefore for the purpose of reciprocal arrangements with Great Britain it
will be convenient to maintain the same date.
The hon. Member thinks there might be some
few cases of hardship. I think cases of hardship should not arise in view of the fact that
it has been universally understood that this
Act would be introduced in Northern Ireland
in pursuance of the principle we have maintained of remaining on parallel lines with
social legislation in Great Britain. I am
sorry we cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw it.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3, page
3, line 18, to leave out paragraph (a) and insert "a child of the marriage has been born
alive; or." The proposal I am making as regards the alteration of line 18 is one which it
may be desirable to make, not that I think
it would make any change in effect if the Bill
becomes an Act, for I think it will be maintained that the child in this connection
means the child who has been born alive, but
at the same time I am inclined to think it
might possibly be a matter of dispute. I
think we would perhaps be well advised to
follow the precedent of our Affiliation Bill
where we expressly limit our proceedings to
cases of children born alive. Accordingly 1
suggest that instead of the words set out we
should say "a child of the marriage has been
born alive."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The words which my hon. Friend proposes to
insert, I submit, would limit the operation
of the Clause in a way which is not intended.
I may return to the argument which I have
used in the previous Amendment with reference to reciprocal arrangements which will
be made with Great Britain, and I think it
is desirable to avoid complications which I
feel might arise if we had a different wording
in connection with this Clause.

:
ゴ
ment of this Act and is insured at the
date of his death and has not attained
the age of seventy before the commencement of this Act;
The object of the Clause, as hon. Members
are aware, is to secure that pensions will only
be paid as a result of a marriage taking place
after the man has reached the age of 60 years
where certain conditions have been fulfilled
which would show that the marriage is a
bona-fide one and has not been arranged for
the purpose of this Act. I feel that the words
which the hon. Member is desirous of inserting would limit the operation of the Clause
in a way which is not intended under the
Bill. I think the hon. Member will admit
that his Amendment is not really one of
great substance. The arguments he has put
forward are fully met in the Clause and
I suggest he should not proceed with his
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I am left somewhat in doubt as to whether the intention of
the paragraph is that a widow should be entitled to a pension in respect of a stillborn child. I took it that the meaning of
the paragraph is not so.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The birth of a child, whether still-born or
living, would probably be sufficient proof of
the bona-fides of the marriage. I think that
answers the hon. Member's question.
Mr. LESLIE: My question is very distinctly answered, and I shall be only too
anxious to withdraw the Amendment.
takes the Bill much further than I anticipated.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
It
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3,
line 21, to leave out the words, "or more"
(years or more). I cannot see any object in
inserting these two words, and as the Bill
will have to be printed in leaflet form and
in the Statute Book I suggest they should be
left out. I do not think these words can
possibly lead anywhere.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
If these words were omitted it might be
argued that at the date of the husband's
death three years precisely and not more
should have elapsed. I suggest the hon.
Member should withdraw the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Certainly.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause 3 ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Orphans Pensions).
(1) Subject to the provisions of this Act,
an orphan's pension shall, if the statutory
conditions hereinafter mentioned are complied with, be payable in respect of the
orphan child-
(a) of a man who, being a married man
or a widower, dies after the commence-
(b) of a widow who dies after the commencement of this Act and is insured at
the date of her death.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 4,
page 4, line 1, to leave out from "who" to
"dies" in line 2 ("being a married man or
a
widower'). This is the most important of the Amendments on the Paper. It
is directed, of course, to the case of the
illegitimate child. It has to be admitted at
once that the fact that Parliament is recognising the illegitimate child at all in this
connection is a great act of generosity on the
part of Parliament. As Parliament has
recognised the interests of the illegitimate
child, it seems to me that certain other considerations follow. Parliament, while giving
that recognition, does not wish to give a
pension in respect of a child, unless the
paternity of the child can be reasonably
established, and Parliament does not intend
to do anything to diminish the incidental
attractions of matrimony. But having gone
so far as to recognise the illegitimate child,
I think we must assume it is the intention of
Parliament to act reasonably in the matter,
and not to perpetrate an absurdity of any
kind.
We have now to turn from this particular
paragraph to Clause 44 of our Bill, where
we get a definition of the word child, and I
think it is perhaps convenient to deal with
the Amendment I have on the Paper at that
stage, because it is scarcelý possible if one
has to endeavour to satisfy the conditions I
have laid down to make any alteration here,
unless one makes an alteration in the definition Clause which will meet the word child,
in order to meet a case of possible lack of
security as regards the paternity of a child,
and so on
The Bill seeks to lay down in Clause 44,
two tests as regards the child. It lays down
a test that it shall be the man's child, and
also that it must be resident with him at
the time of his death. My Amendment at
that point is directed to assist in defining
the word "his" as regards the father, and
towards substituting the maintenance of the
child for residence, because I think the maintenance of the child is likely to be a truer
test of paternity than the mere fact that at
the material date, the death of the man, the
child was living in the father's house. Hon.
Senators will readily appreciate the possibility of bringing a child into a house just
when it happens to be wanted. It can very
easily be done.
Turning from that back to the Amendment
immediately before us, a pension is proposed
to be given to the orphan child, if illegiti-
Committee.
Widows' Pensions Bill-

THE SENATE.
[Mr. Leslie]
mate of course, of a man, being a married
man or a widower, that is to say, if a man is
a married man, and if he can satisfy the
other requirements of the Bill, you are going
to provide for his illegitimate children if any,
also if he is a widower the illegitimate child
is to be provided for, but if the man has never
gone though a form of marriage, if for instance he has taken some one of his choice
and lived with that woman exclusively for
ten or twenty years and children have been
born of that union, which although to all
outward appearances
regular union, is
lacking in the actual marriage tie, the
illegitimate child is not provided for under
the Bill.
a
as
to
1 can only characterise that as an absurd
thing. You make the whole test
whether an insured man has an illegitimate
child or not depend on whether that man has
at some time or other gone through the form
of marriage with someone, not necessarily the
mother of the child. I do not think that
can be the intention of Parliament, but it
seems to me to be what the Bill says, and I
think it ought to be altered. For the sake
of protection we ought to make sure, as I
suggest in the Amendment, that the child is
the child of the man concerned, and I suggest
on the Paper various ways of doing that.
I do not think I need go further into that
for the moment, but I do suggest that we
ought to give full weight to a man's insurance irrespective of whether he has been
married or not, and I do not think we ought
to lay undue stress on the fact of the child
living with the man at the time of his decease, because if you make living the test we
shall only land ourselves in difficulties. I
cannot think it can be the will or intention
of the Bill to exclude one class of illegitimate
children simply because they are the children
of a union not sanctified. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF
LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member has moved an Amendment
which touches a matter of very great importance, but I regret that I am unable to accept
the Amendment. The hon. Senator spoke of
diminishing the attractions of marriage, but
I am afraid that if his Amendment was carried that is the effect it would have. I am
not going to say that the proposals in the
Bill deal satisfactorily with a very difficult
problem-the problem of the illegitimate
children. The effort to provide for the illegitimate children of an unmarried man is, in
the Amendment suggested by the hon. Member, surrounded with tests which would make
the administration very complex indeed. It
departs from the principle embodied in the
Bill-that there will be no pensions apart
from the marriage of the parent. Moreover,
it would exclude from title to benefit certain
children, who, having regard to the subsequent marriage of the parent, it is considered
should not be excluded. Those are the main
reasons on which, on behalf of the Government, I have to oppose the Amendment.
I think the hon. Member will realise that
it is necessary, in order to save administrative costs, that some simple formula should be
adopted for determining in what cases pensions or allowances shall be paid to illegitimate children. I am now dealing with
the Amendment the hon. Member has put
down in Clause 44, line 16, and it is submitted
that Sub-Section 1 of Clause 44 contains a
formula which, while being simple, does substantiative justice without weakening the attractions of marriage. In order that nothing
may be done which would tend to destroy the
marriage tie the Bill provides no pensions
or allowances unless one or other of the
parents marries. Illegitimate children become
possible beneficiaries if they are brought
within the home set up on the marriage of
either of the parents, which is a state of
affairs that often occurs.
The effect of the Amendment by itself
would be to allow the orphan child of an
insured unmarried man to receive an
orphan's pension. That is the object the hon.
Member has in view, but no provision is
made in the Amendment for the unmarried
mother or her child in the event of
the death of the father only. I think
the hon. Senator follows what I mean.
The Amendment would confine pensions to
cases where first of all the mother was dead
in addition to the father, secondly, to where
the parentage of the child has been established in one of four specified ways, and,
thirdly, the father had substantially contributed to its support. The Amendment which
the hon. Member has moved touches a very
complicated subject, and I have only in my
reply to the hon. Member-I preface my
remarks on purpose by saying I do not feel
that the matter is dealt with as satisfactorily as we all wish to say I do not feel
that with the considerations which we have
to keep in mind we can do otherwise at this
time beyond establishing the simple formula
which we have adopted for the purpose of
limiting to a certain extent the scope of
the Bill, and carrying out the object of
the Bill in as just and efficient a manner
as we possibly can. On behalf of the
Government I cannot accept the Amendment
of the hon. Member, although I am bound
to say I certainly have sympathy with him
when he says if the Government go so far
they might go further. I venture to suggest
to him that he should consider the difficulties which surround the Amendments if
they were put into the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: First of all, I am afraid I
must plead guilty to a mis-description in
the wording of the Amendment. It is not
my intention to say in line 2, although I
Widows' Pensions Bill- 3 NOVEMBER 1925
Committee.
do say so in the Amendment. I think you
should allow my Amendment to come in
before the words about the woman. Something or other has gone wrong there. That
is only a minor point. I cannot agree that
the Amendment, even as it does appear,
would be excluding the child of the man
and of the married woman. I am suggesting
that the man has not married the mother
of the child. But the more important thing
is this question of limiting it to the child
of the reasonable father who wants to do
his duty, even if it is illegitimate, and if
the child has lost its mother he is extremely
likely to have boarded the child out.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
That comes under the other Act.
Mr. LESLIE: Only if the man has been
declared the putative father. There is that
difficulty. Therefore, all I can say about
this Amendment is that it is drawing attention to what I suggest is the great generosity
of Parliament in admitting a child of this
kind. The question is what we should do
now. It would be perfectly obvious if one
should press the matter to a division and
be defeated, it would be the greatest possible
mistake I speak subject to correction. It
would be the greatest possible mistake to
divide the Committee on a question of this
sort, because that would effectively close the
door to any future discussion of the subject,
or at least render discussion extremely
difficult in future, and absolutely impossible
as regards this particular Bill. I still hope
that the Government after perhaps, a year
or two's experience of the working of this
Bill, will be able to go a little further along
the lines which I think should be followed
without the risk of giving a pension to the
wrong child. For the moment, if the Committee will permit me, I will ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment in the hope that
in future something will be done.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Statutory conditions as to
widows' and orphans' pensions) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Special conditions as to additional allowances and orphans' pensions)-
6.-(1) Where by order of any court any
child in respect of whom an additional
allowance is payable under this Act is or
has before the commencement of this Act
been removed from the custody of the
person to whom the allowance is or would
be payable, an orphan's pension shall be
substituted for the additional allowance and
shall be paid to the person in whose charge
the child is or has been placed, or in such
other manner as may from time to time
be directed by order of the court.
(2) Where a representation is made to
the Ministry of Labour by the local
authority or otherwise-
*
(a) with reference to an additional
allowance payable in respect of a child,
that the widow or other person to whom
the additional allowance is payable has
deserted or abandoned or ceased to support the child, or has become disqualified
for the time being from receiving a
widow's pension, and that it would be
in the interests of the child that the
additional allowance should be administered by the local authority or some
other person for the benefit of the child;
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 6,
page 5, line 14, to leave out from "Labour"
("to the Ministry of Labour by") to the end
of the line ("or otherwise"). There are two
amendments here and the first of them, by
itself, would scarcely be worthy of bringing
before the Committee. I rather think that
these words "local authority or otherwise"
were introduced in order that the local
authority might enter on the stage. I certainly should not object to these words were
it not for my next Amendment on page 6,
which relates to the same clause, and I would
take, if I may, the opportunity of dealing
with that Amendment now before we pass
from the other one. I am suggesting that
we should provide that any representation
to be made by the local authority should be
made through the Minister of Home Affairs,
rather than direct to the Minister of Labour.
It seems to me that the proper channel of
communication from the local authorities is
the Minister of Home Affairs, who is the
Minister in charge of local administration.
I cannot think it would be for administrative convenience and still more for administrative economy that the local authorities
should put up any proposal direct to the
Minister of Labour and that then the Minister of Labour should have to refer to the
Minister of Home Affairs before the matter
can be dealt with. It seems to me that from
the point of view of administration it would
be better for the local authority to approach
the Minister of Labour through the proper
channel, the Minister of Home Affairs. In
order to get that Amendment. I have to begin
by asking the Committee to take out these
words-to which, but for the other consideration, I would certainly not direct attention
at all. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
With your permission I will deal with the
two Amendments which the hon. Member has
on the Paper and of which, in accordance
with the rules of the House, he has naturally
to move the first one. The hon. Member is
one of those who invariably put down Amend-
'ments for the purpose of expediting matters
Widows' Pensions Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry.]
and making the clumsy wheels of the
machinery of the State revolve more rapidly
and more smoothly. I am afraid in this case
the Amendment he has moved would have the
effect of slowing them down, by introducing
a step between the local authority and the
Minister of Labour.
It is not necessary from the point of view
of the Ministry of Home Affairs that they
should be consulted before the representation reaches
the Ministry of Labour.
Furthermore as the Ministry of Labour
must bear the ultimate responsibility in connection with the payment of pensions it
would not be proper for the Ministry of Home
Affairs to consider representations before
they reach the Ministry of Labour and refuse to transmit any which they consider
impossible. The Ministry of Home Affairs
and the Ministry of Labour are in agreement
that the procedure which is proposed under
the Bill is the proper one for the circumstances. So I venture to suggest to the hon.
Member that the procedure which is outlined
in the Bill is more conducive to the object
which we all have in view, which is that
these matters should be dealt with at the
earliest possible moment.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that is a fair example
of putting down an Amendment in order to
have it considered.
I hope the Amendment
may be, by leave, withdrawn.
Amendment, of course, also.
The next
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 9, page
9, line 31, after "date" to insert "except in respect of an unmarried person who, if he so
long lives, will attain the age of seventy on
or before the appointed day." This Amendment deals with a matter to which I referred
on Second reading. I have put it down, if
I may say so, because I think we ought to
have a deliberate statement from the Government as to what a person of the class indicated stands to get under the Bill. I think
I know the answer I am going to receive.
But I think it should be authoritatively
stated what the person is going to pay in
shillings and pence. The Committee will
appreciate the point. The person who will
become seventy before the 2nd January next,
would, in the ordinary course of events, have
a non-contributory old-age pension. Now
he will have to pay, I think, 4d. per week.
I beg to move.
an
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member has discovered
individual in the Bill who in addition to
being 68 years of age does not benefit under
the Bill in the same manner as other people
do or does not benefit so much as others.
The object of the hon. Member's Amendment is to provide that an unmarried man
who is over the age of 65 years, and who
will reach 70 before January, 1928, shall not
be required to pay his own portion of the
contribution, the assumption being that he
does not stand to gain anything for his
contribution. That is the point the hon.
Member has made?
Mr. LESLIE: Practically.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
That he does not gain anything by the
contribution he is making to the funds? The
assumption there is not correct. When the
individual attains the age of 70 he will, if
he fulfils the conditions as to his insurance,
then be entitled to the old-age pension under
the Old Age Pensions Act without the fulfilment of the statutory conditions under this
Act as to means the hon. Member is aware
of what I refer to which he would otherwise have to. The Amendment would,
moreover, relieve him of the payment of
Health Insurance contributions for which he
gets a return in benefit for these few years
before he reaches the age of 70. Under the
circumstances I feel that the Amendment
is not conducive to the benefit of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I think I may take it that
really this man is going to get the old-age
pension when he becomes 70, free from those
restrictions which ordinarily attach to oldage pensions, and is really going to get
something more than he would have got
under the Old Age Pensions Bill, which
passed the other day. I think I am also
right in saying that the amount that man
is going to pay is something like £1 13s 4d.
Of course, I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment which was put down to get an
explanation.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 22,
page 24, line 24, to leave out the words "absent from," and insert instead thereof the
words, "not resident in." This Amendment
is designed to get an explanation of an apparent difficulty. The Clause says a pension
shall not be paid to or in respect of any person while that person is absent from Northern
Ireland. That seems to me to deprive of her
pension some old woman who decides to go
to Glasgow to see her youngest daughter and
stays there until the sick baby of that youngest daughter has recovered from an illness.
It seems to me to cut out the case of a North-
Ireland resident who pays
visit to
Great Britain. Seeing that it follows the
wording of the Act passed in Great Britain
ern
a
it makes one inclined to think it is all right
although it does not seem to be.
a
One looks for a Clause to cover such
case, and on reference to Clause 33 I find
that arrangements are made whereby the
Minister of Labour may make arrangements
of interchangeability, but I cannot find that
those arrangements quite meet this case. The
exemption given by Clause 33 says: "whereby
pensions payable by Northern Ireland shall
be payable to persons whilst in Great
Britain." Whether the difficulty is met by
my Amendment or not I am not quite sure,
but I do not understand the position created
by Clause 33 in respect of a visitor from Northern Ireland to Great Britain, and I cannot
see that Clause 33 gets us out of the difficulty.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member's Amendment would not
alter the force of the Clause, and it is submitted that the expression "absent from" is
a better one than "not resident in." The
persons entitled to pensions under this
scheme will be those who are resident in
Northern Ireland. The Clause is intended
to provide that during periods of temporary
absence from Northern Ireland pensions will
not be payable. Under Sub-Clause (b) the
amount of pension accruing during absence
from Northern Ireland may be claimed if the
person returns within three months. Reciprocal arrangements will be made with
Great Britain whereby persons going from
Great Britain to Northern Ireland will be
able to continue their pension while resident
temporarily or permanently in the latter
country. The reciprocal arrangement I have
mentioned will be made with Great Britain
when the Bill becomes law. I think that
covers the point raised by Senator Leslie.
Mr. LESLIE: I understand the position
is that the old woman must not stay more
than three months in Scotland.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am trying to explain that if she stays more
than three months she will be able to claim
the amount that has accrued during her absence and she will be able to continue her
pension in Great Britain.
Mr. LESLIE: At all events the Government has examined the question and I am
satisfied.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 23,
page 25, line 19, to leave out the word
"sailors" and to insert instead thereof the
word "seamen." This Amendment again is
on a point that I confess I do not understand.
I have referred to the English Act and I am
left rather doubtful as to whether the intention is that the whole of this Clause
should deal with men who have served his
Majesty in the Forces. If that is the intention the expression "sailors" is not correct,
because a sailor is a rather limited class of
person and "seamen" is rather more extended.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: It
is the other way about.
Mr. LESLIE: I have always thought it was
the opposite. Evidently we are in for a
divergence of opinion as to the meaning of
a very common word. I understood a seaman
was a person who went to sea, and that it was
generally defined as including such persons
as a ship's cook, or even the stewardess. A
sailor is a more limited person, but if the
word sailor is not meant to be confined to
men of the Royal Navy I think my Amendment is right, and we should say seamen,
in the same way as we have used the word
seamen in Clause 17. If on the other hand
we do mean to confine it to the Royal Navy,
I think we ought to alter Section 57 of the
Insurance Act and made it read seamen,
marines, soldiers and airmen." If we mean
the Royal Navy we might have left out all
those words and used the expression men
of the Forces." As I say, at the moment I
am not certain whether it is intended that
this Section should be limited to men in the
Navy or whether you mean everybody in the
Mercantile Marine. I beg to move.

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The substitution of the word "seamen" for
"sailors" would limit the operation of the
provisions in a way which is not intended.
The word "seamen" is used in the Insurance
Act in a limited sense, and would of course
have the same meaning in this Measure if it
were made use of, having regard to the
Interpretation Clause. "Sailors" is a more
comprehensive term, and includes not only
seamen in the Royal Navy and the Merchant
Service, but also marines in the Royal Navy
and masters of merchant ships. The Bill is
intended to include all seafaring men. The
word "airmen is also used, and that includes not only men in the Royal Air Force
but men employed in civil aviation as well.
دو
Mr. LESLIE: The only thing to be done is
for me to get a new dictionary. However,
that is for the future, and I ask leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
