Drainage Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: "That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill."
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
Mr. LESLIE: The question
what date,
as
to on
what
resolve itself
approximately or
should
on
date this House
into Committee on the Drainage Bill
has been before us on two occasions
during the current week. The Bill was
read a Second time after debate on Tuesday.
After that had happened I took it upon me
to suggest that a period of two weeks between the Second Reading and the Committee
Stage would not be too long to allow for consideration of the Measure. The utmost concession that the Government were prepared
to make at the moment was two days instead
of two weeks. Since then Resolutions passed
by two of our important County Councils
have appeared in the Press. Those Resolutions were alluded to yesterday, and I need
not take up the time of the House by repeating what is the purport of them, or in any
way re-discussing a matter that has already
been dealt with. But I would like to point
out that the fact of our taking the Committee
Stage of this Bill to-day in one respect
affects me personally somewhat, because I
have certain matters that I desire to bring
before the House for consideration and I
have been obliged to put them upon the
Paper without giving them as much thought
and as much preparation as I am accustomed
to bestow upon proposals which I bring before this House.
THE MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): You have had since
June.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Marquess says I
have had since June. It is quite true that
the Bill was read a First time in another
place in June, but it is also true that the
Bill was very seriously amended in another
place during the closing days, I think of
last week. When we discussed the Second
Reading of the Bill on Tuesday I do not think
the printed reports of the debates in the
House of Commons were in our hands, but,
in any case, I do not agree, and I do not
think the noble Marquess would suggest that
it is incumbent on Members of this House
to follow the progress of a Measure through
its course in another place. I think the
duties of this House do not usually begin in
connection with any Bill until it is presented to it. I would much prefer, even at
this late stage, if the Government could see
their way to defer the consideration of this
Measure.
So far as I can judge from the course of
the debates we did not get any very definite
expression of opinion on the part of any
County Council that the County Councils as
a whole desired this Measure. We did get
a reference to a speech delivered by the right
hon. Gentleman the Prime Minister, who, as
he always does, went to a good deal of trouble
in the matter. He saw on, I think, two occasions representatives of some, at all events,
of the County Councils, but I understand that
the County of Antrim was not represented at
the second of the meetings, and I also understand that that County Council as a whole
were by no means unanimous in support of
the Measure. Other speakers will, no doubt,
be able to give us more definite information,
but for myself I still wish that the Government had found it possible not to ask the
Senate to take this Bill to-day.
Mr. BARBOUR: I cannot feel that it
can be urged that there is any crying need
for what I must call rushing this Bill
through. I think this Bill has been brought
into being mainly on account of the fact that
the Government intends to deal with two
very large schemes-they have reserved the
power to deal with those two themselvesand that really has a great effect on the
activities that the County Councils will be
called upon to undertake. We have had an
expression of opinion from two County
Councillors that they are rather sceptical
about the Bill, and, although it is a fact that
the Bill has been in draft since June, the
first legal advice that I was able to get on
it is dated the 31st August. There have been
changes since then and, seeing that there is
no crying demand for the Bill, I think we
might well be allowed further time to consider it.
377 Drainage Bill.

5 NOVEMBER 1925
Drainage Bill.
!
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I would join with
the two hon. Senators opposite in urging
upon the noble Marquess, the Leader of the
House, that we should not push this Bill forward too quickly, because it seems to me that
if we pass legislation without proper consideration, instead of doing good, we are only
going to do harm. The whole object of our
House, and of a Second Chamber, is to give
the country time to realise what sort of
laws it is intended to pass. I must say that,
as far as I am concerned, I knew nothing
at all about this Bill until a few days ago.
Of course hon. Members may say "You
ought to keep yourself better up in what is
going on," but I can assure hon. Members
that I have other things to do. We may see
reports in the papers but we have very little
time to read them. I would join with my
hon. Friend in asking the noble Marquess
if he could see his way to postpone this
matter in order that the country may
realise what sort of Measure we propose to
pass.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I have listened to the speeches which have
been made by my hon. Friends and I regret
that I cannot see that any case has been
made out in any way for the postponement
of this Measure which is now before the
House. The hon. Senator (Sir George Clark)
who has just spoken has really made out
the case that all Bills should be brought in
and then postponed for a considerable time
until even the busy people have an opportunity of fully digesting everything that has
been done in connection with the Bill. But
my hon. Friend, who naturally is a very
busy man, is not aware of the immense
amount of negotiation that has been going
on in respect of this Bill by all those concerned, and I will trespass upon the indulgence of the House by quoting verbatim a
great deal of what has been done and a great
deal of what has been said.
Opposition to continuing this Measure
comes from the County of Antrim. The
County Council of Antrim have been fairly
consistent-I will not go further than fairly
consistent-in their opposition to this Bill,
but in their opposition they do not seem to
realise, or even to admit, that there is a
desire that the Bill should be introduced
from other portions of the Province. When
it is realised-and it must be realised that
this is an enabling Bill it seems to me a
curious policy that they should say the Bill
should not be brought in at all or that it
ought to be postponed for some considerable
time, notwithstanding the desire of other
portions of the Six Counties that this Bill
should be introduced and carried through.
If the Bill imposed duties on the County
Councils and compelled them to carry those
duties out under fear of penalties there
would be something in their opposition. But
there is nothing of the kind. The County
Council of Antrim need take no steps at all,
but there are other portions of the Six
Counties who are desirous that this Bill
should be passed. On the last occasion on
which we met, we met with opposition from
County Councillors of the County of Down,
but, on the other hand, here is my hon. Friend
(Mr. Dickson) who sits on my right, and who
took a different view, and he also is a member
of the County Council of County Down.
I would like to tell hon. Members that
since the autumn of 1923 resolutions have
been forwarded to the Government of Northern Ireland from various local authorities
pressing the Government to introduce legislation which would enable County Councils
to undertake at least minor drainage works.
There is one resolution which I will crave
the indulgence of the House to give in extenso. This resolution was unanimously
adopted at a meeting of representatives of
County and Rural District Councils held at
Enniskillen on the 11th October, 1923. The
Resolution reads:
"
That we urgently request the Government of Northern Ireland to proceed at
once with their Drainage Bill, which, we
understand, has been under consideration,
so as to enable the local authorities to open
the streams and rivers in this and other
counties that are at present flooding
thousands of acres of our best land, and
ruining it for productive purposes."
1 think a Government who would be insensible to a demand of that description would
hardly be deserving of the thanks of the
community which it is supposed to represent.
I may observe that at this time no Drainage
Bill had been prepared by the Government
-that was in 1923-and that legislation had
not even been foreshadowed.
A further resolution, which I will refer to
as the Fermanagh Resolution, was adopted
unanimously at a meeting of owners of
flooded lands in County Fermanagh, held at
the Enniskillen Town Hall, on the 4th
March, 1924. This resolution was couched
in these terms:-
"That we urge the Government of
Northern Ireland to bring in without delay
a Measure enacting for Northern Ireland
the provisions recommended by the Viceregal Commission on Arterial Drainage
(Ireland) in 1905
as long ago as 1905with a special view to the creation of such
subsidiary districts as that of the Sillies,
and enabling County Councils to take
action on the lines indicated in paragraph 29 of the Binnie Report, conferring
on the County Council or Committee ap-
Drainage Bill.
THE SENATE
Drainage Bill.
was held on 14th October. My hon. Friend
(Mr. Leslie) is technically right in what he
says, but I regret very much that when he
has been in such close contiguity to people
who have taken, a great interest in this
Bill-
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
pointed (in the mode recommended by that
Report) all the necessary powers for compulsory acquisition of land and water
rights, and the levying of special rates
and charges..."
and so on. It is a very strong Resolution,
but I will not trouble the House by reading
it altogether. This resolution was subsequently adopted in practically identical
terms by the following Local Authorities :-
The County Councils of Armagh, Down and
Tyrone; the County Committees of Agriculture for the Counties of Tyrone, Derry,
Antrim, Armagh and Down; and the Rural
District Councils of Ballymena, Banbridge,
Castlederg, Clogher, Dungannon and Tanderagee. Yet my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
asks that the Bill should be postponed,
although colleagues of his own, and people
with whom he is intimately associated, have
demanded that the Bill shall be brought
into force.
I would draw the attention of hon. Members of this House to the specific request
conveyed in the resolution I have just read
for powers to be conferred on the County
Councils to take action on the lines indicated in paragraph 29 of the Report of the
Viceregal Commission of 1905. As was explained in the debate in this House on the
Second Reading of this Measure, the Drainage Bill was originally drafted to give effect
generally to the recommendations of the Viceregal Commission of 1905, in so far as they
were considered capable of application to
the circumstances of Northern Ireland.
The terms of the present Bill were discussed at a Conference with representatives
of the County Councils, and I am not sure
that my hon. Friend (Sir George Clark) is
aware of the negotiations that have been in
progress for a very considerable time between the Government and the representatives of the County Councils.
In
deference to their representations certain
Amendments were introduced. I will not
go through the Amendments that were agreed
upon, but it was on the basis of the negotiations between the Government and the
County Councils that the Amendments that
were inserted in the Bill were put in. At
these conferences all the County Councils
were represented with one exception, namely,
Londonderry at the former, and Antrim at
the latter. The points with which the
Amendments dealt, and which I have not
detailed because I think I should be out of
order if I discussed the Bill itself, were the
main points on which the County Councils
criticised the text of this Bill as introduced
into Parliament on 5th June of this year,
The first conference with the County
Councils was held on 18th September, after
they had had the Bill in their hands for a
long period, and a subsequent conference
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to interrupt the
noble Marquess, but I am not a member of
any of the bodies to which he has referred.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The question of the drainage of Northern
Ireland is one which has been not only in
our minds but in the minds of our forefathers for generations past, and I am sure
the hon. Member, who is more meticulously
jealous of the welfare of the Province than
any of us, would not have allowed to have
slipped his notice or memory a question of
such vital importance. I regret very much
that his friends on the Agricultural Committee at Ballymena, knowing the position
he occupies in this Assembly, have not used
him as their spokesman to voice the views
they have in their minds. However, I am
sure they will rectify that in the future.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not want them to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I will now take the resolutions of the two
County Councils who appear to disagree
with the views generally expressed at the
conference to which I have referred, and
I again wish to insist that the general
principles underlying the Bill were at no
I will take first the
time questioned.
Antrim resolution passed unanimously at
a meeting of the Antrim County Council on
the 3rd instant. This resolution reads:-
"That the Antrim County Council,
having considered the Drainage Bill now
before Parliament, cannot see their way
to approve of its provisions.
Where drainage works under the provisions of the Bill are carried out and
are successful, the waters will be brought
down more rapidly into the basin of the
present floods. That the entire cost of
such works is made payable from rates.
That the County Councils who have to
pay rates are in a minority on the Consultative Committee and, generally, the
Bill-without dealing with the main question-is premature, and will only raise
hopes which are bound to be disappointed."
This
Members of this House will note that the
resolution states merely that the Bill is
premature, and that in the opinion of the
Antrim County Council it will raise hopes
which are bound to be disappointed.
may be now the views of the Antrim
County Council, but I would remind
them that the County Antrim Committee of Agriculture at their meeting
held on the 27th November, 1923,
The Drainage Bill-
5 NOVEMBER 1925
Committee.

ال

adopted the Fermanagh resolution, which
asks the Government "to give our County
Councils the necessary legal power to promote local drainage schemes whereby leading watercourses may be opened," and so
forth.
I will now take the resolution of the Armagh
County Council, which is in everybody's
mind because it was in the newspapers the
other day. I am inclined to think that the
spirit which prompted this resolution is the
result of a misapprehension of the intentions
of the Government, and this seems all the
more apparent on analysis of the terms in
which this resolution has been couched. The
resolution regards this Bill as a Measure
of more than doubtful utility at the present
time so far as the Lough Neagh drainage
area is concerned." This is precisely the
point at which the views of the Armagh
County Council and the Government diverge.
The Government, as is our duty, regard
drainage as a problem for which a solution
has to be found, capable of application not
only to the difficulties of County Armagh but
also to those of the whole Province.
The Armagh County Council apparently
regard the solution of the farm drainage
problems of Ulster as bound up with the introduction of certain modifications in the
Drainage and Navigation Works in Lough
Neagh and along the Lower Bann. The
Government, whilst fully alive to the necessity for speedy action, would hesitate to
express an opinion on the proper solution
of the difficulties presented by the Bann
Valley drainage problem until
proper
survey of this problem had been made. This
the Government has undertaken, and I may
say we are pressing forward with it.
a
The present Bill is, as it were, an instalment, pending such Bill, as must subsequently be introduced to deal in a comprehensive manner with the Bann, Lough
Neagh and Lough Erne systems, but much
useful work can be done and certainly will
be done by County Councils in the meantime
under the powers conferred by the Bill. It
does appear to me that it would be objectless
to abolish the Drainage and Navigation
Boards to which the resolution refers without substituting something else. No suggestions are made as to what should be substituted, and the Government itself is not
yet in a position to make a suggestion on
that point.
Both resolutions also fail to appreciate the
functions of the Advisory Committee. They
are fully set out in the Bill, and I would refer hon. Members to Clause 18, in particular,
sub-Sections 1 and 2. No action on the part
of the Committee can affect the local financial expenditure: they can only deal with
such schemes as the County Council itself
proposes. Its duties will be precisely those
recommended for such a body by the Viceregal Commission of 1905.
I should not be in any degree worthy of the
post I have the honour to hold if I did not
to the fullest extent consider the objections
which any hon. Member might raise to the
manner in which the business is conducted
in this House, and the speed or the slowness
with which Bills pass through this House,
but I do feel, and I am sure the majority of
the House will agree with me, that this is a
Measure which has been fully considered
throughout the Six Counties by the various
bodies that are closely in touch with the substance of the Measure. Therefore, I feel that
we should be wanting in our duty if we did
not continue to press this Bill forward and
pass it into law. It is for that reason that,
much as I am invariably influenced by suggestions that come from hon. Members of the
House, and notably from my hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie), who takes such a conspicuous and useful part in the business of this House, I
regret that on this occasion I find myself
in total disagreement with the suggestion
he has put forward.
On Question, Motion (That this House do
now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill) agreed to.
In Committee accordingly,
[The Marquess of DUFFERIN and AVA
in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Preparation, confirmation and
carrying out of schemes for drainage works).
(1) The council of each county in Northern Ireland other than a county borough
(in this Act referred to as "the county
council") shall be a drainage authority for
the purposes of draining any lands within
their county which are liable to be flooded
or injured by water, or whose drainage is
capable of being improved; and, subject to
the provisions of this Act. the county council shall have power-
*
are

*
*
*
*
*
*
(5) The county surveyor, or any other duly
authorised officer of the county council,
may enter upon any lands and execute al!
such works and do all such other things as
necessary for the purposes of any
scheme or for the execution of any drainage or other works under this Act, and
may make all such inquiries and investigations as are necessary for the purposes of
any surveys, reports or plans in connection
with such scheme or works; and the county
council shall make reasonable compensation, subject to the provisions of this Part
of this Act, for any injury done by the
county surveyor or other such officer.

THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
sub-Clause 1, line 8, to leave out the word
"each" (The Council of each County), and
to insert instead thereof the word "any."
This Amendment really covers the Second
Amendment, and I am not sure that they will
detain us very long. If you take the two
Amendments together the point is to leave it
open to any county council in Northern Ireland to reject the Bill so far as it concerns
itself by refraining from passing a resolution adopting its provisions. We have
been told ever since the Bill was introduced
into this House that it was a permissive
Measure. I desire to direct the attention of
the House to the words of the whole of this
sub-Clause. We are told that the county
council of each county "shall” be a Drainage
Authority for various purposes, and I should
like to ask what precedent there is for using
such a term, when the intention of Parliament is only to pass an enabling Bill.
of
were
the Notification
of
was power
to
was
As the House knows there are several
other Measures usually connected with
Local Government-there is the instance
Births Act,
1907, in which
given
adopt by resolution. and the Public
Libraries (Northern Ireland) Act-our own
Act of only last year and there
another Act dealing with some regulations
in relation to dogs. I have no doubt there
But the particular
many others.
point, of course, which I want to make is
this: If the County Council does not put
this Bill into operation is it perfectly clear
that the County Council will not expose
itself to any pressure on the part of the
Minister of Home Affairs, or, still more,
that it will not expose itself to action by
some outside person? It is always possible,
as the Committee knows, to enforce action
by a municipal body by means of a writ
of mandamus, and as the Committee also
knows your chances of getting a writ of
mandamus for simple nonfeasance are not
always particularly rosy. All the same
there have been instances where courts have
held that there was a statutory duty to
exercise the power, and a writ has issued.
The Government will, no doubt, before
bringing in this Bill have considered that,
and they will be able to clear away any doubt
there may be.
The object of my Amendment is to make
it perfectly plain in the wording of the Act
that the County Council in not adopting
the Bill shall not expose itself to any risk
of legal proceedings or to any risk of pressure to apply the Bill. If the Bill is to
confer powers on the County Councils one
would think they should be left without
pressure as regards the question whether
they shall or shall not exercise those
powers. Therefore, in the first instance, I
have only to move to leave out "each"
and insert any." I hope the Committee
"
will go on to insert the other words
limiting the operation of the Bill to those
counties which by resolution have adopted
the Bill.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: As the hon. Member stated, the
second of these Amendments really covers
the first as well. As has already been
pointed out, this Bill is a permissive Bill,
and if this Amendment were adopted it
seems to me it would strike at the root
of the whole Bill. The hon. Member having
been desirous that this Bill should be postponed or possibly thrown out altogether is
now afraid that something will be done to
the County Councils if they do not make
use of the Bill. This Bill does not compel
County Councils to undertake local schemes,
and therefore they cannot be subject to any
penalty if they do not do so. I think that
is quite clear. I am afraid we cannot
accept the hon. Member's Amendment,
because, as I said, it strikes at the very
root of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that probably
everybody will be satisfied with getting that
definite statement. Therefore, I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood in the
Order Paper in the name of Mr LESLIE:
"Page 1, line 7, after 'borough' to insert
'which by resolution adopts the provisions
of this Act." "
Amendment not moved.
"
"
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 3, line 8, after Council" ("County
Council, may enter") to insert after
notice." In case of the county surveyor or
any authorised officer entering on the land,
and so on, I suggest to insert the words
after notice." Such words are in use, and
sometimes notice is required. As a general
rule in the case of a reasonable surveyor,
and so forth, the strict intention of the
words is not necessary, but I think it is
worth while to put the words in, because
it always seems to me very necessary when
officials and surveyors, and SO on, come
down to see people or lands in the country
the occupiers should know that they are
coming. It is not very much, therefore, to
require of anybody who has got to go and
see a man that the man he has got to go
and see shall get a letter beforehand to say
that he is coming. Therefore, I think it
might make it a little more certain that
these very useful interviews shall take place
if we insert in the Bill the words after
notice." I do not think putting them in
would mean that if there was a succession
of visits there should be a succession of
notices. I think before the first entry
notice should be given. I beg to move.
"

Gentleman
(Col. Crawford) had raised
in another clause and I am inclined
to think I gave an answer that was
not correct. The County Council is empowered to appoint such officers and servants
outside their own staff. With reference to
the payment of such officers I take it the

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member in the closing
words of his speech has given the reason
why we cannot insert these words. Does
the hon. Member realise that if we inserted
the words "after notice it would mean
that every time every officer or other official
of a County Council came he would have
to give notice-every single time? I do feel
that that would be unreasonable. I think the
hon. Member will understand, and will
agree that any officer of a County Council
would as a matter of course and of courtesy
give notice, but if these words were inserted
it would mean that every single time any
official wished to go on any land he would
have to give notice. For that reason the
Government cannot accept the Amendment.

Mr. LESLIE: It seems to be
example of the danger of rushing matters
of this kind. A little clause could have been
inserted in the Bill. Of course I saw that
danger. At the same time I am inclined
to think my point is a good one. But I do
not observe that anyone attaches any great
inmportance to it. Therefore, I do not
proceed, and ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
On Question ("That Clause 1 stand part of

Mr. DICKSON: Before the Clause is
passed I would like to ask a question on page
2, line 15 ("(c) to appoint such officers and
servants, execute"). I understand that in
Committee we have a certain latitude in
getting details in connection with a Bill of
this kind. I would like to find out if in line
15 the appointment of such officers and servants to execute such works does not necessarily mean that it is the staff of the County
Council, and that we can employ outside
surveyors. If that is so the Amendment
which I have on page 4 would not be necessary and consequently I would withdraw it
both on the major and minor schemes. If
the County Council have power to appoint
outside surveyors I would also like to know if
we have power to pay them and get one half
refunded.
Mr. LONG: This is a point to which the
County Council of Londonderry attached
very considerable importance. They feel that
their surveyor's time is fully occupied on
ordinary maintenance work and reconstruction schemes for roads amounting to over
£150,000, and they would be glad to be assured that for major and minor schemes they
are empowered to employ outside aid.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
This is a question which I think my hon.
Friend intended to ask on a previous occasion when we were discussing this Measure.
J was considering a point the right hon.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

(6) The county council may, by any bylaws made under this section, impose on

penalties as they think fit, not exceeding

the offence from the county council.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to move in Clause 11,
page 9, line 10, after "fit" ("as they think fit,
not exceeding") to insert "not less than the
sum of forty shillings and." The reason for
this is we have in petty sessions courts a
maximum penalty for cases like sheep-dipping
where a person who does not comply with the
law is brought before an ordinary court of
summary jurisdiction and fined a shilling with
a maximum penalty of perhaps £15 or £20.
Unless there is a maximum penalty there is no
good in bringing these people before a petty
sessions court. I beg to move
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am afraid the Government cannot accept the Amendment moved by the
hon. Member for one reason, and a very
clear one. What the hon. Member proposes
is to ask Parliament to fix a minimum

penalty for offences however small and however slight they may be. I do not know
whether the hon. Member is aware it is open

to insert in these by-laws a provision fixing

Mr. DICKSON: If the County Council has

CLAUSES 12 (Powers to enforce cleansing
of water courses, 13 (Assessment of compensation in respect of drainage),
(Liability for consequential damage),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Transfer of business arising
under existing Drainage Acts).
(1) The business of the drainage
authority of any drainage district within
the meaning of this section shall on such
day as the Ministry of Home Affairs may
appoint (unless previously transferred in
pursuance of section twenty of the Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1898) be transferred to the county council of the county
within which that business arises, or, if
the business arises within two or more
counties, to the county councils of those
counties jointly, and, subject to the pro.
visions of this part of this Act, the county
council or councils (as the case may be)
shall, in respect of that business, have all
the powers and duties of the authority
from whom the business is transferred.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to keep myself
in order-in Clause 15, page 11, line 6, to
leave out "shall" ("this section shall on
such") and insert "may." I think it will be
the feeling of the Committee that these two
Amendments to Clause 15 are practically
covered by the discussion on Clause 1.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: 'The hon. Member is correct; they are
consequential.
Mr. LESLIE: Therefore, I beg leave to
withdraw.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. LESLIE:-In Clause
15, page 11, line 9, after "1898" "Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1908") to insert
"if the council of the county or counties so
request."
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 16 (Schemes for transfer and
carrying out of business).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to insert at
the end of the Clause:-"(h) the compensation of any person injuriously affected
by the abandonment of any drainage works."
This is an Amendment which I. hope I did
not put down unduly quickly, but it seemed
to me on reading the Clause that you wanted
a provision of this sort. We are giving
power for a scheme with such provision as
may be necessary and expedient in relation
to the abandonment of any drainage works,
and it seemed to me not unreasonable that
such a scheme should include, if necessary,
and the facts support it, the abandonment of
a drainage scheme.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It might perhaps shorten discussion
if I stated that the Government are prepared to accept this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 17 (Power of Lough and River
Erne, Lough Neagh and Lower and Upper
Bann Drainage Authorities to make new
schemes).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave out
The Clause. I do not know whether I correctly interpret the views expressed by
hon. Senators in this House and by people
outside as regards this particular Clause
which is the whole of Part III of the Bill. I
suggest to leave it out altogether, the idea
being that there is not, so far as I can form
an opinion, a general desire that either the
Lough and River Erne Drainage and Navigation Board or the Lough Neagh Drainage
Frustees should be given any further power
in the way of striking a drainage rate. I
am afraid the general opinion is that money
hitherto obtained by these two authorities
has not brought to the ratepayers the advantages which they expected when they paid
the money. So far as I can see, if we leave
out the Clause altogether we simply leave
these two authorities in the position in which
they are at the moment, and we are leaving
that over until that larger Measure for
which my Friend the Chairman of the
Londonderry County Council (Mr. Long) asked
on Tuesday but as to which he has not
received a definite assurance-we are leaving
that over until the larger Measure comes
before us. Then the Lough Neagh Drainage
Trustees can be dealt with. The question of
the Erne Board will have to be specially considered because, as the Committee know,
there are special difficulties as regards that
Board which need not be referred to
this afternoon. I think that on this
matter we should hear from hon.

t

I
neither of these Drainage Boards can carry
out or undertake any enlargement of the
scope of their duties unless after consultation
with the County Council within which their
jurisdiction lies. This seems to me to afford
a very ample safeguard. I regret, therefore,
the Government cannot accept the amendment.

am sorry that the hon.
Senator from County Fermanagh (Mr. Porter

He spoke on this matter on the Second

debate is not even in print.
I suggest
leaving out the whole of Clause 17, in order
that the matter may be fully debated.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
support the Amendment moved by the hon.
Senator opposite. There is a widespread
feeling in my part of the country affected by
one of the Drainage Boards in question-the
Lough Neagh Drainage Trustees that those
ratepayers who contribute towards this cost
do not get advantage of it. I venture to
support the Amendment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Amendment moved by the hon.
Member opposite (Mr. Leslie) and supported
by the hon. Member behind me (Viscount
Charlemont) proposes to leave out the
whole of Part 3, and the object, undoubtedly, as the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie)
frankly stated, is to ensure that no fresh
powers of any kind are given to these Drainage Authorities. I think I ought to inform
the Committee that this Clause gave the
Government a great deal of difficulty, and it
was the subject of grave consideration by
them. They consulted certain County
Councils concerned, and it is important from
the point of view of the Fermanagh County
Council in its relations with the Lough Erne
Drainage Board, because the Council and the
Board, unless such provisions as are embodied in this Clause were introduced, might
find themselves hampered by operations
undertaken by the County Council under the
Bill. I will give the Committee an example.
It is understood that the Lough Erne Drainage Board might, not only for its ordinary
maintenance duties but also in order to
deepen channels consequential upon works
carried out by the County Council, require to
purchase a dredger, but as the law stands at
present in relation to Lough Erne Drainage
Board they have no power to do so. There
is another reason. Some of the assessments
on lands made for the purposes of the original
schemes in both Lough Neagh and Lough
Erne areas are no longer applicable, but there
is no power to revise such assessments. I
might also mention that the Government
regards
of particular importance the
powers conferred under sub-sections 5 and 6
of this Clause, which enable the Ministry of
Finance to inspect works maintainable by
these authorities, and to find out whether
these duties are being properly performed
or not. I should have thought that the hon.
Member (Mr. Leslie) would have welcomed
provisions of that sort. Furthermore, I
think the hon. Member will observe that
as
Mr. LESLIE: I see of course the provision
about consultation with the County Council,
but consultation does not necessarily mean
that the County Council has the last word.
However, there is every reason to hope that
in one case at all events the impracticable
attitude which is likely to be taken by one
of the County Councils will prevent any mischief arising. Therefore, unless my noble
Friend (Viscount Charlemont) desires to pursue the subject I shall ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

(2) The Drainage Advisory Committee,
in giving advice upon matters and schemes
coming before them in pursuance of this
section, shall have regard to the uniform
treatment of arterial drainage throughout
Northern Ireland, and shall consider all
such matters and schemes in their relation to inland navigation, fisheries, agriculture, power production and other similar interests, or in their relation to any
of those interests which may appear to
the Drainage Advisory Committee to be
affected.

line 36. leave out from "section" ("of this

I put down this Amendment deliberately
to ask the Government to say what they

difficult. Arterial drainage, one would be

to be liberally used more or

minor form

is perhaps rather capillary than arterial.
Therefore, before Parliament passes this

CLAUSE 20 (Constitution of Drainage
Advisory Committee)-
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
One member of each County Council to
be appointed by each Council;
Such additional persons (not exceeding
two in number) as the Minister of Finance
may think fit to appoint from time to
time as being representative of interests
affected by drainage questions.
The members of the Drainage Advisory
Committee shall hold office for such term
and subject to such conditions as the
Minister of Finance may determine, and
the chairman of that Committee shall be
such one of the official members
may be designated by the said Minister.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
as
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 16,
line 34, leave out "one" ("One Member
of each"), and insert "three."
We are now dealing with the composition
of the Drainage Advisory Committee, and I
gather that everybody is more or less of
the view that the constitution of this
Drainage Advisory is altogether too official
for dealing with a matter which is to be
entrusted to the County Councils. The
County Councils are the directly elected
representatives of the ratepayers. They are
the directly elected representatives of the
people who have got to pay for these
schemes, and it seems to me that it is not
desirable that on a Committee consisting of
fourteen members there should be only six
County Council representatives, that is one
for each county in Northern Ireland. Even
if you imagine that the whole six for some
reason or other coalesced and voted together
am not too sure it is a likely contingency. It might very well happen that
I
they would be entirely outvoted by the
official representatives. I do not think that
is desirable. We know that the representatives of the Civil Service Departments are
of great assistance on Advisory Committees,
but they have not individually always got
a great knowledge of the technical details
of the matter in hand, neither have they
the local knowledge.
If you strengthen
your Committee by increasing the number
allotted to each County Council, I should
suggest three instead of one.
One of the main sets of questions to be
referred to or to be advised on by this Committee is the question of confirming any
scheme prepared by any County Council of
other drainage authority. You will get on
such a matter a good deal of local knowledge,
more or less trained local opinion, and certainly representative opinion. I think that
should be availed of. Also as the meetings of
your Advisory Committee, as I mentioned on
the Second Reading, are likely to be
held in Belfast, if you allow three
members to each County Council you are
much more likely to get a representative
from County Derry or County Fermanagh if
there are three to choose from, because one
individual cannot invariably attend. The
attendances at County Council meetings already, whether of Committees or the whole
Council, impose a very severe strain on the
country members of the Council. They have
almost invariably to travel a very considerable distance. The Antrim County Council
meets in Belfast. It has been a question
discussed for years, and it is generally agreed
that there is inconvenience. In County Derry.
the meetings are alternately held in Derry
city and Coleraine. I think it is only fair
both for the sake of practical convenience
of attendance and for the sake of securing
local elected opinion that you should increase
the number of members on this Advisory
Committee in the manner I have indicated.
Mr. LONG: I very earnestly trust that
the Government will see their way to accept
this Amendment of Senator Leslie's. The
County Councils attach considerable importance to this matter. If the Government in
their wisdom cannot see their way to accept
three members from each County Council I
suggest they might in all fairness allow two.
I really think the principle is important and
I would very strongly press the Government
to accept this proposition.
Mr. DICKSON: I was one of the representatives appointed by the Down County Council to attend the conference in the Home
Office in connection with this matter. Those
gentlemen who had studied the Bill previously found there were only four representatives. At that conference it was agreed to
increase the membership from four to six,
giving each County Council one representa-
Drainage Bill-
Committee.
5 NOVEMBER 1925
tive. By accepting this Amendment of
Senator Leslie's and Senator Long's you would
increase the Committee to such an extent
that it would be almost unworkable. I regret
I cannot see my way to support the Amendment. I think the representation of each
county by one member is sufficient because
all the other members of the Committee will
be experts in the business.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I venture to say that the hon. Senator who has just sat down (Mr. Dickson)
has really answered the question, but at the
same time I should like to elaborate it somewhat. After all, the nomination of a member
to this Drainage Advisory Committee was
arranged after consultation with the County
Councils simply for the reason that their
local knowledge might be available. I would
also like to point out that that Drainage Advisory Committee is, so to speak, really taking the place of a Drainage Department.
I do not see there is any necessity for having three members from each County
Council on this Drainage Advisory Committee, who have no executive functions.
They are merely an Advisory Committee. I
do not see the necessity for having so many
county councillors on this committee, which
will consist, no doubt, of men of technical
knowledge, and so on. If, as I rather
gathered from the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie),
there was the inconvenience that when this
Advisory Committee might meet a member
of a County Council might not be able to
attend when a scheme was being considered
in which he was interested, I think we may
reasonably assume that this Advisory Committee is not going to discuss some scheme
when the member from the county in which
that scheme is going to be put up is not
present. I am quite sure the business will
be so arranged as to avoid that difficulty.
I really do not think three members of each
County Council are necessary, even on an
Advisory Committee, and, therefore, I regret
I must oppose the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I must say that this is a
matter in which I should be inclined to persist, but I am quite prepared to accept the
suggestion of my hon. Friend opposite (Mr.
Long) and say "two" instead of "three."
The noble Viscount talks of this matter
having been discussed with the County Councils. As a matter of fact, I think those discussions were only with representatives
of the County Councils, and the County
Councils do not appear to have invariably
supported the men who represented them on
that occasion. Also, I cannot admit that the
discussion by a Minister, even the Prime
Minister, with the County Councils or the
representatives of the County Councils, can
in any way be permitted to take the place of
discussion in Parliament. It is for Parliament to settle this; it is not to be settled
outside with the County Councils. It seems
to me to be a very reasonable suggestion that
there should be two members of each County
Council in order that if one cannot be there
the other can. I am afraid I have not very
much faith in the suggestion made that the
Drainage Advisory Committee would not proceed in the absence of one member, because
there is always the practical difficulty that
if you put it off to-day to suit one member,
you will put it on to-morrow to the inconvenience of another. That is a hopeless
position, and I would ask the Government
to reconsider it.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I think my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) is not
perhaps taking an absolutely correct view of
the situation. The Drainage Advisory Committee is an advisory committee. It does not
seem to me that we need envisage it as a
wrangling, divided body like, perhaps, the
Senate of Northern Ireland-(Laughter)-but
a peaceful assembly where matters will be
debated for the benefit of the whole of the
Province. Then on the advice of the Committee steps will be taken. I think, as the
noble Lord who is in charge of this Bill has
said, if something affects one particular
county, it would be only a matter of amenity
that the particular representative should be
present when the question is being debated.
But I hardly think the hon. Member (Mr.
Leslie) need have any apprehension that the
remainder of the Committee would take advantage of the absence of one member to
bring in some nefarious scheme to the detriment of the county he represents. I hope,
under these circumstances, the hon. Member will not see the necessity of persisting
with his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: It is difficult to fail to respond to an appeal for peace in this matter.
I may perhaps suggest before asking leave to
withdraw the Amendment if my hon. Friend
opposite (Mr. Long) consents to a withdrawal
-that the hon. Marquess might represent to
the Minister in charge the fact that opinions
have been expressed here that the representation of the County Councils is not quite sufficient. Perhaps the Minister of Finance will
give consideration to that matter when
appointing the two additional persons referred to in line 36. I gather that those
persons are intended to be appointed, so to
speak, ad hoc, but I am not quite sure. At
all events, it is a matter which will be within
the control of the Minister and can be easily
arranged. Therefore, with the consent of
my hon. Friend (Mr. Long), I will ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I would ask the hon.
Member (Mr. Leslie) not to withdraw this
Amendment for this reason, that on this

Advisory Committee there should be strong

It is all very well at present when we have a

when a different Government may be in
power which might not be so solicitous as
this one for the welfare of the Counties, and
might not be prepared to listen to the
opinions of the representatives of the County
I think certainly that on the
Councils.
Advisory Committee it would be advisable
should have
larger representation
from the County Councils. The
bers need not all attend. They will not

if you have twelve you will ever get

that if some big question came up they should

and have their voices heard, and they may
possibly get alterations made to the proposals. I would suggest to the hon. Member
not to withdraw his Amendment?

Colonel SINCLAIR: If it is in order I

to be appointed by each County Council is

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to my hon. Friend on my
left (Sir George Clark) I would point out
that this is merely an advisory committee,
and must have on it two representatives of
the Ministry of Finance, one officer of the
Ministry of Home Affairs, one officer of the
Ministry of Agriculture, one officer of the
Ministry of Commerce, and one officer
appointed by the Secretary of State. I
cannot quite see the force of the argument
of my hon. Friend on the left. It is purely
an advisory body of technical men with
knowledge of the various matters that concern their Departments. They are to
advise on whatever scheme may be brought
up before them. I think to have more members of the County Councils on this Advisory
Committee is really not necessary.
The CHAIRMAN: The withdrawal of the
Amendment being unanimous, does Sir

Sir GEORGE CLARK: I withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSES 21 (Power to make regulations,

to hold local inquiries), and 23 (Power of the

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after Clause
23 to insert the following new Clause:-
"If any person shall give notice in the
prescribed form to the Ministry of Finance
or to the Ministry of Home Affairs, as the
case may be, that he is aggrieved by anything
done, or proposed to be done, under this Act,
such Ministry shall not decide the question,
and the person aggrieved may appeal to the
County Court Judge, whose decision shall be
final.
(2) This section applies, amongst other
things, to any assessment, valuation, or
rating under this Act, and for the purposes
of this section the expression 'person' includes the County Council."
I think this is perhaps the most difficult of
the questions which I have brought before
the Committee this afternoon. What I have
in mind is to give any person aggrieved an
opportunity of appealing, if he so desires,
to the courts of law provided for him by his
country. I am also anxious, in order to
meet the wishes expressed by the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman, who is not with us
this afternoon (Colonel Sharman Crawford),
to make it clear that the right of appeal
should apply to the expenses which might
devolve on a County Council under, I think,
Part 1 of the Bill, the point being that it is
the duty of a County Council to protect those
ratepayers within the county area who are
not concerned with the actual drainage and
are not likely to derive any benefit therefrom.
Therefore it seems desirable that the County
5 NOVEMBER 1925
Drainage Bill-

Committee.
من

There is a somewhat similar case in one of
the earlier Labourers' Cottages (Ireland)
Acts, where an appeal might be made either
to the Local Government Board or to the
County Court Judge. I think there are
others, but I confess I had not time to look
them up.
Council should have power to appeal against
assessments, valuations and rates just the
same as anybody else. There has always been
a difficulty in any question such as general
drainage and so on of an infringement of the
private rights of property and the due protection of those private rights. One recalcitrant property owner must not be allowed
to retard or obstruct any general scheme,
and he does not generally do so.
So far as I can find it has been almost
invariably the practice to give a man who
thinks he is aggrieved the right of appeal to
some wholly independent authority. I find
that in the Bill as it was at first introduced
elsewhere, on the 5th June, there is a Clause of
some length which does not appear in the
Bill printed on the 29th October, which we
are now discussing. Clause 23, which was
struck out, provided that-
Nothing in this Act shall authorise or
enable any county council or other drainage authority-
(a) to interfere with ornamental water
in any private demesne or park without
the previous consent in writing of the
owner or occupier thereof; or
(b) to curtail or cut off any domestic
water supply without previously making
provision in lieu thereof to the satisfaction of the sanitary authority; or
(c) to encroach by means of their works
or otherwise upon any park, garden or
demesne without the consent in writing
of the owner or occupier thereof.
That Clause did not contain a not unusual
clause to the effect "provided that the consent of the occupier should not be unreasonably withheld." If it were withheld there
should be an appeal to some outside authority, such, for instance, as is provided for the
benefit of the Postmaster-General, and therefore for the Government, in connection with
the erection of telegraph posts. If the Postmaster-General differs from a railway company about the erection of telegraph posts,
the question goes to the Railway and Canal
Commission. Under this Bill, as I see it, I
can find no protection for private rights
other than that which may be contained in
Clause 25, which requires that
Every scheme for carrying out drainage
works under this Act shall contain such
provisions for the protection of rights or
interests affected by the scheme (whether
those provisions are expressly required by
this Act or not) as the Ministry having
power to confirm the scheme may think
necessary.
That gives, of course, a possible power of
appeal to the Ministry, but it does not give
an appeal to the ordinary courts of law.
There is the precedent of the telegraph posts.
I am not quite sure that my Amendment
is in the form in which I should like to bring
it before the House. I think it could be
urged that I ought to give power to the
County Court Judge to hear the question, and
so on, and I think there may be an objection
to the expression "amongst other things."
I think, perhaps, I could have done better,
but what I want the Committee to do is to
accept the principle of this Amendment, that
there shall be a definite appeal on the part of
any person or the County Council-who
thinks that his interests are being prejudiced, and that the appeal shall be to the
County Court Judge instead of to the
Ministry. It is, in my judgment, no part
of the duty of a Government Department to
act in a semi-judicial capacity, and unless
you have someone acting in a judicial capacity it seems to me that private owners'
interests are not sufficiently protected. There
was Clause 23 in the original Bill. Therefore
I presume that the Government thought at
one time it was desirable that private
interests should be protected, and that is
why they put the clause in. Whether that
was an arrangement made with the County
Councils
Mr. DICKSON: No.
Mr. LESLIE: At all events the Clause has
disappeared from the Bill as originally introduced, and there seems to me to be nothing
to take its place. Clause 25, as it now is, was
represented in the original Bill by Clause 24,
which has not been altered. Clause 23 has
gone, and there is nothing put in its place.
I venture to ask the Committee to accept the
principle of this Amendment, and if the
Government desire to improve the wording I
hope they will do so. I think I have covered
all the ground both with regard to County
Councils and private owners.
Mr. DICKSON: I desire to support the
Amendment proposed by the hon. Gentleman
opposite (Mr. Leslie). I do not think it is
right that private water rights, lakes or
waterfalls, should be interfered with, without
the consent of the owner, and 1 think the
private owners should be protected. Therefore, I support the Amendment.
Mr. LONG: I should also like to support
the Amendment proposed by Senator Leslie.
I suggest that no possible harm could be done
by accepting this Amendment, and it is conceivable that a wrong might possibly be done
in its absence. Therefore, I very strongly
support the hon. Senator.
Drainage Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Amendment of my hon. Friend
opposite (Mr. Leslie) really proposes, as I
read it, to allow an appeal to the County
Court Judge against something which may
be embodied in a drainage scheme, and the
effect would be apparently to enable the
County Court Judge to review the entire
administration of the Act. The hon. Member referred to the protection afforded by a
former Clause that is not now contained in
the Bill. The reason why that Clause was
removed from the Bill was because it was
felt that ample protection was already given
to private interests. If hon. Members will
look at the Bill, they will find protective
provisions. In Clause 1, Section 5, it is
stated:
the county council shall make
reasonable compensation, subject to the
provisions of this Part of this Act, for any
injury done by the county surveyor
other such officer.
Clause 13 states:
or
If any dispute arises as to the amount
of compensation to be made under this
Part of this Act for any injury done by an
officer in entering upon land, executing
works or otherwise in the exercise of his
powers under the said Part, the question
of disputed compensation shall be referred
to and determined by an official arbitrator
under the Acquisition of Land (Assessment
of Compensation) Act, 1919, and sections
three and six of that Act shall apply
accordingly.
Also Clause 14 deals with liability for consequential damage. What we have got to remember, I think, is this, that in whatever
major scheme comes up there is to be a public
inquiry. Every person concerned is entitled to
raise an objection and to have his case fully
considered. He is entitled to raise an objection, and this public inquiry is to be held;
and not until after this public inquiry has
been held does the scheme go forward to the
Advisory Committee. Ample opportunity is
given for anyone who feels that he is injured
or in any way molested, or that his rights are
in any way being affected, to make representations to the proper authority before any
scheme can be approved.
Mr. LESLIE: Yes, but not to the County
Court Judge.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not see that the Amendment is
really needed. As I said before, you are
going to enable the County Court Judge to
review the whole administration of this Act,
which really does not seem to me to be
reasonable.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think the Amendment, hastily drawn as it is owing to lack of
time, bears the construction which the noble
Viscount seeks to put upon it. I want to get
the owner away from the power of the
Ministry, and to give him the protection
which the Constitution provides for him of
going to a legal tribunal rather than to the
Ministry. I think it is not fair to give
authority of this sort to the Ministry; it is
not fair to the Ministry, and it is not fair to
the man. It is very unlikely that anyone
would go to the County Court Judge with a
hopeless case, and it seems to me not at all
improbable that the Ministry might decide
the matter on considerations which would
not have the same weight with the Judge as
with the Ministry. I am not, of course, as I
said before, in any way bound to the wording
of a very hastily drawn Amendment, but I
do hope that the principle will be accepted,
and that the Government will undertake to
bring up some Amendment on the Report
stage to meet the views put forward. The
Amendment has been supported by representatives from two other counties.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1
feel that the private owner is adequately protected. I am not quite clear as to what cases
he specifically refers to, but I think Clause
13 covers the question of compensation to
the satisfaction of everybody.
Mr. LESLIE: If I may say so, it is not
entirely a question of compensation. That
Clause 23 dealt with certain amenities.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The suggestion of the hon. Member is to
bring the County Court Judge in to review
the policy of a drainage scheme, but I feel
that anybody who feels himself aggrieved has
ample latitude for his case to be discussed
and for receiving compensation. Having
considered the matter, I do not think that
any injustice could be perpetrated under
this Act.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: There is
another view of this matter. I am not so
much concerned about the protection of
private rights, but I do feel there is the
possibility of an autocratic official making
himself offensive to some poor person, and
probably experimenting on some poor person's land in a way for which there would
be no compensation. That is one fear I have.
Somehow I think that a Clause such as this
one would afford the necessary protection for
a man of that description.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Senator (Mr.
Joseph Cunningham), I have to say if he
will look up Part 1 of the Bill, Clause 1,
sub-section 5, he will see that the county
surveyor may enter upon any lands and that
the County Council shall make reasonable
compensation. I think the poor man is amply
covered by that Clause.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

Dickson) would look at Section 25 he would

Barbour, Mr. H. A. Milne

point my hon. Friend (Mr. Dickson) is making It is a general provision for the protection of rights, and reads as follows:

such provisions for the protection of rights
or interests affected by the scheme
(whether those provisions are expressly

may think necessary."

CLAUSE 24 (Protection for fisheries).
(1) Every county council or other drainage authority to which this Act applies
shall give notice, in such manner, and to
such fishery conservators and other persons, as may be prescribed after consultation with the Secretary of State, of any
scheme or work under this Act, which involves the construction, alteration or remodelling of any dams, weirs or other
works in the bed of any watercourse.
or re-
(2) Every scheme under this Act involving the construction, alteration
modelling of any such works as aforesaid
shall contain-
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move in Clause 24, page
18, line 17, at end of sub-Section (1) to add
"or affects in any way the flow of water over
or through any such works as aforesaid,"
and in page 18, line 19, after "of" ("alteration or re-modelling of any such works")
to insert "or affecting in any way the flow
of water through."
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
Those latter words are consequential on
the above. This Clause is intended, as hon.
members will realise, to safeguard the rights
of fishery owners. As hon. Members know
matters connected with fisheries are a service reserved to the Council of Ireland. The
fishery administration did not pass to the
Government of Northern Ireland under the
Government of Ireland Act, and the Secretary of State for Home Affairs is at present
the authority for that branch of the administration. In these circumstances Clause 24
of this Bill ensures that any recommendation
made by the Secretary of State for the protection of fisheries shall be embodied in any
local drainage scheme. Under this Clause
notice must be given of schemes or works
involving the construction, alteration, or remodelling of dams, weirs, or other works in
the bed of any watercourse, and the scheme
must contain such provisions as the Secretary of State may require in order to afford
sufficient safeguard for the passage of fish
up and down such watercourse. Accordingly,
the two Amendments, which I have the
honour to move, will ensure that the flow of
water is not altered in this way without
provision for the protection of fisheries. I
beg to move.
The
CHAIRMAN: I will take these
two Amendments together.
Amendments agreed to.
Clause 24 as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Drainage Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
CLAUSES 25 (General Provision for Protection of Rights, &c.), 26 (Interpretation and
Repeal), and 27 (Short Title) ordered to
stand part of the Bill..
FIRST SCHEDULE-Description of Drainage Works.
*
1. Clearing of rivers, watercourses, &c.,
that is to say:-
(a) the removal of obstructions such as
fallen or ingrowing trees or bushes, refuse
or other material;
(b) the removal of shoals and cesses;
(c) the shaping, trimming, and revetting of banks.
2. Works in connection with main drains
and their feeders, that is to say:-
or
(a) the cleansing, widening, deepening
otherwise improving of existing
drains;
(b) the cutting of new drains where
required;
(c) the raising and repairing of existing embankments, and the construction
of new embankments;
(d) the sinking of pumping sumphs;
(e) the construction and repairing of
sluices.
(f) the removal and disposal of old
spoil banks.
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 21,
Section 1, paragraph (b) to leave out "cesses”
("the removal of shoals and cesses") and in
Section 2, paragraph (d) to leave out
"sumphs" ("the sinking of pumping
sumphs").
Perhaps by consent I might put these two
Amendments together. I only put these
two Amendments down for the purpose of
asking what is the meaning of the two words
used. I cannot find them in the very large
dictionary provided for the
of this
House nor in any other; so the first Amendment is to leave out "cesses" and the second
to leave out "sumphs." I presume there is
a definition, but they are not common words.
use
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The words referred to by the hon.
Member (Mr. Leslie) can be found in the
dictionary. The two words are not common
words, but it is desirable to retain them.
They are words that are constantly used in
drainage matters, and frequently occur in
pamphlets issued by the Board of Agriculture in England and by drainage authorities.
It is considered advisable that these words
be retained. A sumph is a pit into which
water flows before being pumped.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: And what is a cess?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: A cess, I understand, is practically
the same thing.
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment, I think,
can be withdrawn.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Second Schedule agreed to.

6. The Ministry may, where it appears to
it reasonable that such an order should be
made, order the payment of the whole or
any part of the costs of the inquiry either
by the drainage authority to whose
administration the inquiry is incidental or
by any other drainage or local authority
with whose administration the inquiry
appears to be concerned, and may require
the applicant for the inquiry to give
security for the costs thereof.
*

*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 25,
section 6, to leave out from "may require"
("and may require the applicant") to the
end of the section.
I propose to leave out the words "and may
require the applicant for the inquiry to
give security for the costs thereof." I know
such a provision is occasionally necessary, but
it seems to me to be a little severe and
scarcely necessary on the present occasion.
The inquiry is a public matter, and it does
not seem to me that the applicant ought to
be required to make himself liable for the
costs thereof.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It is considered necessary that these
words should be retained in order to make
provision for trivial and litigious complaints.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Third Schedule agreed to.

Sale of Dangerous Drugs.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
any regulations have been made by the Min-
405 Sale of Dangerous Drugs.
5 NOVEMBER 1925
Sale of Dangerous Drugs. 406
F


the issue of consolidated Regulations in
hand, and it is hoped that it will be possible
to arrange for the issue of the new Regulations before the end of the year.
ister of Home Affairs for controlling or restricting the production, provision, sale or
distribution of any drugs to which the
Dangerous Drugs Act (Northern Ireland),
1920 to 1925, apply; and if not, whether the
Minister is prepared to make such regulations
should such action appear to him desirable;
and whether contraventions of any "corresponding law" referred to in Section 1 of
the Imperial Dangerous Drugs and Poisons
Amendment Act, 1923, can be dealt with in
Northern Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: The question that stands in
my name arises out of something that was
left not very clearly defined when we passed
the Dangerous Drugs Act, 1925, which, it
will be remembered, we passed very quickly,
though not too quickly, in order to conform
with the law in Great Britain. It left it in
doubt whether the Minister of Home Affairs
still retained the power of regulating, and
therefore I have asked whether the Minister of
Home Affairs is prepared to make such Regulations, or whether he has done so, and the
answer to the one question practically answers the other.
The third point is really more difficult,
because it is limited by the restriction placed
upon us in dealing with importations under
the Government of Ireland Act. If it should
be inconvenient to answer that question on
account of negotiations in progress I shall
not press it, but the noble Leader will be
able to satisfy us on that point.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
In answer to the question which the hon.
Member has put to me, no Regulations have
as yet, been made by the Minister of Home
Affairs under the provisions of the Dangerous
Drugs Acts of 1920 and 1925, but it will, of
course, be understood that the original
Regulations made by the Secretary of State,
under the 1920 Act still apply and are enforceable in Northern Ireland. Consolidated
Regulations under the Dangerous Drugs Acts
are at present in process of preparation, and
these will include any amendments in the
original Regulations under the Act of 1920,
which the working of the Act has shown to
be necessary, and also any amendments which
may be required as the result of subsequent
legislation, and I need hardly add that if
Senator Leslie has any suggestions to make
in this connection, the Minister of Home
Affairs will be most happy to receive them.
It is desirable, so far as possible, to keep
the Regulations which may be made in
Northern Ireland in conformity with those
which apply in Great Britain, so as to avoid
trade complications, and the question of the
issue of the consolidated Regulations is being
considered in consultation with the Imperial
Home Office, who have also the question of
It may be pointed out that neither of the
recent Acts necessitates any serious amendment of the existing Regulations, as the effect
of these Acts in Northern Ireland, so far at
any rate as the provisions which would require to be dealt with by Regulation are concerned, is not of immediate necessity for the
full enforcement of the Statutes.
With regard to the point raised in the
latter part of Senator Leslie's question, somewhat complicated legal questions are involved. It will be noted that the Imperial
Act passed in 1923 (the corresponding Act in
Northern Ireland being that of 1924) deals
with certain Acts which would, if done outside Great Britain, be offences against the
laws of a foreign country to which the International Opium Convention of 1912 applies,
but the use of the expression "place outside
Great Britain" and "if committed in Great
Britain" in Sections 1, 2 and 6, exclude such
provisions of the Act of 1923 from application to Northern Ireland, whilst the
enactment of such provisions is outside the
powers of the Parliament of Northern Ireland by reason of Section 4 (1) (4) of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920 (as relating
to offences connected with treaties or relations with foreign States).
Portion of Section 1 of the Imperial Act of
1923 in so far as it relates to such offences
against "corresponding laws" had, therefore,
to be deleted from the Northern Ireland
Act of 1924. It will, therefore, be seen that
neither the Imperial Act nor the Northern
Ireland Act enables such matters to be
dealt with in Northern Ireland, and in
order to remedy this position of affairs representations were made to the Imperial
Government suggesting that legislation
should be introduced which would place
Northern Ireland in the same position as
Great Britain in this respect, but so far
the necessary legislation to this end has
not been introduced.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move: That this House do now
adjourn. It is proposed that we should
meet to-morrow at mid-day, when I am
proposing, with the leave of the House, to
move the suspension of Standing Orders so
as to enable us to take the Report Stage
and the Third Reading of the Bill we have
just been discussing.
407 Business of the House.

THE SENATE.
Adjournment.

Mr. LESLIE: I do not know whether the
Government propose to make any suggestion
as regards inserting in the Bill on the
Report Stage the Amendment adopted by
the Committee this afternoon.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
As the hon. Member is aware, if an hon.
Member's Amendment is carried it is put
into the Bill, which will then go to another
place, where the Amendment will be discussed.
The Amendment will be either agreed to or
disagreed with, and if it is disagreed with
it will be returned to this House.
Mr. LESLIE: Possibly with an Amendment?
The MARQUESS OF LONDON DERRY :
Yes, possibly with an Amendment. I should
add that after our meeting at 12 o'clock
it will probably be necessary to meet again
at the ordinary time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes after Five o'clock.
