On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: After what took place in the
Committee yesterday afternoon the House
will be prepared for the Amendment which 1
now have the honour to suggest. It will be
remembered that yesterday we inserted new
words in the Bill with a clear intimation
that it would be necessary probably to reconsider the actual wording of the addition
on the Report stage. Since the Committee
dissolved yesterday afternoon, I have had the
opportunity of consultation with the Parliamentary Draftsman and with the officials of
the Department concerned in the administration of the provisions of the Act. I need
scarcely say that I have received from them
the assistance they always afford any Member
of Parliament in any conditions whatever.
Consequently I am in a position this afternoon to bring up a new Clause which, while
it conforms absolutely to the principles
which we established yesterday, avoids
certain minor difficulties with which I will
not at the moment deal.
I have to make two Motions to the House,
and the first of them is the omission of the
Clause which was passed yesterday afternoon,
and which would in the ordinary course of
things be numbered as 24, that is, I ask the
House for the moment to omit Clause 24 as
passed yesterday afternoon, in order that I
may subsequently move the new Clause, the
terms of which, in my opinion, carry out the
intention of the Committee and the terms of
which are agreed to by the Government. I
beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I have now to move the
insertion of the new Clause. This new Clause
has, so far as practicable, been circulated to
hon. Senators. But I may perhaps say that
what I may call the first edition of the Clause
has undergone slight alteration. The Clause
should read number 26. That is the most
appropriate place for it in the Bill.
Clause which I move is :-

The
26. Where any person deems himself
aggrieved by anything done, or proposed to
be done, by a county council or other drainage authority, in pursuance of a scheme
confirmed under this Act, or by a decision
of a county council or of the Ministry of
Home Affairs under Part I of this Act upon
the question of his liability to scour out or
cleanse a watercourse, such person may
appeal to the county court in like manner
as a person aggrieved by any matter or
thing done under the Public Health Act,
1878, by a court of summary jurisdiction
may appeal under Section 269 of that Act;
and paragraphs (1), (2), (3), (6) and (7) of
that Section shall apply accordingly, with
such adaptations as may be prescribed by
regulations to be made jointly by the
Ministry of Finance and the Ministry of
Home Affairs under Part IV of this Act;
Provided that nothing in this Section
shall be construed as enabling objections
to any assessment of the value of lands for
the purpose of a drainage rate to be made
or determined, or any such assessment to be
revised or amended otherwise than as is
expressly provided in Part I of this Act."
I think, perhaps the House will see that
the New Clause offends to some extent
their sense of decorum in the matter of
legislation by reference. I venture to remind the House that the section of the
Public Health Act which is referred to is
a section covering fully two pages full of
Drainage Bill-
Report Stage.
THE SENATE
a
[Mr. Leslie]
extremely technical matter, and it is, as
I understand,
canon of draftsmanship
that legislation by reference is not only
lawful, but laudable, when the object is
to incorporate a section deliberately inserted in the former Act for the purpose
of being incorporated in subsequent Acts.
I venture to suggest that this particular
section of the Public Health Act was intended for incorporation as necessity arose.
At all events it has frequently been incorporated before now.
The second paragraph of the new clause
invites some attention, because it seems at
first sight to run counter to the second sub-
Clause of the Amendment carried, dealing
with assessment valuations and ratings. I
had an opportunity of comparing that sub-
Clause, as suggested, with the previous one,
and I am satisfied, as the noble Viscount
intimated yesterday afternoon, it would not
be desirable to insert the words exactly
in that form. I have also been able to
satisfy myself and this is a point to which
the House will attach importance that the
Commissioner of Valuation referred to in
the Bill is himself to some extent a court
of appeal, or at all events a revising officer
for the original valuation, and, further, that
that officer is an entirely independent officer.
He is appointed by the Minister of Finance,
and questions are referred to him, but his
decisions on those matters cannot be questioned by the Ministry. There is no direct
appeal from him on the matter of assessment or valuation, but it is, as the House
will readily understand, always open to any
person who may be aggrieved on a point
of law, if he thinks the Commissioner of
Valuation has not taken into consideration
certain matters which he is by law required
to take into consideration, to apply to the
ordinary Courts in the ordinary manner.
That protection remains in this Bill.
I have full confidence in submitting this
revised Clause to the House as a Clause
which I am satisfied carries out our intentions, and which the Government is, I
understand, prepared to accept. It will not,
I apprehend, entail the least difficulty in
another place, and, therefore, will not
operate in any way to secure the extermination of the Bill. None of us, however
anxious we were for further consideration
of the Bill-and we were anxious to have
further time-desired to kill the Bill,
because all of us, even those of us who did
not expect that our own localities would be
affected, were anxious that any locality that
could take advantage of the Measure should
not lose the opportunity. I am satisfied
this Clause will not impede the operation
of the Bill, and with confidence I commend
it to the consideration of the House.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Senator
Leslie has told the House what kind assistance he received from the Parliamentary
Draftsman and the Government in preparing
this new Clause. I have come to the
conclusion that the hon. Member has been
overcome by that kindness, because there
is no Member of this House who has complained more about legislation by reference
than the same hon. Member. It is almost
impossible for an ordinary layman to read
the new Clause and obtain any sense from
it. It would necessitate a lawyer being feed
to explain it. However, I accept the word
of the hon Senator that the Clause is quite
in order, and carries out the intention of
the Committee, when it passed the Amendment yesterday. At the same time, I am a
little bit surprised to find Senator Leslie
straying from the path he has been
very particular to tread in the past.
SO
Mr. DICKSON: I desire to support
the Amendment moved by Senator Leslie. 1
think it meets our objections in every way.
Of course the procedure will not be so costly
as going direct to the County Court Judge.
I think the other House might not have
accepted the Amendment agreed to by the
Committee yesterday, and that would have
endangered the Bill, a thing we do not want
to do. I am quite satisfied the Amendment
should be accepted in its present form.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I desire to inform the House that
the Government accept this Amendment. I
think Senator Leslie has explained it very
fully and explicitly, and I do not think it
requires any further words from me to recommend it to the House. I merely wish to
say I am obliged to the hon. Member (Mr.
Leslie) for the trouble and time he has taken
in preparing this Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am authorised by the Prime

Wright's Divorce.
Question to Minister.
DAIRYING INDUSTRY OF NORTHERN
IRELAND.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Barbour:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
his attention has been drawn to the report
of a meeting held recently at Coleraine, at
which it was stated that the Dairying Industry of Northern Ireland was in a moribund condition, and that this statement was
in agreement with the opinion of officials
of the Ministry of Agriculture and whether
the Government will take steps to correct
the unfortunate impression that might be
caused by such statements.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to ask the
Question standing in my name, which refers
to a report that appeared in the Press yesterday, of a meeting held in Coleraine at which
the following statements were made: That the
Dairying Industry in Northern Ireland was
dying; that the Ministry of Agriculture
officials agreed with this statement; and
that the Ministry was shy of coming down
to the creameries throughout the Northern
area and pronouncing their death. This is
a very serious statement, and I do think it
is a pity that anyone should give expression
to a thought like this about an industry
that is carrying on at its full volume in the
light of the times.
If one were to judge and condemn other
industries on their output to-day one would
draw a very gloomy picture of the whole
country and would say that every industry
was moribund and was shortly going to
breathe its last. I think it is a very cruel
thing that such a statement should have
been made about institutions that at one
time rescued the butter trade of the whole
of Ireland and that are now successfully
carrying on their business. They are beset
with difficulties, but they have surmounted
the worst of them, and are quite capable of
expanding when better times come along.
Not only have these institutions made
butter, but last year they were able to perform other useful services. There was a
failure of turf, and farmers in the
districts where these societies were established were able to use these organisations
to supply themselves with coal. Those of
us who know rural conditions can measure
the blessing that was to the people who live
where these institutions exist. I hardly
like to believe that the Ministry of Agriculture sympathises with this gloomy outlook.
I am connected with an Institution that
looks after the Co-operative Creameries.
We have seventy-seven of them in the
Northern area, with a membership of 70,000
and a capital of £63,000. Their turn-over
for 1924 was £900,000. It is true that it will
hardly be as big this year, but that is explained by the bad seasons we have had.
That has led to a reduction in the number
of cows, and consequently there has been
a falling off in milk, with the result that
less butter has been produced. But to quote
from figures like that and to say that our
butter or dairying industry in the North of
Ireland is dead, is a very cruel and dangerous
statement to make at this time. Every industry is hard, hit, and when any industry
is suffering, it is not a friendly action to
add to its agony, especially by a statement
that is not warranted by fact.
Therefore I trust the Government will see
its way to issue some statement that will
re-assure, not only the members of these
Societies, but the public generally, with
whom these institutions trade, as it is very
desirable that nothing should be allowed to
go unchecked that in any way might shake
the credit which these institutions and 1
know. them fairly well-enjoy, and justly
enjoy.
417 The Dairying Industry.

10 NOVEMBER 1925
Ejectment Suits.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The House knows, as well as I do,
the very great interest the hon. Member who
has raised this question takes in agricultural
matters and particularly in Co-operative
Creameries, and the hard work he has done
to ensure that they are carried on with
success.
I am informed that the attention of the
Ministry of Agriculture was drawn to a
Press report of a meeting of the Coleraine
branch of the Ulster Farmers' Union last
Saturday, at which it was stated that the
dairying industry was dying, and that this
fact was admitted by the Ministry's officials.
It is hardly necessary to remind Members of
this House that the years 1922, 1923, and 1924,
were abnormal years in so far as they were
characterised by extremely wet, cold summers,
and that the winter of 1924-25 was also unusually unfavourable to agriculturists.
a direct result of a succession of bad seasons
the mortality rate for dairy cattle showed an
upward tendency, which was largely accounted for by the unavoidable use of inferior
fodder.
As
The unfavourable weather during the last
four years was one of the major causes which
brought about the period of acute depression
in the agricultural industry generally. To
meet immediate financial necessities, farmers
in many cases found no course open to them
but to immediately realise
of their
capital, and unfortunately this was too easily
done by reducing their herds by selling
numbers of their dairy cows and heifers.
some
It is not surprising, therefore, to find that
the production of milk throughout Northern
Ireland has decreased during the last four
years, but it is unfair to regard recognition
of this fact as an admission that the dairying industry is dying. The decrease, as has
been pointed out, is entirely due to natural
causes and the Ministry is sanguine that with
better summer seasons the volume of dairy
produce will as naturally increase. It is
admitted that the supply of milk has fallen
consideralbly for the reasons I have stated,
but the Ministry does not for one moment
admit that the dairying industry is in a
dying condition. On the contrary they are
quite sure it will recover.
The Ministry cannot but think that to say
the creamery industry is dying is absolutely
over-stating the case. On the information
obtained from the operation of the Ministry's
Surprise Butter Inspection Schemes, quality,
consideration of
less importance
than quantity, has maintained
improvement.
a
no
Mr. BARBOUR: Thank you.
a
steady
Ejectment Suits in Belfast,
MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move: That this House do
now adjourn to such day as Mr. Speaker may
appoint. I should like to inform the House
that in all probability the House will be required to meet some time next month, but
I am unable to say on what date.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to draw the attention of the Government to a statement made
by the Recorder of Belfast in the Recorder's
Court yesterday with regard to ejectment
suits in Belfast. A large number of Ejectment Orders are being obtained from week
to week against the working classes, and
owing to the depreciation in the value of
money it is found necessary to have the
ejectment suits brought in the Court of
Quarter Session instead of in the Petty
Sessions Court, with the result that large
numbers of the working classes are put to
great expense, especially when they defend
and lose a case brought against them for
ejectment.
I want to ask if the Government will, at
the earliest possible moment, take steps to
amend the law, as a long period will probably
elapse before money recovers its pre-war
value. By way of illustrating the necessity
of some amendment of the law, I have in
my hand particulars of one house that is
let to seven different families, and the rent
of all of these families is so high that if an
ejectment suit was brought against them
and they defended the case each of them,
according to the Recorder, would be put to
the expense of £6 13s 0d, which would be a
grievous burden on a working-class family.
I will give the particulars of these seven
tenants to show the importance of the question I am asking. One family of four persons is living in a room and paying 9s a
week. Three people live in the kitchen of
the same house and pay 8s 6d a week. Four
people who are living in a single return
room pay 8s 6d a week, and in another small
back room there are seven people living who
pay 8s 6d a week. Two people are living in a
top front room for which they pay 10s 6d
a week, and for another top room three
people pay 7s 6d a week, while five people,
who are living in another top room, in the
same house, are paying 6s 6d a week. That
is seven families in one house, each of whom
is paying a rent which would necessitate them
defending an action for ejectment in the
County Court. That would leave each one
of them liable to an expense of £6 13s Od.
I noticed that when the Rent Restrictions
Bill was before this House an hon. Senator
Ejectment Suits.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
moved an Amendment, which the Government did not accept, and with regard to this
the Recorder said:-
All I know is that an Amended Rent
Act was passed by the Northern Parliament last Session, that the Government
were pressed to adopt this remedy, that an
Amendment to effect the remedy was moved
in the Senate, and that the Government
refused to accept it and insisted that the
hardship must continue.
I would like the noble Viscount to use his
influence with the Government to amend the
law in this respect at the earliest possible
moment, in the interest of the working-class
tenants of the city of Belfast.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Senator who has raised
this important question courteously informed
me shortly before the House met that he
was going to raise it, but he, as well as the
House, will understand that during that
short time I have not had an opportunity
of consulting with the Government on this
matter. All I can tell the hon. Senator is
that I will make strong representations to
the Government, quoting the case he has
mentioned, to see whether legislation cannot be brought forward somewhat on the
lines he has suggested. Beyond that I cannot go at the present.
On Question, Motion (That this House do
now adjourn to such date as Mr. Speaker
may appoint), agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes before
One o'clock to such date
as Mr. Speaker may appoint.
Papers Presented.
Oral Answers.

MEMBER SWORN.
Mr. William Barclay, sworn, and signed
the Test Roll.
Timber Cultivation in Northern
Ireland.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
any action has been taken, or is in contemplation, to counteract the serious shortage
of timber which the Report recently presented to Parliament suggests is likely to be
experienced in Northern Ireland during
coming years.
Mr. LESLIE: The question which stands
in my name, as indeed appears from the terms
of the question itself, has its origin in the
Report of the Commission on the Natural
and Industrial Resources of Northern Ireland, which is presided over by one of our
own Members, the noble Viscount (Viscount
Charlemont) who is not with us this afternoon. That Commission issues Reports on
what we might call the instalment system,
and the particular Report to which I am
directing attention is that dealing with
Afforestation.
We see from the Report a statement based
on the evidence of Mr. Forbes, a former
official of the Department of Agriculture,
whose authority on matters relating to
timber is beyond question. We see from the
evidence of Mr. Forbes that the acreage in
Northern Ireland under timber is only one
and a quarter per cent. of the whole area and
that that percentage is a smaller percentage
than that of any other European country
with the exception of, I think, Iceland. We
also see that the annual production of soft
wood timber in Northern Ireland amounts
to only one-seventh of the consumption.
From those facts it may easily be deduced
that we are faced in the not distant future
with a serious shortage of timber in Northern
Ireland. The Report deals particularly with
soft wood timber but I am afraid that the
condition of hard wood timber such as oak is
not less serious, and hard wood timber is more
difficult to deal with because hard wood trees
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
take longer to develop. Accordingly I think
it might be worth while directing attention
to the matter in order that we might consider what has been done already and I
know something has been done because the
Report bears witness to that and whether
there is anything more our not inexhaustible
resources will permit being taken in hand.
I think it is rather to the State than to
private enterprise that one must look in this
matter because it takes so many years for
any forest to become productive, and again
the returns are perhaps to some extent
speculative, but if the area under forest is
sufficiently large the undertaking might be
expected to become commercially profitable.
For the moment all I have to do is to ask
the Question which I have put on the Paper,
as to whether anything is being done to
counteract this very serious shortage of timber
with which we must inevitably be faced in
perhaps thirty to forty years.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I am sure the House
will be grateful to the hon. Member for
having raised a question which naturally is
of great importance In reply, I can only say
to him that no decision has yet been arrived
at by the Government in regard to the recommendations made in the Report on afforestation in Northern Ireland presented by
the Commission on the Natural and Industrial Resources of Northern Ireland.
Perhaps, however, a brief outline of the
forestry operations conducted by the Ministry
of Agriculture might interest the House.
The following areas are being developed by
that Ministry :-
Acres.
Ballykelly, County Londonderry
Baronscourt, County Tyrone

3,080
Castlecaldwell, County Fermanagh

Knockmany, County Tyrome

Newcastle, County Down

...
4,683
or a total of
Included in the area of 3,080 acres at
Baronscourt are 260 acres, which were acquired by the Ministry in the past season.
The land at Newcastle (578 acres) was acquired by the Ministry in 1923, and planting
operations were commenced at that centre in
the autumn of that year.
The principal nursery-which is at Baronscourt-extends to about six acres, and from
this nursery seedlings are distributed to the
smaller nurseries at the other centres. When
the Ministry took over the work, the area
already under plantations was 503 acres only.
Since that time 1,226 additional acres have
been planted, or an average of about 300
acres each year. In the present season a
further area of 320 acres will be planted, and
next year it is hoped to plant a much larger
area. The total number of trees planted, including replacements, during the past three
seasons was 1,550,000.
This is a beginning by the Ministry of
Agriculture during the last few years in the
direction in which the hon. Member is pointing. He has certainly stated what is true,
namely, the alarming fact that the consumption of timber in this country vastly exceeds
the timber which is produced in the country.
I think this is a matter that is gradually
being brought home to this country and also
to Great Britain. The activities in Great
Britain are increasing, and I sincerely hope
the example set by the Ministry of Agriculture will be followed by those landowners in
this country who may be in a position to do
so.
Of course, we are all faced with the question of cost, and, as the hon. Member put it
himself, with the speculative value of the
crop, as well as with the idea of planting for
posterity and not seeing an actual return for
the money expended. We are faced with all
these difficuities, but as I have said, I believe the importance of the matter is being
borne more and more on the people throughout the country and I most sincerely hope
that in the next few years we shall see a
development which will surprise us, built on
foundations for which we owe a deep debt
of gratitude to our Ministry of Agriculture.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
British Women Married to Aliens.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Glendinning:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government will make representations
to the Imperial Government with the object
of having the British Nationality and
Status of Aliens Acts, 1914-1918, so amended
as to provide that British women married
to aliens, but residing in Northern Ireland,
in cases where marriage to an Alien deprives them of their British citizenship
while not immediately conferring upon
them citizenship of the country of which
the husband is a citizen, shall retain their
British citizenship until they become
naturalised in the country of which the
husband is a citizen.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I shall not put the
indulgence of hon. Members to a severe strain
in making, through the most hon. the
Leader of this House, the appeal to the
Government which stands in my name. The
question of the status of women of Northern
Ireland, married to aliens but still residing
in this country, may appear, at the first
glance, to be a matter of little importance.
Hon. Members may be inclined to take the
Oral Answers.
Oral Answers.

8 DECEMBER 1925
コ
view that very few women can be affected,
and that those so affected, having married
aliens, cease to be any concern of ours.
With regard to the first point, I regret
that I cannot submit any figures, but I am
informed by an authority which, through its
staff, comes in contact with such persons,
namely the U.S. Consulate in Belfast, that
a considerable number of women are affected,
and in any case reasonable consideration
should not be withheld from any section of
the community merely owing to their
numerical inferiority. As to the second
point, if a slight amendment to the law can
remove an inconvenience of a very aggravated character, and facilitate women, whose
only fault, if it can be called a fault, is to
have married foreigners, and if such amendment can be made without endangering any
other interest, I trust the Government will
not think it unreasonable on my part in asking that representations be made to the
Imperial Government.
As the law stands at present, women residing in Northern Ireland, but married to
certain aliens, have no nationality. For
example, an Irish woman-and of course this
applies also to women similarly situated in
Great Britain-married to a citizen of the
U.S. of America, does not become naturalized
until one year's residence in the U.S. is completed, after her marriage. In the meantime she suffers very considerable inconvenience in this country. Not being a citizen
of the U.S. she cannot get to that country,
to qualify by residence, unless under the
ordinary law governing immigration in that
country, and she may be obliged to remain
here for a certain time. But the majority of
cases on behalf of whom I speak are Irish
women residing here as wives of representatives of business firms, or Government
officials, of whom there are, as I have said,
a considerable number. Such women may
have to wait for years or may never have the
opportunity of residing for the required
period in the U.S. to enable them to become citizens thereof. They are neither U.S.
citizens, nor British citizens.
They may desire to visit the continent for
business reasons, or for pleasure or for
health, but they cannot obtain passports as
subjects of either country. A police registration certificate, or document of identity,
can be got which may permit such a person
to leave this country, but to get back again
raises all sorts of difficulties. Officials at
foreign ports or frontiers recognise no document but the official passport, and on presentation of any other document she is regarded with suspicion. If a lady, travelling
with her husband presents one of these certificates she is frequently subjected to positive
insult. This actually happened to a lady of
my acquaintance. The consequence is that
a person so situated must either be prepared
to risk difficulties of this nature, including
probable serious delay, or remain here in
durance, if not vile, at least inconvenient
and tantalising.
If these women remained British until
they became naturalised elsewhere the difficulties would never arise. I understand that
this matter has already been brought before
the Imperial Parliament, and if the Government of Northern Ireland supported the
proposal the change might be expedited. 1
am informed that other countries intend
legislation in the direction indicated. There
may be some objection of an international
character to the amendment, but if not I
submit that a genuine case exists, and that
a change in the law is urgently required.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I am sure the House will be sympathetic to
the question the hon. Member has asked, and
I regret I am not in a position to give him
an answer that he would deem to satisfactorily meet the point he has raised.
The existing law on this subject is laid
down in Section 10 of the British Nationality
and Status of Aliens Act, 1914, which provides
that the wife of an alien shall be deemed
to be an alien.
This Section regulates the
nationality of British female subjects married to aliens all over the Empire.
It is understood that the Imperial Government are considering this question and are
consulting the Dominions on the subject, but
no decision has yet been reached as to the
introduction of legislation. The matter is
reported in Hansard of 30th November, and
the Home Secretary's reply was:-
The present position is that the views of
the Governments of some of the selfgoverning Dominions on certain outstanding points have not yet been ascertained.
I feel sure the hon. Member (Mr. Glendinning) will realize this is a matter in which
it is obviously necessary that there should
be uniformity. Questions of nationality are
an "excepted" service in the Government
of Ireland Act, 1920, and legislation can only
be effected by the Imperial Parliament. I
have no doubt that hon. Members will share
my view that it is scarcely right for the
Northern Government to express any opinion,
but I will assure the hon. Member that I will
forward his question to the Home Office in
London, so that they will be well aware that
there is great interest being taken in this
question in Northern Ireland. Beyond that
I do not feel that I am capable of adding
anything by way of answer to the question
which the hon. Member has so eloquently
put before the House,
Business of the House.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move, That this House do now adjourn. I am proposing to-morrow, simultaneously with the action taken in the other
House, to move that this House do approve
of the Agreement which was made in London
on 3rd December, 1925-the Agreement which
has been entered into between the Governments of Great Britain, the Irish Free State,
and Northern Ireland.
Col. SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am sure
everyone is aware that the Prime Minister
is going to make a statement in the other
we
House to-morrow, and I suggest that
should be given an opportunity of hearing
it and that this House should not meet until
after that statement has been made by the
Prime Minister.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
That is a matter for you, Mr. Speaker, to
decide, and perhaps I might communicate
with you on the subject.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes before
Four o'clock.

9 DECEMBER 1925
THE SENΑΤΕ.

Wednesday, 9th December, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPERS PRESENTED TO PARLIAMENT.

under Section 32 of the Widows', Orphans'

on

The Boundary Agreement.
STATEMENT BY THE MARQUESS OF
LONDONDERRY.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): The Prime Minister
has thought it necessary-and I am in entire
agreement with him, and I feel that all the
Members of this House will be in agreement
with him that the announcement of the
Boundary Agreement should be made simultaneously in both Houses, and I would venture, therefore, to move the Motion which
appears in my name on the Paper. I will
commence the few remarks I desire to place
before the House by reading the Agreement
which has been arrived at between the
British Government, the Government of the
Irish Free State, and the Government of
Northern Ireland.
AGREEMENT
amending and supplementing the Articles
of Agreement for a Treaty between Great
Britain and Ireland, to which the force of
law was given by the Irish Free State
(Agreement) Act, 1922, and by the Constitution of the Irish Free State (Saorstat
Eireann) Act, 1922.
WHEREAS on the sixth day of December,
nineteen hundred and twenty-one, Articles
of Agreement for a Treaty between Great
Britain and Ireland were entered into.
AND WHEREAS the said Articles of
Agreement were duly ratified and given the
force of law by the Irish Free State (Agreement) Act, 1922, and by the Constitution of
the Irish Free State (Saorstat Eireann) Act,
1022.
AND WHEREAS the progress of events
and the improved relations now subsisting
between the British Government, the
Government of the Irish Free State, and
the Government of Northern Ireland, and
their respective peoples, make it desirable
to amend and supplement the said Articles
of Agreement so as to avoid any causes of
friction which might mar or retard the
further growth of friendly relations between the said governments and peoples.
AND WHEREAS the British Government and the Government of the Irish Free
State, being united in amity in this undertaking with the Government of Northern
Ireland, and being resolved mutually to aid
one another in a spirit of neighbourly comradeship, hereby agree as follows:-
1. The powers conferred by the proviso
to Article 12 of the said Articles of Agreement on the Commission therein mentioned
are hereby revoked, and the extent of
Northern Ireland for the purposes of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, and of
the said Articles of Agreement, shall be
such as was fixed by sub-Section (2) of
Section one of that Act.
2. The Irish Free State is hereby released from the obligation under Article 5
of the said Articles of Agreement to
assume the liability therein mentioned.
3. The Irish Free State hereby assumes
all liability undertaken by the British
Government in respect of malicious
damage done since the twenty-first day of
January, nineteen hundred and nineteen,
to property in the area now under the
jurisdiction of the Parliament and Government of the Irish Free State, and the
Government of the Irish Free State shall
repay to the British Government, at such
time or times and in such manner as may
be agreed upon, moneys already paid by
the British Government in respect of such
damage, or liable to be so paid under
obligations already incurred.
4. The Government of the Irish Free
State hereby agrees to promote legislation
increasing by ten per cent. the measure of
compensation under the Damage to Property (Compensation) Act, 1923, in respect
of malicious damage to property done in
the area now under the jurisdiction of the
The Boundary Agreement. 432
The Boundary Agreement.
THE SENATE
['The Marquess of Londonderry]
Parliament and Government of the Irish
Free State between the eleventh day of
July, nineteen hundred and twenty-one,
and the twelfth day of May, nineteen
hundred and twenty-three, and providing
for the payment of such additional
compensation by the issue of Five per cent.
Compensation Stock or Bonds.
5. The powers in relation to Northern
Ireland which by the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, are made powers of the
Council of Ireland, shall be and are hereby transferred to and shall become powers
of the Parliament and the Government of
Northern Ireland; and the Governments
of the Irish Free State and of Northern
Ireland shall meet together as and when
necessary for the purpose of considering
matters of common interest arising out of
connected with the exercise and
administration of the said powers.
or
6. This Agreement is subject to confirmation by the British Parliament and
by the Oireachtas of the Irish Free State,
and the Act of the British Parliament confirming this Agreement shall fix the date
as from which the transfer of the powers
of the Council of Ireland under this
Agreement is to take effect.
Dated this 3rd day of December, 1925.
Signed on behalf of the British Government, the Government of the Irish Free
State; and the Government of Northern
Ireland:
The occasion on which we meet to-day is
indeed a very important one, and I need
hardly say it is with very great pride and
also with great pleasure that I have read out
the document to which you have just listened.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). The Boundary
question is one which has been in our minds
now for some considerable time. In fact,
that question has absorbed all our thoughts,
it has hampered all our activities, and it has
in my judgment, to a very large extent,
sterilised our capacity for progress. Now we
have the satisfaction of feeling that the
Boundary question is out of the way, and
that we can continue our avocations and
work without the over-shadowing influence
of something of which we did not know what
the outcome would be. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear).
I should like very much and I know that
in this I am voicing the opinion of every
Member of this House-to heartily congratulate our Prime Minister. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear). As you know quite well,
he has presided over our fortunes for five
years, and we know that during the whole of
that time he has concentrated his energies
and his activities for the benefit of the
Province which he loves so well. He has
kept a definite policy in front of him.
He
has been determined to maintain the integrity of this Province, and he has done
everything that lay in his power to maintain
relations of amity with the Free State, and
I do feel that in the Agreement which I have
had the opportunity of reading out to-day
the policy which he supported on every
possible occasion has been vindicated in
every detail. I do feel that the thanks of
this Province are due to the man who has
sacrificed a great deal on our behalf, the man
who has come through all the dangers and
difficulties with success, and has become
entitled to his name being placed on the Roll
of great Ulstermen who have served this
Province and who have served this Empire
in the past. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
I feel also that we should take this opportunity of expressing our gratitude to the
British Government and to the people of
Great Britain. We know that the Boundary
Commission has been in existence for some
considerable time, and that we have watched
anxiously, as far as we were able to do so,
its activities and deliberations, or what we
knew of them, which was very little, but all
we could know and realize was the measure
and the degree of the dangers and difficulties
which would come about if that Commission
ever reported. By the action of Mr. Baldwin
all that has been obviated, and the policy
which Sir James Craig has always had in his
mind of settling the Boundary question amicably between North and South has been
brought about by the action of Mr. Baldwin,
the Prime Minister of Great Britain.
With reference to the Agreement itself, we
can feel that many points can now be amicably arranged. We can discuss and I hope
modify, and perhaps eventually remove entirely the present Customs barrier regulations. We can work together with the Free
State to secure better and more humane
handling of our live stock. We can join with
them in discussing the question of the foot
and mouth disease scourge, so that we can
present a united certificate to our largest markets across the water that our stock are free
from all disease, so that they will be received
on the other side on that understanding from
the Governments of the North and South.
With regard to the Services handed over to
us in their entirety, we can consider the development of our fisheries, and I do hope that
business men especially will realize that there
is a great onportunity now for re-opening the
channels of business between North and
South on which naturally the prosperity of
this Province depends, and on which depends
the prosperity of the whole of Ireland.
There is in the further Agreement arrived
at the question of prisoners, which is a matter that interests my hon. Friend opposite
(Mr. Dorman) no less than it interests all
433 The Boundary Agreement.

9 DECEMBER 1925
The Boundary Agreement.
of us. There have been negotiations between Mr. Baldwin and the Prime Minister
of Northern Ireland, and I am entitled to say
to the House that the cases of prisoners convicted in Northern Ireland in respect of
offences committed during the period of the
disturbances shall be reviewed by the
British Government, whose decision in each
case shall be accepted by the Government of
Northern Ireland. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) That, I think is a satisfactory solution of the problem of the political prisoners
who are at the present moment incarcerated.
The Agreement, in my judgment, marks the
beginning of a new era, and I would venture
to ask those opponents of the Agreement
-and I am sorry to think that some existwhat alternative they would suggest. We
welcome the Agreement as evidence of what
I may call the spirit of Locarno, that spirit
which breathes the longing of the whole
human race after ten years of suffering of
the curse of war for the blessings of peace
and good-will amongst people. We feel that
during and since the war, by the evolution
of time and by the bitter experiences through
which we have all passed, people all over the
world have come to realise that nothing can
really be achieved in the direction of progress
except by the amicable settlement of our
differences and by co-operation for the benefit of the community at large.
Article 12 of the Treaty set up the Commission. That Article has now disappeared.
Article 5 is another Article which has also
gone by the board, but it is an Article which
does not actually concern us, and I would
venture to suggest to all my friends in
Ulster that they should dismiss that Article
entirely from their minds. It may appear
a great concession which Great Britain has
made to Ireland, but I do feel that we are
inclined to think that no concession would
be too great if peace was the result of that
concession. This concession is not as great
as it might appear to be. There are many
adjustments to be made which were eloquently put forward in the speech of Mr.
Churchill in the Imperial House of Commons
yesterday, and we can believe that if we have
peace on the one side the concession made is
in no sense a heavy one.
In these few words I would heartily commend this Motion to the House. This Agreement embodies the policy which the Government, and the people of Ulster have maintained from the very beginning of this unhappy controversy, which I feel has now
happily concluded. That policy which we
maintained was that the Boundary between
Ulster and the Free State could only be
settled by negotiations between the two
Governments themselves concerned, and in
no other way could it be settled. I have
great pleasure in moving,
That this House approves of the Agreement, dated 3rd December, 1925, entered
into between the Governments of Great
Britain, the Irish Free State, and Northern
Ireland.
Mr. DORMAN: We opened our proceedings
to-day, as we open them every day, by offering up a prayer that we might be enabled to
approach the subjects under consideration
free from personal interest and prejudice. 1
do not think there has ever been a subject
submitted to the consideration of the representatives of this country that more requires
on the part of those who are called upon to
give it consideration the principle and the
spirit contained in that prayer. Representatives of the Free State and representatives
of Northern Ireland both addressed a message
to the peoples of their various areas on the
completion of this particular Agreement, and
I desire personally that in the future the
spirit that is displayed in these messages
should be the ruling factor in my life in
view of this great crisis in the history of
our country.
The representatives of the Free State
stated:-
To-day we have sown the seeds of peace,
and the right hon. Gentleman, the Prime
Minister of Northern Ireland, stated: that,
The signatories of the Agreement separated with a cordiality which I sincerely
trust will result in more friendly relations
being permanently maintained among all
classes and creeds throughout Ireland.
This is not the first occasion in the history
of our country when a final settlement was
supposed to have been arrived at to satisfactorily remove all the differences that were
dividing our people and perpetutating a spirit
of hatred and ill-will, instead of a spirit of
peace and consideration for one another.
On all these occasions a final settlement was
proved in operation to be not a final settlement, but on not one of these occasions have
the same opportunities been accorded to the
representatives of the people, and to the
people themselves, to enter into the spirit
of the final settlement and by their conduct
make it a really final settlement of the
divisions of our native country which, I
believe, all Irishmen are anxious to see
obtained.
I notice that at both ends of the country
already there are individuals who are appealing to the public in a spirit which, if it is
allowed to spread, will not be at all likely to
lead to this particular Agreement being a
final settlement of this great question.
notice that one gentleman in the South has
made this statement:-
I
Now we are to be sold in perpetuity.
Our Belfast masters who flogged us with
The Boundary Agreement.
The Boundary Agreement.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
whips in the past will flog us with scorpions
now.
Another gentleman has made use of this
statement:-
We have been swindled, and we have
bribed the burglar. We abandon for ever
a large number of Irishmen to be overruled and overlorded by the old Orange
ascendancy; we know what that means.
Those are public expressions of eminent
men in the Free State.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Not
eminent men.
Mr. DORMAN: I will not mention names.
I am trying to bring before the right hon.
and hon. Members of the Senate the necessity
of approaching this question in a spirit of
good-will. On the other hand there was an
utterance in the Northern area, wherein a
gentleman stated:-
In 1641 a deliberate attempt was made to
extirpate us, and in the same century a
wonderful deliverance was granted to our
ancestors at Derry. It is in no boastful or
vain-glorious spirit that we refer to these
outstanding historical facts, but rather to
emphasise the note struck in so many
churches yesterday. God has been our sure
defence through all generations, and it is
but right that we should keep this solemn
and all-important fact before our minds,
and before the minds of the young.
The same spirit is manifested in all those
statements, and it is because our people have
in the past unfortunately been guided by a
spirit such as this, on the part of both sides
in Ireland, that the unfortunate position
exists, that does exist, to-day. The same
gentleman also stated:-
We have too often fallen far short of the
ideal that should be constantly before us,
but God has been gracious and He has once
more delivered us out of a difficulty, and
there is, therefore, good cause for deep
thankfulness in all our hearts. The God
of battles was on our side, and we came
out triumphant.
I believe that if this settlement is to be a
final settlement the people at both ends of
this country must cease appealing to the God
of battles, and instead of appealing to the
God of battles to assist them in settling
differences inside the national area, they
must be satisfied to make an appeal to the
God of peace, and in their own conduct and
operations follow the principles laid down
by the Prince of Peace. I know there is considerable opposition to the abolition of
Clause 5 of the original Treaty, but it
appears to me that the present Agreement,
which places the responsibility of coming to
a settlement on all questions between North
and South upon the Governments of those
two countries, is a better method of arriving
at a satisfactory conclusion than for an
extra Parliamentary body to be appealed to
to recommend certain measures
assembly such as the Parliaments of
Northern and Southern Ireland.
to
an
I whole-heartedly support this Motion, and
I hope the ultimate outcome of it will be
that both ends of Ireland will be brought
together and will dwell in amity and peace
together, and not manifest any of the strife
of the past. If this is to be brought about
Pressmen, public men, clergymen, and the
public in general must seek a solution in a
spirit of peace and not in a spirit of enmity
towards any other section of the country.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I would just like
to associate myself with the noble Marquess,
the Leader of the House, in approval of this
Agreement. I represent a Border County
that has been very much in evidence during
the past few years in connection with this
question, and we will accept this Agreement
with the same determination that we showed
to remain in Ulster. We accept this Agreement, so ably made by the Prime Ministers
of Great Britain, the Irish Free State, and
Northern Ireland, in the spirit in which we
are determined to live in future with our
fellow countrymen in Ireland, and that will
be in an amicable spirit.
Mr. LESLIE: There seems little need for
further speech this afternoon, but it would
perhaps not be altogether well that any undue
measure of silence should be self-imposed on
our Members, because there must be a natural
anxiety to join in the chorus of satisfaction
which is felt and is reverberating throughout
our Province as a consequence of the news
which we received first in the newspapers and
which is now confirmed by the noble Marquess. We here, I am sure, are cordially in
agreement with the Leader of the House that
it
was only right and fitting that the
announcement should be made to us sitting
here, for this Chamber is one of the elements
of the Constitutional construction, and it
would not have been seemly that so important an announcement should not have been
made here.
I do not propose, of course, to follow the
noble Marquess through the details of the
Agreement, nor do I propose to follow the
hon. Gentleman in front of me (Mr. Dorman)
through his historical researches of the 17th
century. Rather would I join in rejoicing
that the bogey of the Boundary question has
now been laid, and in congratulations to the
Prime Minister on the result of his five years
of office, five years spent, as indeed it has
been spent, in the service of the Province.
When congratulating the Prime Minister. I
cannot refrain from offering my congratula-
437 The Boundary Agreement.
The Boundary Agreement. 438
9 DECEMBER 1925

tions also to the members of the Prime Minister's Cabinet, because I recognize, as I am
sure we all recognize, that the policy the
Prime Minister has so consistently pursued
throughout this difficult period is a policy
which he had arrived at in constant consultation with his colleagues, and in which he had
their unvarying and unfailing support. The
whole Cabinet must be joined in our congratulations to Sir James Craig.
Again, Great Britain, as the centre of the
British Empire, seems also entitled to congratulations, for by taking the step which
they did take to meet the difficulty, or
rather to forestall the difficulty which would
have arisen had the Boundary Commission
actually reported-had not the Government
of Great Britain, acting, I believe, on the
initiative of the Prime Minister of Great
Britain, taken steps to forestall the issue of
the Boundary Report, events which cannot be
contemplated with equanimity would possibly
have followed. One of the results must
almost certainly have been to have left something in the nature of a canker in the very
heart of the Empire. Any risk of that has
been entirely removed by the very statesmanlike action which has been taken in Great
Britain, and we do not admire that statesmanlike action less because it is perhaps to
some extent based on advice which has been
tendered to them again and again from
Northern Ireland.
The advantages that may be expected to
flow from the new Treaty have been outlined by the noble Marquess. Both trade
in the ordinary sense, and agriculture, particularly as regards live stock, will benefit. We
all look forward to the I do not know what
word to use-I cannot say the Council of
Ireland because that has gone, but we all
look forward to the joint meeting of the
representatives of North and South, and we
look forward to great results from their
meetings on such points as are necessarily
open to discussion between both Governments. We had an instance of such
subject the other day when we were discussing drainage matters. That is one of
the points on which the new system will have
a chance of getting to work.
a
The noble Leader of the House alluded to
some consideration being shown to prisoners.
I do not think, in fact I am sure, that that
will not awaken any feeling of resentment on
the part of anyone. That has always been
the custom, and I think it is a good custom,
when a settlement has been arrived at after
a period of political strife. Even if it passed
from the more civilised methods of controversy into more savage methods of attack it
has always been the custom to exercise the
Royal Prerogative, and although I should not
myself have thought that there was any great
cause of complaint as regards the severity of
the sentences, one has to remember that
there is no Court of Criminal Appeal here,
and it is, therefore, natural that those sentences should be reviewed and advice should
be tendered to this Government on the
matter.
I understand, I think correctly, that the
examination of these sentences only means
that advice will be tendered to our Government as to the advice our Government will
tender to his Grace, and that anything that
is done will be purely an act of grace on the
part of Northern Ireland. We look forward to
the new era, which, we may expect. I am sure
there is no responsible person in Northern
Ireland who will want to look into the finance
of the matter, whether it is the finance of
the till or the finance of the ledger. We
shall no more raise any question on that
point than we should on the number of windows to be provided in the Parliament House
which is to be erected. Rather should we
all join in complete satisfaction at the result arrived at, and we shall, of course, do
our best to influence people in the direction
of peace and good-will.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I will
not detain the House a couple of minutes,
but I would like to be associated with this
Motion. I heard a statement by the Prime
Minister in the other House which I had not
heard before, and which a great many people
had not heard, namely, that practically the
same Agreement as this had been arrived at
between Sir James Craig and the late Mr.
Michael Collins in the early days of this
controversy. The Council of Ireland was to
disappear, and there would have been only a
rectification of the frontier between the two
countries. That is a wonderful justification
of our action in approving of this Agreement.
I do not think the hon. Member (Mr. Dorman) need have any qualms about there being
any further appeals to the God of War. The
appeal in the future will be all to the God
of Peace. As I said in another place, the less
we say, either laudatory or condemnatory, the
better for the peace of the country. I see for
the future of Ireland a better and brighter
era than has ever been possible before, and 1
have great pleasure in supporting the Motion.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN: Speaking as
one from our north-west frontier, I also
should like to offer congratulations to our
Prime Minister. The settlement of this Border question affects me more than any other
Member of the Senate, because I live not more
than a stone's throw from the Border. I am
overlooked from the Free State, and my house
suffered from Republican bullets during the
last trouble we had. I cannot tell how relieved are the feelings of all of us who live
on the Border by this settlement, which I
hope will bring in a new era of peace and
prosperity for all Irishmen.
Adjournment.
The Boundary Agreement.

THE SENATE
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I would like
to embrace this opportunity of joining
in the congratulations which our Prime
Minister and the Government are deservedly receiving on the occasion of the
settlement of this Boundary question. I feel
it is difficult to find words to adequately express the gratitude which the loyal people of
Northern Ireland owe to the Prime Minister
and the Government. Ever since they took
office five years ago they have displayed
ability, courage, wisdom, and perseverance,
and they have placed us under an immense
debt of gratitude to them. We cannot but
feel that in the settlement of this Boundary
question they have added one more to the
many debts which we owe them.
I am glad to hear from the noble Marquess,
the Leader of the House, that there are
likely to be more friendly relations established between the two parts of Ireland, and
I sincerely trust they will be for the benefit
of the country.
On Question, Motion "That this House
approves of the Agreement, dated 3rd December, 1925, entered into between the Governments of Great Britain, the Irish Free State,
and Northern Ireland," agreed to.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
It is not proposed that the House should sit
to-morrow, but there will be a purely formal
sitting on Monday for the purpose of receiving
Bills from another place.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes before Four o'clock.
t
Adjournment.


Superannuation Bill.

ated


THE SEΝΑΤΕ.

Monday, 14th December, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance: Minute appointing a
new member and Chairman of the Charities
Advisory Committee in place of the Right
Hon. Sir Denis Henry, Bart., deceased.
Laid before the House, pursuant to Act,
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Superannuation Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Superannuation Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill ordered to be read a Second time
to-morrow.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move, that
this House do now adjourn. To-morrow we
will have a more or less formal sitting to receive three Bills from another place for
First Reading, namely, the Education
(Administrative Provisions) Bill, Appropriation (No. 2) Bill, and Unemployment Fund
(No. 2) Bill.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Can the
noble Leader of the House give us some little
idea of what are likely to be the sittings of
the House during the remainder of the
Session?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
It is proposed that we should sit on Tuesday,
Wednesday, and Thursday, and it is hoped
that all the business will be completed on
Thursday. The Prorogation of Parliament
takes place on the Tuesday following. It
might be necessary to sit on Friday next, but
I am not able to anticipate that that is at all
probable.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
Minutes after Three o'clock.
Interned Prisoners.
Interned Prisoners.
THE SENATE
THE SENΑΤΕ.

The Senate met at a quarter after Three of
the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Message from the Commons,
They have passed the Appropriation (No.
2) Bill, the Unemployment Fund (No. 2) Bill,
and the Education (Administrative Provisions) Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be read a Second
time to-morrow.
Unemployment Fund (No. 2) Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be read a Second
time to-morrow.
Education (Administrative
Provisions) Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First

Interned Prisoners.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. DORMAN:
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Government intend to take any steps
with regard to prisoners kept in custody without trial under the Civil Authorities (Special
Powers) Act, many of whose families are thus
left dependent on charity, and if, in view of
the recent agreement with the Free State, all
interned prisoners will either be releasd, or
brought to trial at the earliest possible
moment.
were
Mr. DORMAN: At the time I put this
question on the Order Paper I had no knowledge of the fact that a similar question was
being asked in another place. I have no
knowledge whatever of my fellow citizens who
were deprived of their liberty and
incarcerated without trial, nor have I been
asked on their behalf to place this question
on the Paper. I have done so under a deep
sense of personal responsibility and acknowledging my duty in trying to preserve the
glorious rights that have been inherited from
our forefathers, the right of personal liberty
which has been guaranteed to the citizens of
Great Britain for a period of over seven
hundred years.
Some few years ago, when the Parliament
Act was placed on the Statute Book, a book
was published entitled "British Citizenship,"
as a protest against the deprivation of the
Civil rights of the community through the
Executive passing into law an Act of Parliament which was depriving them of the right
to exercise the vote on a Bill which meant a
great constitutional change. These articles
were written by prominent members of the
great Unionist Party, and in one of these
articles, written by Lord Willoughby de-
Broke, he speaks of the tendency of modern
times, a tendency to destroy liberty that must
be noticed. This tendency, he says, is to
remit either to a Government Department or
special bodies of Commissioners jurisdiction
over matters which in this country it has
hitherto been customary to reserve either to
Parliament or to the Courts of Justice, and in
this way to hand over the liberty and
property of the subject to fresh authorities
from whose decision there is in many cases
to be no appeal to a Court of Law.
I have been associated for many years with
a Society, thousands of whose members in the
Seventeenth Century--at one period over four
thousand of them were deprived of their
freedom and incarcerated in gaol, in many
instances without trial. These men, being
conscious of their own good intentions,
refused to give security for their good
behaviour.
Many of them languished and
died in gaol. I see the possibility of an Act
such as this, which may and has no doubt
been considered necessary by the Executive,
being abused. For instance, during the late
pogrom in Belfast it was my custom to go
every week into a most disturbed area where
people were barbarously done to death in the
presence of their families. I went into a
street to sympathise with a man who was a
Socialist and who had no sympathy whatever with the outrageous and scandalous
conduct being perpetrated by both sides in
Belfast. While I was there one evening my
friend's child was shot at his own door.
Shortly after that I contested a seat for
Municipal honours in the Corporation, and
from the platform of the man whom I was
opposing the question was several times
asked, "Where was Mr. Dorman when the
shooting was going on?" I publicly declared where I was; that I was going in and
out of a Catholic district to see a friend,
notwithstanding all the violence that was
being perpetrated. Is it not possible that
a matter like that can get to the ears of
some officer of the Executive, and that he
might impute to me, as a law abiding citizen.
an evil intention to perpetrate a crime, and
that in consequence of that intention being
445 Interned Prisoners.

15 DECEMBER 1925
Superannuation Bill.
imputed to me as a citizen, I am liable to
be deprived of my liberty and deprived of
the right to appear before
Court of
Justice?
a
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): No.
Mr. DORMAN: The noble Marquess says
no. I will come to that just now. I am
liable, I believe, to be deprived of my
liberty, at least without the right to appeal
to a Court of Justice, before whom it becomes the duty of the Executive and the
Crown to prove me, as a citizen, guilty of
some crime. That is one of the inalienable
rights of every citizen of Northern Ireland.
Were I thus arrested I would refuse on
principle to appeal to any Committee, other
than to the properly constituted Court, before whom I have the right to be considered
innocent until my guilt is proved by my
accusers. The legal profession, throughout
the centuries, have put up a magnificent fight
for the retention of British freedom. Men
of property, and professional men, have
suffered, and their names are reverenced by
the community to-day because of what they
did suffer for the retention of this right of
personal freedom. In this part of the country we are very fond of proposing toasts. I
often propose two myself, and I have drunk
a toast to a living King and a dead King.
But there is a third toast I frequently participate in honouring, and it is this: "Our
Civil and Religious Liberties we will maintain."
I hold that this particular Act of Parliament under which our fellow-citizens are
incarcerated and deprived of their freedom,
is to a very large extent, calculated to undermine the foundation of freedom in the area
of Northern Ireland. Therefore, I hope the
Government will take the earliest opportunity to review the cases of these men, who
are, in my opinion, improperly deprived of
their freedom and their rights as citizens.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The persons referred to in this question were
arrested and interned as being persons who
were suspected of acting, or of having acted,
or of being about to act in a manner prejudicial to the preservation of the peace or
the maintenance of order.
I am not prepared to make any statement
in regard to the matters raised in this question, but I would draw the attention of the
Senator to the reply, which was given by my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, to a supplementary question asked
in another place on the same subject on the
8th instant, to the effect that these men have
the right to go before the Advisory Committee, and that the Minister of Home Affairs
was prepared to give every consideration to
any advice which he might receive from that
Committee. I may add that I am informed
that some of these men have already availed
themselves of their right to appear before
this Committee.
Superannuation Bill,
Order for Second Reading read.
The
MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
This Bill deals purely with administrative
difficulties which have arisen in regard to
the superannuation of certain members of
the Civil Service of Northern Ireland, and
supplies the necessary statutory basis for the
undoubted pension rights of some of our
Civil Servants. For instance, one of the conditions governing the right of a Civil Servant
to pension is the possession of a certificate
from the Civil Service Commission.
That
Commission was not set up in Northern Ireland for some time after the establishment
of our Government with its Civil Service.
Clause 1 provides for meeting the difficulty
which has arisen, owing to the delay which
has consequently occurred in the issue of
these certificates. It empowers the Ministry
of Finance, for superannuation purposes, to
certify the commencement of an officers' service at the date on which it actually began,
although that date be necessarily prior to
the date of the issue to him of the Civil Ser-
This power is limited to
vice certificate.
twelve months.
The Bill further deals with the superannuation of certain transferred Irish officers,
and includes within the ambit of the Superannuation Acts, those transferred Irish
officers who, at the time of the transfer, were
not permanent Civil Servants, but have since
been received into the ranks of established
Civil Servants in Northern Ireland. Hon.
Members will find that in sub-Section (1) of
Clause 2.
Sub-Section (2) of the same Clause deals
with transferred officers, whose salaries were
paid from certain funds administered by De
partments in Dublin, which were on apportionment merged into a Northern Ireland
Capital Fund. The salaries of these officers
are now met directly from the Parliamentary Vote. Some of these officers have now
been given established posts in Northern Ireland, but their past service in Dublin cannot,
under present circumstances, count for pension, because at the date of transfer a Superannuation Scheme, although authorised by
the Treasury, had not yet been set up. This
sub-Section empowers the Ministry of
Finance to admit for pension service formerly paid out of a fund, where the conditions appear to warrant it.
Superannuation Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
a
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
as
carried
Clause 3 repeals Section 72 of our Education Act of 1923 and re-enacts it in a more
suitable form. The Clause provided that any
person appointed as an Inspector in the
Ministry of Education after the passing of
the Education Act, if he had previous service
as a teacher, would be allowed to reckon that
service for purposes of pension. No such person has, in fact, been yet appointed, and in
one respect the clause is considered to be too
wide, and in another respect too limited.
It is too wide, because service
teacher may not have
with it the same pension privileges
as are accorded to a Civil Servant. The
new clause gives power to the Ministry of
Finance to modify the pension terms if there
was a difference in benefits, and also provides
that the pension may be apportioned between
voted moneys and the Teachers' Pension
Fund concerned. The former clause was too
narrow, inasmuch as it did not include in
its scope those Inspectors who at the date of
the passing of the Education Act were in the
service of the Ministry of Education and had
as teachers. The amendprevious service
ment provides that they shall be placed in
the same position as persons subsequently
appointed to be Inspectors.
Clause 4 provides that the Ministry of
Finance, in the exercise of its powers under
Clauses 2 and 3 of this Bill, shall take into
consideration any awards made to transferred
Irish Officers by the Civil Service Committee.
As I have said, this is a technical measure
of purely administrative scope rendered
necessary to ensure and preserve the rights
of certain of our Civil Servants. I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
a
Committee of the
Bill committed to
whole House for to-morrow.
COLERAINE ELECTRICITY SPECIAL
ORDER.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move: That the Special
Order made by the Electricity Commissioners
for Northern Ireland under the Electricity
(Supply) Acts, 1882 to 1919, in respect of the
Urban District of Coleraine, and parts of the
Rural District of Coleraine in the County of
Londonderry, be approved.
This is the usual order empowering the
Urban District of Coleraine to supply electricity for public and private purposes.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to suggest that this House do now adjourn during pleasure. We are awaiting a
Bill from another place, the Intoxicating
Liquor (Finance) Bill.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Message from the Commons.
They have passed the Intoxicating liquor
(Finance) Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Intoxicating Liquor (Finance) Bill.
Moved, That this Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be read a Second

Adjournment.
