Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading
of this Bill it is not necessary for me
to make any speech. It is purely a financial
Measure providing that a certain sum of
money should be set aside out of the Consolidated Fund, and how it should be appropriated. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.
Unemployment Fund (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
now are
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move the
Second Reading of the Unemployment Fund
(No. 2) Bill. The object of this Bill is to
enable payment of Unemployment Insurance
benefit to be continued by renewing the
statutory powers of the Ministry of Finance,
which
almost exhausted, of advancing moneys to the Unemployment Fund.
Existing Statutes have empowered the Ministry to advance up to a total of £4,250,000,
and as the actual amount of advances made
to the Fund up to the present date is in
excess of £4,000,000, it is clearly necessary
that Parliament should extend this authority so as to cover the period up to the next
Session of Parliament. It is, of course, impossible to make an accurate forecast of the
conditions of trade and industry and the degree of unemployment in the immediate
future, but it is expected that the further
sum of £500,000 will suffice to carry the Fund
along up to the 31st March next.
to
our
Reference has been made in another place
by the right hon. Gentleman the Minister
of Finance to an agreement which has been
reached with the Imperial Governmentan agreement which contemplates the
giving of assistance
Government in respect of the condition of unemployment, which, I need not remind you, is
relatively so much heavier in this area than
in Great Britain. The details of the arrangement which has been made must first be
placed before the Imperial Parliament and,
therefore, I am not at liberty to go further
than to express the hope that it will afford
such a measure of relief as will enable us to
continue to meet these heavy claims until
such time as the conditions of trade and industry improve.
I beg to move.
Mr. DORMAN: I am sure hon. Members
of this House regret that the Government
has found it necessary again to seek an extension of loans for the purpose of financing
the Unemployment Fund of Northern Ireland. The Government in the other House nas
asked for suggestions, and I thought it would
perhaps be appropriate if I took the opportunity, if I am in order, to make some suggestions to the Minister of Labour, as to some
methods that might be adopted for the purpose of alleviating the present unfortunate
condition of unemployment in Northern Ireland. So far certain remedies have been put
forward, and have, unfortunately, been
largely adopted, the result of which has not
proved an effective remedy for unemployment.
The first remedy set forth was the remedy
of reducing the remuneration of the workers
themselves in the various industries of the
country. During the pre-war period in this
country and in the United States of America
that particular remedy was widely adopted,
followed by disastrous results in both countries, but fortunately for the people and the
industries of the United States of America
the Princes of Industry, those who controlled the material products and the machinery
of that country, led, I have reason to believe,
mostly by the inspiration of Mr. Henry Ford
and a few other industrial magnates of the
United States, started a widespread protest
in the Press against a reduction in wages as
a solution of the problem of unemployment.
The peculiar thing about that particular
propaganda was this: that the languishing
P
Unemployment Fund Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman.]
industries of the United States of America,
who, in conjunction with Great Britain, if
my memory serves me right, had at that particular epoch an unemployed mass of workers of between 6,000,000 and 7,000,000, revived. As the aggregate number of unemployed in Great Britain and Northern Ireland has never attained to 1,500,000, it would
therefore follow that when the United States
widely adopted that theory of reducing wages
as a remedy for unemployment they must
have had an aggregate of between 4,000,000
and 5,000,000 in the ranks of their unemployed. Now they have comparatively few, if
any, unemployed in the industries of that
particular country.
I would, therefore, suggest that the further
reduction of the purchasing capacity of the
people in our own area will not prove a satisfactory solution of the unfortunate position
of the unemployed. We know that one particular section of the workers of Northern
Ireland have during recent years had their
wages, which means their purchasing capacity, reduced, to the extent of from 20 per
cent. to probably over 50 per cent. of their
average weekly wage. These men and women
are necessarily compelled to expend the aggregate amount of their earnings from week
to week, and thereby stimulate the production
of goods they consume, and in so far as their
purchasing power is reduced it is re-acting
detrimentally upon those engaged in the
other industries supplying their needs.
I find that during the three years ending
December, 1924, the aggregate wages of the
weekly wage-earners in Northern Ireland
and Great Britain have been reduced
by a sum equivalent to over £500,000,000.
During the year 1924 the total aggregate foreign trade of Great Britain and
Northern Ireland of products manufactured
in Great Britain and Northern Ireland only
came to the sum of between £700,000,000 and
£800,000,000. During those years there was
practically no increase of the foreign trade
of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, but
I contend we have reduced the demand for
products inside the area to a sum equivalent
to five-eighths of our total foreign trade; that
is to say, had we doubled our foreign trade
during those three years we would only have
the advantage of the balance of £300,000,000,
taking into consideration the reduced purchasing capacity of the people in Great
Britain and Northern Ireland.
I notice there was sent to all Members of
the Imperial Parliament during the last
week, a letter on behalf of those engaged in
the production of iron and steel in Great
Britain. They prove that their producing
capacity has been increased by fifty per
cent. since pre-war years, and that their present aggregate volume of trade is just 96 per
cent. of their pre-war trade. That is to say,
it is now but 4 per cent below the pre-war
volume of trade. But I notice also that in
six or seven of the largest firms engaged in
that particular industry they have increased
their capital out of reserves from 25 per
cent. to 50 per cent, and in some cases to
100 per cent. and 200 per cent. It seems to
me that instead of aiming at reducing the
purchasing capacity of those engaged in those
particular industries attention might profitably be directed to the burden on capital,
of which the people must of necessity bear
a larger share if the pre-war rate of dividend upon investments is to be kept up.
There is another question, and that is
this. What is being done to stimulate a
demand for the products of the Northern
area over which the Government has control?
I speak of things I know of and of things I
see. We have many products, large volumes
of which are consumed in Northern Ireland,
and which are produced outside that area,
that could be produced as good, as pure, and
as cheap inside the Northern area. But
the desire to purchase these products
does not possess the minds of the people.
I will mention one or two industriesjam, for instance. We can produce as pure
and as good jam in Northern Ireland, and
are producing it, as can be produced in any
part of the world, and manufactured from
Northern Ireland fruit. I make bold to say
that if Members of this House took a journey
through the principal streets of Belfast, or
sent a messenger into the various grocers
shops to purchase a one or two-pound pot
of jam without giving a description of the
jam they required, they would have returned
to them jam manufactured outside Northern
Ireland.
That holds good with regard to nearly all
industries. For instance, I understand the
Government has given a subsidy of £8,000
to those who have sunk their capital in the
exploitation of coal at Coalisland. Would
it not be more advisable and perhaps more
profitable, if the Government started some
kind of propaganda to create in the minds
of the coal consumers of Northern Ireland a
desire to support this particular industry
which, as I have stated before in this House,
is capable of absorbing from the ranks of
the unemployed anything from 800 to 1,000
Then we have another stunt or propaganda being carried on just now in what
we are told is the newspaper with the largest
circulation in Great Britain and Northern
Ireland, the stunt of reducing the standard
of comfort of those engaged in the Civil
Service. I do hope our Statesmen and our
Cabinet are not liable to be obsessed with
the mass psychology that these stunts produce when they are repeated often enough.
men.
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Education Bill.
16 DECEMBER 1925
I make this statement, because in another
place the Opposition was called upon for
some useful suggestions that might be calculated to alleviate the problem of unemployment that unfortunately exists in our midst.
I hope these suggestions may contain some
little grain of wisdom which Ministers may
in their wisdom see is in some measure
applied as part of the solution of this unemployment problem.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I rise to
say, like the previous speaker, how much
I regret that these Unemployment Fund
Bills are necessary. I am sure everyone in
this House deplores the fact that trade has
not improved as we hoped it would, and that
there is such a considerable number of unemployed who cannot be absorbed in industry.
I would not venture to say I have any
suggestions to put forward to remedy
the terrible trouble through which
are passing. I listened very attentively
to the previous speaker when he said he
was about to make some suggestions that
might be useful, but I think a solution of
this unemployment problem is beyond the
capacity of any person at the present time.
It does not afflict Northern Ireland or even
Great Britain alone. All other countries
seem to be suffering, if not to the same extent, at least to some extent.
we
So far as the support of home industries
is concerned I am pleased to say that that is
not by any means a new suggestion. For
twelve months or more we have had that
suggestion being put very forcibly
before the people of the British Isles
-how necessary it is for them to buy all
that is produced within the Empire in preference to that manufactured in foreign
countries. I see that the Queen made such a
suggestion to the housewives of the country
recently. As for manufactured jams, I think
it is a small matter, and the suggestion in
regard to that is not likely to benefit us to
any great extent. If the coal fields at Coalisland can supply all the coal consumed in
Northern Ireland it would be a good thing if
we could produce coal there cheaper than it
costs to buy it from across the water, and
supply our industries with it at a cheaper
rate.
With reference to the reduction of wages,
as a tradesman, I feel that so for as that
class of labour is concerned the workers have
suffered a sufficient reduction. At the same
time I cannot blind myself to the fact that
the position has considerably changed in comparison with pre-war days. If a tradesman
to-day is receiving £2 17s 6d a week for the
skill and experience and knowledge he has
acquired by a long apprenticeship, surely a
man who is doing a less skilled work cannot
expect to receive the same rate of wages. Yet
it is so throughout the whole of the British
Isles to-day. The lesser skilled man, in fact
the non-skilled man, is receiving exactly the
same remuneration as a skilled person, who
has spent a number of years in making himself skilled. I think there is something wrong
with a system such as that, and until we do
come back to something like a pre-war level,
or something equivalent to it, I do not think
our industries will be able to compete with
the foreigner in producing goods at a reasonable price.
As it is, there is no doubt that it must be
a heavy drain on firms to have to carry on
with unskilled workers and at the same time
bear the cost of the upkeep of the unemployed on the streets. I do hope that before another Bill of this kind is necessary
there will be some improvement in trade.
Those who are unemployed would be very
pleased if they could obtain work rather
than receive the dole, but at present I must
confess that I see little or no prospect of
that. Personally I would be very glad if the
noble Leader of the House could indicate to
us the slightest possibility of any improvement. It would be some encouragement at
this time of the year. I have great pleasure
in supporting the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into a Committee on the said Bill.
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.
Education (Administrative
Provisions) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
This Bill has been brought forward-it is
only a small Bill-in order to meet certain
difficulties of local administration which
have arisen in the course of the working of
the Education Act of 1923. It has been
found, for instance, in the County Borough
of Belfast that an extension of the security
for borrowing under the Act is necessary in
order to enable the Education Authority to
borrow on the most favourable terms. That
difficulty is dealt with in the first two sub-
Sections of Clause 1.
The next sub-Section, sub-Section (3) of
Clause 1, puts the County Councils on the
same footing as the County Boroughs, so far
as borrowings for educational purposes are
concerned, that is to say, they can borrow
on the County Fund, instead of on the Education Rate alone, while the limitation on
Education Bill.
Superannuation Bill.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry.]
their borrowings is removed for educational
purposes.
Again, it was uncertain whether the
borrowing powers of an Education Authority
under the Education Act were exercisable in
connection with works of a permanent
character for which the Education Authority
were not themselves responsible. The House
will remember that Education Authorities
are empowered under Sections 16 and 19 of
the Education Act to assist in the provision,
by voluntary associations, of new elementary
and higher schools. There are other clauses
to empower them to assist the capital undertakings of voluntary bodies for other purconnected with education.
Hon.
Members will find that point dealt with
specifically in sub-Section (4) of this Clause.
poses
The next sub-Section, sub-Section (5) deals
with a purely temporary difficulty which has
arisen owing to the Regional Committees
having found it impossible at the beginning
of their control of local education affairs to
budget with sufficient accuracy to cover all
their needs. This sub-Section, therefore,
gives the Local Education Authority, the
County Council, power to overdraw for the
purpose of keeping a Regional Committee in
funds. The limit of time for the repayment
of the overdraft is two years, while the
power to overdraw in this way is only given
for the next two financial years, that is to
say, until April the 1st, 1928. By that date
we anticipate that experience will have enabled the Regional Committees to budget
with the necessary accuracy.
The following sub-Section simply gives the
same power of overdrawing temporarily for
repayment within the following two years,
to Urban District Councils who have elected
to continue their responsibility for the conduct of technical instruction in their Urban
Districts, as they are empowered to do under
Section 23 of the Education Act. I may,
perhaps remind the House that SO far
as these Urban District Councils are
concerned, they exercise the powers of
Education Authority only in
spect to technical instruction. For all
other forms of education and all the other
activities of an Education Authority under
the Education Act an Urban District is
included in a Region under the County
Council as Local Education Authority.
an
re-
The last sub-section makes the Ministry of
Education the supervising Ministry in regard
to borrowings of County Borough Councils
and County Councils for educational purposes
in their capacity of Local Education
Authorities under this Act in place of the
Ministry of Home Affairs, which is the
general sanctioning authority for the borrowings of local bodies.
This is a single-clause Bill of a very simple
character, and I hope the House will find no
difficulty in giving it a Second Reading.
beg to move.
I
Mr. LONG: I support the Second Reading
of this Bill. While it is true that the Education Authority of which I happen to be Chairman-the County of Londonderry-has not
yet felt the want of such a measure they very
soon would, because at the moment our
borrowing powers in the County have reached
their limit, due to the provision of auxiliary
Asylum Buildings and so on. It has already
been suggested to the Education authority
that economy and efficiency could be secured
by an amalgamation of schools; for instance,
in discontinuing three or four small schools
and building one large efficient school. The
difficulty of providing the money was keenly
felt when this matter was discussed in our
County, but our needs are met in this Bill if
we decide to embark upon what I believe will
be a wise and prudent measure of reform in
this direction. There is no doubt that in
country districts there are quite a number of
small inefficient schools, and it would be in
the interests of the general public if these
were amalgamated and one efficient school
substituted. For these reasons I have very
great pleasure in supporting the Second
Reading.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: May I say how
much I value the action of the Ministry of
Education in bringing forward this Bill. It
has removed a very great difficulty that was
felt by one of the County Boroughs in
Northern Ireland and has made the matter
quite plain sailing for them. We greatly
value this Bill and hope it will be passed.
Mr. DORMAN: I would like to add my
word of approval of the action of the Minister
of Education, and of all action in the
direction of improving the opportunities of
the authorities responsible for the education
of our people. I will whole-heartedly support
the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.

Intoxicating Liquor (Finance) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
of
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of this
Bill, I mav say in brief that its object is, as
its title implies, to enable the charges required to pay off the compensation awarded
to spirit grocers and others deprived of their
licences to be considerably increased. These
charges, as hon. Members know, have to be
met by the remaining licence holders.
Hon. Members
this House who
have read the Interim Reports of
the Claims Tribunal set up under
the Intoxicating Liquor Act of 1923, which
was presented to the House in September
last, will be aware that the amount awarded
up to date as compensation for the abolition
of licences is considerably in excess of the
estimate which was made at the time when
the original measure was considered by this
House, and on which the maximum rates of
charges provided in the Act were based. In
a matter of this kind where it was possible
to make only a very rough guess as to
the number of licence holders who were likely
to surrender their licences and claim compensation, and where the average of the amounts
which would be awarded to these persons by
the Claims Tribunal was an unknown quantity, any estimate of the sum which would be
required to finance the Claims Fund had of
necessity to be largely in the nature of guesswork.
The actual amount advanced from Government funds to date to meet the compensation
awards stands at a figure of approximately
£380,000. That figure, however, does not include anything in respect of the compensation payable to the holders of on-licences who
are unable to comply with the requirements
of Section 6 of the Act of 1923. Hon. Members
are aware that Section 6 is the Section which
applies to structural alterations of premises.
The claims under this head will be considered
by the Tribunal during the course of the next
few months, and these awards will, I
anticipate, add considerably to the figure I
have quoted.
As hon. Members are aware, the money to
meet these payments is advanced, as required,
from the Government Loans Fund, and the
Intoxicating Liquor Act, 1923, provides for
repayment being made by the Claims Fund
within such time as may be prescribed by the
Ministry of Finance. Repayment is effected
by means of a special annual charge on
licence-holders. The rate of charge was
fixed in the Act of 1923 at a maximum figure
for the past three years, but it produces only
fast and in the rest of the province respectively. This maximum charge has been levied
for the past three years, but it produces only
a little over £18,000 a year. It is obvious that
this amount, so far from being sufficient to
enable repayment of the principal and interest
to be made within the period of twenty
years at present prescribed, is inadequate to
pay even the interest charge alone, and consequently a considerable increase in the present rates of levy is necessary.
a
The present Bill provides for such an increase, ranging from 50 per cent. up to, in
one case 100 per cent. This increase will
not take effect till the licensing year commencing 1st October next. Moreover, as it
is realised that repayment within 20 years
would form
crushing burden on the
licensed trade, provision is taken in the Bill
to enable the repayment period to be extended from 20 up to 45 years from the date of
passing of the original Act. I may add that
the maximum rates set out in the Schedule
to the Bill now before the House have been
fixed so as to allow of the smallest possible
margin consistent with the repayment of
the Government advance in the period. It
is not, in fact, anticipated that it will be
necessary in any one year to levy at the full
maximum rates provided for in the Bill. I
may say that these arrangements have been
agreed with by the vast majority of the
existing licence holders as the most equitable
in the circumstances.
The Second Clause of the Bill makes provision whereby licence holders will be permitted in certain cases to pay the compensation levy in two moieties. Section 6 of the
459 Intoxicating Liquor Bill—

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.]
Finance (1909-10) Act, 1910, allows payment of
the licence duty itself to be made in two
moieties where the amount of the duty exceeds £20, and the main provisions of that
section are now applied here. The clause,
briefly, means that where the total charge,
that is the payment of the combined licence
duty and compensation levy exceeds £20, that
charge can be paid in two moieties.
There is only one other matter to which I
ought to draw the attention of the House, and
that is the provision in sub-Section 3 of
Section 1. The effect of this is to provide
that the Claims Tribunal, whose operations
under the original Act were to terminate on
the 30th September, 1926, or such later date
as the Ministry of Finance might determine,
shall now, under this Bill, cease to operate
on the 31st March, 1926, or such later date as
the Ministry may determine. The reason for
this is that originally when the Act of 1923
was first drawn up, it was not definitely
known within what period the assessment of
compensation would take place. Accordingly,
the ample margin of three years
allowed. It is now quite clear that compensation will have been finally assessed and
paid by the close of the present financial year,
and accordingly the transfer of functions is
arranged for an earlier date.
was
I do not think there is anything else I can
inform Members of in connection with this
Bill, but I shall be very pleased to answer,
as far as I can, any question any hon.
member may wish to ask. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered: That the Bill be read the Third
time to-morrow.
SENATE OF QUEEN'S UNIVERSITY.
Colonel SINCLAIR: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House a question, of which I
have given him private notice, namely,
whether the Government is prepared to announce what steps, if any, they have taken
to fill the vacant seat in the Senate of the
Queen's University of Belfast, rendered
vacant by the death of the late Lord Chief
Justice, Sir Denis Henry.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am grateful to the hon. Member for having
given me private notice of this question, and
I am happy to say I am in a position to
answer it. The Government, on my recommendation, has decided to nominate Rev. J.
M'Caughan, Principal of St. Malachy's College, Belfast.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Four
o'clock.

Finance Bill.
MOTOR TAXATION.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First

The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: In
moving the Second Reading of the Finance
Bill I feel that, in spite of the limited powers
of this House in regard to the Measure, hon.
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
Members will expect me to say a few words
upon the subject of the alteration of duties
on licences for mechanically propelled
vehicles which will be found in the first
Clause of this Finance Bill.
I may take this opportunity of saying how
much I regret that we have had to move the
suspension of Standing Orders for the purpose of taking this Bill in one sitting. But
I think hon. Members are aware of the
reason, and that is that we hope to finish
up the business this week and to have the
prorogation of Parliament on Tuesday of
next week.
With reference to these rates of duty on
these mechanically propelled vehicles to
which I have referred if we compare themboth those upon passenger motor vehicles and
also those upon motor lorries with the old
rates it will be clearly seen that we are asking those who use the roads to make a profit
for themselves, to pay a higher contribution
towards the upkeep of the roads. It will
be noted that we have carefully drawn the
new scales, as indeed we were bound so to do.
to fit in with the actual facts so far as
wear and tear on the high roads is concerned.
The increase in the new rates is ever steeper
and steeper as the size and the weight of the
motor vehicle increases; while the increase
in the rates for lorries is much greater than
that for passenger vehicles, the reason, of
course, being that the tax upon lorries hitherto has been very light as compared with that
upon the passenger vehicle of comparable size
and weight.
Over the whole of the United Kingdom,
ever since the war, local authorities everywhere have been waging an unequal contest
in their unceasing efforts to maintain the
roads to meet the stresses laid upon them by
a motor traffic which year by year increases,
not only in volume but in the weight and
dimensions of the individual vehicles that
compose the whole. That contest has been
especially severe and unequal here in Ulster,
where our climate joins with the enemy
against the county councils, where there are
many miles of roads laid upon the unstable
foundations of bog land, and where the road
mileage per thousand of the population is
more than twice that in Great Britain, being
ten miles per thousand here against four-andthree-fifths miles in Great Britain.
be
The Government, fortified by the representations from local authorities all over the
Province, have come to the conclusion that
the conditions of the struggle must
changed or the battle will be irretrievably
lost and either the ratepayer reduced to a
state of bankruptcy or the roads allowed to
fall into ruinous disrepair.
It is useless for the opponents of this Bill
with its changes in the duties for motor
vehicles, to put forward the plea that we
have no right to lay heavier taxes upon motor
vehicles here than in Great Britain. Our
reply must be, that conditions here are
demonstrably different from conditions in
Great Britain and that the sole advantage
that Ulster can reap from the measure of
self-government which is hers, is that she
may give to local problems the particular
treatment that local conditions render necessary.
What then is this local problem? If the
House will pardon me, I will give them a
few figures, very few, but I think very convincing. We are faced with a bill for the
upkeep and maintenance of our roads amounting to nearly one million and a quarter
pounds for the present year. Motor taxation
yields the sum of three hundred thousand
pounds annually. Of this amount all but
thirty thousand pounds comes from motor
vehicles under two tons in weight. The
heavier vehicles of which the five-ton lorry,
with a tax of only £30, is estimated to do
£300 damage annually to the roads-these
heavy vehicles only contribute a beggarly
£30,000 a year to the enormous cost of road
maintenance.
It is plain on the face of things that this
anomaly must be corrected, and that the tax
paid upon motor vehicles must bear some
recognizable relationship to the amount of
wear and tear that the vehicle inflicts upon
the road. If hon. Members will look at the
proposed new scales they will find that we
have endeavoured to achieve this, and I think
endeavoured with a very fair amount of
success. For instance, with the passenger
vehicles the increase in each class is a negligible amount-only £4 annually-until we
come to the larger classes of these vehicles,
when the figures become ever more and more
serious with the increase in the size of the
machine. The same thing will be noted with
respect to motor lorries. There is no increase
in the first two classes-the lighter class of
these vehicles. With the other classes the
increase is graded more steeply to correspond
with increase in weight. The position shortly
is this, that it is by no means certain that
these very large motor vehicles, weighing five
tons and upwards, unladen-whether motor
lorries or the largest class of passenger carrying vehicles are at all desirable or at all
suitable to the road conditions generally in
the Province.
We want to inflict no hardship upon a
young and flourishing industry. We want to
make hard times no more difficult for any
class of the community. We do not want to
discourage the activities of those individuals
or companies whose business it is to make a
Second Reading.
Finance Bill-
17 DECEMBER 1925


$
livelihood by organizing cheap motor passenger transport, but as a Government we
are bound to hold the scales fairly, and to
see that the interests of the ratepayer are
not sacrificed, because it is upon the ratepayer that the great volume of the charge
for the upkeep of roads must necessarily fall.
If it is for the good of the motor industry
and the users of motor vehicles that the roads
must be maintained in a condition of excellence far exceeding the needs of the ordinary
ratepayer, at whose expense and for whose
convenience the roads are on the whole provided, then we can only be fair, and no one
can reasonably resist the proposals of the
Government when we ask this particular
form of locomotion to pay, not the whole,
but at least some proportion of the increased
charge which its necessities entail upon the
general public. I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: I just wish briefly to support
the Bill. There has been a revolution in
road traffic since 1914. In that year the roads
as a rule were fairly well suited for the traffic
they were called upon to bear, which was
mainly horse traffic. However, mechanically
propelled vehicles came into use, with the
result that our thin road surface soon became
cut up and the roads became almost impassable. To cope with this, local authorities
were at their wits' end to find a method by
which they could withstand the effects of
this extraordinary traffic. To give you an
illustration of the difference in cost, I may
mention that in the county of Londonderry
the expenditure on roads in 1914 was £31,233.
In 1924-25 the ordinary maintenance contracts
were £114,555, in addition to which £153,000
was spent in reconstruction.. Out of what we
expended in 1924-25 we received £18,049 from
the Road Fund, but it will readily be seen
from a glance at these figures that the expenditure has increased at an enormous rate,
and experts attribute the necessity for this
expenditure entirely to heavy mechanically
propelled vehicles using the roads.
My information in the matter differs very
much from that of the noble Leader of the
House when he says that the heavy mechanically propelled vehicles do £300 worth of
damage to the roads in one year. I should be
inclined to put it at a very much higher
figure. The information I received was that
such a vehicle did £1,000 worth of damage to
the roads in a year if continuously used. I
appreciated very much the speech of the
noble Marquess in moving the Second Reading of this Bill. I appreciated especially his
reference to the unequal contest, as regards
providing the money for the upkeep of these
roads, in which the County Councils have
hitherto been engaged. I do not believe in
the least that the duties proposed will act
in restraint of trade. I am convinced that
in the near future motor owners will see
that it is to their advantage to use a lighter
class of vehicle, and I am delighted to know
that no increase has been put on one and two
ton lorries. I am quite pleased that the
Government have not yielded to the demand
to differentiate between farmers and others
using thse motor lorries. It is an exceedingly dangerous thing to discriminate in a
case of that sort, and farmers have already
come to realise that horse traction is cheaper
than motor traction for them.
On the whole I welcome this Bill and I
appreciate the manner in which it has been
introduced.
The Ministry of Home Affairs
have been inundated with resolutions
from all the various road authorities in
Northern Ireland asking them to take steps
to have this matter dealt with, and 1 am
very glad that they have decided to hold the
scales evenly between the road users and the
taxpayers. As the noble Marquess has
pointed out, the tax that is proposed to be
put on heavy motor vehicles does not at all
represent the whole of the amount they
should in equity be called upon to pay. We
recognise that they render considerable service to the community. They are of inestimable value to shopkeepers in conveying goods
from the various centres to their shops, and
also to the farmers in transporting their
produce to shipping centres. For these
reasons I think it is only right that the
Government should not place such a tax on
these vehicles as would put them off the
roads. I think the decision of the Government is an equitable and fair one and J
support the Bill.
Mr. GLENDINNING: While fully in
agreement with the Government in regard
to this Measure, and while I intend to
support it, I should like to express my own
disappointment, and the disappointment of
the ordinary users of motor vehicles throughout the country that the Government did not
avail itself of this opportunity of reverting
to the simple method of placing the tax on
the petrol consumed instead of on the horse
power oг the weight of the vehicle. The
former method would have been equally advantageous to the Government so far as the
provision of the necessary money for the upkeep of the roads is concerned, and it would
have been much fairer to motor users. There
are a great many arguments which might be
used on both sides, but motorists are generally of opinion that a tax on petrol is the
fairest and easiest method of taxing all
classes of motor vehicles.
Mr. LONG: Hear, hear.
Mr. GLENDINNING:
I am firmly convinced that that is the general feeling
throughout the country, and that is the
reason I mention it, although I am prepared
to support the Measure.
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It is rather
a pity it is found necessary to pass this Bill
through all its stages in one day. It is
creating a considerable amount of interest
amongst people outside and I believe it
would have been well if the Bill could have
been left over for a short time for further
consideration. However, we are more or less
indebted to the Marquess of Londonderry for
the clear explanation he has given us of the
Bill. There is no doubt that a considerable
amount of criticism has been levelled at the
Government in regard to this Measure, but
it is only what anyone might expect when
increased taxes are suggested. Those who are
likely to be affected by them are sure to cry
out and wish the burden to be placed on
some other person's shoulders. Even if they
think they are not likely to escape the
burden they like to cry out to let their neighbours know of the burden they will have to
shoulder.
There is considerable competition going on
at the present time between road and rail
transport. It has been stated many times
outside this House that the Government is
favouring the railways in preference to road
transport. While I believe the Government
have no intention whatever of scotching or
handicapping that competition, at the same
time it is very necessary that the Government should see that the competition with the
railways is not hampered or favoured at the
expense of the ratepayers. The railways have
their own track and a considerable staff to
maintain, and they pay their employees good
wages and work them under the best conditions. On the other hand, road traffic is a
new adventure for which roads have to be
specially made.
The old roads that were suitable for the
farmer would not now suit the heavy traffic
they are called upon to bear, and the roads
that are specially built to carry this heavy
traction are to a considerable extent unsuitable for the farmer. Therefore, I can quite
understand the farmer complaining that he
has to pay extra taxation for the upkeep of
these roads. I understand that the surface
of these roads is so smooth that horses are
unable to walk on them, and I cannot understand those who own heavy motor vehicles
complaining if they are asked to pay for the
upkeep of roads that are specially required
for that particular class of traffic.
Arguments have been put forward that
these increased taxes are likely to increase
the cost of living to the workers of the city.
I have gone carefully into the matter, and
I do know that there is a considerable number
of these heavy motor vehicles travelling for
some distance around Belfast, collecting farm
produce, and particularly milk, and while
that must be a very cheap means of transport,
because they have no extra handling and not
much labour to pay, yet the consumer has not
benefited to any great extent by obtaining
cheaper milk. Someone has pocketed the
profits, and, therefore, these people have no
reason to complain if they are asked to contribute some little towards the upkeep of
the roads. Good roads, if properly kept in
order, should benefit this heavy motor traffic.
It will reduce to a considerable extent their
annual repair bill, because there is nothing
more severe on machinery than jolting over
potholes along the road, which is very injurious to pins and springs. I am very
pleased that the Government has taken action
in this matter, because I would like to see
in Northern Ireland a really good class of
road. Not only should old roads be repaired
and maintained, but straighter and more
direct new roads should be constructed from
the city to other towns in the Northern area.
If that could be done it might be an attraction
to tourist traffic, and Northern Ireland would
benefit to
considerable extent in that
respect. We have fine scenery here, but I
think it cannot be said we have the best of
roads. Therefore, the Government are to be
complimented on taking this step to improve
the roads, to repair them and make them
as good as possible for traffic. I am strongly
in favour of this Bill, and I am pleased to
give it my support on the Second Reading.
Again I wish to say I am glad the noble
Marquess has taken so much pains in explaining the Bill as we have not had an
opportunity of examining it.
a
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I consider this Bill
has been long overdue. It is now several
years since I was one of a deputation who
waited on the Prime Minister on the subject and impressed on him the injustice of
the existing situation. The county from
which I have the honour of coming may be
described as a corridor county, because all
the traffic from Belfast or Down or Antrim
has to pass through Armagh in order to go
to the West and South-West. We complain
not only of the ordinary heavy lorries but
of the enormous lorries which carry building materials-bricks and other heavy
materials and which are destructive to the
roads. Without going into the statistics of
the matter, anybody who observes the road
after one of these lorries has passed will see
how the whole material of the road is shaken
to pieces. The injury done must be enor-
The burden on the ratepayers must
be very serious indeed. The road between
Belfast and Armagh has been reconstructed
at enormous cost by borrowed money, and,
of course, the interest on that added to the
expensive upkeep is a very serious injury to
the ratepayers. Their rates are continually
going up with the various calls upon them,
education and other matters which are of
great importance. I think this Bill will be
cordially welcomed as it has been called for
so long by the people of the country.
mous.
Finance Bill-
Third Reading.
17 DECEMBER 1925

Sir GEORGE CLARK: I listened with
great interest to the speech of the noble
Marquess in introducing this Bill. I consider he has made a very good case, and for
my part I intend to support the Bill. I
notice there has been an increase made in
the duties according to the weight of the
vehicles, but there is no limit on the weight
that may be on any vehicle. It seems to
me there should be some arrangement made
to prevent very heavy vehicles such as those
weighing up to 20 tons using the roads because the damage that would be done by
them if they were to go at any speed would
be considerable. I see no provision in the
Bill for anything of that nature. I would
like to ask the noble Marquess whether this
matter has been considered.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am very grateful to hon. Members for the reception they have given to the Bill which I
have had the honour of introducing in this
House. My hon. Friend (Mr. Joseph
Cunningham) like myself is jealous of the
dignity of this House and resents, and
rightly resents, if we are hurried through
cur work to the detriment of the legislation
which it is necessary for us to bring forward,
but I would only say in mitigation of the
circumstances of to-day that we are unable
to have a Committee Stage on this Bill.
That is a stage which does not count in this
House, and the only opportunity we have of
discussion on matters relating to finance is
on the Second Reading. Consequently, the
hastening of the next stage to the Third
Reading is merely a matter of form, but
I
am fully sympathetic with him in the
principle of the point which he was raising.
My hon. Friend behind me (Mr. Glendinning) spoke about a tax on petrol. I do
not think he is aware that we have not the
power under the 1920 Act to tax petrol even
if we wished to do so, but I also understand
he has not been through the difficulties
which are inherent in anybody's desire to
put a tax on petrol when it is discovered
that motor-cars can run on almost anything.
However, this is a matter which does not
arise on this Bill so I will not pursue that
point further. My hon. Friend, Sir George
Clark, has spoken of the limits as regards
the weight of motor vehicles on the roads
and suggests that there is a limit in the
Bill, I think, of £100.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: It says anything
over 5 tons.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As a matter of fact, at the present moment
these lorries which he speaks of are not in
existence, but when they do make their
appearance in leviathan dimensions on the
roads I feel sure he will raise the matter in
this House, and I have no doubt the Government will address themselves immediately to
placing duties on those vehicles consistent
with the damage they do to the roads. My
hon. Friend, who sits beside me (Mr. Long),
spoke of the damage which these large motor
vehicles do to the roads, and he suggests
the damage is more like £1.000 than £300.
The figure which I gave, which I am sure is
the minimum figure and not the maximum
figure, came from the expert evidence of the
county surveyor at the Ulster Development
Commission. In his judgment the damage
done to the roads was in the nature of £300
per annum.
My hon. Friend suggests that this tax may
have the effect of raising the cost of food to
the people, but I am not sure that if he
works it out when he sees the amount per
ton per mile which is included in this tax
on vehicles used for the transport of food it
could be said that the extra cost put on these
vehicles could in any sense add to the cost of
living. The other point which he made about
the farm produce which is sold in Belfast
is a matter which, I think, has already been
raised in this House, and I think Members
expressed wonder at the fact that the prices
in Belfast are far higher than the prices
which exist in the country, but that also is
not a matter which comes up for discussion
on this Bill.
J
I hope I have given hon. Members the explanation which they desire on the speech
which I made in introducing this Bill. It
is impossible to put everything in an opening
speech, and I am grateful to hon. Members
for the points which they have made.
think I may owe the House an apology
because I have an idea in my mind that by
inadvertence I gave the wrong figure to the
House, that the upkeep and maintenance
of the roads in the country amounted
to a quarter of a million. I think I gave
that figure. The amount is one million and
a quarter, a very different thing-£1,250,000
and not £250,000. The expenditure for upkeep and maintenance of our roads amounts
to a million and a quarter and not a quarter
of a million.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) put, and agreed to.
Moved: That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That the Bill be now read the
Third time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Finance Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to the said Bill without any amendment.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I beg to move, That this House do now
adjourn. The business which we have completed to-day completes the business for the
Session, and it is proposed that the proroguing
of this Parliament shall take place on
Tuesday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes before Four o'clock until
Tuesday next.
The King's Speech.
The King's Speech.

22 DECEMBER 1925
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 22nd December, 1925.
The Senate met at a quarter past Twelve
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Regulations made under the Pharmacy and
Poisons Act (Northern Ireland), 1925, by the
Council of the Pharmaceutical Society of
Northern Ireland and approved by the Minister of Home Affairs.
Presented pursuant to Act and ordered to
lie on the Table.
Royal Assent.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am authorized by the Prime Minister on his behalf to inform the Senate that
his Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland
in the name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King has been pleased to give his Assent to
the following Bills:
Appropriation (No. 2).
Unemployment Fund (No. 2).
Education (Administrative Provisions).
Superannuation.
Intoxicating Liquor (Finance).
Finance.
PROROGATION.
Moved: That the Gentleman Usher of the
Black Rod be commanded to inform the Commons that the Speech of his Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland at the Prorogation
of Parliament is to be communicated to the
Senate forthwith, and that this House invite
the Commons to attend in order to hear the
same.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
THE KING'S SPEECH.
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Speaker, the King's Speech was delivered to
both Houses of Parliament by the Speaker of
the Senate (in pursuance of His Grace's command) as followeth:
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I have to congratulate you upon the
measures which have been placed on the
Statue Book during the first Session of
this present Parliament.
I regret that Northern Ireland still
continues to suffer from the general depression in trade which exists throughout the United Kingdom. The problem
of unemployment is the greatest that
confronts us at the moment in common
with Great Britain, and it is gratifying
that representatives of our manufactures
and commerce and the people of Northern
Ireland have freely responded to the
invitation of the Government to take
part in an Unemployment Conference
which is carefully exploring the postion
as it affects Northern Ireland. By means
of the Loans Guarantee Act continued
assistance has been rendered to the. Shipbuilding Industry; while it is hoped that
the endeavour to hasten a revival of the
Linen Trade by the co-operation with
local manufacturers in a special advertising campaign in America will meet
with success. Financial assistance is also
being afforded to the Coal Industry in
Northern Ireland. In addition, the Local
Authorities are continuing to give their
valuable co-operation in the promotion
of schemes of public utility designed
expressly to give useful employment to
our people.
The results which have attended
Northern Ireland's participation in the
British Empire Exhibition this year are
most satisfactory. In organising a larger
and more fully representative exhibit of
Ulster products than was possible in
the previous year the Government and
exhibitors alike have again emphasised
Ulster's attachment to the Imperial connection.
An Act has been passed making
further provision for the payment of
Unemployment Benefit to those insured
persons who cannot obtain work and for
protecting the interests of those most in
need of the assistance of the Unemployment Fund.
It is with great pleasure that I am
able to refer to the Widows', Orphans'
and Old Age Contributory Pensions Act.
The measure marks one more stage in
the great Social Insurance code which
has been built up in the last few years,
and guarantees for our insured workpeople the same provision against proverty
and distress which persons similarly
situated in the rest of the United Kingdom will be afforded in times of bereavement and old age.
It is satisfactory that, notwithstanding the severe industrial depression, the
thrifty habits of the people continue to
be demonstrated by the interest shown
in Ulster Savings Certificates, in which a
total of £2,000,000 has now been invested.
The King's Speech.
End of Session.
THE SENATE
Your Drainage Act provides machinery
which will enable remedial measures to
be undertaken to prevent the flooding of
our agricultural land.
I
Operations under the scheme of loans
to farmers for the replacement of live
stock lost owing to the recent unfavourable season have now been concluded.
am satisfied that this scheme eased considerably a serious situation, particularly
in the poorer districts. More than £46,000
was spent on this work.
It is gratifying to learn that the
recent legislation passed by Parliament
granting additional financial inducements
to private individuals and local authorities
for the erection of houses has been attended with such success and that the
rate of building during the last few
months has been steadily increasing.
The Government Loans and Exchequer
Provisions Act has effected a number of
improvements in our financial system
and has provided an economically sound
method of financing the loans which are
made from time to time for capital purposes. The reception given to the issue
of Ulster Loan Stock has provided
satisfactory indication of the credit of
Northern Ireland.
a
a
It gives me satisfaction to observe
continuance of peaceful conditions and the
absence of abnormal crime in Northern
Ireland, and I am glad to note the restoration of that respect for the law which is
the foundation of Society. This happy
result has been attained by a firm and
impartial enforcement of the law and by
the vigilance of the Constabulary Forces,
and I earnestly pray that no untoward
event may occur to disturb the conditions
which now prevail.
Members of the House of Commons.
I thank
you for the provision of
supplies for the public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I am deeply moved and gratified at
the Agreement entered into between the
Governments of Great Britain, Northern
Ireland, and the Irish Free State.
As a
result it is my earnest prayer that
friendly relations between the Governments and peoples of Northern Ireland
and the Irish Free State may be promoted
and cemented by
spirit of goodwill
and friendship to the common advantage
of both.
a
Recent developments in the field of
world politics give ground for hope that
the new spirit of mutual confidence between nation and nation may bring in
its train the long-awaited revival in trade
which cannot fail to affect beneficially
the industries of Northern Ireland.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your labours.
Then the Proclamation for proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland was
read by the Speaker as followeth:
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION.
For Proroguing the present Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section eleven
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as
modified by and in pursuance of certain
enactments amending the said Act, that the
Governor of Northern Ireland shall in His
Majesty's name summon, prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that this
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
should be prorogued to the ninth day of
March, One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty-six.
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
and declare His Majesty's Pleasure that the
said Parliament be, as from the reading of
this Proclamation in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, prorogued to the ninth day
of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty-six.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be that
the said Parliament shall on the said ninth
day of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-six, assemble and be holden for
the dispatch of divers urgent and important
affairs, and the Senators and Members of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland are
hereby required to give their attendance
accordingly.
Given at Government House, Hillsborough,
this Eighteenth day of December, One Thousand Nine Hundred and Twenty-five.
Signed by his own hand,
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the First Session (opened

Tuesday,
14th April, 1925) of the Second Parliament of
Northern Ireland, in the Sixteenth year of
the Reign of His Majesty King George the
Fifth.

UNIVERSITY OF ILLINOIS-URBANA

Tuesday, 10th March, 1925.
The Fifth Session of the First Parliament
of Northern Ireland, appointed to meet 10th
March, 1925, in the fifteenth year of the
Reign of King George V, was opened by the
Governor (His Grace the Duke of Abercorn,
K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock.
Mr. SPEAKER (Most Hon, the Marquess of
Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O., D.L.) in the
Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace, the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know "It is His Grace's pleasure that they
attend him immediately in this House."
:-
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Speaker, His Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne, and then retired :-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
A year ago I expressed satisfaction that
law and order had been re-established and
maintained throughout Northern Ireland,
and I am glad to learn that now it has
been found possible to relax the enforcement of the special powers granted by
Parliament for this purpose, and that all
interned persons have been released and
the Curfew restrictions have been entirely
removed.
It is a matter of great regret that
unemployment to such an extent still
continues in our midst; there was recently
welcome improvement in the linen industry, and although this has not been
maintained lately, I am aware that a
diminution in trade is unfortunately not
unusual in the winter months, and it is
hoped that a revival may take place in
the Spring.
The more settled conditions prevailing
on the Continent give hopes of an improvement in trade. It is anticipated
that the valuable recommendations made
by our Commission on Development,
which among other things is investigating
the possibility of electrical power for industrial purposes, may prove of benefit.
The legislation passed for the prevention of unfair competition in industry
and of sweating is working satisfactorily,
and improvements have been made in
the ventilation of our mills and factories
and in other conditions affecting the
welfare of employees.
King's Speech.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
The relief works initiated by my
Government since its establishment have
received the generous and sympathetic
co-operation of our Local Authorities and
involve a capital cost of £3,000,000. These
works include the construction of workers'
houses, improvements of roads and harbours, water supply, tramway reconstruction, and many other works of public
utility. By this means over 17,000
situations for unemployed persons have
been found which would not otherwise
have existed, and every effort will be made
to continue the promotion of schemes of
public utility to provide useful work. In
addition, the facilities afforded under the
Loans Guarantee Acts have contributed
materially to the reduction of unemployment, and you will be asked to extend
still further the provisions of these Acts.
Nevertheless, in spite of continued
efforts to increase the field of employment,
large numbers of insured workers have
had to rely on the resources of the
Unemployment Insurance Scheme, which
has been maintained on the same lines as
in Great Britain. Since the 1st January,
1922, the contributory Insurance Fund has
provided benefit for unemployed workers
to the extent of £4,600,000.
I have acceded to the request of my
Ministers to dissolve Parliament, and consequently the only business which will
be laid before you is that dealing with
finance and other matters which require
consideration before the end of the current
financial year.
A Bill will be brought in to amend
the Education Act (Northern Ireland),
1923, and the Bill for the extension of the
Loans Guarantee Acts to which I have
already referred.
Members of the House of Commons.
The report of the Colwyn Committee
is completed, but until its effect has been
fully explored, it may be necessary to
present tentative figures for the estimates
of the coming year.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
The machinery of local administration
contemplated under the Education Act is
I
now complete, the necessary negotiations
between the County Councils and the
Ministry of Education having resulted in
an agreed scheme for every County.
look forward to an immediate extension
of the operation of the Act by the genera!
application of its provisions to the
Counties and to the completion of its
operations in the County Boroughs by the
application of that part of the Act which
deals with the employment of children
and young persons. I have noted with
pleasure the energy and the enthusiasm
with which the Education Committees in
the County Boroughs of Belfast and
Londonderry are undertaking the important duties which have devolved upon
them, and I expect with confidence a
similar stirring of local interest in educational administration when the Act is in
full operation in the County Districts.
The Local Government Commission
appointed to inquire into the administration of Poor Law and Public Health is
now sitting, and has already received
evidence from the majority of the Boards
of Guardians and also from the Rural
District Councils' Association. This
Commission has made arrangements for
hearing evidence from the County Councils and Urban Authorities, as well as
from representatives of all the County
Infirmaries and other Public Bodies.
I am glad to learn that the Acts
passed in the interests of agriculture are
already proving beneficial, and that as a
result there has already been an improvement in the cattle bred in Northern
Ireland, and in the development of our
egg industry. There is no doubt that
the steps taken have resulted in greater
confidence in British markets in the
excellent quality of our agricultural
produce.
I am pleased to learn that a decision
has been reached to re-open the Ulster
Pavilion on an extended scale in the great
Empire Exhibition at Wembley, as it once
again testifies the readiness and determination of Northern Ireland to bear her
part in the development of the Empire.
AND I pray that Almighty God may
bestow His abundant blessing upon your
deliberations.
Papers Presented
Papers Presented
10 MARCH 1925
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
