Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO HIS MAJESTY'S
MOST GRACIOUS SPEECH.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN (in military
uniform): I hope this House is fully aware
how deeply I appreciate the honour of
being selected from among the Members of
the Senate to move the Address in reply to
the most Gracious Speech from the Throne.
I am fully aware how much I fall short of
what is required in one to whom this great
duty is entrusted, but I feel it is not only an
honour to me personally, but even more so
to that important part of our Province with
which I have been all my life identified, the
County of Tyrone, where I was born and
brought up.
I also feel how great is my responsibility
in undertaking this honourable duty, and I
only hope that the House will extend to me
the forbearance and indulgence it always
extends to one who has the privilege of
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[ Sir Emerson Herdman]
addressing the House for the first time.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
The Gracious Speech strikes a note of confidence as regards the public peace throughout our beloved Province of Ulster, and if I
may venture to say so, this note is and will
be amply justified. Once more our people
throughout the length and breadth of Ulster
breathe the atmosphere of peace and confidence, and on all sides there is general
satisfaction that once again lawful authority
has been re-established, and the people of
Ulster can pursue their daily lives and
ordinary peaceful avocations without fear of
hindrance or annoyance.
For this result so gratifying to our Sovereign
and his Representative we can heartily congratulate Sir James Craig and the Government of Northern Ireland and also the Royal
Ulster Constabulary forces, whose fine record is now SO well recognised and
appreciated.
The release of all interned persons and the
removal of the obnoxious Curfew are the outward and visibe signs of the beginning of a
new era of peace and prosperity in Ulster in
which I venture to hope with confidence that
however much the people of Northern Ireland
may differ on other matters all sections of
the community will work together strongly
for the maintenance and continuation of
these satisfactory conditions so essential for
the common weal. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
The reference in the most Gracious Speech
to trade and industry reminds us of the
difficulties which in varying degree, year by
year, arise to affect the happiness and
prosperity of our large industrial centres, and
I fully sympathize with the Government in
the sincere and practical interest being taken
by them both in studying the causes and
alleviating in the best way they can the
inevitable results of trade depression.
Connected as I am with an important
industry, that of flax spinning, I naturally
can claim to speak with some practical knowledge on this subject, and I do most heartily
share in the hopeful view in the Gracious
Speech that ere long the time will come when
our staple industries will revive and the
establishment of settled conditions in other
countries will once more bring work and
prosperity to the hard-pressed people of our
industrial towns where unwished for unemployment now carries in its train sorrow
and depression.
The recommendations of the Commission
on Development will be looked forward to
with general interest, and the possibility of
making Electrical Power more extensively
available will undoubtedly awaken the
keenest satisfaction. The maintenance of
our reputation in the industrial world
where competition, not always fair, is
keener than ever, makes it of the highest
importance that all necessary measures
having this end in view should receive our
loyal and sympathetic support. In combining measures for the greater comfort
and efficiency of the artisans and workers
with well thought out plans for protecting
our industries against unfair competition, I
think the Government of Northern Ireland
have proved themselves fully alive to the
true methods of increasing the prosperity
of Ulster and establishing her reputation
on a sound and firm foundation.
the
The existing unemployment has been
relieved in a gratifying degree by generous
aid, and at the same time useful works
which will, when trade revives and unemployment becomes a less menacing
problem, find us ready to start under
greatly improved conditions. In
wonderful working of the Unemployed
Insurance Fund our workers have had
ample evidence of the concern of the
Ministry of Labour for their welfare, and
I firmly believe that the working men and
women of Ulster are looking forward
anxiously to the prospect of work being
plentiful, when in lieu of benefits they will
earn wages.
We rejoice to know that the Land Purchase problem in Northern Ireland will
shortly be solved by an Act of the Imperial
Parliament, and this coupled with the
Drainage Bill promised in the Gracious
Speech, by which it is hoped to overcome
the disastrous floods so familiar in many
farming districts, are encouraging steps towards securing for our agricultural
industry a new impetus towards prosperity
and contentment, whilst the efforts of the
Minister of Agriculture to raise the standard
of our products in the eyes of the world are
deserving of our fullest acknowledgment.
I do not propose to refer to the other
important matters foreshadowed in the.
Gracious Speech, which I leave to be dealt
with by Senator Sir Thomas Dixon, who
seconds the Address, when they will be
dealt with much better than I could do. I
desire to express my thanks for the kindly
forbearance with which the House has
listened to me.
I beg to move-
That a humble Address be presented to
his Grace in the following words:-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal Subjects, the Senate of the Parlia-
Debate on Address.

Debate on Address.
10 MARCH 1925
ment of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to Your Grace
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
Sir THOMAS DIXON (in the uniform
of His Majesty's Lieutenant for the County
of the City of Belfast): I beg to second the
motion for the Address in reply to the
Gracious Speech from the Throne, so ably
moved by Senator Sir Emerson Herdman.
It is a matter of great satisfaction to all that,
as the Gracious Speech says, Law and Order
have been restored in our beloved province
and that as a result all those who were
interned have been released, Curfew restrictions have been withdrawn, and we are
now back to our happy normal conditions.
The Speech from the Throne also dwells
upon numerous works that have been carried
out by Local Authorities and others in cooperation with the Government, in order
that unemployment may be lessened. All
these activities by the various bodies indicated have been of great benefit to the
working classes; but at best these relief
schemes are only of a temporary nature.
What we want is an improvement in trade
that will absorb our unemployed. I am
glad to be able to say that I think we can
see signs that the desired improvement is
steadily if slowly coming.
The reference to the Education Act is also
very satisfactory. A conference has had the
result of clearing away misunderstandings.
We may now, I think, hope that all those
who were divided on this question before
will unite to make this Act, as amended, the
great blessing which its framers desired it
to be. I beg to second the motion.
THE MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): Before I turn to the
business for which we are assembled here
to-day I feel sure that I shall be voicing the
sentiments of all the members of this House
when I refer to the sad event of a few
weeks ago which has certainly cast a cloud
over our Assembly to-day. I refer to the
death of Mr. Hotham. Mr. Hotham joined
us here on the inception of our new Parliament, and no one worked harder for its
success than he did. I feel that for the
position in which we find ourselves at the
present moment we are indebted to a very
large extent to the whole-hearted services
which he always devoted, not only to the
assembly as a whole, but to each individual
Senator who might at any time find himself
in a difficulty. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). I know that I am expressing the
feelings of all present to-day when I say how
deeply we feel his loss and how we desire,
on the first occasion on which we have been
able to do so, to express our sympathetic
condolence with the members of his family
who have suffered so great a bereavement.
I should like first of all to congratulate
the Mover and the Seconder of the Address
to His Grace the Governor for the speeches
which they have delivered to us this afternoon. There is no need for me in this
assembly to say very much about either of
those Senators. They are both very well
known to us. Of both their records we are
very proud, and we are glad to number
them in this body of the Senate of Ulster.
Sir Emerson Herdman comes from the
furthest of our Counties. He lives on the
Border, and not only here but also there his
name is a household word. He has a record
of which he has every reason to be justly
proud. He took his part in the Great War
and he has always shown himself to be a
thorough representative of the family to
which he belongs. I do sincerely hope that
we shall have an opportunity of hearing
him speak in this assembly more frequently
than perhaps he has done in the past, and
that he will thus give us the benefit
of his knowledge and his experience
on all those questions which so deeply
affect the people of the Six Counties.
Sir Thomas Dixon, whom perhaps I may also
be allowed to congratulate on his speech in
seconding the Motion, is also very well known
to us. His family have a splendid record of
service in this great city of Belfast.
father was very well known to many of us
who are here to-day, and we can all testify
to the splendid position in which he placed
the Civic authority of this great city. We
are fortunate in having his successor with us
to-day, and I know quite well that he
will testify to the fact that the importance
of the position he occupies now and the
traditions which belong to that position have
been handed down to him by his illustrious
father. I need say no more about my hon.
Friend. He is one of those who take a
great interest in the North of Ireland, and
he is one of those business authorities who.
can speak with knowledge on all those great
questions and problems with which we as a
Province are assailed. I welcome very much
his advent to our assembly where he can
bring his wide experience and knowledge to
bear on the problems with which we have
been faced, and with which we will be faced
so long as this Province continues.
His
As we have been told in the Gracious
Speech from the Throne, this is the last
Session of the first Parliament of Northern
Ireland, and I feel that perhaps it is my duty
to consider in restrospect the functions and
duties which this, our first Parliament, has
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Marquess of Londonderry]
performed, and I shall do so as briefly as
possible. I do not think there is any necessity for me to make an apology for what our
new institution has done. We were faced
with immense difficulties, and I think that
without undue presumption we may congratulate ourselves that we have discharged those
duties to the best of our ability. The
Government can claim to have grappled with
the problems with which they were faced.
Perhaps the first one to which I should make
allusion was the internal disorder that existed
in our midst. Peace has been restored, and
I think we can feel a sense of satisfaction
that it has been restored by the equal
administration of the law and the establishment and maintenance of regular police
forces and of special police forces. We can
certainly at this moment look with far
greater satisfaction our position as a
self-governing unit than we could some four
years ago. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
on
The next problem which we have had to
face has been the problem of unemployment.
It is naturally one in which we one and all
must take the deepest possible interest, and
I am sure most of us have read with benefit
the Speech which was delivered yesterday by
Sir Arthur Steele-Maitland. It is a problem
which we should do every single thing in
our power to bring home to every one of the
inhabitants of these Six Counties. It is a
disease which if allowed to continue will sap
our vitality and threaten our existence as a
race. It is a problem to which all Governments should turn their foremost attention
with the purpose of alleviating the scourge
and in the hope of eventually diminishing it
to proportions which will not threaten us as
We can say
they do at the present moment.
on behalf of the administration, or rather on
behalf of Parliament, that our unemployed
have been given the same advantages in regard to unemployment insurance as in Great
Britain, and that legislation has been brought
forward by our Minister of Labour to keep
a similar nature
step with legislation of
which has been enacted at Westminster.
The third matter to which I should like to
make some allusion is trade depression.
Trade and employment have been fostered
by legislation enabling the Ministry of
Finance to guarantee the repayment of loans
for capital undertakings; and I think we
may claim to have done what could possibly
be done for the purpose of assisting trade as
far as it lay in the hands of the administration to do 80. Agriculture has
encouraged by legislation which has dealt with
stock-raising and also with the marketing of
been
agricultural produce. The Housing problem
has been tackled by the encouragement not
only of municipal building schemes but particularly of the enterprise of the private
builder.
The main legislative activities of the
administration, which may perhaps he said
to have been in fulfilment of pledges which
the Government gave when they first came
into office, have been the passing of the
Licensing Bill of 1923, which I think all will
agree was a great step forward, and which I
feel, has already done a great deal to sweep
away old abuses.
The other Act of our legislative activities
was the Education Act. The problem of
education, I think all will agree, has been
dealt with in a very comprehensive fashion
by the Education Act of 1923. The Act,
whose amendment in the present session is
foreshadowed in the gracious Speech, will
I hope result in the diversion of public
interest from the barren fields of religious
to
controversy
the more fertile and
infinitely more profitable study of the
educational advantages which may be reaped
under the system of local control.
I think that we as legislators may claim
to be proud of the record of our legislation.
We all remember that the Parliament of
which we are Members was not of our
seeking. We accepted it as an assistance to
the British Government, and I think that
we may claim to have done our best to discharge those duties and those responsibilities which were laid upon us by the Act
of 1920. We can claim to have worked
whole-heartedly, and we can also congratulate the people whom we represent on the
manner in which they have accepted the
new conditions and worked whole-heartedly
for the benefit of the Province to which we
all belong. We know quite well that there
is a tendency throughout Ireland, both in
the North and in the South, to imagine
that they are against the Government, and
everyone feels that he is entitled-as he is
entitled-to criticize the actions of his
Government. When Parliament legislated
for Ireland from Westminster the fulminations which were uttered by the North and
by the South at that Parliament did not
make very much difference. All that time
we prospered under the beneficent legislation which came from Westminster and
controlled us in Northern Ireland, and we
were inclined in our criticism to forget the
beneficent conditions under which we
existed. The Parliament at Westminster
delegated its authority to the Parliament
here, and this Parliament is naturally subject to the same forms of criticism. I am
not going to take any exception to the
criticism which has been levelled at this
10 MARCH 1925
Debate on Address.

Debate on Address.
have been discharging the duties which
have been allotted to them under the Act.
The remainder of the Act for the counties
comes into force very shortly, and it has
been a great satisfaction and pleasure to
me to realize the keen interest which has
been taken in the Act and in the duties
which will shortly be allocated to the local
authorities.
Parliament. I would only venture to say
that these critics should realize that we here
are faced with difficulties and dangers which
mean more to, and affect more, a small
Parliament than the great Parliament at
Westminster, and I think it is vital, no
matter what our smaller differences are,
that we should present a united front to
those enemies who threaten our existence as
a Province and who are not so well disposed
to us as we should like them to be. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
The Prime Minister has thought fit to ask
the Governor to grant a dissolution of this
Parliament, and I feel sure that all our
supporters throughout the Six Counties will
realize that he has taken a very wise step.
The Government certainly feels that it has,
if I may use the expression, exhausted its
mandate. We have been in office four years
and I think, as I have said at the
beginning of my speech, that we can claim
to have discharged to the best of our ability
those duties which were placed upon our
shoulders and which we told the people of
the Six Counties that we were willing and
anxious to discharge for their benefit. I do
feel that the Government should receive
another mandate from the people of this
Province to assure them of their confidence
and also to strengthen the Government in
the difficulties with which they are faced at
the present moment. There is what is
known as the problem of the Boundary.
It is in the hands of a Judicial Committee,
and no one has any right-and I do not
feel that anyone in the Six Counties will do
so-to impugn that tribunal. It is composed of men whom we hold in respect. But
from our local and internal point of view it
is of vital importance, not only to the
Prime Minister and the Government, but
also to all of us, to prove to those of our
enemies who are anxious to show that we
are divided in support of our Prime
Minister and our Government, that we are
thoroughly united on the existence of this
Province and that we are prepared to stand
shoulder to shoulder behind the Prime
Minister in maintaining and asserting our
right to the Province to which we are all
proud to belong. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
non-
With reference to the work of the session,
the session will naturally be a very short
one in view of the forthcoming dissolution,
and the chief work of the session will be a
short and, I am glad to think, a
controversial Bill to amend the Education
Act. The Education Act has now been on
the Statute Book for some considerable
time, and I should like to take this opportunity of congratulating the Belfast
authority and the Derry authority for the
active and efficient manner in which they
us
During the time that the Act has been on
the Statute Book we have all naturally been
aware that there has been a certain amount of
controversy. Those of who read the
newspapers have read reams of correspondence on the subject, and those letters have
been in some cases very forcible. They have
been signed by eminent citizens of the Six
Counties, and they have also been anonymous,
but they have one and all shown that a real
interest has been taken in this Act, and they
have embodied misgivings, which have been
honestly in the minds of the writers, that the
Act did not carry out the professions of myself and my colleagues of the Government. As
hon. Members know, the Education Act was
very carefully thought out. I had before me
the duty of drafting an Education Bill to deal
with education in the Six Counties. I had
the great difficulty before me of moving from
a centralised system of education to a decentralised system, and I knew the difficulties
which surrounded that change. I had the
opportunity of receiving a report from an
excellent and thoroughly efficient committee,
and on the report of that committee the Bill
was drawn. And while I may be said to have
been the promoter of the policy in conjunction
with my colleagues in the Government, I
received most admirable assistance from all
my subordinates in drawing up a measure of
a very far-reaching character.
I naturally was as much interested as anyone in the Six Counties that religious instruction should not be endangered or diminished
by the action of the Act which I was bringing
into force, and I felt that that Act had
covered the ground and that there was no
danger that the religious education of the
children should be neglected owing to some
provision in the Act and owing to anything
unforeseen that could have been included in
its Clauses. I am convinced that time would
have shown that I was right. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) But I knew the dangers which
naturally are fostered and which grow up by
differences of opinion and by misunderstanding, and I did my best by every public
utterance I could make on every occasion I
could make it to explain that those fears were
groundless, and that if there was misunderstanding I would do all in my power to
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Marquess of Londonderry]
remove it. I felt that this controversy would
go on until the Act had been in force for
such time that everyone would see that their
fears were groundless. I can only say that
these last few days have been some of the
happiest days of my life because I have
realised that by acceding to the wishes of
those who really have at heart the religious
education of the children of this Province and
by making some alteration, which, in no sense,
infringed the principle of the Bill-which is
democratic control of education throughout
the Six Counties-we could come to an agreement, and this controversy, which naturally
presaged dangers and would be responsible
for differences in our midst, could be swept
away by the stroke of the pen. I have the
greatest satisfaction in feeling that on Thursday I shall be introducing what is called an
Amending Bill, which will once and for all, I
believe, remove these differences and misunderstandings and will unite in the common cause
of education throughout the Six Counties
those great forces of public opinion which
have been divided during the last few months,
and which, I believe, are now united in the
great object of benefiting the children of the
Six Counties. I do not think I need say
anything more on the subject on this occasion.
As I have said, I shall have an opportunity of
introducing an Amending Bill and we can look
forward to the fact that by our united
efforts in the House of Commons and in
the Senate we shall have working in our
midst an Act which will improve the opportunities of education and the building up of
character and youth throughout the Six
Counties, which was the duty imposed upon
Ministers in
us when we took office as
Ulster. I do not feel that on this occasion
there is anything more that I need say. I
sincerely hope that the election struggle
which will be upon us now in a very short
time will show, as I have very little doubt
it will show, that the Province is as united
now as it was four years ago, and that we
are determined to discharge those duties
and responsibilities which the Act of 1920
placed on our shoulders in a manner worthy
of ourselves and worthy of the people whom
we have the honour to represent.
•
PERCEVAL
I
MAXWELL:
Colonel
should like to associate myself with the
Leader of the House in the words of condolence which he used in regard to the
death of Mr. Hotham. We all feel that we
have lost a friend, and our deepest sympathy
goes out to his relatives. I would like to
associate myself also with my hon. and
gallant Friend, the mover of the Address,
in congratulating the Government on the
restoration of peace in this Province. I think
we all realize that they entered upon a very
difficult and arduous duty, and it is a great
satisfaction to all of us to feel that they
have performed it to the entire satisfaction
of the people of Ulster. With regard to
the reference in the Gracious Speech to the
Amending Bill to the Education Act I would
also like to congratulate the Government
on acceding to the widespread feeling that
the Act needed some slight amendment. I
think that what the people of Ulster want
is an amendment to assure religious instruction, and I have no doubt that the
Amending Bill which we have been promised
in the Gracious Speech from the Throne
will provide all that we could desire in
that respect.
Reference has been made to the Land
Purchase Bill which I hope will shortly
become law, although it does not come within our own jurisdiction. I hope it will prove
a benefit to the Province, and that it will
help to cement the good feeling that has
always existed between the farmer and the
landlords of Northern Ireland. I do not
think I have anything more to say, but I
should like to associate myself with the remarks of the Mover and Seconder of the
Address.
Mr. LESLIE: I desire to take this, the
first, opportunity of addressing you on the
occasion of our meeting for another Session
of this Parliament to join as far as I can
and in such words as I can command in the
tribute which the noble Marquess, the
Leader of the House, and other hon. Members have paid to the memory of our
friend, Mr. Hotham, and also to join in the
message of sympathetic condolence to which
the noble Marquess has referred. I cannot
but think that in Mr. Hotham we have lost
one whose assistance was always invaluable
to us.
He possessed, as the Leader of the
House has said, a wealth of knowledge and
experience of technical matters which were
entirely beyond the ken of the ordinary
members of a new Parliament, and he was
gifted with that singular urbanity
manner which enabled him to impart instruction and tender assistance which was
most acceptable, particularly perhaps-and
this is a matter with which I should
like specially to deal-when dealing with
the Private Bills which occasionally
before
and as to which
know SO very little. It was impressed
on me only last Session during the passage
of the lengthy and somewhat complex
Londonderry Corporation Bill before the
Joint Committee how necessary expert
assistance was to Members of Parliament
and how fortunate we were in having Mr.
Hotham with us.

us,

Debate on Address.
that they might possibly follow the
example of those countries, also of
small holdings, which are our keenest

help to bring to our small farmers the same

in saying with what pleasure I listened to
the Mover and Seconder of the Address, and
how I hope we may have further
opportunities of hearing them in this
House. The Gracious Speech itself does
not, I think, provide very much food for
comment. It is necessarily short owing to
the circumstances in which it has been
prepared, and it consists for the most part
of a record and an appreciation of the
work which has been well done by this
Parliament. The matter that attracts most
attention of course is the Bill that will be
brought before us for amending the
Education Act of 1923. Having regard to
what I have already said myself in this
House and outside as regards that measure,
I cannot pretend to be of those who consider that the Act stood in need of amendment, but at the same time I welcome most
cordially any proposal brought before us by
the Minister of Education which will make
for immediate peace and tend towards the
efficient administration of the Act.
I am quite sure that the Bill, when we see
it, will be found to contain nothing discordant with the main principles on which
the Act was based, and will be strictly in
accordance with the somewhat limited
powers imposed upon us by the
very
constitution under which we sit. Satisfied
on this point, as I am sure we will be, I
cannot imagine that the consideration of
the measure will detain us any length of
time, and then we will be clear for the
dissolution. The dissolution is perhaps not
a matter in which this House is very
particularly concerned, because our term of
office is unaffected by a dissolution of
Parliament, but we cannot pass from it
without recording our conviction that the
Government which we support can face the
people of the Six Counties with a very clear
conscience, and conscientiously ask for
approval for what has been done and come
back again. 1 have much pleasure in
supporting the Address.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to say a
word in connection with that part of the
Gracious Speech which has to do with
agriculture, and I am glad to think it can
be recorded that the Government have
succeeded by their legislation in really
bringing about an improvement in agriculture. It is very easy for the Government
to wish agriculture well, but it is a rather
different story to interpret that wish into
action and in a short time to be able to
state that the industry involved has really
benefited by it. I sincerely trust that the
Minister of Agriculture and the Government will be encouraged by the success
which has already attended their efforts to
go further in this matter, and as we are a
country of small holdings, I would suggest
I should
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
just like to say how pleased I am to see that
reference is made in the Gracious Speech to
the Education Act, and that an Amending Bill
is being introduced. There has certainly been
a deep-rooted feeling, particularly in the
minds of the clergymen of the Protestant
churches, that there might be a danger of
religious instruction in the schools being discontinued, and I think that we can hardly
blame them for being anxious in that direction. I have not the same fear myself about
it, but at the same time it was there, and I
think it is right to do all we possibly can to
relieve it. Furthermore, I was present at
the County Grand Orange Lodge of Belfast
and I heard the discussion there regarding
this matter. While I have still the same
feelings with reference to the Bill-I consider
it is one of the best that could possibly be
introduced for the benefit of the people of this
part of the country, particularly the workingclass people-yet, at the same time, a large
number in connection with that great organization certainly had some fears regarding
religious instruction. I am very pleased
indeed to find that a satisfactory
arrangement has been come to and
that an Amending Bill is to be
introduced. When that is done I am sure
that those who criticized will be the very
best supporters the Government could possibly have in the future. Their discussion
and criticism of the Bill was certainly done
in a good-natured way. It was done for the
purpose of safeguarding the welfare of the
children and the people of Northern Ireland.
I was very sorry that any difference of
opinion did exist, and am glad to find now
that it has been satisfactorily arranged.
The other part of the Speech from the
Throne deals more or less with a review of
the past, which appears to be very satis
factory. For instance, it is very pleasing to
find that we have law and order restored
in our midst, the interned prisoners released, and this Curfew removed, so that
we are altogether free. But on the other
hand it is rather deplorable to find that unemployment is still very prevalent in

THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
Northern Ireland. We were no doubt building on the hope that the linen industry was
likely to take a turn for the better
and that we should have a period
of good trade. Unfortunately in that
respect we have been disappointed, and in
spite of the relief schemes encouraged to
such an extent by the Government, we
have considerable distress in our midst.
I do hope everything possible will be done
to relieve it. I know that the Government
are deserving of a considerable amount of
credit for the anxious time they have given
to that question. I am perfectly satisfied
that they are doing all that they possibly
can. If it is possible to introduce any
other measure-I suppose it cannot be done at
present-but even if it were promised I
believe the people would be very well
satisfied. I am sure the present Government will have the entire support of the
greater portion of the people of Northern
Ireland, and, I do think, particularly of the
working classes. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).

Portstewart Electricity Special
Order.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving that the Portstewart
Electricity Special Order be approved, I
would like to say that this Special Order,
granted by the Electricity Commissioners
for Northern Ireland, and confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce, will confer on the
Urban District Council of Portstewart
statutory authority to supply electricity for
public and private purposes within the
Urban District. The town of Portstewart at
the present time is lighted by electricity.
The undertaking is being carried on by a
committee of the townspeople, who purchased from the original owners a few years
ago, and hold for the beneût of the town
until the Council obtains special authority
to take it over. The Rules of Procedure
have been complied with, and no objections
have been received in response to the
statutory advertisements advertising the
application for the Special Order.
The
Order granted follows the usual form. I
beg to move, "That the Portstewart Electricity Special Order, 1924, made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern Ireland under the Electricity Supply Acts,
1882 to 1919, and confirmed by the Ministry
of Commerce under the Electricity (Supply)
Act, 1919, in respect of the Urban District
of Portstewart, in the County of Londonderry, and which was presented to the
House on the 10th day of March, 1925, be
approved."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The House adjourned during pleasure.

Education Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
I think that a very few words from me will
suffice to explain this short Bill to the
House.
Its object, as its title expressly states, is
simply to remove apprehension as to the
adequacy of the recognition of voluntary
religious instruction in the principal Act,
the Education Act of 1923. An impression
appears to have arisen that that Act in
some way diminished and endangered the
status of the voluntary religious element
in our scheme of public elementary
education. It was feared that limitations
had been placed upon the local education
authorities beyond those imposed by
constitution under the Government
Ireland Act of 1920.
our
of
Hon. Members are well aware that the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, imposed
certain restrictions on our activities as a
legislature, and I may also say, that hon.
Members probably know already, that the
Home Office which is the channel of communication for us with the Imperial
Government, keeps a very vigilant and very
watchful eye upon all legislation which
passes our Parliament here with a view
to ascertaining very clearly whether it infringes
in any way the provisions of the Government
of Ireland Act of 1920.
The omission of the passages referred
to in sub-section (2) of the first
Clause of this Bill from the principal Act will have the effect of removing the impression to which I have just
referred and the local education authorities
will remain limited by the bare provisions of
the Act of 1920 alone, without any suggestion
being any longer possible that we have
introduced any new and more extensive
limitations upon them.
While I am personally convinced that the
principal Act merely required an adequate
period of trial to have convinced the
doubters that their fears were without
foundation and that religious instruction
would have proceeded in our schools exactly
along those lines upon which it had proceeded for nearly a century, I am very
happy to recommend the adoption of this
measure to the House. We shall have done
a good day's work if by the passage of this
short and I am glad to say quite non-contentious measure we succeed, as I am sure
we shall succeed, in placing the new system
of education beyond the reach of religious
and
controversy, where all creeds
classes
may join whole-heartedly in taking advantage of the opportunities of progress which
it affords, to the immediate welfare of the
children of Ulster and the lasting benefit of
the Province. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: After what the noble
Marquess has said it seems unnecessary to
detain the House even for a few moments
from passing this explanatory measure into
law. It is a very simple explanation; it is
nothing more. There has been as we know
a good deal of discussion about some of the
provisions of the principal Act, and I cannot help thinking, in looking back over
those discussions, that one or two matters
have been rather overlooked perhaps—
certainly not fully appreciated. And it is
perhaps worth while just for a minute to
mention them in order to help to prevent
any further misapprehension. What is
mainly in my mind is the fact that the
various restrictions and limitations imposed
by the Government of Ireland Act of 1920 are
not all comprised in Section 5 of that
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Education Bill-

THE SENATE.
I
[Mr. Leslie]
measure. The Act is a wide one and there
are different clauses in it of course, and, as
I say, Section 5 is not the only one to which
we have to have regard. Another matter is
that a possible infringement of the terms
of the Act of 1920 might be found just as
much in contraction as in expansion.
think that is often entirely overlooked,
and also I think it is not fully appreciated
that a committee, whether it is a Regional
Education Committee or any other committee,
is really nothing but the aggregation of the
individual units which compose that committee, and therefore the individuals before
they become the committee, so to speak, may
do things which perhaps when they are
sitting as a committee the whole body cannot do. I think that is liable to be overlooked. Altogether I anticipate the speedy
success of the principal Act, now that every
possible excuse for misunderstanding is
venture
about to be removed, and I
to
course
the
which the
that
say
Government has taken is dignified and
most courageous from the moral point
of view. It is far better to ensure immediate
peace, when you can do so without sacrifice
of principle, rather than to wait until peace
arrives through the exhaustion of possible
causes of war. I have much pleasure in
supporting the measure.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.)
CLAUSES 1 (Amendment of Sub-section 3
(3), 26 and 66 (3) of 13 and 14 Geo. 5, c. 21),
and 2 (Short Title), ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported to the
House without amendment.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment.
Moved: That this report be now received.
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
And a message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have
agreed to said Bill without any amendment..
Loans Guarantee Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a Second
time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
a
Mr. LESLIE: I just wish to say that it
is almost a pity that this measure should
leave the House without public attention
being called to the indication which it conof the credit of
veys of the strength
Northern Ireland. It is really a remarkable
be
fact that we should
able to
pass
measure which involves a guarantee of no
less a sum than six million pounds, which
is about five pounds a head of our population, and that people should be ready, as
they are ready, to lend money on the credit
of this Guarantee Fund. It is and must, I
think, remain a cause of the utmost satisfaction to us all that those responsible for
our finances have brought them to such a
condition that the public is prepared to
support them to the extent indicated in this
measure.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
Senate have
to acquaint them that the
agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The object of this Bill is to increase the
total amount of advances which
may be
made by the Ministry of Finance in the
shape of loans to assist the Unemployment
Fund. The measure which is now before the
a further
House sanctions an advance of
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Royal Assent to Bills.
13 MARCH 1925
£750,000 to the amount of the loan already
sanctioned, increasing the total sanctioned to
£3,000,000. Unemployment is unfortunately
still very severe in our midst, and while the
Government are doing all in their power
to remove its cause and also to assist in the
recovery of trade, we are determined to continue the policy already repeatedly approved
by both Houses of Parliament, the principle
of which is the maintenance, at whatever
sacrifice, of equal social conditions for our
workpeople with those which obtain for
their brothers and sisters in Great Britain.
I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate have agreed
to the said Bill without any amendment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to suggest that this House do now adjourn during pleasure, as there is a further
Bill which is to be brought down to us from
another place.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
MESSAGE FROM THE GOVERNOR.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
have received from His Grace, the Governor,
the following acknowledgement of the Address passed at the opening of this Session:-
1 have received with great satisfaction
the expression of thanks for the Speech
with which I have opened the present
Session of Parliament.
ABERCORN.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That they have passed the Appropriation
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Appropriation Bill.

Royal Assent.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am authorised by the Prime Minister, on
his behalf, to inform the Senate that His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland, in
the name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his Assent to
the following Bills:-
Education.
Loans Guarantee.
Unemployment Fund.
Appropriation.
Prorogation of Parliament.
Moved: That the Gentleman Usher of the
Black Rod be commanded to inform the
Commons that the Speech of His Grace the
Governor of Northern Ireland, at the Prorogation of Parliament, is to be communicated
in the Senate forthwith, and that this
House invite the Commons to attend in
order to hear the same.-(The Marquess of
Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Commons being come with their
Speaker, the Speaker of the Senate read the
Speech of His Grace as follows:-
KING'S SPEECH.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I thank you for passing the legislative
measures foreshadowed in my Speech at
the opening of Parliament upon Tuesday
last
King's Speech.
THE SENATE
End of Session.
Members of the House of Commons.
I thank you for the provision of supplies
for the Public Services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
In bidding you farewell, I pray that the
blessing of Almighty God may rest upon
your labours.
Then the Proclamation for proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland was
read by the Speaker as followeth :-
:--
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR
OF NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION FOR PROROGUING
THE PRESENT PARLIAMENT
OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said Act,
that the Governor of Northern Ireland shall
in His Majesty's name summon, prorogue
and dissolve the Parliament of Northern
Ireland;
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
Duke of Abercorn, Governor of Northern
Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish and
declare His Majesty's Pleasure that the
said Parliament be, as from the reading of
this Proclamation in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, prorogued to Thursday,
the Second day of April, one thousand nine
hundred and twenty-five, to be then holden.
Given at Belfast this 13th day of March,
1925.
(Signed by his own hand)
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the Fifth Session (opened

Tuesday,
10th March 1925,) of the First Parliament of
Northern Ireland, in the Fifteenth year
of the Reign of His Majesty King George the
Fifth.
A PROCLAMATION
FOR DISSOLVING THE FIRST PARLIA-
MENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
AND DECLARING THE CALLING
OF ANOTHER.
WHEREAS it is provided by section eleven
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as
modified by and in pursuance of certain
enactments amending the said Act, that the
Governor of Northern Ireland shall in His
Majesty's name summon, prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
And whereas it is desirable that the present
Parliament of Northern Ireland, which
stands prorogued to Thursday the Second day
of April next, shall be dissolved and that a
new Parliament should be called:
Now therefore I, James Albert Edward,
Duke of Abercorn, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation, do publish and
declare His Majesty's pleasure that the said
Parliament be, as from the date of this
Proclamation, dissolved, and that the Senators and Members of the House of Commons
be discharged from their Meeting and Attendance on the said Thursday, the Second day of
April next;
And I do hereby make known to all His
Majesty's loving subjects His Royal Will and
Pleasure to call a new Parliament of Northern Ireland, and do further declare that I
have taken Order that Writs shall be issued
forthwith in His Majesty's Name under the
Great Seal of Northern Ireland. in due Form
and according to Law, for calling a Parliament of Northern Ireland to meet at the
City of Belfast on Tuesday, the Fourteenth
day of April next, and for causing the Senators and Commons who are to serve in the
said Parliament to be duly returned to and
give their Attendance in His Majesty's said
Parliament on Tuesday, the Fourteenth day
of April next, which Writs are to be returnable in due course of Law.
Given the Fourteenth day of March, in
the year of our Lord One Thousand Nine
Hundred and Twenty-five.
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
INDEX.

THE
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATES:
(OFFICIAL REPORT.)
IN THE
FIRST SESSION OF THE SECOND PARLIAMENT OF NORTHERN IRELAND
APPOINTED TO MEET THE FOURTEENTH DAY OF APRIL, IN THE
FIFTEENTH YEAR OF THE REIGN OF
HIS MAJESTY KING GEORGE V.
FIRST SERIES-VOLUME VI.
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 14th April, 1925.
Opening of Parliament.
His Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland being seated on the Throne, commanded
the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let
the Commons know "It is His Grace's
pleasure they attend him immediately in this
House," who being come,
His GRACE was pleased to speak
follow:-
as
Senators and Members of the House of
Commons of Northern Ireland, in the name
and on behalf of His Majesty I have to let
you know that, as soon as the Members of
both Houses shall have been sworn, the causes
of His Majesty's calling this Parliament of
Northern Ireland will be declared to you;
and, it being necessary that a Speaker of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland
should be first chosen, I declare His Majesty's
pleasure that you, Members of the House of
Commons, do proceed to the choice of a
Speaker, and that you present such person
whom you shall so choose here for His
Majesty's Royal approbation.
Then the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Speaker of the House of Commons
His Grace the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, again commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know "It is His Grace's pleasure they attend
him immediately in this House," who being
come,
The SPEAKER-ELECT OF THE HOUSE
OF COMMONS (The Right Hon. Robert
William Hugh O'Neill) said: Your Grace,
I have to acquaint you that in obedience
to His Majesty's commands His Majesty's
most faithful Commons of Northern Ireland
have, in the exercise of their undoubted
the
rights and privileges, proceeded to
election of a Speaker, and that their choice
has fallen upon myself. I now present
myself to Your Grace, and submit myself
with all humility for His Majesty's gracious
approbation.
HIS GRACE: Mr. O'Neill, being sensible
of your zeal in the public service, and of
your ample sufficiency to execute all the important duties of the position which His
faithful Commons of Northern Ireland have
selected you to discharge, I do on His
Majesty's behalf most readily approve and
confirm you as their Speaker.
The SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF
COMMONS: Your Grace, I submit myself
with all humility and gratitude to His
Majesty's gracious commands. It is now my
duty, in the name and on behalf of the Commons of Northern Ireland, to lay claim by
humble petition to the rights, privileges, and
immunities assured to the Commons of
Members Sworn.
Election of Speaker.
THE SENATE
Northern Ireland by the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, and that the most favourable
construction shall be put on all their proceedings.
In regard to myself, I pray that, if in discharge of my duties I should inadvertently
fall into any error, it may be imputed to myself alone, and not to His Majesty's most
faithful Commons of Northern Ireland.
HIS GRACE: Mr. Speaker of the Commons
of Northern Ireland, in the name of His
Majesty I do most readily confirm the rights,
privileges, and immunities to which you
have laid claim.
With respect to yourself, Sir, although His
Majesty is sensible that you stand in need of
no such assurance, His Majesty will ever put
the most favourable construction on your
words and actions.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons, it will now be the duty of
Members of both Houses of Parliament of
Northern Ireland to take the required
oath.
To-morrow, at noon, the causes of His
Majesty's calling this Parliament of Northern Ireland will be declared to you.
Then the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.
Members Sworn.
The Senators following took and subscribed the Oath, or made and subscribed
the Affirmation required by law, and signed
the Roll.
Mr. Speaker (the Most Hon. the Marquess of
Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O., D.L.)
The Most Hon. the Marquess of Londonderry,
K.G., M.V.O., H.M.L., County of Down.
Viscount Massereene and Ferrard, D.S.O.,
H.M.L., County of Antrim.
The Lord Mayor of Belfast (Sir William
George Turner).
Sir Thomas James Dixon, Bart.
John Clements Glendinning, Esquire.
Viscount Bangor, O.B.E., D.L.
Sir Emerson Crawford Herdman.
Henry Bruce Wright Armstrong, Esquire.
James Hill Dickson, Esquire.
John Andrew Long, Esquire.
The Mayor of Londonderry (John Gilbert
Magee, Esquire).
The Right Honourable Samuel Cunningham.
John Porter Porter, Esquire.
James Graham Leslie, Esquire.

Joseph Cunningham, Esquire.
William John McDowell, Esquire.
House adjourned at
One o'clock.
King's Speech.
King's Speech.
15 APRIL 1925
THE
SENATE

Wednesday, 15th April, 1925.
The Senate met at Twelve of the Clock, Mr.
SPEAKER (Most Hon. the Marquess of
Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O., D.L.,) in the
Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know "It is His Grace's pleasure that they
attend him immediately in this House".
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Speaker,
His Grace delivered the following Speech
from the Throne:--
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I feel confident that the new Parliament
now inaugurated will conduct with wisdom
and moderation the important matters entrusted to its control.
My Government recognises that the
most important and urgent subjects with
which it is faced are the state of our
Industries and the problem of Unemployment. Every effort will be made to encourage agriculture, to aid industry and
commerce through the operation of the
Loans Guarantee Acts and, in association
with Local Authorities, through schemes
of public utility, to find useful employment for our people.
You will be asked to authorise the provision of seed potatoes in certain districts
where necessitous circumstances prevail.
A Bill will be introduced this Session
continuing the principle of the Rent Restriction Act, but amending it in certain
directions.
A Bill will be laid before you to provide
for the better administration of the
Drainage Acts. A Committee established
to examine this subject has made suggestions, which will be embodied in the
Bill, investing Local Authorities with
certain powers and providing more elastic
machinery to cope with the problem of
the destructive flooding of our agricultural
lands in various districts. For more than
a century this has exercised the minds of
the best engineers and the phenomenal
rainfall of the last three years has emphasised the urgency of seeking some
palliative.
A Bill will be introduced to make provision for the improvement of mining
facilities.
A Bill will be laid before you to make
provision for granting moderate Compassionate Pensions or Gratuities to
persons, or the widows and children of
persons who have rendered distinguished
service in the field of science, literature
or the arts, or who have rendered exceptionally meritorious service in a public
capacity.
Amongst other measures which will
engage your attenton are a Bill dealing
with the Preservation of Ancient Monuments, a Bill to establish a Pharmaceutical Society for Northern Ireland, a Bill to
prevent the sale in our State of inferior
brands of condensed and dried milk, and
a Jury Bill, which is being prepared in
pursuance of the findings drawn up by the
recent Commission.
Members of the House of Commons.
Estimates for the public services will be
laid before you in due course. I am glad
to learn that the general financial position
is sound, but nevertheless it is recognised
that economical administration is essential for the well-being of Northern Ireland, and every effort will be made to reduce public expenditure to the lowest
possible limit consistent with efficiency.
Members of the Senate and
House of Commons.
My Ministers, recognising the importtance of the provision of electric power in
the industrial areas of Northern Ireland,
are giving this question close attention.
They are also giving careful consideration
to the valuable recommendations of the
Development Commission on afforestation.
harbours and other matters.
Your labours upon these and all other
matters I humbly commend to the blessing
of Almighty God.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire, and
the Commons withdrew.
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
