MEMBER SWORN.
William Frederick Coates, Bart.,
sworn and signed the Test Roll.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO HIS MAJESTY'S
MOST GRACIOUS SPEECH.
Mr. ARMSTRONG (in the uniform of His
Majesty's Lieutenant of the County of
Armagh): I beg to move:
That a humble Address be presented to
his Grace in the following words:-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal Subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to Your Grace
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
15 APRIL 1925
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
I am deeply sensible of the honour which
has been conferred on my county by the
selection of its Representative to move the
Address of Thanks for the Gracious
Speech at the opening Session of our
Second Parliament. But I cannot but be
conscious of my inability to perform
adequately the honourable and important
duty entrusted to me.
The Gracious Speech opens with an expression of confidence that wisdom and
moderation will be conspicuous in dealing
with important matters entrusted to the
control of Parliament. I feel that that
confidence has not been misplaced because I
notice a disposition generally throughout the
community of moderation and good sense and
good feeling. Throughout our county, which
borders on the Free State for 66 miles, and
indeed all over the county, good feeling between us and our old opponents prevailed
during the recent election to such an extent
as to induce one to hope that the day may
not be so far distant when they may join with
us in welcoming such a programme of useful
and practical Measures as those outlined in
the Gracious Speech, all designed to meet the
needs of the whole community.
Foremost among these are the steps to be
taken to alleviate the unsatisfactory condition of our industries, including our
greatest, agriculture, which employs no less
than 60 per cent, of our people, and the relief
of unemployment. All that the Government
can do in this direction will, I am sure, be
done, and the achievements of His Majesty's
Government in the past have been so successful in various directions as to fill us with
hope. But it is no easy task. The causes lie
deep, and beyond the control of any Government, and His Majesty's Ministers are not
alone in endeavouring to solve these most
difficult problems.
The evils of unemployment can scarcely
be exaggerated, and there is, I fear, a generation fast growing up which has never had
the chance of acquiring the habit of work,
but has been driven to lean upon public
assistance, with consequent general demoralization and loss of self respect. An officer
lately told me that on questioning a batch
of recruits he learned that, though calling
themselves labourers, not one of them had
ever worked or knew how to use the simplest
tool. This is a deplorable state of affairs,
and an alarming one for the future.
The paragraph in the Gracious Speech that
legislation is intended for the better administration of the Drainage Acts is specially
welcome to the many who suffer from defective drainage and destructive floods.
Arterial drainage in this country has been
much neglected for many years from various
causes, and much land which used to produce
valuable crops is reverting and has reverted
to marsh and swamp.
Among the larger rivers which cause
extensive flooding I know of no worse nor
older offender than the Bann. No one passing Portadown after heavy rains can have
failed to notice the wide tract of land
covered by water, almost resembling an inland lake. This water frequently remains a
considerable time on the land, souring it, and
rendering the herbage unwholesome for stock.
Indeed, defective drainage is largely responsible for the terrible ravages among
our flocks and herds of "Fluke" this season.
As stated in the Gracious Speech the Bann
floods have been suffered for many years. A
pamphlet by a civil engineer named Glasgow
gives an interesting account of the attempts
made from time to time to reduce these floods.
They engaged the attention of the Irish
Parliament so long ago as 1785, and as much
as £250,000 or £300,000 has been expended
and yet the floods have not ceased to cause
ruinous injury. My hon. Friend opposite
knows more about the Bann, particularly the
Lower Bann than I do, but I fancy he has
regarded it from a somewhat different standpoint, namely as a source of power for electric energy, another most important matter
referred to in the Gracious Speech. We hear
with great satisfaction that the importance
of providing electric power in our industrial
areas which require this help to equip them
for the competition they encounter has not
been overlooked, and that the Report of the
Development Commission is receiving that
careful consideration which is a necessary
prelude to taking beneficial action. I feel
that I have now said all that I am able to
say upon the Gracious Speech, and after
thanking the Senate for SO patiently
listening to me I beg to move.
Mr. PORTER PORTER (in morning dress):
I have the honour to second this motion that
has been moved by my hon. Friend Senator
Armstrong. But first I have to ask for the
indulgence of this House and for that forbearance which they always give to Members addressing the House for the first time.
I am perfectly sensible of the fact that it is
only as the representative of a small Border
county and not for my own self I am asked
to second this resolution. I may remind hon.
Senators that the Border county I belong to
is the smallest of the six, but it is the most
loyal of the six. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
May I refer to the last election before I go
It
into His Majesty's Gracious Speech
must be with the greatest satisfaction that
we look to the results. It shows that there
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Porter Porter
is great confidence in the Prime Minister and
in his Government. It shows that the
in the
people are determined to remain
security of Ulster and not follow the Free
State. The Boundary question was the main
fact of the election, and the answer was
emphatic. The people, the heart and bone of
Ulster, were determined to remain under the
security of the Northern Government. They
were determined they would not go under
the Government of the Free State. I would
like to make that quite clear. I am not saying a single word against the Government
of the Free State, but, for all that, we see
by the papers and we know by the facts that
the Government are still active and very
much alive.
Now in the Gracious Speech I see the Government are determined to advance agriculture as much as they possibly can. I know
the Minister of Agriculture is determined to
try to bring agriculture in Ireland up to a
par with those other countries who by means
of their agriculture, live by what they export
It is a difficult task.
into Great Britain.
Everyone in the country is aware that the
small Irish farmer is an obstinate man, if
there is one. He thinks to go on farming in
the way his father and his grandfather
farmed. That cannot be done at the present
time.
Farming is advancing and will advance. I only hope that the Minister of
Agriculture will bring agriculture in Ireland up to a higher level and place us on a
par with countries like Holland and Belgium.
There is one fly in the ointment, and that
is the Summer Time Bill--the most disastrous
Bill that ever was framed and another
burden on the shoulders of the farmer, as
if the farmer had not enough with the
weather as well as other things. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
The mover of this Motion has referred to
It is a most
drainage and to the Bann.
intricate thing and a very large operation
to carry out drainage. There is no doubt
Ireland wants it, and I only hope that
the experts will be able to devise some
means to mitigate the evil of flooding--something practical and something really useful
to the country.
The Gracious Speech also refers to pensions.
Now everybody--and none more than myself-wishes to see old people cared for, but
I was on committees on old age pensions
in my own county area and I very much
regretted to see that sons did not think
more of their old fathers. They might have
done something for them themselves instead
of leaving it to public outlay to assist them.
I think before I sit down I may congratulate the noble Marquess, the Leader of the
House, on the Education Act and the great
good it will do in this country. If there were
small dissensions-very small in their waythe noble Lord has met them with give
and take, and his opponents met him with
give and take, and he has now pleased everybody. I have no more to say except to thank
the House for their forbearance in listening
to me seconding this Motion.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I hardly think I
need emphasise the importance of the occasion on which we are assembled to-day, which
is the opening of the second Parliament of
Northern Ireland. I must congratulate-and
I do so with diffidence-the mover and
seconder of this resolution. Each in his eloquent and able speech has suggested that the
invitation given to him was by reason of
his representative character-the fact that
they represent two of our important counties. But I can assure them that while that
naturally was in the minds of this House we
welcome them individually-(Hon. Members:
Hear, hear)-and we were particulary gratified to listen to the speeches which they
delivered. Mr. Senator Armstrong, who, I
may say, was an old colleague of mine in the
now almost forgotten Irish Convention,
speaks
naturally
with great authority from the County of Armagh,
and he has told us of the great
importance he attaches to the agricultural
question which naturally is uppermost in our
minds. The seconder of the Motion Mr.
Senator Porter-Porter, I would venture to
congratulate on his maiden speech. I can
assure him, speaking for this House, that we
were very gratified indeed to listen to the
words which fell from his lips, and we most
sincerely hope that he will often in future
take part in the debates in this House. (Hon
Members: Hear, hear).
This House at the moment remains unchanged, notwithstanding the fact that we
have been passing through a General Election.
And while we naturally mourn the loss of
more than one of our Members, still I think
we can congratulate this House on the work
it has achieved, and perhaps while not taking
anything of an unduly pessimistic view I
somehow feel that perhaps our labours may
be of a more arduous character than they
have been in the past, and that we may be
called upon to take decisions which may
have very far-reaching results.
I think it is perhaps necessary on this
occasion that I should say a few words about
the General Election which has just taken
place. I feel that I should be in agreement
with everyone when I brush aside those
various criticisms which we have heard from
different quarters as to the wisdom of holding a General Election. It is in the knowledge of everyone that have passed
We
Debate on Address.

Debate on Address.
our
train of this disease by which we are assailed at the present moment. My hon. Friend
Mr. Senator Armstrong referred particularly
to that question, and he spoke in moving
phrases of the harm which this constant and
continuous unemployment is working
amongst the population of this country, and
we do feel that no Government would be
worthy of the name unless they did everything in their power to remedy the state of
things in which we find ourselves at the
present moment.
15 APRIL 1925
through four very strenuous years. I think
I am entitled to say that, in accordance with
Parliamentary custom and the political
ideas which have held for many years past,
we had to use an expression which everyone
will understand-exhausted
mandate,
and it was only right that we who claim to
be a democratic Government, should call
upon the electors of this Province to decide
as to the continuance or non-continuance of
the Government. That is the domestic aspect of the question, but I think there is
another one, and one which will appeal to
hon. Members, and that is the importance
of deliberately expressing our opinion
through our duly elected representatives that
our position in regard to the Boundary
question remains unchanged now and is exactly the same as it was four years ago when
we were called upon to undertake these
arduous duties. I think we can say that the
Province has deliberately and without any
shadow of doubt strengthened the hands of
the Government in putting forward our
policy with regard to our determination to
maintain the integrity of the Six Counties
not only before the people of Great Britain
but before the citizens of the Empire.
The legislative programme in the
Gracious Speech from the Throne is one
which I think will command the sympathy
and support of hon. Members of this House.
I am not claiming that it is in any sense
an ambitious programme, but I somehow
feel that after these four years which we
have spent in legislating for the benefit of
this Province now is the time for us to turn
to those enactments which are of vital importance and which deal with the great
question of agriculture to which my
two hon. Friends have referred in
such eloquent and such wise phrases.
We know quite well in this Province that our staple industry is agriculture
and that it is of vital importance that
everything which conduces to the benefit of
agriculture should be done by the Government. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) The
drainage of the Bann is a question which
has been before us and before our forefathers for generations past. I think we
can hope that something may be done to
relieve the country from the ravages and
devastation caused by the flooding of that
river. We have certainly passed through an
abnormal season,, and I do not feel that
we could have delayed much longer in
bringing about legislation which we hope
will have beneficial and important results.
The question of unemployment is one in
which we are unfortunately not in an unique
position. It is one which has assailed politicians also on the other side of the water,
and we can only hope that trade may improve
and that we may be able to mitigate the
drawbacks and disasters which follow in the
There is also the question of Rent
Restriction which will come before
Parliament during the Session. That is
a matter in which we try to keep on
parallel lines with Great Britain, and
I feel sure it is a Measure which the
Members of this House can deal with
very adequately. They can bring the benefit
of their experience to bear on it because it is
one of the most important questions of the
present time.
The Gracious Speech deals with Mining
facilities. I think we can look with satisfaction on the mining undertakings which
are being carried out at the present moment,
and congratulate Sir Samuel Kelly on his
enterprise in that connection. (Hon. Memhers: Hear, hear.) It is proposed that a Bill
should be introduced for the preservation of
Ancient Monuments. This is a matter in
which a great many people take a deep
interest, but the whole people should be
interested in maintaining the great traditions which belong to the Province. I feel
sure that that is a Measure which will pass
without any difficulty through both Houses
of Parliament. There is a Measure for
establishing a Pharmaceutical Society, and
also a Jury Bill. These in the main
constitute the work that will come before us
during the Session.
Perhaps on this occasion you will expect me
to take something in the nature of a retrospective view of the last four years, and in
that connection I should begin with the
question of Finance. Our position, as hon.
Members are aware, is a satisfactory one, and
I feel that our congratulations are due to the
Minister of Finance (Mr. Pollock) for what
he has done on our behalf. He has watched
with great vigilance all expenditure in connection with the Province, and I do not feel
that the criticisms which have been levelled
against him can be justified. I can speak as
a Minister, and I have had difficulty on more
than one occasion in obtaining from our
Chancellor of the Exchequer funds which I
felt were requisite for carying out the duties
entrusted to me. That has also been the
experience of my colleagues in the Government. He has been assailed on all sides for
heavy and large expenditure, but that is the
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
lot of all Chancellors of the Exchequer. From
another quarter comes the apparent desire
for economy, and an accusation that the
Minister of Finance is wasting the money of
the State. I feel that Mr. Pollock has
adopted a very sensible and wise via media,
and that in his conduct of the finances of
the Province there is very little to be
desired.
These four years through which we have
passed have been strenuous years of effort.
We undertook our duties at a time when the
country was in a chaotic condition. The
Government of the United Kingdom was confronted with many difficulties, and we felt
that the only way to ease the situation was
by accepting the Act of 1920. In that connection I feel that our thanks and congratulations are due to our leader, Sir James Craig.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) He has carried
a great burden on his shoulders with neverfailing courage, and the fact that we are now
living in peaceful conditions and are continuing on lines as prosperous as we can hope for
under the circumstances is due to the wise
manner in which he has led the people of the
Six Counties. It is quite true that we have
a certain number of friends in Great Britain,
and I would venture to express the thanks
which we owe to them. On the other hand,
we are in a more isolated position than we
were four years ago, and perhaps we will be
in an even more isolated position in the
future. The people of Great Britain are
naturally obsessed with the difficulties with
which they themselves are faced. They are
burdened with heavy taxation, which I believe
does more to turn people's attention away
from the great problems that face them than
anything else, and we must therefore realise
that our success in the future depends almost
entirely on ourselves. We must acquire the
power of having confidence in ourselves and
confidence in our ability to work out our own
destiny.
During the last four years we passed two
great Measures. I refer to the Temperance
Act and the Education Act, and I feel that
both these Acts are in accordance not only
with the wishes but with the spirit and
the temperament of the people whom we
represent. I do not think that I can usefully
trespass on your indulgence by going into
the details of those two great Measures. The
Education Act is naturally one that is very
close to my heart and on its success depends
a great deal of the future of this Province.
In this connection I would venture to say
how deeply we deplore the loss of the late
Parliamentary Secretary to the Ministry of
Education, Mr. McKeown. (Hon Members :
Hear, hear.) No one threw himself more
whole-heartedly or more enthusiastically into the work of the Goverarient than he did,
and it was with great regret that he was compelled, as hon. Members know, to resign his
position owing to the demands made on his
time by the businesses in which he took
such an important part. When we said
farewell to him some few days ago we lost
a great friend and a great supporter of the
in which we are all SO deeply
cause
interested.
With regard to the Education Act, I welcome the remarks which have fallen from my
hon. Friend, Senator Porter Porter, in which
he said that all our difficulties are over and
that we shall now sail forward on the calm
waters of peace and the absence of controversy. The difficulty which I personally experienced in regard to that Act was that it
was almost impossible to fight it as a political question. It was a Measure which I
knew would excite a great deal of controversy, more because of the difficulty
of
I
the
understanding 80 complicated an
Act than of the dangers which were
comprised within the four corners of
the Measure itself. As hon. Members
know well, I was assailed from all quarters,
by laymen, by clerics, in the newspapers, and
from the public platform, but I somehow
feel quite convinced that if those attacks had
been anything more than of an oratorical
character I should not have the honour of
standing before you at the present moment.
The controversy was a very bitter one and
regret to a very large extent
weapons that were used throughout that controversy. The suggestion
put
forward, and put forward in
the most approved political manner, which
those who have contested constituencies know
something about, was that the Government
was opposed to the maintenance of religious
teaching in the schools, and that I for one
was perhaps an atheist, and being an atheist
was determined that the Bible should be banished from the schools of the Six Counties.
Well, I think that by now everybody has
realised that that was an entirely erroneous
opinion. We were faced with the limitations
of the 1920 Act. We had to contend with
an even greater difficulty. That was the fact
that if you admit we should have free education and that is an admission with which
we all agree and which we do not discuss
at the moment--you then call upon the
people of the country, regardless of their
denominational opinions, to contribute to the
maintenance of education. That being so the
State is placed in the position first by the
Act of 1920, and secondly by the recognised
custom of nations prevailing at the present
time, that it cannot endow denominational
religious instruction. I think the majority
of people will agree that we have done everything that we could possibly do. We have
established an education system which I believe satisfies, as far as they can be satisfied, the educational needs of the Province,
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
15 APRIL 1925
and we have given those facilities and those
opportunities for imparting religious instruction to the children that can be given.
The question of religion is one that is
deeply embedded in the hearts of the people
of Ulster and nobody recognises that more
than I and my colleagues in the Government
do. But I would venture to say to those
who have been so loud in their attacks
on the Government and on myself that there
is no Act of Parliament that human brains
can devise which will make people religious. Much depends upon the people themselves whether the religious spirit of which
we are so proud in this Province will continue or not continue. As hon. Members are
aware. I was met by more than one deputation-in fact I saw many deputations which
asked me to amend the Education Act in directions in which I could not amend. The
difficulty was increasing and I felt that the
division between us was becoming more intense until a few weeks ago, when I was
happy to make those concessions which considered opinion believed were in the interests
of the Act. We introduced an Amending
Act which I am glad to say has brought us
together again, and I do feel that, if the
Act on the Statute Book is worked by the
education authorities which are now being
set up, we shall reap in the course of the
next few years the benefit of an enlightened
system of education.
There are many questions on which I
should like to address the Senate, but I have
no doubt that in the course of the next few
months I shall have an opportunity of doing
So. I can only say that I am not looking to
the future with anything in the nature of
apprehension. I am certain that the people
of this Province are imbued with that peculiar genius for the administration of selfgovernment which belongs to the British
race-are imbued with that genius as much
as any section of the community which exists
in any of the British dominions, and it is for
that reason I believe we shall be able to pursue our course with success. Our position
at this moment is different to what it was in
the last Parliament. At that time we possessed a great majority of Members supporting the Government-a highly unsatisfactory
and perhaps, if I may use the expression, unhealthy position to be in. It gave opportunities to the ardent and enthusiastic
Members to attack the Government
because they knew quite well there
no other Government
take
to
its place. Now while that position is
not altogether altered there are signs that
there are possibilities of alternative Governments. There is the possibility of a Nationalist Government, and there is perhaps the
possibility of a Socialist Government.
was
These are matters which I would venture
to call upon the Province to consider and
to realise which is the Government which
they believe serves them best. Times may
change, and we may find ourselves in the
--not the near future, perhaps, but in the
future-in a very different position to that
in which we are at the present time. I do
feel that, at this present moment when we
are confronted with external problems, the
people of this Province should realise in
which direction their safety lies. We have
given a completely authoritative answer as
regards the Boundary, and I think we can
look forward to the integrity of our position.
But there are many social questions with
which we are now faced, and whereas, the
people of this Province may have considered
that it was the duty of the Government to
think for them and to carry out enactments
for their benefit, the people themselves taking
perhaps only a distant and critical view of the
proceedings, now I would venture to call upon them to understand that they are responsible for the Government which represents
them. I believe that if we look upon our
Parliamentary institutions and our Parliamentary endeavours and our activities with
that idea and that view in our minds we
need have no qualms for the future of this
Province.
Mr. LONG: I should like if I may to congratulate the mover and seconder of the
Address on being selected for the high and
honourable duty which they have so capably
carried out. I should also like to associate
myself with the remarks of the seconder, the
hon. Senator Mr. Porter Porter, when he
stated that the Minister of Agriculture was
doing great work for Northern Ireland. I
can assure him that the farming population,
for whom I speak, very highly appreciate the
efforts that are being made on their behalf
by the Ministry of Agriculture. There is no
doubt what he states is true, that the small
farmer is very conservative, but he is
gradually getting to know that if he is to
compete successfully with the foreigner he
must farm on scientific lines. I am very
pleased to say a welcome improvement in
that direction has already taken place. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
I should also like to express my pleasure
that agriculture looms so
the
largely in
King's Speech. I welcome the announcement
that the drainage question is to be dealt with,
and I can assure the Government that the
County Councils will with pleasure administer an Act that is long overdue and one that
will bring much needed relief to a large
and important section of the community. I
should also like to express the pleasure with
which, I am sure, the intention to introduce
a Jury Bill will be hailed by jurors who
suffered in the past from a number of
Debate on Address. 20
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE.
[Mr. Long]
grievances. I am sure it will give great
pleasure and be a very useful Act.
It is a matter for satisfaction that the
election passed off without any disturbance
of the peace, and I join in congratulating the
Government on that fact and also on the fact
that law and order prevail throughout our
Border. For the peace we enjoy I believe
the Government are largely indebted to the
"B" Specials. The services they have
rendered should not be forgotten. In war our
soldiers and sailors are very popular and
highly esteemed. In piping times of peace
they are almost forgotten. So it is with the
Special Constabulary. In the time of our
troubles
their self-sacrificing exertions
on our behalf were highly appreciated,
and now in some quarters there is a disposition to dispense with their services
altogether. These services are practically
honorary.
No working man after
a hard day's work, weary and tired as he is,
would like to forego his night's sleep and
patrol the roads in his neighbourhood in fair
and foul weather, and also attend regularly
at the call for drill for a paltry three and
sixpence per week. I admit that the amount
received is a welcome addition to the income
of many a poor family, and I think it is only
right that they should get this trifling sum.
I therefore plead for the continuance of the
Special Constabulary for at least a year in
their full strength or almost full strength.
Mr. DICKSON: The "B" Specials?
Mr. LONG: Yes, the "B" Specials. It
must be remembered that there are elements
of danger in our midst and there are elements of danger outside our borders and the
course prudence dictates is to be prepared
for every emergency. I therefore think they
should be continued, as I have said, at almost full strength for at least another year.
The dominant wish in each heart in Ulster
is peace. I am persuaded that given a period
of peace to pursue our legitimate occupations
an era of prosperity will ensue and contentment and happiness will characterise all our
people.
Mr. WOODS: I would like to support
the Address in reply to the Gracious Speech
from the Throne, and also to congratulate
the proposer and seconder for the eloquent
way they put it before this House. I listened with great attention to the speech
which the noble Marquess delivered, and he
made one remark with which I cannot altogether agree. He said that the legislation
proposed in the King's Speech could not be
claimed by the Government to be very ambitious. I would disagree with that remark,
because the first clause entirely does away
with that. think they are soaring to very
ambitious heights if they expect to solve the
problem of unemployment. I am sure if they
are able to solve that problem they will be
looked upon with great satisfaction by the
people of Ulster and with great jealousy by
the people on the other side of the water.
Many Governments have tried to solve that
problem. One Government, which had the
only solution of the problem of unemployment, after nine months, I think, had to retire without coming to any solution of that
most difficult problem. I hope this Government will in its wisdom find some solution
of the problem of unemployment.
Another matter of legislation referred to
in the King's Speech is one in which I am very
much interested-the Rent Restriction Act.
I would again appeal to the Government-I
entered an appeal one time before-I would
again appeal to them when the drafting of
this Measure is under way to remember to
have a clause inserted for the protection of
the small shopkeepers. There never has been
a clause for the protection of that particular
class of tenant, and they are very much put
upon by the landlord in the very high rents
they are forced to pay. Some of them are
only reaping merely a livelihood out of the
small shops. I hope something will be done
to insert a clause for the protection of the
small shopkeeper when that Act comes to be
drafted.
I listened with great pleasure to the noble
Marquess's speech with regard to the education question. I am one of those who were
entirely in favour of the original Education
Act. I personally objected to the alteration
of even one comma. I would rather the
Education Act had remained as it was, as
the part taken out was, in my opinion, the
best protection they had in the Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope that I may be permitted to add my own humble tribute to the
hon. Senators who moved and seconded the
Address in reply to the Gracious Speech, for
the very well chosen words in which they
have performed their duty. It is at least a
very happy coincidence that those hon. Senators selected for that duty to-day should be
representatives of those Border counties
which have been so much in the mind of us
all during the past few weeks and indeed
I may say for an even longer period. Speaking to-day here at the inaugural meeting of
the second Parliament of Northern Ireland
it seems to me impossible that our thoughts
should not go back to our first meeting in
the City Hall some four years ago. And
when we look round the chamber this afternoon we are reminded of the changes in our
personnel which have taken place during
those four years. Apart from the normal
changes amongst our official Members, one
of whom has been returned to us as an
elected Senator, we have experienced translation and resignation and above all we have
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
15 APRIL 1925
lost by death no fewer than four of our
original Members, and an additional one who
joined us in the month of April in our
second year. The changes are very great
and I think that we cannot but recall them.
However, we are concerned to-day with the
present and the future rather than with the
past, and the matter that is particularly before us is the Gracious Speech. The
Gracious Speech invites attention partly, as
is usual, on account of what it includes and
partly indirectly on account of matters to
which it does not refer. I need add nothing
to what has been said on the important
question of drainage, but I should like to
welcome particularly the proposal to
establish in Northern Ireland something in
the nature of the Imperial Civil List, which
I understand is what is the intention of the
reference to Compassionate Pensions and
Gratuities to persons or their widows and
children who have rendered distinguished
service in certain branches of our national
life. It would be something of a disgrace to
us if this part of the United Kingdom were
left out of a scheme which exists for the
State recognition of special cases of that
nature.
Then we observe-and indeed one of the
previous speakers has mentioned it--the reference to the Jury Bill which is to be
drawn up in pursuance of the findings of the
recent Commission. That would be, of course,
the Commission presided over by Lord Justice
Andrews, and I gather that one of the main
objects will be to relieve the citizens of
Belfast from the burden which must have
been becoming too grievous to be borne.
The report was a somewhat lengthy one
and, if I may say so, a very able one, but
I trust that Parliament will not be asked to
agree to all the proposals contained in that
report, as to some of which the members of
the Commission
themselves
not
unanimous.
were
Passing now to the matters which are not
mentioned in the Gracious Speech, and which
I myself should have liked to have seen in
it, I may say at once that I recognise that in
a non-controversial programme in a Session
which was not designed to be of undue length
it was obvious that certain matters would
have to stand over, and possibly the matters
to which I refer are included amongst them.
I should have liked to have seen a proposal to
deal to some extent with the recommendations
of the comparatively recent report on the
general question of Valuation, with particular reference to the relief of those heavy
lands which it is admitted in that report, as
indeed it was known to us before, bear a
somewhat
proportion of Imperial
Taxation. I recognise that that is part of
a larger question, and I am not disposed to
complain that nothing is said about it to-day.
big
Then there is the question of the establishment in Northern Ireland of a Court of
Criminal Appeal. That is a matter which
should be taken up as soon as an opportunity
can be found and I feel that the Government is just as anxious to institute such a
Court as I am myself. There is also the
question relating to the further protection of
those men and women who have not only
been unfortunate in the matrimonial lottery
but some of whom have had the misfortune
to draw a sort of booby prize. There has
been a Measure introduced in the Imperial
House which gives them very much further
protection than obtains in Northern
Ireland to-day. I cannot but feel that a
matter of that kind might not inappropriately be dealt with in the first instance
by a Private Member's Bill, because the
matter could then be considered and it could
be ascertained what demand there is for it
and how public opinion regards it. All that
could be done without committing anybody
to anything. A Private Member's Bill rarely
reaches the Statute Book in the form in
which it is originally introduced in Parliament, but I venture to suggest that its
introduction and discussion do sometimes
serve a useful purpose, and I have it in my
mind to bring the matter before the House in
that way.
Taking the situation as a whole, I feel
satisfied that those of us who sit here may
regard the recent verdict of the Province
as a verdict in favour of the Government
which it is our privilege and duty to support.
We have no reason to be pessimistic as a
result of the last election and I think we may
go forth on much the same path as we were
endeavouring to pursue before in furtherance
of the same Government and the same policy
I have much
we have supported hitherto.
pleasure in supporting the Address.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would also
like to congratulate the mover and the
seconder of the Address. The Gracious
Speech makes particular reference to agriculture. That is a matter I am not very well
acquainted with, but as an industrial worker
I am very much interested in the prosperity
of the agricultural worker. When good production is yielded from the land it reduces the
cost of living and gives a plentiful supply for
the industrial workers of the city. Reference
is also made to unemployment and the industrial stagnation that prevails at the present time. Unfortunately there does not
appear to be any immediate or easy remedy
for that problem, although we would all be
very glad if something could be done to
relieve it. The particular point I would like
to draw attention to, however, is the proposal
to grant Compassionate Pensions. Up to now
we have been marching side by side with
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
legislation passed in the Imperial Parliament.
During their tenure of office the Labour
Government made an alteration in the law
regarding Old Age Pensions. Unfortunately
at the present time our Old Age Pensioners
are not enjoying exactly the same advantages
and priviliges as do Old Age Pensioners
across the water.
A tradesman who has been thrifty and has
paid into a Trade Union for thirty-five or
forty years receives from his Society a superannuation allowance of 10s. or 14s. a week.
Previously, owing to the thrift he had thus
practised he was limited in the amount he
received as an Old Age Pension. That disability has been removed in England, and I
would be glad to know if there is any intention on the part of our Government to
remove that disability in Northern Ireland.
Considerable dissatisfaction exists with regard to that matter at the present time. It
was very evident during the elections, and
many of the older electors complained very
bitterly that it was a terrible strain to live
on the small amount they had at their
disposal. Hitherto the Government has been
very anxious to keep pace with legislation
passed in the Imperial Parliament, and I
should be very pleased to know if they intend
to follow in the footsteps of the Imperial
Government with regard to Old Age Pensions.
If they do it will remove a considerable
hardship and I believe it would satisfy the
electors to a great extent. It was a subject
of frequent complaint during the recent
elections.
The Leader of the House warned us that
in future our duties may be a little bit more
arduous than they have been in the past,
but if I happen to be here after next June
I shall not complain, and I do not think anyone will complain if our duties are more
arduous in the future than they hitherto
have been. They have been rather light up
to the present and if they become heavier we
will develop more interest in our work. Therefore, I hope the noble Marquess's forecast may
prove to be correct. So far as the Education
Act is concerned I would like to say that
I think the Minister of Education should be
very well satisfied with the reception that
Act has received. The relatively small complaints that were made about it were easily
remedied. One thing is sure, and that is
that if the Act had been accepted without any
complaint it would not have been appreciated
to the same extent. After the controversy
that has taken place both on the platform
and in the Press the people of Northern
Ireland know that they have got a really good
Education Act with which they can find no
fault, because any fault that could be found
with it by even the most critical has been
remedied and everybody is satisfied. Therefore, I think the Minister of Education is
highly fortunate in having had placed on the
Statute Book an Education Act which has
been so favourably received by the people of
Northern Ireland.
Mr. BARBOUR: I am very gratified to see
so prominent in the Gracious Speeech the
question of Agriculture, and I was delighted
to hear the seconder of the Address-who
I think we may take it is pretty well cognisant of the aims and objects of our Minister
of Agriculture-say that one of the aims
of the Minister is to have our Province at
least equal to Denmark and Holland. Might
I suggest that following the example of these
two and other countries of small holdings
would be one of the ways we might attain
the prosperity which the farmers of those
countries enjoy. We have in Northern Ireland, thanks to some thirty years' pretty hard
work, an organization similar to that which
has done so much for Denmark and Holland.
I mean the Agricultural Co-operative move
ment. It is true that we were handicapped
in trying to spread this movement by not
having a population as highly educated in
the matter as was the Dane and the Dutchman, but I am glad to think that one of the
results of the passing of the Education Act
will be a higher standard of comprehension
amongst the farmers, and that will make our
work easier and will bring great advantages.
In fact it may offer the means of salvation
to our agriculture in Northern Ireland, but
I would plead with the Government not to
destroy the work that has been accomplished
already. I think it would be a perfect
tragedy if, after having followed these enlightened countries as we have, by overlooking these vital interests the work of thirty
years was wasted. It would be a tragedy
the effect of which no one can realise.
There is another aspect of this question
which is important. Northern Ireland,
England, Scotland, and other countries are
trying to remedy this awful problem of unemployment. If by any sin of omission we
failed to help our farmers at this time we
tend directly to increase that unemployment
and so further drain the funds we have at
our disposal to provide for those who cannot
find anything to do. On all these considerations I hope the Government will give very
serious attention to the claims of this
movement, which has been built up after so
many years of hard and efficient work, work
which in the main part is regarded by all
the English-speaking parts of the world as
absolutely essential to the welfare of agricultural work amongst small holders.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Address be presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors of
Northern Ireland.

Charitable Loan Societies Bill-

THE SENATE.

Speaker) in the Chair.

of Enniskillen, and the townlands of Breandrum, Coleshill, Cornagrade, Cross, Carron,
Drumclay, Derrychara, Drumcco, Derrygore,
Drumlyon, Knockalough, Kilmacormick, Killynure, Portora, Rossorry, and Windmillhill, in
the Rural District of Enniskillen, all in the
