(Marquess of Londonderry.)
as the Committee will remember, the percentage of illegitimates is four per cent. It
also leaves out the children of the area whose
fathers or widowed mothers attempt to escape
the return. Therefore, it seems to me that
we cannot hope to get an absolutely inclusive
return under Paragraph 14, and the point
remains can we do any better. If it were
agreed to leave out the Paragraph now something better might be inserted at a later
stage. As the paragraph stands I understand it includes all children under 16 years
of age who are at home. The probabilities
are that owing to the census day being the
18th of April the great majority of the
children will be at home at that time, but
there may be children in the Free State and
so on. I imagine the object is to obtain
statistical information which in abstract
form may be produced for the purpose of
certain social measures which are understood to be under contemplation at the
moment. I think that is the purpose of the
paragraph. It is not for the purpose of ascertaining the fertility of marriage by asking
the number of children born alive. For the
moment I beg to move that we leave out
this paragraph.
VISCOUNT MASSERREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the hon. Member, the
object of this paragraph is to provide information which is essential for the purpose of
calculating the cost of social insurance
schemes and also for statistical purposes.
Although it is perfectly true that a good
deal of this information can be deduced from
the answers to other questions on this census
form, it is felt that we should get the information for the purpose of ascertaining the
cost of social services more correctly under
the wording of this paragraph. The hon.
Member raised a point about illegitimate
children. I doubt whether that is a proper
question to ask and I doubt if we should
get a correct answer if we did ask it. This
again is a new paragraph which was not in
former census Bills, but it corresponds to a
question asked in Great Britain. If we find
by experience that the information obtained
under this paragraph is not worth the
trouble then we shall drop it. For the
moment we desire to retain it.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the information with
regard to legitimacy would be sufficiently
obtained from some of the other inquiries in
the form. I have no wish to press the matter
at all, and, therefore, I ask leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Second Schedule agreed to.
Preamble agreed to.
Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported with
Amendments to the House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND AVA in
the Chair.
CLAUSE 1 (Validation of Supply of Seed
Potatoes by Guardians and Repayment of
Price by Purchasers).
Mr. BARBOUR: I want to ask the Leader
of the House if he can tell us if there is no
other way that can be devised for ensuring
the repayment of any amount of relief
granted under this Bill other than by special
assessment in each individual case. The
making out of these assessment notes is a
very complicated job, and there are a good
many special assessments now. I think that
from the point of view of the people who have
to pay the rates it would be desirable if we
could in any way obviate the necessity of
adding a further special rate for them to
have to take a note of.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am to a large extent in sympathy with the
hon. Member. The question has not been
raised before, but between now and the Third
Reading I will make inquiries as to whether
there is some more convenient machinery by
which these payments can be made, and if
there is such machinery it can be provided
for on the Third Reading.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Power of Guardians to Borrow,
and Power of Ministry of Finance to make
Loans), 3 (Saving as to Disqualification), 4
(Validation of Orders and Proceedings of
Ministry of Home Affairs), and 5 (Short
Title), ordered to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble agreed to.
Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without
¡Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.

to carry down the said Bill and desire their

Old-Age Pensions Bill.
Second Reading.
27 OCTOBER 1925
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 27th October, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Business of the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
'RARD: I beg to move: That Standing
Order No. XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting, in respect of the Census Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the noble
Viscount has some good reason for the suggestion he has made in moving this Motion,
but I think it is only fair to point out that
the Bill in question is one of some importance and one which breaks new ground in
several respects. If the House assents to the
Motion it means the sacrifice of the right of
re-committing the Bill, and it also rather
handicaps any hon. Senator who might wish
to speak on the Third Reading or to contribute to the debate initiated by any hon.
Member, inasmuch as the Debates of Thursday last are not in the hands of Members
of this House. If there is a real definite
necessity to put the Bill through its final
stages to-day I should be the last to object,
but I think it is asking a little too much of
the House to assent to that course without
any explanation.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I fully appreciate what the hon.
Senator has stated to the House, but the
point is that the Bill is required very
urgently in another place and we must get it
through its stages in this House as early as
possible.
On Question, Motion agreed to
Ordered: That the Standing Order be dispensed with in respect of the said Bill.
Old Age Pensions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Leader of the House): I do not anticipate
that I shall meet with any opposition in this
House in moving the Second Reading of the
Old Age Pensions Bill. Hon. Senators will
remember the debate which took place here
on the 21st of April last, when my hon
Friend, Senator Joseph Cunningham, raised
the question of the differences between the
conditions governing the qualifications for
Old Age Pensions in Northern Ireland and
in Great Britain. He spoke very earnestly,
as he always does, on behalf of the old and
the poor, and he urged the Government.
with that moderation and sanity of outlook
'which invariably secure him an attentive
hearing in this House, that we should take
steps as early as possible to amend our Old
'Age Pensions law and bring it into line with
the law in Great Britain, which had recently
been amended by the Old Age Pensions Act
of 1924.
In the course of his remarks the hon.
Member was scrupulously fair. He never
suggested that our hesitancy in following the
British law on Old Age Pensions was due to
lack of sympathy, and I am sure that was
never in his mind. I will venture to repeat
what I said in my reply to him on that
occasion. I said;
"I can assure the hon. Member that the
Government are not behind him in any
way in the desire to place this Province
on exactly the same level as in Great
Britain. However, until our financial relations with Great Britain are fully estab
lished to the complete understanding of all
parties concerned, I think the Minister of
Finance would be incurring a responsibility
he is not entitled to incur by running the
risk of imposing upon our finances a burden
which in the future we may not be able
to bear".
The hon. Member will remember my using
those words. I concluded by expressing the
hope that, when he again asked a question
on the same subject, I should be able to give
him a far more favourable answer than I
could give him then. I feel sure all these
matters are fully within the recollection of
the hon. Member.
The House will note with pleasure that
our performance has not lagged far behind
our promise, because the Bill before the
House this afternoon embodies proposals for
the removal of the so-called thrift disqualifications from our Old Age Pensions
scheme, and those who in their working days
have put by a little to eke out the assistance
offered from State resources will no longer
be discouraged by having the results of their
thrift brought into account against them in
reckoning the amount of their pension.
This Bill brings the law as to Old Age
Pensions into line with that in Great Britain.
The memorandum circulated with the Bill is,
I feel, a sufficient explanation of the effect
of the change proposed, but I should, perhaps,
inform hon. Members that the effect of the
proviso to sub-section (1) of Clause 1 will
be to prevent persons living on our borders,
but not within them, from expatriating
themselves and coming in simply and solely
for the purpose of taking advantage of our
provision for old age under the present Bill.
The Act comes into operation on the First
of April next, and hon. Members will find
Old-Age Pensions Bill.
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
in the memorandum that the estimated cost
of the change is approximately £143,000 per
annum, while the estimated addition to the
pensionable population is 5,500 persons. In
addition, we expect that the great number
of the 3,500 pensioners at present drawing
pension will receive an increase in their
pensions. I beg to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am very
glad that the Government has found itself
in a financial position to be able to remove
the Thrift Disqualification Clause in the
Old-Age Pensions Act. It is true that when
the matter was raised on a previous occasion
I was satisfied that it was only the financial
position of the Government that prevented
it keeping step with Great Britain in regard
to this matter. I am su
am sure there will be a
considerable number of old people who will
be very thankful that this action has been
taken by the Northern Government, and
none will appreciate it more than the old
trade union members who have subscribed
their contributions for forty years
Superannuation benefit and who now find
that they will be able to draw full benefit as
well as a State pension.
for
It has been unfortunate in the past that
in most cases of State aid the person who
was not so thrifty as others was the one to
benefit. The thrifty person has generally
been penalised. In many cases I have noticed
that the person who has been careless as to
whether he worked or not or as to whether
he contributed anything towards his old age
or bad times was the person who benefited
to the full extent by any State aid.
very glad that this Thrift Disqualification
Clause has now been removed, and that industrious working people will receive the
full benefit of the Old Age Pensions Act.
I am
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite sure the Bill
will be received with acclamation throughout
the Province, and I think that in congratulating the Government on its introduction
we ought not to forget that our congratulations are due to the hon. Member on my
left (Senator Joseph Cunninngham) who, as
the Leader of the House has just said, first
initiated the discussion on this question. As
regards the Bill itself there is little to say.
The first point that occurs to me is, where
is the money to come from? I think I am
right in saying-although I would like
to corrected if I am wrong that this
money, or practically the whole of it,
will be obtainable by a reduction
in the amount of our Imperial contribution, inasmuch as, if I understand
the matter correctly, we shall be allowed this
sum provided we do not exceed the same
amount per head of our population as is
I think
being expended in Great Britain.
that is right, but I should like to be reassured on the point.
There is another small matter I should
like to be assured upon. I want to ask the
Government whether they can do something
rather better for us than Column 1? I make
the request because this is a Bill which,
unlike many other Bills, the public is likely
to want to buy, to read, and to refer to, and
I cannot help thinking that the first thing
any member of the public who may happen
to become possessed of Column 1-"A person
entitled to an old-age pension whose resi.
dence qualification depends on paragraph (f)
in the proviso to sub-section (2) of section
three of the Old Age Pensions Act, 1911, as
adapted by Article 9 of the Irish Free State
(Consequential Adaptation of Enactments)
Order, 1923"-will do if he wants the infor
mation will be to turn up sub-section (2) of
section three of the Old Age Pensions Act,
1911, and look for paragraph 8. If he takes
that not unnatural course he will be somewhat bitterly disappointed because paragraph 8 is not there. Paragraph 8 is the
creation of the Adaptation Order, which is
referred to in the same column, and I suggest that if possible the Government should
bring up something at a later stage expressed
in language which is a little more understandable by the public. I have nothing
more to say about the Bill except to wish it
success in its operation.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I feel gratified at the manner in which hon.
Members have received the Bill, which I
have had the privilege of presenting to them
to-day. I am glad to see that the hon.
Member who has just spoken (Senator Leslie)
is fully aware of the formula which governs
the adjustment of finance between this
country and Great Britain, and is aware of
the manner in which the contribution is re
gulated as passing from this country to
Great Britain and being returned to us on
behalf of services in certain capita.
With reference to the further remarks
the hon. Senator made he has, I understand,
taken the trouble to have dived into the
Act referred to in the Schedule, and he has
discovered a discrepancy which I must admit
I have not discovered myself. Nothing will
give me greater satisfaction or pleasure than
to probe into the question as deeply, and
perhaps more deeply, than the hon. Member
has done, and on a further stage of this
Bill I shall be very happy to enlighten him.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for to-morrow.
Census Bill-
Report Stage.
27 OCTOBER 1925
CENSUS BILL (8).
Order for receiving Report of Amendments
read.
Moved: That this Report be now received.
--(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendments were accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.
SECOND SCHEDULE. (Particulars to be
stated in the Returns.)
*
8. Nationality.
#
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in the Second
Schedule, paragraph 8, before the word
"Nationality" to insert the words, "If born
outside the British Islands." The first thing
I have to do is to ask the permission of the
Senate to alter a word in the Amendment
which I propose to move. I see I have inadvertently used the expression "British
Isles" instead of the proper expression,
"British Islands." The paragraph in the
Second Schedule which I desire to amend was,
as the Senate will remember, rather sprung
upon us, if I may say so, because no notice
of it was given on the Paper, and I believe
the circumstances of its introduction were
such that no notice could have been given
on the Paper.
But the fact that notice was not given was
really unfortunate, because the House might
have considered this paragraph in conjunction with the preceding paragraph 7, which
deals with the place of birth. The points
we have to consider as regards paragraph 8
are, what the information is that we are
really desirous of obtaining, and whether we
are likely to get that information unless we
give some special instructions on the form
or otherwise. The great majority of the
people who will be required to fill up this
form will be people who were born within
the British Islands, and if so, they are of
course British subjects, and there is nothing
more to be said about it. The intention of
the inquiry, as I understand it, is to find out
what aliens there may be in Northern Ireland on the night of the Census, and perhaps
the country of their origin.
It will be remembered that the question of
being a British subject or not depends on
place of birth, and a person born within the
British Islands of alien parents is still a
British subject unless it happens to be one
of those rare cases where the parents have
taken out certificates of alienship. We do
not want to impose on our population-
1,250,000 of them, more or less-any unnecessary labour, either
the subject
making the return or on the officials
engaged in examining the returns, and if we
on
can relieve a great number of our people of
the necessity of specifying Nationality, I
think it would be a good thing to do.
What we really want to get at is the
number of aliens in Northern Ireland, and it
occurred to me that the words I have
suggested might possibly have that result. I
am quite prepared to admit there are other
ways of doing the same thing, but I think
the point is to limit the number of replies.
The House will remember that there are
already fifteen questions on which information
is desired, and I think that if those fifteen
questions are examined it will be found that
the number of questions really works out at
between thirty and forty, and nearer forty
than thirty. It would be desirable to
abstain from increasing that number if it
can be avoided. Accordingly I beg to move
the Amendment which stands in my name.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Government are quite in accord
with the object embodied in Senator Leslie's
Amendment. From his speech I gather that
his object is to prevent the necessity of those
people who were born in the British Islands
answering the question as to Nationality.
The Government have no objection to the
hon. Member's Amendment, except that
they consider the form of the Amendment
is not quite so good as the words in
the one I propose to submit to the House.
If the
to
hon. Member is prepared
accept the form of the Amendment I propose
to move I would ask him to withdraw his
Amendment and agree to the Amendment of
propose
the Government. The Amendment
and which I think will satisfy the hon. Senator, is in the Second Schedule, to leave out
paragraph and insert instead thereof the
following new paragraph:-
8. In respect of persons born in a foreign
country, nationality, and whether resident
in Northern Ireland or a visitor.
Mr. LESLIE: I am very much obliged to
the noble Viscount. I quite agree that the
Amendment the Government proposes fully
carries out the principle which I had in
mind, and I also agree that the words are
more descriptive and better than my words.
Therefore I should like to withdraw my
'Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move, in the Second Schedule, to leave out paragraph 8, and to insert
instead thereof the following new paragraph:-
:-
8. In respect of persons born in a foreign
country, nationality, and whether resident
in Northern Ireland or a visitor.
Amendment agreed to.

Seed Potatoes Supply Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Ahe MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In moving the Third Reading of the Seed
Potatoes Supply Bill there is one question
which the hon. Member sitting opposite me
(Senator Barbour) asked me to which I
should like to give him a reply.
The scheme adopted in the Bill may, no
doubt, cloaked as it is in Parliamentary verbiage, seem to the average man in the street
to be a cumbersome and complicated mode of
repayment, but in point of fact it is the
simplest and most effective system available
to us.
Before we carried out the arrangements
for the distribution of the seed, we consulted
the Fermanagh County Council and their
officials regarding the system of repayment,
and they cordially fell in with the scheme
and assured us that it would not involve any
appreciable additional trouble to them.
Apart from that the scheme now introduced is eminently convenient from the point
of view of the poorer classes of farmers who
cannot pay cash. This principle is a principle that is frequently resorted to in local
government administration, and is not,
therefore, anything new.
Other methods have been considered and
tried, and have been proved to be unworkable.
I hope the explanation I have ventured to
give the hon. Member may satisfy his
natural desire to facilitate the course of these
operations, because I believe that in the
methods we have adopted we have found
the least difficult way of carrying out this
transaction.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to convey
my thanks to the noble Marquess for the
very considerate way my question has been
answered. I must confess that in reading
the Bill I did not at first realise that it was
confined to the County of Fermanagh. As
the acquiesence of the people there concerned
has been gained by the Government, I have
nothing further to say except that I am ob
liged for the information.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate agree to
the said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move: That this House do now
adjourn. The business to-morrow will be
formal.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
a Quarter before Four
o'clock.

Committee.

That they have passed the Widows',

Bill, and the Unemployment Insurance Bill,

Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Bill.

time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)

Old Age Pensions Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly,
The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair.
CLAUSES 1 (Amendment of statutory condition as to means, 8 Edw. 7, c. 40; 9 & 10
Geo. 5, c. 102), (Amendment of law as to
penalty for false statements, etc.), 3 (Short
title) ordered to stand part of the Bill.
SCHEDULE.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In the course of the debate on the
Second Reading of this Bill here yesterday
my hon. Friend, Senator Leslie, who, I regret,
is not in his place to-day, asked us whether
it would not be advisable to amend the
schedule of the Bill, in order to remove from
it a taint of obscurity, which the hon.
Gentleman finds attaching to it, owing to
the difficulty which may be experienced in
following a somewhat tortuous course of legal
reference.
I may say at once, that, after giving to
the suggestion that consideration which we
always give to anything from my hon. Friend,
we do not think it necessary to bring forward
an amendment, in view of the fact that we
propose, as soon as the Bill becomes law,
to issue leaflets explaining the effect of the
Bill to all persons now in receipt of pensions
at less than the maximum rate, who might,
therefore, by virtue of the provisions of the
Bill, be entitled to have their pensions increased. Such persons will receive with these
explanatory leaflets forms on which application for increase of pension may be made.
There will also be available at the various
Customs and Excise Offices throughout Northern Ireland a supply of similar leaflets for
issue to inquirers generally, so that any
person not now in receipt of a pension may,
by obtaining one of these leaflets, make himself fully acquainted with the provisions of
the Bill.
I think the hon. Member will agree that
these measures will be sufficient to render
any amplification of the text of the Bill
itself unnecessary, especially in view of the
fact, which I think is indisputable, that very
few of the old people over seventy are themselves ever likely to read the Statute. The
position will be made perfectly plain to them
in the explanatory leaflets, which are the
only documents in which they are at all
likely to interest themselves. I think it is
necessary to make this explanation to the
House in view of the question raised by
Senator Leslie.
Schedule agreed to.
Title and Preamble agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without
Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved: That this Report he now received.
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
Col. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I would
like to know from the noble Viscount when
the Drainage Bill is likely to reach this
House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman, I am afraid I am not
The Drainage Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
in a position to say when the Drainage Bill,
in which he takes such a great interest, will
reach this House. It all depends on the
progress it makes in another place, but I
hope we shall be able to have the First
Reading to-morrow. I am afraid 1 cannot
give him a more definite answer than that.
Col. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: In case the
Bill reaches the House to-morrow I hope it
will not be rushed through in one day.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: Without binding myself in any way,
I do not think the Government is likely to
rush a Bill of that character through the
House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
at
Adjourned accordingly
Twenty-five Minutes after
