Part III
rates
I do not know whether the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman (Colonel Crawford) intends to persist to the bitter end with this
"six months from now" Amendment, but I
do hope that whatever may be done, either
the matter should be postponed or the Bill
withdrawn or this debate adjourned. I
understand that it is possible that this
House may be meeting again next month
and perhaps an opportunity will then be
found. But if this Bill were left alone
altogether until the opening of the Session
next year I cannot see that any great harm
will be done. No rate has been struck. It
can scarcely be struck by the County Councils
until April. The fact that the Bill is now in
print, having passed the other House, is of
great assistance to the public, because
hitherto discussions necessarily have been
based on reports in the newspapers. The
Measure is now in complete form and is
available for discussion not only in this
House but in the far wider circle outside,
which consists of those people who
affected by the Measure.
are
Mr. PORTER PORTER: My apology for
addressing the House now and trespassing
on their time is that I have been a member
of the Lough Erne Drainage Board for
twenty years, and perhaps I belong to as
wet a county and one that will be more
affected by this Bill than any other. I welcome this Bill, and I am very glad indeed
that the Government are bringing it in.
The drainage question has been a canker
in Co. Fermanagh for fifteen years. I am
not interested in the Bann. I know nothing
about it. Drainage has been a canker which
is growing yet. It is quite time, I think,
that the Government should do something
for those people who are complaining. 1
heard my right hon, and gallant Friend
(Colonel Grawford) speaking about the duty
being put on the County Councils. My idea
always was that the County Councils should
be able to shoulder any duty that was for
the good of their county. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) That has been their business,
at least it always was when I was in the
County Council. I thought it was our duty
to see the county helped in any way, either
in agriculture or any other form.
I quite agree with the hon. Senator (Mr.
Leslie) when he suggested, I think, that more
County Councillors should be on the Advisory Committee. I agree with him in that,
and it is contrary to the right hon. and
gallant Gentleman (Colonel Crawford). He
wants the County Council to have nothing to
do with drainage. But if you knew as I have
known the dissatisfaction in my own county
at nothing being done by the Government
you would welcome this Bill with acclamation. We spent a sum of over £200,000 in the
Lough Erne drainage. We did all that was
laid down to do, and we were relieved of
floods that came constantly, and instead of
water being over the land six months the
water has gone off in three weeks. At the
same time give an Irishman an inch and he
asks for an ell. These men who live on the
border of the lake would like some more.
They used to be flooded, and they want to
go further into the lake and they think they
will have land there. I do not think they
will. This is a matter for experts. I do
not think I can bring this before you to
day. I rather think it is a very difficult
question, and always will be. Touching water
has always been a difficult question, but I
am very glad the Government are going to
take up this Bill and do something. I welcome it with my whole heart.
Mr. LONG: I should like to express our
indebtedness to the noble Viscount for the
lucid explanation he has given of this important Bill. The legislation of this House
has been sometimes characterised as overdue and belated, but there is a disposition
in some quarters to regard the present Bill
as somewhat premature. With those people
I do not agree. In County Londonderry the
principal source of all our worries in
this matter of flooding is the River Bann
and its tributaries, and if the tributaries
are first attended to, deepened and widened,
it is inevitable that the principal river will
be more quickly flooded, and more risk
will ensue to those liable to be
flooded by the main river. Therefore,
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading.
3 NOVEMBER 1925
if the Government can give this House
any assurance that what I regard as
the main question-and in this matter I am
in agreement with the mover and seconder
of the postponement that it would perhaps
have been more desirable that the main question should have been dealt with first-but
if the Government can give the House any
assurance that that will be dealt with not
in a heroic Measure, spending almost
£10,000,000 and paralysing this country, but
with reasonable expenditure I shall be satisfied. I certainly agree with the hon. Gentleman (Mr. Porter Porter) that this is a Bill
which should be welcomed. I need not go into
the harrowing details of the sufferings of these
people whose land is flooded, neither need I
explain to the House the productive character of a great deal of this land which is
subject to flooding.
us
I cannot quite understand the purport of
the figures which Senator Leslie gave
regarding the loss in growing wheat. If it
was to insinuate that this land was not
worth cultivating, I beg to differ from him
so far as County Londonderry is concerned,
because the most productive land in the
whole County of Londonderry is that which is
subject to flooding by a number of our rivers
and tributaries. The people of Londonderry
know that the Government are in earnest
in bringing forward this Bill. They know
that when the Government introduce a useful and benevolent Measure like this they
will pass it. I think the Government are
right in doing so. I have already been
approached in Londonderry regarding the
putting into operation of this Bill, and
these people are already so far forward with
their plans that they have counted on the
area of benefit being the area of charge,
and every beneficiary under the scheme is
willing to pay the fixed proportion agreed
on, covering the entire cost, so that the
cost to the ratepayers of carrying out the
scheme would be nil.
With reference to the observations of
Senator Colonel Sharman Crawford and
Senator Leslie as to the increased work the
Bill will involve on the County Councils,
I quite agree with them, but I am also
in entire agreement with Senator Porter
Porter when he says that our County Councillors are men of public spirit, and are
willing to shoulder any burden involving
work that they believe will be for the benefit
of the county. In the County Council of
Londonderry we are fortunate in having a
Nationalist minority which vies with the
Unionist majority in the promotion of any
scheme for the welfare of the community.
I am very glad that is so, and I am very
pleased as the Chairman of that Council
to bear testimony to that fact. I hope the
larger scheme will not be lost sight of. We
believe this Measure will be for the good
of the country, and we have every confidence that much good can be effected by it.
I have, therefore, very great pleasure in
supporting the Bill.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Anyone who has
listened to this debate cannot fail to be
struck by the enormous difficulty, complexity, and expense which surround this
question. I think the Government deserve a
great deal of credit for having tackled this
problem as they have done in accordance
with the paragraph contained in the Speech
from the Throne. There is a great difference of opinion, which is, perhaps, natural
on a question so old and difficult as the
question of drainage. Speaking of the particular district from which I come, that
difference of opinion does exist. There is
a disposition to ask for a postponement of
the Bill, but at the same time this is a
permissive Measure, and I do not see how
anyone can be aggrieved by it. The minor
part of the scheme will be dealt with first,
although, of course, the cleaning of these
drains will add to the height of the Lough
for the time being, while, on the other
hand, it will facilitate the clearing of the
land when the river falls.
At present the land is left wet, because
the drains are all choked, and a great deal
of the flooding, particularly about Portadown, is due to the drains not having been
cleaned out. The major question, of course,
is a most difficult and expensive one, and I
do not know how far the County Councils
will be able to deal with that without
assistance of some kind. When one thinks
of all the interests that have to be compensated, and various things on the Lower Bann,
one is almost discouraged, but I think that
on the whole the Bill as it stands is one
which the country generally will heartily
welcome.
Mr. DICKSON: Any person who came up
from the country to-day and saw the crops
of potatoes under water would begin to
think that it is time something was done
to relieve that flooding. Even potatoes out
of dry land are not worth very much, but
when they come out of water-logged land
they are worth nothing at all. I am inclined
to support the Bill simply because, as the
previous speaker has said, it is a permissive
Measure. It is
the
not compulsory on
County Council to adopt any particular
scheme. Senator Colonel Sharman Crawford,
the Chairman of my County Council, who
has moved the rejection of the Bill-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Not the
rejection, the postponement,
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Mr. DICKSON: The postponement of the
Bill does it on the ground of the extra work
it will involve on the officials of the County
Council. I would point out that the Bill
gives us power to employ outside surveyors.
CRAWFORD: No,
Colonel SHARMAN
only assessors.
Mr. DICKSON:
I think that when the
Leader of the House replies he will tell you
that the County Council can employ an outside surveyor of valuation, and half the cost
will be repaid by the Government.
Colonel
SHARMAN CRAWFORD: For
valuation only.
Mr. DICKSON: The County Council representatives who attended the inquiry at the
Home Office were in favour of the rating
authority being the authority to have charge
of the drainage. Of course the major scheme
will not come into existence until the
engineer submits his report, but there are
many minor schemes where the flooding would
not go into the Bann or Lough Erne but
direct into the sea, and would thus injure
nobody. I think the County Councils can be
trusted not to drain one area only to flood
another area further down. If the Bill is
passed now it will take time for the engineers,
no matter where they come from or by whom
they are appointed, to make the necessary
preparations, because drainage can only be
carried out in the summer time, and it would
provide work that would greatly relieve
unemployment. I am convinced that the cost
would be levied on the areas to benefit, and
would not be a county-at-large rate. I have
pleasure in supporting the Measure.
Mr. DORMAN: This is a very important
question, and in my estimation a very important Bill. There is an old proverb which
says procrastination is the thief of time. I
have heard this particular subject discussed
for well over fifty years, and I have travelled
all over the country, although I cannot in
any way attempt to express an expert opinion
upon such a question as drainage. I have
been convinced from things I have seen in
travelling through the country that hitherto,
notwithstanding the reports of the Royal
Commissions which were set up in the past
for the consideration of this question, practically nothing of a constructive character
has been done to remove this grievance from
which the occupiers of flooded lands suffer.
Their crops are periodically destroyed, because the question of drainage has not in the
past received the attention we would have
expected Governments to have given it.
I welcome the Bill, because it is an example
of collectively shouldering the responsibility
for the improvement of the individual. The
noble Leader of the House the other day referred to the fact that he might possibly in
the future hear Socialistic speeches in the
Chamber, but 1 would respectfully remind
him that this Drainage Bill is to a certain
extent a Socialist Measure. It is giving
to the people of the county collectively the
choice of shouldering the responsibility of
improving the condition of the individual and
developing and extending the productivity
of the soil of their own particular county.
Mr. DICKSON: And paying for it.
Mr. DORMAN: Quite so, but it is collectively that they are paying for it, and it is
the individual who is going to benefit, and
inasmuch as we are shouldering the responsibility for the benefit of the individual we
are shouldering the responsibility for the
increased productivity of the soil of the
country. I welcome this Measure because in
travelling through the country I have seen
the consequences of not taking any steps
hitherto to deal with this problem.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: 1 must
congratulate the previous speaker on the
great stretch of imagination he has displayed
when he accuses the Government of bringing
in a Socialistic Measure. I did not intend to speak on this matter because
it is foreign to me. One who is born and
reared in the town knows very little about
flooding or drainage in the country, but there
is one thing that strikes me very forcibly
and that is that if this work is carried out
at the present time it will do a great deal
to relieve unemployment. There must be a
good many unemployed throughout the
country who are willing and anxious to work,
and if a big scheme like this was carried out
now it would not only greatly benefit the
land concerned but those who are seeking
Senator
employment and cannot find it.
Dorman may say that this is a collective
scheme, intended for the benefit of the individual farmer. But it will increase the
value and production of the land, and the
people in the industrial centres are bound
to benefit as a result of that. I cordially
support the Measure.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1
do not feel really that at this stage of the
passage of this Bill there is any great necessity for me to go into the various details.
The points that have been raised are nearly
all Committee points and will be more efficiently and adequately discussed when that
stage is reached, as I think it will be. The
right hon. Gentleman (Colonel Crawford) has
disclaimed all desire to kill the Bill, but his
process is not so gentle if it were carried into
effect as to leave it with very much animation. I think the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie)
in seconding him is rather in the same position. I regret the attitude they have taken
up, because I do feel that after these many
years-the hon. Member opposite (Mr.
Dorman) spoke of 50 years-since we and
our predecessors-
Drainage Bill-
Second Reading.
3 NOVEMBER 1925
Colonel CRAWFORD: We remember, too,
but it was long before our time.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: It
was long before 50 years ago-we feel
that no time should be lost until something
is done to relieve the depredations of the
floodings which occur throughout the country.
The right hon. Gentleman has moved the
postponement of the Bill, the Parliamentary
phraseology which means the rejection of the
Bill, but I think the House will agree with
me that the right hon. Gentleman's bark
is worse than his bite. I remember well
being very much alarmed, I am willing to
confess, at the bark of the right hon. Gentleman in connection with the Education Bill
when he told us that the County Councils
were so overloaded that it was quite impossible for them to undertake any further
work. But great was my surprise when I did
go so far as to put the work on other bodies
throughout the country, the right hon.
Gentleman himself came forward and said
that was a scheme that could not be countenanced and that the County Council must
undertake the work entirely, to the exclusion
of the scheme I had brought forward. I have
no doubt that long before six months the
right hon. Gentleman will be a strong supporter of this Bill in determining that no
authority will come between the County
Council and the carrying out of this work.
I can go forward very optimistically in that
connection.
The reception of the Bill, on the other
hand, is certainly very gratifying, and I do
feel that the Government have been right in
taking up this Measure. The Prime Minister,
in another place, told the Members very
clearly that there were two ideas before him.
There was the minor Bill, and there was the
major Bill, and it was quite open to Parliament to say nothing should be done until the
greater drainage scheme could be brought
into effect. I think he put it very clearly
when he said it was very important-and in
his idea he had been backed by the majority
of the County Councils-that this smaller
scheme should be put into operation, so that
all those preparations should be made which
could be carried on pari passu with the other
Bill when it came into force. The greater
scheme is not ripe at the present moment for
bringing before Parliament, but I am sure
that everyone of us hopes that the scheme
can be brought before Parliament at no distant date, so that there will be a real endeavour on the part of the Province to cope
with the depredations of flooding, and deal
with the drainage proposal from an efficient
point of view.
But I feel that the County Councils are the
bodies which should deal with this matter.
After all, if we followed the principle laid
down by the seconder of the Amendment, it
means that our forefathers long ago would
have laid down the limits to which their
activities should be devoted and that nothing
in the nature of progress should be countenanced at all. If the County Councils think
they have come to the end of their activities,
I should certainly like to be one of those who
would do their best to relieve them. But as
years go by, I feel sure that additional duties
will be heaped upon the shoulders of County
Councils. This is why everyone is prepared
to pay a tribute of honour and praise to
those who undertake the work of the County
Council. Surely we can point with satisfaction and pleasure to the work which has
been done by County Councils in Northern
Ireland. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
we
can
The hon. Member has mentioned various
matters. He spoke of the Advisory Body, and
he was not very satisfied with the representation on that Advisory Body. That is a point
for the Committee, and I am sure
adequately discuss it there. There are representatives from all the County Councils
and there are representatives of the Government. The financial provisions of the Bill
allow the Government to assist the County
Councils in raising the money which will be
required in the major scheme. But in the
minor scheme, I think we are all aware that
a great deal of work can be done at very
little cost. At the present moment no one
has really undertaken
in
this work
decided manner, and the duties of landowners and occupiers, too, have not been
carried out as they should be.
a
The time these watercourses can be cleared
begins somewhere about the end of June,
and we get the months of June, July,
August and September. I feel sure that with
the additional stimulus to the County
Council we should find that a great deal of
efficient work can be done throughout
the country by the clearance of these
smaller watercourses. The point I should
like to emphasise is that this Bill is merely
an enabling Bill. The County Councils can
make use of it, or they need not. It rests
with the County Councils, and we shall be
paving the way for the greater scheme which
is in the minds of all of us to be introduced
at no distant date. It is not ripe
at the present moment-not from the point
of view of the investigations which can
be made, but from the point of view
of the steps that can be taken to carry out
what we all have in mind. As I have said
the points to which the hon. Senator Leslie
has referred are matters for the Committee
Stage. I have no doubt that with his well
known vigilance and ability we shall have
very constructive Amendments to the Bill.
There is one other matter to which I wish
to refer in connection with what was said
363 Drainage Bill.
Unemployment Insurance Bill. 364
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
by Mr. Senator Hill Dickson. It is provided in the Bill that only in connection
with valuation a surveyor can be appointed-
Colonel CRAWFORD: A surveyor of
valuation.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The right hon. Gentleman (Colonel Crawford)
was quite correct in his contention.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I would
just like to make one or two points. I think
the House has not understood me. I was
out at the time Mr. Porter Porter was making his speech, and I only came in at the end
of it. I heard him saying I had made the
statement that the County Councils wanted
to have nothing to do with this Bill. If you
all know Mr. Porter Porter as I do you will
know he exaggerates a little. I never said
anything of the kind.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I beg the hon.
Gentleman's pardon. I do not think I said
anything of the kind.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: You
said I did not want the County Councils to
have anything to do with it. If the hon.
Gentleman says he did not say that I accept his statement.
Mr. PORTER PORTER: I apologise if I
said anything of the kind.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Quite
the contrary. There is no one more anxious
than the County Councils to undertake it.
The only point I object to is that if there
was extra work and an extra staff needed
we should get some assistance. There will
be extra work. Nobody will deny that, and
I think there is some claim for assistance to
relieve them from paying the whole expense. The county must be put to some expense, because if you look at where it is
mentioned in the Bill you will see that the
preliminary expense has to be borne by the
county fund. The Chancellor of the Exchequer was in charge of the Bill in another
place, and the people think he has a golden
pot which can pay all the money. He says
the County Council has a golden pot, but I
can assure you the pot is very often empty.
We will have to go to the bank to borrow
the money or get a loan on which we will
have to pay interest to carry out this scheme.
I
I am very glad I put my suggestion.
think it has raised the very point I wished
raised, that is, to draw attention not only
of the Members of this House but outside,
to the great importance, not of the Bill we
are considering, but of the Bill that is going
to be brought in, because my whole argument
was that that was the important work. I am
sure if my hon, and gallant Friend opposite
(Colonel Sinclair) would allow me to say
so, it is like what he would suggest. Knowing that a very serious operation was needed
he would recommend a minor operation to
clear the way for that work. In that spirit
I am rather disposed to accept it. I will
leave it at that. We all know the story of
the curate's egg. We know what answer
he made to the bishop when he said it was
not a good egg. He said that parts of it
were good. There are parts of this Bill good,
and I hope by the time it emerges from the
Committee it will be a good fresh egg.
No one wishes more success to the farmers
in their drainage than I do if they carry it
out in the proper way. I think I have
achieved what I wanted in drawing attention
to the importance of the matter. The hon.
Member on my left (Mr. Dorman) referred
to inquiries 50 or 60 years ago. It was
not the subject of this Bill; it was the
major operation that was referred to.
I hope the County Councils with the assistance of the Government will succeed. Therefore, I ask leave to withdraw my Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
On Question, "That the Bill be read a
Second time."
Mr. SPEAKER: I regret I did not hear.
Did the right hon. Gentleman challenge the
Question?
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I did
not challenge the second time.
Mr. SPEAKER: I have not put it the
Second time.
On Question, Motion was then agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly, and
committed to a Committee of the Whole
House, the House to be in Committee on
Thursday.
Unemployment Insurance Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that this House
agree to the said Bill without any Amendment.
PORTADOWN ELECTRICITY
SPECIAL ORDER.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that the Special Order
made by the Electricity Commissioners for
365 Business of the House. 3 NOVEMBER 1925

Adjournment.
Northern Ireland under the Electricity
Supply Acts, 1882 to 1919, in respect of the
Urban District of Portadown, in the County
of Armagh, which was presented to the
House on the 29th day of October, be
approved.
On Question, motion agreed to.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
I should like to put the House in possession
of the business to come before them. There
is the Committee Stage on the Drainage Bill,
which is a Measure of great importance. I
should have liked to put the Committee Stage
down for to-morrow, Wednesday, but I feel
somehow, unless the House agrees to it, the
time is really too short in which to put
Amendments down. Therefore, with the approval of the House I would suggest that we
take the Committee Stage on Thursday.
Mr. LESLIE: This Drainage Bill has to
be considered and thought out well. I would
say it is impossible to take the Committee
Stage on Wednesday, and as it is really very
important I think it might be postponed
for a fortnight.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon. Gentleman seems to desire that his
Amendment, which was withdrawn, should be
carried out. The Bill cannot be postponed
for a fortnight. The Bill has been in the
hands of everybody since last June. I think
we can begin the Committee Stage on Thursday. The House may have to sit on Saturday
to carry out the business. I think it will
be in accordance with the wishes of the majority of the House that the Committee Stage
should be taken on Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five Minutes before Six of the Clock.
Finance (Estate Duty) Act. 368
Widows' Pensions Bill.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE

Wednesday, 4th November, 1925.
The House met at Quarter after Three of
the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Home Affairs.
(1) Abstract of the accounts of all Fines
and other Penal Sums accounted for under
the Act 14 and 15 Vic., cap. 90, for the year
ended 31st December, 1923.
(2) Statement of Accounts under the Dogs
Regulations (Ireland) Acts, 1865 and 1919, for
the year ended 31st December 1923.
Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I beg to move that
the Widows', Orphans' and Old-Age Contributory Pensions Bill be read the Third time.
This Bill has been before the House in
all its stages and I am sure hon. Members
will believe that it is a Bill brought in for
the purpose of assisting those who come
within its terms.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before putting the Question, I wish to ask hon. Members to remember that if they wish to catch my eye
they must stand straight up in their places.
I regret to say that yesterday I did not call
upon an hon. Member who wished to address
the House because I thought he was merely
uncomfortable. As a matter of fact he wished
to make a speech. The Question is " That
this Bill be now read the Third time."
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.- (The
Marquess of Londonderry).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
have agreed to the said Bill without any
amendment.
restricting the production, provision, sale or
distribution of any drugs to which the
Dangerous Drugs Act (Northern Ireland),
1920 to 1925, apply; and if not, whether the
Minister is prepared to make such regulations should such action appear to him
desirable; and whether contraventions of any
"corresponding law" referred to in Section
1 of the Imperial Dangerous Drugs and
Poisons Amendment Act, 1923, can be dealt
with in Northern Ireland.
Mr. LESLIE: I have to ask leave to postpone the first question standing in my name
till to-morrow.
Finance (Estate Duty) Act,
(Northern Ireland), 1925.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
the Finance (Estate Duty) Act (Northern
Ireland), 1925, has yet come into operation;
and, if so, whether agricultural property is
entitled to the same concession, in respect
of increased duties, as has been given in
Great Britain.
Mr. LESLIE: It will be within the recollection of the House that when the Finance
(Estate Duty) Act in the form of a Bill was
before us I drew attention amongst other
things to the particular hardship which the
new rates of Estate Duty imposed upon
owners of agricultural property. At that
time the Imperial Finance Act had not
reached its final stages, and in the interval
the agricultural owner in England found
friends who were both active and articulate.
A concession has been made in Great Britain
whereby the new duties, as I understand, do
not apply to agricultural land. I wish to
know whether the same concession will be
made in Northern Ireland and also I see I
have put it first-whether his Grace has yet
declared that the new Act is in operation.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The Finance (Estate Duty) Act, No. 1, 1925.
is in operation as from 30th June last by
virtue of an Order in Council of 27th
August. The answer to the second part of
the hon. Member's question is in the
negative.
Dangerous Drugs.
The following question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:-
To ask the Leader of the House whether
any regulations have been made by the
Minister of Home Affairs for controlling or
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move, That this House do now adjourn.
Mr. BARBOUR: I feel sure every Member
of this House recognises gratefully the kindly
consideration which the Leader of the House
has always given to our convenience and to
any method that will help us in our labours
369 Delivery of Order Papers.
4 NOVEMBER 1925
in this House. There is one thing to which
I would like to call his attention, and that
is in connection with the method at present in vogue of supplying us with the
Papers of the Day. Living in a rural
district as I do, I always find the Paper of
the Day awaiting me on my arrival home
after the work has been done. I think
other Members of the House suffer from the
same inconvenience, and consequently this
service, which is for our help, is rendered
really useless. It seems to me that by some
arrangement of our work, either by delaying
it just for a day or by agreeing to a longer
Session and fewer days, it might be possible
that full advantage might be taken of this
service of sending us the Bills and Papers to
our homes, thus giving us a chance to con
over the work before we come to the House.
If any change in the present state of affairs
can be brought about which would enable
this to be done I am sure it would be a
concession which every Member of the House
would welcome.
Mr. LESLIE: I want to take advantage of
the opportunity of the adjournment to ask
the noble Marquess, the Leader of the House,
a question of which I have given him private
notice. I wish to know whether his attention has been drawn to the proceedings yesterday at the Armagh County Council at a
special meeting and at the Antrim County
Council at their ordinary meeting. The
Armagh County Council passed a resolution
in opposition to the Drainage Bill, now in
Parliament, and the Antrim County Council
passed a somewhat similar resolution, thereby confirming the opinion expressed by their
Finance and Law Committee. I should like
to know whether the representations made
by those resolutions have reached the Government and whether, under the circumstances,
it is still proposed to take the next stage
of the Drainage Bill to-morrow and to press
it forward through its concluding stages
either before the adjournment or even during the present Session of this Parliament.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
With reference to the question which my
hon. Friend (Mr. Barbour) has put to me first
of all, I need hardly say I feel greatly in
sympathy with him. He labours under the
disadvantage, naturally, of living in a rural
district. He labours under the same disadvantage as many Members, not only in this
Parliament but in the Parliament across the
water, that they only find the Papers referring to the business for the day when they
return in the evening.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: That also
applies to Members in the city.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
My hon. Friend tells me that this applies
also to Members in the city. These are
Delivery of Order Papers. 370
matters which I think can and ought to be
rectified, and I can assure the hon. Member
that in regard to those Members who are
handy and close by I shall do my best with
respect to the printing to see if steps cannot
be taken which would enable them to receive
their papers in the morning by the first post.
The hon. Member, Senator Barbour, opens
perhaps a wider question. We in this House
labour under unfortunate circumstances in a
certain respect, because the business is done
in another place and it is pushed on to us,
sometimes with what we might call very
little ceremony, and we are expected to deal
with it in a hurry so that the arrangements
which are a convenience to the other House
may be all completed ostensibly to the satisfaction of all concerned.
I need hardly say I appreciate what the
hon: Member (Mr. Barbour) has said about
my personally endeavouring to consult the
convenience of all Members of the House.
That is one of my foremost duties, and I
should be very sorry indeed if I were
deficient in that respect. I can assure hon:
Members that I do my best to guard the
dignity of this House, and not to allow,
as far as I am able, Measures to be forced
upon us and pushed through with expedition, which must mean in the long run
that they do not receive the consideration
to which they are entitled, and the consideration which hon. Members here are
desirous they should receive. But there are
a great many difficulties, and I feel sure
hon. Members will sympathise with me as
Leader of the House, inasmuch as when
we come towards the end of the Session
Members are very much inclined to wind
up that Session as quickly as possible. This
House is then placed in a very invidious
and very unfortunate position by making
it necessary for the other House to sit
longer while we consider the Measures. I
am assailed from both sides-by the officers
of the other House, who are desirous of
getting the business through, and by my
own desire as representing hon. Members
in this House to see that the dignity of
this House is respected, and due consideration given to the Measures brought before
them.
With reference to the specific point which
my hon. Friend (Mr. Barbour) has raised,
I will certainly make what representations
I can with regard to the printing of the
Papers. To make it absolutely certain, of
course, we could consider some procedure
by which this House might not meet so often,
but that would have the effect of prolonging
business and making it difficult for those
who are responsible for the arrangements
in the other House to complete their
arrangements to the satisfaction of those
whom they represent in that place. Never-
The Drainage Bill. 372
The Drainage Bill.
THE SENATE.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
theless, I can assure the hon. Member that
all these matters will have my consideration.
I will discuss them with those who are
concerned, and see whether in the first place
we cannot arrange the business more to the
convenience of the Members here, and,
secondly, whether we cannot arrange that
the printing of the Papers shall be carried
out in such a manner that every Member
before he comes down to the House will
be equipped with the necessary documents,
and that the knowledge which should be
his regarding the affairs of this House shall
be placed in his possession as far as possible.
On that point I will say no more.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: May I
make one remark on this question? I quite
recognise the great difficulty the noble Marquess, the Leader of the House, is in, but
still I suppose he has a certain amount of
latitude allowed him in regard to Bills from
the Lower House. He does not take them
up simply because they are sent to him to be
passed. The noble Marquess is a Member of
another assembly on the other side of the
water where, I think, they rather resent
Bills coming from the House of Commons
in that way. However, I am sure there is a
great difficulty in that respect, but I think,
as an important Member of the Government,
he ought to represent the difficulty he is
placed in with the Senate and should ask the
Lower House to give this House more consideration than they have done in the past.
I am sure there is a general feeling amongst
the whole Senate that we do not get the consideration from the lower House we ought to
get. I know that the noble Marquess does
what he can, but there is a general feeling
that there is not sufficient time to discuss
in a proper manner an important Bill such
as the one we had yesterday.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
To resume, I am obliged to the hon. Member
(Colonel Crawford) for his interruption. I
must take the responsibility entirely on my
own shoulders, because if I made some representations we might be in a difficult position.
I can assure him it is continually in my
mind, because I hope I am as vigilant and
as anxious as he is for the dignity of this
House.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I appreciate that.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
With reference to the Question which my
hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie) has asked me in regard to the Drainage Bill, I certainly have
seen the resolutions which have been passed
by the two County Councils and which appeared in this morning's papers. I am not
sure that this is quite the place in which I
should enter into a discussion with those particular County Councils, The proposal is
that the Committee stage shall be taken tomorrow, and my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
is aware that on the Motion. "That this
House do go into Committee," he will have
the opportunity of putting his case forward.
I should only like to say that this is a Bill
which has been before the Province since last
June. There have been meetings between
the authorities concerned with the passing of
the Bill and the authorities in the counties.
and yesterday I had before me a report of
Hansard of another place in which the
Prime Minister said that the proposition had
been before the counties whether they desired this Bill to go forward or wait. They
unanimously came to the decision that this
Bill should go forward, and it appears to
me that on those representations and on
that understanding the Bill has gone forward.
The Bill, as I have said, has been before
the Province for five or nearly six months.
The provisions have been altered by means
of negotiations behind what I may term
the Speaker's Chair, and the Bill has
come forward in what I understand is
more or less an agreed form. I regret
very much that at this late hour it should
be brought forward in this House
that the County Councils seem to have
changed their minds with reference to the
Bill. The Committee Stage in this House
will be on the Paper for to-morrow, and if it
is thought advisable that discussion should
be taken as to whether this House should go
into Committee or not on the said Bill, it
seems to me that that is the opportunity
which the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) could
take. We could have a full discussion on
that point.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I am
sorry to intervene again, but I find it
will be impossible for me to be here tomorrow.
Mr. SPEAKER: May I remind the hon.
Member that he is only allowed to speak
with the leave of the House.
The House indicated assent.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I only
intervene because it is impossible for me to
be here to-morrow. It is the day of the
half-yearly meeting of the Down County
Council, and I must be there. I do not propose to move any Amendments. I just wish
to state one fact. There has been a misbeen
understanding that this Bill has
brought forward with the consent of the
County Councils as a whole. As a matter of
fact it has never been before the Down
County Council at all. There were certain
representatives from the County Councils at
one meeting. I was one of those representatives, and it was the only meeting at
which I was present. The only other meet-
The Drainage Bill.
Adjournment.
4 NOVEMBER 1925
ing held was in regard to the Baun Drainage and Lough Erne Drainage, which do
not much concern the Down County Council.
There was
no unanimous consent at the
meeting at which I was present. I objected
to it from the very beginning. There was
unanimous consent to parts but not to the
whole. As far as the Down County Council
is concerned it was never before them at all.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the right hon, and gallant
Senator (Colonel Crawford), I can only say
this, that this Bill is only an enabling
Bill. It does not force the County Councils.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: I quite
recognise that.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: My right hon. Friend, the Prime
Minister, distinctly stated in another place
that the Bill had received the unanimous
consent of the County Councils. The right
hon. and gallant Gentleman has challenged
that.
Mr. SPEAKER: I must stop this discussion
now, because it is not in order to discuss
on the Adjournment a Bill that is still in
progress in the House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Twenty
minutes before Four of the Clock.
Drainage Bill.
Drainage Bill.

Drainage Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: "That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill."
-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
on
on
Mr. LESLIE: The question as to
what date,
or approximately
what
date this House should resolve itself
into Committee on the Drainage Bill
has been before us on two occasions
during the current week. The Bill was
read a Second time after debate on Tuesday.
After that had happened I took it upon me
to suggest that a period of two weeks between the Second Reading and the Committee
Stage would not be too long to allow for consideration of the Measure. The utmost concession that the Government were prepared
to make at the moment was two days instead
of two weeks. Since then Resolutions passed
by two of our important County Councils
have appeared in the Press. Those Resolutions were alluded to yesterday, and I need
not take up the time of the House by repeating what is the purport of them, or in any
way re-discussing a matter that has already
been dealt with. But I would like to point
out that the fact of our taking the Committes
Stage of this Bill to-day in one respect
affects me personally somewhat, because I
have certain matters that I desire to bring
before the House for consideration and I
have been obliged to put them upon the
Paper without giving them as much thought
and as much preparation as I am accustomed
to bestow upon proposals which I bring before this House.
THE MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): You have had since
June.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Marquess says I
have had since June. It is quite true that
the Bill was read a First time in another
place in June, but it is also true that the
Bill was very seriously amended in another
place during the closing days, I think of
last week. When we discussed the Second
Reading of the Bill on Tuesday I do not think
the printed reports of the debates in the
House of Commons were in our hands, but,
in any case, I do not agree, and I do not
think the noble Marquess would suggest that
it is incumbent on Members of this House
to follow the progress of a Measure through
its course in another place. I think the
duties of this House do not usually begin in
connection with any Bill until it is presented to it. I would much prefer, even at
this late stage, if the Government could see
their way to defer the consideration of this
Measure.
So far as I can judge from the course of
the debates we did not get any very definite
expression of opinion on the part of any
County Council that the County Councils as
a whole desired this Measure. We did get
a reference to a speech delivered by the right
hon. Gentleman the Prime Minister, who, as
he always does, went to a good deal of trouble
in the matter. He saw on, I think, two occasions representatives of some, at all events,
of the County Councils, but I understand that
the County of Antrim was not represented at
the second of the meetings, and I also understand that that County Council as a whole
were by no means unanimous in support of
the Measure. Other speakers will, no doubt,
be able to give us more definite information,
but for myself I still wish that the Government had found it possible not to ask the
Senate to take this Bill to-day.
Mr. BARBOUR: I cannot feel that it
can be urged that there is any crying need
for what I must call rushing this Bill
through. I think this Bill has been brought
into being mainly on account of the fact that
the Government intends to deal with two
very large schemes-they have reserved the
power to deal with those two themselvesand that really has a great effect on the
activities that the County Councils will be
called upon to undertake. We have had an
expression of opinion from two County
Councillors that they are rather sceptical
about the Bill, and, although it is a fact that
the Bill has been in draft since June, the
first legal advice that I was able to get on
it is dated the 31st August. There have been
changes since then and, seeing that there is
no crying demand for the Bill, I think we
might well be allowed further time to consider it.
377 Drainage Bill.

5 NOVEMBER 1925
Drainage Bill.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I would join with
the two hon. Senators opposite in urging
upon the noble Marquess, the Leader of the
House, that we should not push this Bill forward too quickly, because it seems to me that
if we pass legislation without proper consideration, instead of doing good, we are only
going to do harm. The whole object of our
House, and of a Second Chamber, is to give
the country time to realise what sort of
laws it is intended to pass. I must say that,
as far as I am concerned, I knew nothing
at all about this Bill until a few days ago.
Of course hon. Members may say "You
ought to keep yourself better up in what is
going on," but I can assure hon. Members
that I have other things to do. We may see
reports in the papers but we have very little
time to read them. I would join with my
hon. Friend in asking the noble Marquess
if he could see his way to postpone this
matter in order that the untry may
realise what sort of Measure we propose to
pass.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I have listened to the speeches which have
been made by my hon. Friends and I regret
that I cannot see that any case has been
made out in any way for the postponement
of this Measure which is now before the
House. The hon. Senator (Sir George Clark)
who has just spoken has really made out
the case that all Bills should be brought in
and then postponed for a considerable time
until even the busy people have an opportunity of fully digesting everything that has
been done in connection with the Bill. But
my hon. Friend, who naturally is a very
busy man, is not aware of the immense
amount of negotiation that has been going
on in respect of this Bill by all those concerned, and I will trespass upon the indulgence of the House by quoting verbatim a
great deal of what has been done and a great
Ideal of what has been said.
Opposition to continuing this Measure
The
comes from the County of Antrim.
County Council of Antrim have been fairly
consistent I will not go further than fairly
consistent-in their opposition to this Bill,
but in their opposition they do not seem to
realise, or even to admit, that there is a
desire that the Bill should be introduced
from other portions of the Province. When
it is realised-and it must be realised-that
this is an enabling Bill it seems to me a
curious policy that they should say the Bill
should not be brought in at all or that it
ought to be postponed for some considerable
time, notwithstanding the desire of other
portions of the Six Counties that this Bill
should be introduced and carried through.
If the Bill imposed duties on the County
Councils and compelled them to carry those
duties out under fear of penalties there
would be something in their opposition. But
there is nothing of the kind. The County
Council of Antrim need take no steps at all,
but there are other portions of the Six
Counties who are desirous that this Bill
should be passed. On the last occasion on
which we met, we met with opposition from
County Councillors of the County of Down,
but, on the other hand, here is my hon. Friend
(Mr. Dickson) who sits on my right, and who
took a different view, and he also is a member
of the County Council of County Down.
I would like to tell hon. Members that
since the autumn of 1923 resolutions have
been forwarded to the Government of Northern Ireland from various local authorities
pressing the Government to introduce legislation which would enable County Councils
to undertake at least minor drainage works.
There is one resolution which I will crave
the indulgence of the House to give in extenso. This resolution was unanimously
adopted at a meeting of representatives of
County and Rural District Councils held at
Enniskillen on the 11th October, 1923. The
Resolution reads:
"That we urgently request the Government of Northern Ireland to proceed at
once with their Drainage Bill, which, we
understand, has been under consideration,
so as to enable the local authorities to open
the streams and rivers in this and other
counties that are at present flooding
thousands of acres of our best land, and
ruining it for productive purposes."
I think a Government who would be insensible to a demand of that description would
hardly be deserving of the thanks of the
community which it is supposed to represent.
I may observe that at this time no Drainage
Bill had been prepared by the Government
-that was in 1923 and that legislation had
not even been foreshadowed.
A further resolution, which I will refer to
as the Fermanagh Resolution, was adopted
unanimously at a meeting of owners of
flooded lands in County Fermanagh, held at
the Enniskillen Town Hall, on the 4th
March, 1924. This resolution was couched
in these terms:-
"That we urge the Government of
Northern Ireland to bring in without delay
a Measure enacting for Northern Ireland
the provisions recommended by the Viceregal Commission on Arterial Drainage
(Ireland) in 1905as long ago as 1905with a special view to the creation of such
subsidiary districts as that of the Sillies,
and enabling County Councils to take
action on the lines indicated in paragraph 29 of the Binnie Report, conferring
on the County Council or Committee ap-
Drainage Bill.
Drainage Bill.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
pointed (in the mode recommended by that
Report) all the necessary powers for compulsory acquisition of land and water
rights, and the levying of special rates
and charges. "
.
and so on. It is a very strong Resolution,
but I will not trouble the House by reading
it altogether. This resolution was subsequently adopted in practically identical
terms by the following Local Authorities:--
The County Councils of Armagh, Down and
Tyrone; the County Committees of Agriculture for the Counties of Tyrone, Derry,
Antrim, Armagh and Down; and the Rural
District Councils of Ballymena, Banbridge,
Castlederg, Clogher, Dungannon and Tanderagee. Yet my hon. Friend (Mr. Leslie)
asks that the Bill should be postponed,
although colleagues of his own, and people
with whom he is intimately associated, have
demanded that the Bill shall be brought
into force.
I would draw the attention of hon. Members of this House to the specific request
conveyed in the resolution I have just read
for powers to be conferred on the County
Councils to take action on the lines indicated in paragraph 29 of the Report of the
Viceregal Commission of 1905. As was explained in the debate in this House on the
Second Reading of this Measure, the Drainage Bill was originally drafted to give effect
generally to the recommendations of the Viceregal Commission of 1905, in so far as they
were considered capable of application to
the circumstances of Northern Ireland.
The terms of the present Bill were discussed at a Conference with representatives
of the County Councils, and I am not sure
that my hon. Friend (Sir George Clark) is
aware of the negotiations that have been in
progress for a very considerable time between the Government and the representatives of the County Councils. In
deference to their representations certain
Amendments were introduced. I will not
go through the Amendments that were agreed
upon, but it was on the basis of the negotiations between the Government and the
County Councils that the Amendments that
were inserted in the Bill were put in. At
these conferences all the County Councils
were represented with one exception, namely,
Londonderry at the former, and Antrim at
the latter. The points with which the
Amendments dealt, and which I have not
detailed because I think I should be out of
order if I discussed the Bill itself, were the
main points on which the County Councils
criticised the text of this Bill as introduced
into Parliament on 5th June of this year,
The first conference with the County
Councils was held on 18th September, after
they had had the Bill in their hands for a
long period, and a subsequent conference
was held on 14th October. My hon. Friend
(Mr. Leslie) is technically right in what he
says, but I regret very much that when he
has been in such close contiguity to people
who have taken a great interest in this
Bill-
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to interrupt the
noble Marquess, but I am not a member of
any of the bodies to which he has referred.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The question of the drainage of Northern
Ireland is one which has been not only in
our minds but in the minds of our forefathers for generations past, and I am sure
the hon. Member, who is more meticulously
jealous of the welfare of the Province than
any of us, would not have allowed to have
slipped his notice or memory a question of
such vital importance. I regret very much
that his friends on the Agricultural Committee at Ballymena, knowing the position
he occupies in this Assembly, have not used
him as their spokesman to voice the views
they have in their minds. However, I am
sure they will rectify that in the future.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not want them to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I will now take the resolutions of the two
County Councils who appear to disagree
with the views generally expressed at the
conference to which I have referred, and
I again wish to insist that the general
principles underlying the Bill were at no
time questioned. I will take first the
Antrim resolution passed unanimously at
a meeting of the Antrim County Council on
the 3rd instant. This resolution reads:-
"That the Antrim County Council,
having considered the Drainage Bill now
before Parliament, cannot see their way
to approve of its provisions.
Where drainage works under the provisions of the Bill are carried out and
are successful, the waters will be brought
down more rapidly into the basin of the
present floods. That the entire cost of
such works is made payable from rates.
That the County Councils who have to
pay rates are in a minority on the Consultative Committee and, generally, the
Bill-without dealing with the main question-is premature, and will only raise
hopes which are bound to be disappointed."
Members of this House will note that the
resolution states merely that the Bill is
premature, and that in the opinion of the
Antrim County Council it will raise hopes
which are bound to be disappointed. This
may be now the views of the Antrim
County Council, but I would remind
them that the County Antrim Committee of Agriculture at their meeting
held on
the 27th November, 1923,
5 NOVEMBER 1925
The Drainage Bill-

Committee.
"6
proposes. Its duties will be precisely those
recommended for such a body by the Viceregal Commission of 1905.
adopted the Fermanagh resolution, which
asks the Government to give our County
Councils the necessary legal power to promote local drainage schemes whereby leading watercourses may be opened," and so
forth.
I will now take the resolution of the Armagh
County Council, which is in everybody's
mind because it was in the newspapers the
other day. I am inclined to think that the
spirit which prompted this resolution is the
result of a misapprehension of the intentions
of the Government, and this seems all the
more apparent on analysis of the terms in
which this resolution has been couched. The
resolution regards this Bill as a Measure
of more than doubtful utility at the present
time so far as the Lough Neagh drainage
area is concerned." This is precisely the
point at which the views of the Armagh
County Council and the Government diverge.
The Government, as is our duty, regard
drainage as a problem for which a solution
has to be found, capable of application not
only to the difficulties of County Armagh but
also to those of the whole Province.
The Armagh County Council apparently
regard the solution of the farm drainage
problems of Ulster as bound up with the introduction of certain modifications in the
Drainage and Navigation Works in Lough
Neagh and along the Lower Bann. The
Government, whilst fully alive to the necessity for speedy action, would hesitate to
express an opinion on the proper solution
of the difficulties presented by the Bann
Valley drainage problem until
survey of this problem had been made. This
the Government has undertaken, and I may
say we are pressing forward with it.
a proper
The present Bill is, as it were, an instalment, pending such Bill, as must subsequently be introduced to deal in a comprehensive manner with the Bann, Lough
Neagh and Lough Erne systems, but much
useful work can be done and certainly will
be done by County Councils in the meantime
under the powers conferred by the Bill. It
does appear to me that it would be objectless
to abolish the Drainage and Navigation
Boards to which the resolution refers without substituting something else. No suggestions are made as to what should be substituted, and the Government itself is not
yet in a position to make a suggestion on
that point.
Both resolutions also fail to appreciate the
functions of the Advisory Committee. They
are fully set out in the Bill, and I would refer hon. Members to Clause 18, in particular,
sub-Sections 1 and 2. No action on the part
of the Committee can affect the local financial expenditure: they can only deal with
such schemes as the County Council itself
I should not be in any degree worthy of the
post I have the honour to hold if I did not
to the fullest extent consider the objections
which any hon. Member might raise to the
manner in which the business is conducted
in this House, and the speed or the slowness
with which Bills pass through this House,
but I do feel, and I am sure the majority of
the House will agree with me, that this is a
Measure which has been fully considered
throughout the Six Counties by the various
bodies that are closely in touch with the substance of the Measure. Therefore, I feel that
we should be wanting in our duty if we did
not continue to press this Bill forward and
pass it into law. It is for that reason that,
much as I am invariably influenced by suggestions that come from hon. Members of the
House, and notably from my hon. Friend (Mr.
Leslie), who takes such a conspicuous and useful part in the business of this House, I
regret that on this occasion I find myself
in total disagreement with the suggestion
he has put forward.
On Question, Motion (That this House do
now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill) agreed to.
In Committee accordingly,
The Marquess of DUFFERIN and AVA
in the Chair.]
CLAUSE 1 (Preparation, confirmation and
carrying out of schemes for drainage works).
(1) The council of each county in Northern Ireland other than a county borough
(in this Act referred to as "the county
council") shall be a drainage authority for
the purposes of draining any lands within
their county which are liable to be flooded
or injured by water, or whose drainage is
capable of being improved; and, subject to
the provisions of this Act, the county council shall have power-
(5) The county surveyor, or any other duly
authorised officer of the county council,
may enter upon any lands and execute al!
such works and do all such other things as
are necessary for the purposes of any
scheme or for the execution of any drainage or other works under this Act, and
may make all such inquiries and investigations as are necessary for the purposes of
any surveys, reports or plans in connection
with such scheme or works; and the county
council shall make reasonable compensation, subject to the provisions of this Part
of this Act, for any injury done by the
county surveyor or other such officer.

will go on to insert the other words

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
sub-Clause 1, line 8, to leave out the word
"each" (The Council of each County), and
to insert instead thereof the word "any."
This Amendment really covers the Second
Amendment, and I am not sure that they will
detain us very long. If you take the two
Amendments together the point is to leave it
open to any county council in Northern Ireland to reject the Bill so far as it concerns
itself by refraining from passing a resolution adopting its provisions. We have
been told ever since the Bill was introduced
into this House that it was a permissive
Measure. I desire to direct the attention of
the House to the words of the whole of this
sub-Clause. We are told that the county
council of each county "shall" be a Drainage
Authority for various purposes, and I should
like to ask what precedent there is for using
such a term, when the intention of Parliament is only to pass an enabling Bill.
As the House knows there are several
other
with
Measures usually connected
Local Government-there is the instance
the Notification of
which
of
1907, in
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: As the hon. Member stated, the
second of these Amendments really covers
the first as well. As has already been
pointed out, this Bill is a permissive Bill,
and if this Amendment were adopted it
seems to me it would strike at the root
of the whole Bill. The hon. Member having
been desirous that this Bill should be postponed or possibly thrown out altogether is
now afraid that something will be done to
the County Councils if they do not make
use of the Bill. This Bill does not compel
County Councils to undertake local schemes,
and therefore they cannot be subject to any
penalty if they do not do so. I think that
is quite clear. I am afraid we cannot
accept the hon. Member's Amendment,
because, as I said, it strikes at the very
root of the Bill.

Act,
Mr. LESLIE:

was
adopt by resolution.
Libraries (Northern Ireland) Act-our own
Act of only last year-and there
another Act dealing with some regulations
in relation to dogs. I have no doubt there
were many others. But the particular
point, of course, which I want to make is
this: If the County Council does not put
this Bill into operation is it perfectly clear
that the County Council will not expose
itself to any pressure on the part of the
Minister of Home Affairs, or, still more,
that it will not expose itself to action by
some outside person? It is always possible,
as the Committee knows, to enforce action
by a municipal body by means of a writ
of mandamus, and as the Committee also
knows your chances of getting a writ of
mandamus for simple nonfeasance are not
always particularly rosy. All the same
there have been instances where courts have
held that there was a statutory duty to
exercise the power, and a writ has issued.
The Government will, no doubt, before
bringing in this Bill have considered that,
and they will be able to clear away any doubt
there may be.
The object of my Amendment is to make
it perfectly plain in the wording of the Act
that the County Council in not adopting
the Bill shall not expose itself to any risk
of legal proceedings or to any risk of pressure to apply the Bill. If the Bill is to
confer powers on the County Councils one
would think they should be left without
pressure as regards the question whether
they shall or shall not exercise
powers. Therefore, in the first instance, I
have only to move to leave out
"each
and insert any." I hope the Committee

those
I
think
that probably
everybody will be satisfied with getting that
definite statement. Therefore, I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
2.
The following Amendment stood in the
Order Paper in the name of Mr LESLIE:
"Page 1, line 7, afterborough to insert
'which by resolution adopts the provisions
of this Act.'"
Amendment not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 3, line 8, after "Council" ("County
Council, may enter") to insert "after
notice." In case of the county surveyor or
any authorised officer entering on the land,
and so on, I suggest to insert the words
"after notice." Such words are in use, and
sometimes notice is required. As a general
rule in the case of a reasonable surveyor,
and so forth, the strict intention of the
words is not necessary, but I think it is
worth while to put the words in, because
it always seems to me very necessary when
officials and surveyors, and so on, come
down to see people or lands in the country
the occupiers should know that they are
coming. It is not very much, therefore, to
require of anybody who has got to go and
see a man that the man he has got to go
and see shall get a letter beforehand to say
that he is coming. Therefore, I think it
might make it a little more certain that
these very useful interviews shall take place
if we insert in the Bill the words "after
notice." I do not think putting them in
would mean that if there was a succession
of visits there should be a succession of
notices. I think before the first entry
notice should be given. I beg to move.
Drainage Bill-
Committee.
5 NOVEMBER 1925
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member in the closing
words of his speech has given the reason
why we cannot insert these words. Does
the hon. Member realise that if we inserted
the words after notice" it would mean
that every time every officer or other official
of a County Council came he would have
to give notice-every single time? I do feel
that that would be unreasonable. I think the
hon. Member will understand, and will
agree that any officer of a County Council
would as a matter of course and of courtesy
give notice, but if these words were inserted
it would mean that every single time any
official wished to go on any land he would
have to give notice. For that reason the
Government cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to be a fair
example of the danger of rushing matters
of this kind. A little clause could have been
inserted in the Bill. Of course I saw that
danger. At the same time I am inclined
to think my point is a good one. But I do
not observe that anyone attaches any great
inmportance to it. Therefore, I do not
proceed, and ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
On Question ("That Clause 1 stand part of
the Bill").
Mr. DICKSON: Before the Clause is
passed I would like to ask a question on page
2, line 15 ("(c) to appoint such officers and
servants, execute"). I understand that in
Committee we have a certain latitude in
getting details in connection with a Bill of
this kind. I would like to find out if in line
15 the appointment of such officers and servants to execute such works does not necessarily mean that it is the staff of the County
Council, and that we can employ outside
surveyors. If that is so the Amendment
which I have on page 4 would not be necessary and consequently I would withdraw it
both on the major and minor schemes. If
the County Council have power to appoint
outside surveyors I would also like to know if
we have power to pay them and get one half
refunded.
Mr. LONG: This is a point to which the
County Council of Londonderry attached
very considerable importance. They feel that
their surveyor's time is fully occupied on
ordinary maintenance work and reconstruction schemes for roads amounting to over
£150,000, and they would be glad to be assured that for major and minor schemes they
are empowered to employ outside aid.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This is a question which I think my hon.
Friend intended to ask on a previous occasion when we were discussing this Measure.
I was considering a point the right hon.
Gentleman (Col. Crawford) had
in
raised
inclined
another clause and I am
to think I
gave an answer that was
not correct. The County Council is empowered to appoint such officers and servants
outside their own staff. With reference to
the payment of such officers I take it the
cost would fall on the County Council.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 11 (By-laws of County Councils).
(6) The county council may, by any bylaws made under this section, impose on
offenders against the same such reasonable
penalties as they think fit, not exceeding
the sum of five pounds for each offence,
and, in the case of a continuing offence, a
further penalty not exceeding forty shillings for each day after written notice of
the offence from the county council.
Mr. DICKSON: I beg to move in Clause 11,
page 9, line 10, after "fit" ("as they think fit,
not exceeding") to insert "not less than the
sum of forty shillings and." The reason for
this is we have in petty sessions courts a
maximum penalty for cases like sheep-dipping
where a person who does not comply with the
law is brought before an ordinary court of
summary jurisdiction and fined a shilling with
a maximum penalty of perhaps £15 or £20.
Unless there is a maximum penalty there is no
good in bringing these people before a petty
sessions court. I beg to move
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am afraid the Government cannot accept the Amendment moved by the
hon. Member for one reason, and a very
clear one. What the hon. Member proposes
is to ask Parliament to fix a minimum

Drainage Bill—

penalty for offences however small and however slight they may be. I do not know

Mr. DICKSON: If the County Council has
the power, as

CLAUSES 12 (Powers to enforce cleansing
of water courses, 13 (Assessment of compensation in respect of drainage),
(Liability for consequential damage),
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 15 (Transfer of business arising
under existing Drainage Acts).
(1) The business of the drainage
authority of any drainage district within
the meaning of this section shall on such
day as the Ministry of Home Affairs may
appoint (unless previously transferred in
pursuance of section twenty of the Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1898) be transferred to the county council of the county
within which that business arises, or, if
the business arises within two or more
counties, to the county councils of those
counties jointly, and, subject to the provisions of this part of this Act, the county
council or councils (as the case may be)
shall, in respect of that business, have all
the powers and duties of the authority
from whom the business is transferred.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move-to keep myself
in order-in Clause 15, page 11, line 6, to
leave out "shall" ("this section shall on
such") and insert "may." I think it will be
the feeling of the Committee that these two
Amendments to Clause 15 are practically
covered by the discussion on Clause 1.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The hon. Member is correct; they are
consequential.
Mr. LESLIE: Therefore, I beg leave to
withdraw.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. LESLIE:-In Clause
15, page 11, line 9, after "1898 "Local
Government (Ireland) Act, 1908") to insert
"if the council of the county or counties so
request."
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 16 (Schemes for transfer and
carrying out of business).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to insert at
the end of the Clause:-"(h) the compensation of any person injuriously affected
by the abandonment of any drainage works."
This is an Amendment which I hope I did
not put down unduly quickly, but it seemed
to me on reading the Clause that you wanted
a provision of this sort. We are giving
power for a scheme with such provision as
may be necessary and expedient in relation
to the abandonment of any drainage works.
and it seemed to me not unreasonable that
such a scheme should include, if necessary,
and the facts support it, the abandonment of
a drainage scheme.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It might perhaps shorten discussion
if I stated that the Government are prepared to accept this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 17 (Power of Lough and River
Erne, Lough Neagh and Lower and Upper
Bann Drainage Authorities to make new
schemes).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave out
the Clause. I do not know whether I correctly interpret the views expressed by
hon. Senators in this House and by people
outside as regards this particular Clause
which is the whole of Part III of the Bill. I
suggest to leave it out altogether, the idea
being that there is not, so far as I can form
an opinion, a general desire that either the
Lough and River Erne Drainage and Navigation Board or the Lough Neagh Drainage
frustees should be given any further power
n the way of striking a drainage rate. I
am afraid the general opinion is that money
hitherto obtained by these two authorities
has not brought to the ratepayers the advantages which they expected when they paid
the money. So far as I can see, if we leave
out the Clause altogether we simply leave
these two authorities in the position in which
they are at the moment, and we are leaving
that over until that larger Measure for
which my Friend the Chairman of the
Londonderry County Council (Mr. Long) asked
on Tuesday but as to which he has not
received a definite assurance--we are leaving
that over until the larger Measure comes
before us. Then the Lough Neagh Drainage
Trustees can be dealt with. The question of
the Erne Board will have to be specially considered because, as the Committee know,
there are special difficulties as regards that
Board which need not be referred to
this afternoon. I think that on this
matter we should hear from hon.
Drainage Bill-

hon.

sorry that the
Senator from County Fermanagh (Mr. Porter
Porter) is not in his place this afternoon.
He spoke on this matter on the Second

leaving out the whole of Clause 17, in order
that the matter may be fully debated.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I beg to
support the Amendment moved by the hon.
Senator opposite. There is a widespread
feeling in my part of the country affected by
one of the Drainage Boards in question-the
Lough Neagh Drainage Trustees-that those
