Finance Bill.
MOTOR TAXATION.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a First
time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: In
moving the Second Reading of the Finance
Bill I feel that, in spite of the limited powers
of this House in regard to the Measure, hon.
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
Members will expect me to say a few words
upon the subject of the alteration of duties
on licences for mechanically propelled
vehicles which will be found in the first
Clause of this Finance Bill.
I may take this opportunity of saying how
much I regret that we have had to move the
suspension of Standing Orders for the purpose of taking this Bill in one sitting. But
I think hon. Members are aware of the
reason, and that is that we hope to finish
up the business this week and to have the
prorogation of Parliament on Tuesday of
next week.
With reference to these rates of duty on
these mechanically propelled vehicles to
which I have referred if we compare themboth those upon passenger motor vehicles and
also those upon motor lorries-with the old
rates it will be clearly seen that we are asking those who use the roads to make a profit
for themselves, to pay a higher contribution
towards the upkeep of the roads. It will
be noted that we have carefully drawn the
new scales, as indeed we were bound so to do.
to fit in with the actual facts so far as
wear and tear on the high roads is concerned.
The increase in the new rates is ever steeper
and steeper as the size and the weight of the
motor vehicle increases; while the increase
in the rates for lorries is much greater than
that for passenger vehicles, the reason, of
course, being that the tax upon lorries hitherto has been very light as compared with that
upon the passenger vehicle of comparable size
and weight.
Over the whole of the United Kingdom,
ever since the war, local authorities everywhere have been waging an unequal contest
in their unceasing efforts to maintain the
roads to meet the stresses laid upon them by
a motor traffic which year by year increases,
not only in volume but in the weight and
dimensions of the individual vehicles that
compose the whole. That contest has been
especially severe and unequal here in Ulster,
where our climate joins with the enemy
against the county councils, where there are
many miles of roads laid upon the unstable
foundations of bog land, and where the road
mileage per thousand of the population is
more than twice that in Great Britain, being
ten miles per thousand here against four-andthree-fifths miles in Great Britain.
The Government, fortified by the represen
tations from local authorities all over the
Province, have come to the conclusion that
the conditions of the struggle must be
changed or the battle will be irretrievably
lost and either the ratepayer reduced to a
state of bankruptcy or the roads allowed t>
fall into ruinous disrepair.
It is useless for the opponents of this Bill
with its changes in the duties for motor
vehicles, to put forward the plea that we
have no right to lay heavier taxes upon motor
vehicles here than in Great Britain. Our
reply must be, that conditions here are
demonstrably different from conditions in
Great Britain and that the sole advantage
that Ulster can reap from the measure of
self-government which is hers, is that she
may give to local problems the particular
treatment that local conditions render necessary.
What then is this local problem? If the
House will pardon me, I will give them a
few figures, very few, but I think very convincing. We are faced with a bill for the
upkeep and maintenance of our roads amounting to nearly one million and a quarter
pounds for the present year. Motor taxation
yields the sum of three hundred thousand
pounds annually. Of this amount all but
thirty thousand pounds comes from motor
vehicles under two tons in weight. The
heavier vehicles of which the five-ton lorry,
with a tax of only £30, is estimated to do
£300 damage annually to the roads-these
heavy vehicles only contribute a beggarly
£30,000 a year to the enormous cost of road
maintenance.
It is plain on the face of things that this
anomaly must be corrected, and that the tax
paid upon motor vehicles must bear some
recognizable relationship to the amount of
wear and tear that the vehicle inflicts upon
the road. If hon. Members will look at the
proposed new scales they will find that we
have endeavoured to achieve this, and I think
endeavoured with a very fair amount of
success. For instance, with the passenger
vehicles the increase in each class is a negligible amount-only £4 annually-until we
come to the larger classes of these vehicles,
when the figures become ever more and more
serious with the increase in the size of the
machine. The same thing will be noted with
respect to motor lorries. There is no increase
in the first two classes-the lighter class of
these vehicles. With the other classes the
increase is graded more steeply to correspond
with increase in weight. The position shortly
is this, that it is by no means certain that
these very large motor vehicles, weighing five
tons and upwards, unladen--whether motor
lorries or the largest class of passenger carry.
ing vehicles-are at all desirable or at all
suitable to the road conditions generally in
the Province.
We want to inflict no hardship upon a
young and flourishing industry. We want to
make hard times no more difficult for any
class of the community. We do not want to
discourage the activities of those individuals
or companies whose business it is to make a
17 DECEMBER 1925
Finance Bill-

Second Reading.
class of vehicle, and I am delighted to know
that no increase has been put on one and two
ton lorries. I am quite pleased that the
Government have not yielded to the demand
to differentiate between farmers and others
using thse motor lorries. It is an exceedingly dangerous thing to discriminate in a
case of that sort, and farmers have already
come to realise that horse traction is cheaper
than motor traction for them.
livelihood by organizing cheap motor passenger transport, but as a Government we
are bound to hold the scales fairly, and to
see that the interests of the ratepayer are
not sacrificed, because it is upon the ratepayer that the great volume of the charge
for the upkeep of roads must necessarily fall.
If it is for the good of the motor industry
and the users of motor vehicles that the roads
must be maintained in a condition of excellence far exceeding the needs of the ordinary
ratepayer, at whose expense and for whose
convenience the roads are on the whole provided, then we can only be fair, and no one
can reasonably resist the proposals of the
Government when we ask this particular
form of locomotion to pay, not the whole,
but at least some proportion of the increased
charge which its necessities entail upon the
general public. I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: I just wish briefly to support
the Bill. There has been a revolution in
road traffic since 1914. In that year the roads
as a rule were fairly well suited for the traffic
they were called upon to bear, which was
mainly horse traffic. However, mechanically
propelled vehicles came into use, with the
result that our thin road surface soon became
cut up and the roads became almost impassable. To cope with this, local authorities
were at their wits' end to find a method by
which they could withstand the effects of
this extraordinary traffic. To give you an
illustration of the difference in cost, I may
mention that in the county of Londonderry
the expenditure on roads in 1914 was £31,233.
In 1924-25 the ordinary maintenance contracts
were £114,555, in addition to which £153,000
was spent in reconstruction.. Out of what we
expended in 1924-25 we received £18,019 from
the Road Fund, but it will readily be seen
from a glance at these figures that the expenditure has increased at an enormous rate,
and experts attribute the necessity for this
expenditure entirely to heavy mechanically
propelled vehicles using the roads.
My information in the matter differs very
much from that of the noble Leader of the
House when he says that the heavy mechanically propelled vehicles do £300 worth of
damage to the roads in one year. I should be
inclined to put it at a very much higher
figure. The information I received was that
such a vehicle did £1,000 worth of damage to
the roads in a year if continuously used. I
appreciated very much the speech of the
noble Marquess in moving the Second Reading of this Bill. I appreciated especially his
reference to the unequal contest, as regards
providing the money for the upkeep of these
roads, in which the County Councils have
hitherto been engaged. I do not believe in
the least that the duties proposed will act
in restraint of trade. I am convinced that
in the near future motor owners will see
that if is to their advantage to use a lighter
On the whole I welcome this Bill and I
appreciate the manner in which it has been
introduced. The Ministry of Home Affairs
have been inundated with resolutions
from all the various road authorities in
Northern Ireland asking them to take steps
to have this matter dealt with, and I am
very glad that they have decided to hold the
scales evenly between the road users and the
taxpayers. As the n ble Marquess has
pointed out, the tax that is proposed to be
put on heavy motor vehicles does not at all
represent the whole of the amount they
should in equity be called upon to pay. We
recognise that they render considerable service to the community. They are of inestimable value to shopkeepers in conveying goods
from the various centres to their shops, and
also to the farmers in transporting their
produce to shipping centres. For these
reasons I think it is only right that the
Government should not place such a tax on
these vehicles as would put them off the
roads. I think the decision of the Government is an equitable and fair one and I
support the Bill.
Mr. GLENDINNING: While fully in
agreement with the Government in regard
to this Measure, and while I intend to
support it, I should like to express my own
disappointmeat, and the disappointment of
the ordinary users of motor vehicles throughout the country that the Government did not
avail itself of this opportunity of reverting
to the simple method of placing the tax on
the petrol consumed instead of on the horse
power or the weight of the vehicle. The
former method would have been equally advantageous to the Government so far as the
provision of the necessary money for the upkeep of the roads is concerned, and it would
have been much fairer to motor users. There
are a great many arguments which might be
used on both sides, but motorists are generally of opinion that a tax on petrol is the
fairest and easiest method of taxing all
classes of motor vehicles.
Mr. LONG: Hear, hear.
I am firmly congeneral feeling
Mr. GLENDINNING:
vinced that that is the
throughout the country, and that is the
reason I mention it, although I am prepared
to support the Measure.
Finance Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It is rather
a pity it is found necessary to pass this Bill
through all its stages in one day. It is
creating a considerable amount of interest
amongst people outside and I believe it
would have been well if the Bill could have
been left over for a short time for further
consideration. However, we are more or less
indebted to the Marquess of Londonderry for
the clear explanation he has given us of the
Bill. There is no doubt that a considerable
amount of criticism has been levelled at the
Government in regard to this Measure, but
it is only what anyone might expect when
increased taxes are suggested. Those who are
likely to be affected by them are sure to cry
out and wish the burden to be placed on
some other person's shoulders. Even if they
think they are not likely to escape the
burden they like to cry out to let their neighbours know of the burden they will have to
shoulder.
There is considerable competition going on
at the present time between road and rail
transport. It has been stated many times
outside this House that the Government is
favouring the railways in preference to road
transport. While I believe the Government
have no intention whatever of scotching or
handicapping that competition, at the same
time it is very necessary that the Government should see that the competition with the
railways is not hampered or favoured at the
expense of the ratepayers. The railways have
their own track and a considerable staff to
maintain, and they pay their employees good
wages and work them under the best conditions. On the other hand, road traffic is a
new adventure for which roads have to be
specially made.
The old roads that were suitable for the
farmer would not now suit the heavy traffic
they are called upon to bear, and the roads
that are specially built to carry this heavy
traction are to a considerable extent unsuitable for the farmer. Therefore, I can quite
understand the farmer complaining that he
has to pay extra taxation for the upkeep of
these roads. I understand that the surface
of these roads is so smooth that horses are
unable to walk on them, and I cannot understand those who own heavy motor vehicles
complaining if they are asked to pay for the
upkeep of roads that are specially required
for that particular class of traffic.
Arguments have been put forward that
these increased taxes are likely to increase
the cost of living to the workers of the city.
I have gone carefully into the matter, and
I do know that there is a considerable number
of these heavy motor vehicles travelling for
some distance around Belfast, collecting farm
produce, and particularly milk, and while
that must be a very cheap means of transport,
because they have no extra handling and not
much labour to pay, yet the consumer has not
benefited to any great extent by obtaining
cheaper milk. Someone has pocketed the
profits, and, therefore, these people have no
reason to complain if they are asked to contribute some little towards the upkeep of
the roads. Good roads, if properly kept in
order, should benefit this heavy motor traffic.
It will reduce to a considerable extent their
annual repair bill, because there is nothing
more severe on machinery than jolting over
potholes along the road, which is very injurious to pins and springs. I am very
pleased that the Government has taken action
in this matter, because I would like to see
in Northern Ireland a really good class of
road. Not only should old roads be repaired
and maintained, but straighter and more
direct new roads should be constructed from
the city to other towns in the Northern area.
If that could be done it might be an attraction
to tourist traffic, and Northern Ireland would
benefit to a considerable extent in that
respect. We have fine scenery here, but I
think it cannot be said we have the best of
roads. Therefore, the Government are to be
complimented on taking this step to improve
the roads, to repair them and make them
as good as possible for traffic. I am strongly
in favour of this Bill, and I am pleased to
give it my support on the Second Reading.
Again I wish to say I am glad the noble
Marquess has taken so much pains in explaining the Bill as we have not had an
opportunity of examining it.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I consider this Bill
has been long overdue. It is now several
years since I was one of a deputation who
waited on the Prime Minister on the subject and impressed on him the injustice of
the existing situation. The county from
which I have the honour of coming may be
described as a corridor county, because all
the traffic from Belfast or Down or Antrim
has to pass through Armagh in order to go
to the West and South-West. We complain
not only of the ordinary heavy lorries but
of the enormous lorries which carry building materials-bricks and other heavy
materials--and which are destructive to the
1oads. Without going into the statistics of
the matter, anybody who observes the road
after one of these lorries has passed will see
how the whole material of the road is shaken
to pieces. The injury done must be enormous. The burden on the ratepayers must
be very serious indeed. The road between
Belfast and Armagh has been reconstructed
at enormous cost by borrowed money, and,
of course, the interest on that added to the
expensive upkeep is a very serious injury to
the ratepayers. Their rates are continually
going up with the various calls upon them,
education and other matters which are of
great importance. I think this Bill will be
cordially welcomed as it has been called for
so long by the people of the country.
17 DECEMBER 1925
Finance Bill-

Third Reading.
placing duties on those vehicles consistent
with the damage they do to the roads. My
hon. Friend, who sits beside me (Mr. Long),
spoke of the damage which these large motor
vehicles do to the roads, and he suggests
the damage is more like £1,000 than £300.
The figure which I gave, which I am sure is
the minimum figure and not the maximum
figure, came from the expert evidence of the
county surveyor at the Ulster Development
Commission. In his judgment the damage
done to the roads was in the nature of £300
per annum.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: I listened with
great interest to the speech of the noble
Marquess in introducing this Bill. I consider he has made a very good case, and for
my part I intend to support the Bill. I
notice there has been an increase made in
the duties according to the weight of the
vehicles, but there is no limit on the weight
that may be on any vehicle. It seems to
me there should be some arrangement made
to prevent very heavy vehicles such as those
weighing up to 20 tons using the roads because the damage that would be done by
them if they were to go at any speed would
be considerable. I see no provision in the
Bill for anything of that nature. I would
like to ask the noble Marquess whether this
matter has been considered.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
am very grateful to hon. Members for the reception they have given to the Bill which I
have had the honour of introducing in this
House. My hon. Friend (Mr. Joseph
Cunningham) like myself is jealous of the
dignity of this House and resents, and
rightly resents, if we are hurried through
cur work to the detriment of the legislation
which it is necessary for us to bring forward,
but I would only say in mitigation of the
circumstances of to-day that we are unable
to have a Committee Stage on this Bill.
That is a stage which does not count in this
House, and the only opportunity we have of
discussion on matters relating to finance is
on the Second Reading. Consequently, the
hastening of the next stage to the Third
Reading is merely a matter of form, but
I am fully sympathetic with him in the
principle of the point which he was raising.
My hon. Friend behind me (Mr. Glendinning) spoke about a tax on petrol. I do
not think he is aware that we have not the
power under the 1920 Act to tax petrol even
if we wished to do so, but I also understand
he has not been through the difficulties
which are inherent in anybody's desire to
put a tax on petrol when it is discovered
that motor-cars can run on almost anything.
However, this is a matter which does not
arise on this Bill so I will not pursue that
point further. My hon. Friend, Sir George
Clark, has spoken of the limits as regards
the weight of motor vehicles on the roads
and suggests that there is a limit in the
Bill, I think, of £100.
Sir GEORGE CLARK: It says anything
over 5 tons.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
As a matter of fact, at the present moment
these lorries which he speaks of are not in
existence, but when they do make their
appearance in leviathan dimensions on the
roads I feel sure he will raise the matter in
this House, and I have no doubt the Government will address themselves immediately to
My hon. Friend suggests that this tax may
have the effect of raising the cost of food to
the people, but I am not sure that if he
works it out when he sees the amount per
ton per mile which is included in this tax
on vehicles used for the transport of food it
could be said that the extra cost put on these
vehicles could in any sense add to the cost of
living. The other point which he made about
the farm produce which is sold in Belfast
is a matter which, I think, has already been
raised in this House, and I think Members
expressed wonder at the fact that the prices
in Belfast are far higher than the prices
which exist in the country, but that als) is
not a matter which comes up for discussion
on this Bill.
I
I hope I have given hon. Members the explanation which they desire on the speech
which I made in introducing this Bill. It
is impossible to put everything in an opening
speech, and I am grateful to hon. Members
for the points which they have made.
think I may owe the House an apology
because I have an idea in my mind that by
inadvertence I gave the wrong figure to the
House, that the upkeep and maintenance
of the roads in the country amounted
to a quarter of a million. I think I gave
that figure. The amount is one million and
a quarter, a very different thing-£1,250,000
and not £250,000. The expenditure for upkeep and maintenance of our roads amounts
to a million and a quarter and not a quarter
of a million.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be now
read a Second time) put, and agreed to.
Moved: That the House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That the Bill be now read the
Third time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That the Bill do now pass.--(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Finance Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
And a Message was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to the said Bill without any amendment.
Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move, That this House do now
adjourn. The business which we have completed to-day completes the business for the
Session, and it is proposed that the proroguing
of this Parliament shall take place on
Tuesday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes before Four o'clock until
Tuesday next.
The King's Speech.
The King's Speech.
22 DECEMBER 1925
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 22nd December, 1925.
The Senate met at a quarter past Twelve
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Papers Presented.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
Regulations made under the Pharmacy and
Poisons Act (Northern Ireland), 1925, by the
Council of the Pharmaceutical Society of
Northern Ireland and approved by the Minister of Home Affairs.
Presented pursuant to Act and ordered to
lie on the Table.
Royal Assent.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am authorized by the Prime Minister on his behalf to inform the Senate that
his Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland
in the name and on behalf of His Majesty the
King has been pleased to give his Assent to
the following Bills:
Appropriation (No. 2).
Unemployment Fund (No. 2).
Education (Administrative Provisions).
Superannuation.
Intoxicating Liquor (Finance).
Finance.
PROROGATION.
Moved: That the Gentleman Usher of the
Black Rod be commanded to inform the Commons that the Speech of his Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland at the Prorogation
of Parliament is to be communicated to the
Senate forthwith, and that this House invite
the Commons to attend in order to hear the
same. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
THE KING'S SPEECH.
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Speaker, the King's Speech was delivered to
both Houses of Parliament by the Speaker of
the Senate (in pursuance of His Grace's command) as followeth:
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I have to congratulate you upon the
measures which have been placed on the
Statue Book during the first Session of
this present Parliament.
I regret that Northern Ireland still
continues to suffer from the general depression in trade which exists throughout the United Kingdom. The problem
of unemployment is the greatest that
confronts us at the moment in common
with Great Britain, and it is gratifying
that representatives of our manufactures
and commerce and the people of Northern
Ireland have freely responded to the
invitation of the Government to take
part
in an Unemployment Conference
which is carefully exploring the postion
as it affects Northern Ireland. By means
of the Loans Guarantee Act continued
assistance has been rendered to the Shipbuilding Industry; while it is hoped that
the endeavour to hasten a revival of the
Linen Trade by the co-operation with
local manufacturers in a special advertising campaign in America will meet
with success. Financial assistance is also
being afforded to the Coal Industry in
Northern Ireland. In addition, the Local
Authorities are continuing to give their
valuable co-operation in the promotion
of schemes of public utility designed
expressly to give useful employment to
our people.
The results which have attended
Northern Ireland's participation in the
British Empire Exhibition this year are
most satisfactory. In organising a larger
and more fully representative exhibit of
Ulster products than was possible in
the previous year the Government and
exhibitors alike have again emphasised
Ulster's attachment to the Imperial connection.
An Act has been passed making
further provision for the payment of
Unemployment Benefit to those insured
persons who cannot obtain work and for
protecting the interests of those most in
need of the assistance of the Unemployment Fund.
It is with great pleasure that I am
able to refer to the Widows', Orphans'
and Old Age Contributory Pensions Act.
The measure marks one more stage in
the great Social Insurance code which
has been built up in the last few years,
and guarantees for our insured workpeople the same provision against proverty
and distress which
persons similarly
situated in the rest of the United Kingdom will be afforded in times of bereavement and old age.
It is satisfactory that, notwithstanding the severe industrial depression, the
thrifty habits of the people continue to
be demonstrated by the interest shown
in Ulster Savings Certificates, in which a
total of £2,000,000 has now been invested.
The King's Speech.
End of Session.
THE SENATE
Your Drainage Act provides machinery
which will enable remedial measures to
be undertaken to prevent the flooding of
our agricultural land.
I
Operations under the scheme of loans
to farmers for the replacement of live
stock lost owing to the recent unfavourable season have now been concluded.
am satisfied that this scheme eased considerably a serious situation, particularly
in the poorer districts. More than £46,000
was spent on this work.
It is gratifying to learn that the
recent legislation passed by Parliament
granting additional financial inducements
to private individuals and local authorities
for the erection of houses has been attended with such success and that the
rate of building during the last few
months has been steadily increasing.
The Government Loans and Exchequer
Provisions Act has effected a number of
improvements in our financial system
and has provided an economically sound
method of financing the loans which are
made from time to time for capital purposes. The reception given to the issue
of Ulster Loan Stock has provided a
satisfactory indication of the credit of
Northern Ireland.
It gives me satisfaction to observe a
continuance of peaceful conditions and the
absence of abnormal crime in Northern
Ireland, and I am glad to note the restoration of that respect for the law which is
the foundation of Society. This happy
result has been attained by a firm and
impartial enforcement of the law and by
the vigilance of the Constabulary Forces,
and I earnestly pray that no untoward
erent may occur to disturb the conditions
which now prevail.
Members of the House of Commons.
I thank you for the provision of
supplies for the public services.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
I am deeply moved and gratified at
the Agreement entered into between the
Governments of Great Britain, Northern
Ireland, and the Irish Free State.
As a
result it is my earnest prayer that
friendly relations between the Governments and peoples of Northern Ireland
and the Irish Free State may be promoted
and cemented by a spirit of goodwill
and friendship to the common advantage
of both.
Recent developments in the field of
world politics give ground for hope that
the new spirit of mutual confidence between nation and nation may bring in
its train the long-awaited revival in trade
which cannot fail to affect beneficially
the industries of Northern Ireland.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your labours.
Then the Proclamation for proroguing the
present Parliament of Northern Ireland was
read by the Speaker as followeth :
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION.
For Proroguing the present Parliament of
Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section eleven
of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, as
modified by and in pursuance of certain
enactments amending the said Act, that the
Governor of Northern Ireland shall in His
Majesty's name summon, prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that this
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
should be prorogued to the ninth day of
March, One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty-six.
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, Governor of Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish
and declare His Majesty's Pleasure that the
said Parliament be, as from the reading of
this Proclamation in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland, prorogued to the ninth day
of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Twenty-six.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be that
the said Parliament shall on the said ninth
day of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Twenty-six, assemble and be holden for
the dispatch of divers urgent and important
affairs, and the Senators and Members of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland are
hereby required to give their attendance
accordingly.
Given at Government House, Hillsborough,
this Eighteenth day of December, One Thousand Nine Hundred and Twenty-five.
Signed by his own hand,
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the First Session (opened Tuesday.
14th April, 1925) of the Second Parliament of
Northern Ireland, in the Sixteenth year of
the Reign of His Majesty King George the
Fifth.
INDEX.
SECOND PARLIAMENT.
VOLUME OF FIRST SESSION, 1925.
14th APRIL to 22nd DECEMBER.

Tuesday, 10th March, 1925.
The Fifth Session of the First Parliament
of Northern Ireland, appointed to meet 10th
March, 1925, in the fifteenth year of the
Reign of King George V, was opened by the
Governor (His Grace the Duke of Abercorn,
K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock.
Mr. SPEAKER (Most Hon, the Marquess of
Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O., D.L.) in the
Chair,
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace, the Governor, being seated on
the Throne, commanded the Gentleman
Usher of the Black Rod to let the Commons
know "It is His Grace's pleasure that they
attend him immediately in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with their
Speaker, His Grace delivered the following
Speech from the Throne, and then retired :-
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
A year ago I expressed satisfaction that
law and order had been re-established and
maintained throughout Northern Ireland,
and I am glad to learn that now it has
been found possible to relax the enforcement of the special powers granted by
Parliament for this purpose, and that all
interned persons have been released and
the Curfew restrictions have been entirely
removed.
It is a matter of great regret that
unemployment to such an extent still
continues in our midst; there was recently
welcome improvement in the linen industry, and although this has not been
maintained lately, I am aware that a
diminution in trade is unfortunately not
unusual in the winter months, and it is
hoped that a revival may take place in
the Spring.
The more settled conditions prevailing
on the Continent give hopes of an improvement in trade. It is anticipated
that the valuable recommendations made
by our Commission on Development,
which among other things is investigating
the possibility of electrical power for industrial purposes, may prove of benefit.
The legislation passed for the prevention of unfair competition in industry
and of sweating is working satisfactorily,
and improvements have been made in
the ventilation of our mills and factories
and in other conditions affecting the
welfare of employees.
THE SENATE
Debate on Address.

King's Speech.
means
over
works initiated
The relief
by my
Government since its establishment have
received the generous and sympathetic
co-operation of our Local Authorities and
involve a capital cost of £3,000,000. These
works include the construction of workers'
houses, improvements of roads and harbours, water supply, tramway reconstruction, and many other works of public
17,000
this
utility. By
situations for unemployed persons have
been found which would not otherwise
have existed, and every effort will be made
to continue the promotion of schemes of
public utility to provide useful work. In
addition, the facilities afforded under the
Loans Guarantee Acts have contributed
materially to the reduction of unemploy
ment, and you will be asked to extend
still further the provisions of these Acts.
Nevertheless, in spite of continue]
efforts to increase the field of employment,
large numbers of insured workers have
the
had to rely on
resources of the
Unemployment Insurance Scheme, which
has been maintained on the same lines as
in Great Britain. Since the 1st January,
1922, the contributory Insurance Fund has
provided benefit for unemployed workers
to the extent of £4,600,000.
I have acceded to the request of my
Ministers to dissolve Parliament, and consequently the only business which will
be laid before you is that dealing with
finance and other matters which require
consideration before the end of the current
financial year.
A Bill will be brought in to amend
the Education Act (Northern Ireland),
1923, and the Bill for the extension of the
Loans Guarantee Acts to which I have
already referred.
Members of the House of Commons.
The report of the Colwyn Committee
is completed, but until its effect has been
fully explored, it may be necessary to
present tentative figures for the estimates
of the coming year.
Members of the Senate and of the
House of Commons.
The machinery of local administration
contemplated under the Education Act is
now complete, the necessary negotiations
between the County Councils and the
Ministry of Education having resulted in
I
an agreed scheme for every County.
look forward to an immediate extension
of the operation of the Act by the general
the
its provisions to
application of
Counties and to the completion of its
operations in the County Boroughs by the
application of that part of the Act which
deals with the employment of children
and young persons. I have noted with
pleasure the energy and the enthusiasm
with which the Education Committees in
the County Boroughs of Belfast and
Londonderry are undertaking the important duties which have devolved upon
them, and I expect with confidence a
similar stirring of local interest in educational administration when the Act is in
full operation in the County Districts.
The Local Government Commission
appointed to inquire into the administration of Poor Law and Public Health is
now sitting, and has already received
evidence from the majority of the Boards
of Guardians and also from the Rural
District Councils' Association. This
Commission has made arrangements for
hearing evidence from the County Councils and Urban Authorities, as well as
from representatives of all the County
Infirmaries and other Public Bodies.
I am glad to learn that the Acts
passed in the interests of agriculture are
already proving beneficial, and that as a
result there has already been an improvement in the cattle bred in Northern
Ireland, and in the development of our
egg industry. There is no doubt that
the steps taken have resulted in greater
the
confidence in British markets in
excellent quality
our agricultural
produce.
of
I am pleased to learn that a decision
has been reached to re-open the Ulster
Pavilion on an extended scale in the great
Empire Exhibition at Wembley, as it once
again testifies the readiness and determination of Northern Ireland to bear her
part in the development of the Empire.
AND I pray that Almighty God may
bestow His abundant blessing upon your
deliberations.
Papers Presented
Papers Presented
10 MARCH 1925
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
The House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.

Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO HIS MAJESTY'S
MOST GRACIOUS SPEECH.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN (in military
uniform): I hope this House is fully aware
how deeply I appreciate the honour of
being selected from among the Members of
the Senate to move the Address in reply to
the most Gracious Speech from the Throne.
I am fully aware how much I fall short of
what is required in one to whom this great
duty is entrusted, but I feel it is not only an
honour to me personally, but even more so
to that important part of our Province with
which I have been all my life identified, the
County of Tyrone, where I was born and
brought up.
I also feel how great is my responsibility
in undertaking this honourable duty, and I
only hope that the House will extend to me
the forbearance and indulgence it always
extends to one who has the privilege of
11 Debate on Address

THE SENATE
Debate on Address
[ Sir Emerson Herdman]
addressing the House for the first time.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
The Gracious Speech strikes a note of confidence as regards the public peace throughout our beloved Province of Ulster, and if I
may venture to say so, this note is and will
be amply justified. Once more our people
throughout the length and breadth of Ulster
breathe the atmosphere of peace and confidence, and on all sides there is general
satisfaction that once again lawful authority
has been re-established, and the people of
Ulster can pursue their daily lives and
ordinary peaceful avocations without fear of
hindrance or annoyance.
For this result so gratifying to our Sovereign
and his Representative we can heartily congratulate Sir James Craig and the Government of Northern Ireland and also the Royal
Ulster Constabulary forces, whose fine record is now SO well recognised and
appreciated.
The release of all interned persons and the
removal of the obnoxious Curfew are the outward and visibe signs of the beginning of a
new era of peace and prosperity in Ulster in
which I venture to hope with confidence that
however much the people of Northern Ireland
may differ on other matters all sections of
the community will work together strongly
for the maintenance and continuation of
these satisfactory conditions so essential for
the common weal. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
The reference in the most Gracious Speech
to trade and industry reminds us of the
difficulties which in varying degree, year by
year, arise to affect the happiness and
prosperity of our large industrial centres, and
I fully sympathize with the Government in
the sincere and practical interest being taken
by them both in studying the causes and
alleviating in the best way they can the
inevitable results of trade depression.
Connected as I am with an important
industry, that of flax spinning, I naturally
can claim to speak with some practical knowledge on this subject, and I do most heartily
share in the hopeful view in the Gracious
Speech that ere long the time will come when
our staple industries will revive and the
establishment of settled conditions in other
countries will once more bring work and
prosperity to the hard-pressed people of our
industrial towns where unwished for unemployment now carries in its train sorrow
and depression.
The recommendations of the Commission
on Development will be looked forward to
with general interest, and the possibility of
making Electrical Power more extensively
available will undoubtedly awaken the
keenest satisfaction. The maintenance of
our reputation in the industrial world
where competition, not always fair, is
keener than ever, makes it of the highest
importance that all necessary measures
having this end in view should receive our
loyal and sympathetic support. In combining measures for the greater comfort
and efficiency of the artisans and workers
with well thought out plans for protecting
our industries against unfair competition, I
think the Government of Northern Ireland
have proved themselves fully alive to the
true methods of increasing the prosperity
of Ulster and establishing her reputation
on a sound and firm foundation.
the
The existing unemployment has been
relieved in a gratifying degree by generous
aid, and at the same time useful works
which will, when trade revives and unemployment becomes a less menacing
problem, find us ready to start under
greatly improved conditions. In
wonderful working of the Unemployed
Insurance Fund our workers have had
ample evidence of the concern of the
Ministry of Labour for their welfare, and
I firmly believe that the working men and
Women of Ulster are looking forward
anxiously to the prospect of work being
plentiful, when in lieu of benefits they will
earn wages.
We rejoice to know that the Land Purchase problem in Northern Ireland will
shortly be solved by an Act of the Imperial
Parliament, and this coupled with the
Drainage Bill promised in the Gracious
Speech, by which it is hoped to overcome
the disastrous floods so familiar in many
farming districts, are encouraging steps towards securing for our agricultural.
industry a new impetus towards prosperity
and contentment, whilst the efforts of the
Minister of Agriculture to raise the standard
of our products in the eyes of the world are
deserving of our fullest acknowledgment.
I do not propose to refer to the other
important matters foreshadowed in the
Gracious Speech, which I leave to be dealt
with by Senator Sir Thomas Dixon, who
seconds the Address, when they will be
dealt with much better than I could do. I
desire to express my thanks for the kindly
forbearance with which the House has
listened to me.
I beg to move--
That a humble Address be presented to
his Grace in the following words:-
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal Subjects, the Senate of the Parlia-
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.

10 MARCH 1925
ment of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to Your Grace
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
Sir THOMAS DIXON (in the uniform
of His Majesty's Lieutenant for the County
of the City of Belfast): I beg to second the
motion for the Address in reply to the
Gracious Speech from the Throne, so ably
moved by Senator Sir Emerson Herdman.
It is a matter of great satisfaction to all that,
as the Gracious Speech says, Law and Order
have been restored in our beloved province
and that as a result all those who were
interned have been released, Curfew restrictions have been withdrawn, and we are
now back to our happy normal conditions.
The Speech from the Throne also dwells
upon numerous works that have been carried
out by Local Authorities and others in cooperation with the Government, in order
that unemployment may be lessened. All
these activities by the various bodies indicated have been of great benefit to the
working classes; but at best these relief
schemes are only of a temporary nature.
What we want is an improvement in trade
that will absorb our unemployed. I am
glad to be able to say that I think we can
see signs that the desired improvement is
steadily if slowly coming.
The reference to the Education Act is also
very satisfactory. A conference has had the
result of clearing away misunderstandings.
We may now, I think, hope that all those
who were divided on this question before
will unite to make this Act, as amended, the
great blessing which its framers desired it
to be. I beg to second the motion.
THE MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): Before I turn to the
business for which we are assembled here
to-day I feel sure that I shall be voicing the
sentiments of all the members of this House
when I refer to the sad event of a few
weeks ago which has certainly cast a cloud
over our Assembly to-day. I refer to the
death of Mr. Hotham. Mr. Hotham joined
us here on the inception of our new Parliament, and no one worked harder for its
success than he did. I feel that for the
position in which we find ourselves at the
present moment we are indebted to a very
large extent to the whole-hearted services
which he always devoted, not only to the
assembly as a whole, but to each individual
Senator who might at any time find himself
in a difficulty. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). I know that I am expressing the
feelings of all present to-day when I say how
deeply we feel his loss and how we desire,
on the first occasion on which we have been
able to do so, to express our sympathetic
condolence with the members of his family
who have suffered so great a bereavement.
I should like first of all to congratulate
the Mover and the Seconder of the Address
to His Grace the Governor for the speeches
which they have delivered to us this afternoon. There is no need for me in this
assembly to say very much about either of
those Senators. They are both very well
known to us. Of both their records we are
very proud, and we are glad to number
them in this body of the Senate of Ulster.
Sir Emerson Herdman comes from the
furthest of our Counties. He lives on the
Border, and not only here but also there his
name is a household word. He has a record
of which he has every reason to be justly
proud. He took his part in the Great War
and he has always shown himself to be a
thorough representative of the family to
which he belongs. I do sincerely hope that
we shall have an opportunity of hearing
him speak in this assembly more frequently
than perhaps he has done in the past, and
that he will thus give us the benefit
of his knowledge and his experience
on all those questions which so deeply
affect the people of the Six Counties.
Sir Thomas Dixon, whom perhaps I may also
be allowed to congratulate on his speech in
seconding the Motion, is also very well known
to us. His family have a splendid record of
service in this great city of Belfast. His
father was very well known to many of us
who are here to-day, and we can all testify
to the splendid position in which he placed
the Civic authority of this great city. We
are fortunate in having his successor with us
to-day, and I know quite well that he
will testify to the fact that the importance
of the position he occupies now and the
traditions which belong to that position have
been handed down to him by his illustrious
father. need say no more about my hon.
Friend. He is one of those who take a
great interest in the North of Ireland, and
he is one of those business authorities who
can speak with knowledge on all those great
questions and problems with which we as a
Province are assailed. I welcome very much
his advent to our assembly where he can
bring his wide experience and knowledge to
bear on the problems with which we have
been faced, and with which we will be faced
so long as this Province continues.
As we have been told in the Gracious
Speech from the Throne, this is the last
Session of the first Parliament of Northern
Ireland, and I feel that perhaps it is my duty
to consider in restrospect the functions and
duties which this, our first Parliament, has
Debate on Address. 16
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Marquess of Londonderry]
performed, and I shall do so as briefly as
possible. I do not think there is any necessity for me to make an apology for what our
new
institution has done. We were faced
with immense difficulties, and I think that
without undue presumption we may congratulate ourselves that we have discharged those
duties to the best of our ability. The
Government can claim to have grappled with
the problems with which they were faced.
Perhaps the first one to which I should make
allusion was the internal disorder that existed
in our midst. Peace has been restored, and
I think we can feel a sense of satisfaction
that it has been restored by the equal
administration of the law and the establishment and maintenance of regular police
forces and of special police forces. We can
certainly at this moment look with far
greater satisfaction on our position as a
self-governing unit than we could some four
years ago. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
The next problem which we have had to
face has been the problem of unemployment.
It is naturally one in which we one and all
must take the deepest possible interest, and
I am sure most of us have read with benefit
the Speech which was delivered yesterday by
Sir Arthur Steele-Maitland. It is a problem
which we should do every single thing in
our power to bring home to every one of the
inhabitants of these Six Counties. It is a
disease which if allowed to continue will sap
our vitality and threaten our existence as a
race. It is a problem to which all Governments should turn their foremost attention
with the purpose of alleviating the scourge
and in the hope of eventually diminishing it
to proportions which will not threaten us as
We can say
they do at the present moment.
on behalf of the administration, or rather on
behalf of Parliament, that our unemployed
have been given the same advantages in regard to unemployment insurance as in Great
Britain, and that legislation has been brought
forward by our Minister of Labour to keep
similar nature
step with legislation of
which has been enacted at Westminster.
a
The third matter to which I should like to
trade depression.
make some allusion is
Trade and employment have been fostered
by legislation enabling the Ministry of
Finance to guarantee the repayment of loans
for capital undertakings; and I think we
may claim to have done what could possibly
be done for the purpose of assisting trade as
far as it lay in the hands of the administration to do So. Agriculture has
encouraged by legislation which has dealt with
stock-raising and also with the marketing of
been
agricultural produce. The Housing problem
has been tackled by the encouragement not
only of municipal building schemes but particularly of the enterprise of the private
builder.
The main legislative activities of the
administration, which may perhaps he said
to have been in fulfilment of pledges which
the Government gave when they first came
into office, have been the passing of the
Licensing Bill of 1923, which I think all will
agree was a great step forward, and which I
feel, has already done a great deal to sweep
away old abuses.
The other Act of our legislative activities
was the Education Act. The problem of
education, I think all will agree, has been
dealt with in a very comprehensive fashion
The Act,
by the Education Act of 1923.
whose amendment in the present session is
foreshadowed in the gracious Speech, will
I hope result in the diversion of public
interest from the barren fields of religious
to the more fertile and
controversy
infinitely more profitable study of the
educational advantages which may be reaped
under the system of local control.
I think that we as legislators may claim
to be proud of the record of our legislation.
We all remember that the Parliament of
which we are Members was not of our
seeking. We accepted it as an assistance to
the British Government, and I think that
we may claim to have done our best to discharge those duties and those responsibilities which were laid upon us by the Act
of 1920. We can claim to have worked
whole-heartedly, and we can also congratulate the people whom we represent on the
manner in which they have accepted the
new conditions and worked whole-heartedly
for the benefit of the Province to which we
all belong. We know quite well that there
is a tendency throughout Ireland, both in
the North and in the South, to imagine
that they are against the Government, and
everyone feels that he is entitled as he is
entitled-to criticize the actions of his
Government. When Parliament legislated
for Ireland from Westminster the fulminations which were uttered by the North and
by the South at that Parliament did not
make very much difference. All that time
we prospered under the beneficent legislation which came from Westminster and
controlled us in Northern Ireland, and we
were inclined in our criticism to forget the
beneficent conditions under which we
existed. The Parliament at Westminster
delegated its authority to the Parliament
here, and this Parliament is naturally subject to the same forms of criticism. I am
not going to take any exception to the
criticism which has been levelled at this
10 MARCH 1925
Debate on Address.

Debate on Address.
have been discharging the duties which
have been allotted to them under the Act.
The remainder of the Act for the counties
comes into force very shortly, and it has
been a great satisfaction and pleasure to
me to realize the keen interest which has
been taken in the Act and in the duties
which will shortly be allocated to the local
authorities.
Parliament. I would only venture to say
that these critics should realize that we here
are faced with difficulties and dangers which
mean more to, and affect more, a small
Parliament than the great Parliament at
Westminster, and I think it is vital, no
matter what our smaller differences are,
that we should present a united front to
those enemies who threaten our existence as
a Province and who are not so well disposed
to us as we should like them to be. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear).
The Prime Minister has thought fit to ask
the Governor to grant a dissolution of this
Parliament, and I feel sure that all our
supporters throughout the Six Counties will
realize that he has taken a very wise step.
The Government certainly feels that it has,
if I may use the expression, exhausted its
mandate. We have been in office four years
and I think, as I have said at the
beginning of my speech, that we can claim
to have discharged to the best of our ability
those duties which were placed upon our
shoulders and which we told the people of
the Six Counties that we were willing and
anxious to discharge for their benefit. I do
feel that the Government should receive
another mandate from. the people of this
Province to assure them of their confidence
and also to strengthen the Government in
the difficulties with which they are faced at
the present moment. There is what is
known as the problem of the Boundary.
It is in the hands of a Judicial Committee,
and no one has any right-and I do not
feel that anyone in the Six Counties will do
so-to impugn that tribunal. It is composed of men whom we hold in respect. But
from our local and internal point of view it
is of vital importance, not only to the
Prime Minister and the Government, but
also to all of us, to prove to those of our
enemies who are anxious to show that we
are divided in support of our Prime
Minister and our Government, that we are
thoroughly united on the existence of this
Province and that we are prepared to stand
shoulder to shoulder behind the Prime
Minister in maintaining and asserting our
right to the Province to which we are all
proud to belong. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
non-
With reference to the work of the session,
the session will naturally be a very short
one in view of the forthcoming dissolution,
and the chief work of the session will be a
short and, I am glad to think, a
controversial Bill to amend the Education
Act. The Education Act has now been on
the Statute Book for some considerable
time, and I should like to take this opportunity of congratulating the Belfast
authority and the Derry authority for the
active and efficient manner in which they
During the time that the Act has been on
the Statute Book we have all naturally been
aware that there has been a certain amount of
controversy. Those of us who read the
newspapers have read reams of correspondence on the subject, and those letters have
been in some cases very forcible. They have
been signed by eminent citizens of the Six
Counties, and they have also been anonymous,
but they have one and all shown that a real
interest has been taken in this Act, and they
have embodied misgivings, which have been
honestly in the minds of the writers, that the
Act did not carry out the professions of myself and my colleagues of the Government. As
hon. Members know, the Education Act was
very carefully thought out. I had before me
the duty of drafting an Education Bill to deal
with education in the Six Counties. I had
the great difficulty before me of moving from
a centralised system of education to a decentralised system, and I knew the difficulties
which surrounded that change. I had the
opportunity of receiving a report from an
excellent and thoroughly efficient committee,
and on the report of that committee the Bill
was drawn. And while I may be said to have
been the promoter of the policy in conjunction
with my colleagues in the Government, I
received most admirable assistance from all
my subordinates in drawing up a measure of
a very far-reaching character.
I naturally was as much interested as anyone in the Six Counties that religious instruction should not be endangered or diminished
by the action of the Act which I was bringing
into force, and I felt that that Act had
covered the ground and that there was no
danger that the religious education of the
children should be neglected owing to some
provision in the Act and owing to anything
unforeseen that could have been included in
its Clauses. I am convinced that time would
have shown that I was right. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) But I knew the dangers which
naturally are fostered and which grow up by
differences of opinion and by misunderstanding, and I did my best by every public
utterance I could make on every occasion I
could make it to explain that those fears were
groundless, and that if there was misunderstanding I would do all in my power to
Debate on Address.
Debate on Address.
THE SENATE
[Marquess of Londonderry]
remove it. I felt that this controversy would
go on until the Act had been in force for
such time that everyone would see that their
fears were groundless. I can only say that
these last few days have been some of the
happiest days of my life because I have
realised that by acceding to the wishes of
those who really have at heart the religious
education of the children of this Province and
by making some alteration, which, in no sense,
infringed the principle of the Bill-which is
democratic control of education throughout
the Six Counties-we could come to an agreement, and this controversy, which naturally
presaged dangers and would be responsible
for differences in our midst, could be swept
away by the stroke of the pen. I have the
greatest satisfaction in feeling that on Thursday I shall be introducing what is called an
Amending Bill, which will once and for all, I
believe, remove these differences and misunderstandings and will unite in the common cause
of education throughout the Six Counties
those great forces of public opinion which
have been divided during the last few months,
and which, I believe, are now united in the
great object of benefiting the children of the
Six Counties. I do not think I need say
anything more on the subject on this occasion.
As I have said, I shall have an opportunity of
introducing an Amending Bill and we can look
forward to the fact that by our united
efforts in the House of Commons and in
the Senate we shall have working in our
midst an Act which will improve the opportunities of education and the building up of
character and youth throughout the Six
Counties, which was the duty imposed upon
Ministers in
us when we took office as
Ulster. I do not feel that on this occasion
there is anything more that I need say. I
sincerely hope that the election struggle
which will be upon us now in a very short
time will show, as I have very little doubt
it will show, that the Province is as united
now as it was four years ago, and that we
are determined to discharge those duties
and responsibilities which the Act of 1920
placed on our shoulders in a manner worthy
of ourselves and worthy of the people whom
we have the honour to represent.
MAXWELL:
I
Colonel PERCEVAL
should like to associate myself with the
Leader of the House in the words of condolence which he used in regard to the
death of Mr. Hotham. We all feel that we
have lost a friend, and our deepest sympathy
goes out to his relatives. I would like to
associate myself also with my hon. and
of the Address,
gallant Friend, the mover
in congratulating the Government on the
restoration of peace in this Province. I think
we all realize that they entered upon a very
difficult and arduous duty, and it is a great
satisfaction to all of us to feel that they
have performed it to the entire satisfaction
of the people of Ulster. With regard to
the reference in the Gracious Speech to the
Amending Bill to the Education Act I would
also like to congratulate the Government
on acceding to the widespread feeling that
the Act needed some slight amendment. I
think that what the people of Ulster want
is an amendment to assure religious instruction, and I have no doubt that the
Amending Bill which we have been promised
in the Gracious Speech from the Throne
will provide all that we could desire in
that respect.
Reference has been made to the Land
Purchase Bill which I hope will shortly
become law, although it does not come within our own jurisdiction. I hope it will prove
a benefit to the Province, and that it will
help to cement the good feeling that has
always existed between the farmer and the
landlords of Northern Ireland. I do not
think I have anything more to say, but I
should like to associate myself with the remarks of the Mover and Seconder of the
Address.
Mr. LESLIE: I desire to take this, the
first, opportunity of addressing you on the
occasion of our meeting for another Session
of this Parliament to join as far as I can
and in such words as I can command in the
tribute which the noble Marquess, the
Leader of the House, and other hon. Members have paid to the memory of our
friend, Mr. Hotham, and also to join in the
message of sympathetic condolence to which
the noble Marquess has referred. I cannot
but think that in Mr. Hotham we have lost
one whose assistance was always invaluable
to us. He possessed, as the Leader of the
House has said, a wealth of knowledge and
experience of technical matters which were
entirely beyond the ken of the ordinary
members of a new Parliament, and he was
gifted with that singular urbanity of
manner which enabled him to impart instruction and tender assistance which was
most acceptable, particularly perhaps-and
this is a matter with which I should
like specially to deal-when dealing with
the Private Bills which occasionally
before us, and
come
as to which we
know SO
was impressed
very little. It
on me only last Session during the passage
of the lengthy and somewhat complex
Londonderry Corporation Bill before the
Joint Committee how necessary expert
assistance was to Members of Parliament
and how fortunate we were in having Mr.
Hotham with us.
Passing from that, I should like to join

that they might possibly follow the
example of those countries, also of
small holdings, which are our keenest

help to bring to our small farmers the same
measure of comfort and the same degree of

in saying with what pleasure I listened to
the Mover and Seconder of the Address, and
how I hope we may have further
opportunities of hearing them in this
House. The Gracious Speech itself does
not, I think, provide very much food for
comment. It is necessarily short owing to
the circumstances in which it has been
prepared, and it consists for the most part
of a record and an appreciation of the
work which has been well done by this
Parliament. The matter that attracts most
attention of course is the Bill that will be
brought before us for amending the
Education Act of 1923. Having regard to
what I have already said myself in this
House and outside as regards that measure,
I cannot pretend to be of those who consider that the Act stood in need of amendment, but at the same time I welcome most
cordially any proposal brought before us by
the Minister of Education which will make
for immediate peace and tend towards the
efficient administration of the Act.
I am quite sure that the Bill, when we see
it, will be found to contain nothing discordant with the main principles on which
the Act was based, and will be strictly in
accordance with the somewhat limited
powers imposed
upon us by the very
constitution under which we sit. Satisfied
on this point, as I am sure we will be, I
cannot imagine that the consideration of
the measure will detain us any length of
time, and then we will be clear for the
dissolution. The dissolution is perhaps not
a matter in which this House is very
particularly concerned, because our term of
office is unaffected by a dissolution of
Parliament, but we cannot pass from it
without recording our conviction that the
Government which we support can face the
people of the Six Counties with a very clear
and conscientiously
conscience,
ask for
approval for what has been done and come
back again. I have much pleasure in
supporting the Address.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to say a
word in connection with that part of the
Gracious Speech which has to do with
agriculture, and I am glad to think it can
be recorded that the Government have
succeeded by their legislation in really
bringing about an improvement in agriculture. It is very easy for the Government
to wish agriculture well, but it is a rather
different story to interpret that wish into
action and in a short time to be able to
state that the industry involved has really
benefited by it. I sincerely trust that the
Minister of Agriculture and the Government will be encouraged by the success
which has already attended their efforts to
go further in this matter, and as we are a
country of small holdings, I would suggest
I should
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
just like to say how pleased I am to see that
reference is made in the Gracious Speech to
the Education Act, and that an Amending Bill
is being introduced. There has certainly been
a deep-rooted feeling, particularly in the
minds of the clergymen of the Protestant
churches, that there might be a danger of
religious instruction in the schools being discontinued, and I think that we can hardly
blame them for being anxious in that direction. I have not the same fear myself about
it, but at the same time it was there, and I
think it is right to do all we possibly can to
relieve it. Furthermore, I was present at
the County Grand Orange Lodge of Belfast
and I heard the discussion there regarding
this matter. While I have still the same
feelings with reference to the Bill-I consider
it is one of the best that could possibly be
introduced for the benefit of the people of this
part of the country, particularly the workingclass people-yet, at the same time, a large
number in connection with that great organization certainly had some fears regarding
religious instruction. I am very pleased
indeed to find that a satisfactory
arrangement has been come to and
that an Amending Bill is to be
introduced. When that is done I am
that those who criticized will be the very
best supporters the Government could possibly have in the future. Their discussion
and criticism of the Bill was certainly done
in a good-natured way. It was done for the
purpose of safeguarding the welfare of the
children and the people of Northern Ireland.
I was very sorry that any difference of
opinion did exist, and am glad to find now
that it has been satisfactorily arranged.
sure
The other part of the Speech from the
Throne deals more or less with a review of
the past, which appears to be very satisfactory. For instance, it is very pleasing to
find that we have law and order restored
in our midst, the interned prisoners released, and this Curfew removed, so that
we are altogether free. But on the other
hand it is rather deplorable to find that unemployment is still very prevalent in
Debate on Address.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
Northern Ireland. We were no doubt building on the hope that the linen industry was
likely to take a turn for the better
and that we should have a period
of good trade. Unfortunately in that
respect we have been disappointed, and in
spite of the relief schemes encouraged to
such an extent by the Government, we
have considerable distress in our midst.
I do hope everything possible will be done
to relieve it. I know that the Government
are deserving of a considerable amount of
credit for the anxious time they have given
to that question. I am perfectly satisfied
that they are doing all that they possibly
can. If it is possible to introduce any
other measure-I suppose it cannot be done at
present-but even if it were promised I
believe the people would be very well
satisfied. I am sure the present Government will have the entire support of the
greater portion of the people of Northern
Ireland, and, I do think, particularly of the
working classes. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That the said Address be
presented to his Grace by Privy Councillors
of Ireland.

beg to move that this House do now adjourn
until Thursday. The House will then meet
for formal business-to receive Bills that
may come down from the Commons. I Leg
to move.
