Charitable Loan Societies Bill (S).
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I desire to move the
Second Reading of the Charitable Loan
Societies Bill. I feel that this is a measure
which does not require many words from me
to introduce to hon. Members. The main
object of the Bill is to strengthen the control
of the Ministry of Finance over Loan Fund
Societies operating in Northern Ireland. As
hon. Members are aware the number of these
societies is rapidly diminishing, and, at the
present time, only the following are in active
operation, namely:-Kilrea, Coalisland and
Crichton (Lisnaskea). The position of the
four remaining societies is as follows:-
Antrim is about to be wound up; Londonderry and Drumquin have been wound up,
save for the disposal of surplus; Irvinestown
is being wound up compulsorily by order of
the Minister of Finance, owing to mismanagement.
""
The history of Loan Fund Societies, as hon.
Members are aware, dates more or less from
the year 1843. The effect of the Act passed
in that year was to empower persons to create
such societies for the purpose of lending out
industrious
sums not exceeding £10 to the
poor." The money lent was obtained either
by gifts or on debentures, and the societies
were supervised generally by a Loan Fund
Board in Dublin. During the earlier part of
the last fifty years, numerous societies were
created all over Ireland, and in a considerable
number of cases. the societies rapidly became
bankrupt through mismanagement, defalcations, and other irregularities, and their
numbers fell off gradually till the year 1914.
By that date the system, which at first was
intended to benefit in the main workmen in
towns, had almost, without exception, become agricultural in its scope, loans being
made for purchase of seed for crops, stock,
etc., but the borrowers were very often
A Comneither thrifty nor industrious.
mittee of Inquiry into Agricultural Credit
in Ireland-usually known as the Murnaghan
Committee set up by the Department of
Agriculture and Technical Instruction, in its
report published in 1914, reported, amongst
other things, on the Loan Fund System, and
condemned it as being out of date, owing in
the main to the facilities now offered to
small farmers by Joint Stock Banks. Owing
to the financial improvement of farmers
during the war, the number of borrowers in
societies was further reduced and has never
since reached any considerable dimensions.
It might be considered that legislation for
the purpose of improving control over some
three or four societies is superfluous, but the
difficulty is that the only other solution
would be to wind up societies compulsorily,
and hon. Members will realise that this
could not be done without paying compensation.
The Bill also proposes to make provision in
regard to the disposal of surplus funds of
Societies or of the funds of Societies voluntarily wound up, including power to grant
pensions or gratuities to officers of such
Societies. Annual profits can under existing
legislation be handed over to local charities
without consultation with the Ministry of
Finance, while surplus funds or funds available on dissolution can be similarly disposed
of, but only with the consent of the Ministry
of Finance. The Agricultural Credit Committee's Report of 1914 was strongly opposed
to such a mode of treating profits or surplus
funds, and considered that they should be
Second Reading. 30
Charitable Loan Societies Bill-
21 APRIL 1925
used in some manner more in accord with the
objects of the Societies which had brought
such funds together.
The Bill, therefore, while strengthening the
existing powers of the Ministry of Finance
in regard to approving the disposal of funds
for local charities, also provides that funds,
which on dissolution of a Society revert to
the Ministry of Finance, may be invested and
the income utilised for making advances to
farmers in necessitous circumstances. The
measure cannot be described as a very large
one and I shall be very happy to answer any
questions which hon. Members may desire to
ask me. I have great pleasure in moving the
Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure that no one will
be disposed to quarrel with anything that is
included in this Bill, which might, perhaps
be described as a measure of adaptation
rather than anything else. I confess that
when I first saw it I rather wondered why
the opportunity had not been taken to consolidate the law dealing with the subject, but
it is evident from what the noble Marquess
has told us that the matter is scarcely worth
dealing with in that way. However, I think
we might perhaps take this opportunity of
getting rid of the small Statute which is referred to in an early section of the Bill, and
at a later stage I will suggest that course.
The most interesting thing, as I see it, in
the Bill is the proposal in the Third Section,
which is new, and apparently, from what the
noble Marquess has said, based on that report of 1914, to allow these funds to be used
for the relief of those engaged in agriculture.
1 venture to think that perhaps such a section as that which is before us will not be as
convenient a method of carrying out our
intention as might be devised. I think there
are rather too many adjectives in it and we
may find ourselves a little more restricted
than is desired. Therefore again, at a later
stage, I will ask the House to re-consider that
matter. Those are matters of detail, and
there is really nothing in the principle of the
Bill that seems to call for any further
observation.
Mr. GREER: I think that if we can in any
way strengthen these Charitable Loan Acts
we ought to do it, and it would be a very
good thing if their object were better known
in the country than it is. I believe that in
Northern Ireland we had very few of these
Loan Societies started, but in the South and
West of Ireland very many of them were
established and if they had only been
managed properly I think they would have
been of great benefit to the country. It
would be a good thing if the small farmer
knew he could apply for a loan of £10 to put
manure into his land, or to get a better
quality of seed to put into the ground.
He
He
could easily repay that money from the
proceeds of the crop. The only security he
has to give is a Promissory Note in a special
form which I think would be supplied by
the Ministry of Finance for a penny.
would have to get a surety, and in that way
he could get a loan up to £10 to use profitably in the cultivation of his farm. I am
quite sure that if the number of small farmers in Northern Ireland were aware that
they had that power they would make use of
it. I therefore welcome this Bill and I
think it is a step in the right direction. At
the same time it seems to me that the Government have been some time in becoming alive
to the fact that it was necessary. They have
had the power since 1922 and they have only
now decided to use it. I strongly approve of
the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Thursday next.
Public Health (Milk Regulations)
Bill (S)
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The Bill which I have the honour of
recommending the House to give a Second
Reading to provides for the introduction in
Northern Ireland of regulations regarding
the dried and condensed milk trade similar
to those which are in force in Great Britain
in consequence of the recent amendments
made by the Milk and Dairies (Amendment)
Act of 1922, Section 8 of which imposes upon
the Minister of Health the duty of making
regulations under the Public Health (Regulations as to Food) Act, 1907, for the prevention of danger arising to public health
from the importation of milk intended for
sale for human consumption, or for use in
the manufacture of products for human consumption," and amends the Public Health
Act of 1907 in a similar way to that which
the Government is proposing to adopt in the
present Measure.
"
In this Act we empower our Minister of
Home Affairs, who is responsible for public
health in Northern Ireland, to make regulations for the prevention of danger arising to
public health from the preparation, storage
and distribution of milk intended for sale,
instead of its importation as in the British
Act, inasmuch as we have not the power to
deal with the importation of articles as they
have in Great Britain.
If we here do not give our Minister of Home
Affairs the power and duty to make regulations governing the sale of milk of this
description in Northern Ireland Corresponding with those enjoyed and imposed upon
the Ministry of Health in Great Britain
31 Charitable Loan Societies Bill- THE SENATE.

Second Reading.
[Marquess of Londonderry]
there is grave danger that Northern Ireland
would become the dumping ground of all milk
of this description which did not meet with
British standards under the Ministry of
Health regulations, which would be a most
undesirable state of affairs. I do not think
it is necessary for me to say anything more
on this Bill, but I think the House will agree
with me that the health of our people is as
dear to our Parliament as is the health of
the British people to the Parliament at Westminster, and I do not anticipate any opposition to a measure which simply operates to
give the Minister of Home Affairs the power
to ensure that the standard of articles sold
in our markets shall be equal to the standard
required elsewhere in the United Kingdom.
I beg to move.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I am
anxious to know if the Government will
agree to insert in this Bill a clause that will
provide for cream, and at the same time define what cream is. Some time ago I had
occasion to ask an Inspector of Food about
cream, and he told me that it was greatly
adulterated, but that there was no way in
which they could meet it. There was no
legal definition as to what cream is, and it
would be almost impossible to deal with that
matter. I think that anything that can be'
done for the health of the people should be
done, and in the case of milk produce the
people should at least know that they are
getting what they are paying for. In the
case of cream they should know that it is
the fat from the milk that they are getting.
I should be very pleased if the Government
would look into this question with the object
of dealing with cream, which I believe is not
provided for in any of the Acts.
Mr. BARBOUR: I regret to have to say
that I have read this Bill with the greatest
apprehension. I have been for a long time
now trying to promulgate in Ireland the
means by which our Continental competitors
have so successfully helped to build up their
present position in the British market. As
I read the Bill it is mainly concerned with
dried milk and skimmed milk, and may I
say that I and the organisation with which
I have been identified are second to none in
our anxiety that the consumers of our agricultural produce should have that produce
in its purest form and put before them in the
most attractive way. I claim on behalf of
our organisation that we have played our
part in bringing that about. I think we
may recognise that as far as Northern Ireland is concerned we need not be at all
apprehensive about any importation of
skimmed or separated milk. In fact, I think
the farmers of Northern Ireland would be
only too glad to get rid of it, and I do not
think that we as a producing country need be
at all afraid of the English or Continental
farmer attempting to send to Ireland
skimmed or separated milk. If that could
be left out of the Bill one of the objections
I have to it would be removed.
I would like to point out that we have in
Northern Ireland between 10,000 and 12,000
farmers who are suppliers to creameries and
who under the Bill as it stands at present
would be immediately affected by any Regulations drawn up by the Ministry of Home
Affairs in connection with skimmed or
the
separated milk. Anyone who knows
business can see the difficulties. A farmer
delivers his milk to the creamery and the
butter fat is taken from it. He takes home the
skimmed milk in the same receptacle that he
delivered the milk in. While one should give
credit to the Minister for his desire to aid
rather than to impede the industry it is possible that some Regulation might be framed
that would affect the very large number of
suppliers to our creameries. Every Member
in this House knows that at present agriculture is in an extremely depressed condition.
It was with alarm that I heard only this
morning that in some districts in County
Fermanagh that are usually progressive and
very successful the farmer was so hard put to
that this season not two tons of artificial
manures have been sold. One has got to
remember that the farmer has had two bad
seasons. This creamery industry which may be
affected so seriously by this Bill has the ordinary difficulties that any commercial enterprise has to meet, but at this juncture there
is a possibility of legislation being passed
which will add considerably to the difficulties
of those people, and the farmer is not in a
position financially or mentally to contemplate
with a light mind any legislation which would
empower the Ministry of Home Affairs to draw
up Regulations which would add in any way
to the difficulties of a business which, at the
moment, is beset with the gravest difficulties,
and has just managed to struggle through two
very adverse seasons.
The organisation I know so well is not
anxious to put any obstacle in the way of
legislation that will help. We are anxious to
help and we will be quite willing to try to
help to solve the difficulty the Bill is intended
to meet. But one need not regard as a
serious possibility the importation of skimmed
or separated milk into Northern Ireland. If
an opportunity were given to us to help to
solve this problem we would put our abilities,
such as they are, freely at the disposal of any
Ministry concerned. As the Bill stands I
regret that I must move its rejection.
Mr. GREER: I should like to ask the
Noble Viscount, who introduced this Bill,
33 Charitable Loan Societies Bill--
Second Reading. 34
21 APRIL 1925
whether there is any similar Clause in the
legislation in England. I believe there is, but
I am not quite certain. If there is such a
Clause in the English Act I think we are
bound to act in conformity with it, because the
Amending Act of 1896 says that in the making
of Regulations regard should be made to the
expediency of uniformity in practice. Therefore, if there is a similar Clause in England
I think we ought to have it in this Act. At
any rate I consider that the Clause is a good
one, and notwithstanding the trepidation of
Senator Barbour I do not think that any
Regulations that could be made under the Act
could be very expensive to the farmers. I am
quite sure that the Minister of Home Affairs
will be as anxious to consider the interests of
the farmers as anybody else. The Measure
will be to the advantage of the whole body of
the people of this Province. We do not want
to be the dumping ground of manufactures
that cannot be sold in England, and I hope
the Bill will pass.
Mr. BARBOUR : With the permission of
the House, and with your permission, Mr.
Deputy Speaker, I would like to withdraw my
Motion to reject the Bill, and to move instead
to delete in the Motion the word "now
(Bill be now), and to add at the end of the
Motion the words, this day Six Months."
""

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: With regard to the Amendment
which has been moved, I must oppose it on
behalf of the Government. Senator Barbour,
who moved the Amendment, seems to be under
the impression that the Regulations that may
be made by the Minister of Home Affairs
under this Bill may cause a lot of trouble
and inconvenience, and possibly expense to the
members of the society in which he is so
interested, and to which he has given
such great attention for SO many
years. This Bill gives to the Minister power
to make Regulations and I feel sure that he
will not go out of his way to make Regulations that will in any way injure or destroy
an industry which is of such vast importance
to Northern Ireland. After all, the most
important thing we have got to consider, as
I think Senator Barbour will himself admit,
is the public health, and this Bill is for the
purpose of creating a high standard of milk.
It is therefore in the interests of the members of the Society which the hon. Member
(Senator Barbour) has done so much to foster,
and to bring to its present successful
position.
As regards Senator Greer's question, there
is a similar Clause in the English Bill.
Therefore I hope the House will give this
Bill a Second Reading and will not accept the
Amendment put forward by Senator Barbour.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I have
listened very attentively to the discussion on
this Bill, and as one who is not very well
acquainted with country life I have to judge
the Bill by its effect on the industrial population. So far as I am concerned I will give
it my support because it protects the consumer from being imposed upon. There is
no doubt that in the city of Belfast even
skimmed milk is being sold as pure milk.
By this Bill it will become necessary that it
should be labelled, so that it will be known
what it is. I support the Bill in the interests
of the poor and the working-classes, who are
the chief consumers of the articles dealt with
in the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not intend to say anything while this measure was before the
House to-day, but having regard to the
Amendment moved by Senator Barbour it
does occur to me that I might perhaps take
it upon myself to suggest to Senator Barbour
that there will be subsequent stages for the
consideration f the Bill, and that if he were
to withdraw his Amendment that the Bill be
read a Second time this day six months it
might be possible for him on the Committee
stage to propose some amendment to the
effect that Regulations made under the Bill
should be submitted to the House. That
would serve as a vehicle for further discussion. It does occur to me that that is a
possible way of dealing with the problem
which really arises out of the Regulations
rather than out of the principle of the Bill.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
My hon. Friend, as he always does, tries to
throw oil on the troubled waters, and I feel
grateful to him for the line he has taken. I
would urge the hon. Senator (Senator Barbour) to follow the course Senator Leslie has
indicated. He has moved an Amendment
which in Parliamentary language virtually
amounts to the rejection of the Bill, but I
hardly think it is his desire that the whole
Bill should be rejected. I am not sure
whether the hon. Senator who moved the rejection of the measure is aware that this Bill
merely empowers the Minister of Home
Affairs to make Regulations and naturally
they will come under the purview of Parliament and can be dealt with as the situation
arises. With reference to the Society in
which the hon. Senator takes so prominent
a part I am sure he will agree with me that
the great difficulty which we in this country
have to meet is the inequalities of our farm
produce. That brings us back to the question
of grading in which other countries are
superior to us, and for that reason they are
more successful than we in this country are
in the sale of their farm produce.
B
THE SENATE.

Old Age Pensions.
35 Old Age Pensions.
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
I feel that we should apply ourselves to
regularizing as far as we can the marketing
of our agricultural produce, and this is a
matter which touches very closely the health
of the people. There is no doubt that people
are very anxious to know what they are
buying. My hon. Friend is quite right when
he calls upon us to recognise the condition
in which agriculture is at the present time
and the fact that any further hampering restrictions might do grave injury to the industry. But I am inclined to think that the
apprehension that exists in Senator Barbour's
mind is somewhat magnified by the difficulties
through which he naturally considers the
When the Act
farming industry is passing.
for
some
has been in operation
time I think he will realise that there
are no hampering restrictions and that
the Act is merely in the interests of
the great community we are all anxious to
serve. He may also find that the Bill will
give the people greater confidence in the
agricultural community than they may have
at the present moment because of the fact
that they do not know what they are purchasing. If the hon. Members will take steps
to put forward his opinions on the Committee stage of the Bill I think his purpose
will be as well served as they would be by his
Amendment, the Parliamentary equivalent
of which is the rejection of the Bill. The
hon.
Samuel
(Senator
Senator opposite
Cunningham) referred to the desirability of
introducing in the Bill a clause dealing with
cream. I am sure the Noble Viscount who
introduced the measure will be happy to consider between now and the Committee stage
not that
the question as to whether or
matter comes within the purview of this Bill.
Senator BARBOUR: By leave of the
House I will withdraw my Amendment.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

Bill committed to a Committee of the whole

Old Age Pensions
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I beg to
ask the Leader of the House, how far the
administration of Old Age Pensions in
Northern Ireland differs from that in Great
Britain; and whether he will state what
steps, if any, the Government propose to take
to remove differences of administration.
I think I should ask the House to excuse
me for again bringing this question before it
when it was discussed at the last sitting.
Unfortunately on that occasion the Leader
of the House did not make a statement on
he did not
We
and
should
give
am
I
us
most
have
his
I
and
the matter
information,
any
anxious that
views before the matter is dropped.
may say that on questions such as this the
Government has always been inclined to
treat the workers of Northern Ireland as
favourably as the workers are treated else
where. They have always been anxious to
march side by side with the legislation that
has been passed by the Imperial Government,
and I cannot understand why they desire to
make an exception in the case of Old Age
Pensions. Those who are particularly
affected by the Disqualification Clause in the
Old Age Pensions Bill are the veterans from
the workshops, old trade union members,
men who, I believe, have rendered to their
country services as valuable as any rendered
on the field of battle, and I think further
that their services have been more beneficial
to the country than have the services of any
other part of the community. They are
people who have worked most diligently and
industriously in the workshop practically
during their whole lives, very often in very
monotonous occupations, and they generally
paid a contribution to their Trade Union to
provide against sickness, unemployment, and
old age. The people I am referring to were
thrifty and industrious, and because they
tried to make some provision for themselves
in their old age they are deprived of the
benefits of the old-age pension. At least,
they are disqualified to some extent.
On the other hand, take the position of
the person who only occasionally works and
who has not been at all anxious to provide
for himself out of his earnings. When he
arrives at the age of 70 the State seems to
say to him, "Come along, you good and
faithful servant, you have been particularly careful to protect your health.
You have not been too fond of work
and you have been very anxious to look
after your health. You have arrived at the
age of 70 when other fools who worked so
The
hard have fallen out of the race."
State gives such a man the whole benefit of
the Old Age Pension. It is certainly a
peculiar position. It is a discouragement to
thrift, and something should certainly be
done in the matter.
It was said here on the last occasion the
matter was discussed that families should provide for their parents, and that the latter
should not look to the old-age pension to
keep them in their old age. Anyone who
knows anything about the industrial position
at the present time will admit that in the
case of those who have a family it is difficult
to provide for themselves, apart altogether
from their parents, so that can be left out of
Old Age Pensions.
Old Age Pensions.
21 APRIL 1925
the question. In my own case a boy of mine
who has just finished his apprenticeship has
had to take his ticket to America where he
will have a better chance of obtaining employment than he would have here. One does
not expect much assistance in a case of that
kind. It has been said that the removal of
the Disqualification Clause would cost the
Province a considerable amount of money,
but I think the cost has been exaggerated.
The people I am referring to are trade
unionists, and in the case of one trade union
of which I am a member, the Amalgamated
Engineering Union, members are entitled to
superannuation of 14s a week, but it is
definitely laid down in the Rules of that
Society that when a man arrives at the age
of 70 he must forego 4s, and be satisfied with
10s from the Society. Therefore, by removing
the thrift disqualification the loss would fall
on the trade unions. I am most anxious that
the Government should understand the
position thoroughly. It is not only the oldage pensioners who are dissatisfied with the
present position of affairs, but the trade
unions are also very much dissatisfied.
Therefore, I would like the Noble Marquess
to use his persuasive powers with his colleagues in the Cabinet to bring the Old Age
Pension Act into line with the law in Great
Britain, and thus give the people of Northern
Ireland the same privileges as are enjoyed by
the people across the water. I do claim that
in Northern Ireland you have the finest class
of workman that you can find in any part of
the United Kingdom, and you will not find
a more loyal class of workman anywhere. I
was surprised to learn the other day that in
Belfast we have one of the strongest Trade
Union centres in the United Kingdom. Even
on the Red Clyde, Trade Unionism is in a
very bad state. Therefore you must not
think that all those who are trade unionists
are Communists or Bolshevists. In Belfast
we have a particularly loyal class of working
man and it is up to the Government to do all
they can to encourage thrift. I have heard
it stated by Socialists at our delegate
meetings that a thrifty and industrious
person is a handicap on progress. They have
condemned a man who owns his own house
because they think he becomes satisfied with
his lot and will not agitate in the future and
that he is therefore a hindrance. If the
Government want to encourage thrift they
cannot do better than show their interest
in the old-age pensioners, and particularly
those who have made some provision for
their old age by contributing for forty or
fifty years to the Trade Union. I hope the
reply of the Noble Marquess to my question
will be a favourable one.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
do not in any sense regret the question
which the hon. Member has put down for the
purpose of eliciting an answer from the
Government, and, as he says, a favourable
answer. It is naturally my duty to tell the
House all I possibly can on this subject, and
to enlighten the Members as to the difficulties which we may find ourselves in from
time to time in regard to the financial
situation, which, as everybody knows, is not
a very favourable
The information
asked for in the question is,
one.
How far the administration of Old Age
Pensions in Northern Ireland differs from
that in Great Britain; and whether he will
state what steps, if any, the Government
propose to take to remove differences of
administration.
I think hon. Members are fully aware of
the working of the Old Age Pension Act, but
perhaps it would be as well if I went briefly
through its provisions. The principal Act
providing for Old Age Pensions is the Old
Age Pension Act of 1908. Section 2 of the
principal Act lays down three statutory
conditions for the receipt of an Old Age
Pension. The first is that the applicant
be 70 years of age, and this is a provision
that I am not sure everybody in Northern
Ireland is aware of, judging by the Amendment that was moved the other day in
another place. I gathered from the debate on
that Amendment that it was not fully
realised that the qualifying age is 70, and
that it has not been reduced to 60 in
England. Therefore we stand on exactly
the same footing as they do in England on
that point. The other provisions are:-
(2) British subject, and resident in the
United Kingdom for twenty years and upwards previously to the receipt of pension.
(3) The person must satisfy the Pension
Authorities that his yearly means as calculated under this Act, do not exceed
£31 10s Od
The special provision laid down by
Section 4 of the Act for calculating the
means of a person for the purposes of the
Act is, that account shall be taken:
(1) of the cash income, exclusive of Old
Age Pension, which he might reasonably
expect to receive during the next year.
(2) of the yearly value of the use or enjoyment by himself of property belonging
to him;
(3) of the income which he might derive
from property if it were properly invested
or used, even though it were not so invested
or used;
(4) of the yearly value of any benefit or
privilege enjoyed by him.
Old Age Pensions.
Old Age Pensions.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
The Schedule to the Act gave the maximum
rate of pension of five shillings a week where
the yearly means of the pensioner under the
Act did not exceed £21. The rate of pension
dropped by one shilling a week with each
£2 12s 6d of additional yearly means in the
pensioner's possession until it vanished altogether where the yearly means of the
pensioner exceeded £31 10s Od.
The hon. Member has alluded to this point.
He says it is a deterrent to thrift, and with
that I fully agree. It is the desire of us all
that those pensioners who have been so
thrifty as to be able to save a certain sum
of money should not be penalised, by not
being put on the same footing as those who in
the days of their industry have not been so
thrifty and perhaps have not worked half
so well.
The amending legislation of 1919 increased
the maximum pension rate from five shillings
per week to ten shillings per week and made
important changes in the calculation of
means which increased the maximum yearly
means of the pensioner to qualify for the
maximum pension to £26 5s 0d, and raised
the disqualification for a pension to a yearly
income of £49 17s 6d.
was remeans
In Great Britain, the Old Age Pensions
Acts were further amended by the Old Age
Pensions Act of last year by which the socalled "thrift disqualification"
moved. That Act enacted that
derived from any source other than earnings
should be excluded from calculations in so far
as they did not exceed £39 per annum in the
case of a single person, or £78 per annum in
the case of a married person. The effect was
clearly laid down in the memorandum which
was presented to Parliament explaining the
financial resolution upon which the Bill was
based.
The Government of Northern Ireland
have, with much regret, not been able so far
to adopt the principle of the amendment
introduced by the Act of the Imperial Parliament. As hon. Members are aware, this
subject was discussed very fully in another
place on Thursday last, when my right hon.
Friend the Minister of Finance (Mr. Pollock)
stated that, while the Government had every
sympathy with the old people and have no
desire that they should be placed at any dis.
advantage as compared with the Old Age
Pensioners of Great Britain, "in the present
condition of our finances, when no definite
arrangement has been made with the Imperial Government in regard to our position
in relation to expenditure of this character,
no Minister of Finance could think of plunging the Province into debt to the extent of
£350,000 a year, even assuming the first part
of the scheme only, until he knew with some
definiteness what his resources were."
I am not sure that hon. Members are fully
aware of the negotiations that have been
continuously in progress for a very long time,
in fact ever since our Government was set
up, with regard to our financial relations
with Great Britain. The Colwyn Committee has sat for a considerable time, and
the Report is in the hands of the Minister,
but we
are not yet in a position to say
exactly what the financial relations will be
between Great Britain and Northern Ireland. Until we know definitely where we
are, it is quite impossible for the Government to burden Northern Ireland with a
large amount of debt. The hon. Member
suggests that perhaps we are not fully aware
of the actual cost which this proposal would
impose on the finances of Northern Ireland.
The sum is £350,000, and although the
Minister of Finance was able to show a
surplus at the end of the financial year, that
surplus would not in any sense meet the demand which would be made
upon it by
placing Northern Ireland on exactly the
same footing as the Imperial Government.
I am not saying that we are not fully in
sympathy with the speech which the hon.
Member has made. He has put forward a
very good case indeed. He has not, however,
mentioned the financial position in which we
find ourselves and in which the Imperial
Government finds itself across the water.
He has made some allusion to the Labour
Party, but he did not state that those rebates
which were given were given by the Labour
Party. Some of us who may, perhaps, be regarded as political partisans think the Labour
Party, when they came into power, were not
very mindful of the future finances of the
country, because they were more anxious to
see how much support they could gain by
lavishly disposing of the funds of the
country.
While I should be the last person to strike
a pessimistic note in the present condition
in which we find ourselves, I do not see
clearly a way in which this country is going
continuously to pay higher old-age pensions
or benefit in unemployment. Certainly it is
a question to which we should devote ourselves, and if the Imperial Government in
their wisdom are increasing expenditure
under this head, the time may come when we
in Northern Ireland may not be able to do
exactly the same thing. When hon. Members come forward and almost upbraid-I
will not say upbraid, but suggest that we
are falling behind in our duty by not maintaining these services on exactly the same
scale as they are maintained on the other
side I would ask them to remember our
financial difficulties, and to remember that
the Government are anxious that the scale
of existence in this country should not fall
Old Age Pensions.
Adjournment.
21 APRIL 1925
But
behind the scale across the water.
when we contemplate so cloudy an outlook as
regards our commercial prosperity, it is the
duty of all of us to consider what we can do
to maintain the position as it is at the
present moment.
The hon. Member will not feel that I have
been able to give him a very favourable
answer to his question, but I can assure him
that the Government are not behind him in
any way in the desire to place this Province
on exactly the same level as they are on in
Great Britain. However, until our financial
relations with Great Britain are fully
established to the complete understanding of
all parties concerned, I think the Minister
of Finance would be incurring a responsibility he is not entitled to incur by running
the risk of imposing upon our finances a
burden which in the future we may not be
able to bear. I hope this answer will more
or less satisfy the hon. Member, of whose
ideas I am well aware. I know exactly the
questions he is asked by those he meets
continually, and I am also fully confident
that he fights the battle of economy on the
part of the Government better and probably
far more eloquently than I could do it from
this box. That is the reason why I am so
glad he has come to the Senate, of which he
is so distinguished a Member, and put the
case of the old-age pensioners as clearly
and forcibly as he could. I can assure him
that this is a question that has not been lost
sight of, and I hope that when he asks it
again I shall be in a position to give him a
far more favourable answer than I can give
him now.
SUMMARY JURISDICTION (MARRIED

beg to move that this House do now adjourn.

Members that we should meet again on
Thursday, when we will proceed with the
Committee stage of the Bills now before the
House.

o'clock until Thursday next.

of the principal Regulations for the purpose
of clarifying the provisions of paragraphs
(1) and (2) of the aforesaid Articles and extending the scope of the additional benefits
provided for in the aforementioned Schedule.

MESSAGE FROM THE GOVERNOR.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): I have received from
his Grace the Governor, the following
Message:-
I have received with great satisfaction
the message of thanks for the Speech with
which I have opened the present Session of
Parliament.
ABERCORN.
Charitable Loan Societies Bill (S).
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.--(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)

of

Mr. LESLIE: I desire to take this opportunity of mentioning the Statute which is
referred to in paragraph (c) of Sub-Clause 2
of Clause 1. The Statute is the Charitable
Loan Societies Amendment Bill, 1872. When
speaking on the Second Reading of this Bill
I suggested that we might perhaps take the
opportunity, for the sake of clearing the
Statute Book, so far as Northern Ireland is
concerned, of repealing this little Measure
which, as it now stands, contains nothing
but permission to increase the charge from a
penny to twopence. Accordingly, I put a
notice down on the Paper, and it appears
to-day, but since I put the notice on
the Paper, it has been pointed out to
me that the Statute, although obsolescent,
will not be completely spent until the
Ministry of Finance have made the Regulations in accordance with Sub-Clause 2 of this
Bill. Consequently, it would not be desirable
to repeal this small Statute at the moment,
and, therefore, I do not propose to move the
second Amendment that stands on the Paper
in my name.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Amendments of Charitable
Loan Societies Acts and extension of time
for taking proceedings thereunder), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Investment and application of
surplus funds and property).
(3) The Ministry of Finance, on being
satisfied after consultation with the
Ministry of Agriculture for Northern
Ireland that acute distress exists in any
agricultural district in Northern Ireland,
may employ any such funds, property or
investments representing the same, and any
income arising therefrom, in making, in
accordance with a scheme to be framed
after consultation as aforesaid, advances to
farmers in necessitous circumstances
residing in such district; and such scheme
may provide for the payment of the
advances to the said Ministry of Agriculture
to be applied by way of loans for the
benefit of such farmers.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
45 Charitable Loan Societies Bill_-_ 23 APRIL 1925

Committee.
Clause 3, page 5, lines 32 to 43, leave out
sub-section (3) and insert the following new
sub-section-
(3) The Ministry of Finance may employ
any such funds, property or investments
representing the same, and any income
arising therefrom, in making, in accordance with a scheme to be framed after consultation with the Ministry of Agriculture
for Northern Ireland, advances to or for
the benefit of any persons residing on and
in occupation of agricultural holdings, in
any rural district in Northern Ireland, and
such scheme may provide for the payment
of such advances to the said Ministry of
Agriculture, to be applied subject to such
conditions as the Ministry of Finance considers expedient by way of loans to or for
the benefit of any such persons.
Mr. LESLIE: With reference to Sub-
Clause 3 of Clause 3, I ventured to point out
when the Bill was before us for Second Reading that some difficulty might arise in
practice if we left the Sub-Clause exactly as
it came before us in the first instance. I
thought then, as indeed I think now, that
some of the words used were perhaps not
very happily chosen to effect the purpose for
which they were intended: that there was
rather too much qualification of the powers
proposed to be conferred on the Ministry, and
that those qualifications might be the cause
of difficulties in the future, either because in
the case succeeding the first case the question
of precedent would arise, or because dissatisfaction might be caused in cases where
it was necessary to decline assistance.
The points to which I took particular
objection were the reference to "acute distress," the term "agricultural district," the
use of the word "farmers," and the phrase
"necessitous circumstances." Therefore I
thought that the simplest method to adopt
was to put a new sub-clause on the Paper. I
accordingly did so, and that is the subclause I desire to bring before the Committee this afternoon. I think it may well
be that the Government, being particularly
anxious in this new sub-clause to keep in
touch with the spirit of the old legislation,
may object to leaving out all reference to
distress, and they may perhaps be disposed
to bring up their own amendment which will
Ideal with this point.
It is, of course, extremely important that
it should be made clear that this sub-clause
is not entirely novel. It is a development of
the principle of the older Act rather than a
complete departure from it, and it may very
well be that in order to keep that before the
mind of anybody having occasion to refer to
the Act it might be thought better to introduce some words which in the sub-clause as
it stands are absent. I think that if we get
something to assure us that the intention is
to use the clause for Rural Districts, and not
to impose any undue hardship in definition,
everything I desire to achieve will be effected,
and in that case I may desire to ask permission to withdraw the Amendment. For
the moment, of course, I move the Amendment as it stands.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: 1
am happy to say that there is no point of disagreement between the hon. Member who has
moved the Amendment and the Government
on this subject. He has alluded to the point
that we rather hold to the phraseology that
we have used in the Bill. It is my intention
to move various Amendments to that subclause which virtually carry out the object
the hon. Member has in view. I do not think
there is any need for me to say any more on
this sub-clause beyond repeating that there
is no difference of opinion between the hon.
Member and myself, and if the hon. Member
can see his way to withdraw the
Amendment it will be in the interests of the
progress of the Measure.
Mr. LESLIE I hope the Committee will
permit me to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
In pursuance of the few remarks I have
already made, I beg to move, in Clause 3,
page 5, line 34, to leave out the word "acute"
(that acute distress exists).
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The next Amendment I have to move in
this Clause is on page 5, lines 34 and 35, to
leave out the word "agricultural" (in any
agricultural district), and to insert instead
thereof the word "rural."
Amendment agreed to.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:

of persons residing on or in occupation of
agricultural holdings."
Mr. DICKSON: With regard to the term
"agricultural holdings," I would
like a
definition of the size of the holdings.
Mr. SPEAKER: We have not yet reached
that point. The question is that certain
words be left out.

CLAUSE 1. (Regulations of Ministry of
Home Affairs as to milk, 7 Edw. 7. c. 32).
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: When this Bill was given a Second
Reading in this House the other day Senator Samuel Cunningham raised a question
as to whether it would be possible to introduce a Clause empowering the Ministry of
Home Affairs to define and to make
Regulations regarding cream. I regret that
the hon. Senator is not in his place to-day,
but I will tell the House that this matter
comes under the Sale of Food and Drugs At
1899. Under that Act the Minister of Agriculture has full power to make Regulations
regarding Cream, and it is not in any way
affected by this Bill.
On the Second Reading Senator Barbour
moved the rejection of the Bill owing to his
fear that the Measure would have a serious
effect on skimmed and separated milk. My
information from the Ministry of Home
Affairs is that the Bill refers solely to condensed and dried milk, but I can give the
hon. Senator the assurance that if the wording of the Bill can be taken to refer to
skimmed or separated milk any Regulations
the Ministry of Home Affairs may bring in
under the Bill will refer solely to condensed
and dried milk and not to skimmed or
separated milk. I hope that will satisfy the
hon. Member.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Short title) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without Amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment to the
House.

CLAUSE 1 (Regulations of Ministry of
Home Affairs as to milk. 7. Edw. 7, C. 32).
*
(d) provide for the manner in which any
tin or other receptacle containing dried,
condensed, skimmed or separated milk is
to be labelled or marked, and prescribe
the minimum percentages of milk fat and
milk solids in dried or condensed milks.
Mr. BARBOUR: I beg to move, in Clause
1, page 2, line 2, after the word "dried "
(other receptacle containing dried) to insert
the word "or." We have been told that this
Bill seeks to take power to deal with dried
and condensed milk only, and I venture to
suggest that it would be a wise change if in
the Bill, as we finally pass it, we confined
the interests of the Bill to dried or condensed
milk. I respectfully submit that the Bill as
at present worded can be interpreted to cover
the separated or skimmed milk, which has
to be handled so largely in connection with
the creameries throughout the country.
have read the Bill and the other Bill to
which it is to be an addendum, and I confess
that in the way I read it I can see that milk
taken away from the creamery after separation would be affected by the measure. Considering that the Government only wish to
deal with dried or condensed milk I think it
is a pity that the door should be left so wide
open that an entirely different kind of milk
should come within the purview of the Bill.
I, therefore, beg to move the Amendment
standing in my name.
I
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am obliged to the hon. Senator for
what he has said, and on behalf of the
Government I am perfectly prepared to
accept his Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. BARBOUR: I beg to move in Clause 1,
page 2, line 3, in paragraph (d), to leave out
the words skimmed or separated."

VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: On behalf of the Government I
accept that Amendment.

Rules Publication Bill (S).
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: This Bill, the Second Reading of
which I have the honour to move in the
Senate this afternoon, is designed, in the
first place, to remove certain legal and
technical difficulties which have arisen in
the application of the Rules Publication
Act of 1893 in Northern Ireland, and, in the
second place, to amend the law in regard to
the publication of statutory rules and orders
in the light of the experience which has
been gained during a period of more than
thirty years under the system established
by the Rules Publication Act which I have
just mentioned.
Further, a Clause, Clause 4, which I shall
come to later on, is included which, while
amending the law in regard to the laying
of statutory rules and orders before Parliament, gives ample time for either House of
Parliament to examine and deal with any
rule or order lying before it, and avoids
much unnecessary delay which experience
of the working of the Parliamentary
machine in Northern Ireland has shown may
occur under the law as it now stands.
I may venture to say in relation to modern
legislation, that perhaps nothing has been
more remarkable than its tendency to provide for the application of the general
principles laid down in an Act of Parliament by the drawing up of rules and orders
by the administrative departments con
cerned in order to fulfil the purpose of a
particular statute. It has therefore beco.e increasingly important that the
machinery governing the whole procedure in
regard to the publication and editing of
these rules and orders should be brought up
to date, and made as effective as possible in
order that it may carry out the important
functions in regard to legislation which it is
being called upon with ever-increasing
frequency to fulfil, and that the volumes
comprising this delegated legislation may be
as accessible to the public as is the statute
law which it supplements.
The existing law on the subject is, as I
Lave already s: id, the Rules Publication
Act of 1893. There has been, so far as I am
aware, no difficulty in the adaptation or
application of this statute in relation to the
exercise of rule-making powers by Imperial
Departments or authorities which still exercise jurisdiction here in Northern Ireland.
But difficulties have arisen which prevent
the satisfactory application of the Act to
the departments of the Government of
Northern Ireland which have been created
under the Government of Ireland Act, 1920,
and to which have been transferred so many
-indeed the large majority-of those rulemaking powers originally exercised
departments of the Imperial Government.
by
Section 61 of the Government of Ireland
Act provides for the continuance of existing
laws with the modifications
necessary for
adapting them to that Act, that is, the Act
of 1920. But the modifications required to
be supplied in order to adapt and apply the
Rules Publication Act of 1893 are so numerous that hardly a single one of the references
to existing rule-making authorities
good.
holds
Again, in applying the Act of 1893 to the
Rules Publication Bill--
Second Reading.
29 APRIL 1925
exercise of rule-making powers by the department of the Government of Northern
Ireland, difficulties and doubts arise owing
to the fact that the distribution of administrative services is not the same in the newlyestablished Government of Northern Ireland
as it was when the departments of the Imperial Government exercised rule-making
powers before the creation of the Government of Ireland Act and the transfer of services. For example, our Ministry of Finance
exercises certain functions originally exercised by the Irish Local Government Board,
which under Section 1 of the Rules Publication Act was specifically exempted from the
provisions of that Section of the Act, while
the Treasury, which has its counterpart in
our Ministry of Finance, was not so exempted. The question arises, is our Ministry
of Finance to be exempt, as the Irish Local
Government Board, or to be included, as the
Imperial Treasury Department? That is a
technical problem, one of
many similar,
which makes the proper administration of
the Act of 1893 so difficult and so doubtful.
Hitherto, it is true, our departments have,
so far as is practicable, regarded and obeyed
the provisions of the Rules Publication Act
of 1893, but I think I have shown the House
quite clearly that the observance of the Act
without doubt or question arising is impossible. We, therefore, propose the adoption of
the measure to which I am asking the House
to give a Second Reading to-day.
I will now deal with the Clauses of the
Bill.
Clause 1 reproduces with some necessary
modifications Section 3 of the Act of 1893. It
deals with the printing, numbering and sale
of statutory rules.
Sub-section (1) of Clause defines the expression "statutory rules" in general terms.
It has been necessary to modify the definition found in the Act of 1893 in order to
adapt it to our circumstances, and opportunity has been taken at the same time to
from
remove doubt which has arisen
the
wording of the definition in that Act in the
course of its operation in Great Britain.
Sub-section (2) of this Clause cites the
authorities, the exercise of whose rulemaking powers is covered by this Bill. I
should, perhaps, call the attention of the
House particularly to paragraph (f) of this
sub-section. It is an addition to the existing
law and brings within the scope of the Bill
bodies, the proper exercise of whose rulemaking powers, though generally supervised
by a department of State, is of the greatest
moment to important sections of the community.
Sub-section (3) of this Clause is also im
portant as a departure from the Act of 1893,
which left to Treasury regulations the precise definition of an exercise of the rulemaking power which is to be regarded as
statutory.
Experience of the Act of 1893 has led to
the generally agreed conclusion that any
Rules
change in the law of
Publication
should embody a policy more strongly and
more definitely on the side of publication.
We have, therefore, put into this Bill itself
the general principle that, with certain exceptions covered by proviso to the sub-section which I am describing, "every exercise
by a rule-making authority of a statutory
power which is of a legislative character
(whether described as a rule, regulation or
order or by any other name) shall be held
to be a statutory rule," and so covered by
this Bill and regulations made thereunder.
The last detailed reference which I feel
called upon to make now is to Clause 4,
which provides a very important amendment of the existing law. It lays down the
general principle that any period, during
which it is enacted by any statute, whether
of the Imperial Parliament or of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, that rules or
orders shall be laid before either or both
Houses of Parliament, shall be construed to
mean a period of not less than ten days altogether, of which not more than five days
need be days upon which either or both
Houses shall be actualy sitting, while the
same laying period may extend over more
than one session of Parliament, instead of
being necessarily included in a single session, as is usually the case now.
This general principle has been found, as
it could only have been found, from the
actual working of our Parliamentary institutions, to be the one most applicable to rules
in Northern Ireland. We shall secure that
Parliament has ample time in which to
examine and to register such criticism as
it may think necessary to make upon any
rule or order lying before it. Hon. Members
may rest assured that the Government would
not suffer any derogation of that control exercised by Parliament, which is the base and
foundation of all systems of democratic government. At the same time, we shall avoid
a useless and hampering waste of time, which
may clog the wheels of Government and can
add nothing to the authority of Parliament
save the very doubtful power of inflicting
delay in the carrying into effect of the legislation, which Parliament itself has enacted.
Before I sit down-and I must ask the
House to forgive me if I have taken some
time upon a rather technical subject-I
should call attention to the fact that Sections 1 and 2 of the Act of 1893 are being
repealed, in view of the general agreement
Rules Publication Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
of all competent authorities that they have
in practice failed to achieve their object, and
that their working is unsatisfactory. The
safe-guards which they provide are illusory,
rather than real, and in revising the law on
the subject of Rules Publication we propose to
abandon the two sections altogether as unnecessary. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I agree generally, as I am
sure the whole House will agree, with the
proposals outlined by the noble Viscount, as
indicated in the Bill which is now before us.
We must all, I am sure, be only too willing
to get rid of the absolutely absurd provisions
indirectly alluded to by the noble Viscount-
I mean the provisions in the first section of
the Act of 1893, which require that before
any rule can be altered no less than forty
days' notice must be given of the intention
to do it. If that were the only factor contained in the Bill it would be a sufficient
justification for bringing in the measure in
order to relieve the Parliament of Northern
Ireland and the administrative departments
of Northern Ireland of the difficulties entailed by the existence of the Imperial Statute.
So far as I can see from a somewhat hasty
examination of the Bill there is nothing in
it which in any way alters the existing
difference between prima facie evidence and
conclusive evidence as regards documents
in the two Evidence Acts cited
in the Third Clause of the Bill.
I had no doubt that the Government
would have taken due pains to protect that
difference, and I am satisfied it has been
done.
The only thing that caused me some little
difficulty is paragraph 3 in the Schedule,
which would appear at first sight to place
a not altogether desirable power in the hands
of the rule-making authority. The power to
make rules, as the noble Viscount reminded
us, is more or less a modern development,
but it is a development of which Parliament
is, I think, very rightly inclined to be
jealous, and as this particular paragraph
comes before us it rather seems that power
is to be given to the authority making the
rule to say whether or not the rule is to
be put in print and obtainable by the public.
I rather hesitate to come to the conclusion
that that is the real intention of the paragraph, but I should like some further enlightenment on the subject. Therefore, I
propose to put something on the Paper at
a later stage, not in any spirit of hostility,
but in order to offer, perhaps, a more convenient opportunity for discussion than can
be obtained this afternoon. Apart from that
matter, which after all is but one item of a
somewhat lengthy and technical Bill. I can
do nothing except support the Bill.
Mr. GREER: I have not had time to go
very fully into this Bill, but so far as I can
see from glancing through it it is a very
excellent measure. These rules have the
full force and effect of Statutes, and I think
it is very desirable that they should all be
dealt with similarly and printed in volumes
indicating the special subjects for which the
rules are made. I do not quite understand
the difference between statutory rules and
rules that are not statutory, but I take it
that they are simple rules not of a lasting
nature, and they may be rules that do not
require to be printed. With regard to the
point that Senator Leslie made in reference
to the rule-making authority, deciding
whether a rule should be published or not,
the same difficulty occurred to me, but I
think there is a provision somewhere in the
Bill that if there is any difficulty about it
the point is to be referred to the Ministry
of Finance, and determined by that Ministry,
with the concurrence of the Lord Chief
Justice. If that is so I think it keeps the
matter perfectly safe. However, I would
like a little more time to look into the subject, and I hope the Committee stage of the
Bill will not be taken for some days, so that
we may have time to study the measure.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am glad the House has given this
Bill such a cordial reception. As regards
the point raised by Senator Greer, we propose that the Committee stage of this Bill
should be taken on Tuesday next. I think
that will give hon. Members time to put
down any Amendments they desire to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
ENNISKILLEN AND DISTRICT
ELECTRICITY SPECIAL ORDER, 1925.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-

That the Enniskillen and District Electricity Special Order, 1925, made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern

District of Enniskillen, and the Townlands
of

Rossorry, and Windmillhill, in the Rural
District of Enniskillen, all in the County
of Fermanagh, be approved.

the Electricity Commissioners and confirmed
by the Ministry of Commerce, will confer

Power Company statutory authority to supply electricity for public and private pur-
