Civil Pensions Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD. In the absence of my noble Friend,
the Leader of this House, who is away on
public service, I have great pleasure in moving the Second reading of the Civil Pensions
Bill. I have particular pleasure in doing so,
because I feel sure that this House will welcome a Bill which is directed to enable the
State to give a useful, though small, pecuniary
recognition in their time of need to both men
and women who have devoted themselves to
the great honour of the State by distinguished
services in some public office which has no
provision to offer to its retired servants; or by
their excellence in the field of science, literature, or the fine arts, or their devotion to
social work and progress.
It is, I feel, a great honour to be connected
with a Bill of this sort, because in my judgment there is no worthier cause in which
public money can be spent than in the
succour and support of these who, forgetful of
themselves, have contributed their all to the
benefit of their fellow-countrymen.
Coming to the clauses of the Bill I should
like to mention to this House that Clause I
gives power to the Minister of Finance to
grant a pension or gratuity for distinguished
services in Northern Ireland, either to the
person who has rendered such services after
Pules Publication Bill. 66
Civil Pensions Bill..
5 MAY 1925
he has attained the age of 70 years, or to his
widow, children or dependants after his death.
The Minister, in making a grant, shall take
into consideration the private means of the
recipient.
Clause 2 limits the maximum pension or
gratuity awarded to £100 to any person in
respect of his own services, and to an aggregate of £200 to his family after he be dead;
while the aggregate amount of all pensions
and gratuities under the Bill is limited to
£500 in any single financial year.
Clause 3 provides for Parliamentary control
in the granting of pensions and gratuities
under the Bill, and gives the House of Compower
to
the
require
Minister
mons
to lay before
the granting of pension or gratuity in any
particular case.
it the reasons for
This is a simple Bill, and I do not think it
requires any further words of explanation on
any part. I beg to move.
Mr. GREER: I am sure we will all agree
with the object of this Bill. It is an excellent thing that persons who have benefited the country and the State in any of the
various ways mentioned in the Bill, if they
have been unfortunate in life and if it is
necessary that they should do so, should get
a pension. It is a right and proper thing
that they should. There is only one difficulty
I have in the matter, and the only reason I
rise to say something about it is to ask
whether a pension can be granted under this
Bill to any persons outside Northern Ireland,
whether the intention of the framers of the
Bill is that any person whosoever, no
matter where he lives and no matter what
his nationality be, could get a pension under
the Bill. I do not say whether it would be
desirable to limit it to persons residing altogether in Northern Ireland or persons of
Northern Ireland nationality. I would just
like to know what we are doing before we
give the Bill a Second reading.
Mr. LESLIE: The only comment that I
should be disposed to make on the Bill is
that the maximum amount which can be
spent in any one year appears to be very
small. If I understand the provisions in the
Bill correctly, whatever may be the services
which it is desired to recognise there will be
no power to recognise those services by pension or gratuity if it should he that the
charge during the financial year in which
the need for recognition occurs had already
reached £500. Therefore I conclude that it
is the intention of the Government, if such
circumstances arose, that recourse should
again be had to Parliament for additional
powers. So far as I am aware neither of the
Imperial Acts limits the total amount payable in any one year. Both, I think, do
limit the amount which may be granted in
any one year. The amount, I think, is
£1,250, but the amount which may be paid
very probably does exceed that sum very
considerably. I presume it is the intention
of the Government that not more than £500,
whatever may be the circumstances, shall
be paid out in any one financial year without
further recourse to Parliament.
AND FER-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE
RARD: I am glad to see that the House has
given this Bill a favourable reception. In
reply to the question raised by my hon.
Friend, Senator Greer, the point of the Bill
is that the qualification for pensions given
under the Bill is service to Northern
Ireland, and it does not necessarily apply to
residence or locality or nationality. It is
service rendered to Northern Ireland that
is the substance of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly and
committed to a Committee of the whole
House, the House to be in Committee on
Thursday next.
Rules Publication Bill (S).
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said Bill.-(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN AND
AVA in the Chair).
CLAUSES 1 (Publication and sale of
statutory rules), 2 (Rules and authorities to
which the Act applies), 3 (Application of
Documentary Evidence Acts to certain rulemaking authorities), 4 (Amendment of law
to laying of statutory rules before
Parliament, &c.), 5 (Saving for regulations,
&c., of National Health Insurance Joint
Committee), 6 (Short title, repeal and
transitory provisions), ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
as
SCHEDULE (Provisions as to Publication
of Statutory Rules).
1. All statutory rules when sent to the
Government Printer shall be numbered consecutively, as nearly as may be, in the order
in which they are received by the Government Printer.
2. There shall be
a separate series of
numbers for each calendar year, and all
C
Rules Publication Bill- -
Committee.
THE SENATE
statutory rules made in any year shall whereever possible be numbered with the statutory
rules of that year and included (subject to
the provisions hereinafter contained in this
Schedule) in the annual volume of that year.
3. All statutory rules shall be printed and
sold unless, in the case of rules not required
to be published in the Belfast Gazette, the
rule-making authority declares that it is unnecessary to print and sell them, and such
declaration is not over-ruled by the determination of a question under this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in the
schedule, page 6, paragraph 3, line 1, to leave
out from unless (" and sold, unless in the
case") to "the" in line 4, ("over-ruled by the
determination") and to insert "such printing
and selling is declared to be unnecessary in
pursuance of."
I put down this Amendment to paragraph
3 of the Schedule in order that we might
discuss the provisions of that paragraph and,
I must confess, not perhaps with any definite
expectation that the Amendment I was
suggesting would be adopted. The position
seems to me as follows: The paragraph to
which I object provides that in the case of
rules which need not be published in the
Belfast Gazette the question as to whether
they are to be actually printed and sold to the
public is left for decision to the rule-making
authority. That is to say, that those who
make the rules have, in the first instance, at
all events, the power of saying whether they
shall or shall not be printed and put on sale,
and so made available to the public, whom
it may be presumed, the rule actually
concerns.
It is possible under the paragraph to have
this declaration over-ruled in the manner
provided, but apparently the obligation of
applying to get a determination of that body,
and to get a declaration on the rule is cast
upon the public. I do not think that in
principle this method is correct. It seems to
me really to tend rather too much in the
direction of Government by Departments,
which is unconstitutionnal, instead of government by Parliament, which is constitutional.
All the protection that seems to be obtainable
is that given in an early clause in the Bill.
It is in Clause I where the rule, whether it is
to be printed or whether it is not, has still
got to go to the Government printer. The
Government printer is presumably in charge
and may have no objection to the rules being
printed without being put on sale. I cannot
find that there is any obligation cast on the
Government by the Bill to make any objection
at all. Therefore in order that we might
discuss the matter I put down the Amendment
to paragraph 3. We want to provide that
these rules shall be printed, and shall be put
on sale, unless by the determination of the
constituted authority it is decided that that
should not be necessary.
I think it might be possible that some other
method might be adopted which would give
complete satisfaction, because I know there
must necessarily be a large number of rules
which are of no interest to anybody, which
nobody would buy, and which would scarcely
require to be printed. But it is important,
I think, that whenever a rule is made any
member of the public who wants to get a
copy of the rule should be able to do so easily
and promptly. The rules ought to be
brought to public notice at once. There is
not much use, in any case, in waiting until
such time as the next edition of the volume
happens to come out. In all probability I
may be thinking of something theoretical
rather than practical, but for the moment
I beg to move the amendment standing in my
name.
Mr. GREER: The same difficulty that has
occurred to the hon. Member occurred to me,
but I took a different way of dealing with it,
to which I may refer later on when my
amendment comes on. But I may say that I
prefer my child to his child. I think that
the hon. Member is calling in a steam hammer to crack an egg-shell, so to speak, and
some simpler way should be discovered of
dealing with this question. What I would
like to see would be that it should be the
duty of some person, I do not care whom. to
get notice when a rule or order is not going
to be printed, and to consider whether, in
his opinion, it was necessary to print it or
not, and that if he agreed that it was not
necessary to print it and concurred with the
rule-making authority it would not be
printed.
As I understand the Act of Parliament.
the rule would still be numbered, but would
not be printed and there would be no great
trouble or difficulty, if it was some officer
who would always be easily accessible. If
he disagreed with the rule-making authority
and if he thought the rule ought to be
printed in the public interest and the rulemaking authority thought it should not be
then a question would have arisen under
the Act and would be determined by the tribunal which is set up in the first instance.
that is to say, by the Minister of Finance,
by the Lord Chief Justice, and by the
Speakers of both Houses. It may be difficult to get them together to come to some
determination, but I think in ninety-nine
cases out of a hundred if some officer were
named to deal with the question he could
agree with the rule-making authority.
Rules Publication Bill-
Committee.
5 MAY 1925
When he would not agree it would be a case
naturally where some principle was involved
and in that case it might be right and proper for the Lord Chief Justice, the Ministry
of Finance, and the Speaker of each House
to determine the matter. I do not think I
need say anything further except that I have
raised the matter in my own amendment.
Mr. MAGEE: Of course it is all very well
for us who happen to be Members of one
House to say we can know all that happens,
but take the case of an outsider who knows
nothing whatever of what is going on in
Parliament. It is grossly unfair, I think, to
him that he should not be in a position to
know all the enactments of our Parliament.
The amendment proposed by Senator Leslie
is a very reasonable one and I think it should
be adopted.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I do not know whether the hon.
Senator Leslie who moved this amendment
realises I presume he does-what the effect
of this amendment would be. The effect of
this amendment to Rule 3 in the Schedule
would be this, that every statutory rule as
soon as it was made would have to come up
for the determination of the question as to
whether it is unnecessary to print and sell
it. As the hon. Member knows, questions
under this Bill have to be referred for determination to the Ministry of Finance, acting
with the concurrence of the Lord Chief
Justice and the Speakers of the Senate and
the House of Commons. This House, I am
sure, is aware that statutory rules are made
from day to day, and this Bill, as it stands,
requires that all statutory rules are forthwith, after they are made, to be sent to the
Government printer to be numbered,
printed, and sold by him, with special
exceptions. There is no exception in the
Schedule to the numbering. Therefore the
unprinted rules must be numbered, and the
number appears in the annual volume in a
list under rule 5 (b) of the Schedule.
It would be almost impossible for this Bill
to be worked if the full tribunal, intended
only for cases of great importance and
difficulty, is to be appealed to almost daily,
which would be the effect of hon. Senator
Leslie's amendment. This Bill quite clearly
makes it the duty of the Government
printer to see to its enforcement as regards
publication. And if the rule-making
authority makes a declaration as to which
the Government printer should be in any
doubt, it is equally his duty to bring the
matter before the deciding authority in order
to ensure that the provisions of this Bill are
not evaded.
I may perhaps be allowed to inform this
House that in the course of the working of
the Act of 1893 the editor, who is the expert
actually in charge of the work, has only
twice had occasion to refer a question to the
decision of the tribunal, and on each
occasion on which he referred it he has been
upheld. I think it is somewhat unreasonable to assume that the authority expressly
charged by the Bill with the duty of publication would immediately conspire with some
Government Department in order to defeat
the Bill.
It may be mentioned that the control
of His Majesty's Stationery Office, which is
the Government printer of Northern Ireland
charged with the publication of Northern
Ireland Statutory Rules and Orders, is under
the general supervision of the Statute Law
Committee, of which the Lord Chief Justice
is Chairman, while the services of an
eminent counsel, Mr. T. E. Maxwell, K.C.,
have been secured for the actual editorial
work. I feel that if this House passes the
amendment of the hon. Senator it would
mean that every rule would have to come up
for determination before this special
tribunal as provided in the Act. In these
circumstances I am afraid I cannot accept
the amendment, and I trust the hon. Member will see his way to withdraw it.
Mr. LESLIE: I must admit that what the
noble Viscount has said is quite correct.
The apparent effect of adopting the amendment might be that almost every rule would
have to be referred to the Committee or body
consisting of these persons, but I think it
would be found, when some decision on a
question of principle had been obtained
from that body, it would not be necessary
to refer every case to them again.
As regards the protection that is given to
the public by the fact that these rules are to
be numbered and indexed, I do not really
see that that is a protection at all. It would
really make no difference to me if I saw that
a rule was number 5. The mere numbering
makes no difference whatever that I can see.
I confess that I am not satisfied that the
Government printer is under any obligation
whatever to question the decision of the rulemaking authority. I do not think the paragraph is satisfactory. I still hold it may be
possible to devise some alteration which
might allay what I think are reasonable
fears. I do not think it will be necessary,
and I do not suppose anyone wishes me to
proceed with this amendment further today, because the Bill is still in an elementary
stage, an even more elementary stage, perhaps, than is usually the case with Bills
which we are considering. I will, by leave
of the Committee, withdraw the amendment,
but I hope the Government will give further
consideration to the point I have raised.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am obliged to Senator Leslie for
Rules Publication Bill--
Committee.
THE SENATE.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
consenting to withdraw the amendment. In
the meanwhile I will see whether what he
suggests can be met, but I do not hold out
any hope that I can do so.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. GREER: I beg to move in Paragraph
3 of the Schedule, page 6, line 3, after
authority" ("rule-making authority declares") to insert "with the concurrence of
the Ministry of Finance."
I do not think it is necessary for me to
say very much in support of this amendment.
I know that the Government printer in
England is an official who generally does look
into this question as to whether it should be
published or not. 1 do not think he would
be a very suitable person or official to be
appointed in Northern Ireland, because he
never comes to Northern Ireland, but acts
through an assistant here, and I do not know
much about him. He might be a suitable
person or he might not. All I wish and
all that I think we should know is that
there should be some person named whose
duty it would be to see if a rule were not
going to be published whether it should be
or not.
I quite agree with Senator Leslie that putting a number or saying that an order was
made number 5 or number 6 gives a person
no information whatever as to what that
order may be. At the same time it lets the
public know that an order was made dealing
with the question referred to and I presume
it would be possible for him to get a copy of
that order if he wanted to see it. I think
he should have power to get a copy somehow
of that order, and I put the Ministry of
Finance in as the official that I thought
ought to see whether an order should be
published or not, because the Ministry of
Finance is mentioned in the Act as one of
the tribunal that ultimately would have to
determine the question. It would appear to
me to be quite a simple thing to leave it to
the Ministry of Finance in the first instance,
and if they were satisfied that an order
should not be published why there would be
no publication. If the Ministry thought the
order should be published then they would
bring the matter up as a question to be determined under the Act and the Lord Chief
Justice and the Speaker of each House would
in conjunction with them decide. They
would be at hand here and it would be quite
simple to refer to them. I still think my
amendment should be carried. I beg to
move.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The amendment moved by Senator
Greer deals with the same rule as was the
subject of Senator Leslie's amendment.
Senator Greer, I understand, proposes to
bring in the Ministry of Finance in the first
instance to decide whether it is necessary
to print and sell a statutory rule. I have
already in reply to hon. Senator Leslie explained the machinery that exists to
secure publication. It seems to me that
introduce the Ministry
of Finance
to
at
the stage at which the hon.
Senator desires it to be introduced,
would only involve duplication of work, because the Ministry of Finance with the concurrence of the other high authorities which
I have mentioned before, namely, the Speaker
of the Senate and the Speaker of the House of
Commons would have to decide the question
under the Bill. Personally, I am of opinion,
and it is the opinion of the Government, that
the working of this Bill from day to day
ought to be left to the Government printer
acting on the advice of the editor.
I may mention that the Government
printer is himself a member of the Statute
Law Committee, and I do not think that the
fear that must be in Senator Greer's mind-I
conclude that that is the fear in the hon.
Senator's mind--that certain statutory rules
might escape printing, is well founded.
Statutory rules that Departments of State may
consider are not necessary to be printed,
would only be very minor points, and only
statutory rules that might appear from day
to day. I do not think that there is honestly
any fear that rules, of any importance at all,
would not be printed, and, as I mentioned
in reply to Senator Leslie, as these rules have
to be numbered it is possible for anyone who
wishes to see that rule to get a copy of the
rule through the Department. It may be a
little more difficult-there is no great difficulty
-for a member of the public to get a copy
of the rule if he wishes. Under the circumstances I cannot hold out any hope of accepting the hon. Member's Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I would like to ask how soon
it is expected that a member of the public,
relying on the number, might expect to be able
to get a copy of the rule. Would he be able
to get it the day after the rule is published?
He will not desire to wait until the annual
volume is published, which may be some ten
months hence. I do not know if there is any
explanation as to that.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: So far as I understand the number
comes out in the annual volume, but I will
look into that question, and give the hon.
Senator an answer on the Third reading.
Mr. GREER: Having regard to what the
noble Viscount has said, I am quite willing
to withdraw my amendment by leave of the
Committee.

Rules Publication Bill.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Schedule agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported without amendment to the House.

House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment.
Moved: That this report be now received.-

On Question, Motion agreed to.

75 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 6th May, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Summary Jurisdiction (Married
Persons) Bill (S).
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: On rising this afternoon to
move the Second Reading of the Bill the title
of which is before you I must in the first
instance entreat the indulgence of the Senate.
I cannot but be aware how greatly I shall
stand in need of all the indulgence that can
be afforded me, for I know that the proposals
which I am about to submit are couched in
language which is somewhat crude and unmistakably marked as the production of an
amateur. For the wording of the Bill as it
stands I am myself entirely responsible. The
Bill has not yet had the advantage of revision by the Parliamentary draughtsman for
I did not feel justified-in spite of his wellknown accessibility-in seeking the assistance of that heavily-burdened officer before
this House had at all events affirmed the
principles of the measure I am bringing forward.
To me it seems that the first requisite,
more especially in the case of a private
Member's Bill, is to obtain the acquiescence
of the House before which the Bill is brought
both to the principles involved and to the
provisions which it is proposed to insert, and
that it is not until after that has been done
that it becomes necessary to consider in the
light and with the aid of expert counsel
and advice the actual wording and
machinery to be employed. I think
that it is to the Committee Stage that
we must look for the transformation of the
jagged corners and the ragged edges of a
roughly hewn stone into the polished facets
of a gem which shall reflect no discredit on
our Parliament. It is acting on those views
that I have brought in this Bill.
The sole object of my Bill is to ameliorate,
so far as we can, the condition of those commiserable persons to whom so far as we can
judge the marriage, whether of themselves
or of their parents, has not brought the comfort or the happiness which is experienced
by those who are more fortunate. My Bill
is not directed in any way towards the
loosening of the marriage tie or of any
snapping of the matrimonial chain, nor is
it intended to allow anything to be done
which will result in the breaking up of
homes. On the contrary I hope that the
Bill, if it becomes an Act, will exercise a restraining influence and have a contrary effect.
What I am asking Parliament to do can be
stated very briefly. It is to afford to illtreated wives and their children the same
statutory protection in Northern Ireland
which they could obtain were they resident
in England. I want to establish here that
the person of a married woman is as sacred
as her purse, that maternal feelings ought
to be regarded, and should not be permitted
to be outraged, and that the rights of the
child of an ill-treated married woman are
at least equal to those of the child of an
unmarried mother. I want to give all
reasonable facilities for obtaining such redress as may be necessary in a prompt and
inexpensive way.
And so I am asking Parliament to extend
the powers of our Courts of Summary
Jurisdiction in the same way as has
been done in England. I want to give
Courts of Summary Jurisdiction in Northern
Ireland the power to make an order, which
order while in force and only while in
force-shall be tantamount in effect to what
is called in England a Judicial Separation.
Such orders in England are, I believe, commonly called separation and maintenance
orders. And in extending these powers of
summary jurisdiction I want to incorporate
not only all the modern English legislation
on the subject, but also certain very valuable
provisions of our own Affiliation Orders Act
of last year.
It will be necessary for my argument,
I think, to compare the situation here
with that in England, and taking England
first I must refer very briefly to the state
of the law there, past, present, and prospective. Beginning with the past, we find
in 1878 an Act of Parliament applying to
England which gave protection to a married
woman, and to some extent to her children,
but only children under ten years of age,
if I remember rightly, and it gave protection
to the ill-treated married woman whether
she was deserted either actually or
structively.
con-
In 1886 there was another Act passed somewhat hurriedly. It received the Royal
Assent towards the end of June, 1886, and
bears the impress that it does not appear in
the form in which it was originally intended
to appear owing to the fact that Parliament
was much occupied at that time and it was
necessary to wind up the affairs of the
Session with much greater rapidity than
usual. At all events the Act of 1886 gave
protection to the deserted wife whether or
not she had been ill-treated. Therefore we
have ill treatment with or without desertion,
6 MAY 1925

Second Reading.
77 Summary Jurisdiction Bill—
I
and desertion with or without ill treatment.
Both those Acts were repealed in 1895, when
the law was very materially extended.
shall have to refer again to the Act of
1895, for it is the foundation of my Bill.
There was again a little extension in 1902
when, only in England, the provisions of the
Act of 1895 were extended to include a habitual
drunkard, whether male or female, and in
that year Parliament provided for special
property,
protection for the household
whether of the wife herself or the children,
and even for the school requisites of those
children in order that their slates and so
on might not be taken from them. But it was
not until 1920 that the English Parliament
directly provided for the maintenance of the
children of separated married women.
that year that was done.
In
All this legislation which I have mentioned
is about to be extended, so far as England
is concerned, by a Bill which is now in the
Imperial Parliament. It has passed through
all its stages in the House of Commons, and
it has been read at least a first time in
As Mr. Lockyer
the House of Lords.
Lampson, the Parliamentary Secretary to
that the
the Home Office, has stated
English Government is anxious to pass the
Bill at the earliest possible moment one is
justified in regarding it for our purposes
here to-day as part of the English law.
But none of this legislation applies, at all
events directly, to Northern Ireland, and it
will not so apply unless action to that end is
taken by our Parliament. All that we have
in Northern Ireland-I am not forgetting of
course the Married Woman's Property Act
of 1882, or the Children Act of 1908, both of
which apply to England and Northern Ireland
-but all we have here is the Act of Parliament of 1865 dealing with the property of a
deserted married woman and giving that
married woman certain protection both as
The Act of 1865
to status and property.
did that and there was also the Act of 1886 of
which I have just spoken. I would yenture to
remind the House that under the Act of 1886
desertion might be either actual or constructive, and the qualification has been of
very great use to magistrates in times past.
And we have got in Northern Ireland a short
Statute passed in 1908 which, I am afraid,
is sometimes overlooked, though not perhaps
very often or with any serious results. The
effect of that Act of 1908 was to extend to
Ireland
Northern
sions as regards

give
of
of the provihabitual drunkthe protection
the wife and
ard,
to the property
children to which I have referred. None of
those Statutes go far enough. Their cumulative effect leaves us far behind Great Britain.
None of those Acts applicable to Northern
Ireland legalise the cessation of cohabitation,
nor do they deal with the custody of the
children, and none of them make any provision for the maintenance of the children.
In fact, were it not for the Poor Law, the
child of the married woman in Northern
Ireland who had had to leave her husband
owing to his cruelty or desertion would be
left without any statutory provision at all
for maintenance. I think that the House
will agree with me that it is time, high
time, that such a gross inequality as between one part of the United Kingdom and
another ought to be removed, and I feel that
I shall not be asked to give any statistics
of the number of such cases that might be
expected in Northern Ireland, for I believe
it will be agreed that if even only one case
of ill treatment can be established it is for
Parliament to relieve that one case.
I must now ask the House to come back
with me to 1895, the year in which, so far
as England is concerned, the Act of 1886
repealed.
dealing with deserted wives was
The Member for the South-Western Division
of Essex, Mr. Byrne, as he then was-he was
afterwards a judge of the High Court-introduced the measure of that year and he introduced it as a Private Member's Bill. In
doing so he said that his Bill was intended
to get rid of anomalies and to give relief in
cases of persistent cruelty, and that such a
Bill had been suggested by the magistrates
of the Essex Bench, and had received the
of
approval two of the Metropolitan
stipendiary magistrates. Mr. Byrne's Bill
had a swift and easy passage through the
Commons and also through the House of
Lords. It was introduced in the second
Chamber by the Lord Chancellor of the day,
the venerable and, I think I may add,
venerated Lord Halsbury, who said that it
the poorer
would be found of benefit to
classes. It was supported by Lord Herschell,
whose comment was that it would to some
extent consolidate the law and would make
it more easy to protect the ill-treated wife.
And so Mr. Byrne's Private Bill became an
Act-the Act of 1895. Fortified as I am by those
expressions of opinion by the learned and enlightened jurists I have named, I think I need
make no apology for using the English Act
of 1895 as the foundation of my Bill, through
the several Clauses of which I will now ask
hon. Senators to do me the honour of accompanying me.
Clause 1 is based entirely on the Act of
1895 as extended and amended by subsequent
Statutes, or about to be amended by the Bill
I think that the
now before Parliament.
first four paragraphs at all events of Clause
1 explain themselves, but I should like to
draw the attention of the House to paragraph
(e), because that contains the first hint in

[Mr. Leslie]
the Bill that it is intended for the protection of the child. As regards the very repulsive matter that is dealt with in paragraph (f) and Sub-Clause 2, I should tell the
House that I have taken those proposed
provisions direct from the Bill now before
the English Parliament, and in regard to
this I have heard of at least one case
which recently occurred in Northern Ireland that seems to show that such a
provision is required here also. In
any case the matter is not one in
which our Parliament would wish to be
behind the law across the water. Sub-
Clause 3 is the one Sub-Clause in the Bill
which gives relief to a married man, that
is a married man who has the misfortune
to be married to a woman who is a habitual
drunkard or one who has been guilty of
persistent cruelty to his children.
comes from the English Act of 1902.
protection was given in Ireland in 1908,
though not so fully as in England. So far
as cruelty to the children is concerned the
reference to English legislation is the Bill
of the present year.
That
This
Clause 2 deals with the powers which I
think are necessary, and which I hope this
House will be prepared to give. I must tell
the House at once that the drafting of paragraph (a) gave me very considerable dithculty. I was anxious to base my paragraph
as far as possible on the corresponding provision in the English law, and that corresponding provision is Section 5 of the English
Act of 1895, which is based on Section 4 of the
Act of 1878. But that Section in the English
Act uses the phrase "judicial separation,"
and "judicial separation" is not a term
known to us in Northern Ireland. I found,
on reference to the English Statute Book,
that this expression was first used in an Act
so old as 1857, an Act well known in England.
but not here, and that Act says in the
plainest possible terms that the phrase
"judicial seperation" shall be regarded as
tantamount to a decree a mensa et thoro. I
did not want to use that expression, at all
events until I was fortified and supported by
the advice of those well acquainted with the
various decisions on those lines which rule in
Northern Ireland. I found that the Married
Woman's Property (Ireland) Act, 1865, used
the same definitions as in the English Act,
and so in that respect I have drafted this
paragraph on those lines.
A further difficulty was my desire to enable
the court to incorporate in an Order under
this clause those provisions of the Act of
1908, which deal with special classes of
property both of the woman and her
children. I wanted to obviate multiplying
the provision of Magistrates' Orders and the
necessity for two applications where, in my
judgment, one application should suffice. At
all events, I am not particularly enamoured
of my wording. Indeed I regard it as a
rather glaring instance of the need for expert examination. Then follows the provision for the legal custody of any children
of the marriage. As regards paragraph
(b) I must not shirk the issues
that are involved in that paragraph.
It does mean, as I intend that it shall
mean, the possibility of the father
losing the legal custody of his own children.
We have faced that question before. It had
to be dealt with in the Children Act referred
to just now-Section 21 of the Act of 1908,
applicable to both England and Northern
Ireland, where a man under certain conditions has to lose the custody of his children.
No man can come under such a penal provision as this paragraph contains unless he
has brought himself, by his own misconduct,
within the provisions of Clause 1 of this Bill.
I do not think we need waste our time on
that matter, for we must regard the interest
of the child as of paramount importance
The
The next provision for the payment to the
wife of a sum, not exceeding £2, follows that
already in existence in Northern Ireland
under the Act of 1886. Then comes the new
provision adopted in England in 1920 of a
sum not exceeding 10s. for the child.
sum of 10s. has been attacked in England as
insufficient, and it is only half the amount
we have allotted to an illegitimate child, but
it was held in England, and I think it will
be held here, having regard to the fact
that we provide what may amount to 40s.
for the married woman, whereas the unmarried mother gets nothing for herself at
all, and to the fact that the people
likely to be concerned are not likely to be in
very affluent circumstances that 10s. as a
maximum is probably sufficient.
In the next Sub-Clause I ask Parliament
to give the same protection of an additional
year's maintenance to the female child, as
we did under the Affiliation Orders Act. In
the next Sub-Clause I provide a power which
has been given in England of enabling the
woman in serious cases of physical ill-treatment to obtain immediate relief at the
discretion of the Court which convicted the
husband on indictment. In Sub-Clause 4 I
give the necessary saving from liability
whilst the married parties are resident together, such a saving being, as I claim, one
of the indications given in the Bill that the
marriage tie is not dissolved, and is not
to be regarded as dissolved, by any
order made in pursuance of this Clause.
Clause 3 deals with those enactments of
the Affiliation Orders Act relating to procedure to which I have referred. I think
they will be found of use. I might have
done it by a rule-making power, but I know
that Parliament is not fond of that method
of proceeding. I think I may be not un-
81 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

6 MAY 1925
Second Reading.
fairly attacked for having given a fairly
strong example of legislation by reference-
Mr. GREER: Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: It is so. I admit it. I plead
in extenuation that in following that course
I have left it open for anybody who likes
to do so to move at a later stage that
we should set out the details in full. Not
to do it in full to-day saves six pages of
print in the Bill, and I think the sections
are such that neither misunderstanding nor
difficulty will arise. I have put those
Sections out rather more fully than usual
in the first Schedule on page 8 to which I now
ask you to turn and see what we are referring to. I refer to Section 3 of the Affiliation
Orders Act. Hon. Members may recollect
that under that Section the House gave
protection, for what it may be worth, to
the illegitimate child in the event of the
father being so inconsiderate as to die while
order against him was in force,
and being so considerate as to leave
property behind him. As we gave that protection in the one case we must give it in
the other, but I do not suggest that it will
be found of much practical assistance. The
same section provides for the payment of
sums decreed to be paid and gives the court
the power of altering those sums and of
generally varying the order on production
of fresh evidence. I may mention that such
a power of variation has always existed in
England in these married persons cases, and
that it exists to some extent in Northern
Ireland under the Act of 1886.
an
Section 6 explains itself. Section 7 deals
with the very important question of appeal.
The Appeal is to the County Court Judge or
Recorder sitting as the sole judge. Under
the Act of 1895 it is to the Probate,
Divorce, and Admiralty Division of the
High Court, but I venture to think
that the course I have suggested here
is likely to be core satisfactory and
less troublesome than the English course.
Then we provide for the service of a Collecting Officer. The collecting officer made his
first appearance in England under the Act
of 1895. Incidentally the same Section 8
imposes the obligation on a person liable to
make payments of keeping the payee informed of his address, a very useful protection for the married woman.
That pro
vision is being inserted in this year's Bill
in England. Up to now apparently they
have not had it. We put it in the Affiliation
Orders Bill last year.
Section 9 applies the Summary Jurisdiction
Acts generally with special provisions as to
the service of summonses and the payment
of costs, and to enable the child wife to
make use of the Act notwithstanding that
she is under the legal age. It will have to
be considered whether the same power should
be given to a boy in the one Section in which
such a husband is permitted to apply.
Section 10 gives rule-making powers as regards certain formal matters and such
powers will I think be necessary.
Returning to the body of the Bill, Clause
4 deals with a necessary saving for the
husband, but introduces a protection for the
child. Clause 5 provides for the possible
discharge of an order of the Court on the
voluntary resumption of co-habitation or on
the commission of an act of adultery. This
Clause also introduces us to the second
schedule and I desire to say that I doubt
whether the Senate will be advised to retain that Schedule as it stands, because
generally speaking the enactments it enumerates have not any direct application in
Northern Ireland. I have, however, thought
it well to insert the Schedule in the first
instance in order to bring the enactments
which it specifies to the attention of the
House. That course is one which is not
altogether without precedent in the practice
of the House of Lords.
The real trouble is that we have not yet
got any satisfactory reciprocal arrangement
in matters of this kind between Great
Britain and Northern Ireland. There is
some arrangement of that sort between the
United Kingdom and the Dominions but we
have not got it here and I think we want it
rather badly. That reciprocal arrangement
was given in the Act of 1920-not the
Government of Ireland Act but another Act,
and we have not yet dealt with the matter.
I felt that it was outside the scope of the
present Bill because it is a large question
that will require legislation in the Parliament at Westminster as well as here, and if
we could bring in Dublin also it would be all
the better. Clause 6 is a simple method of
getting prompt redress in the case of the conviction of a husband for an aggravated
assault upon his wife.
Clause 7 is for the enforcement of orders
as to the custody of children. This difficulty
is being met in England by a somewhat
roundabout reference to a statute which
does not apply to Ireland and I venture to
think that for summary jurisdiction purposes
the provisions I am suggesting will be found
to meet the case. Clause 8 gives the definition of "Habitual Drunkard" which appears
in the Act of 1879, but following the Bill
now in the Imperial Parliament I have extended the definition to include the abuse of
drugs such as cocaine, heroin and morphine
dealt with in the Dangerous Drugs Act.
Tobacco, of course, is not included, but it
83 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE.
Second Reading.
[Mr. Leslie]
is perhaps questionable whether we should
not at a later stage insert a special reference
to methylated spirits and methylated ether.
If this Sub-Clause is accepted by the House
it seems to follow as a logical consequence
that the definition of a habitual drunkard
in the Act of 1908 should also be amended.
Clause 9 is of the usual formal character.
It deals with citation, explanation, and the
commencement of the Act. I have suggested
that the Act should come into operation on
1st January next year and nobody would be
better pleased than myself if it were found
possible to antedate it, but રી Private
Member's Bill goes through many phases before it gets to the Statute Book. In addition,
this Bill requires a very great deal of consideration and I thought it would be wrong
and altogether against the objects I had at
heart to do anything which could be described as tending to rush the Measure. 1
have repealed the Act of 1886, and it may
perhaps be thought that I should have given
it a companion in extinction. But that is for
the future.
Of the two schedules I have already
spoken.
Now I have finished. I hope that
I have established my case for an amendment
of the law. I shall be pleased to answer to
the best of my ability any questions that
may be addressed to me, and while thanking
hon. Senators for the forbearance with which
I have been heard I would pray their
further kindly consideration-first for those
unhappy persons for whom I have been
pleading, and, secondly, for myself, having
regard to the fact that this is my first
attempt at legislation. I have the honour to
move that the Bill be read a Second time.
Mr. GREER: I think I shall be expressing
the feeling of all the Members of this House
if I begin by congratulating the hon. Member
who has just sat down on being the first
Member of either House to bring forward
a Private Bill on his own account. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) I think I must also
congratulate him on the very clear statement
he has made on the provisions of this Bill.
The matter is somewhat complicated, but he
has made it extremely clear by the manner
in which he has dealt with it. The hon.
Member takes his business here seriously,
and I think the speech he has given us this
afternoon proves that he has gone very exhaustively into this question in making himself acquainted with the law on this subject
in England and Ireland and in drafting the
Bill himself. I will have a word to say later
on about the drafting of the Bill, but I
think he is also to be congratulated on
having got hold of a subject upon which
legislation in Ireland has been far too long
delayed. The subject he has brought before
us this afternoon is a very important one
and I certainly think he has treated it in the
right way. Subject to any alteration that
may be made in the details of the Bill hereafter, I think that on the whole it will be
a very valuable Bill and for the benefit of.
the people concerned, and I think it ought
to be passed. I hope the Government will
see its way to take up the Bill, or at any
rate consider the Bill and suggest any amendments, which I am sure the hon. Member,
Senator Leslie, will be very glad to agree to.
I do not intend to go exhaustively into the
Bill, but I would like to say a word or two
about it. While the Measure is founded
upon the English Act of 1895, in some
important respects it goes beyond that Act,
and it will have to be very carefully considered by the House before it is passed in its
present form. For example, in the English
Act it provides that a married woman can
only take steps under the Act where the
husband has been convicted of assault upon
her and sentenced to a fine of more than £5
or imprisonment for more than two months.
The hon. Member has not inserted those
words in this Private Bill, and it is a
question for serious consideration whether
it is enough to say, "has been convicted summarily of an aggravated assault," and leave
it to the court to decide whether the assault
was of such a description that the very
strong powers this Act gives should be put
into force.
The next point I would like to refer to is
in paragraph (e) of Clause 1, where it says:
have been guilty of persistent cruelty to
her, or to her children, or of wilful neglect
to provide reasonable maintenance for her
or her children whom he is legally liable to
maintain,
My recollection of the English Act is that
it adds the words, which do not appear here,
by reason whereof a married woman has
left her husband.
Those words are not included in this Bill.
Then in Clause 1 he proposes to leave all these
great powers in the hands of the Court of
Petty Session. We will have to consider
very carefully whether these powers should
not be placed in the hands of the Resident
Magistrates. In the other Bill we passed here
we did so because we thought it was dealing
with matters that were so serious that they
should be brought before the Resident Magistrates. I understand the difficulty that
occurred to the hon. Member in regard to
Section 2, but I think he has dealt with the
matter very well by that Section, and if the
Government approve of the Bill the Parlia-
85 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-
6 MAY

Second Reading.
mentary Draughtsman will no doubt consider
that Section, and see whether he can improve
upon it, but as it stands it seems to me to be
a very good attempt to deal with the question.
I have said so much in praise of the hon.
Senator, that I am sure he will not be very
angry with me when I say that I was extremely surprised to find that he had drawn
Clause 3. In my opinion it is not worthy
of him. In this House he has more than once
agreed with me in saying that legislation
should not be by reference. By the time he
came to Clause 3 I suppose he was tired of
writing out or dictating the Bill, but for
some reason or other he has departed from
the principles he has always upheld, and he
has, I consider, been guilty of as bad a piece
of legislation by reference as I have seen for
some time, because he not only refers to
other Acts, but he says that they are to be
altered in certain respects. The expression
"illegitimate" in relation to a chud, and the
words "and such person or Board may be
made a party to any proceedings taken for
the commutation of weekly sums," are to
be taken out of the Acts referred to
altogether. I do not think that is quite
worthy of the hon. Member. I should have
mentioned that he has extended the operation of Clause 1, giving power for proceedings to be taken by making the words
"habitual drunkard" include an habitual
drug-taker. That is done in paragraph (a),
and I approve of that, because I can see no
difference in principle between a habitual
drunkard and a habitual drug-taker. They
both lose their senses owing to their having
done something, and I put them both in the
same category.
I do not think I need make any further
special reference to the Bill. I may say that
in general I approve of the Bill, and I hope
the Government will look carefully into it
and see if it ought to be amended on any
particular points. I must confess that I
have not gone as fully as I would have liked
into the English law on the subject, but I
am quite certain that when the Parliamentary Draughtsman gets at it he
will do So, and if it passes him
I will be perfectly satisfied with the Bill.
I believe it will be a great improvement to
have the same law in Northern Ireland as
there is in Englard.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I would
like to join Senator Greer in congratulating
the hon. Member (Senator Leslie) on the able
Speech he has made in introducing this Bill,
and particularly in having the unique distinction of being the first to introduce a Private
Member's Bill in either of the two Houses.
I am not in a position to criticise the legal
phraseology of the Bill, but I do support the

Measure, because it brings our legislation into
line with the legislation in Great Britain.
There may not be so many cases in this part
of the country that require a Bill such as this,
but at the same time, as Senator Leslie has
said, if there should be only one solitary case
it is only right that the persons concerned
should have the same privileges as are enjoyed on the other side of the water. I agree
with the remarks made by Senator Greer as
to the care that is necessary in considering
the Bill, because when such drastic powers
are given in a case where a woman complains
of abuse or cruelty one would need to know
to what extent that cruelty had been practised,
or whether it was only imagination on the
part of the woman. There are certain women
who are peculiar in that respect, and it will
be necessary to be very careful in framing
the Bill. I hope that when the Parliamentary
Draughtsman comes to examine the Measure
he will make provision against such a
tingency as that I have mentioned. I have
no doubt there may be cases amongst the
lower classes where they will be only too
pleased to have an opportunity of changing
their partners in life, and it is necessary to
guard against such a possibility. I agree that
Senator Leslie has devoted great care to the
preparation of his Bill. I have always noticed
that he takes the work of the Senate very
seriously, and I think we are all more or less
greatly indebted to him for the great assistance he gives us in criticising and amending
Bills that come before this House.
con-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I, along with Senator Greer and
Senator Joseph Cunningham would like to
add my meed of congratulation to the hon.
Member for the able speech he has delivered
to us this afternoon in moving the Second
Reading of this Bill. The hon. Member,
Senator Leslie, has explained the Bill with
the facility of expression and the clarity
of outlook which he brings to bear on every
subject on which he addresses this House,
and I think he is to be congratulated on
the speech he made this afternoon. He is
particularly to be congratulated, because
as hon. Members have already said, he is
the first Member in either House to introduce a Private Bill into our Parliament.
I would like to tell Senator Leslie that
I am assured by those who are competent to
form an opinion on the point that his Bill
has been drafted with an ability and accuracy
which does great credit to the hon. Member.
He has brought great care and much research
to bear in drafting the Bill and I think the
House will agree with me that its subject
matter is of vital importance to our State.
Ireland has been excluded from legislation
on this subject since the year 1886. The Eng-
87 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

THE SENATE.
Second Reading
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
lish Bill of that year was extended to Ireland, and Ireland has not shared in the English legislation which has been passed since
that date, namely, the Summary Jurisdiction
(Married Women) Act, 1895, as amended by
Section 5 of the Licensing Act, 1902, and the
Married Women's Maintenance Act, 1920. I
may mention that prior to 1886 there was
an Act passed in the year 1878 which applied
to Ireland and the nett effect of both these
Acts is that in Northern Ireland the only
grounds upon which an Order can be made
by a court of summary jurisdiction are:--
(a) an aggravated assault on the wife
but only in a case where there is reason to
fear for her future safety, and (b) wilful
neglect to maintain the wife and family,
coupled with desertion.
The Bill before the House proposes in effect
to bring the Law in Northern Ireland into
conformity with that in Great Britain and
thus by one step to carry out the changes
which were effected in England over a period
of 40 years and at long intervals. He has
also included in his Bill provisions which
are contained in the measure which is now
before the Imperial Parliament but which
has not yet reached the Statute Book, although I have no doubt they will. In effect
the Bill proposes to extend the grounds upon
which Orders can be made, and for the sake
of clarity, I would repeat the grounds upon
which an Order can be made in England
under the existing Law:-
1. Conviction summarily of the husband
of an aggravated assault as defined by the
Offences against the Person Act, 1861.
2. Conviction on indictment of assault
on the wife where the sentence is a fine of
more than £5 or a term of imprisonment
exceeding two months, which the Hon.
Senator, Senator Greer, mentioned in his
speech.
3. Desertion.
4. Persistent cruelty to his wife.
5. Wilful neglect to provide reasonable
maintenance for her or her infant children
whom he is liable to maintain .
6. (By the Licensing Act) If the husband
is proved to be a habitual drunkard.
The English Bill which is now being discussed by the Imperial Parliament proposes
still further to extend these grounds by including
(a) Persistent cruelty to her children, and
(b) That her husband has compelled her
or been guilty of such conduct as was likely
to result and does result in her submitting
herself to prostitution.
There are other subsidiary provisions
which, though important in themselves, I
do not propose to refer to at the present
time. In England the decision of the court
of summary jurisdiction is expressely not
subject to appeal but the hon. Member in his
Bill proposes to make the decision of the
Lower Court in Northern Ireland subject to
review by the County Court.
The House will appreciate that the provisions of the Bill would effect a sudden and
extensive change in the Law here in Northern
Ireland. In a matter of this kind the Government feel that they must proceed with
caution and that legislation on this point
should not be undertaken without a clear
expression of public opinion. I would like to
read here an extract from the speech of the
hon. Member (Senator Leslie) in the debate
on the Address in which he referred to
matters of this kind. He said:
I cannot but feel that a matter of this
kind might not inappropriately be dealt
with in the first instance by a Private
Member's Bill, because the matter could
then be considered and it could be ascer
tained what demand there was for it and
how public opinion regards it.
That is the attitude of the Government.
I should like to have a clear expression of
public opinion as to the merits or demerits
of the Bill the hon. Member has introduced
So far as I know, since this Government was
formed there has been no great public demand for the changes, or at least for all
of them, which are proposed.
Under the Act of 1878, an Order made by
a court of summary jurisdiction, in a case
where the husband is convicted summarily
or otherwise of an aggravated assault upon
his wife, may be made to have the effect of a
judicial separation. The law in this particular
respect has not changed in Ireland since the
year 1878, but in England the court has
power in the case of all Orders to grant
a judicial separation. Whether public
opinion here is ripe for such an extensive
change is a matter for careful investigation,
and the Government feel considerable
hesitation in assenting to the introduction
of such a change without public opinion
being fully consulted. There is perhaps
one point on which there may be unanimity,
namely, the extension to Northern Ireland of
the express provisions dealing with the maintenance of the children, though no doubt the
courts at present indirectly have regard to the
children's welfare.
The hon. Member, by the preparation of
his Bill and by presenting in concrete
form the changes which he proposes, has
rendered a great public service. It will be
for the public now carefully to consider the
changes which are proposed and, as soon
as the Government have a clear indication
89 Summary Jurisdiction Bill-

6 MAY 1925
Second Reading

of the public's views, though possibly
unanimity may not be expected, they will
undertake to bring forward amending legislation on the subject. If this undertaking
is acceptable to the hon. Member the
Government will not oppose the Second
Reading of the Bill, but the hon. Member
will understand that the Government cannot undertake to afford any further
facilities for the passage of the Bill.
I would ask the hon. Member, therefore,
to be good enough to accept our declaration
of adherence to the principle of this Bill
as an earnest of our intention to bring forward legislation of a similar character at
a future date. I would request him not at
the moment to press upon us the precise
details of the application of that principle
by seeking to secure the further consideration of this Measure in Committee.
That step we should be bound to oppose for
the reasons which I have endeavoured to
explain to the House. The hon. Member in
taking this Bill through a Second Reading
will have done a very considerable service
to the Province in securing the proper
ventilation of this important subject, and
in sounding public opinion upon the matter.
If the hon. Member should find signs that
we are not moving as quickly in the matter
as he considers necessary, he will always be
able to revive this Bill in a new Session of
Parliament. The Government do not propose to offer any objection to the Second
Reading of the Bill if the hon. Member will
accept what I have outlined in my remarks.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I should be indeed ungracious were I to do otherwise than acknowledge
humbly and with hearty thanks the kindness
with which this Bill has been received.
should like to thank my hon. Friends, Senator
Greer and Senator Joseph Cunningham, and
the noble Viscount for their kind congratulations, and to say how gratifying it has been
to me to have the support of the Members of
the House on the Measure I have brought
forward. Now let me turn to deal with the
remarks that have been made. I must
begin with Senator Greer, than whom I know
no Member of this House who is more entitled to express criticism on a matter of
this kind.
I want to suggest that in omitting from my
Bill all reference to punishment-I have
simply used the expression "conviction"-I had
in mind that the Bill from which I was taking
it was thirty years old, and it seemed to me
that conviction was a more important matter
than the punishment thought necessary at the
time. As regards the cruelty being such as
to compel a woman to leave her husband,
I must point out that that requirement is
expressly repealed in the English Bill, because
it has been found rather inconvenient. I am
referring, of course, to the Bill now before
the Imperial House. What the Bill actually
says is, "accordingly the words, 'when he, by
such cruelty and neglect, causes her to leave
and live separately apart from him' are
hereby repealed."
As regards the more serious question of
the Court of Summary Jurisdiction before
which these cases shall go, I think this
matter is different from the Affiliation Orders
case, in this way. In the latter case we
were giving a new power and the Magistrates
never had the power of proceeding upon a
question of Affiliation. In the present case
they have had the power given them, both in
the Acts of 1865, and the Act of 1886. There
is another reason for leaving the jurisdiction
to the ordinary magistrates. We have now,
though we had not in 1895, women magistrates, and one of the claims put forward
for conferring the Commission of the Peace
on women was that their services would be
invaluable in dealing with cases of this kind.
We have not got female Resident Magistrates, and I do not think we shall have
them. I will not express any view as to
whether we should have them or not. I
think that this question might perhaps be
raised at a later date.
As regards legislation by reference, to
which I pleaded guilty, I have already made
such explanation as I can and thrown myself
on the mercy of the House. If anybody prefers to have the whole thing set out, no
doubt it can be arranged at a later date. I
should like to specially thank Senator
Joseph Cunningham for the interest he has
shown in this matter, and to assure him that
mere verbal abuse of a woman or slight
assault would not be sufficient to set the provisions of this Bill in motion. The offence
against a woman must be aggravated assault
within the meaning of the Section, and that
aggravated assault is an assault which in the
opinion of the magistrates cannot be adequately punished by a sentence of two months
imprisonment or a fine of £5, and it has tc
be an assault either upon a boy under 14
years of age or upon a female. The alternative is conviction on indictment, which
means conviction of assault more serious
than the aggravated assault to which I have
referred. There is nothing, I assure Senator
Joseph Cunningham, in the Bill which will
enable anybody to change wives with anybody else.
I come now to Viscount Massereene and
Ferrard, who spoke last, and I thank him
for the kindness of his remarks. With
reference to what he said regarding the Act
of 1878 I am afraid he is not quite right. I
am not satisfied that that Act applies to
Northern Ireland. It is not referred to in
Summary Jurisdiction Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
any of the Justices of the Peace handbooks
to which I have referred. As it is an Act to
amend an earlier Act which did not apply
to Ireland I cannot think it is of very much
practical service to us, although on account
of its indirect application I thought it better
to put it in the Second Schedule. The Act
describes itself as an Act to amend the
Matrimonial Causes Act, and that, I think,
is the Act of 1859, which is an English Act.
As regards appeal under the Act of 1895 that
Act, which I have now before me, provides
that an appeal shall lie from any Order
or refusal of any such Order to the
Probate, Divorce, and Admiralty Division.
Turning to the more important point to which
the noble Viscount has alluded-that is the
desire that full time should be given to deal
with this matter-I am entirely at one with
him in that. In fact I did give some
indication of it by suggesting 1st January,
1926, as the date when the Act should come
into operation. The proposal of the noble
Viscount is that this House should give the
Bill a Second Reading, that then it should go
on the Paper for Committee Stage with no
date named so that the Government may,
if it is SO advised, bring in
Measure of its own. I hope that
if that course is taken the Government
will find it possible to have that Bill during
the Autumn Session. If that can be done
we shall be standing in the same position as
suggested in the Bill, because the Government
Bill cannot be brought into operation before
1st January next, and in that case I should
be quite content.
a
I think it will be quite the best course if
the House will give my Bill confirmation as
regards principle by a Second Reading, and
then let it wait for some little time for the
Committee stage, or for the introduction of a
Government Measure. We know perfectly
well that when the noble Viscount makes a
suggestion of that sort he is not putting the
matter off indefinitely. I know from previous
experience that when the Government makes
a promise of that kind it means to keep it.
We have had such promises before, and the
Government has kept those promises, so that
in assenting to the course suggested by the
noble Viscount, I am not sacrificing my cause.
After all, it is quite fair to say, as
the noble Viscount suggested that it took
the Parliament of Great Britain 660 years,
if I remember my history correctly, to
produce the Act of 1895, and we have not
been in existence for a hundredth part of
that time. I am willing to accept the
suggestion of the Noble Viscount.
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill be
now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House.
Rules Publication Bill (S).
Order for Third Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Third Reading of this
Bill I would like to inform Senators Leslie
and Greer that I regret I am unable to add
anything to what I said in Committee with
regard to the point they raised. All I can
do is to say that the Government will see if
they can
can possibly introduce anything in the
Bill to meet the point raised by the hon.
Senators when the Bill
passes through
its stages in another place, although I can
give them no assurance that the Government
will be able to do so.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite content to leave
the matter in that way. I should certainly
not think of offering any opposition to the
passage of the Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to carry down the said Bill and desire their
concurrence thereto.
Adjournment
Moved: That this House do now adjourn.-
(Viscount Massereene and Ferrard).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at half
after Four o'clock.
Administrative Provisions Bill 94

The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.

of which is approved by the Inspecting Officers
appointed under Rule 2 of the Schedule
appended to Council Order, Northern Ireland, 20th January, 1925.

under Rule 2 of the Schedule appended to

on the Natural and Industrial Resources of

Resources of Northern Ireland.

way a Clause can be introduced in the Bill
to
the
secure
exemption of Queen's
