Canals (Continuance of Charging
Powers Bill).
Order for Second Reading read.
FER-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
RARD: In the absence of my Noble Friend,
the Leader of this House, who, as hon. Members are aware, is at present in England on
business of some public importance, I have
the honour to move the Second Reading of
this Bill. As hon. Members will see, this
is a short and simple Bill which extends
until the close of 1926 the power to levy increased tolls which had originally been
granted to the Minister of Transport under
the Ministry of Transport Act, 1919, in respect of the Canals in Northern Ireland
which that Minister had taken over under
that Act. I may say that the Canal
Companies received back the Canals when the
control of the Minister of Transport ceased
in August, 1921. Paragraph (e) of subsection (1) of Section 3 of the Ministry of
Transport Act, 1919, gave the Canal Companies the continuation of the increased
tolls for eighteen months after they had resumed control from the Minister.
Our own Canals Act of 1923 continued
those powers further until February 15th,
1925, and the present Measure simply seeks a
further extension until the close of the year
1926. The reason for allowing increases upon
the statutory scales of charges is, of course,
the enormous increase in the cost of labour
and material. I may perhaps explain that
the present Bill gives no power whatsoever
to the Canal Companies to increase their
charges beyond the maximum figures at which
they were entitled to stand on 15th February,
1923. The Ministry of Commerce has power
to revise charges within certain limits to
meet any serious movement of the market
for labour and material. I do not think
there is any other point that arises under
the Bill. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Thursday next.
Question to Minister.
THE NEW LAW COURTS.
The following Question stood on the Order
Paper in the name of Mr. Greer :--
To ask the Leader of the House whether
he can state when building operations will
on the site of the new Law
commence
Courts.
Mr. GREER: I am aware that the question
I have put down on the Paper relates to a
Reserved Service and ought more properly to
have been asked in the Imperial House of
Commons, but I hope I will be able to get
an answer to my question here. I understand
that the Ministry of Finance acts for the
Imperial Parliament in regard to these
matters, and therefore I hope that Ministry
will be in a position to give me a satisfactory
answer. The question is of very considerable
importance. The result of the passing of the
Government of Ireland Act, 1920, has been
that Belfast takes the place of Dublin as the
centre for the administration of legal matters
in Northern Ireland, and the County Courthouse has been made use of by the judiciary
here for the purpose of carrying out their
business.
I know of my own knowledge that before
the Act of 1920 was passed the County Courthouse was not large enough for the business
for which it was then required and the
authorities were contemplating enlarging the
building. Suddenly the duty of providing
Courts for the judges who were holding
Courts continuously, and for providing offices
for the public departments connected with
The New Law Courts.
The New Law Courts.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Greer]
those Courts, was thrown on the County
Courthouse, and the resources of that Courthouse were not nearly large enough for the
purpose. Representation was made by the
Benchers and by the Incorporated Law
Society of Northern Ireland to have additional accommodation provided. What they
asked for was to have a new Courthouse constructed in the heart of the city, somewhere
where it would be of benefit to the public as
well as to the officials and the judges engaged.
Their memorial was granted. A site for
the construction of the new Courts was chosen
more than a year ago and plans were submitted to the Benchers and to the Law
Society, who finally approved of them about
a year ago. Nothing has been done since.
That appears to me to be most disgraceful,
and I want to know when the Imperial
authorities will waken up and do what it is
their bounden duty to do, because they have
handed these additional duties on to Northern Ireland. They professed to be willing to
do it and they acquired a site, but there the
matter rests. I want to refer to a Memorial
that was sent forward on 15th April last. It
reads:--
That we, the Committee of the Benchers
of Northern Ireland, desire to call attention
to the great and, as it seems, unreasonable
delay in the erection of the New Law
Courts for Northern Ireland.
Though three and a half years have now
elapsed since the establishment of a
separate judicial system for Northern Ireland, and twelve months since a site for the
new Courts was acquired, no buildings have
yet been commenced, nor has the site for
same been cleared; and we fear that no
adequate steps are being taken to press
the matter forward in the manner that
its urgency demands.
The due and proper administration of the
law is being greatly hampered by the inadequate accommodation; and the attempt to
carry on the work of four Courts in the
two public Courts available repeatedly
leads to much inconvenience and delay.
The judges are frequently compelled by
force of circumstances to hold Courts in
their own private Chambers, and in other
small rooms quite unsuitable for the purpose; and on one occasion it was found
necessary to hold the trial of an action
in a temporary galvanized structure without sufficient floor space to provide even
standing accommodation for the litigants
and their witnesses. The office work of the
Courts is also greatly impeded by the
limited space at the disposal of the staff.
We most strenuously urge the Government of Northern Ireland to call upon the
Imperial Government to take all necessary
and proper steps towards the erection and
equipment of our new Courts with the least
possible delay.
The Incorporated Law Society of Northern
Ireland has also sent forward a Memorial
much to the same effect. It does seem to me
scandalous that our superior Courts of
Justice should be held even once in a galvanized building with insufficient standing
room to conduct the business, and I do think
our Government should make very strong
representations to the Imperial Government
to begin building at once. They have
acquired a site, which has to be cleared, but
that can easily be done, and I hope they
will lose no more time in getting on with
this work. The delay that is taking place is
not fair to the public, it is not fair to the
practitioners, and it is not fair to the judges.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think 1 should explain
that this Question appeared on the Paper
as a starred question in error, and therefore
I propose to treat it as an unstarred question.
Mr. LESLIE: There is another minor
aspect of the position detailed by my hon.
Friend, Senator Greer, to which I think we
might pay attention for a few moments. It
is entirely a minor aspect as compared with
the deleterious effect on the administration
of justice which the present insufficiency of
accommodation entails. It is the inconvenience to the County Council administration which is caused by the use of so
much of the County Antrim Courthouse for
the purpose of administering justice.
The County Antrim Courthouse on the
Crumlin Road was, as the House will
remember, originally provided as the Courthouse to be used for the purpose of criminal
justice in County Antrim and for the purposes of the Grand Jury-I mean those Grand
Jury purposes which were the precursors of
the present County Council administration.
Subsequently, when the city of Belfast was
given a separate existence for criminal purposes, arrangements were made between the
city of Belfast and the County of Antrim
to enable the city of Belfast to enjoy the
joint use of the Courthouse. For that the
city of Belfast pays a consideration which I
do not say is inadequate, and the city contributed very largely to the necessary
enlargement of the Courthouse which was
made some eighteen or nineteen years ago.
The present position is that His Majesty's
Courts of Justice, by arrangement, have first
call on all the accommodation at the Courthouse, and that is found to be of considerable
inconvenience to the ordinary County Council
administration, not so much perhaps in the
actual meetings of the Council and its Committees as in the office work. There is the
137 Pharmacy and Poisons Bill-

21 MAY 1925
Second Reading.
County Surveyor's office there, and there
has been a great increase in the amount of
work owing to the increase of motor-car
licenses. The premises it is possible to devote to that sphere of the County Council
administration are far too small for the purpose, and the inconvenience is very great.
That inconvenience is felt both by the officials
and the public, and I would therefore join
Senator Greer in urging the Government to
bring such representations to bear as might
be possible on the Imperial authorities, on
whom the responsibility rests, to discharge
what is a statutory duty, and to perform
that duty without further delay.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I am sure the hon. Member who
raised this Question (Senator Greer), and the
hon. Member who supported him (Senator
Leslie), will be the first to agree that the
Government recognise the very serious inconvenience that is caused by the fact that the
new Law Courts have not yet been built. I
do not quarrel with the statement Senator
Greer has made. I think it is a very frank
and a very true one. He said in his opening
remarks that this is a matter for which the
Imperial Government is responsible.
correct.
That is
I am sorry I am not at present in a position
to state when building operations will actually
commence on the site of the new Law Courts,
but I can assure my hon. Friends and this
Senate that representations have been made
and are continuing to be made to the Imperial
Government as to the necessity for expediting
the work. I may say that the Minister of
Finance never goes over to London-and he
has to go very frequently-without pressing
this matter on the attention of the Imperial
authorities. We recognise how very necessary
it is that these new Courts should be erected
as quickly as possible, and all that the Government can do is being done to expedite the
building of the Courts.
Mr. GREER: I have to thank the noble
Viscount for his explanation of the delay. I
can only hope that some additional pressure
will be brought to bear on the Imperial authorities, and that when I refer to the matter
at a later period I will be able to get a more
satisfactory reply.
Pharmacy and Poisons Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In moving the Second Reading of the
Pharmacy and Poisons Bill I think I am
justified in saying it is a Bill of some importance in Northern Ireland. It is a lengthy
measure, but at the same time I do not think
the House will find any great difficulty in
dealing with it.
I am happy to be able to inform the House
that this Measure comes here in its present
shape as a result of agreement between all
the parties principally concerned. It is,
therefore, an agreed Bill, and in that way,
although it appears a formidable document
with its 32 sections and 4 lengthy schedules,
I do not, as I said, anticipate that this House
will find any great difficulty in dealing with it.
The main purpose of the Bill, as its title
plainly states, is the establishment of a Pharmaceutical Society for Northern Ireland, the
functions of which will be the regulation and
government of the body of Dispensing
Chemists in this Province. The circumstances which render the creation of such a
Society necessary are, I think, perfectly well
known. The Pharmaceutical Society of Ireland, which, prior to the Government of
Ireland Act, prescribed the qualifications of
candidates who desired to become Pharmaceutical Chemists, and conducted the
examinations of these candidates, has its
headquarters in Dublin, and is governed from
there. It has, therefore, become necessary
for us to set up our own Pharmaceutical
Society here in Northern Ireland. That,
really, is the main purpose of the Bill. We
have also taken the opportunity to amend and
extend the law relating to Pharmacy and
Poisons.
In Part I and the First Schedule will be
found the constitution of the new Pharmaceutical Society, and the machinery for setting
this into motion. I think I had better explain
to the House that one of the basic principles
of the Bill is to substitute a single grade for
the present three grades of Chemists. We
have at present Pharmaceutical Chemists who
are alone licensed to compound prescriptions.
They are known as Dispensing Chemists.
There are Chemists, and Druggists, and Registered Druggists. The two latter grades are
not allowed to dispense. All three grades
may deal with poisons. Our proposal is that
for the future as a result of this Bill all
Chemists shall be qualified Pharmacists, that
is, they shall have passed those tests of their
knowledge and attainments which the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland set up
under this Act may deem it advisable to impose
before granting its licence and enabling the
holder of such licence to undertake the responsible duties of compounding and dispensing
prescriptions for the general public. Hon.
Members will find that arrangements have
been made for the proper representation of
139 Pharmacy and Poisons Bill-
THE SENATE

Second Reading.
[Viscount Massereene and Ferrard]
Chemists and Druggists and Registered Druggists in the new Pharmaceutical Society, but
their representation will naturally be on
diminishing basis as those grades of Chemists
will eventually drop out altogether.
a
I am sure that the hon. Senator, Senator
Leslie, will note with pleasure that any regulations made by the Council under Section 3
of the Bill are not only to be approved by
the Minister of Home Affairs, but notice of
such approval must be published in the
"Belfast Gazette," while all such regulations
must also be laid before both Houses of
Parliament.
Part II of the Bill and the Second and Third
Schedules deal with the examinations to be
conducted for ascertaining the qualifications
of candidates for registration as Pharmaceutical Chemists or their apprentices or
assistants. The Second Schedule is somewhat
complicated by the fact that provision must
be made to safeguard the interests of existing
Chemists and Druggists and Registered Druggists from hardship. Opportunities are,
therefore, provided in Parts I, II, and III of
the Second Schedule to these persons to be
registered as Pharmaceutical Chemists upon
passing a modified examination, whose severity
varies according to the candidate's previous
practice and experience.
Section 7 sets out the various persons who
are entitled to be registered. It will be noted
that provision has been made enabling all
existing Pharmaceutical Chemists who were
registered on the existing Irish Register to
obtain registration under this Act, and the
same applies to Chemists and Registered
Druggists or apprentices or assistants.
Sub-section (3) provides for any case of
hardship.
Section 8 provides for the appointment by
the Minister of Home Affairs of a Registrar
and an Inspector.
Sections 9-15 deal generally with the
method of keeping the Registers and the
powers of the Registrar.
Section 17 is important, as it provides for
the taking out of an annual licence to carry on
the business of a Pharmaceutical Chemist, or
of a Chemist and Druggist, or Registered
Druggist on payment of a fee fixed by the
Ministry of Home Affairs after consultation
with the Pharmaceutical Council and with the
consent of the Ministry of Finance.
Sub-section (2) of Section 27 deals with the
way these fees should be applied. For the
first two years after the commencement of
Part II of the Bill the treasurer of the Pharmaceutical Society will receive the whole of
the fees for the purposes of the Society.
After that period a certain proportion of the
fees, not to exceed one-half, will be taken as
an appropriation-in-aid for defraying the expenses incurred under this Bill, the remainder
being paid to the treasurer of the Society as
before. It is estimated that the whole of the
fees will amount to some thousand pounds a
year.
The only other part of the Bill to which
I feel bound to call attention is Section 20
in Part III, which settles a point which has
frequently given rise to considerable difficulty
and enables companies doing business
Pharmaceutical Chemists, provided the management is under the direction of a duly
qualified Pharmaceutical Chemist, to take
apprentices.
as
I
As I have said, this is a lengthy Bill.
hope I have touched upon most of the points
of interest, but I shall be happy to deal, so
far as I am able, with any further points
which Members may desire to raise. I beg
to move.
Mr. DICKSON: I think every Member of
the House will agree that this Bill is a good
one. It is a good thing to amalgamate the
three different trades engaged in the sale of
drugs. There is one matter I would like to
see provided for in the Bill. Perhaps on the
Committee stage an amendment might be introduced to prevent poisons being sold in the
free manner they are sold now. I understand
that any person can go to a druggist's shop
and buy arsenic or any other poison if he is
prepared to sign his name. I would like to
see a Clause inserted in the Bill to the effect
that no poison should be dispensed except
on the certificate of a Medical Officer or a
veterinary surgeon. I would ask the Government to consider that point between now and
the Committee stage of the Bill.
Not
Mr. LESLIE Like my hon. Friend who has
just sat down, I join in saying that I am sure
everybody will regard this Bill as a useful
measure, and they will think it reflects a good
deal of credit on the Ministry of Home Affairs,
as representing the culmination of several
months of work. The Bill made its first
appearance in the last Session, although it did
not then come before this Chamber.
only before the Bill was introduced, but subsequently, spade work-if one may call it so
--has been continuously in progress. The
result is the Bill which we see before us, and
we have the satisfaction of knowing now, as
indeed the Noble Viscount has just told us,
that the Bill comes before us to-day as an
agreed measure.
141 Pharmacy and Poisons Bill-
MAY 1925


Second Reading.
Undoubtedly, the Bill is of a somewhat
technical character, and I do not know whether
we number amongst our Members a very large
proportion of Members who have any great
knowledge either of Pharmacy or Toxicology,
which are the two subjects dealt with in the
Bill. Personally, I should be very timorous
of making any suggestion of alteration as regards any of the technical portions of the
Bill. I may tell the Noble Viscount that I
entirely concur as regards Section 3, which I
think is a very good Section indeed.
The only difficulty I have-and I feel some
diffidence in mentioning it partly because of
the length of the Bill and partly because
what I am going to say perhaps has more
relation to the Committee stage-and partly
because the point to which I desire to direct
attention may quite possibly be covered by
Adaptation Orders. Adaptation Orders are,
and always will be, something of a terror to
amateur legislators, because they are usually
carefully concealed in a red bound book
which is only obtainable at an expense of 35s,
and one does not possess any knowledge of
their contents. But my point is of a good
deal of importance, and it relates to the future
status in Northern Ireland of any republication of the British Pharmacopoeia.
The British Pharmacopoeia may perhaps be
described as an Encyclopedia rather than a
Dictionary, dealing with drugs and compounds and infusions, and it owes its
existence to the Medical Act of 1858.
That Act prescribed that the Medical Council which was then established should have
authority to issue such a volume, and it was
hoped that their doing so would enable the
new volume to take the place of a large
number of unofficial works which had been
brought forward by this and that body at
different times. That was provided for in
1858, but before the first edition of this
Pharmacopoeia was published some one had
the bright idea that it would be a good thing
if the Pharmacopoeia that was to be published
should apply not only to England but to
Scotland and Ireland.
Accordingly, in 1862, an Act to that end
was passed and the two Acts together gave
the sole rights of publishing and re-publishing editions as often as might be decided to
the Medical Council, and provided that when
the first book did come out its appearance
should be notified to an expectant world by
advertisements in the London, Dublin, and
Edinburgh Gazettes, and when that had been
done the production of that notice, giving
the name of the person authorised to print
the book, should be sufficient to ensure that
a copy of the Pharmacopoeia should be received in evidence. The reason that comes
into our Second Reading debate to-day is
that by Clause 18 of the Bill we refer to the
Pharmacopoeia in terms which show that any
attempt to perpetrate what we may call fraud
is a criminal offence. Therefore we must
be quite sure that we have covered the
ground sufficiently.
The section in the Act of 1862 to which I
have referred did not expressely say that republication had to be notified in this way,
but I think the intention must have been
that that should be so. Therefore, presumably the present copy of the Pharmacopoeia,
which, I think, was issued in 1914, although
I am not quite sure, has full authority in
Northern Ireland, but I am a little doubtful, speaking subject to correction, of the
effect of the Adaptation Order of July 7th,
1923, as to whether that status will be fully
maintained in Northern Ireland unless we
put something in this Bill to effectually safeguard the position. The only point to consider is whether it is necessary, and if so,
exactly how we should do it. If the question
cannot be answered at the moment perhaps
something could be put on the Paper at a later
stage that would serve as a basis for further
discussion, but it is a point that ought not
to be left in doubt.
As regards the rest of the Bill I am sure
it will meet with general approval, and I
think it will effect the objects it desires to
attain. It does not, so far as I can see, make
any change as regards the law of poisons,
because there is a distinct provision in one
of the earlier Clauses pointing out that nothing in this Bill is to affect the Arsenic Act
of 1851, and at a later stage other provisions
are either saved or reproduced. I have no
hesitation in supporting the Second Reading
of the Bill.
Mr. GREER: Like all other hon. Members
who have spoken on this Bill I must say I
highly approve of it. I have been in touch
with a great many pharmaceutical chemists
and druggists on the subject of the Bill and
I am in a position to say that they also
highly approve of it and think it will be of
very great advantage to them. As the Noble
Viscount has told us, the Measure incorporates a Pharmaceutical Society for
Northern Ireland, and puts it under Government control. In that way it will raise the
status of all Pharmaceutical Chemists, and
Chemists and Druggists in Northern Ireland,
and it will be of great advantage in many
respects. They highly approve of it and
consider it has been brought into this House
in a much better form than that in which
it was originally printed. They have to
thank the Government for meeting them on
many points during the long discussions that
have taken place in regard to the Bill.
With respect to the very ingenious point
raised by Senator Leslie, we will have an
143 Pharmacy and Poisons Bill.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.
[Mr. Greer]
opportunity of looking into it before the Committee stage, and it can easily be set right
if what the hon Member fears has been
omitted from the Bill. With regard to the
point raised by Senator Dickson, about the
sale of poisons, I think he will find on looking into the matter that the Bill incorporates
several Poisons Acts of Parliament which do
sufficiently protect the public if only the persons who sell the poisons observe the Regulations. My recollection of the effect of
these Acts is that persons can only sell
poisons to people they know, and who declare the purpose for which they desire to
buy the poison.
Mr. DICKSON: To poison dogs?
Mr. GREER: I mean rat poison and things
of that kind. A person has to sign a book
at the time he buys the poison so that anyone can find out who has had the poison.
There is also a provision about poisons
that are used in agriculture. On the whole
I think it is an admirable Bill and I cordially
support it.
FER-
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND
RARD: I am glad the House has given the
Bill such hearty approval. The various
points that have been raised in the course
of the debate are really points for the
Committee stage. As regards the question
raised by Senator Dickson, the hon.
Member opposite (Senator Greer) has
really answered it. As regards the point
referred to by Senator Leslie I think he is
mistaken in the view he takes of the matter,
but I will look into it more closely. I think
that amongst the Orders in Council there has
been an Adaptation Order to substitute the
Belfast Gazette for the Dublin Gazette, and
in that case the matter will be in order, but
I will examine the point more fully between
now and the Committee stage.
On Question, Motion, "That this Bill be
now read a Second time," agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the whole
House for Thursday next.
ROYAL ASSENT.
Viscount MASSEREENE AND FERRARD:
I am authorised by the Prime Minister, on
his behalf, to inform the Senate that His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland, in
the name of and on behalf of His Majesty the
King, has been pleased to give his assent to
the following Bills:-
Administrative Provisions.
Civil Pensions.
Public Health (Milk Regulations).
Adjournment.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I beg to move that this House do
now adjourn.
Mr. GREER: I would like to ask when we
will meet again?
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM : Can the
Noble Viscount tell us when the House is
expected to rise for the Summer Recess?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It is proposed that this House
should meet again on Thursday next. With
regard to the question asked by Senator
Joseph Cunningham as to when the House
will rise for the Summer Recess, that depends
on how the business in another place progresses and how long it is before Bills come
up to this House. If the hon. Member is
referring to the Whitsuntide Recess I expect
this House will sit on Tuesday of Whit week,
and I hope it may be possible for the House
to adjourn for the Summer Recess at the end
of that week, and if not then, during the
following week. I can make no definite
statement, but I think the House is almost
sure to sit on Tuesday of Whit week.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
afraid that in the other House they are
adopting the ca' canny system that is so often
attributed to the workers in the workshops.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Four
o'clock until Thursday next

Canals Bill.

CLAUSES 1 (Constitution of Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland), 2 (Qualification of Members, etc., of the Society and
the Council), 3 (Regulations for purposes of
the Act), 4 (Appointment of officers by the
Council), 5 (Holding of Examinations), 6

(Qualifications for registration under the
Act), 8 (Appointment of Registrar and Inspector), 9 (Registers of pharmaceutical
chemists, etc.), 10 (Power of Registrar to

CLAUSE 18 (Provisions as to offences).
Mr. LESLIE: It may be within the recollection of the House that when the Second
Reading of this Bill was under discussion I
brought to the notice of the Government the
possible necessity for taking steps to protect
in Northern Ireland the status of any future
issues of the British Pharmacopoeia. The
point was, as I think it will be remembered,
that owing to the perhaps not very explicit
wording of the old Act of 1862 it did not
seem at all certain that, having regard to
the Constitutional changes which have recently been made, the future position of the
British Pharmacopoeia-that is all editions
subsequent to the first edition published in
1864-would be protected.
The Government were so kind as to promise to look into the matter, and I have
reason to believe that the Noble Viscount will
be able to assure us that there is no necessity
to introduce a protective clause in the Bill.
But as the matter is of very great importance, because a mistake or oversight would
have very far-reaching consequences, I have
thought it right to again direct the attention
of the Committee to it.
VISCOUNT
MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: In reply to the point raised by
Senator Leslie with regard to the British
Pharmacopoeia, the Government have inquired into this matter, and they are advised
that every new edition of this Pharmacopoeia
as distinct from a mere reprint of an existing
edition, must be announced in the Gazette.
By an adaptation of the Enactments Order
references to the Dublin Gazette will be construed as references to the Belfast Gazette.
Therefore, I think the hon. Member may
rest assured that no amendment to the present Bill on this subject is necessary.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite satisfied. I was
only anxious that the position should be
made clear, and should appear
Journals of this House.
on the
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

149 Business of the House.

28 MAY 1925
Adjournment.
Business of the House.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: It may perhaps be convenient to
the House if I deal with the course which
we propose to take in regard to business
prior to the Summer adjournment.
We propose to conclude our business in
three further sittings-on Wednesday,
Thursday and Friday of next week.
On Wednesday we hope to take the Second
Reading of the Rent Restrictions Bill, the
Housing Bill and the Local Authorities (University Grants) Bill.
On Thursday we shall take the Committee
Stages of these three Bills; and the Second
Readings of the Finance Bill, the Unemployment Insurance (Temporary Provisions)
Bill and the Appropriation Bill and their
Committee Stages, if any.
On Friday we shall hold a sitting at 12
noon for the purpose of the remaining stages
of all six Bills, in order that any amendments here may be considered in another
place in the afternoon. It is hoped to adjourn finally on Tuesday, the 9th of June,
after the Royal Assents have been announced.
If
I trust that hon. Members will not feel
that they are being unduly pressed.
further time is desired, the House could, of
course, sit to-morrow, Friday, and on Whit
Tuesday, the 2nd of June, but I feel that
sittings on either or both of these days
would be very inconvenient to the House
generally, and that members, upon consideration of the measures which will come before them, will agree with the Government
that the programme which I have outlined
does, in fact, give this House sufficient time
to deal adequately with the business.
I suggest that the House should now adjourn during pleasure to await other Bills
from another place.
Mr. LESLIE: I think we have all listened
with attention to the programme outlined
for us by the Noble Viscount. Although at
first sight I think we should all be disposed
to say that we would have liked rather more
time to consider some of the measures, at
the same time, knowing what we do know
about the Bills-and we cannot conceal from
ourselves the facts as we do know them-
I think we must admit that in the circumstances we can do all that is necessary in
the time suggested. Personally I should certainly not be inclined to oppose the suggestion of the Noble Viscount.
I quite agree that it would be very inconvenient to most of us to meet here next
Tuesday, and I cannot see that any object
would be served by doing so. It may be,
of course, that this House may decide to
reject some of the measures on Second Reading, or it
be
may
may not desire to
that the House
take that course.
In any case I believe, subject of course to
the opinions of others, that the time allotted
will be found sufficient for the purpose, although I am bound to say that it is a short
allowance, and one would have preferred that
more consideration should be given to these
Bills. However, I shall not oppose the proposal.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I should like to inform the House
that Mr. Speaker has asked me to notify the
Senate that the ballot for the Members of the
Senate who have to retire under the Act of
1920 will take place directly after the sitting
on Wednesday next, instead of Tuesday next,
as was originally intended.
Col. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: May I ask
when the term of office of the retiring Members actually expires?
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: The term of office expires on June
11th, the date of their election.
House adjourned during pleasure.
House resumed.

Local Authorities Bill. 152
Belfast Corporation Bill.
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 3rd June, 1925.
And a mesage was sent to the House of
Commons to acquaint them that the Senate
agree to the amendments made by the Joint
Committee without Amendment.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
PAPERS PRESENTED.
MINISTRY OF COMMERCE : (1) Census of
Production (No. 4) Rules (Northern Ireland),
1925. (2) Census of Production (No. 5) Rules
(Northern Ireland), 1925.
Laid before the House (pursuant to Act),
and ordered to lie on the Table.
Belfast Corporation Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: That the Bill be now read the
Third time (The Marquess of Dufferin and
Ava, Chairman of Committees).
Sir WILLIAM COATES: I desire in a
word or two to express the pleasure and
satisfaction it gives me that this Bill is
about to reach the Statute Book. The chief
provision of the Bill, of course, so far as the
Corporation is concerned, is the consolidation
of rates. In the Bill the Corporation are
bringing the collection of rates into line
with most of the English boroughs. Under
the old system the collection of the Borough
Fund, the General Purposes Fund, and the
Police Rate was very intricate and difficult,
both from the ratepayers' point of view and
that of the Corporation, and the consolidation
of the rates will make for much simplification from every point of view.
In the Third Schedule of the Bill will
be found the percentage allowances which
under certain conditions may be deducted
from the normal rate. These deductions have
been very carefully worked out, and the
ratepayers will find that their interests in
this connection have been most carefully
safeguarded.
Part IV and Part V of the Bill refer to
certain works which it is proposed should
be undertaken, and gives power for the
acquirement of the necessary land. These
will be of great benefit to the city, and the
carrying out of the work will provide very
considerable work for the unemployed. I
have, therefore, great pleasure in supporting
the motion for the Third Reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Local Authorities (University
Grants) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): In moving the Second
Reading of this Bill I may, perhaps, be
allowed to remark that the reproach of
obscurity so often levelled at legislative
proposals can by no manner of means be
fairly applied to this short and very simple
Measure. I cannot find words which more
fully and more clearly describe its object
than those of the title. It is designed "To
enable the councils of counties and county
boroughs to make grants to the Queen's
University of Belfast."
Section 10 of the Irish Universities Act of
1909, besides making provision for the grant
of scholarships to individual students at our
universities, gave to our county councils
similar powers. That section was repealed
in April last on the general coming into
force of the Education Act, which, however,
continued the provisions of scholarships by
the county councils, as being expenditure of
an educational character coming properly to
be included within the expenses to be met by
the education rate to be levied under that
Act.
The second part of the repealed section of
the Irish Universities Act, which enabled
county councils to make direct grants to the
universities, as distinguished from indirect
payments through the award of scholarships
to individual students, is to be preserved for
the benefit of the university by the present
Bill.
The financial needs of the Queen's University, like those of other universities, are
very pressing indeed, and it is, therefore,
only right that we should give to our
university, a university of which we have
every reason to be proud, the same sources
of income which other universities enjoy. I
am sure the House is aware that the English
universities, other than the universities of
Oxford and Cambridge, enjoy substantial
additions to their incomes from grants made
directly by local authorities, quite apart
from those made by way of scholarships.
Short and simple though this Measure is,
it is, to my mind, one of the highest importance. In my opinion, in these days of
strenuous industrial competition between
the nations for the markets of the world,
the salvation of British industry lies in the
Local Authorities Bill-
3 JUNE 1925

Second Reading.
other counties do the same, so that there is a
disposition on the part of the counties to
respond to an appeal of this sort, and I am
sure what the Noble Marquess has said on
the Second Reading will have a good effect.
increasing application of science to manufacture. In these days when social questions
seem surrounded with so much difficulty,
when the danger of class warfare seems so
menacing, I find the best hope of the solution of our problems to lie in the education
of the democracy. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) And for these reasons, nothing that
makes for the proper and adequate provision
and also for the welfare of our University
can be deemed to be of small account.
Before I sit down, I should perhaps
emphasise the fact that this Bill lays no
obligation whatever upon the local althorities. It is simply an enabling Measure
which restores to them powers which they
have held ever since the enactment of the
Act of 1908 to which I have already referred.
It is entirely a matter for these authorities
themselves to decide the time and occasion
of their exercise. I beg to move.
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I add a word or
two to the very admirable introductory remarks which fell from the Noble Marquess,
the Leader of the House? For a long time
the Queen's University was scarcely recognised by the townspeople as a local institution, but happily that time has long since
passed, and it is a very favourable augury
that the townspeople as well as the county
authorities recognise the University as
institution of far-reaching importance and
worthy of support.
an
The increasing demands of the University
may appear irksome, but the Noble Marquess
has pointed out their importance. Demands
from time to time for the institution of new
laboratories and new chairs are inevitable,
and on that account I should like to support
very warmly the passing of this Measure.
We have already indications from some of
the counties that they are favourable to the
University, and grants are made to the
scholarship department, but we have far
larger wants than anything in the way of
scholarships would provide, and I view with
very great satisfaction indeed and great confidence this indirect appeal to the city and
to the counties for grants from time to time
to enable us to compete with other universities that are very active and energetic in the
prosecution of both literary and scientific
studies.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I hope that the county
councils will take advantage of this
Measure, and I know that one county-in fact
several counties took advantage of the legislation that was repealed by our Education
Act. The county council with which 1 was
then connected voted not only scholarships
but also made direct contributions towards
the professorial department, and has continued that up to this day. I think also
Mr. DICKSON: I would like to join with
those who have already spoken in welcoming
the Second Reading of this Bill. The county
with which I have been connected has been
in the habit of giving university scholarships, and the difficulty this year when we
came to allocate these scholarships was that
the Education Act had taken the power from
us. The introduction of this Measure will
now restore this power, and I am glad to
welcome it.
Mr. GREER: Like other hon. Members
who have spoken I am very much in favour
of passing this Bill. I think it will be of
great benefit to the University of Belfast,
which really has not been properly endowed
or supported in the past. It will restore to the
county councils a very valuable power which
they hitherto possessed, and which, in the case
of my county, County Antrim, was exercised.
The County Antrim founded, I think, four
scholarships, and I should be very sorry that
that power should be taken away from them.
As the Noble Marquess says, it is merely an
enabling Bill, and I hope it will be taken
advantage of by the county councils. I have
pleasure in supporting it.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure it would be quite
impossible for any of us to refuse an appeal
put forward by the Chancellor of Queen's
University for assistance in a matter of this
kind. But I should like to have it quite
cleared up whether this Bill is not intended
to enable county councils to make grants for
purposes other than scholarships. If I understand it correctly the power to give scholarships remains. It is entirely unaffected by
the Education Act. This is a new power-or
a renovating power-to give grants for other
matters. The amount of the burden to be
imposed on the rates will really be a matter
entirely for the county councils themselves,
and if they are extravagant they will be
responsible to their own electors. I imagine
they were not extravagant in the past, and
there is no reason to suppose that they will
be extravagant in the future. I do not
desire in any way to oppose this Measure.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
It is very gratifying that this Bill has had
so satisfactory a reception at the hands of
the hon. Members of the Senate. I think
the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie) who has just
spoken has no need of an assurance from me.
He has coined a Parliamentary phrase which
I am sure will live long in the annals of
this House" a renovating measure
"
Rent Restriction Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: A renovating power.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
"a renovating power," and I think that
is the best way of describing what has
occurred. The County Councils will have an
opportunity of contributing to the funds of
the University in the same
way as the
Universities on the other side of the water
receive assistance in this matter.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for to-morrow.
Rent and Mortgage Interest
Restriction Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I beg to move the Second Reading of the
Rent and Mortgage Interest Restriction Bill.
The Increase of Rent, etc. (War Restriction)
Act, 1915, fixed the standard rent in Ireland
for houses whose valuations did not exceed
the sum of £26 at the rent on which the
houses were let on the 3rd day of August,
1914. This Act remained in force until 1919,
when the Amending Act was passed, which
brought within the scope of the restriction
above referred to all houses in Ireland of
valuations not exceeding £52, and at the
same time allowed a statutory increase of
rent on the houses coming within the ambit
of the Act, amounting to 10 per cent. of
the standard rent.
The very rapid rise in prices following the
termination of the war during the years 1919
and 1920 led to the passing of the principal
Act, which was called the Increase of Rent
and Mortgage Interest (Restriction) Act,
1920, and which, so far as Northern Ireland
is concerned, took in a further class of
houses, namely, those houses up to and not
exceeding £78 valuation, and at the same
time authorised further statutory increases
of rent. I mean by that increases which
empowered the landlords to charge an
additional 15 per cent. on the net rent instead
of 10 per cent. on the standard rent as prescribed by the Act of 1919, the net rent being
defined as the standard rent less the rates, if
paid by the landlord.
The Act of 1920 further authorised the landlord to recover from the tenant an additional
25 per cent. of the net rent in cases where
the landlord is responsible for the whole of
the repairs, and a reduced percentage in
cases where the landlord is responsible for
only portion of the repairs. The landlord
was also authorised in cases where he paid
the rates on the tenant's behalf to recover
from the tenant any additional sums paid by
him by way of rates in consequence of an
increase in the rates.
The Act of 1920 was a temporary Act only,
and it was provided in that Act that it
should cease to operate on the 24th
June, 1923. The Imperial Parliament Act,
which was passed in July, 1923, prolonged the
principal Act until the 24th June, 1925, and
there is now a Bill before the Imperial
Parliament still further prolonging the duration of the principal Act until the 25th
December, 1927, when all classes of houses will
cease to be controlled, subject to the proviso,
however, that a further period of five years
is fixed during which the courts will be empowered to determine cases of excessive rents
or unnecessary eviction.
It has come to be regarded as an axiom
that State control of trade or industry is uneconomic-(hon. Members: Hear, hear)—and
it has been urged and held by eminent experts that the State control of house rents
has acted as a powerful deterrent against
building operations. It has, therefore, been
a matter of principle with all Governments
that the artificial conditions of rent control
rendered necessary by the war should be removed at the earliest possible date in order
that the ordinary law of supply and demand
and the influences of competition should be
able to prevail.
At the same time it has also been clearly
established that any immediate general
system of de-control would weigh heavily on
the working classes, and it has been for this
reason that the Government of Northern Ireland has, during the last three years, prolonged the operation of the Rent Restriction
Act of 1920 by the machinery of the Expiring
Laws Continuance Act. It appears, however, that the time has now come when it is
no longer necessary to make use of the indirect method of applying the Expiring Laws
Continuance Act, and when definite steps can
be taken towards removing the rent restrictions, without at the same time introducing
conditions which would weigh or tend to
weigh harshly on the tenants of smaller
houses.
It will be noted, on examination of the
present Bill, that these two conditions have
been embodied. In the first place, definite
dates have been fixed at which restrictions
are to cease to apply, and in this connection
it will be noted that the Government of
Northern Ireland have arrived at the considered decision of instituting a system of
de-control by gradual steps. The Bill provides that the houses of valuations from £52
to £78, which first became controlled by the
Act of 1920, will come out of control on the
1st November, 1926. On the 1st May, 1928,
that is a year and a half later, the houses
which were first controlled by the Rent Act
of 1919, that is, houses ranging from £26 to
£52 valuation, will be freed from control,
3 JUNE 1925
Rent Restriction Bill-

Second Reading.
with the landlords, and secondly, having regard to the long period-five years-of
security that has been provided for the
tenants of these houses, the question of contracting out of the Act does not seem to
arise.
whilst the houses of under £26 valuation,
which comprise the large bulk of our houses
in Northern Ireland, will not come out of
control until the 1st May, 1930, or in other
words the tenants of these houses will be
freed from any fear of eviction or
extravagant increases of rent for a further
period of five years from the present date.
The reasons why the Government has
adopted this principle of gradual de-control
are many. It has the advantage of safeguarding the interests of that portion of the
community which have most to fear and
most to lose. On the other hand it affords
to the building community and to the landlord classes an index of the Government's
intention to remove control. Once de-control
has been effected in any particular degree,
it is believed that the psychological objection
to building will be removed.
The tenants of houses of the higher
valuations, which first become liable to decontrol, are, as a general rule, persons who,
in the unlikely event of their rents being
excessively increased, would be in a position
through the medium of the Small Dwellings
Acquisition Acts, to buy or to build houses
for their own occupation. Consequently, it
is not considered that there would be any
great hardship in removing control of these
higher valuation houses on the dates specified
in the Bill.
As regards the tenants of the lower valuation classes-I mean all those houses of
valuations of £26 and under-a period of five
years further grace has been accorded to
them in the Bill. It is hoped that during
that period building operations in Northern
Ireland will have reached such a stage as to
remedy substantially, if not actually to relieve, the present house shortage, and so
bring the rents of houses out of the
region of artificial inflation into something
more in accordance with competition.
this connection the Government has taken
every possible step within its resources to
expedite house building in Northern Ireland,
and a Bill will be shortly introduced making
provision for further financial assistance in
the case of more expeditious building.
In
Consequential on the subject of de-control
it will be observed that, in order to pave the
way for the de-control of the two higher
classes of houses, provision has been inserted
in the Bill enabling the landlords and
tenants of these houses to contract out of
the Act prior to the date on which the houses
respectively become subject to de-control. It
will be noted that no such provision in the
nature of contracting out of the Act has been
made for the lower valuation houses. The
reasons for this are fairly obvious. In the
first place the tenants of smaller houses
rarely, if ever, enter into long-term leases
Another point in connection with de-control is in relation to vacant houses. This
matter has been carefully considered, and it
has been decided to adopt the recommendations of the Departmental Committee set up
by the Government of Northern Ireland, and
it will be observed that the Bill provides
that houses of valuation of over £8, which
become vacant after the passing of the Act,
will cease to be controlled in regard to rent,
but houses of £8 valuation, and under, which
comprise practically all the working-class
houses in the Northern Ireland area will remain subject to rent control even if they become vacant prior to the appointed date for
de-control.
The next question dealt with in the Bill is
that of repairs and the allowance to landlords for repairs provided by the Act of
1920. Section 2 of the Act of 1920 authorised
the landlord to charge an additional 25 per
cent, on the net rent in cases where he was
responsible for the carrying out of the whole
repairs of the house. This 25 per cent. increase has been the subject of some misapprehension. It is a common but fallacious idea
that this 25 per cent. was given by way of
covering the whole cost of repairs, and that
a new burden was imposed on the landlord in
consequence of this addition to the rent.
The facts are that in practically all
tenancies made in Belfast and other cases in
Northern Ireland, it has been usual for the
landlord to carry out repairs even though
under Deasy's Act, to which reference will
be made later, the obligation of carrying out
the repairs was imposed on the tenant in the
absence of anything to the contrary. The
25 per cent. allowed to the landlord was,
therefore, in respect only of the additional
cost of effecting repairs, and was not intended
to cover the whole cost of repairs.
The cost of wages and materials has fallen
appreciably since 1920. Between May, 1920,
and May, 1924, the wages paid to bricklayers
and joiners have fallen from 2s 5d to 1s 9d
an hour, and there have been similar cuts in
the wages of glaziers, plasterers, plumbers,
and slaters.
Further, it is noted that in the case of
bricks and timber the cost has fallen by as
much as 50 per cent. since 1920, while the
cost of slates has fallen by upwards of 30 per
cent., and it is not unreasonable to assume
that in the next five years the cost will not be
higher, and that it will possibly be lower
than to-day.
Rent Restriction Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
The Government is satisfied, therefore, that
it would be unjustifiable to impose on the
tenants, particularly of the smaller houses,
the continuance of this full charge of 25 per
cent., which was fixed rightly or wrongly as
being the appropriate figure representing the
difference in cost of repairs between the years
1914 and 1920. Accordingly, it will be observed
that on and after the 1st day of November,
1925, the 25 per cent. increase on foot of repairs is to be reduced to 15 per cent.
Reference has been made to what is commonly called Deasy's Act, that is the Landlord and Tenant (Ireland) Act, 1860. Under
this Act a tenant is in the absence of express
agreement to the contrary normally responsible for repairs.
The view has been held that this Act has
been over-ridden by the Rent Restriction Act
of 1920, but as the matter is not clear from
doubt steps have been taken in the present
Bill to make it clear that in any case where
the rent has been increased under Section 2
of the Act of 1920, the landlord shall be
deemed to be responsible for keeping the
dwelling-house in a reasonable state of repair.
This provision will be found in Sub-Clause
3 of Clause 6 of the Bill.
As a further corollary to this principle of
liability the Bill provides machinery whereby
the tenant can be relieved of the payment of
additional rent on foot of repairs in cases
where the landlord neglects to keep the
premises in a reasonable state of repair.
Besides dealing with the two general principles of de-control and repairs already referred to, the Bill makes special provision
to meet the undoubted grievances of one particular section of the landlord class, namely,
the one-house owner. Under recent legislation passed in Great Britain provision is
made whereby a man who purchased a house
prior to the 31st December, 1921, and who
required the house for his own occupation,
or occupation by his father or mother or by
a son or daughter over 18 years of age, was
entitled to obtain possession without having
to provide alternative accommodation for the
tenant.
The question of the adoption of similar
provision for Northern Ireland has been
and although the
considered,
carefully
Departmental Committee set up for Northern
Ireland in 1923 recommended that a similar
provision should be introduced into Northern
Ireland, the Government has, after careful
consideration, arrived at the conclusion that
this provision would be unnecessarily wide
in its effect, and also that it leaves a way
open for abuses. Accordingly, the Government has decided to restrict this privilege to
the case of the one-house owner who requires
the house for his own occupation, but as an
off-set the qualifying date which is to entitle
the one-house owner to possession of his
house without having to provide alternative
accommodation has been post-dated to the
1st May, 1923.
In other words the man who purchased
his house over two years ago in the hope
that the Rent Restriction Act would have
lapsed and who requires the house for his
own occupation will be entitled to get
possession after having given six months'
notice, and without the necessity of having
to provide alternative accommodation.
of
This privilege will not apply to a very
large number of cases, possibly not more
than a thousand, in the whole area
Northern Ireland, and from the representations made to the Government from various
sources it would appear that those onehouse owners are labouring at the moment
under considerable disabilities and
necessary hardships, and it appears to the
Government that greater hardship would be
imposed on them by depriving them of their
houses any longer than can be imposed on
the present tenants who have had the benefit
of the houses for so long.
un-
The above points represent the essential
features of the Bill, and it is unnecessary
at the moment to enter into the minor
points which are generally of a consequential
nature.
I am proposing in Committee to introduce
two small amendments which I hope will
clear up the question of the landlord's
liability for repairs in the past where he
has received the permitted increase of rent.
I will bring those two amendments in on
the Committee Stage. I beg to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am very
pleased the noble Marquess, the Leader of
the House, has gone to such trouble in
explaining this Bill, because to an amateur
legislator it is a little bit difficult to understand, as so much reference has been made
to previous Acts on the same subject. There
is considerable interest being taken in this
Bill. I do not know that there is any
question that is affecting the people more at
the present time than that of rents, and they
are not all of one opinion on the question.
A considerable number are of opinion that
control of rents has been injurious to the
private building of houses, but on the other
hand there are a large number who think
and believe that control is absolutely
necessary, and for a longer period than the
Government is providing for in this Bill.
I suppose we must all admit that control
cannot continue for ever, and that some
change will have to be made. I agree that
the Government has taken a very wise course
in deciding that decontrol shall take place
gradually. I do not think that the proposal
Rent Restriction Bill-
Second Reading.
3 JUNE 1925
to de-control a certain class of house next
year will injure anyone, because there is
such a number of that class of house being
built that I believe the supply is equal to the
demand. On the other hand, I am afraid
that so far as the workers' houses are concerned it will be impossible in the course of
five years to have provided sufficient to meet
the demand. The Government probably
intend, as the noble Marquess said, to introduce another Bill with the object of offering
some further inducement to expedite the
building of houses, but I am very much
afraid that five years hence there will not
be a sufficient number of houses erected to
satisfy the demand of the working classes.
However, I am very glad that the Government proposes to continue control for even
five years. Without that provision it would
leave an opening for any greedy landlord or
other person to impose excessively on the
workers in regard to house rents, and I
do not think there could be a worse
time than the present for the workers to be
so imposed upon. Unfortunately unemployment is worse now than it has been for some
years past, and, therefore, it would inflict a
terrible injury on the workers if control was
removed from that class of house Although,
as I have said, the Government has taken a
wise course in de-controlling houses gradually,
I am of the opinion that control of the rents
of working-class houses should be extended
even further that is proposed under this Bill.
One point in the Measure I am strongly
in favour of is the restoration to the onehouse owner of the house he bought for his
own occupation. I have known many cases
where inability to obtain possession of such
a house has entailed considerable hardship
on the owner. There are many workers who
during their lifetime make sacrifices in order
to save sufficient money to acquire a house
to live in in their old age, but owing to the
operation of the Rent Restrictions Acts they
have been unable to obtain possession of the
property they bought. I am very pleased
the Government has now made it possible
for these people to get possession of their
houses. At the same time one has got to
realise that there are other people who will
reap benefit from this concession.
Small investors who bought property without possession a few years ago at a cheap
price because there was no expectation that
Government control would be removed so
early, will benefit to a considerable extent
by the removal of this restriction. But the
class of people who will mostly benefit are
those who bought a house for their own
occupation. I have in mind one person who
served his time in the workshop with me. He
bought a house in Bangor and he told me
that that house is occupied by a man and
his wife. It is a larger house than the one
my friend occupies, although he has a larger
family than has the present tenant of his
house in Bangor. The removal of this restriction will be a God-send to that man, who will
now be able to get possession of his house at
the seaside from where he can travel backwards and forwards to his work in Belfast.
The Noble Marquess said he was of opinion
-and I am not sure whether he said it was
his own opinion or the opinion of the
Government-
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
It is the same thing.
That
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
the control of house property had restricted
building to a considerable extent, but I
cannot understand for the life of me how
you can control profiteering if there is no
restriction on the increase of rents. The
supply and demand seems to regulate the
price in every respect. Had there been no
control on the rents of workers' houses when
there was such a considerable scarcity I am
afraid it would have been a splendid thing
for the profiteer. I am satisfied that control
was really necessary during the time houses
were so scarce. After all we have pulled
through, and I believe that with this
gradual scheme which is introduced now we
shall be able in a few years to see normal
conditions resumed. There should then be
houses sufficient to meet the demands of the
workers. This was one of the cheapest
districts to live in so far as house rent is
concerned in pre-war days. I know that in
other cities the house rent was twice as
large as in Belfast, and I would be glad if
the time would come when houses would be
rented to the workers at the reasonable
rents obtaining in pre-war days.
Mr. GREER: The Leader of the House
has explained in his usual clear and precise
manner the rather complicated and
contentious provisions of this Bill, and
I am sure it will pass the Second
Reading. We all agree that it should. I
should like to make a few observations in
regard to the situation in the North of
Ireland that led up to restriction so far as
we are concerned. Hon. Members will remember that the Restriction Act came about
mainly because of difficulties on the Clyde
and in Glasgow and large towns in England
where there was very serious overcrowding
in consequence of the shortage of houses, and
a general Act was passed in 1915 by the
Government which was then in power. The
record of Belfast, I consider, is a very good
Before the Restriction Acts
thought of, almost immediately after the war
started, I remember being much struck by
seeing in the newspapers an account of a
one.
were
F
Rent Restriction Bill-
THE SENATE
Second Reading.
[Mr. Greer]
meeting that was held by the Auctioneers'
and Agents' Society in Belfast. This
society is a very large society extending
over England, Scotland and Ireland, and
has at least 5,000 or 6,000 members. The
branch in Belfast met shortly after the war
begun, and passed a resolution that so far
as they were concerned they would raise no
rents during the war. No doubt there were
rents raised to an extraordinary extent in
a few individual cases, but on the whole 1
do not think that in Belfast there was much
overcharging of rents.
The way in which houses were built in
Belfast was this. People belonging to the
mercantile and professional classes, as well
as farmers, who had sold their farms, and
had some money in hand, would take a lease
of a tract of ground in the neighbourhood
of Belfast, sufficiently large to make a street
or a portion of a street upon it. They would
then borrow money-some of them at any rate,
because none of them were very wealthy
people and begin to build. They would
build one house on this site, and completely
finish it, and then proceed to sell it or let
it. They would then begin another house,
and so on, until they exhausted the whole
site. Well, they made very good profits at
that time, and numbers of persons did embark on that kind of speculative building.
That was done at the end of the last century,
and the result was that in 1906 Belfast was
over-built. It was calculated that there were
something like 10,000 houses over-built without any tenants for them, because there were
no tenants to come. Building operations
ceased then for some time. About the year
1911 the supply was about equal to the
demand, and from 1911 on the shortage began.
In 1915, at the time of the first Restriction
Act. it was estimated that there was a shortage of 10,000 houses. There were 52,000
houses of £8 valuation and under-that is
to say, workers' houses; 25,000 of from £8
to £20 valuation, and 5,000 over £20 valuation. It was further stated that the natural
increase in the population required at least
from 1,500 to 2,000 new houses to be built
every year.
, I want to point out that the class of persons
who became owners of house property in
the way I have mentioned were not the class
that are usually called landlords' class, but
were persons who went into the business in
order to get a reasonable profit. They succeeded in getting a reasonable profit until
the war came on. At first I was rather surprised that the result of the war, which
took so many of our men away to France,
should have led to a shortage at all. A very
large number of our men did go to the war.
I made inquiries, and found there were other
causes which contributed to the shortage. In
the first place emigration was not allowed;
it ceased by operation of the law. Building
materials were largely commandeered by the
Government for its own purposes, and there
was feverish activity in our shipyards,
munition works, and factories for manufacturing war material. That led to high prices
being given to the workers and to the people
flocking in from the country in large numbers
to the shipyards. I understand that in some
of the shipyards they never refused employment to any able-bodied man who applied
for work. They were always glad to get him.
All that increased the shortage of houses in
Belfast. Then a number of young men got
married just before they went off to the war,
and that was
also another contributing
cause.
With regard to the Bill itself, I think it is
an excellent attempt on the whole to deal
fairly with a very awkward and difficult position. There are some points that I want to
call attention to on the Committee stage.
As
I say, I think on the whole it is a very satisfactory Bill. I am not quite satisfied in my
own mind that the ten per cent. should be
taken off the cost of repairs, because really if
the landlord is to do very much in the way
of repairs 20 or 25 per cent, was barely sufficient to cover it. I see that the ten per cent.
that was allowed to be put on to the rent in
the case of sub-letting a house is not put into
this Bill, so that there is really a reduction
of 20 per cent. so far as the landlord is concerned. I am not at all sure that that is not too
much. I approve of the provisions in regard
to the restriction of the right to possession.
Of course in a Bill of this kind it would be
impossible to please everyone. I think it
must be a pretty fair Bill, because everyone
is not pleased with it. On the whole, I think
it is a satisfactory Bill.
The situation will not be cleared up until
a sufficient supply of houses is obtained, and
therefore I welcome the Bill to be introduced
to the House at a later stage to facilitate the
building of houses. I do not agree with my
hon. Friend (Mr. Joseph Cunningham) that
restriction does not have a bad effect on the
building of houses. I think it has stopped
completely the building of houses by private
enterprise, and there are so many houses to
be built that we will never be able to overtake the shortage unless we can find some
way of restoring private enterprise. Houses
will not be built by local authorities and
corporations in sufficient numbers, and therefore we must do something to restore private
enterprise. A Committee has been appointed
in England to consider the cheapest method
of building houses and the cheapest material
to build them with, and I think a solution
will be found on their decision. I believe it
will be found that the cheaper class of house
can be built for £350 or so. If a house costs
£350 a grant of £80 will be available and pos-
Rent Restriction Bill.
Rules Publication Bill.
3 JUNE

!
sibly £40 more, which would reduce the cost
of the house to a price which would encourage
private enterprise, because there would be a
chance of making a reasonable profit out of
the house when built. I have very much
pleasure in supporting the Second reading.
Mr. LESLIE: Like my hon. Friend (Mr.
Greer) I welcome this Bill, and I should like
if I may to commend particularly the line
taken by the Government in accelerating the
period of de-control in respect of houses. I
also learn with satisfaction that the rateable value rather than the actual rent has
been taken as the measure for valuation. 1
have only one small point to mention, and
I would like the Government to be so kind
as to look into it before the Committee stage,
and that is, amongst the Machinery Clauses
of the Bill, Nos. 7 and 8, there seems to me
to be some little confusion as between the
certificate and the report as to repairs. We
have it provided that a certificate that the
house has not been put into a reasonable
state of repair shall be received in evidence.
We have it also provided that when a house
has been put into proper repair a report
shall be made, and further on again we have
a certificate coupled with a report as an
alternative. I imagine that the intention is
that the certificate and the report should
have equal privileges, but I confess at first
sight it seems doubtful. venture to
suggest that it should be looked into before
we deal with the Bill on the Committee
stage, which is likely to be taken to-morrow.
I believe the Bill as a whole will be found
of considerable assistance in solving a
problem of very great difficulty, and I
support it.
The
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I have no great
knowledge of conditions in a large industrial centre like Belfast, but I have some
knowledge of them in the country.
housing question there, of course, is not so
acute for various reasons, one being that the
Boards of Guardians built a great number of
labourers' cottages for a considerable
number of years, and another being the
flow of population from the country to the
towns owing to the country being so much
laid out in grass, and tillage not paying. I
welcome the prospect of de-control because
since control was established I have no
knowledge of any houses having been built
in the country or adjoining any considerable
town except for the occupation of the owner
or for sale. I do not think it is satisfactory
from the working man's point of view that
he should be compelled to buy his house, because naturally he wants to transfer his
labour to the market where he will find it
remunerative. He has to break up and
Ieave his home to get work. I have
experience of it in the case of small farmers
who have had to go to Scotland and England to work and leave their families
behind. The result is very unsatisfactory. I
think, therefore, we can look forward with
great pleasure to the time when this Act
will pass out of existence. At the same
time it was absolutely necessary, no doubt,
as a war measure. It was simply as a war
measure it was desirable to interfere with the
flow of supply and demand.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
