CLAUSE 7 (Suspension of increase of ing anything to the contrary contained in
rent on ground of disrepair.)
the tenancy agreement or implied therein
by law."
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The hon Member (Mr. Leslie) asked questions
on the Second Reading, and perhaps I might
be allowed to give him the information which
he requires on the Question that Clause 7
stand part.
The word "certificate" applies to a
specific document signed by an executive
officer of the sanitary authority, and indicates
in detail the actual works, if any, required
to be executed in order to put a dwellinghouse into a reasonable state of repair. The
certificate can be used as documentary evidence by the tenant in resisting a claim for
permitted increase of rent on the grounds of
repairs.
The word "report" in sub-section 2 of
Clause 7 is intended merely to cover the
statement by the sanitary authority that the
works mentioned in the certificate have been
properly carried out, and is not necessarily
required as documentary evidence in the
courts. The question of the terms to be used
in this connection is of trivial importance,
and in the opinion of the Government it
would be ludicrous to make any change in the
Bill now in this connection. I hope that
answers the questions which the hon. Member asked me on the Second Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I am quite content, and I
thank the Government. I found myself that
part of the expression was borrowed from an
earlier Act. I must say in both Clauses 7
and 8 the use of the word "report" is a
little obscure, but I have no doubt but that
if having regard to the experience of usage
it is shown that no inconvenience has been
experienced we may safely leave the matter
as it stands.
*
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Provisions as to repairs).
*
*
(4) Where the landlord has, whether before
or after the passing of this Act, served a
notice to increase the rent of a dwellinghouse by the maximum amount permitted
by paragraph (d) of sub-section (1) of
section two of the principal Act or by that
paragraph as amended by this Act and the
tenant has paid the increased rent in
accordance with such notice, the landlord
shall be deemed to be responsible for
keeping the dwelling-house in a reasonable
state of repair, as if he had entered into
an express covenant in that behalf.
*
*
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: }
beg to move in Clause 8, page 13, line 12,
after the word "shall" (the landlord shall be
deemed) to insert the words, "notwithstand-
Under the Landlord and Tenant (Ireland)
Act, 1860, which is commonly known as
Deasy's Act, a tenant is responsible for repairs in the absence of any express agreement to the contrary. In the case of the
normal tenancies in Belfast this Act has
been generally ignored, and the landlords
have in the majority of cases, without any
express covenant to this effect, regarded
themselves as being responsible for the repairs, and on the strength of that assumption they demanded and received the
additional 25
per cent. allowed by the
principal Act of 1920.
This Amendment will make it clear
beyond doubt that notwithstanding anything
in the Act which I have just mentioned, or
in any covenant or agreement entered into
between the landlord and the tenant, the
landlord shall be deemed to have been
responsible in any cases where he has served
the notice of increase of rent and has received the increased rent.
Amendment agreed to
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The next Amendment in the same Clause is
in line 13, after the word "be" (to be responsible) to insert the words, "and always
to have been since immediately prior to the
service of such notice." This is consequential on the previous amendment
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Powers of Courts to determine
certain questions).
Mr. MAGEE: I beg to move after Clause
9 to insert:-
Section fifteen of the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Act, 1851, shall have effect
as though two pounds five shillings were
substituted for one pound in Sub-section
(1) of the Section aforesaid.
This Amendment is intended to extend the
jurisdiction of the Petty Sessions Courts. At
present that jurisdiction extends to cases of
weekly and monthly lettings where the rent
does not exceed £1 per month. The recommendation that this Amendment should be
carried into effect was passed by the
Committee which sat to consider the
Rent Restrictions Act, and according to the
printed Report no member of that Committee dissented from that recommendation.
It was in 1851 that the jurisdiction of the
Petty Sessions Court was fixed, and there
201 Rent Restrictions Bill-
4 JUNE 1925

Committee.
!
have been enormous changes since then.
There is no comparison between values at
that time and now. In 1851 a tenant who
paid over £1 a month in rent was looked
upon as a man of means, and he could afford
the luxury of any Court he liked to go to.
At the present time, largely owing to increased taxation, rents have risen so high
that there are very few dwelling-houses of
any description with a rent which is under
4s 7d per week. The consequence is that
practically every ejectment which has to be
brought must come before the County Court.
This
What is the result of this? Proceedings
for an ejectment at the Petty Sessions Court
can be carried out at a cost of half a
sovereign. In the case of a tenant paying
over £1 a month whose rent falls into
arrears a notice to quit is served on him
and then an ejectment process is issued.
This costs about 30/-. In the course of a few
days the process has to be entered.
doubles the cost. If a decree is taken out
and lodged with the Sheriff for execution the
cost becomes too cruel for words. Imagine
the poor unfortunate man who cannot afford
to pay his rent of 5/- or 6/- a week having to
make up not only his arrears of rent but
this cruel cost of legal proceedings, which
goes into the pockets of the legal profession.
It is not right.
I was told the question had been fully considered in another place. I was there when it
was considered, and the consideration it received was very scant, because certain people
were not agreeing with certain other people,
and when temper arises over any question
we do not give it the thought and attention
we ought to. The only valid reason I can
suggest for anyone declining to accept the
Amendment I have moved is that it would
inflict an injustice. I cannot see where any
injustice can arise, because if you accept my
Amendment and the jurisdiction of the Petty
Sessions Court is extended, it will always be
open to the landlord or the tenant, if he
feels he has a grievance, to appeal to the
Recorder's Court and have the expensive
luxury of paying the enormous costs
incurred.
It has been suggested to me that this is not
a proper matter for inclusion in the Rent
Restrictions Bill. If it is not proper we
have precedent for it, because Section 17 of
the Act of 1920 alters the jurisdiction by
empowering the County Court to deal with
any questions under the Act which were
previously confined to the High Court. If
the Mother of Parliaments agrees to the
principle of putting an Amendment of this
nature into a Rent Restrictions Act I fail to
see why the Parliament of Northern Ireland
should be so superior that it cannot do the
same. I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I am afraid that it is quite impossible for
the Government to accept this Amendment,
involving as it does a most important change
in the basis of jurisdiction of the Petty
Sessions Courts. This Amendment is similar
in effect to an Amendment moved by the
Opposition in another place in the Committee
stage on this Bill. That Amendment was
rather more easy to follow than it is in its
present guise to-day. It was simply that
"all claims for arrears of rent of houses
under twenty-six pounds valuation shall be
decided by the Petty Sessions Court."
The hon. Senator's Amendment of the Summary Juridiction Act of 1851 would have the
same effect, as it would extend the powers of
the Petty Sessions Court to adjudicate on
lettings of two pounds five shillings a month
and under, that is, twenty-seven pounds per
annum.
In the first place, the determining of
questions dealing with rent, standard rent,
net rent, repairs, etc., under the Rent
Restriction Act is of the most complicated
character and has formed the most difficult
part of the work of the County Court Judges.
Such questions with all their difficulties are
not appropriate to be dealt with by the Petty
Sessions Courts. The magistrates, from the
very nature of their appointment, are not
competent to deal with such intricate cases.
The result would conceivably be that practically every case coming before them would
be appealed, and instead of reducing the cost
both for the landlords and tenants it might
be found in the end that the introduction
of such a change would prove much more
costly, and involve considerably more delay
than under the present system.
In the second place, the acceptance of this
Amendment would be a departure from the
governing principle of the Summary Jurisdiction Act. Such a departure would have
to be dealt with by definite and considered
amendment of that Act, rather than by a
side wind such as this Amendment of the
Rent Restrictions Bill. Under these circumstances I feel that it is impossible for the
Government to accept the Amendment of
the hon. Gentleman.
Mr. GREER: I feel personally in a rather
difficult position, because the Departmental
Committee, of which I was chairman, recommended that this Clause should be inserted,
and the Government have not inserted it in
the Bill. If my recollection serves me right
I think the matter was brought before us
by a Derry association, and it passed without
much discussion in the Committee. They did
not consider the effect of it very fully. It
appears to me that it is not at all certain
that the costs would be reduced in every case.
They certainly would be reduced if a case
Rent Restrictions Bill--

THE SENATE
[Mr. Greer]
were brought at petty sessions or a court of
that description in a city like Belfast or
Derry, but if the case were brought in a
country district, not a town, where solicitors
do not reside, there would probably be legal
assistance required by both complainant and
defendant, and in that case the costs might
be considerably more. Then I think if a case
were brought in the first instance at petty
sessions, neither the complainant nor the defendant would be satisfied with the decision
of that court, because, as the Noble Marquess
has said, very often extremely difficult questions of law arise in these cases, and the

on the point, although it is against my

CLAUSES 1 (Provisions as to application
of s. 1 of 13 and 14 Geo. 5, c. 30 in rural
districts), and 2 (Increase of grant in respect
of construction of certain houses), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Exemption of houses from
rates).
(1) A local authority may, in pursuance
of a general resolution approved by the
Ministry and applying to all houses in the
area of the authority which comply with
the prescribed conditions, exempt all such
houses from payment of rates leviable by
that local authority in respect of those
houses for a period not exceeding ten years
from the date of the completion of the
houses as certified under section one of
the principal Act.
5 p.m.

Magee, John Gilbert
Massereene and Ferrard, Viscount
¡McDowell, William John
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
Woods, Joseph Andrew
(2) A county council may, in pursuance
of a general resolution passed on the application of the council of a rural district
situate within their county and approved
by the Ministry, exempt from poor rate,
for a like period as is mentioned in subsection (1) of this section, all houses within
the rural district which comply with the
prescribed conditions.
(3) In this section, the expression "prescribed conditions" means-
(a) the conditions for the payment of
grants under section one of the principal Act as amended by section two of
the Act of 1924 and this Act; and
(b) the condition that the house has been
commenced after the passing of this
Act.
(4) The powers conferred by this section
shall be in addition to and not in derogation of any powers of a local authority or
rural district council under sub-section (9)
of section one of the principal Act or subsection (4) of section two of the Act of
1924.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause 3,
page 2, line 27, to leave out "payment of "
("houses from payment of rates").
This Amendment is a very small matter.
I suggest we had better take out the two
words "payment of" in line 27 because similar
words do not appear in line 34. If we leave
them in we are making an apparent distinction between the urban rate and the
rural rate, which I am quite sure we do not
mean, and it is desirable that we should
bring the two sub-clauses into conformity.
In doing that we must either leave out the
words here or put them in in the other subclause, and since the insertion of the two
Rent Restrictions Bill-
Committee.
4 JUNE 1925
IT

words is not absolutely correct, if we examine them very carefully-it is not the
house that pays the rate; it is the occupier
or owner-I suggest that we take the two
words out in order to secure conformity.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
I was not very clear as to the purport of the
Amendment which has been moved by the
hon. Member, but after his explanation, while
I am clearer, 1 am inclined to suggest to him
that it is not a matter really of supreme importance. I think he means it more
drafting Amendment than an Amendment of
substance, and I would suggest to him that
we leave the Bill as it is drafted at the
present moment, unless he feels very strongly
on the matter.
as a
Mr. LESLIE: I think if we leave it as it
stands it is really passing a Bill from this
House not quite in the usual manner. We
ought not really to have different phrases in
two successive sub-clauses. It does suggest
that there is some distinction which there
certainly is not. I do not think there is any
authority for the suggestion. I certainly
should prefer to leave out the words, but at
the same time, at this late hour of the evening, I would not like to go so far as to ask the
Committee to divide.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in line 28
to leave out "ten" ("not exceeding ten
years") and insert five." This is a more
important matter. The question of whether
we should allow a term of exemption of houses
from payment of rates by their owners or
occupiers is one that occupied our attention
for a short time yesterday afternoon. The
point at issue seems to be as to giving greater
elasticity, and I am generally in favour of
elasticity when power is to be conferred. The
question seems to be as to whether it is better
here to give elasticity for a period of ten
years or whether, having regard to the fact
that the experiment is distinctly a new one,
we ought not, in the first instance at all
events, to limit it to a period of five years.
It may be claimed that ten years would act
as a greater inducement to the builder to
build houses. I am afraid I do not think that
the owner or occupier ever gets any advantage
out of the non-payment of rates whatever it
is. I think that the price or rent would be
increased in order to provide for that. But
during the ten years, if we make it ten years,
and if the local authorities concerned go the
full length of their tether, and they are not,
as the noble Marquess told us yesterday,
bound to give ten years-but if they do so we
are paying £80 grant, plus either £100 loan
or £40 grant from the local authority, plus
exemption from rates for ten years. That is
a very considerable concession and the concession of ten years' freedom from rates does
impose some charge, I think, on the ratepayers.
If we can shorten that to five, if we can
satisfy ourselves that five years is a sufficient
attraction we shall antedate the period when
the local authority gets a profit out of its investment, and the property becomes rate-paying; and we shall also decrease the unfairness
as regards other ratepayers. If we exempt
from rates for so long as ten years we give a
man an opportunity of getting three of his
children educated absolutely free of charge,
and so an advantage over a man whose family
arrangements are perhaps of a different character. And also during that period the occupier
will not share responsibility in the matter of
malicious injuries. I attach a great deal of
importance to all occupiers being responsible
for payments under malicious injury claims.
And as far as malicious injury claims are
concerned we must not think of the very
heavy charges. We have other smaller
malicious injuries which always arise. It
seems to me very dangerous to exempt from
payment towards them any considerable body
of house-owners for any length of time.
Some may think as we are making a concession we may as well do it handsomely, but
it is not our own money we are dealing with;
it is the money of others. Others may think
five years sufficient. I confess I myself would
prefer five years. If this Bill, when it becomes an Act, is extended, and I think it may
be, then of course power can be given. As it
stands I think we should be wiser if we
limited the period of exemption to five years.
I beg to move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The ingenuity of the hon. Member leads him
to see great dangers in the provision of ten
years in this Bill-prognostications which I
sincerely hope will not be fulfilled. The Bill,
as it stands, gives discretionary power to
local authorities to fix the period for which
they will exempt houses from rates, and 1
am bound to say that I cannot see that there
will be any great alacrity on the part of
the local authorities unduly to extend the
term during which property will be exempt
from rates. It might be an inducement to
build, and whereas they will have at the end
of ten years a valuable property on which
rates may be levied, if that inducement is not
successful they will have no property on
which to levy rates at all.
It is also to be observed that in the case
of urban districts the approval of the
Ministry is to be obtained for the Council's
resolution, whereas in the rural areas the
Rent Restrictions Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
rural district councils must in the first
instance make representations to the county
councils, and the action of the county couneils is also subject to the approval of the
Ministry. Accordingly it will be seen that
every possible step is being taken in drafting the Bill to insure against injudicious or
inappropriate resolutions under this Clause.
There would be no object in my judgment in
tying the councils down to five years, as in
most cases-in nearly every case-they will
be the best judges of the needs of their
district in regard to houses, and of the
value or otherwise of exempting new houses
from rates for a specific period. For these
reasons I feel sure the hon. Member (Mr.
Leslie) need have no apprehensions that the
provision in this Bill will be abused, and
that the owners, as he imagines, will get
their children educated while they are
exempt from rates. I hardly think the hon.
Member need have any apprehensions on
that score, and I would venture to suggest
that he allows the ten years to stand, in the
belief that the County Councils are the best
judges of the case, and that they will carry
out their duties in the interests of the communities which they represent.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon. Member
press his Amendment?
Mr. LESLIE: I do not think it is worth
while pursuing the matter now. I beg leave
to withdraw.
Amendment by leave withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 3, line
9, at end to add the following new subsection:
("(3) Any general resolution to which
this section applies shall have effect only
in respect of houses commenced either
after or not earlier than three months
before the passing of such resolution.")
As the Bill stands before us it does not
seem to me to provide any means of determining the time during which this general
Resolution exempting from rates has to be
passed. We have to rely principally on the
Ministry, and I must say that the Ministry
seem to be prepared to take a great deal of
responsibility upon themselves. I am not at
all sure that Parliament ought to leave all
the responsibility in this matter to the
Ministry. I think Parliament itself ought
to do something partly to protect the
Ministry and partly to protect the
councils from pressure that may be brought
to bear upon them.
So far as I can see there is nothing which
requires that this general Resolution is to be
passed-if it is passed at all-before the
building of the houses is actually commenced,
although I think that is really the intention.
There is distinctly nothing to prevent a
council at any time being asked to pass what
might be in fact a retrospective resolution.
It is obvious that, if these houses are built
in any great quantity, and if they are
exempted in
any great quantity, they
must bring into the localities a certain
number of people who will have votes
and may
use them. It seems to me
that such a protection as this sub-section
suggests would be of value; but it may be
thought that this is a matter in which we
should leave everything to chance, give the
freest rein to our optimistic feelings and rely
on the councils doing the right thing, which
I am sure they will do, and not being unduly
pressed to do something, and on the Ministry
being able to keep everything straight. I
think we ought to protect the ratepayers by
the insertion of this sub-section, and I beg to
move.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The Clause as it stands clearly provides that
exemption from rates shall only apply in the
case of houses commenced after the passing
of the Act, and it will be observed that this
limitation pervades the whole Bill. In other
words, the extension of the subsidy to rural
districts applies only in the case of houses
commenced after the passing of the Act.
Similarly the £20 bonus for the acceleration
of building only applies also after the passing
of the Act. I feel it would be inconsistent to
apply a different principle in regard to the
powers of local authorities to exempt from
rates. I hope the hon. Member (Mr. Leslie)
will not press this Amendment, because, as I
have said, it is inconsistent with the whole
Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: I must confess that I see
nothing in this proposed sub-section which
interferes with the general tenour of the Bill
that the Bill is only to apply to houses commenced after the commencement of the Act.
That is all right. What I am out to protest
against is the general resolution being passed
after houses built after the commencement
of the Act have been completed. What I am
afraid of is pressure being brought to bear
on some Council who have not gone the full
length of the powers given to them. That is
the point I am looking at. I am not suggesting that anything in Clause 3 could apply
to a house which was commenced before the
commencement of the Act. I think that, as
I said before, is not quite clear.
The CHAIRMAN: Does the hon. Member
persist in his Amendment?
Mr. LESLIE: No. I withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
209 Rent Restrictions Bill.

stand part of the Bill.

213 Protection of Married Women.
Further Protection of Married
Women.
5 JUNE 1925
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
It is proposed that this House should now
adjourn during pleasure.
Mr. LESLIE: May I, by leave of the
House, take this opportunity of asking the
noble Marquess a question of which I have
given him private notice? To put it shortly,
I want to ask what are the prospects when
we meet again after the vacation of a Bill on
the lines of the Bill which I recently introduced, and which the House did me the
honour of reading a Second time; a Bill
dealing with the further protection of
married women, and the necessary amendment of the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland)
Acts.
It will be within the recollection of the
House that the Government agreed to the
Second Reading on the distinct understanding that the Committee stage should not
immediately be pressed. It was, I think,
universally agreed that it would have been
the greatest possible mistake to have done
anything tending towards rushing a Measure
which obviously required a good deal of consideration on the part of Parliament, the
Government Departments concerned, and
public outside. Moreover, there
another
reason for delay, and
that was that
Bill was at
that time in the Imperial Parliament--and it
has not yet received the Royal Assent-and
it would be difficult for us to deal satisfactorily with the matter until the final form of
the Imperial Measure is before us.
the
was
a
There was also another question which the
Government was so kind as to undertake to
examine, and that was whether the Imperial
Act of 1878 did or did not apply to Ireland,
or rather I should say Section 4 of the
Imperial Act of 1878. The point involved was
that if that section of that Statute did appiy
to Ireland, the necessity for a Bill of the
character I have introduced was not so apparent, because that Section of that Act of
1878 did give courts of summary jurisdiction
some power of making an Order which was to
be tantamount to a judicial separation, and
giving the custody of the children, under, I
think, ten years of age, and of making payment in respect of them.
In
When I introduced my Bill I ventured to
state quite definitely that the Act of 1878 did
not apply to Ireland. I am still of that opinion,
but it must be admitted that the references
in the books are anything but plain.
my judgment the Act did not apply to Ireland
partly because by its title it limited itself to
an amendment of the Matrimonial Causes
Acts, which refers to the English code, and the
Protection of Married Women. 214
Act did not say whether it did or did not
apply to Scotland. There were other things
in the Act with which I will not trouble the
House, but it is a point of importance as to
whether the Government is now in agreement
with me that the Act does not apply to
Ireland.
As to what might perhaps be done in the
Autumn Session, in regard to this Bill, there
are two courses open. Perhaps one has a
parental affection for one's own child, but
even parental affection does not go so far as
to suggest that the child should not be sent
to school. It might be possible to amend the
Bill to which the House has already given a
Second Reading, either by Government
amendments or amendments suggested to
this House, or in the alternative, the Government might see its way to bring in a Bill of
its own.
Either of those courses might result
in our getting a Bill to come into operation
on 1st January next, and if we do that we
shall have done all that is required.
What I would now ask is that the noble
Marquess will tell us what view the Government takes as to the prospects of getting a
Measure through, and particularly if a decision has been arrived at as regards the Act
of 1878.
I am much obliged to the House for the
concession they have made in allowing me
to speak on this matter at this time.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
will ask the leave of the House to reply to
the question of the hon. Senator, who has
been good enough to give me private notice
of his intention to raise this matter. I am
afraid we are not in any better position to
express an opinion on this Bill than we were
when my noble Friend, Senator Viscount
Massereene and Ferrard, made his statement
in the Senate on 6th May. We are obtaining
the opinion of an experienced resident magistrate, but it will be appreciated that legislation would not really be practicable until
some clear indication of the views of the
public, as distinguished from official opinion,
were forthcoming. So far we have had no
such indication, and I am afraid that it must
be confessed that the public have not taken
the slightest interest in the Bill.
I think that all we can say is that the
Government will consider the position fully
during the Recess, but it would be very
unwise for me to give any pledge that legislation will be introduced until the public have
clearly indicated their general opinion. It
will follow that it would be out of the question to give any pledge regarding the introduction of legislation during the Autumn
Session.
As regards the point made by Senator Leslie
as to the application to Ireland of the Act
of 1878, it will be found on reference to the
215 Protection of Married Women.

THE SENATE
Adjournment.

[The Marquess of Londonderry]
Act of 1895, 58 and 59 Vic., c. 39, that that Act
repeals Section 4 of the Act of 1878, except
so far as it applies to Ireland." This would
seem to show that the Act of 1878 does apply
to Ireland, although it is right to mention
in support of Senator Leslie's view that the
subject matter of the Act of 1878 contains
very many provisions which are wholly inapplicable to Ireland. For instance, the references that are made to the Queen's Proctor
(there being no such official in Ireland) in
Section 2, to the Matrimonial Causes Act,
1859 (an English Statute), in Section 3, and
to an Appeal to the Probate and Admiralty
Division of the High Court of Justice (an
English court), in Section 4, would seem to
indicate that the Act does not apply to this
country.
There is, therefore, a margin of doubt
which could only be settled by the Courts.
The Government is fully alive to this somewhat unsatisfactory state of affairs, and I am
able to inform my hon Friend that, if and
when legislation is undertaken by the Government, steps will be taken to remove all the
present uncertainty.

time will be notified to hon. Members later
on.
Col. SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: Will that
be the last day on which the House will sit
prior to the Recess?
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
Yes.

TRIBUTE TO MR. SPEAKER AND
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FERRARD
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The Motion which I desire to move is "That
this House do adjourn until such day as the
Leader of the House shall appoint."
I feel that perhaps on this occasion it is
only fitting that I, as Leader of the House,
should make some reference to the four years
which have just terminated with the ending
of the present sitting of our House. As hon.
Members are aware, we are elected for eight
years, but for the purpose of allowing the
rotation to operate it is necessary that half
of the House should retire at the end of four
years, and I feel, as I have said, that it is
fitting that I should make some reference to
the period which has been so full of events
and has been responsible for passing the first
legislative programmes of the Government of
Northern Ireland. I think I shall be in
agreement with hon. Senators when I say
that we can look back on the last four years
with every satisfaction. We have had important duties placed upon us, and I think
that we can say that we have discharged those
duties in accordance with the wishes of the
population of the Six Counties on whose
behalf we officiate as Senators here. The
whole history of the Province is one which
naturally is uppermost in our minds, but it
is not on this occasion that I should be
entitled to go through that particular history.
My duty is to refer to the Senators who, by
the inexorable ordinance of the ballot, are
called upon once again to seek election.
During these four years we have lost
more than one eminent Senator, citizens of
these Six Counties, of whom we have every
reason to be justly proud. They discharged
their duties in a manner which certainly
called for our approbation, and I feel that
we shall miss their presence in this assembly.
As for those Senators who are still with us,
and I hope will be with us for long years to
come, it is possible for them to seek reelection, and I am not in a position to state
what steps will be taken in regard to that
matter. Owing to the chances of the ballot
we deeply regret that you, my Lord, have
to seek re-election. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) We had hoped that you would have
continued in your office and would have presided over us for the full term of eight
years, but by the chances of the ballot it has
been ordained otherwise, and you will be
called upon to seek re-election. I hardly
think that there will be any doubt but that
re-election will be carried out to the satisfaction certainly of all Members of the
Senate. I should like to take this oportunity
of saying how much we have valued and
appreciated the invariable courtesy and consideration which you have shown-certainly
to me personally-and I know that I am
voicing the views of all the Senators-(hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)-when I say that you
have shown it to them also. We have been
fortunate, indeed, to have one of your noble
Tribute to Senators.
Tribute to Senators.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
and illustrious family presiding over the
business of the Senate of Northern Ireland;
and we do most sincerely hope that in a very
short time to come you will be back again
and will continue to carry out those duties
which you have carried out in the past with
credit to yourself and honour to the Senate.
I beg to move that this House do now
adjourn.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I do not
wish to delay the proceedings of the House
for more than a very few minutes. The
noble Marquess the Leader of the House has
already made reference to the matter on
which I am about to say a few words. So far
as our House is concerned the ballot is a
unique proceeding. I understand it cannot
occur again, because after this period the
twelve retiring Members will retire without
being balloted for after the next four years.
I do not think we ought to part here to-day
without something being said not only by the
noble Marquess but on behalf of the others
in the way of-I do not know the proper
word, because we owe so much to you personally, my Lord. I speak as one who did not
draw a lucky number, and you, my Lord,
were unfortunate also, and I think it would
not be right to leave this assembly without
saying not only for myself as one of those
who will not return here--and I am sure
someone will speak on behalf of those who
have been fortunate-at any rate on behalf of
those who are here and those who are absent
to-day will you allow me to convey to you
how much during the time we have been
Members of this House we appreciated your
dignity in the Chair and the efficiency with
which you have carried on the business of
the House. Also may I say we are not an
unruly body of Members-but it has sometimes been necessary to give us a few words
of advice-I was a culprit once myself-and
when it was necessary it was with a smile,
like the smile of the greatest ambassador
the Empire has ever possessed-I mean His
Royal Highness the Prince of Wales; your
words of advice were given with that
gracious smile which you also possess and
which made us feel that it was a pleasure to
be called to order by you.
---
May I include in these remarks also one
who was omitted by the noble Marquess the
Leader of the House, that is, the noble
Viscount who sits beside him.
Mr. GREER: Hear, hear.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: He, also,
did not draw a lucky number. I can only
say that every one in this House, whether
fortunate or unfortunate in the ballot, desires to convey his sincere gratitude to both
for the way their duties have been carried
out, and we sincerely trust that both you and
the noble Viscount may speedily return to
those duties which you have so ably fulfilled.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Colonel SINCLAIR: As one of the original
Members of the Senate, and as one who was
fortunate enough to survive in the recent
ballot, may I be permitted to say a word or
two to re-echo the sentiments that have been
expressed so well by the noble Marquess the
Leader of the House and the right hon.
Senator Colonel Sharman-Crawford. We feel
that it will only be a very short time and
possibly a matter of form until you, my Lord
-and I should hope the noble Viscount alsoare restored to the positions which you have
so ably filled with so much efficiency and
dignity. I do not know whether I can say
I have been an unruly Member, but I think
I was called to order once. However, I quite
agree with the right hon. Senator when he
said your authority has been exercised with
dignity and pleasantness. I, along with
those other Members of the Senate who were
fortunate in the ballot, offer to you our sincere
and respectful thanks for the manner in
which the duties of the Chair have been conducted ever since the inception of the Senate.
I therefore wish to emphasise the complimentary remarks that have been made by the
noble Leader of the House and the right hon.
Senator (Colonel Sharman-Crawford)
behalf of those who have had the pleasure of
sitting under you since the Senate was first
constituted.
SPEAKER:
on
Mr.
Before putting this
Motion, perhaps you will allow me to say
how very grateful I am for the kind words
which the noble Leader of the House (the
Marquess of Londonderry), Senator Colonel
Sharman-Crawford, and Senator Colonel
Sinclair, have used in reference to myself.
It is naturally very pleasing to me that after
four years I find that I have given satisfaction to the House. I do not suppose that any
Speaker of any assembly has ever had such
a smooth path as I have had to tread during
the last four years.
I am very grateful to all hon. Senators
for their kindness and courtesy to me
during the time I have held this position. I
came to it, of course, quite new and untrained, and I have made many mistakes.
But hon. Senators have always shown great
understanding, and have forgiven the many
errors that I have made. I am indeed proud
to think that I have held the position of
Speaker of a Senate such as this, which has
done so very much to justify its position
during the last four years. I thank you very
much indeed for your complimentary remarks.
9 JUNE 1925
Tribute to Senators.

Adjournment.
T
upon to discharge. I think I may say
without fear of contradiction that the Whip
I have had to use has always been applied
very gently, and has never fallen on the backs
of hon. Senators with great force.
VISCOUNT MASSEREENE AND FER-
RARD: I feel it very difficult indeed to say
what I would like to say in reply to the very
kind remarks used towards me by Senator
Colonel Sharman-Crawford and Senator
Colonel Sinclair. My task during the past
four years has been a very light one, and I
am very pleased to think that the Senate have
appreciated the manner in which I have performed whatever duties I have been called
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Election of Speaker.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): We are met this morning in order that the Members of the Senate
might take the oath, and also for the purpose
of electing a Speaker. I should like first of
all to say in connection with the composition
of this House that the older Members welcome the new, and at the same time express
deep regret that one of our former Members,
namely, Colonel Perceval-Maxwell, is not a
Member of the Senate for the coming term.
We are all well aware of the reasons which
latterly prevented him from attending regularly to his duties, and I am sure it is the
desire of this House that we should wish him
a speedy recovery of health. We hope that
in future he may be able to devote his time
to the benefit of the Province which he has
so well served in the past.
The Motion that I desire to propose is one
that requires very few words from me, and
one which I have no doubt will be acceptable
to the House. It reads:-
That the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava be
elected Speaker of this Senate.
We have been privileged for the last four
years to work under the presiding influence of
the Marquess of Dufferin, and by the accident of the ballot, if I may use the expression,
we found that Lord Dufferin was no longer a
Member of the Senate. He was re-elected in
due course, and it is now my pleasing duty to
ask the Senate to re-elect his Lordship to the
Office which he has held for the last four
years. I know that on this occasion there is
no need for me to commend his election to
the Senate. I have had an opportunity before
of telling this House, or rather expressing the
feelings of every Member of the House as to
the manner in which Lord Dufferin discharged his duties during the four years he
was Speaker of the Senate. He has certainly
carried out his duties with that dignity and
efficiency which we naturally expected of one
who is a member of the illustrious house of
which the North of Ireland has every reason
to be proud. I feel that in the execution of
the duties of Speaker he has added further
lustre to a great historical name. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.)
The Senate of Northern Ireland, as I have
said, has now been in existence for four years.
It was constituted and set up as far as it
could be on the same lines as the Second
Chamber across the water, and we feel that
we possess the same idea of tradition and of
custom that actuates the House of Lords. 1
H
Election of Speaker.
Election of Speaker.
THE SENATE
[The Marquess of Londonderry]
think I am right in saying that the Senate
of Northern Ireland has fulfilled the expectations which we formed of it, and has discharged the duties which have been entrusted
to it in a manner consistent with the dignity
and the traditions of this Province.
It is not for me on this occasion to enter
into a brief history of our Province. We felt
in the new departure of some four years
ago that it was encumbent upon the Members
of both Houses to realise the great responsibilities which were placed on their shoulders,
and that the Assemblies which had been set
up as the legislature of the Province should
be such as would be consistent with the ambitions that we formed for the Province we
represent. I do not feel that on this occasion
I am required to say any more in connection
either with the Motion or with the Parliament of which I think we have every reason
to be proud. I therefore beg to move "That
the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava be elected
Speaker of this Senate."
Mr. GREER: I rise with much pleasure to
second in a few words the resolution which
has been so ably proposed by the Leader of
this House. As he has reminded us, the
Marquess of Dufferin bears an honoured
name, a name that is known not only
in the neighbourhood of Belfast and
over the Northern area, but throughout the whole British Empire. I consider that he has conferred a distinction on
this House by undertaking to perform the
duties of the office, and I think it is very
desirable indeed that he should continue in
the position which he has occupied so well
during the last four years. During that time
he has certainly conducted the business of the
House to our entire satisfaction.
It has been a troublesome thing to start a
new machine and to construe all the new
Regulations and Standing Orders we made, as
far as possible in conformity with the English
practice, and I think we all agree his Lordship has done it most admirably and that he
has brought to bear in the discharge of his
duties the utmost dignity, courtesy, and impartiality. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) It
is a great pleasure to me to second a resolution which I know will be unanimously accepted, and I can only say that I hope the
Noble Marquess will for many long years
occupy the position of Speaker of the Senate.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I wish to
support the resolution that has been moved,
and I am anxious to associate myself with the
remarks that have been made concerning the
work of the Marquess of Dufferin during the
period he has acted as Speaker of the Senate.
I think every one of us should be highly
pleased that his Lordship has been returned
to the Senate, and that we shall again for a
further period be favoured with his valuable
services as Speaker of the House. As one of
the workers' representatives I should like
to say there was no one who admired the
Speaker more than I did. I entered this
House with a feeling more or less of
fear or timidity, especially when I found the
Speaker wearing a wig and gown.
I found also that underneath the wig and
gown there was one who was
very considerate and very sympathetic to those who
were new to the work. It was difficult, there
is no doubt about it, for such as my colleague
and myself who had no knowledge whatever
of work such as this to try to conduct themselves in the correct way and not in a
manner that would bring any disgrace on
the people we represent, the working classes.
I have very great pleasure in supporting the
resolution, and I hope the Noble Marquess
will be long spared to occupy the honoured
position which he has filled with such
dignity, at the same time showing great consideration and kindness to those under his
care. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. DORMAN: On behalf of the Labour-
Socialist Party, whom I represent in this
Senate, I rise to support the motion so ably
proposed and seconded. I do so with the
greatest pleasure, because I had in my youth
some experience of the manifestation of the
qualities displayed by the distinguished
father and mother of the Noble Marquess,
under extreme physical difficulties on the
part of his venerable mother and extreme
political difficulties on the part of his father
when he had the honour of representing Her
Majesty Queen Victoria in the great
Dominion of Canada. I served on the lower
deck of one of Her Majesty's ships which conveyed his parents in very boisterous circumstances on their way to tour North-West
Canada at a time when there was no Canadian
Pacific Railway and when it was necessary
for Her Britannic Majesty's Governor-
General to proceed across the Trans-
Continental United States Railway to a
Pacific port in order to reach the North-
West territories of Canada.
I learned then to esteem his venerable
mother not only for her physical beauty but
for her great beauty of character and
nobility of soul. Especially did I learn to
esteem his distinguished father for the
attitude he assumed in the interest of his
monarch and his mother country at a time
when conditions in North-West Canada were
very similar to those existing in our native
country to-day, when what I will call narrowminded and provincial-minded politicians
were clamouring for a perpetuation of a
division which the Noble Marquess's distinguished father saw would have been
directly opposed to the well-being of the
great Empire which he represented in
Canada. He displayed in difficult cir-
3 SEPTEMBER 1925
Election of Speaker.

Election of Speaker.
the Senate-(Hon.
that of Speaker of
Members: Hear, hear) and how happy 1
feel to think that I shall again be carrying
out the duties which I have endeavoured to
perform during the last four years.
cumstances great patience, charity, and
firmness. One could imagine what would
have been the position of that great
Dominion to-day if those who were clamouring for the perpetuation of separation from
the vast eastern portion of the Dominion had
succeeded, what position it would be in as
part and parcel of the great Commonwealth of British Nations.
I have the honour to represent a party that
takes a broad and far-reaching view of the
present situation. We hope to see a unification of our native country. We hope to see
our country occupy such a position that those
who live in other lands will not, as they have
apparently been justified in doing in recent
years, point the finger of scorn towards Ireland and say, "Behold how these Irishmen
hate one another." We look forward to a
time when those who are outside the nation
will say, "Behold how these Irishmen love
one another." We look forward to a time
when those of us who are resident within the
national area will be able truly to say, "Behold how good, how pleasant a thing it is
for brother Irishmen to dwell together in
unity." I am sure that if the Noble
Marquess has inherited these peculiar traits
from his distinguished father he will do
something towards the unification of the
country, and he will thereby leave inscribed
indelibly on the history of Ireland the name
of the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava as his
distinguished father has left it already on
the pages of the history of the great
Dominion of Canada.
We are looking forward to a time when
Ireland will not merely be a part of the
Commonwealth of Nations composing the
British Empire, but to a time when Ireland
will take her place amongst the great
federation of the nations of the world, a
federation in which no nation will be out for
the purpose of dominating and exploiting
another in the interests of its own nationals,
but when all the nations will be out for the
development and exploitation of the resources of the earth in the interests of the
race whom God has made of one blood, and
who He intended should dwell in peace and
harmony on the face of the earth.
Mr. SPEAKER-ELECT: Mr. Deputy-
Speaker, I would like first of all to express
to the Marquess of Londonderry, who has
moved this Motion, and to Mr. Greer, Mr.
Cunningham, and Mr. Dorman, my very
grateful thanks for the kind expressions
which they have used towards myself, very
little deserved, I am afraid, but fortunately
known alone to myself how little deserved.
Also I would like to thank the House for
having accepted this Motion unanimously.
which makes the compliment all the greater.
I need hardly say how proud I shall be to
accept the honour which you have offered me,
I can only hope that the same happy relations between myself in my official
capacity and this House will continue as before, and I know that I can count on the
same support from the Members which they
have always hitherto given me. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) The House, of course,
realises that my authority as Speaker rests
entirely on the Members themselves and that
any decision which I make in the course of
our proceedings is only an attempt on my
part to carry out the wishes of the Members
themselves. I can only say, in conclusion.
that I will endeavour to carry out these
duties with all the impartiality and attention
that I can command. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.)
The Senate having again unanimously
called the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava to
the Chair, he was taken out of his place and
conducted to the Chair by the Marquess of
Londonderry and Mr. Greer.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
This afternoon we anticipate that we shall
receive from another place certain Measures
for our consideration, and I propose that we
adjourn during pleasure.
House adjourned during pleasure.
The House resumed.
Messages from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Unemployment Fund Bill and the Dangerous Drugs
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of
this House.
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Moved: That the Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Read a First time accordingly, and ordered
to be read a Second time to-morrow.
Dangerous Drugs Bill.
Moved: That the Bill be now read a First
time. (Viscount Massereene and Ferrard.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Read a First time accordingly, and ordered
to be read a Second time to-morrow.
The Mail Service.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
Adjournment.
THE MAIL SERVICE.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now adjourn.
In moving that Motion I venture to say
that it will be necessary that the House
should meet again to-morrow, at 3-15. I hope
that will be in accordance with the convenience of hon. Members.
Mr. MAGEE: There is a question which I
wish to raise. I do not think it comes within the exact province of this Government,
but I am perfectly certain that with the
influence of this Government no difficulty
will arise. It is the question of the mail
service to Londonderry and the adjoining
district. At present the service comes by
Kingstown and Holyhead, and there has been
a suggestion in the newspapers, with what
foundation I know not, but there is a possibility that the question of altering the service might be taken into consideration by
the Post Office. I would just like to ask the
noble Marquess, the Leader of the House, if
he could see his way to give us an assurance
that so far as the influence of the Government
of Northern Ireland goes no steps will be
taken which will interfere with our mail
service in such a way as to cause later deliveries or earlier despatches of the English
mail without our being consulted in the
matter, and having an opportunity of making any representations which might be
deemed desirable.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY :
The hon. Member who has just sat down has
not really given me formal notice of the question which he has raised, and therefore I
feel sure he will excuse me from giving him
a full reply, because I have been unable to
make the necessary inquiries for the purpose
of answering his question. I feel that on
behalf of the Government I should thank him
very much for his belief in the power of the
Government, which I hope will always be
justified so long as we continue to exist. The
matter is naturally one of importance, and I
would say to him that it is one which is more
under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of
Commerce than any other Ministry. In fact
the Ministry of Commerce acts as agent for
the British Government in regard to Post
Office matters. I will certainly represent the
matter to the Ministry of Commerce, and
will put before the Minister of Commerce the
fears which are in the hon. Member's mind.
Beyond saying that at this moment I am not
in a position to reply more satisfactorily to
the question which the hon. Member has
raised.
Mr. MAGEE: The answer is perfectly
satisfactory, and I thank the noble Marquess
for it.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty-five minutes before
Seven of the clock.
Unemployment Fund Bill-
Second Reading.
4 SEPTEMBER 1925
THE SENATE.

Friday, 4th September, 1925.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Suspension of Standing Order.
Moved: That Standing Order No. XXV be
dispensed with in respect of the Unemployment Fund Bill and the Dangerous Drugs
Bill. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ordered: That said Standing Order be
dispensed with.
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY
(Leader of the House): In moving the
Second Reading of the Unemployment Fund
(Northern Ireland) Bill I shall not find it
necessary to detain the House very long.
It is
a simple measure, with a single
operative Clause, one of a series of measures
which the deplorable condition of trade and
industry have made it necessary for the
Government to bring forward. Its object is
to enable my right hon. Friend, the Minister
of Finance, to make advances to the Unemployment Fund, in order that those who
are suffering under the terrible scourge of
unemployment may receive the benefits to
which the various Unemployment Insurance
Acts entitle them.
Last March, it will be within the recollection of the House, Parliament passed a
precisely similar measure to give the
Ministry of Finance power to advance a
further sum of
seven hundred and fifty
thousand pounds to the Unemployment Fund
beyond the two million, two hundred and
fifty thousand pounds which had already
been authorised. We had hoped that it
would not be necessary to ask Parliament for
further authority until November 1st next
at the very earliest. Unfortunately, unemployment has not decreased. It has not even
stood still, and to-day, as my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance, said in
another place, in moving the financial resolution connected with this Bill, there are
some ten thousand more persons totally unemployed than there were at the date of the
passing of the Unemployment Fund Act in
the month of March last. He said-I will
give the House his words-
-
the figures of unemployment stood at
an average figure of, roughly, forty-six
thousand totally unemployed, and about
ten thousand on systematic short time.
To-day, as nearly as can be computed, the
corresponding figures are, fifty-six thousand
wholly unemployed, and six thousand
partially out of work."
It is under these circumstances-these most
regrettable and menacing circumstances---
that the Government is to-day asking Parliament to pass the measure before the House.
It is, I believe, impossible for us to give
any accurate forecast of the period for which
the additional advances to the Fund contemplated under the present measure will
suffice. I can only tell the House, as I have
already told hon. Members again and again,
that this Government is determined to maintain a social standard for the people of
Ulster equivalent generally to that of the
people of Great Britain. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear.) It was one of the root principles of the Government of Ireland Act,
and the policy connected therewith, that no
man should be the worse off in status or
in prospects for its passage and operation.
We take our stand upon that principle, and
we feel sure that not only the British
Government, but the whole British people
whom they represent, will understand the
elementary justice upon which we have based
our position, and will support us and assist
us, as far as they can, in our resolve.
Before I resume my seat, I should like
to emphasise this point, namely, that the
present measure-this Unemployment Fund
Bill has nothing whatsoever to do with the
conditions of Unemployment Insurance.
Upon those conditions, and upon the changes
in prospect I do not propose to speak to-day.
In fact, I do not know whether you will
allow discussion on that subject, which, so
far as I can see, has no other connection
with the present measure, except that it
also is concerned with unemployment. In
any event, my right hon. Friend, the
Minister of Labour, has very kindly come
into the House this afternoon in order to
reply to any points which may arise in the
course of our debate, and it is to him that
hon. Members will, I am sure, look for
their enlightenment, rather than to myself,
on this very technical measure. I beg to
move.
Mr. LONG: It is very desirable, I think,
and even necessary, that the money asked
for should be voted, so that no person in our
area may want for the necessaries of life.
It must be conceded, however, that the receipt of money which has not been earned
has a demoralising effect. It may not be
possible to provide work-to provide schemes
to supply work for all the unemployed-but
Unemployment Fund Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
I think the utmost ingenuity should be exercised to devise schemes in so many different
localities that an opportunity would be given
to as many as possible of the unemployed to
obtain work, so that in very few cases indeed
should it be necessary to hand over money
absolutely unearned. Schemes of employment should, if possible, be useful and economic, but even a useless scheme is, I think,
better than none. There are one hundred
and one unemployed who would very much
prefer to convey sand from one place to
another and take it back again rather than
receive an unemployment grant without
work.
Now, under the heading of useful work I
would very respectfully suggest that much
might be done by the Ministry of Labour in
improving dangerous corners of our roads in
the Six Counties. The Minister of Labour
has been very sympathetic in the past, and
I am sure he would agree that the road
authorities, and indeed all local authorities,
have co-operated with him in this matter.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon. Member
is now speaking on schemes for the relief of
unemployment. I propose that the House
should stick closely to the Motion before it.
I do not consider that schemes or measures
for the relief of unemployment have anything
to do with the Question before the House.
Mr. LONG: The reason I was digressing
was that I saw by the papers to-day that invitations were extended for helpful suggestions. I quite agree that it is not strictly in
order, and I bow to your ruling. I very
strongly support the Second Reading of this
Bill.
Ι
Mr. DORMAN: If there is a saddening
aspect that one can contemplate it is a
number of able-bodied men able and willing
to work deprived of the right to work.
quite agree with the hon. Member opposite
that money unearned has a demoralising
effect, but I can speak from practical experience, inasmuch as I am probably the only
Member of this House who has ever stood in
the queue, and I signed off yesterday from
unemployment. I can conscientiously say
that for a period of over six months I have
strenuously endeavoured to get some person
to purchase my power of labour, both of hand
and brain, in order that I might bring home
the wherewithal to purchase the necessaries
of life for my wife and family.
I understand I was quite out of order
yesterday in my remarks in this place, and
I therefore crave the indulgence of this
House and also your indulgence, as I am
entirely ignorant of the procedure and order.
I should be obliged, therefore, if you call
me to order and direct my steps if I should
happen to wander.
If you will permit me, I would like to make
a suggestion to the Minister of Labour, and it
is this. The attention of the community
might be drawn by means of propaganda to
the latent industries of the Northern Province. I happened to visit the coal mine at
Coalisland last week. I inspected the face
of the coal, and had a long interview with the
manager. I understand that that undertaking is capable of absorbing from 600 to
800 more men than are at present employed
there. I would, therefore, suggest to the
Ministry of Labour the usefulness of undertaking propaganda with the object of creating
in the public mind a willingness to give preferential treatment to the products of the
Province.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry to interrupt
the hon. Member, but I must point out that
what he is saying now has nothing whatever
to do with the motion before the House. The
Question before the House is whether the
sum of £3,750,000 be substituted for £3,000,000
as the limit of the total amount of advances
which may be made under the Unemployment
Fund Act for discharging the liability of the
Northern Ireland Unemployment Fund.
Mr. DORMAN: I bow to your ruling. 1
regret that I have to stop, because I was engaged for some time in trying to sell the
products of the Six Counties, and I had made
an estimate of the number of men, women.
and boys who might have been employed had
the people given preferential treatment to
the product I was endeavouring to sell in
Belfast, and which I could not sell. I have
no desire to oppose in any way the granting
of this sum of money which is necessary to
permit the Minister of Labour to continue
to pay unemployment benefit. I have heard
charges made that there was very widespread
malingering amongst the unemployed. I can
assure hon. Members that if they stood in a
queue and heard the honest sentiments of
the men unemployed they would find there
are very few indeed of the unemployed in
Belfast who are unwilling to accept honest
toil of any kind in order to earn the wherewithal to live. With these few remarks I
support the Bill.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Perhaps
you would allow me for a moment to leave
the subject before the House to say how
pleased I am to know that we have an
addition of one practical workman to this
House. I would not like him to go away
with the idea that he is the only workman,
and the first workman, to enter the Senate,
because I have been in the unfortunate
position of having to go through the Labour
Exchange to obtain the dole, and I have not
drawn it during the last few years only. 1
have worked in the workshops, and I have
been thrown out of work occasionally owing
237 Unemployment Fund Bill-

4 SEPTEMBER 1925
Second Reading.
to slackness and other causes, and I was
very glad to get the small benefit that 1
thought I was entitled to.
I am glad the Government are continuing
to keep on parallel lines with Great Britain
in the payment of this benefit, and I am
pleased that they are borrowing a further
sum to enable them to do that. There is
no doubt that when work is not obtainable
the people must be provided for. It is
impossible to think that the wives and
children of the unemployed can be allowed
to starve, and I believe our Government
have done all they possibly can, not only
to provide benefit but to find work for the
people of the Six Counties. I do hope the
Government will endeavour to follow the
lead of the Imperial Government in other
respects as well as in regard to unemployment benefit. I refer particularly to the
Disqualification Clause and to Pensions for
Widows and Orphans.
There is, unfortunately, a great deal of
cant and humbug talked with reference to
this unemployment benefit. Our Government
has been accused of being indifferent to the
position of the workers, and the Minister
of Labour has been looked upon as a soulless
individual who has no sympathy with the
unemployed. Anyone who has any acquaintance with the Minister of Labour knows that
there is no man who is more sympathetic
towards these people than he is, and who
is more anxious to do all that is necessary
to further their interests. What troubles
me most is that the very people who are
complaining about the action of the Government and the Minister of Labour do not
seem to be consistent, nor are they in
earnest, because we find that they are
actually fleecing those who are in receipt
of unemployment benefit.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon. Member,
who is an old Member of the Senate, will
remember my ruling.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I can only
repeat I am very pleased that the Government are following on the lines adopted by
the Imperial Government, and intend to
pay benefit as it is paid in Great Britain.
I hope there may be some improvement in
trade, and that we will be able to provide
work for our people instead of giving them
the dole.
Mr. BARBOUR: I would like to support
as ably as I can the passage of this Bill,
and to embrace this opportunity of congratulating the Government on putting into
practice one of the promises or indications
they gave of their policy when they said
they would do their best to see that those
in our midst who are suffering from unemployment should, at least as far as our
Government could see to it, be as well
treated as those across the water. I would
like to pay my tribute to the Minister of
Labour for his action in not confining himself to urging Parliament to pass money in
order that the end the Minister has in view
might be achieved. He has used all the
influence he can to get other spending bodies
to do their utmost to contribute towards
this end, and it gives me great pleasure to
lend my support to this Bill. It is with
extreme regret that I see that such a Bill
as this is necessary, and while I give it
my full support I recognise the grave responsibility that attaches to anyone who supports
the Bill, on account of the heavy burden
it must throw on our financial resources.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): I desire to express my indebtedness to all hon. Members for the sympathetic
way they have looked upon this Bill. There
has really been no criticism put forward
except of a friendly character, and, therefore, I think my duty will be fulfilled by
simply thanking hon. Members for the support which they have given to the Government in connection with this important
matter. When the new Unemployment Bill
comes before this House to enable us to
continue to carry out the Government's
policy of keeping on parallel lines with
Great Britain I am sure my hon. Friends
in this House as well as my hon. Friends
in the other House will have full opportunity of criticising the measure and considering it Clause by Clause.
In the meantime I can assure this House,
as I have already assured the other House,
that the Government will continue with all
the means at its disposal to do everything
that is humanly possible to help
the unemployed persons in our area. We
have the greatest possible sympathy with
them. We wish that industries were in a
state which would enable employment to be
found for them all. In the meantime all
that the Government can do, and all that
anybody has ever suggested the Government
can do, is to encourage those industries that
are at present in existence, and to encourage other industries to be established in our
midst; to co-operate with local authorities
to provide as much work as is possible; and
to pay to those for whom work cannot be
found the same unemployment benefit as is
paid in Great Britain. The Bill which this
House is going to agree to will enable the
Government to continue to carry out this
policy.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill be now committed.
-(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
Dangerous Drugs Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time.--(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass. (The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the Commons
to acquaint them that the Senate agree to
the said Bill without any Amendment.
Dangerous Drugs Bill (Northern
Ireland).
Order for Second Reading read.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY:
The preamble of the Dangerous Drugs Bill
which I have the honour to bring before the
House for a Second Reading this afternoon
gives what I think hon. Members will find
a very clear account of the circumstances
which make the passage of this highly
technical Measure by the Parliament of
Northern Ireland expedient.
Immediately before the adjournment of the
Parliament in
Imperial
August the
Dangerous Drugs Act, 1925, was passed in
order to amend the Dangerous Drugs Acts,
1920, and 1923, for the purpose of enabling
effect to be given to the Geneva Convention
of 19th February, 1925. The Imperial Act
made certain changes and additions to the
present law in relation to opium and certain
Imperial
other dangerous drugs, and the
Government have addressed the Government
of Northern Irelnd as follows:-
"As the Bill does not extend to Northern
Ireland except in so far as it amends the
in relation to
Dangerous Drugs Acts
matters with respect to which the Parliament of Northern Ireland have no power
to make laws, it is desired that a corresponding Measure should be passed Ly the
Parliament of Northern Ireland in order
that His Majesty's Government may be able
to ratify the Convention for the whole of
Great Britain and Northern Ireland."
In these circumstances this Bill has been
drafted in order to assimilate the law in
Northern Ireland to the law in force in
Great Britain in relation to the points dealt
with at the Geneva Convention, so far as
those points are within the competence of
the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
CX-
I feel the House will require some
planation of the haste with which it is asked
to deal with these matters. As hon. Members
are aware, I would be the last person to
attempt to fetter the rights of this House.
The last day for the signature of the Geneva
Convention is the 30th of the present month.
and the Imperial Government is anxious not
to be behindhand with its signature. I have
the greater confidence in asking the House
to agree to our pressing matters forward,
inasmuch as I am sure that all hon. Members
are in agreement with the Government, and
with civilized communities generally in assisting the suppression of the traffic in dangerous
drugs all over the world.
I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: Certainly I do not complain
in any way of the fact that this House is
asked to pass this measure into law with unusual dispatch, but I am a little inclined to
regret that circumstances have rendered it
incumbent upon us to deal with the matter so
very rapidly. I am quite sure it will be impossible for us to do other than pass the Bill
and to do it at once, for the alternative is not
open to contemplation. Were this House to
refuse to pass the Bill the effect would be
that we would be doing everything in our
power-and our powers are limited-to enable
Northern Ireland to become a dumping
ground for human wreckage addicted to
drugs of this description. My regret does not
arise really out of anything in the Bill itself,
but I should have liked the House to have
been in a position to have a little more time
for considering one or two matters which
seem to me to arise out of the Bill.
I am somewhat diffident in referring to
these matters as I fear I may not be strictly
in order, but as I see that the Bill is introducing or extending a definite code dealing
with dangerous drugs I think that what I am
about to say will not transgress the rules
which you have laid down. I am not going
to ask for a definite answer to any of these
matters to-day because their decision would
not either impede or expedite the passage of
the Bill itself. They relate more to the
general question of dangerous drugs, and
therefore I think it would be better that I
should put a separate question down at a more
convenient date during the Autumn Session
in order that later on it may be cleared up.
The point to which I want to direct attention is the regulations indicated in Clause
1 of the Bill before us. We are by Clause 1
simply proposing to add new drugs of a
dangerous and undesirable character to the
list of drugs which are already subject to
regulation. The power of regulation was given
by the Act of 1920-the principal Act-and I
am in some doubt as to whether any power
of regulation is left in the hands of our own
Minister of Home Affairs. Originally by the
1920 measure, as the House will remember,
powers for licensing as regards importation
were given to the Secretary of State, that
being a matter with which this Parliament
Dangerous Drugs Bill-
Second Reading.
4 SEPTEMBER 1925
has no business, and also powers of regulation
were given to the Secretary of State, but the
position seems to be a little in doubt as to
whether our own Minister under this Act and
the other Acts will have any power whatever
for making what I might term domestic regulations, and it is on that point that I propose
to ask the Government at a later date for
a little further information. It is not necessary to do more than point out these matters
for the purposes of the Bill immediately
before us because we are simply extending
the scope of the Act.
The other matter which is of the same
nature arises from the discussions on our own
Dangerous Drugs Act of last year. It may
be remembered that when that Bill--our Bill
was in 1924 and is referred to again and again
in the Bill before us-was introduced both in
this House and in another place, the noble
Marquess here, and the right hon. Gentleman
the Attorney-General there, most clearly laid
before us the limitations of the measure--how
far the Government could go and how far
it could not go. But speaking in another
place the Attorney General drew special attention to the difficulty that our Government
felt as regards certain matters which he described as provisions dealing with persons
committing acts which in a foreign country
would be offences against the laws passed in
that country, in fulfilment of the International Opium Convention, 1912. The International Opium Convention, 1912, as I need
scarcely remind the House, is in reality the
ultimate basis of the Bill now before us.
The right hon. Gentleman went on to say
that our Bill of 1924 could not take the necessary powers in connection with the limitations imposed upon us by the Government of
Ireland Act, and he said representations were
to be made in order that the necessary
clauses may be inserted in some Imperial
measure. But I shall ask at a more appropriate season for information as to whether
that point has been covered in the Imperial
Act, 1925, and if not whether it is the intention of our Government to enter into the
usual Departmental discussions with a view
to that being done. I think the matter ought
not to be left indefinitely open.
As far as the Bill itself goes the only novel
feature of it seems to be the introduction
of Indian hemp. The rest is little more
than an extension of the terms of the
earlier Act. Indian hemp, I am informed,
is capable of being made for abuse in every
conceivable manner. It can be smoked, it
can be drunk, or even eaten as a kind of
sweet. In any case it is most deleterious.
There is nothing beyond that in the Bill
that calls for any attention except that it
may be the extremely convenient manner in
which the Bill has been drawn up. The
preamble and the method of arrangement
are really conceived in such a way as to
make a somewhat difficult subject. exceedingly easy to follow, and also if I may say
so to make the discussion of this subject
even when time is short much more easy
and much more complete than it otherwise
would be. I have no hesitation in supporting
the Second Reading of the Bill, but I will,
as I have said, at a more convenient time
ask for a little more information in regard
to these matters arising out of it.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this House do now resolve
itself into Committee on the said Bill.-
(The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion negatived.
Moved: That this Bill be now read the
Third time. (The Marquess of Londonderry.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: That this Bill do now pass.--(The
Marquess of Londonderry.)
And a Message was sent to the Commons to
acquaint them that the Senate agreed to the
said Bill without any amendment.
The Adjournment.
The MARQUESS OF LONDONDERRY: I
beg to move that this House do now
adjourn. It will be necessary for the
House to meet again at 12-15 p.m. on Monday
next. The business on that occasion will be
of a purely formal character-for the purpose
of receiving the Royal Assent to the Bills
which have been passed by both Houses.
On Question, Motion, "That this House de
now adjourn," agreed to.
Adjourned
accordingly

that all Bills sent up from the House of
Commons will be passed through all their
stages at this day's Sitting? If so, as a
novice and a new-comer to this House I do
not think I would be justified in voting on
any Measure sent up to the Senate for consideration, the contents of which I have not
had sufficient time to digest.
Mr. SPEAKER: The Motion to suspend
Standing Order No. XXV is only in respect
of one financial Bill which it is proposed to
pass through all its Stages at this day's
Sitting. That Question has already been
decided, and therefore the hon. Member is
out of order.
EXPIRING LAWS CONTINUANCE
BILL(S).
A Bill to continue certain Expiring Laws
as respects Northern Ireland, was presented
by Viscount Massereene and Ferrard, read
a First time, ordered to be printed, and
to be read a Second time on Tuesday next.
(Bill No. 4.)
CENSUS BILL(S).
A Bill for taking the Census for Northern
Ireland in the year 1926, and for otherwise
collecting statistical information, was presented by Viscount Massereene and Ferrard,
read a First time, and ordered to be printed,
and to be read a Second time on Tuesday
next. (Bill No. 5).
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
That the Commons have read the Wright's
Divorce Bill a Second time; they have passed
the Government Loans and Exchequer Bill
to which they desire the concurrence of the
Senate.
